# Thread: Energy Saving Bulbs May Harm Your Skin Read more at http://medicaldaily.com/news/2012
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/energy-saving-bulbs-may-harm-your-skin-read-more-at-http-medicaldaily-com-news-2012.2595/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 1
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### chocolate — 2012-07-21T03:16:45+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/energy-saving-bulbs-may-harm-your-skin-read-more-at-http-medicaldaily-com-news-2012.2595/post-39871>

[http://medicaldaily.com/news/20120720/10984/cfl-incandescent-bulbs-energy-saving-lamps.htm](http://http://medicaldaily.com/news/20120720/10984/cfl-incandescent-bulbs-energy-saving-lamps.htm)

> A new study says that energy efficient compact fluorescent light bulbs that benefit the environment may be harmful to your skin.
>
>
>
> For the study, the researchers collected CFL bulbs purchased from different locations across Suffolk and Nassau counties in New York State and measured the amount of UV emissions and the integrity of each bulbâ€™s phosphor coatings.
>
> energy saving bulbs
>
>
>
>
>
> They found that all CFL bulbs that they studied had cracks on their phosphorus coatings and they had significant levels of Ultraviolet C and Ultraviolet A.
>
>
>
> Then the researchers tested the effect of exposure of these bulbs on healthy human skin cells. Researchers repeated tests using incandescent light bulbs (of the same intensity).
>
>
>
> In another part of the study they introduced Titanium dioxide (TiO2) nanoparticles, used in personal care products, to see how its presence along with CFL or incandescent bulbs affects the human skin cells. Titanium dioxide is added to products because it absorbs UV radiations.
>
> "Our study revealed that the response of healthy skin cells to UV emitted from CFL bulbs is consistent with damage from ultraviolet radiation. Skin cell damage was further enhanced when low dosages of TiO2 nanoparticles were introduced to the skin cells prior to exposure," said Miriam Rafailovich, professor of materials science and engineering at Stony Brook University and an author of the study.
>
>
>
> Researchers found that, whether Titanium dioxide was present or not, incandescent light bulb of the same intensity had no effect on skin cells.
>
>
>
> Researchers say that an additional glass covers might reduce some of the harmful effects of CFL bulbs.
>
>
>
>
>
> "Despite their large energy savings, consumers should be careful when using compact fluorescent light bulbs. Our research shows that it is best to avoid using them at close distances and that they are safest when placed behind an additional glass cover," said Rafailovich.
>
> Read more at [http://medicaldaily.com/news/20120720/10984/cfl-incandescent-bulbs-energy-saving-lamps.htm#IGCrXXuqIGK51pcA.99](http://http://medicaldaily.com/news/20120720/10984/cfl-incandescent-bulbs-energy-saving-lamps.htm#IGCrXXuqIGK51pcA.99)

Another reason not to use artificial light.

# Thread: My thought process sucks fasting ends weight up
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/my-thought-process-sucks-fasting-ends-weight-up.2601/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 8
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Raiken3712 — 2012-07-21T17:42:06+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/my-thought-process-sucks-fasting-ends-weight-up.2601/post-40002>

I think I'm ok now...I realize its temporary...its not a big deal, but I just broke my fast yesterday and I went up 3.6 pounds. At first I was annoyed and it bothered me....I guess my thoughts aren't that bad...after all it didn't really take me that long to realize this is just temporary..I knew before that when I started eating again my weight would go up a bit. Also some of it might be used for repaired muscle...it doesn't have to be fat gain. Though I am overweight and someone posted a charge that overweight people...or obese can't remember which tend to use more muscle fiber to fuel when fasting not all muscle but more than a fit person would while fasting.

I'm so hyped and anxious about getting to my low point again...I know its silly and yet I still have a momentary craziness over it. My low point before I started eating crap was 244...I thought it was 249 but looked at my Calorie Count logs and found the 244.6. I started at 285 went to 244 and regained to 277. I'm now back to 251.6 from 248.

I'm probably a bit OCD I know it would probably help to just ignore the scale...but I'm just so used to it. I like seeing the numbers go down. Oh well I"ll get to my goal eventually. On that note any thoughts on whats optimal weight for a 6'1" Male with a large frame? I want to be somewhat strong and athletic....probably not shooting for elite...probably just want to live a long life with lots of energy. Much depends on body fat %.

Thanks as always

### Raiken3712 — 2012-07-21T17:45:15+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/my-thought-process-sucks-fasting-ends-weight-up.2601/post-40003>

The other thing I hoped wouldn't occur is my blood sugar going up as much as it has.....my fbg is 101...high again while I was fasting it was 53-65. Ideal is 70-80...maybe I'm eating too much protein? Or maybe its just my eating last night....I got off work late and ate at around 9. I stayed up till around 12-1 to let my body deal with the food before I went to sleep. I guess I did eat a few steak fries...like maybe 7-8...never eat fries anymore.

I just hoped my FBG after fasting might lower a bit..I guess even with 101 its lower than 110 it was before and it might be the late eating...I'll test tomorrow and see I guess.

### PrimalPam — 2012-07-21T17:59:05+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/my-thought-process-sucks-fasting-ends-weight-up.2601/post-40004>

Raiken, I wouldn't even test BG after eating like that. Then again, we aren't supposed to eat like that to be optimal.

Remember: Eat your last meal before 7 pm. Get to bed around 9-10 pm.

Re-read the blog about Circadian Biology (I'm sorry, I can't remember which one). The 3 most important hours in human biology are sleep from 12 a.m. to 3 a.m., where all of your cells are being repaired. If you do not sleep during these hours, your body is waking up to bits of unrepaired cells trying to allow you to function.

Whenever I have a night where I can't sleep during those hours (so rare these days, only happens when I diss the protocol), I lay awake thinking....dammit...I'm missing my most important circadian hours!!!

Eating some steak fries won't kill you....but these days, before I eat something like that, I think, "Is it optimal?"

And when you do eat/sleep that way for a day, the important thing is, the very next time you eat, make it optimal. Wake up to BP coffee, whole eggs, salmon. Do your CT that day (not sure if you're doing it, but it's become part of my almost-daily routine). And get to sleep by 10.

I've found that it's become habit to eat this way, do the CT, buy fresh seafood, etc.

Also, get off the scale. You'll know when you've lost weight: your pants will be looser. These days, I only weigh when I KNOW I've lost, i.e. my clothes are so much looser, I know the scale will show a loss. Otherwise, we're just adding to our stress, no? Remember, scales are a modern invention.

### AKMan — 2012-07-21T20:17:31+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/my-thought-process-sucks-fasting-ends-weight-up.2601/post-40034>

Let me tell you about my experiences with weight fluctuations and fasting. I'm 46, m, 5'11, 165lbs. When I was in the 190's - 200's, my weight would fluctuate wildly. I could go a week at 190, then all of a sudden be up tp 210 and take weeks to get it off--all eating primally a-la Mark Sisson. Since i've lost nearly all excess body fat, my weight is very stable. My normal waking weight is 165 and my normal before-bed weight is 169. I can eat little or lots on a given day and my body regulates my weight right in that range. I have about 6 months in that range, so this isn't a one week observation.

I started weekly, one-day fasts a couple months ago. I stop eating usually after dinner on Wednesday and resume on lunch Friday. This is an approximate 42 hr fast.

Going in my weight will be 169. Next morning, 165. Next night (after 24 hr fasting--just water) my weight will be about 164. Next morning (after 36 hrs fasted) weight will be about 163. After 42 hours 162.

Then I eat normally for 2 meals, after which my weight will be 165, next morning still 165, next night back to my original 169.

In table form:

Hour 1 - 169 (after last meal)

Hour 12 - 165 (upon waking)

Hour 24 - 164

Hour 36 - 163

Hour 42 - 162

Hour 48 - 165 (after last meal)

Hour 60 - 165 (upon waking)

Hour 72 - 169 (after last meal)

### Raiken3712 — 2012-07-21T20:32:05+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/my-thought-process-sucks-fasting-ends-weight-up.2601/post-40037>

Thanks guys. I've been contemplating BP coffee...but the upgrade coffee is so expensive 18 dollars for a pound or something...how many cups is that. Not sure its worth it with the amount of money I have.

I do try to use coconut oil/butter as part of most meals though. So I get the good butter and oil combination. I have Kerrygold unsalted as well so its good butter. Tropical Traditions coconut oil because they had a nice sale for a 5 gallon with free shipping at the time. I really wish Nutiva had a sale around than...I woulda rather had 5 gallons of Nutiva as I think its better from my reading...at least nutritionally. I heard Tropical Traditions was fermented which is good for many things but wasn't as good for Coconut oil for some reason...I'm not absolutely sure about the truth of it though so maybe Tropical Traditions isn't that much worse....or maybe its better lol.

### PrimalPam — 2012-07-21T21:02:54+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/my-thought-process-sucks-fasting-ends-weight-up.2601/post-40048>

Re: BP coffee. I actually buy my own coffee at the grocery store and make my own version of BP coffee (CO & turmeric).

Also remember the guidelines: eat only 2-3 times a day, no snacking. For me, a huge BAB holds me till about 2, when I feel like a small meal (a couple of spoonfuls of Nutiva CO Manna & a few bites of smoked salmon, for example). Dinner around 5, which is a piece of seafood, veggies, Ghee. But being done eating by 7 pm, no matter what, is really important. If I know I'm not coming home till really late, I pack something to eat around 5, so that when I get home, I go straight to bed.

### Raiken3712 — 2012-07-21T21:23:01+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/my-thought-process-sucks-fasting-ends-weight-up.2601/post-40063>

What I'm curious about is other good coffee they make such a big deal about mycotoxins and make it sound like only their coffee is a good source....is it good for me if its full of mycotoxins....I don't know how big of a deal they are and if I just need some other thing to offset the negative. [http://upgradedself.com/bulletproof-foods.html](http://http://upgradedself.com/bulletproof-foods.html) This coffee is what I'm referring to just to clarify.

Are there other good coffees with less toxins that don't cost an arm and a leg?...Sigh. So many different factors to way and it always ups the cost. Naturally raised animals more expensive, coffee without harmful toxins, expensive as hell, wild caught fish more expensive....drives me crazy. Its nice at least to hear that Selenium in Fish makes Mercury a non-issue...or at least maybe it does? I somewhat understand why binding with Selenium might be beneficial...if its bound to Selenium it can't be bound to receptor sites...but is that the only negative to having it floating around in the body?

Health is so complicated.....sigh.

### bigknitwit — 2012-07-21T23:52:08+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/my-thought-process-sucks-fasting-ends-weight-up.2601/post-40089>

I experimented with fasting starting early last fall. Prior to that I had been low carb eating and doing P90X to maintain my weight, but it was no longer working (I was gaining slowly). I did a 5.5 day fast and lost 14 pounds I was ecstatic! Then the weight started coming back on, real quick. 3 pounds the first day, 2.5 the next, etc. A week or so later I decided it was time to start yet another fast, to drop some of the weight I had lost. I also started mixing in intermittent fast (eating only after 4:30pm). I cycled between intermittent fasting and 24-48 hour fasts all fall. It was the only way to even think about maintaining my new found low weight. Finallly, on Dec 24th, I decided to take a break from all this fasting/intermittent fasting, and I gained 8 pounds over 6 days. I was horrified. But after so many months of such restricted eating, I couldn't keep up the fasting routine (in case it's not obvious, I hadn't heard of JK at this point).

I gained still another 11 pounds in 5 weeks. I tried to eat "less", it didn't matter. The weight kept coming, with no slowing in sight. I finally decided it was time to get some testing done. I also did an elimination diet (down to turkey only for 24 hours) to figure out which foods were causing the explosive weight gains. It took some time but I found I could only eat fresh meats, fish, veggies, limited fruit, and coconut oil. I also found leptin RX around this time, and implemented it pretty strictly (what choice did I have? unlimited weight gain?). Welll, slowly, I lost all the weight - 20 pounds. It took about 4.5 months, and I still have a long way to go healthwise. But even today, I am still ecstatic that I can maintain, even lose weight without fasting!! Obviously I was leptin resistant, among other things.

All this written to say that I completely understand the frustration of regaining the weight lost in a fast.

# Thread: Menstrual cycles and things female :)
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/menstrual-cycles-and-things-female.2602/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 4
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### PrimalPam — 2012-07-21T18:07:05+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/menstrual-cycles-and-things-female.2602/post-40006>

Did my menstrual cycle return exactly 28 days after stopping the pill? Really?

Went to a new OB/GYN (from paleophysiciansnetwork.com), who took me off the pill (I'm 50) and wants me to be off for 3 months before doing hormone testing.

In the past, when I've gone off the pill, it's taken my body a few months to regulate, with spotting, then heavy periods and mind-bending cramps. Last OB/GYN wanted to hysterectomize (lol) me to get rid of the "parts I don't need anymore". I imagined one of those 1920's B&W horror movies where a mad doctor with a huge butcher knife chased me around the OR, and practically left in my gown.

These days, doing CT 5 x/week @ 50-55F, eating Paleo w/seafood almost daily, BP coffee (turmeric, CO) every day, CO in everything, sleeping great, mentally clearer. And 28 days to the DAY that I last took my pill, my period returned...with ZERO cramps. I woke up this morning to it, actually mad at myself that I hadn't even counted the days and worried about my sheets (sorry, graphic). I figured it'd be several weeks, with spotting, etc., but no, this is the actual thing.

I feel like I'm 15 again.

### NWgirl — 2012-07-21T20:05:09+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/menstrual-cycles-and-things-female.2602/post-40029>

Wow that is amazing! Gives me hope that my normal cycle will return as I get healthier. I'm only 43 and I'm already in "pre-menopause". But come to think of it, since I started LR I haven't had ONE hot flash at all. Hmmm...!

Kim

### PrimalPam — 2012-07-21T20:23:25+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/menstrual-cycles-and-things-female.2602/post-40035>

Kim, I was having hot flashes all through my mid to late 40's. One of the symptoms which led my ex-OB/GYN to recommend a hysterectomy. The funny thing is, I forgot all about the hot flashes till you just wrote about it. Haven't had them in so long now (since full-on Paleo & LR).

### NWgirl — 2012-07-21T20:28:23+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/menstrual-cycles-and-things-female.2602/post-40036>

I think we're defying aging! For me it's starting more on the inside though. So I'm getting healthier from the inside out.

# Thread: You're Already Eating Bugs!
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/youre-already-eating-bugs.2606/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 23
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### kathylu — 2012-07-21T20:48:01+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/youre-already-eating-bugs.2606/post-40041>

How closely are shrimps, lobsters and crabs related to insects? Really closely. All are arthropods.

"Arthropods are animals with segmented bodies and six or more jointed legs. They are the largest animal group on Earth. In fact, more than three out of four of all animals are arthropods. They are found everywhere - on land, in trees, in freshwater and saltwater, and even underground. Arthropods are generally small. Most are less than 1 cm long. Some arthropods, however, are quite large. The giant king crab, for example, measures over 3.2 m from the tip of one outstretched leg to another. Some of the most familiar arthropods are butterflies, beetles, flies, ants, bees, spiders, scorpions, shrimp, lobster and crabs."

So, maybe we can find some tasty land based insects? What do you think?

### kathylu — 2012-07-21T20:51:05+0300

### Melinda — 2012-07-21T23:46:23+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/youre-already-eating-bugs.2606/post-40088>

I had a discussion with a Boy Scout Leader a few weeks ago who was in front of a display of live bugs. He proceeded to eat a few. I asked him what they tasted like and he said "dirt". He kept a jar of peanuts in the shell on the table to eat (shell and all) between bugs to get the taste out. He also fed the bugs cheezits and peanuts to improve how they taste. He described how to eat a scorpion and said they tasted good like crab. He had kind of a wild look in his eyes.

### Shijin13 — 2012-07-21T23:55:41+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/youre-already-eating-bugs.2606/post-40090>

Ants taste like lemon-aid. Best way to catch them is put a dollop of honey in a glass jar and put the jar over the top of that ant hill. When you get a good amt put the lid on and put the jar in the freezer. Once they are frozen shake the ants out on to a cookie sheet and broil on high. Add them to any dish. . Learned this in survival school.

swarms of flying ants come and land.

# Thread: Magnesium Oil

**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 19
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Glamazon — 2012-07-21T21:16:39+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/magnesium-oil.2609/post-40060>

Here's a cheap source on Amazon. [http://www.amazon.com/Magnesium-Oil-64-1-9-Liquid/dp/B002I5LO2S/ref=pd\_sbs\_hpc\_8](http://http://www.amazon.com/Magnesium-Oil-64-1-9-Liquid/dp/B002I5LO2S/ref=pd_sbs_hpc_8)

Does anyone have any insights to share about it? I am thinking about getting some.

### kathylu — 2012-07-22T13:33:44+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/magnesium-oil.2609/post-40198>

Why not just buy magnesium chloride and make your own? It's much cheaper that way. To make a saturated solution, just stir MgCl2 into distilled water until it won't dissolve any longer...voila, magnesium oil!

### kathylu — 2012-07-22T13:34:28+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/magnesium-oil.2609/post-40199>

[http://www.amazon.com/Magnesium-Chloride-Flakes-Swanson-Ultra/dp/B002E62HXQ/ref=sr\_1\_1?s=hpc&ie=UTF8&qid=1342952986&sr=1-1&keywords=magnesium+chloride](http://http://www.amazon.com/Magnesium-Chloride-Flakes-Swanson-Ultra/dp/B002E62HXQ/ref=sr_1_1?s=hpc&ie=UTF8&qid=1342952986&sr=1-1&keywords=magnesium+chloride)

### Destiny — 2012-07-22T14:03:38+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/magnesium-oil.2609/post-40202>

> **kathylu;40385 wrote:** Why not just buy magnesium chloride and make your own? It's much cheaper that way. To make a saturated solution, just stir MgCl2 into distilled water until it won't dissolve any longer...voila, magnesium oil!

Kathylu, thank you for the suggestion. What ratio of the magnesium and distilled water do you use? Is it necessary to add any oil?

### kathylu — 2012-07-22T14:20:19+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/magnesium-oil.2609/post-40203>

> **Destiny;40389 wrote:** Kathylu, thank you for the suggestion. What ratio of the magnesium and distilled water do you use? Is it necessary to add any oil?

There is no ratio that I know. A "saturated solution" means to dissolve salt crystals in water until it can't hold anymore, i.e. the crystals will stop dissolving. You may have to stir it for a few minutes to find that point. Calling it magnesium "oil" is a misnomer...there's nothing but MgCl2 and water in the mix, no oil. You don't need any preservative agents because the salt concentration is too high to allow bacteria to grow.

### Destiny — 2012-07-22T15:05:10+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/magnesium-oil.2609/post-40204>

> **kathylu;40390 wrote:** There is no ratio that I know. A "saturated solution" means to dissolve salt crystals in water until it can't hold anymore, i.e. the crystals will stop dissolving. You may have to stir it for a few minutes to find that point. Calling it magnesium "oil" is a misnomer...there's nothing but MgCl2 and water in the mix, no oil. You don't need any preservative agents because the salt concentration is too high to allow bacteria to grow.

Thanks for explaining.

### janagram — 2012-07-22T19:18:50+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/magnesium-oil.2609/post-40233>

I have a gallon jug of it (that I ordered over the net) and its great for adding to baths, or diluting and rubbing on legs, feet...it's not oil, as mentioned.

### NWgirl — 2012-07-23T02:00:56+0300

# Thread: Fish Cookbook?

**Subforum:** Optimal Fitness
**Posts:** 16
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### jeanie@truelongmont.com — 2012-07-21T22:11:18+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fish-cookbook.2610/post-40079>

I have an excuse to go buy a real book! I want to find the quintessential seafood cookbook. . something that shows how to process whole, and all the gory details along with amazing food. Got any ideas?

### Lyndra — 2012-07-22T01:05:50+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fish-cookbook.2610/post-40102>

Not specific to fish, but Joy of Cooking has a section on fish cleaning with illustrations. It also details how to pluck, draw & dress poultry, should you ever need to do that. Also a section on various meat cuts. Now if only the print weren't so small...

### jeanie@truelongmont.com — 2012-07-22T01:53:05+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fish-cookbook.2610/post-40118>

> **Lyndra;40289 wrote:** Not specific to fish, but Joy of Cooking has a section on fish cleaning with illustrations. It also details how to pluck, draw & dress poultry, should you ever need to do that. Also a section on various meat cuts. Now if only the print weren't so small...

Hmm, I have that one already - I am pulling it out.

### SeaHorse — 2012-07-22T01:58:06+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fish-cookbook.2610/post-40121>

I like Hugh Fernley Wittenstall....English guy who is trying to reform that country's diet through organic, local, homegrown, home raised everything. He is a fish fanatic and has fantastic recipes for things that most people don't eat. Loads of books, dvds etc. You can order from his website. "The River Cottage Fish Book" has lots of recipes that work for North America. He likes to smoke his own eels in the chimney...stuff like that.

### nonchalant — 2012-07-22T02:13:29+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fish-cookbook.2610/post-40125>

Jack Kruse has a cookbook. Great recipes, nice pics. Should be updated very soon.

Hint.

### Linz — 2012-07-26T18:23:54+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fish-cookbook.2610/post-41212>

Any (or all )of Rick Stein's books.

### Melinda — 2012-07-26T19:25:32+0300

> **Melinda;41463 wrote:** The Stein books look good at amazon but he looks so poorly. He must be washing all that seafood down with boatloads of pasta or something, poor man.

I see what you mean but he is a real advocate for high quality food and speaks and writes about it with passion and his restaurant is amazing.

Madhur Jaffrey also has some great recipes for Indian spiced fish and shellfish although they are only a small part of her books. The spiced vegetables are good too with a little manipulation of the fats.

### kathylu — 2012-07-28T03:32:24+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fish-cookbook.2610/post-41612>

Julia Child's cookbooks all have classic French seafood preparations (read "drenched in butter"). I'm a sucker for classic French cooking. Ummm...skate wing in butter garlic and herbs. Ummm...Dover sole in butter. Ummm...cocquilles St. Jacques a la provencale (butter, wine and herbs...).

# Thread: Nut butter cravings
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/nut-butter-cravings.2612/>
**Subforum:** Adrenal Rx and Leaky Gut Rx
**Posts:** 31
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Eve — 2012-07-22T03:36:47+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/nut-butter-cravings.2612/post-40160>

I have a leaky gut and I constantly battle cravings for nut butter, knowing that it's a no-no on LeakyGut Rx.

I especially get cravings for it when I'm reacting to a food/supplement (due to leaky gut) or going through a detox. Today in the afternoon my cravings for it were so bad I could have teared up if I pushed it...pretty sad, eh? lol. I think it's also b/c I'm really sick of eating an extremely plain diet -- few veggies, meat, and fish -- for so long.

To try and get rid of the craving, for dinner I had tons of coconut oil with meat, 2 salmon fillets, and broc with coconut oil...It kind of worked but not really.

What's interesting is a couple of weeks ago, I caved and finished about 1/2 small jar of organic almond butter and afterward, I felt a sense of relief and good energy. I wonder if it's b/c almond butter is rich in Mg and other vitamins....?

### Lyndra — 2012-07-23T00:42:03+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/nut-butter-cravings.2612/post-40293>

I was hooked on almond butter and finally had to quit it cold turkey when I learned I had a huge inflammation issues (high hsCRP). That probably wasn't due entirely to PUFAs but I know they didn't help. I think I was craving it for the sweet taste - I've had a lifelong sugar addiction. I know how hard it is to give up, especially when you are feeling like you have given up so many other foods you're accustomed to.

### PrimalPam — 2012-07-23T02:34:40+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/nut-butter-cravings.2612/post-40319>

> **Lyndra;40480 wrote:** I was hooked on almond butter and finally had to quit it cold turkey when I learned I had a huge inflammation issues (high hsCRP). That probably wasn't due entirely to PUFAs but I know they didn't help. I think I was craving it for the sweet taste - I've had a lifelong sugar addiction. I know how hard it is to give up, especially when you are feeling like you have given up so many other foods you're accustomed to.

+1.

I, too, have had a lifelong sugar addiction, and the nut butter fulfilled the creamy, sweet taste when I had given up so much. I just had to say no, and stop buying it. I do eat a couple of spoonfuls of Nutiva CO Manna now instead, but I always eat it as part of a meal, since I no longer snack. See, that was the other problem I had with the nut butter: I'd stick a spoon in the jar several times a day. So now, I have about 2 tbsp. of CO Manna as my "dessert", so I don't "get" to have it till it's dinnertime. Still fulfills that creamy texture sort-of-sweet thing, but way more controlled.

### janagram — 2012-07-23T22:54:54+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/nut-butter-cravings.2612/post-40460>

I too am a sufferer of nutbutter cravings....is the oil all that bad?

### Dali Dula — 2012-07-24T01:46:51+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/nut-butter-cravings.2612/post-40514>

Nuts are the only dietary challenge I have yet to master. Love almond butter spoons rolled in grated coconut. My strategy has been to buy smaller jars. This actually helps.

### jeanie@truelongmont.com — 2012-07-24T02:14:22+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/nut-butter-cravings.2612/post-40520>

what is it about that damn almond butter that makes it taste so sweet and delish? I like dipping the 99% chocolate in it. I quit buying

### Patty Cakes — 2012-07-24T02:48:34+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/nut-butter-cravings.2612/post-40531>

OMG! Here's the greatest thing: peanut butter with sardines. That's kinda good right???

I travel with the small squeeze pkgs of Justins Nut Butter and cans of sardines. Delish!

### Lyndra — 2012-07-24T02:51:32+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/nut-butter-cravings.2612/post-40533>

> **Dali Dula;40702 wrote:** Nuts are the only dietary challenge I have yet to master. Love almond butter spoons rolled in grated coconut. My strategy has been to buy smaller jars. This actually helps.

I'd just end up buying more jars . I have no cravings for it now that I don't eat it at all (almost 7 months now) but I'm sure they'd come back the minute I ate a spoonful.

### BJK77 — 2012-07-24T03:44:33+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/nut-butter-cravings.2612/post-40552>

I used to have such a love affair with nut butters! A spoonful of almond butter was like reading a trashy novel . . . total lust! I haven't had so much as a lick of a spoon for over a year now and honestly don't crave it. I did finally break down last week and have a taste of coconut butter and I was shocked that I could stop at a little taste. That NEVER happened for me before. I hate to admit it, but I could finish entire jars in the past.

### janagram — 2012-07-24T07:18:55+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/nut-butter-cravings.2612/post-40608>

It's all about context on if it's a good idea or not for you. Many factors to consider:

Your omega ratios

Your adrenal/thyroid status

Do you tend to eating disorders

Are you over snacking on them

Is your gut leaking

Many people CAN eat them, but this forum is probably skewed to those that have something wrong, thus you get that statement more often.

I think those that get closer to optimal, end up not on here trying to "figure stuff out" as much.

Are you trying

### ElectricUniverse — 2019-03-09T17:25:56+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/nut-butter-cravings.2612/post-264906>

I have few dietary vices (I eat to live rather living to eat like so many people).

But one thing I indulge in is delicious peanut butter (organic, of course).

I use it rarely as a special treat. Life is short so I don't feel guilty about a bit of extravagant indulgence.

I know it is problematic in many ways, including _mold toxins_.

One hack for those who enjoy a smidgen of peanut butter now and again is to take some activated charcoal before eating it. It should help absorb any mycotoxins you ingest. Like all nut butters, it is important to refrigerate peanut butter at all times to avoid rancidity (that may be masked by PB's strong flavor).

Being of northern European ancestry I steer towards hearty, fat-rich foods in winter and cooler seasons. Fruits and grocery veggies just simply don't satisfy or nourish at that time.

# Thread: fatty acid metabolism variant... makes me feel a little better...less like a zombie
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fatty-acid-metabolism-variant-makes-me-feel-a-little-better-less-like-a-zombie.2619/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 6
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### chocolate — 2012-07-22T19:26:47+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fatty-acid-metabolism-variant-makes-me-feel-a-little-better-less-like-a-zombie.2619/post-40236>

[http://sciencealerts.com/stories/1926012/A\_novel\_FADS1\_isoform\_potentiates\_FADS2mediated\_production\_of\_eicosanoid\_precursor\_fatty\_acids.html#.UAwoBfX4K9o](http://http://sciencealerts.com/stories/1926012/A_novel_FADS1_isoform_potentiates_FADS2mediated_production_of_eicosanoid_precursor_fatty_acids.html#.UAwoBfX4K9o)

A novel FADS1 isoform potentiates FADS2-mediated production of eicosanoid precursor fatty acids

> The fatty acid desaturase (FADS) genes code for the rate-limiting enzymes required for the biosynthesis of long-chain polyunsaturated fatty acids (LCPUFA). Here we report discovery and function of a novel FADS1 splice variant. FADS1 alternative transcript 1 (FADS1AT1) enhances desaturation of FADS2, leading to increased production of eicosanoid precursors, the first case of an isoform modulating the enzymatic activity encoded by another gene. Multiple protein isoforms were detected in primate liver, thymus, and brain. In human neuronal cells, their expression patterns are modulated by differentiation and result in alteration of cellular fatty acids. FADS1, but not FADS1AT1, localizes to endoplasmic reticulum and mitochondria. Ribosomal footprinting demonstrates that all three FADS genes are translated at similar levels. The noncatalytic regulation of FADS2 desaturation by FADS1AT1 is a novel, plausible mechanism by which several phylogenetically conserved FADS isoforms may regulate LCPUFA biosynthesis in a manner specific to tissue, organelle, and developmental stage.

### chocolate — 2012-07-23T01:11:21+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fatty-acid-metabolism-variant-makes-me-feel-a-little-better-less-like-a-zombie.2619/post-40297>

[http://www.nature.com/news/europe-nears-first-approval-for-gene-therapy-treatment-1.11048](http://http://www.nature.com/news/europe-nears-first-approval-for-gene-therapy-treatment-1.11048)

> Europe nears first approval for gene therapy treatment
>
>
>
> Therapy for rare fat-processing disease gains medicines regulator’s approval.
>
>
>
> Daniel Cressey
>
>
>
> 20 July 2012
>
> Article tools
>
>
>
>
>
> Europe’s drugs regulator has for the first time recommended a gene therapy medicine for approval.
>
>
>
> Glybera, a treatment for patients who cannot produce enough of an enzyme crucial for breaking down fat, was backed by the European Medicines Agency's (EMA) Committee for Medicinal Products for Human Use (CHMP). This recommendation has to be endorsed by the European Commission before it becomes available, but it would be unusual for the Commission to reject the recommendation.
>
>
>
> Gene therapy involves transferring genes into patients to treat their diseases. **In this case Glybera uses a virus injected into a patient to deliver a working copy of a gene for producing lipoprotein lipase (LPL). LPL deficiency affect no more than one or two people in a million.**
>
>
>
> Back in 2004 China became the first country to approve a gene therapy product for commercial use, with a treatment for cancer. But Europe and the United States have yet to endorse any gene therapy treatments and the field has been plagued by issues such as carcinogenicity.
>
>
>
> JÃ¶rn Aldag, chief executive of uniQure, the Amsterdam-based company that owns Glybera, says today’s announcement from the EMA is an “overdue signal” to the gene therapy community that things are changing. “It unlocks the potential,” he told Nature. “You will see more investment coming.”

### Cody — 2012-07-23T04:59:40+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fatty-acid-metabolism-variant-makes-me-feel-a-little-better-less-like-a-zombie.2619/post-40341>

### chocolate — 2012-07-23T05:05:49+0300

I'm not one of the two people, I'm more in line with the monkey long chain fatty acid discovery. Hopefully, its a good thing, running on a different octane, just which one?

I just posted the other article because it was a gene therapy that was virus based and I didn't want to start a new thread. There is a consequence to everything, not always good. Maybe the illness isn't an illness, but an immune response.

I know we try to do everything right to figure out if something really is wrong in our house. I wonder if those people even would know. I sure didn't think I was messing up when I was eating fruits and cheeses.

# Thread: man made jelly fish from scratch
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/man-made-jelly-fish-from-scratch.2621/>
**Subforum:** Factor X
**Posts:** 1
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### chocolate — 2012-07-23T00:56:45+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/man-made-jelly-fish-from-scratch.2621/post-40295>

[http://www.nature.com/news/artificial-jellyfish-built-from-rat-cells-1.11046](http://http://www.nature.com/news/artificial-jellyfish-built-from-rat-cells-1.11046)

Artificial jellyfish built from rat cells

Reverse-engineered life form could be used to test drugs.

Bioengineers have made an artificial jellyfish using silicone and muscle cells from a rat’s heart. The synthetic creature, dubbed a medusoid, looks like a flower with eight petals. When placed in an electric field, it pulses and swims exactly like its living counterpart.

“Morphologically, we’ve built a jellyfish. Functionally, we’ve built a jellyfish. Genetically, this thing is a rat,” says Kit Parker, a biophysicist at Harvard University in Cambridge, Massachusetts, who led the work. The project is described today in Nature Biotechnology1.

Parker’s lab works on creating artificial models of human heart tissues for regenerating organs and testing drugs, and the team built the medusoid as a way of understanding the “fundamental laws of muscular pumps”. It is an engineer’s approach to basic science: prove that you have identified the right principles by building something with them.

A jellyfish made of silicone and rat heart cells 'swims' in water when subjected to an electric field.

Harvard Univ./Caltech

In 2007, Parker was searching for new ways of studying muscular pumps when he visited the New England Aquarium in Boston, Massachusetts. “I saw the jellyfish display and it hit me like a thunderbolt,” he says. “I thought: I know I can build that.” To do so, he recruited John Dabiri, a bioengineer who studies biological propulsion at the California Institute of Technology (Caltech) in Pasadena. “I grabbed him and said, ‘John, I think I can build a jellyfish.’ He didn’t know who I was, but I was pretty excited and waving my arms, and I think he was afraid to say no.”

Janna Nawroth, a graduate student at Caltech who performed most of the experiments, began by mapping every cell in the bodies of juvenile moon jellies (Aurelia aurita) to understand how they swim. A moon jelly's bell consists of a single layer of muscle, with fibres that are tightly aligned around a central ring and along eight spokes.

To make the bell beat downwards, electrical signals spread through the muscle in a smooth wave, “like when you drop a pebble in water”, says Parker. “It’s exactly like what you see in the heart. My bet is that to get a muscular pump, the electrical activity has got to spread as a wavefront.”

Form and function

Nawroth created a structure with the same properties by growing a single layer of rat heart muscle on a patterned sheet of polydimethylsiloxane. When an electric field is applied across the structure, the muscle contracts rapidly, compressing the medusoid and mimicking a jellyfish’s power stroke. The elastic silicone then pulls the medusoid back to its original flat shape, ready for the next stroke.

Nature | News

Artificial jellyfish built from rat cells

Reverse-engineered life form could be used to test drugs.

Ed Yong

22 July 2012

Bioengineers have made an artificial jellyfish using silicone and muscle cells from a rat’s heart. The synthetic creature, dubbed a medusoid, looks like a flower with eight petals. When placed in an electric field, it pulses and swims exactly like its living counterpart./>

“Morphologically, we’ve built a jellyfish. Functionally, we’ve built a jellyfish. Genetically, this thing is a rat,” says Kit Parker, a biophysicist at Harvard University in Cambridge, Massachusetts, who led the work. The project is described today in Nature Biotechnology1.

Parker’s lab works on creating artificial models of human heart tissues for regenerating organs and testing drugs, and the team built the medusoid as a way of understanding the “fundamental laws of muscular pumps”. It is an engineer’s approach to basic science: prove that you have identified the right principles by building something with them.

A jellyfish made of silicone and rat heart cells 'swims' in water when subjected to an electric field.

Harvard Univ./Caltech

In 2007, Parker was searching for new ways of studying muscular pumps when he visited the New England Aquarium in Boston, Massachusetts. “I saw the jellyfish display and it hit me like a thunderbolt,” he says. “I thought: I know I can build that.” To do so, he recruited John Dabiri, a bioengineer who studies biological propulsion at the California Institute of Technology (Caltech) in Pasadena. “I grabbed him and said, ‘John, I think I can build a jellyfish.’ He didn’t know who I was, but I was pretty excited and waving my arms, and I think he was afraid to say no.”

Janna Nawroth, a graduate student at Caltech who performed most of the experiments, began by mapping every cell in the bodies of juvenile moon jellies (Aurelia aurita) to understand how they swim. A moon jelly's bell consists of a single layer of muscle, with fibres that are tightly aligned around a central ring and along eight spokes.

To make the bell beat downwards, electrical signals spread through the muscle in a smooth wave, “like when you drop a pebble in water”, says Parker. “It’s exactly like what you see in the heart. My bet is that to get a muscular pump, the electrical activity has got to spread as a wavefront.”

Form and function

Nawroth created a structure with the same properties by growing a single layer of rat heart muscle on a patterned sheet of polydimethylsiloxane. When an electric field is applied across the structure, the muscle contracts rapidly, compressing the medusoid and mimicking a jellyfish’s power stroke. The elastic silicone then pulls the medusoid back to its original flat shape, ready for the next stroke.

When placed between two electrodes in water, the medusoid swam like the real thing. It even produced water currents similar to those that wash food particles into jellyfish's mouths. “We thought if we’re really good at this, we’re going to recreate that vortex, and we did,” says Parker. “We took a rat apart and rebuilt it as a jellyfish.”

Related stories

“I think that this is terrific,” says Joseph Vacanti, a tissue engineer at Massachusetts General Hospital in Boston. “It is a powerful demonstration of engineering chimaeric systems of living and non-living components.”

Parker says his team is taking synthetic biology to a new level. “Usually when we talk about synthetic life forms, somebody will take a living cell and put new genes in. We built an animal. It’s not just about genes, but about morphology and function.”

The team now plans to build a medusoid using human heart cells. The researchers have filed a patent to use their design, or something similar, as a platform for testing drugs. “You’ve got a heart drug?” says Parker. “You let me put it on my jellyfish, and I’ll tell you if it can improve the pumping.”

They also hope to reverse-engineer other marine life forms, says Parker. “We’ve got a whole tank of stuff in there, and an octopus on order.”

# Thread: The whole shrimp
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-whole-shrimp.2630/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 10
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Dali Dula — 2012-07-23T21:42:31+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-whole-shrimp.2630/post-40436>

I read some stuff on the forum about eating whole shrimp shell and all. I am not squeamish , but could use some suggestion on preparation. thanks

### Shijin13 — 2012-07-23T22:08:31+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-whole-shrimp.2630/post-40440>

steam, saute in ghee/butter/coconut oil, throw in soups... eat the whole dang thing... ds pops the whole thing in his little mouth and eats everything...If I don't manage my plate well, he'll just eat the tails...

### ealachan — 2012-07-23T22:35:48+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-whole-shrimp.2630/post-40450>

ooh, I just thought of something - when y'all are eating the "whole shrimp", are you de-veining it first, or is the vein along the back part of what we want / need? All the shrimp I buy frozen are already deveined and de-headed, though not usually peeled (because I can't afford the peeled ones...haha).

### KiwiLauren — 2012-07-23T23:56:31+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-whole-shrimp.2630/post-40487>

> **ealachan;40638 wrote:** ooh, I just thought of something - when y'all are eating the "whole shrimp", are you de-veining it first, or is the vein along the back part of what we want / need? All the shrimp I buy frozen are already deveined and de-headed, though not usually peeled (because I can't afford the peeled ones...haha).

Yes, I eat the WHOLE thing (though I usually leave the tail). I buy whole frozen raw shrimp, heads still on (totally untouched, just frozen). I heat Coconut Oil (but you can use ghee, butter, etc) in a pan with garlic. When hot, I add the shrimp (I often do this when they're still frozen... they cook quickly) and saute for a few minutes. I then add spices of choice (usually curry flavours) and maybe some herbs and sometimes bone broth if I want a soup base, with a squeeze of lemon. If they're frozen, I'll put a lid over the pan for a minute or two to make sure they are cooked (though I'm assuming raw is fine). Then I put them in a bowl and eat. As I said to Soul, I eat the heads first (with my own eyes closed... the ones I buy are big... maybe 5 inches long?... and their eyes are large and they look right at you!),then I go back and eat the bodies. I often don't eat all the shells, but always the entire head, shell, eyes, brains and all. Inside of the head is a bit mushy but the outside is crunchy. They taste very good, it's more the psychological barrier.

### ealachan — 2012-07-24T00:01:21+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-whole-shrimp.2630/post-40489>

> **KiwiLauren;40675 wrote:** Yes, I eat the WHOLE thing (though I usually leave the tail). I buy whole frozen raw shrimp, heads still on (totally untouched, just frozen). I heat Coconut Oil (but you can use ghee, butter, etc) in a pan with garlic. When hot, I add the shrimp (I often do this when they're still frozen... they cook quickly) and saute for a few minutes. I then add spices of choice (usually curry flavours) and maybe some herbs and sometimes bone broth if I want a soup base, with a squeeze of lemon. If they're frozen, I'll put a lid over the pan for a minute or two to make sure they are cooked (though I'm assuming raw is fine). Then I put them in a bowl and eat. As I said to Soul, I eat the heads first (with my own eyes closed... the ones I buy are big... maybe 5 inches long?... and their eyes are large and they look right at you!),then I go back and eat the bodies. I often don't eat all the shells, but always the entire head, shell, eyes, brains and all. Inside of the head is a bit mushy but the outside is crunchy. They taste very good, it's more the psychological barrier.

I know that the "proper" way to eat a crawfish is to suck the head parts out...wonder if one could take the same approach with shrimp. Interesting.

### Souldanzer — 2012-07-24T00:27:18+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-whole-shrimp.2630/post-40494>

> **KiwiLauren;40675 wrote:** Yes, I eat the WHOLE thing (though I usually leave the tail). I buy whole frozen raw shrimp, heads still on (totally untouched, just frozen). I heat Coconut Oil (but you can use ghee, butter, etc) in a pan with garlic. When hot, I add the shrimp (I often do this when they're still frozen... they cook quickly) and saute for a few minutes. If they're frozen, I'll put a lid over the pan for a minute or two to make sure they are cooked (though I'm assuming raw is fine). Then I put them in a bowl and eat. I often don't eat all the shells, but always the entire head, shell, eyes, brains and all. Inside of the head is a bit mushy but the outside is crunchy. They taste very good, it's more the psychological barrier.

I kinda liked this. I bought pretty small ones to start with. The frying in CO makes the shells crunchy. I do eat the tail  My problem was mental and the eyes...

### indigogirl — 2012-07-24T06:15:16+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-whole-shrimp.2630/post-40591>

Just a thought...the "vein" in the back contains the shrimp's waste...I do not eat this...I clean them and use the exoskeleton for stock.

Hate to be a party pooper (literally)

### KiwiLauren — 2012-07-24T07:49:07+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-whole-shrimp.2630/post-40610>

# Thread: journal worth following? Antioxidants  Redox Signaling
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/journal-worth-following-antioxidants-redox-signaling.2639/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 1
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### chocolate — 2012-07-24T05:31:09+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/journal-worth-following-antioxidants-redox-signaling.2639/post-40589>

Powerful class of antioxidants may be potent Parkinsonâ€™s treatment

> A new and powerful class of antioxidants could one day be a potent treatment for Parkinsonâ€™s disease, researchers report.
>
>
>
> A class of antioxidants called synthetic triterpenoids blocked development of Parkinsonâ€™s in an animal model that develops the disease in a handful of days, said Dr. Bobby Thomas, neuroscientist at the Medical College of Georgia at Georgia Health Sciences University and corresponding author of the study in the journal Antioxidants & Redox Signaling.
>
>
>
> Thomas and his colleagues were able to block the death of dopamine-producing brain cells that occurs in Parkinsonâ€™s by using the drugs to bolster Nrf2, a natural antioxidant and inflammation fighter.
>
>
>
> Stressors from head trauma to insecticide exposure to simple aging increase oxidative stress and the body responds with inflammation, part of its natural repair process. â€œThis creates an environment in your brain that is not conducive for normal function,â€ Thomas said. **â€œYou can see the signs of oxidative damage in the brain long before the neurons actually degenerate in Parkinsonâ€™s.â€
>
>
>
> Nrf2, the master regulator of oxidative stress and inflammation, is â€“ inexplicably â€“ significantly decreased early in Parkinsonâ€™s. In fact, Nrf2 activity declines normally with age.
>
>
>
> â€œIn Parkinsonâ€™s patients you can clearly see a significant overload of oxidative stress, which is why we chose this target,â€ Thomas said. â€œWe used drugs to selectively activate Nrf2.â€
>
> **
>
> They parsed a number of antioxidants already under study for a wide range of diseases from kidney failure to heart disease and diabetes, and found triterpenoids the most effective on Nrf2. Co-author Dr. Michael Sporn, Professor of Pharmacology, Toxicology and Medicine at Dartmouth Medical School, chemically modified the agents so they could permeate the protective blood-brain barrier.
>
>
>
> Both in human neuroblastoma and mouse brain cells they were able to document an increase in Nrf2 in response to the synthetic triterpenoids. Human dopaminergic cells are not available for research so the scientists used the human neuroblastoma cells, which are actually cancer cells that have some properties similar to neurons.
>
>
>
> Their preliminary evidence indicates the synthetic triterpenoids also increase Nrf2 activity in astrocytes, a brain cell type which nourishes neurons and hauls off some of their garbage. The drugs didnâ€™t protect brain cells in an animal where the Nrf2 gene was deleted, more proof that that Nrf2 is the drugsâ€™ target.**
>
>
>
> The researchers used the powerful neurotoxin MPTP to mimic Parkinsonâ€™s-like brain cell damage in a matter of days. They are now looking at the impact of synthetic triterpenoids in an animal model genetically programmed to acquire the disease more slowly, as humans do. Collaborators at Johns Hopkins School of Medicine also will be providing induced pluripotent stem cells, adult stem cells that can be coaxed into forming dopaminergic neurons, for additional drug testing.**

# Thread: Telomere Modification Program - Started In February 2008
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/telomere-modification-program-started-in-february-2008.2641/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 16
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### LouTheNavi — 2012-07-24T06:40:10+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/telomere-modification-program-started-in-february-2008.2641/post-40599>

G'day mates,

My name is Lou and I am 168 cm tall and have a chronological age of 61 years.

I'm a new member here and in this thread I'll update you on my progress in a Telomere Modification Program that I began using in February 2008. Prior to starting this program I weighed over 147 kg and had Gout, Asthma, and water retention. I was taking 5 medications for these conditions.

Now I weigh 95 kg and do not have to take any medications at all for Gout, Asthma or water retention, as I no longer have those conditions.

My TMP is based on these two studies:

[http://archinte.jamanetwork.com/article.aspx?articleid=413815](http://http://archinte.jamanetwork.com/article.aspx?articleid=413815)

[http://www.plosmedicine.org/article/info:doi/10.1371/journal.pmed.0050012](http://http://www.plosmedicine.org/article/info:doi/10.1371/journal.pmed.0050012)

I took a biological age test on July 20 and it measured my biological age as 48 years.

I'll explain more in my next post.

Cheers!

### fitness@home — 2012-07-24T12:53:44+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/telomere-modification-program-started-in-february-2008.2641/post-40622>

Hey Lou,

Just wanted to say hi. We will be heading your way next month for vacation (Corpus, then South Padre Island). Lots of seafood will be consumed

Will be following your thread...

### nonchalant — 2012-07-24T15:18:19+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/telomere-modification-program-started-in-february-2008.2641/post-40657>

"A sedentary lifestyle (in addition to smoking, high body mass index, and low socioeconomic status) has an effect on LTL and may accelerate the aging process." I was always irritated when they mentioned 'low socioeconomic status' as a reason for poor health. It seems like a vague catch-all for unknown factors. I couldn't see how the contents of your wallet determined your health. But now I think it could be mostly food ingested, along with other things like living in a high crime neighborhood, stress and whatever.

### steve@wity.net — 2012-07-24T18:48:29+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/telomere-modification-program-started-in-february-2008.2641/post-40711>

Hi Lou

How did you first get involved in this program? Is this a Spectracell intiative because I know they are based in Texas? I'm based in the UK and on the odd occasion I have mentioned the word "telomere" peoples eyes sort of glaze over...interesting that they have based part of your protocol on a UK study.

At your current rate of progress you should be a biological teenager by the time your reach your chronological 80's...

### nosnhojjennifer@yahoo.com — 2012-07-24T21:34:26+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/telomere-modification-program-started-in-february-2008.2641/post-40799>

yes, you have my interest!

### LouTheNavi — 2012-07-24T22:56:35+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/telomere-modification-program-started-in-february-2008.2641/post-40831>

G'day mates,

Before I started the Telomere Modification Program I had severe hypertension. I had to take 2 high powered combination pills along with two pills for water retention and even then my BP was usually too high. Two years ago my BP began to improve and now I only take one pill a day (Lotensin 20mg) and that keeps my BP well within the normal range.

Also before I began the TMP regimen I had trouble sleeping and took Melatonin and Benadryl to help me sleep. Now I don't take either one and I sleep as good as I did when I was 27, which is interesting as that is the last time in my life that I weighed 95 kg as I do now.

This next item about sleeping also has changed during the TMP, it seems like a small thing but perhaps it is another good sign. Before starting the TMP I always used 2 pillows to sleep. Now I just need one and sleep the best I have in years.

Besides Telomere modification, they have done a few enhancements to my body. Just over a year ago they replaced my natural lenses in my eyes with artificial ones. Also I have 6 steel alloy teeth permanently implanted also, so no need for dentures.

I'm leaving in the morning to spend 3 days in Fiesta Texas, a theme park in San Antonio. I celebrated my 61st chronological birthday by spending 3 evenings at "Buc Days" which had some nice roller coasters, thrill rides and a launched freefall ride. I have a lot more energy than before, and I rode 96 rides and coasters. Perhaps I'll do a few more on this trip. Before modifying my telomeres I could not have done all those rides.

Cheers!

### LouTheNavi — 2012-07-24T23:02:50+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/telomere-modification-program-started-in-february-2008.2641/post-40832>

> **Corvus;40901 wrote:** Hi Lou
>
>
>
> How did you first get involved in this program? Is this a Spectracell intiative because I know they are based in Texas? I'm based in the UK and on the odd occasion I have mentioned the word "telomere" peoples eyes sort of glaze over...interesting that they have based part of your protocol on a UK study.
>
> At your current rate of progress you should be a biological teenager by the time your reach your chronological 80's...

I became involved in the program after reading about the 2 studies and the results that could follow.

### LouTheNavi — 2012-07-24T23:06:07+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/telomere-modification-program-started-in-february-2008.2641/post-40834>

> **Nonchalant;40847 wrote:** "A sedentary lifestyle (in addition to smoking, high body mass index, and low socioeconomic status) has an effect on LTL and may accelerate the aging process." I was always irritated when they mentioned 'low socioeconomic status' as a reason for poor health. It seems like a vague catch-all for unknown factors. I couldn't see how the contents of your wallet determined your health. But now I think it could be mostly food ingested, along with other things like living in a high crime neighborhood, stress and whatever.

Probably true as in the Medieval Ages the poor ate the least healthy foods as they were less expensive, while the nobility had better diets though primitive by todays' standards of health.

### LouTheNavi — 2012-07-24T23:12:08+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/telomere-modification-program-started-in-february-2008.2641/post-40837>

> **fitness@home;40812 wrote:** Hey Lou,
>
>
>
> Just wanted to say hi. We will be heading your way next month for vacation (Corpus, then South Padre Island). Lots of seafood will be consumed
>
>
>
> Will be following your thread...

SPI is awesome. Been there many times. There are some lovely beach front condos, the higher floors are best. I've bungee jumped on SPI 4 times and flown ultralites there. Parasailing is another of my favorites.

Here in Corpus I have gone swimming with dolphins off the coast a couple times in the open sea. Just the dolphins and I, not a supervised experience but a solo swim. In the bay beware of the jellyfish, especially the Portugese Man O War. Have a good trip!

Cheers!

### LouTheNavi — 2012-07-29T06:21:26+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/telomere-modification-program-started-in-february-2008.2641/post-41762>

My natural lenses made me near sighted. The new ones corrected this. The procedure did the same thing as lasix surgery, except instead of modifying the old lenses they gave me a new set.

### LouTheNavi — 2012-07-31T05:43:08+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/telomere-modification-program-started-in-february-2008.2641/post-42016>

G'day mates,

Today I have been in the TMP for 54 months.

On 9 August and 11 August I will have a battery of tests to analyze my latest progress and additional lifestyle changes that can be made as this is similar to a building program where you rebuild a section at a time.

On 22 August there will be an evaluation of the results of the latest tests.

Cheers!

### LouTheNavi — 2012-07-31T22:16:34+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/telomere-modification-program-started-in-february-2008.2641/post-42105>

G'day mates,

TMP is really a hybrid of 4 sets of protocols. The Telomere Modification Program involves doing all the protocols in the 2 studies I mentioned in the first post in this thread. These protocols are combined with some other activities and supplements from other sources. Doctors monitor my progress though tests every few months.

Cheers!


══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# Thread: Ceviche recipes
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ceviche-recipes.2647/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 20
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### nonchalant — 2012-07-24T15:42:02+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ceviche-recipes.2647/post-40665>

I'm interested in your favorite ceviche ideas.

I've been making mine with raw seafood, lime juice, and cilantro, sit overnight.

I add stuff like avocado, cucumber, carrots, onion, etc, but I find myself just fishing (haha) out the seafood mostly anyway.

I've decided I don't like adding red cabbage, it ends up making a pink liquid, yuk.

How long does this last in the fridge? Seems to sit a couple days anyway with no problems, for me.

### Patty Cakes — 2012-07-24T18:17:49+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ceviche-recipes.2647/post-40700>

This is my favorite scallop ceviche recipe: [http://www.epicurious.com/recipes/food/views/Scallop-Ceviche-on-Black-Pasta-Cakes-with-Cilantro-Salsa-104384](http://http://www.epicurious.com/recipes/food/views/Scallop-Ceviche-on-Black-Pasta-Cakes-with-Cilantro-Salsa-104384) I have never made the rice cakes so ignore that part. I have made this both where I par-boil the scallops and used raw ones. It's easier raw so that is what I do these days. I'm nightshade free these days so don't use the tomatoes or tomatillos and add avocado instead. It's really good!

### nonchalant — 2012-07-24T19:20:57+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ceviche-recipes.2647/post-40725>

Thanks for the recipe, Patty. Sea salt! I haven't tried that yet.

### nonchalant — 2012-07-26T15:35:11+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ceviche-recipes.2647/post-41187>

Every recipe I see that calls for coconut water in the ceviche also calls for lime juice. Wonder if I need a second coconut?

### nonchalant — 2012-07-26T15:59:09+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ceviche-recipes.2647/post-41191>

Put the lime in the coconut, drink them both together,

Put the lime in the coconut, then you feel better.

### Souldanzer — 2012-07-26T19:02:53+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ceviche-recipes.2647/post-41236>

when you make ceviche, does it "cook" the seafood all the way through or just on the outside? My sea scallops were only white on the outside after overnight soaking in lime juice and a bit of apple cider vinegar, the insides were still entirely raw.

### Patty Cakes — 2012-07-26T19:18:26+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ceviche-recipes.2647/post-41243>

The time you leave the fish in the juices denotes how "cooked" they will be. I've made a white fish ceviche, took some to a party and left the rest in the fridge overnight. It was a huge success at the party but the next morning what I had left in the juices in the fridge was almost rubbery. It had over "cooked". So if you want it more white you'll need to leave it in the juices longer. Also you could try maybe cutting the scallops into fourths.

Rick Bayless has a good ceviche recipe too. Just leave out the items you cannot have and you still have a great dish. I am a huge fan of ceviche. I usually end up making it with juice the lemon and lime juice, onions and fish. Sometimes I add the raw onion during the cooking then take it out when I drain off the juices and add caramelized onion for flavor.

### nosnhojjennifer@yahoo.com — 2012-07-26T19:32:33+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ceviche-recipes.2647/post-41248>

I like ceviche too. I think I like the bay scallops the best. I just use lime juice, white wine, basil and salt. Haven't been brave enough to use white fish yet...

### Souldanzer — 2012-07-26T19:40:19+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ceviche-recipes.2647/post-41256>

Thanks Patty. I guess I should have cut up the scallops. Was thinking of it and then felt too bad about chopping them b/c these were my first ever big sea scallops and not the little bay scallops. I wanted for them to look nice considering the price I paid for them

### Souldanzer — 2012-07-26T19:41:43+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ceviche-recipes.2647/post-41258>

> **Jennifer;41465 wrote:** I like ceviche too. I think I like the bay scallops the best. I just use lime juice, white wine, basil and salt. Haven't been brave enough to use white fish yet...

I'll try it with the little scallops next time. These were.... raw  I was wondering if sole fillets might work well since they are so thin?

### Patty Cakes — 2012-07-26T19:43:58+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ceviche-recipes.2647/post-41259>

I think the size is why the recipe I posted has the person par boil them. I have done it both ways but that is where I first learned my lesson on timing. Sea scallops are so beautiful they are hard to cut up. Also be careful if you mix and match fishies. Different types can "cook" faster than others. I have even gone as far as mixing the juice then dividing it up into separate containers and use one for each of the different fish. Then mix after draining. A lot of work but it realty does make a perfect ceviche.

### nosnhojjennifer@yahoo.com — 2012-07-26T19:57:45+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ceviche-recipes.2647/post-41262>

I just can't use sea scallops for ceviche because they are SO DELICIOUS pan fried in CO! Just too good. Plus they are more expensive. I made a note to myself that grouper, halibut and sea bass are good for ceviche and to avoid cod because they tend to parasites (they have a worm named after them). Although, if you cut up the fish into small pieces, you should be cutting up the buggies and killing them. OR at least that's what I read.

[http://fishcooking.about.com/od/rawfish/a/sushi\_fish.htm](http://http://fishcooking.about.com/od/rawfish/a/sushi_fish.htm)

### Souldanzer — 2012-07-26T20:02:09+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ceviche-recipes.2647/post-41266>

well, therefore, mackerel head smoothies must be perfectly safe

Parasites can be very small and they lay eggs....

### nonchalant — 2012-08-20T03:31:47+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ceviche-recipes.2647/post-45777>

I wonder about the lime juice come Winter time. Sounds like a mismatch. Guess we could rinse the lime juice off before consuming?

### Zorica Vuletic — 2012-08-20T15:20:51+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ceviche-recipes.2647/post-45836>

Nonchalant: For winter you'd most likely want to increase high meat and fermented 'anything'. I mean for daily living. Of course if you're out and even choosing seafood at restaurant...then just get 'whatever' or still, sushi is always easy option.

Ooooooo winter...my fermented use shall increase 10000x fold. For now, I'm looking forward to fresh apples in season. The quercetin in them is nice and having them at the right time is just so ideal, not to mention delicious! End of season harvest---I'm taking advantage!!

### nonchalant — 2012-08-20T17:55:16+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ceviche-recipes.2647/post-45850>

Good point, Gladina. I need to check on the pickles I fermented earlier, see if they are still good. They have been sitting in the back of the fridge, I'm ashamed to say. But I fermented most of them with grass-fed whey, so I might have to just check out the salted ones.

I'm looking forward to some more homemade sauerkraut. This year I'll use organic cabbage. And no dairy. No whey! \[Sorry for the pun.\]

About the apples, perhaps I'll eat a slice or two when DH indulges. Lots of carbs there. One of these days I'll be able to take fuller advantage of the fruit seasons.

### nosnhojjennifer@yahoo.com — 2012-09-05T00:52:51+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ceviche-recipes.2647/post-49474>

Was making ceviche with scallops - lime juice and basil when I remembered I had some mago kevita in the fridge and so I threw some in....Pretty good. Don't know if it does anything to "cook" or not, but tasted ok. I'll let you know if I get deathly ill.

### Stavity — 2012-09-05T15:22:14+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ceviche-recipes.2647/post-49573>

I won't worry about the lime or lemon juice being a "mismatch" because it's the winter time that citrus fruit is available for me. The off season for citrus fruit here is in the summer. I am eating fish everyday, and to think I need to worry about the minutia is not going to happen, as I have a tendency towards being OCD as it is, and will hold that in check. My eating is pretty limited now and my body will tell me when something is amiss.

Though, everything about me is changing, and I have think it is eating seafood and taking vitamin D3. Even my gray hairs are looking blonde and I am getting asked everyday what I am doing to my hair. Well...I am doing nothing, chemically--I am eating fish. I have a calmness about me that has never been there, and I am thinking things out rather then reacting to them. I should also credit the Great Doc for his recommendations of certain vitamins and spices, and feel maybe I should be tested again for Vitamin D to see where I stand. That is after Oct 17 when I see the hepatologist?? again. He is doing extensive blood work and I need to take one thing at a time. Never been this excited about seeing a doctor before, but he had a sense about him I liked. Not much of a personable guy but someone with confidence and who did listen to me and take some of my feelings and ideas into consideration.

Hmmmmm, seems I am rambling. Sorry

### Stavity — 2012-09-05T15:39:41+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ceviche-recipes.2647/post-49575>

> **Gladina;46219 wrote:**
>
> Ooooooo winter...my fermented use shall increase 10000x fold. For now, I'm looking forward to fresh apples in season. The quercetin in them is nice and having them at the right time is just so ideal, not to mention delicious! End of season harvest---I'm taking advantage!!

Our apples have suffered huge losses do to historic weather events in winter and spring this past year. This will really have an impact on our agriculture industry. I will miss making huge amounts of apple sauce this year. It's one of my favorite foods.

I also have mixed feelings about eating loads fermented veggies, so have decided to eat them sparingly and stick to mostly, broccoli and spinach in small amounts during the winter. I feel that eating fish alone is giving me the all the vegetables I need.

### nonchalant — 2012-09-06T01:24:59+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ceviche-recipes.2647/post-49625>

Stavity, I know what you mean about the calmness. It's hard to describe to others. I think they just figure its all in my mind. And it is!
