# Thread: the effect of protein on blood sugar
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-effect-of-protein-on-blood-sugar.2648/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 8
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### ealachan — 2012-07-24T16:09:12+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-effect-of-protein-on-blood-sugar.2648/post-40669>

Question for the far more educated than I:

Is it true that if one were to eat a very large portion of protein in a sitting, that it would potentially raise the level of blood glucose, especially in an insulin-deficient person?

Obviously it wouldn't likely spike the BG in the way that carbs would - that I understand.

Thank you!

### Souldanzer — 2012-07-24T23:14:36+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-effect-of-protein-on-blood-sugar.2648/post-40838>

yes, it can cause a spike.... it does or used to for me

### ealachan — 2012-07-24T23:25:27+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-effect-of-protein-on-blood-sugar.2648/post-40845>

So it's basically a case of trial and error to find what amount of protein one might be able to eat without it causing a spike, then?

Man, diabetes is exhausting already, and I'm not even the one with the disease...

### Souldanzer — 2012-07-24T23:31:37+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-effect-of-protein-on-blood-sugar.2648/post-40848>

protein tends to cause a spike in diabetics but that doesn't mean you shouldn't eat it.... path to health. Medicating it might be best.

### ealachan — 2012-07-24T23:43:49+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-effect-of-protein-on-blood-sugar.2648/post-40853>

> **Souldanzer;41039 wrote:** protein tends to cause a spike in diabetics but that doesn't mean you shouldn't eat it.... path to health. Medicating it might be best.

Doctor's appt is next tuesday. Just bumbling blindly with a blood glucose monitor and info from the internet until then.

### Souldanzer — 2012-07-24T23:47:17+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-effect-of-protein-on-blood-sugar.2648/post-40860>

traditional treatment for diabetes was fasting until BG is acceptable then slowly introducing fat and protein at a rate that BG is controlled

### Cody — 2012-07-26T18:42:38+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-effect-of-protein-on-blood-sugar.2648/post-41220>

Check out Jimmy Moore's N=1 experiments on dietary ketosis.

I've never been diagnosed as T2D, however, I used to weight 456 pounds, and I'm pretty sure I was T2D or pre T2D. I now weigh around 330 lbs.

I cannot eat large amounts of protein without feeling super sleep afterwards, which I'm assuming means that my body is producing quite a bit of insulin in response to the protein intake.

I have to keep my fat pretty high (upwards of 75% of calories) to get into and stay in ketosis.

I haven't tested my BG or BK yet, as I'm in the middle of a move, but I have a monitor, and plan on testing everything I put into my mouth to figure out what is going on.

### Petra — 2012-07-27T12:45:00+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-effect-of-protein-on-blood-sugar.2648/post-41475>

Feels to me, that in this cases it matter if you are leptin sensitive.

And indeed if your body is looking for fat as fuel or sugar, will determine if is converts protein into sugar.

But please correct me if I see this wrong.

It is a gut feeling of mine


══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# Thread: CT helps Control Migraine pain!!!!
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-helps-control-migraine-pain.2651/>
**Subforum:** Cold Thermogenesis
**Posts:** 4
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Shijin13 — 2012-07-24T16:57:46+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-helps-control-migraine-pain.2651/post-40681>

So I woke up feeling a bit off this am (I was up at 515) then around 8am BLAM! a MIGRAINE launched a sneak attack on me....

I waited a bit to see if it truly was a migraine - thinking it away - no such luck.

so about 30mins ago I put ICE Packs on my carotid artery, and held them in place w/an ACE Bandage -

CTing my carotid arteries w/ICE packs to help w/my Migraine... OMG w/in 15mins the pain has decreased measurably ! I still have an aura, and some pain - but I can function!!! I still have a migraine but the pain is bearable!

### Lyndra — 2012-07-24T19:03:04+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-helps-control-migraine-pain.2651/post-40716>

Wow, that's awesome. I'm going to try that myself.

### Souldanzer — 2012-07-24T19:29:38+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-helps-control-migraine-pain.2651/post-40729>

Cold will constrict your arteries just like caffeine will. Most migraines are due to unnaturally relaxed blood vessels. My neurologist refused me migraine meds b/c I apparently am doing very well managing things myself  I told him I dunk my head in buckets of ice water and then pack my entire head in bags of ice including carotid arteries.

He missed the point that I want to \*function\* not be tied to a bucket and a bag of ice all day long. But yes, he was thrilled about the ice and knew that it works.

My mother always treats her migraines with a hefty dose of caffeine.

### Shijin13 — 2012-07-24T19:38:28+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-helps-control-migraine-pain.2651/post-40740>

> **Souldanzer;40919 wrote:** Cold will constrict your arteries just like caffeine will. Most migraines are due to unnaturally relaxed blood vessels. My neurologist refused me migraine meds b/c I apparently am doing very well managing things myself  I told him I dunk my head in buckets of ice water and then pack my entire head in bags of ice including carotid arteries.
>
>
>
> He missed the point that I want to \*function\* not be tied to a bucket and a bag of ice all day long. But yes, he was thrilled about the ice and knew that it works.
>
>
>
> My mother always treats her migraines with a hefty dose of caffeine.

I've done the caffeine thing - but always end up w/rebounds... the BHRT is supposed to help manage this...but I don't think I've got enough Pg! which is why I've got this one....

# Thread: Farmers, forestry workers and fishermen happier than the rest of us, ONS study to fin
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/farmers-forestry-workers-and-fishermen-happier-than-the-rest-of-us-ons-study-to-fin.2655/>
**Subforum:** Factor X
**Posts:** 1
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### chocolate — 2012-07-24T19:31:48+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/farmers-forestry-workers-and-fishermen-happier-than-the-rest-of-us-ons-study-to-fin.2655/post-40731>

Farmers, forestry workers and fishermen happier than the rest of us, ONS study to find

[http://www.bna.org.uk/news/view.php?permalink=KQSP72P2YJ](http://http://www.bna.org.uk/news/view.php?permalink=KQSP72P2YJ)

> Those who work outdoors in the countryside are happier than the rest of us Photo: Alamy
>
>
>
> Results from the Governmentâ€™s first ever survey of National Well Being, published by the Office for National Statistics, are likely to show that people who work in farming, forestry and fishing are happier than others.
>
>
>
> The ONS â€“ more used to weighing up public sector finances or crime statistics â€“ was given the rather subjective task of asking how happy Britons are by Prime Minister David Cameron in 2010.
>
>
>
> Ministers want to use the information to allow young people to make informed choices about their jobs â€“ asking whether they will be happy, rather than whether they will earn a lot of money.
>
>
>
> The prime mover behind the 'happiness index' has been Mr Cameron who said when he became Conservative leader in 2005 that gauging people's wellbeing was among the "central political issues of our time".
>
>
>
> He said then: "It's time we focused not just on GDP but on GWB â€” general well being." Previous surveys have suggested that Britons' happiness has remained static for 25 years
>
>
>
> According to provisional data from the ONS's Well Being study - which have been seen by The Daily Telegraph â€“ 20 different industry sectors were ranked according to â€œmean life satisfactionâ€.
>
>
>
> The study - which is published in full on Tuesday morning - found that at the top of the satisfaction list were people who work in â€œagriculture, forestry and fishingâ€.
>
>
>
> **That was followed in the top third of the satisfaction index - perhaps surprisingly - by â€œpeople working in mining, quarrying, â€œreal estate activitiesâ€, electricity and gas supply and â€œwater supply, sewerage and wasteâ€.
>
> **
>
> Propping up the list were those working in â€œadmin and support servicesâ€, transport and storageâ€, â€œaccommodation and food servicesâ€ and â€œwholesale repair of vehiclesâ€.
>
>
>
> Even people who are paid to cheer us up did not appear to be a very happy with their lot, with those working in â€œarts, entertainment and recreationâ€ ranked below bankers and insurers for job satisfaction.
>
>
>
> The study covered the quality of life of people in the UK, environmental and sustainability issues, as well as the economic performance of the country.


══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

slow cooker is the way to go. put the fat in whole and slice it up right in the pot as it softens. 3-4 hours. pull it before the proteins start browning. strain or filter. pour into jars. freeze.

# Thread: Periclean Athens
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/periclean-athens.2666/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 3
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Michael — 2012-07-25T14:39:17+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/periclean-athens.2666/post-40971>

This a bit off the wall but ...

I was thinking about fish, and I suddenly remembered that fish was _the fashionable food in Athens in the age of Pericles. This was at the time when this tiny city-state went through an intellectual revolution of world-historical importance. Meat seems to have been the staple high-protein food in the Heroic Age, but in classical times it was fish they went for. It had tremendous prestige. See James Davidson's book, Courtesans and Fishcakes_:

[http://www.amazon.com/Courtesans-Fishcakes-Consuming-Passions-Classical/dp/0226137430/](http://http://www.amazon.com/Courtesans-Fishcakes-Consuming-Passions-Classical/dp/0226137430/)

Of course, there would have been all sorts of reasons for the flowering of Athenian society at that time, most of thrm historical and social. But it's kind of interesting that Plato, Xenophon, Thucydides, Aristophanes, and the rest of them would have been eating copious quantities of seafood.

### ealachan — 2012-07-25T15:36:05+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/periclean-athens.2666/post-40974>

That is VERY interesting.

### nonchalant — 2012-07-25T23:45:59+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/periclean-athens.2666/post-41092>

I wonder how many times this wheel has been invented.

# Thread: How do I change my children's diet?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-do-i-change-my-childrens-diet.2670/>
**Subforum:** Optimal Kids
**Posts:** 5
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### itsoversugar — 2012-07-25T16:48:56+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-do-i-change-my-childrens-diet.2670/post-40998>

I have 2 sons, ages 7 and 11. We all eat the standard american diet. Ihave type 2 diabetes and for some time now have suspected my oldest son may have it. I took him to the doctor's office with me yesterday to get him checked out. We went over his symptoms (extreme fatigue, increased thirst, dizzy spells), along with some other things he's been experiencing and the dr wrote up an order for bloodwork. I have to take him to the local children's hospital for that.

He is overweight. He weighed in yesterday at 183. I also am overweight, needing to lose 100+ pounds.

I'd like to change our lifestyle as a whole family. Get rid of the processed foods, sugar and junk. My youngest is picky but he does like most veggies. Oldest would exist on all protein meals if I'd let him. How could I do this with kids who are use to eating pure crap all their lives?

Sent from my M886 using Tapatalk

### Shijin13 — 2012-07-25T17:01:48+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-do-i-change-my-childrens-diet.2670/post-41000>

> **itsoversugar;41213 wrote:** I have 2 sons, ages 7 and 11. We all eat the standard american diet. Ihave type 2 diabetes and for some time now have suspected my oldest son may have it. I took him to the doctor's office with me yesterday to get him checked out. We went over his symptoms (extreme fatigue, increased thirst, dizzy spells), along with some other things he's been experiencing and the dr wrote up an order for bloodwork. I have to take him to the local children's hospital for that.
>
> He is overweight. He weighed in yesterday at 183. I also am overweight, needing to lose 100+ pounds.
>
>
>
> I'd like to change our lifestyle as a whole family. Get rid of the processed foods, sugar and junk. My youngest is picky but he does like most veggies. Oldest would exist on all protein meals if I'd let him. How could I do this with kids who are use to eating pure crap all their lives?
>
>
>
> Sent from my M886 using Tapatalk

BACON its the gateway meat.

The other thing you can do is talk about why your changing the way your eating - and have a family "spring cleaning" event and throw out all the crap together (Grains, boxed cereal, snacks ect) then go the market together - look at the veggies, fruits, meats and seafood - and talk about why they're good for you and what they can do for you. Have them Identify what looks good to them... on a list. Then go home - don't buy anything at this point.

combine your lists - then look for recipes together on the internet, cookbooks etc and make a meal plan, for Breakfast, Lunch, Dinner

then go buy the foods on that list and when it comes time to make the meal involve them in making it! If all they want to do is eat meat (seafood and grass fed/finished beef and pork) and fat - let them. There are days if my kids don't want veggies they don't have to eat them. Eggs are cheap and easy food. If you can afford pastured get them...

additionally - if they don't eat what's for breakfast, then they have to wait to eat it for lunch, same goes if they won't eat it for lunch it becomes dinner.... eventually if they're hungry enough the'll eat it. Will they bitch, moan, and complain - but you will see a marked improvement in their over all moods and behavior, and eventually they'll be asking you for foods you'd never thought they would...

### KiwiLauren — 2012-07-26T02:07:49+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-do-i-change-my-childrens-diet.2670/post-41118>

Your children are about the same age as mine. I think this is a really good age if you didn't start from infancy. What I've found is that they are old enough to understand everything Dr K says, as long as it's put in simplified terms. For example, my kids now laugh and say 'keep your poop where it belongs!' anytime wheat is on offer somewhere. Anytime they felt resistant, I just explained what we were eating/not eating a certain way. Same with using only candles at night or not heating the house. If I couldn't explain the reason, I felt it was my duty to learn and then explain. Dr K's motto is question everything, so their questions were honoured. I work really hard to find foods they like and (though I'm in the minority) don't force them to eat what they detest. But taste buds change and as the months have gone by they now readily eat things they wouldn't eat before (they both now love squid, salmon and soups made with bone broth, for example).

We started by eliminating all legumes, wheat and sugar, except dark chocolate and the occasional treat I make from home with coconut flour and honey. We then eliminated all the other grains. Then almost all dairy except yogurt and the occasional organic cheese. Then we started stepping up the seafood/fish. I also started adding in foods for 'treats' we hadn't had on hand before... toasted pumpkin seeds with sea salt, organic coconut chips (plain, no sugar or oil) so they could have things around that felt like a splurge. We always have olives on hand, too. I have not removed nightshades for them. And they don't eat mackerel head smoothies!

We also aim for a big breakfast, which takes the pressure off the constant hunger for the rest of the day. Both my kids are very active but their lunches are small... we concentrate on breakfast and dinner. Neither liked bacon at first, so we did smoothies (in which I 'hid' raw egg yolks and CO). Now they have a variety of things they like.

The key for us was that BOTH parents were on board AND that all questions were answered. Also, I did not expect them to be happy about the changes. As adults, we struggle with some of the changes we need to make, but it allows for good dialogue.

My kids are now very fluent in terms like melatonin and brain chemistry and understand why we make the choices we do. Their biggest struggle to date is that none of their friends eat this way/live this way nor understand. But as they get more confident (and feel better!) they just smile. As I said elsewhere, my 8 year old has now educated all his friends on why fish head smoothies are super food (even if yucky)!

Good luck.

### Linz — 2012-07-26T11:51:42+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-do-i-change-my-childrens-diet.2670/post-41173>

I changed my diet radically seven years ago for health reasons but I kept cooking rice and potatoes for my family and I bought bread for them and didn't say much about what I was eating (I was not sure it would work and my doctor said it would not!). Within months they were all leaving the starches and I noticed sweet snacks in the cupboard were still there and going stale - they still liked them but I think they just didn't feeling hungry enough between meals to go looking for them.

I had always given the children bacon/eggs for breakfast and they did not notice the extra fats (many of the wrong ones at first) in everything else I cooked. The changes in eating behaviour and perception just happened and my daughter was very quick to tell me she had no energy if she ate like her friends when she first left home. I always encouraged them to taste everything without pressure to finish and both ate shellfish happily from a very young age.

I wasn't trying to change anyone but myself and the speed the rest of the family followed me was amazing. If I had been told I 'had to ' change the children's diet first and stop them eating sugar I think I would have failed miserably. There are still some less than healthy snacks here and I never comment if they are eaten but most are not. Seven years ago I wouldn't have been able to resist finishing them all myself - so how could I have kept them from the children.

### Dovid — 2012-08-07T08:38:28+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-do-i-change-my-childrens-diet.2670/post-43381>

I have three kids,their diet always chocolate,even like the milk shakes or energy drinks they dislike the bread even not use any vegetable or meat.They are physical so sensitive and soft and hard body wise someone suggest me what should be given them sothat their body change into strong posture.

### Michael — 2012-07-25T19:41:03+0300

That's interesting. I'd heard that most are Guernseys A1 -- although most Jersey are A2.

But at any rate, as long as the farmers has tested the cattle and knows what he's got, I guess which the breed usually is is besdie the point.

### Patty Cakes — 2012-07-25T20:31:59+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/guernsy-cows-make-a-2-milk.2675/post-41046>

### Patty Cakes — 2012-07-25T21:06:18+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/guernsy-cows-make-a-2-milk.2675/post-41056>

"There are three types of casein in cow's milk, called alpha, beta, and kappa. Each type comes in certain variants, depending on the genetics of the cow that produced it. For example, more than 70% of Guernsey cows produce the A2 variety of beta casein in their milk, where 70% of Red Danish Dairy cattle produce the A1 variety of beta casein." The researcher also pointed out that one cow could produce both A1 and A2 because it could receive different genes from the mother and father. Black and white Friesian cows, very common in dairy herds in many countries, have this combination of the two genes.

[www.healthfoodemporium.com/beyond-organic/beta-casein.php](http://http://www.healthfoodemporium.com/beyond-organic/beta-casein.php)

# Thread: blind mice see, kinda like the ion channels and fish
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/blind-mice-see-kinda-like-the-ion-channels-and-fish.2681/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 1
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### chocolate — 2012-07-25T22:50:14+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/blind-mice-see-kinda-like-the-ion-channels-and-fish.2681/post-41074>

[http://www.psypost.org/2012/07/chemical-makes-blind-mice-see-compound-holds-promise-for-treating-humans-12980](http://http://www.psypost.org/2012/07/chemical-makes-blind-mice-see-compound-holds-promise-for-treating-humans-12980)

> The approach could eventually help those with retinitis pigmentosa, a genetic disease that is the most common inherited form of blindness, as well as age-related macular degeneration, the most common cause of acquired blindness in the developed world. In both diseases, the light sensitive cells in the retina â€” the rods and cones â€” die, leaving the eye without functional photoreceptors.
>
>
>
> The chemical, called AAQ, acts by making the remaining, normally â€œblindâ€ cells in the retina sensitive to light, said lead researcher Richard Kramer, UC Berkeley professor of molecular and cell biology. AAQ is a photoswitch that binds to protein ion channels on the surface of retinal cells. When switched on by light, AAQ alters the flow of ions through the channels and activates these neurons much the way rods and cones are activated by light.
>
>
>
> â€œThis is similar to the way local anesthetics work: they embed themselves in ion channels and stick around for a long time, so that you stay numb for a long time,â€ Kramer said. â€œOur molecule is different in that itâ€™s light sensitive, so you can turn it on and off and turn on or off neural activity.â€

My thyroid is just all over the place. Thankfully my rT3 is staying low, but everything else is totally whacked! The lab couldnâ€™t even test my TSH or T4 this last time due to â€œheterophilic interference.â€ I had to google that to find out that they are some type of antibodies that cause inaccurate readings. I have no idea what to make of that.

The results that they were able to provide are below along with my last labs. The current set was done after starting T3. I had been on Â½ grain in the AM and Â½ grain PM for about 3 weeks prior to this test. Iâ€™m also on Â½ grain Naturethroid AM and PM.

**TSH

7/13: Test not performed due to heterophilic interference

5/15: 4.63 H

2/21: 0.04 L

RANGE: .4 â€“ 4.50 mIU/L

**FT3

7/13: 6.0 H

2/21: 3.0

RANGE: 2.3 â€“ 4.2 pg/mL

**Total T3

7/13: 232 H

5/15: 62 L

RANGE: 76 â€“ 181 ng/dL

**FT4**

7/13: 0.6

2/21: 0.7

RANGE: 0.6 â€“ 1.6 ng/dL

Total T4**

7/13: Test not performed due to heterophilic interference

5/15: 4.2 L

RANGE: 4.5 â€“ 12.0 mcg/dL

rT3**

7/13: 13

2/21: 17

RANGE: 11 â€“ 32 ng/dL

T3 Uptake**

5/15: 36 H

RANGE: 22 â€“ 35%

I thought for sure my T3 was converting to rT3 because after starting Cytomel I was having a lot of difficulty with CT, but apparently thatâ€™s not the case. I am shocked that my T3 is so high. In the past itâ€™s always been extremely low, less than 0.03 even. I donâ€™t have any signs of it being high â€“ no palpitations, anxiety, etc. My sleep has been a bit off lately, but I thought that was more due to hormones. Maybe not though . . .

# Thread: Sugary drinks can change muscles in a month  Read more: http://www.upi.com/Health_New
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sugary-drinks-can-change-muscles-in-a-month-read-more-http-www-upi-com-health_new.2683/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 1
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### chocolate — 2012-07-25T23:31:39+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sugary-drinks-can-change-muscles-in-a-month-read-more-http-www-upi-com-health_new.2683/post-41088>

> BANGOR, England, July 24 (UPI) -- Sugary drinks lead to alterations in muscles similar to those in people with obesity problems and type 2 diabetes, researchers in Britain said.
>
>
>
> Dr. Hans-Peter Kubis of Bangor University in England said the research showed regularly drinking soft drinks changed the way muscles use food as fuel, making them prefer to burn sugars over fats.
>
>
>
> "This study proved our concerns over sugary drinks have been correct. Not only can regular sugar intake acutely change our body metabolism; in fact it seems that our muscles are able to sense the sugars and make our metabolism more inefficient, not only in the present but in the future as well," Kubis said in a statement.
>
>
>
> "This will lead a reduced ability to burn fat and to fat gain. Moreover, it will make it more difficult for our body to cope with rises in blood sugar. **What is clear here is that our body adjusts to regular soft drink consumption and prepares itself for the future diet by changing muscle metabolism via altered gene activity -- encouraging unhealthy adaptations similar to those seen in people with obesity problems and type 2 diabetes."
>
>
>
> The study, published in the European Journal of Nutrition, found the switch to an inefficient metabolism was seen in the participants who were lightly active, lean male and females drinking soft drinks for only _four weeks_.**
>
>
>
> "Together with our findings about how drinking soft drinks dulls the perception of sweetness, our new results give a stark warning against regularly drinking sugar sweetened drinks," Kubis concluded.
>
>
>
> Read more: [http://www.upi.com/Health\_News/2012/07/21/Sugary-drinks-can-change-muscles-in-a-month/UPI-58301342928218/#ixzz21fUhqLRJ](http://http://www.upi.com/Health_News/2012/07/21/Sugary-drinks-can-change-muscles-in-a-month/UPI-58301342928218/#ixzz21fUhqLRJ)
>
>
>
>
>
> Read more: [http://www.upi.com/Health\_News/2012/07/21/Sugary-drinks-can-change-muscles-in-a-month/UPI-58301342928218/#ixzz21fUXOjmC](http://http://www.upi.com/Health_News/2012/07/21/Sugary-drinks-can-change-muscles-in-a-month/UPI-58301342928218/#ixzz21fUXOjmC)

This is pretty amazing.

[http://www.sciencenews.org/view/generic/id/342495/title/Night\_lights\_may\_foster\_depression](http://http://www.sciencenews.org/view/generic/id/342495/title/Night_lights_may_foster_depression)

Another "gee, I wonder if Dr. K. knows this." LOL

### kathylu — 2012-07-26T20:53:41+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/science-news-night-lights-may-foster-depression.2686/post-41294>

I knew this intuitively. On the nights I have forgotten to turn my son's night light off (4 watt bulb), he sleeps almost an hour less and is cranky and has mood swings. Total darkness = good sleep and great behavior for him.

### Shijin13 — 2012-07-26T20:59:10+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/science-news-night-lights-may-foster-depression.2686/post-41299>

you know what's interesting...we've been working on cutting down night lights; so we got her an orange light night light. last night she decided it was too bright and asked for us to turn it off!

**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/july-webinar-bab-question.2687/post-41132>

I listened to the July webinar and Jack spoke a lot about the Big Ass Breakfast. I got the impression that this is something we should eat everyday, even while eating Epi-paleo. Previously I thought we just needed to eat a BAB while on the Leptin RX and could stop the BAB afterward. Did I mis-interpret what he was saying during the webinar?

### vkiernan — 2012-07-26T05:19:01+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/july-webinar-bab-question.2687/post-41155>

# Thread: No self requesting labs available in Australia.
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/no-self-requesting-labs-available-in-australia.2690/>
**Subforum:** Optimal Labs
**Posts:** 10
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Jude — 2012-07-26T06:45:31+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/no-self-requesting-labs-available-in-australia.2690/post-41162>

Like the title says.....we are just as restricted as the folk in Canada!... .so am looking for other avenues?

Am without a co operative GP... so I'm sunk at the moment (small country town..blah blah). Have app with BHRT Dr in Sydney next month and would like some lab results to take. Some labs in US offer home labs for international clients.

Anyone have any info about how reliable these are? Thanks Jude.

### KiwiLauren — 2012-07-26T07:59:28+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/no-self-requesting-labs-available-in-australia.2690/post-41164>

Hi Jude. I don't know anything about requesting overseas labs, but I do know that one doc I found here requests labs out of Australia for functional medicine things! My strategy with my GP (to get some basic labs done) was to say that I'd changed my diet significantly and wanted to make sure I wasn't 'messing anything up' so wondered if he'd order some basic tests... cholesterol panel, B12 and zinc (I was vegetarian making a HUGE switch... even if you weren't previously a vegetarian you could claim you were!), D3 (cause it's winter, ya know), TSH/T3/T4 (cause I'm always cold... these are just the reasons I used with my GP to not trigger any reaction but get the tests ordered), hsCRP, DHEA S. He ordered them all. Then my husband repeated this test with his own GP. He got more resistance, but was able to barter... the GP said he'd order the 'unnecessary' tests if we paid for them.

The only US person I know who I've checked into is Dan Kalish... he tells you what testing he wants you to have and organises it, all by post. Only thing is he is VERY expensive.

### Jude — 2012-07-26T09:05:49+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/no-self-requesting-labs-available-in-australia.2690/post-41167>

Thanks Lauren. Saw my GP Tuesday and she isn't amenable to any tests other than those specifically for my new diagnosis of SVT!

Just when I thought all these palpitations were reactions to supplements! And, maybe they are?, but she's (GP) totally unhelpful for the optimal journey I'm wanting to take. Have app in Syd next month and just wanted a few test results to take with me. While I'm moaning-fresh fish once a week here-Christchurch looks good!!!

### caroline — 2012-07-26T09:25:50+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/no-self-requesting-labs-available-in-australia.2690/post-41169>

I live in the middle of woop woop, 90kms from nearest country town with all the GP's chanting the same theme-no BHRT blah blah. Have app in Syd next month and just wanted a few test results to take with me. While I'm moaning-fresh fish once a week here-Christchurch looks good!!!

Hi Jude. I popped into a compounding pharmacy in Sydney and she \[Chemist\] gave me names of doctors that are more tuned in. If you google compounding pharmacies a lot come up and you can contact them. Don't know if you have tried that or not - also some naturopaths. There is a paleo community in Sydney - have you tried her?

### Jude — 2012-07-26T11:32:26+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/no-self-requesting-labs-available-in-australia.2690/post-41171>

### KiwiLauren — 2012-07-31T00:39:39+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/no-self-requesting-labs-available-in-australia.2690/post-41980>

She is pretty agreeable but we will see. I wish I knew more so I could guide her if need be.
>
> Thanks for any info you can give me. Have a great day!

Hi Caroline. I am in New Zealand, not Australia. I did get hsCRP and DHEA S done and paid for by the national healthcare system. My GP ordered these, with a bunch of other tests (vitamin D, cholesterol panel, electrolytes). I have a history of breast cancer and have never had anyone do any blood tests... so I told my doc "I've totally changed the way I'm eating and living and want comprehensive labs to make sure I'm on the right track." He was ok with that. Though yearly is probably as often as I will be able to get them done because I can't self-order here either. I did not get any cortisol or hormone panels done, but have found a doc who I think will order them, though I will pay. The labs will likely be out of Australia, ironically enough.

Thanks Lauren. I think if you check - some naturalpaths will order the cortisol saliva test and a chemist at a compounding pharmacy is looking into it for me. # Thread: Hack these Labs Please!!!  Very high LDL-P
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hack-these-labs-please-very-high-ldl-p.2694/>
**Subforum:** Optimal Labs
**Posts:** 3
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Ray — 2012-07-26T17:56:51+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hack-these-labs-please-very-high-ldl-p.2694/post-41204>

My wife just got her first NMR panel done. First the good news:

HDL-C 63 mg/dL (>40)

Tryglycerides 65 mg/dL

Looks good, until you see:

LDL-C 138mg/dL

LDL-P 2311 nmol/L (

### KiwiLauren — 2012-07-27T00:13:46+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hack-these-labs-please-very-high-ldl-p.2694/post-41356>

Well, her vitamin D is also super low (should be above 80) and I'm guessing she has inflammation (which an hsCRP would show) and probably thyroid issues, too. She needs more tests to get a complete picture. Has she cut out ALL grains and all sugars? As well as all PUFA sources from her diet? That will create the high LDL. Looks like a classic inflammation-oxidation picture.

### trishalinn@gmail.com — 2012-07-27T05:04:14+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hack-these-labs-please-very-high-ldl-p.2694/post-41448>

Dr Peter attia has an excellent long series on his website about cholesterol, but it is VERY technical, so be sure to use your thinking cap if you go over there to read it. It's at eatingacademy.com.

I've written a few posts on cholesterol, that I hope, or fairly easy for the lay person to understand. I'm not an expert, but I've done a lot of reading and studying. Probably the most applicable post is my most recent one: when good cholesterol does bad things. [http://intoxicatedonlife.com/2012/07/18/when-good-cholesterol-does-bad-things/](http://http://intoxicatedonlife.com/2012/07/18/when-good-cholesterol-does-bad-things/)

I would reiterate what kiwilauren says above. Make sure she eradicates PUFAs, grains, and sugar!

# Thread: Ketosis and seafood
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ketosis-and-seafood.2695/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 20
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Cody — 2012-07-26T18:23:48+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ketosis-and-seafood.2695/post-41211>

OK, so we know to be in ketosis, it takes a high fat intake. For me, it's upwards of 75%.

So if we are to eat a pound of seafood per day (based on recent blog articles from the doc), and the recommended seafood is mostly protein (and what's up with all the carbs in shellfish???), how the heck am I supposed to do this?

For instance, I buy polar brand canned oysters from Walmart.

1 pound (16 ounces) has the following:

-   cals:560
-   fat:24
    cho:32
    pro:56

To make this work with 75% of my calories from fat, I would need to add 1300 more fat calories. I would need to add 13 TB of butter or 11 TB of coconut oil.

Is this what you all are doing???

Also, any idea how much DHA is in this pound of oysters? Apparently it depends on whether they are atlantic or pacific oysters (with the latter being higher in DHA and EPA than the former).

### Souldanzer — 2012-07-26T18:40:38+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ketosis-and-seafood.2695/post-41218>

I think your math is wrong. A tablespoon of fat has about 120cals so if you're looking for 1300cals that's more like 10-11T than 21.

I wouldn't worry about how much DHA is in the oysters.... eat your seafood, shoot for the best quality you can, and don't worry about the rest. If you want better DHA absorption have the sat fat away from your seafood.

Oysters are unusually high in carbs and not representative for seafood in general with regards to carbs me thinks.

### quelsen — 2012-07-26T18:44:03+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ketosis-and-seafood.2695/post-41222>

sounds like oyster stew to me

### Cody — 2012-07-26T18:45:31+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ketosis-and-seafood.2695/post-41223>

You are correct, a TB of butter is 100 calories, so I would need 13 TB of butter. And a TB of coconut oil is 120 calories, so 11 TB of coconut oil. I'll tweak my original post. Thanks for the correction Souldanzer!

### quelsen — 2012-07-26T18:47:38+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ketosis-and-seafood.2695/post-41226>

oysters, cream, onion, celery, butter

sounds fine to me

a pinch of nutmeg

### Cody — 2012-07-26T18:48:02+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ketosis-and-seafood.2695/post-41227>

> **quelsen;41439 wrote:** sounds like oyster stew to me

I've been thinking about trying oyster stew, clam chowder, or even some kind of shellfish alfredo with oysters, clams, and baby shrimp made out of coconut milk. Add some onion, some garlic, and some cauliflower, it might be awesome. Not sure about the coconut flavor in it, though.

### quelsen — 2012-07-26T18:55:20+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ketosis-and-seafood.2695/post-41233>

will coconut milk one makes curry

with snowville cream one makes chowder

### Shijin13 — 2012-07-26T19:02:06+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ketosis-and-seafood.2695/post-41235>

when I have my seafood (say shrimp) I've been adding extra ghee to it. what ever is left I drink up and lick the bowl

yesterday - we had left over seafood stew - I was still hungry afterwords so I had some liverwurst and then I made Queslen's Keto Shake...nom, nom, nom.

I've been drinking that daily to get me back into ketosis... damn summer light cycles and Carbs (Read fresh berries) threw me from the Ketosis train...

So I'm getting back on... in my 1st consult w/Jack he told me I should be eating btwn 6-8 bricks of Kerry Gold/week! I'm doing my best to achieve that...

### ealachan — 2012-07-26T19:10:06+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ketosis-and-seafood.2695/post-41240>

> **quelsen;41450 wrote:** will coconut milk one makes curry
>
> with snowville cream one makes chowder

Say it right. It's CHOWDAH.

### kathylu — 2012-07-26T19:32:55+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ketosis-and-seafood.2695/post-41249>

Well, a lb of seafood per day over 2 or 3 meals is about 5.33 to 8 oz per meal. For me that means I can't eat much in addition. I don't know how you all do it, but if I eat more that about 1800 calories per day, I gain weight, even if it's mostly fat. So for me a ketotic meal meal would be 6 oz fish or shellfish, enough fat to reach 70-75% of total calories and 2 cups of veggies (no more than 10 g carbs per meal). If I do this, I stay in ketosis.

### kathylu — 2012-07-26T19:34:30+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ketosis-and-seafood.2695/post-41251>

Sorry to mention the dreaded "c" word (calories), but for us LR folks they count big time...

6-8 bricks a WEEK??? OMG! I thought I was going through alot eating one every 10 days!

### Souldanzer — 2012-07-26T19:53:08+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ketosis-and-seafood.2695/post-41260>

> **kathylu;41466 wrote:** Well, a lb of seafood per day over 2 or 3 meals is about 5.33 to 8 oz per meal. For me that means I can't eat much in addition. I don't know how you all do it, but if I eat more that about 1800 calories per day, I gain weight, even if it's mostly fat. So for me a ketotic meal meal would be 6 oz fish or shellfish, enough fat to reach 70-75% of total calories and 2 cups of veggies (no more than 10 g carbs per meal). If I do this, I stay in ketosis.

Leptin rx is about letting go of the calorie counting. Maybe you gain weight so easily b/c you're keeping your body in starvation mode all the time? Maybe your hormones and thyroid aren't up to speed b/c they need more nutrition? Maybe your brain is malnourished?

I guess what I'm saying is that by strictly avoiding the potential weight gain you might not be helping yourself. Calorie restriction is not part of the leptin rx. Eating protein and fat to satiety is.

I'm LR and I would lose weight left and right on your caloric intake.

### Souldanzer — 2012-07-26T19:58:28+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ketosis-and-seafood.2695/post-41264>

I'm not sure I could do the 7 bricks a week.... maybe if I cut all other fats but then CO is supposed to be important. Is there a reason you're supposed to focus on the grassfed butter, Shijin?

Thinking of it, if I only eat CO I do think I get close to a cup a day, that's about equivalent to a brick of kerry gold, isn't it? Lol, good AN doesn't know this, hehe  I tend to spread my fats out with a can of coco milk a day, a few T of CO, some ghee, bacon for a treat....

>
> I'm LR and I would lose weight left and right on your caloric intake.

I do eat to satiety. including the BAB. Working on the hormones, as most of us are. I'm not hungry, I get plenty of protein and fat...I just do it with 1800 to occasionally 2000 calories per day. I don't see any reason to gain weight if I can do the leptin rx in a way that prevents that. Remember, we are all individuals. I absolutely believe that you can eat more than I do.

### Patty Cakes — 2012-07-26T20:46:05+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ketosis-and-seafood.2695/post-41287>

> **Souldanzer;41477 wrote:** Leptin rx is about letting go of the calorie counting. Maybe you gain weight so easily b/c you're keeping your body in starvation mode all the time? Maybe your hormones and thyroid aren't up to speed b/c they need more nutrition? Maybe your brain is malnourished?
>
>
>
> I guess what I'm saying is that by strictly avoiding the potential weight gain you might not be helping yourself. Calorie restriction is not part of the leptin rx. Eating protein and fat to satiety is.
>
>
>
> I'm LR and I would lose weight left and right on your caloric intake.

Yep, totally agree! This was me pre-Leptin Rx. I now eat a ton of fat and just enough protein to satisfy me and hardly any veggies but will add the sea type in next week after my trip. My weight issues were from hormonal imbalance. Still working on that but at least I know it's not from food.

# Thread: butter/ghee vs coconut oil

**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 25
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Souldanzer — 2012-07-26T19:07:31+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/butter-ghee-vs-coconut-oil.2696/post-41238>

What are the advantages of using butter (or ghee for the AI crowd ) over coconut oil?

I've been using CO and coco milk primarily and just introduced ghee. Why am I using ghee now as well? It does take away from my CO consumption. The only thing I know of is butyric acid which is good for the leaky gut. I guess it might have some more vitamins A and K2 and D b/c I use Kerry Gold.

Am I missing something?

Thanks.

### Patty Cakes — 2012-07-26T19:22:50+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/butter-ghee-vs-coconut-oil.2696/post-41244>

I use ghee for the taste. Sometimes I just don't think I can take another bit of CO so I cook with the ghee. I have always said any food is just a conduit to get butter in me. I never even tasted margarine until I was in my 20s. We bought raw milk and churned our own butter from that. I love it! So I cook a lot with the ghee and spoon feed myself the CO to get the goodness of that.

### Lyndra — 2012-07-26T19:35:49+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/butter-ghee-vs-coconut-oil.2696/post-41252>

From what I understand for general use, CO is best used in long light cycles and animals fats (butter/ghee) are appropriate for winter, based on following circadian rhythms. I think Dr. K has suggested CO is good for LR people year-round but don't quote me on that. I, unfortunately, don't seem to tolerate CO, though so many things are going on right now it's hard to say what is triggering migraines.

### Souldanzer — 2012-07-26T20:06:07+0300

So I cook a lot with the ghee and spoon feed myself the CO to get the goodness of that.

The many times I binged there was always ridiculous amounts of butter involved  If I can ever eat butter again in my life, it will be with a spoon.... enjoyed like a piece of very very dark chocolate. Or maybe in combination, how's that for kicking AN in the butt  Holy cow, that sounds good.... dark choc with butter

### Michael — 2012-07-26T20:49:32+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/butter-ghee-vs-coconut-oil.2696/post-41291>

> **Souldanzer;41455 wrote:** What are the advantages of using butter (or ghee for the AI crowd ) over coconut oil?
>
>
>
> I've been using CO and coco milk primarily and just introduced ghee. Why am I using ghee now as well? It does take away from my CO consumption. The only thing I know of is butyric acid which is good for the leaky gut. I guess it might have some more vitamins A and K2 and D b/c I use Kerry Gold.
>
>
>
> Am I missing something?
>
>
>
> Thanks.
>
>
> I think all the things you said. Additionally, it's a good source of short-chain fats, which are easy to burn. It's also relatively high in selenium, and has CLA.
>
>
>
> Weston Price used to prescribe cold liver oil with butter oil to many patients, and considered that the two worked together in a synergistic way. It seems to be in particular the MK4 form of K2 in the butter that he was after. In his day it hadn't yet been isolated but he had a sense that "something" was there and named it "Activator X".
>
>
>
> I was interested to see that Ramiel Nagel suggests that skate liver oil might be a good alternative/auxiliary to cod liver oil. He notes that it's a relative of the shark, and says, which impresses me:
>
>
>
> ... in the South Seas, natives would risk their lives to hunt for sharks even though they didn't need to for food. They risked their lives for the oil of the shark's liver. To extract the oil they would store the shark liver in the shark's stomach and let it ferment hanging in the warm air for months.

[http://www.amazon.com/Cure-Tooth-Decay-Cavities-Nutrition/dp/1434810607/](http://http://www.amazon.com/Cure-Tooth-Decay-Cavities-Nutrition/dp/1434810607/)

I guess people don't risk their lives on a whim.

Skate liver oil, he says, as well as being high in A and D has chondroitin, squalene, and alkoxyglycerols.

Green pastures do a fermented skate liver oil. I'ver thought about it, but never tried it.

So I did a new saliva test. My 12noon cortisol came up, 4pm cortisol went down some, but my hormones just further went down. I have been using adrenal support since then, and progesterone cream since Nov. I started DHEA keto (50mg) a few months ago. Clearly what I am currently doing is not really making much of an impact. Interestingly, I think the progesterone cream I am on is all being converted to cortisol, not progesterone. I am gaining roughly 1-2lbs a month, (on my waist) and the thought is bcs my progesterone is becoming cortisol. What things should I consider next? I review this with my new dr in less than 2 weeks, but I want to be prepared.

**Cortisol****

**6am:

Jan12 11 (range 13-24) July12 1.7 (1.8-3.8)

**12pm:

Jan12 4 (range 5-10) July12 .32 (.3-1.4)

**4pm:

Jan12 4 (range 3-8) July12 .24 (.1 - 1.1)

**10pm:**

Jan12 2 (range 1-4) July12 .22 (.1-.8)

DHEA-S**

Jan12 6 (range 3-10) July12 .07 (.1 - 1.3)

17OH Progesterone**

Jan12 25 (22-100) July12 14.7 (15.4-60.5)

Also tested this time:

E1** .6 (range

### Patty Cakes — 2012-07-26T20:24:01+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/saliva-test-update-help-please.2698/post-41277>

Where did you do your testing?

### tweety — 2012-07-26T21:03:08+0300

I am pretty good on my light cycles. I usually go to bed around 9 or 9:30. I do read a little and will be switching to an orange bulb by the bedside. But I wake without an alarm, usually at the same time every day.

Yes, cortisol is my big issue. I am so tempted to tweak supplements, but I suppose I will wait until my appointment. Pregnenolone would probably be good, maybe some PS to reduce my cortisol?

### tweety — 2012-07-26T23:30:24+0300

She believes that each individual has their own personal threshhold, so thats what she aims for.

I have only ct'ed in the temps that occur naturally. Our water is around 75 from the tap. Even adding a bit of ice only brings it down a degree or two. So yes I am CTing, but nothing deep.

I am currently taking diflucan for candida. It doesn't seem to be changing things. I had done detox initially, but I still have the same symptoms as before. Could this be interfering?

### vkiernan — 2012-07-27T01:32:19+0300

> I am currently taking diflucan for candida. It doesn't seem to be changing things. I had done detox initially, but I still have the same symptoms as before. Could this be interfering?

Not sure. I was in your boat but that was due to detox from Ct but I was in 53 degree water too. I was always that way every month before I had thermal ablation done. It was due to est. dominance. I am not really sure but it seems you are not that dominant but who is to say that things don't go awry at that time. I don't know when you did the assay in relation to cycle day. Gotta know that. I am also confused as to which day you should do it. I have heard day 21 and I thought Jack said day 2 or 3 but I can't find it anymore. You appear to be low in prog. and perhaps the good E too. Maybe you need both. Colleen C. is the master of this. Maybe you could hijack her HCG thread and ask her. Hang in there another month and see what happens. I am not sure that progesterone fixes things in a month or two. Might take more time and different dosing. If you could do blood tests at the right cycle day, I think you would know if that is the problem or not.

I thought pregnenolone was a good suggestion, because I am dealing with preg steal. ### Tizawee — 2012-07-27T15:57:44+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/saliva-test-update-help-please.2698/post-41511>

The body makes between 20 - 40 mg progesterone daily. Supplementing with cream, you would use that much. (I prefer twice daily doses because it's more natural than dumping it on all at once.) Supplementing orally, you need to use 200 - 400 mg because it passes through the liver and bioavailability goes down to 10%.

I know it sounds counter-intuitive, but you might need some estrogen. I had estrogen dominance symptoms like crazy. PCP put me on sublingual Biest/Prog/DHEA and symptoms have subsided somewhat (we still need to tweak the dose). I was skeptical, to say the least, but it's working.

It's probably a good idea. My salivary estradiol was at 0.7. I was convinced I didn't need any because of my symptoms. After awhile with just the progesterone cream, my estrogen dominance symptoms returned. I have been on the combination for 2 months now & so far, so good.

My labs are here: [http://forum.jackkruse.com/showthread.php?1833-Tis-A-Wee-Optimal-Journey](http://http://forum.jackkruse.com/showthread.php?1833-Tis-A-Wee-Optimal-Journey) . Salivary hormones have their own section. When looking at my labs, please bear in mind that I'm 3 years post-menopause.

# Thread: how to interpret my thyroid labs

**Posts:** 7

### JimG — 2012-07-26T20:52:52+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-to-interpret-my-thyroid-labs.2699/post-41293>

can some one point how to calculate the ratios and if some parameter is missing? Thanks

Reverse T3, Serum - 29.4 ng/dL

TSH - 1.65 uIU/mL

Triiodothyronine,Free,Serum - 3.4 pg/mL

T4,Free(Direct) - 1.27 ng/dL

### Patty Cakes — 2012-07-26T23:04:42+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-to-interpret-my-thyroid-labs.2699/post-41336>

Here is the ratio calculator for the rT3: [http://www.stopthethyroidmadness.com/rt3-ratio/](http://http://www.stopthethyroidmadness.com/rt3-ratio/)

If you want help interpreting, please post the ranges.

### vkiernan — 2012-07-27T01:40:16+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-to-interpret-my-thyroid-labs.2699/post-41392>

I get 11.6 for a ratio, needs some work to get your RT3 down and your FT3 up.

### JimG — 2012-07-27T06:46:06+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-to-interpret-my-thyroid-labs.2699/post-41468>

i get that too from the above calculator, i guess i need a eat of seafood now...

### vkiernan — 2012-07-27T14:44:44+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-to-interpret-my-thyroid-labs.2699/post-41489>

> **jimg;41686 wrote:** i get that too from the above calculator, i guess i need a eat of seafood now...

I've been working on my ratio for a year. I started with 7.8 and am now 17.8 or the near vicinity. Anyway, what are you currently doing for it?

### JimG — 2012-07-27T20:31:43+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-to-interpret-my-thyroid-labs.2699/post-41561>

> **vkiernan;41710 wrote:** I've been working on my ratio for a year. I started with 7.8 and am now 17.8 or the near vicinity. Anyway, what are you currently doing for it?

o thats good, congrats! quite a jump but a year sounds long to me, I need to ask you what you have been doing to help the ratio, I mean I know seafood will be the obvious response but any supplements ?

I recently added seafood to my template from being a vegetarian so its a rough patch for me, I have eating butt load of eggs for a year now with the top supplements, like vit-B, minerals, fish oil and many more & yes some small time CT... I am wondering any more different kind of testing I need to do to zero in into any underlying issues...

### vkiernan — 2012-07-27T23:14:06+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-to-interpret-my-thyroid-labs.2699/post-41586>

> **jimg;41782 wrote:** o thats good, congrats! quite a jump but a year sounds long to me, I need to ask you what you have been doing to help the ratio, I mean I know seafood will be the obvious response but any supplements ?
>
>
>
> I recently added seafood to my template from being a vegetarian so its a rough patch for me, I have eating butt load of eggs for a year now with the top supplements, like vit-B, minerals, fish oil and many more & yes some small time CT... I am wondering any more different kind of testing I need to do to zero in into any underlying issues...

Yes, you need to figure out why your T4 is converting to RT3 instead of T3. A lot of people go on T3 only to clear out the RT3 but you still have to know why it is not converting to what it is supposed to otherwise you just treating the thyroid and not the real problem.

Unfortunately it has been a year, well over a year now. I had to figure out why mine was converting to RT3. but I didn't know this out of the gate. I knew something was wrong and started reading 2 years ago and last year had sinus/reconstructive nose surgery and I didn't recover very fast. This was a huge red flag for me. I realized I had a thyroid problem. Started on T4 - of course, CW! I knew that wasn't right and begged for armour but it was a no go. Found a new doc and she put me on Naturethroid. She also gave me progesterone. I was having a rough summer, 2nd one in a row and this I found strange. Injured the summer before along with bizarre rashes and bee sting that swelled my whole arm up. The next summer (last) I got into poison ivy 2x along with whatever else happened that I can't remember. The first time I had it OMG bad. Ended up on prednisone for awhile. In the mean time, the new doc did a ASI test and my cortisol was whacked but didn't show fatigue per say. But life goes on and you believe what you are told even though you know better. She should have listened to me and learned my history instead of assuming it was nothing. I had been on prednisone which probably screwed up my ASI results. Got fed up with "here, if you are cranky put more on (progesterone) and take L-theanine for the adrenals. Found another new doc who knew immediately that I had severe adrenal fatigue. This doc ended up putting me on HC for the adrenals but doesn't believe in bio's and I needed them. So I am seeing yet another new doc in 1.5 weeks and took myself of the HC. Don't know what will come of that, but I will be seeing Dr. K when he sets up his new deal. Tired of messing around and wasting my life, my time and my dollars.

Sorry about the life story. My point is you need to test further. There is a problem cuz you are converting to RT3. Could be a mulitude of things. I'm not sure seafood or supps is going to fix the problem or at least probably not for awhile. I CT as much as I can. I can get my water to 53 but only last 30 minutes. I have been CT'ing since February and don't feel like I am making progress with length of time but it is helping me for sure. I think CT'ing and eating this way is what is working for me and paying attn. to my circadian rhythm. I have to get my hormones balanced and my adrenals healed if I am ever going to get to optimal. You cannot fix this stuff overnight but you can fix it. First though you have to know what is wrong. If you can get tests, go for as many as you can and start solving your problem today. Don't wait for seafood and supps to solve it all. Remember, food always trumps supps per Dr. K. If you can, get labs done and do a consult with Jack if you haven't. Money well spent.

Did you read the lab blog? It will tell what you need if you can get them. ASI with DHEA, HS-Crp, VAP these are a good place to start. Tells you your circadiam rhythm, how much inflammation and your cholesterol along with particle size which is very important. I'm guessing your o6/03 ratio is bad from being a veg. By having a ratio under 20 means you are LR; didn't know if you knew that. You can read the hormone 101 blog, lots of good info. Keep us informed of how you fare. Good luck on that ratio.

# Thread: Probiotics and our gut flora
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/probiotics-and-our-gut-flora.2700/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 6
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Souldanzer — 2012-07-26T21:27:44+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/probiotics-and-our-gut-flora.2700/post-41308>

So I learned that our gut flora will change with the location we live at. I.e. I was born in Germany but have been living in the U.S. for 13 years in August )). Therefore, I have an American gut flora and have to eat like an American (lol, not!).

But b/c I live in CO, I have to eat things seasonal and indigenous to CO b/c that's what does my gut flora good. How does this fit with taking supplemental probiotics? They're sort of one size fits all.

Should probiotics be "local" specific? Maybe another reason (other than that probiotic ## are way too low in supplements) to choose locally fermented veggies over supplements... b/c then the bacteria are the correct ones?

### Shijin13 — 2012-07-26T21:40:52+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/probiotics-and-our-gut-flora.2700/post-41309>

Can you make your own ferments? That's what I'm doing - I've got 2 heads of cabbage in a fermentation crock to make kraut? that way your environment is populating the kraut...

### villjamur_stevenson — 2012-07-26T21:52:41+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/probiotics-and-our-gut-flora.2700/post-41312>

Grow your own garden and make sure you eat plenty of raw dirty produce from it - so you get the soil microbes. You can also make your own homebrews from wild yeasts. I used to make hard cider that way all the time. Every batch is different!

### kathylu — 2012-07-26T21:58:10+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/probiotics-and-our-gut-flora.2700/post-41314>

Yup. Make your own kraut from local organic cabbage. Don't wash it. It's full of local soil sourced buggies.

### Souldanzer — 2012-07-26T22:11:54+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/probiotics-and-our-gut-flora.2700/post-41316>

I don't take probiotics  this was a theoretical question.... more like if you take supplements, maybe you'd have to get the ones specific to your location, like raw honey it's better to eat locally produced.

I never quite understood why people spend the money on pills if you can grow it in your kitchen. I'm a bit lazy with the kraut lately (I'm embarrassed to say that since I'm German ) b/c the chopping is so time consuming (and boring). I have some cucumbers that are ready to eat... but don't like them. So I stick with the fermented fizzy drinks. Love those.

### Souldanzer — 2012-07-26T22:13:53+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/probiotics-and-our-gut-flora.2700/post-41318>

Have my own garden and eat plenty of the dirt. Intentionally or not

### Huck — 2012-07-26T23:22:10+0300

### nonchalant — 2012-07-26T23:29:58+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/anything-safe-to-take-for-headache-with-leaky-gut.2704/post-41342>

+1 on the ice. It works for everyone. Try putting it on the sides of the neck.

Also krill oil worked wonders for me, when I used to have headaches. Takes about 45 minutes, same as ibuprophen.

Since I've been seeing a chiropractor, as soon as I sense a headache coming on, I "lift" my head, from behind the ears or the back of the head, to stretch the neck a bit. Stops the headache in its tracks for me. Guess mine are blood circulation related.

Good luck!

### Souldanzer — 2012-07-27T19:38:57+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/anything-safe-to-take-for-headache-with-leaky-gut.2704/post-41539>

### Souldanzer — 2012-07-27T19:40:11+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/anything-safe-to-take-for-headache-with-leaky-gut.2704/post-41540>

tons and tons of supplements have corn in them.... cellulose. or it could be from pine. or any other tree. or plant. and it can change within the same supplement and they don't have to tell you. they just buy whatever happens to be cheapest.

### CarolMorris — 2012-07-27T20:04:24+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/anything-safe-to-take-for-headache-with-leaky-gut.2704/post-41546>

To help with the food reactions I use Allergy Research/Nutricology powdered Vit C. It helped with food sensitivities for a few years, but my list of foods causing a problem increased. Then i found this forum and bingo...leaky gut...it began to make sense!

Here's the list of ingredients...could there be corn??--ugh!

Buffered Vitamin C Powder contains high-purity ascorbic acid buffered with carbonates of potassium, calcium and magnesium. Buffered C has an acid-alkaline buffering action that can help control the increased acidity often associated with allergic reactions. Developed by Stephen A. Levine, Ph.D.

These nutrients are of the highest quality and purity obtainable, and do not contain preservatives, diluents, or artificial additives.

Supplement Facts

Serving Size 1 teaspoon (4.7 g)

Servings per container 51

Amount per serving:

Vitamin C (as Ascorbic Acid) 2135 mg

Calcium (as Calcium Carbonate) 405 mg

Magnesium (as Magnesium Carbonate) 215 mg

Potassium (as Potassium Carbonate) 90 mg

Other ingredients: Cellulose.

### Souldanzer — 2012-07-27T20:07:58+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/anything-safe-to-take-for-headache-with-leaky-gut.2704/post-41550>

Ascorbic acid and cellulose could have come from corn. I wouldn't take this b/c of the calcium.

> **Souldanzer;41779 wrote:** harden your arteries

>
> you don't need calcium if you're eating Kruse style

Where is calcium coming from? I get confused with calcium issue. rda says 1000 mg calcium a day. without dairy I can't figure out where we are getting it from.

then there is the vitamin d and vit k issue.....taking vit d and not getting enough calcium.....wondering if that could cause an issue...

### kathylu — 2012-07-28T14:55:06+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/anything-safe-to-take-for-headache-with-leaky-gut.2704/post-41656>

> **Joann;41860 wrote:** Where is calcium coming from? I get confused with calcium issue. rda says 1000 mg calcium a day. without dairy I can't figure out where we are getting it from.
>
> then there is the vitamin d and vit k issue.....taking vit d and not getting enough calcium.....wondering if that could cause an issue...

Lots of calcium in meat and seafood...it's essential for cell membrane function and structure...bones too. Many plants have significant calcium, broccoli for instance.

### Souldanzer — 2012-07-28T17:06:14+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/anything-safe-to-take-for-headache-with-leaky-gut.2704/post-41665>

> **Joann;41860 wrote:** Where is calcium coming from? I get confused with calcium issue. rda says 1000 mg calcium a day. without dairy I can't figure out where we are getting it from.
>
> then there is the vitamin d and vit k issue.....taking vit d and not getting enough calcium.....wondering if that could cause an issue...

The problem is actually opposite, taking vitamin d will reduce the need for calcium. Vitamin K tells the calcium where to go (NOT to your heart and blood vessels). You might not need the RDA of calcium if your diet is dialed in. Now if you drink tons of cola or other stuff like that that leaches the calcium from your bones.... you might want to follow the RDA. Mg / calcium ratio is actually more important than total calcium intake. Dairy gives you way too much calcium with too little mg and in the process leaches calcium from your body.

I focus on the best foods I can find and dialing in my diet. Bone broths have calcium. Any fish eaten with bones has tons of calcium. Veggies, especially leafy green ones. I doubt transitional apes ever counted their calcium intake.

### vlynnb — 2012-07-28T19:48:23+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/anything-safe-to-take-for-headache-with-leaky-gut.2704/post-41692>

Thanks to all for this thread. I rarely have headaces anymore but had one last night. Simply splashed cold water on face for a couple of minutes, it was feeling so much better but not completely gone so I used an flexible ice pack. I could only stand it for 10 seconds or so at a time on the side of my forehead and cheek bone but it took the ache away almost immediately. I am so amazed. My mom used to use ice packs for her migraines but when I had one I only wanted warm wash cloths. No more warm washcloths for me.

### vlynnb — 2012-07-28T19:53:28+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/anything-safe-to-take-for-headache-with-leaky-gut.2704/post-41695>

> you don't need calcium if you're eating Kruse style

So, I knew we didn't need a calcium supplement when eating correctly but didn't think about getting small amounts in other supplements as being a problem. For instance I take a multivitamin with Ca, 200 mg, I think. Then when I looked at the ingredients list I decided not to take the multi at all. I say looked because it was so long I didn't want to read it.

Thoughts on taking multivitamins?

### Joann — 2012-07-28T22:13:50+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/anything-safe-to-take-for-headache-with-leaky-gut.2704/post-41705>

I get that sad diet is full of calcium. they eat a lot of dairy. The only food I see with a good amount of calcium is sardines, anchovies. meat has a lot of phosphorous ?

Guess my concern is having osteo, teeth wearing,ibs, lactose intolerant, aging ......

I take vitamin c, Twinlabs Allergy C, it has 50 mg calcium, 25 mg magnesium to 750 mg C Corn free, made from sago palm. pricey but worth it.
