# Thread: fish ain't people, but this is interesting
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fish-aint-people-but-this-is-interesting.2707/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 6
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### chocolate — 2012-07-27T01:28:45+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fish-aint-people-but-this-is-interesting.2707/post-41389>

[http://www.springerlink.com/content/n2102rpw13436834/](http://http://www.springerlink.com/content/n2102rpw13436834/)

> 11 (2008), 989-997, DOI: 10.1007/s11745-008-3228-8
>
>
>
>
>
> the American Oil Chemists' Society
>
>
>
> Original Article
>
> Sesamin Supplementation Increases White Muscle Docosahexaenoic Acid (DHA) Levels in Rainbow Trout ( Oncorhynchus mykiss ) Fed High Alpha-Linolenic Acid (ALA) Containing Vegetable Oil: Metabolic Actions
>
> Abstract
>
> The effects of including an equi-mixture of sesamin and episesamin in fish diets based on vegetable oils of different fatty acid composition were examined. Sesamin/episesamin (hereafter named sesamin) was included at 0.58 g/100 g diet. The oil used in the feed was either a mixture of linseed and sunflower oils (6:4, by vol) or 100% linseed oil. **Addition of sesamin increased the percentages of docosahexaenoic acid (DHA) in white muscle phospholipid and triacylglycerol fraction **by up to 37% but the fatty acids in red muscle and liver were not affected**. The expression of the peroxisome proliferator-activated receptor PPARα was significantly down regulated in the liver of the fish fed sesamin and mixed oil diet (P < 0.05). Sesamin and episesamin were detected in liver and muscle tissues of the fish that had been fed sesamin. Fish fed sesamin had elevated levels of total cytochrome P450 (CYP) enzymes and EROD activity in the liver, indicating an induction of CYP1A in this tissue.** Our conclusion was that supplementation of fish feed with sesamin increased the proportions of DHA in the white muscle.

### chocolate — 2012-07-27T02:23:10+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fish-aint-people-but-this-is-interesting.2707/post-41397>

Sesamin ameliorates oxidative stress and mortality in kainic acid-induced status epilepticus by inhibition of MAPK and COX-2 activation

> Abstract
>
> Background
>
>
>
> Kainic acid (KA)-induced status epilepticus (SE) was involved with release of free radicals. Sesamin is a well-known antioxidant from sesame seeds and it scavenges free radicals in several brain injury models. However the neuroprotective mechanism of sesamin to KA-induced seizure has not been studied.
>
> Methods
>
>
>
> Rodents (male FVB mice and Sprague-Dawley rats) were fed with sesamin extract (90% of sesamin and 10% sesamolin), 15 mg/kg or 30 mg/kg, for 3 days before KA subcutaneous injection. The effect of sesamin on KA-induced cell injury was also investigated on several cellular pathways including neuronal plasticity (RhoA), neurodegeneration (Caspase-3), and inflammation (COX-2) in PC12 cells and microglial BV-2 cells.
>
> Results
>
>
>
> **Treatment with sesamin extract (30 mg/kg) significantly increased plasma α-tocopherol level 50% and 55.8% from rats without and with KA treatment, respectively. It also decreased malondialdehyde (MDA) from 145% to 117% (p = 0.017) and **preserved superoxide dismutase from 55% of the vehicle control mice to 81% of sesamin-treated mice, respectively to the normal levels (p = 0.013). The treatment significantly decreased the mortality from 22% to 0% in rats. Sesamin was effective to protect PC12 cells and BV-2 cells from KA-injury in a dose-dependent manner. It decreased the** release of Ca2+, reactive oxygen species,** and MDA from PC12 cells. Western blot analysis revealed that sesamin significantly reduced ERK1/2, p38 mitogen-activated protein kinases, Caspase-3, and COX-2 expression in both cells and RhoA expression in BV-2 cells. Furthermore, Sesamin was able to reduce PGE2 production from both cells under KA-stimulation.
>
> Conclusions
>
>
>
> Taken together, it suggests that sesamin could protect KA-induced brain injury through anti-inflammatory and partially antioxidative mechanisms.

### chocolate — 2012-07-27T02:28:06+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fish-aint-people-but-this-is-interesting.2707/post-41398>

[http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/19794909](http://http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/19794909)

> Sesamin modulates tyrosine hydroxylase, superoxide dismutase, catalase, inducible NO synthase and interleukin-6 expression in dopaminergic cells under MPP+-induced oxidative stress.
>
> Lahaie-Collins V, Bournival J, Plouffe M, Carange J, Martinoli MG.
>
> Source
>
>
>
> Department of Biochemistry, Neuroscience Research Group, UniversitÃ© du QuÃ©bec, Trois-RiviÃ¨res, QuÃ©bec, Canada.
>
> Abstract
>
>
>
> Oxidative stress is regarded as a mediator of nerve cell death in several neurodegenerative disorders, such as Parkinson's disease. Sesamin, a lignan mainly found in sesame oil, is currently under study for its anti-oxidative and possible neuroprotective properties. We used 1-methyl-4-phenyl-pyridine (MPP(+)) ion, the active metabolite of the potent parkinsonism-causing toxin 1-methyl-4-phenyl-1,2,5,6-tetrahydropyridine, to produce oxidative stress and neurodegeneration in neuronal PC12 cells, which express dopamine, as well as neurofilaments. Our results show that picomolar doses of sesamin protected neuronal PC12 cells from MPP(+)-induced cellular death, as revealed by colorimetric measurements and production of reactive oxygen species. We also demonstrated that sesamin acted by rescuing tyrosine hydroxylase levels from MPP(+)-induced depletion. Sesamin, however, did not modulate dopamine transporter levels, and estrogen receptor-alpha and -beta protein expression. By examining several parameters of cell distress, we found that sesamin also elicited a strong increase in superoxide dismutase activity as well as protein expression and decreased catalase activity and the MPP(+) stimulated inducible nitric oxide synthase protein expression, in neuronal PC12 cells. Finally, sesamin possessed significant anti-inflammatory properties, as disclosed by its potential to reduce MPP(+)-induced interleukin-6 mRNA levels in microglia. From these studies, we determined the importance of the lignan sesamin as a neuroprotective molecule and its possible role in complementary and/or preventive therapies of neurodegenerative diseases.

### chocolate — 2012-07-27T02:44:07+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fish-aint-people-but-this-is-interesting.2707/post-41406>

[http://www.lipidworld.com/content/8/1/34/](http://http://www.lipidworld.com/content/8/1/34/)

Skeletal muscle structural lipids improve during weight-maintenance after a very low calorie dietary intervention

Steen B Haugaard1,2,3\*â€ , Allan Vaag4\*â€ , Huiling Mu5 and Sten Madsbad1

\* Corresponding authors: Steen B Haugaard [sbhau@dadlnet.dk](mailto:sbhau@dadlnet.dk) - Allan Vaag [avaa@steno.dk](mailto:avaa@steno.dk)

â€  Equal contributors

> Abstract
>
> Background
>
>
>
> The objective was to investigate in a group of obese subjects the course in skeletal muscle phospholipid (SMPL) fatty acids (FA) during a 24-weeks weight maintenance program, which was preceded by a successful very low calorie dietary intervention (VLCD). Special focus was addressed to SMPL omega-3 FA, which is a lipid entity that influences insulin action.
>
> Methods
>
>
>
> Nine obese subjects (BMI = 35.7 Â± 1.0 kg/m2), who had completed an 8 weeks VLCD (weight-loss = -9.7 Â± 1.6 kg, P < 0.001), had obtained skeletal muscle biopsies (vastus lateralis) before and after a dietician-guided 24-weeks weight-maintenance program (-1.2 Â± 1.5 kg, P = ns). SMPL FA composition was determined by gas liquid chromatography. During the preceding VLCD, insulin sensitivity (HOMA-IR) and glycemic control (HbA1c) improved but no change in SMPL omega-3 FA was observed. During the weight-maintenance program five subjects received the pancreas lipase inhibitor Orlistat 120 mg t.i.d. versus placebo.
>
> Results
>
>
>
> HOMA-IR and HbA1c stabilized and SMPL total omega-3 FA, docosahexaenoic acid and ratio of n-3/n-6 polyunsaturated FA increased by 24% (P < 0.01), 35% (P < 0.02) and 26% (P < 0.01), respectively, whereas saturated and monounsaturated FA did not change. Plasma total-cholesterol and LDL-cholesterol, which decreased during the VLCD, reverted to pre-VLCD levels (P < 0.01). Orlistat therapy was associated with weight-loss (P < 0.05), trends for better glycemic control (P = 0.15) and greater increase in SMPL docosahexaenoic acid (P = 0.12) but similar reversal of plasma cholesterols compared to placebo.
>
> Conclusion
>
>
>
> The data are consistent with the notion that greater SMPL omega-3 FA obtained during a weight-maintenance program may play a role for preserving insulin sensitivity and glycemic control being generated during a preceding VLCD.

### chocolate — 2012-07-27T17:47:23+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fish-aint-people-but-this-is-interesting.2707/post-41527>

[http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/11074268](http://http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/11074268)

> Kainate, a double agent that generates seizures: two decades of progress.
>
> Ben-Ari Y, Cossart R.
>
> Source
>
>
>
> The INMED, INSERM U29, Parc scientifique de Luminy, BP 13, 13273, Marseille, France.
>
> Abstract
>
>
>
> Studies using kainate, an excitatory amino acid extracted from a seaweed, have provided major contributions to the understanding of epileptogenesis. Here we review pioneering and more recent studies aimed at determining how kainate generates seizures and, in particular, how inhibition is altered during seizures. We focus on target and subunit-specific effects of kainate on hippocampal pyramidal neurons and interneurons that lead to an excitation of both types of neurons and thus to the parallel increase of glutamatergic and GABAergic spontaneous currents. We propose that kainate excites all its targets, the net consequence depending on the level of activity of the network.
>
>
> Sesamin ameliorates oxidative stress and mortality in kainic acid-induced status epilepticus by inhibition of MAPK and COX-2 activation.
>
> Hsieh PF, Hou CW, Yao PW, Wu SP, Peng YF, Shen ML, Lin CH, Chao YY, Chang MH, Jeng KC.
>
> Source
>
>
>
> Division of Neurology, Department of Medical Research, Taichung Veterans General Hospital, Taichung, Taiwan. [pfhsieh@vghtc.gov.tw](mailto:pfhsieh@vghtc.gov.tw)
>
> Abstract
>
> BACKGROUND:
>
>
>
> **Kainic acid (KA)-induced status epilepticus (SE) was involved with release of free radicals.** Sesamin is a well-known antioxidant from sesame seeds and it scavenges free radicals in several brain injury models. However the neuroprotective mechanism of sesamin to KA-induced seizure has not been studied.
>
> METHODS:
>
>
>
> Rodents (male FVB mice and Sprague-Dawley rats) were fed with sesamin extract (90% of sesamin and 10% sesamolin), 15 mg/kg or 30 mg/kg, for 3 days before KA subcutaneous injection. The effect of sesamin on KA-induced cell injury was also investigated on several cellular pathways including neuronal plasticity (RhoA), neurodegeneration (Caspase-3), and inflammation (COX-2) in PC12 cells and microglial BV-2 cells.
>
> RESULTS:
>
>
>
> Treatment with sesamin extract (30 mg/kg) significantly increased plasma α-tocopherol level 50% and 55.8% from rats without and with KA treatment, respectively. It also decreased malondialdehyde (MDA) from 145% to 117% (p=0.017) and preserved superoxide dismutase from 55% of the vehicle control mice to 81% of sesamin-treated mice, respectively to the normal levels (p=0.013). The treatment significantly decreased the mortality from 22% to 0% in rats. Sesamin was effective to protect PC12 cells and BV-2 cells from KA-injury in a dose-dependent manner. It decreased the release of Ca2+, reactive oxygen species, and MDA from PC12 cells. Western blot analysis revealed that sesamin significantly reduced ERK1/2, p38 mitogen-activated protein kinases, Caspase-3, and COX-2 expression in both cells and RhoA expression in BV-2 cells. Furthermore, Sesamin was able to reduce PGE2 production from both cells under KA-stimulation.
>
> CONCLUSIONS:
>
>
>
> Taken together, it suggests that sesamin could protect KA-induced brain injury through anti-inflammatory and partially antioxidative mechanisms.

### chocolate — 2012-08-27T05:43:12+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fish-aint-people-but-this-is-interesting.2707/post-47432>

[http://stemcell.childrenshospital.org/treating-patients/a-stem-cell-boosting-drug-goes-from-fishtank-to-bedside-2/](http://http://stemcell.childrenshospital.org/treating-patients/a-stem-cell-boosting-drug-goes-from-fishtank-to-bedside-2/)

> Several years ago, the ancestors of some of these rapidly-breeding fish helped hematologist Leonard Zon, MD, Director of the Stem Cell Program at Boston Childrenâ€™s Hospital, discover a way to help patients with cancer. Zonâ€™s team placed tiny zebrafish embryos into laboratory dishesâ€”five fish to a well, 48 wells to a plate. The fish were bathed in more than 2,500 different test chemicalsâ€”in the hopes that at least one chemical would increase their numbers of blood stem cells.
>
>
>
> Zon had long sought a treatment to help patients recover their immune function more quickly after chemotherapy or bone marrow transplant. Currently, when patients canâ€™t find a suitable marrow donor to replenish their blood stem cells, the only other option is umbilical cord blood. But the number of blood stem cells in a single cord often isnâ€™t enough for older children and adults, and using two cords increases the risk of immune complications.
>
>
>
> â€œTwo cords go into a person and their immune systems are different, because they come from two different babies,â€ Zon explains. â€œThe immune systems start fighting each other.â€ Cords are also expensive and in limited supply. Could a drug be found that would help blood stem cells multiply and eliminate the need for a second cord?
>
>
>
> The zebrafish on the second floor provided an excellent platform for answering the call. The embryos can take up chemicals through their skin, and because theyâ€™re transparent, their blood stem cell numbers can be easily counted under the microscope. Before long, they gave Zon and former research fellows Trista North, PhD, and Wolfram Goessling, MD, PhD, a potent â€œhit.â€ A derivative of prostaglandin E2 (PGE2), originally developed to fight stomach ulcers but then abandoned, boosted blood stem cell formation three- to four-fold. As reported in the Journal Nature in 2007, PGE2 helped both adult zebrafish and mice recover their blood cell populations after injury.
>
>
>
> â€œWe werenâ€™t specifically looking for prostaglandins,â€ says Zon. â€œThis was a surprise finding.â€
>
>
>
> Because PGE2 is already approved by the FDA for other uses, the drug moved swiftly to testing in patients. In May, 2009, a Phase I clinical trial of PGE2 began at the Dana-Farber Cancer Institute and Massachusetts General Hospital, led by Corey Cutler M.D., M.P.H., of Dana-Farber and sponsored by Fate Therapeutics.
>
>
>
> In the two-year Phase I trial, which is still open for enrollment, 12 adults undergoing treatment for leukemia or lymphoma will receive blood stem cells from two donor umbilical cords, one or both of which will be pre-treated with PGE2.
>
>
>
> If the drug works as hoped, a single cord may provide enough stem cells to successfully engraft in patientsâ€™ bone marrow and produce the range of healthy blood cells needed. This could make cord blood a more viable option for patients who lack a matched donor for a marrow transplant. Zon hopes to move to Phase II testing, which would involve 40-80 patients at multiple centers.
>
>
>
> PGE2 is a first-of-a-kind discovery in several ways. Previous studies, including one from Zonâ€™s own lab, had found ways to boost formation of blood stem cells, none of them lent themselves to broad medical use. PGE2 is the first
>
> small-molecule drug found to induce stem-cell production, the first drug discovered using zebrafish to go to clinical trial, and the first drug discovered through stem cell research to reach patients. Interestingly, PGE2 also seems to stimulate regeneration in other tissues, such as the liver.

# Thread: Thinking about ct in the fall/winter in Michigan
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/thinking-about-ct-in-the-fall-winter-in-michigan.2712/>
**Subforum:** Cold Thermogenesis
**Posts:** 5
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### trishalinn@gmail.com — 2012-07-27T05:25:35+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/thinking-about-ct-in-the-fall-winter-in-michigan.2712/post-41450>

So, I'm really bad. Ever since we got back from. Action I haven't gotten back into ct. Thats a little less than two months. BUT I am thinking ahead and looking forward to the weather cooling off. I'd like to utilize my in ground pool for ct, but wondering how it will logistically work. Do I close up my pool and blow the lines out like normal in the fall? Would it be alright to keep using the pool? Should I at some point put on the safety cover and call it done for the season and just use the tub?

On a side note, we homeschool and are part of a co-op. My husband thinks we should start a polar club and invite kids and parents from the co op over to ct with us and to talk about the benefits of the cold! He's totally gung ho about this.

### fitness@home — 2012-07-28T03:29:38+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/thinking-about-ct-in-the-fall-winter-in-michigan.2712/post-41611>

Aren't you in the upper lower part of Michigan? Kinda remember somewhere by Indian River.

A polar bear club sounds fun. What about lakes in the area - like Burt Lake. When does it freeze over - December? I didn't have a pool when we lived in Michigan. Is your built in or above ground?

Using it in fall/winter would extend your swim season. Getting more out of your investment that way

### trishalinn@gmail.com — 2012-07-29T04:51:43+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/thinking-about-ct-in-the-fall-winter-in-michigan.2712/post-41752>

We live in Mid-MI (near Lansing). We alreadyhave extended the swimming season by starting early! We got in the pool March 15 for the firsst time and it sure was cold! We had flurries a few times after that. That started the swimming season a good 2.5 months early. It would be cool to extend it that much on the other side too

Our pool is in-ground, gunite. Wondering what we should do about blowing out the lines?

### villjamur_stevenson — 2012-07-31T16:14:53+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/thinking-about-ct-in-the-fall-winter-in-michigan.2712/post-42042>

I kind of stalled out after my vacation from Orlando, but I keep doing as much CT as I can.

However, I am definitely looking forward to this fall/winter - I'm hoping for lots of snow so I can have a cheap source of "ice"

### WestmorelandGrape — 2012-08-01T04:47:11+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/thinking-about-ct-in-the-fall-winter-in-michigan.2712/post-42202>

Hi, Trisha, I'm in Midland, Michigan!

When the temps start getting to the freezing point at night, I would make sure the pipes on your pool are blown out. You don't want the pipes to crack from freezing, leading to a costly repair. Can you still swim when the pool is winterized - I don't see why not, the water level may be lower but you should still be able to get in.

After this brutal summer, I am looking forward to rolling naked in the snow! That will be my CT!

### lee — 2012-07-27T05:30:46+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/user-experience-between-wordpress-site-and-the-forum-can-be-seamless.2713/post-41452>

It's lovely, but as a brand new user, I was confused for a little while about why I couldn't post on the forum. Then I needed to create a new account?

You can have Wordpress and the forum share a user table and have the experience be seamless - so double logging in isn't required. There are ways to do this and people who can help you. Your user experience can be seamless.

Thanks,

Lee

# Thread: Circadian tutorial
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/circadian-tutorial.2715/>
**Subforum:** Educating Doctors
**Posts:** 4
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### nonchalant — 2012-07-27T06:40:03+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/circadian-tutorial.2715/post-41467>

I wish someone would create a video to explain the circadian rhythm, and some of the hormones that are triggered by it.

Not someone standing in front of a chart, but something with music, and interesting video. Pictures of the sunrise and sunset, the cortisol and melatonin curves moving with the time of day, and the effect of meals and such.

### nonchalant — 2012-07-27T23:58:19+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/circadian-tutorial.2715/post-41590>

Yes, CT 7 set to music and video. Precisely.

### nonchalant — 2012-07-28T03:56:01+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/circadian-tutorial.2715/post-41618>

What better way to get this out to everyone? Youtube.

The circadian cycle is of prime importance. Most people know that they need good sleep, but they don't know what a marvel of coordination it all is. Most people know know eating a huge meal just before bed can cause problems, but not that they should go to bed long after their last meal. They don't know that cooler temps are important, and darkness is essential. They know that "breakfast is the most important meal of the day" but they don't know why, so they dismiss it.

We need some people with some creativity. Dr K could narrate.

### terryfischer39@yahoo.com — 2012-08-02T06:17:30+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/circadian-tutorial.2715/post-42448>

Nonchalant, you are too, too funny and of course I agree with you. Music, video, birds chirping, sun shining, then quiet stillness of the evening twilight, the last twittering of the birds, then the faint glow of the brightest stars, then a hush as the sky is aglow with the ribbon of the milky way and we can stop thinking of actually wanting to eat one!


══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# Thread: Any Oregonians?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/any-oregonians.2718/>
**Subforum:** Cold Thermogenesis
**Posts:** 4
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### maggie — 2012-07-27T15:02:39+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/any-oregonians.2718/post-41492>

Hi Everyone

I'm planning to move from Virginia to Oregon in September. I'm going to live in Portland for a year, but was eyeing the coast as my ultimate destination. The Oregon coast looks so beautiful! But I'm wondering if it's possible at all to swim anywhere along that coast. What I'm reading says --- rip tides, sharks, big logs (!) sneak waves, and very cold (of course for me that would be the good part).

Would love to hear from anyone in the know.

Thanks.

### NWgirl — 2012-07-28T04:20:43+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/any-oregonians.2718/post-41620>

I live in the Portland area, you can definitely swim in the ocean here in most places. It's just that most people don't due to the cold and wind. I haven't done it much but then I just got into CT. We are going to rent a beach house in two weeks so I'll be trying something when I'm there.

Good luck with your move! PM me if you need anything.

Kim

### maggie — 2012-07-29T01:40:26+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/any-oregonians.2718/post-41733>

Thanks so much NW girl. Now I'm really excited about the move. Please report on your trip to the beach, would really love to hear all about it. That coast looks so gorgeous, it would be painful to live there and not be able to go into the water.

### Tiki333 — 2012-07-29T11:09:48+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/any-oregonians.2718/post-41769>

I live in the Willamette Valley, and oddly, we're renting a beach house in a couple of weeks too! I grew up on the Oregon Coast and we often body surfed. It is very cold, but once you're numb it's great fun! I think my early experience with that may have made adapting to CT much easier than for most. I wouldn't worry too much about sharks. The sneaker waves, logs and dangerous tides are usually things that occur more during very big storms and common sense should protect you.... We may have more to worry about along those lines when the debris from Fukushima begins to arrive.

Welcome to Oregon!


══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# Thread: Kids  Circadian Cycles
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/kids-circadian-cycles.2720/>
**Subforum:** Optimal Kids
**Posts:** 5
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Shijin13 — 2012-07-27T15:30:42+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/kids-circadian-cycles.2720/post-41502>

Ok. so we've been working the lights out early, early dinner etc. everyone has orange glasses now, we're using candles and redlights to light our world at night... Suddenly the boy (2yo), who's always been an early riser - is not only going to bed earlier, but he's getting up earlier, and earlier every day - yesterday was 5am, which was ok b/c I was planning on getting up at 515.

This morning he got up and was raring to go at 415! I was not ready for that. 4;15 is not an acceptable wake up time. 5:30 is appropriate, though I'd prefer 6am b/c then I'd get a little "me" time in the am before getting all touched out!

In addition to this... DD is now waking up earlier too w/o being harassed to wake up... today she was up at 6am all on her own! MIRACLE! normally I cannot get her out of bed before 7am - which would make getting to work next week when I transition to my client's site that much more difficult!

She's now up ready to eat and asking for food w/in 15mins... this is a WIN!

I think I'm going to ask her to start picking out her clothes for the next day before she goes to bed...

### BJK77 — 2012-07-27T16:07:03+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/kids-circadian-cycles.2720/post-41512>

Awesome progress Shijin! Though I don't know if I'd like the 4:15 AM wakeup time!!

I've had my kids wearing the orange glasses for a few months now and we've pretty much always done early dinners, but I need to do more to help my 12-year old daughter. My son (7) has always been a great sleeper - falls asleep within 5 minutes of his head hitting the pillow, usually around 8:30 and wakes with the sun nearly every morning. My daughter, OTOH, is like me and takes longer to fall asleep and struggles to get out of bed in the morning. Considering she'll be starting junior high soon and needing to wake at 6 AM, I'm stressing over how I'm going to help her!

We do lights out shortly before sunset and everyone dons their orange glasses for the rest of the night. We even turned her bed around so she's now facing the window - I figured that would help in the mornings.

What are the red lights that you use for reading? She is a total bookworm and likes to read before bed. Maybe that would help her a bit.

### Shijin13 — 2012-07-27T16:15:07+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/kids-circadian-cycles.2720/post-41520>

yeah 4:15 is not acceptable... I posted in ask Jack... he say DS need to go to bed later, or no glasses for him right now... considering DD has only been wearing the glasses for 2 days - and she's suddenly up at 6am - but still going to bed btwn 8:30/9pm I'm all for her still wearing them.

so it looks like no orange glasses for dS right now, and shoot for that 8:30/9pm bedtime for him...

These are the red light headlamps we've been using [http://www.amazon.com/gp/product/B002HMTNUW/ref=oh\_details\_o05\_s00\_i01](http://http://www.amazon.com/gp/product/B002HMTNUW/ref=oh_details_o05_s00_i01)

you do need to make sure you order the red LED lights - other wise you just get straight white light LEDs...

### BJK77 — 2012-07-27T22:24:56+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/kids-circadian-cycles.2720/post-41580>

> **Shijin13;41741 wrote:**
>
> These are the red light headlamps we've been using [http://www.amazon.com/gp/product/B002HMTNUW/ref=oh\_details\_o05\_s00\_i01](http://http://www.amazon.com/gp/product/B002HMTNUW/ref=oh_details_o05_s00_i01)
>
> you do need to make sure you order the red LED lights - other wise you just get straight white light LEDs...

Thanks for the link. Just ordered a set. We'll see how it works. Hope you get a little more sleep tomorrow morning

### Shijin13 — 2012-08-10T14:42:58+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/kids-circadian-cycles.2720/post-44094>

Update on this. We've been working real hard w/keeping the lights off at night etc. this am the girl was up around 5:30 raring to go telling us we should look at the pink sky!! Not sure where she saw the pink since it was raining & we couldn't physically see the sun rise. But hey. She's waking up on her own!!!

# Thread: I need sleep......

**Subforum:** The Leptin Rx

### janagram — 2012-07-27T19:50:20+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/i-need-sleep.2725/post-41543>

sure, I wanted to lose 30 lbs....but my sleep was horrid and I came to see that I won't lose weight until I sleep and now sleep is the major goal, forget the 30 lbs! my journal shows a pattern of sleep getting better, then bad again...

Things I've tried:

1\. Improve circadian rhythms....blublocker light in bedroom, try to get to bed by 11 (or earlier)

2\. taking small dose pregnenolone...(now I read that for some this works the other way)

3\. no alcohol (not a cure-all)

4\. no eating 4 hrs before bed

I'm sure these things help some but.....(thyroid tests came out pretty ok...)

### ealachan — 2012-07-27T20:01:16+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/i-need-sleep.2725/post-41544>

My sleep has improved a lot since I started respecting the daylight cycles more, but it's definitely still not perfect. I think it might just be another one of those non-linear things that takes different amounts of time for different people, depending on what you've got going on.

Did you sleep ok before you started making all these life changes?

### janagram — 2012-07-27T20:04:58+0300

### jeanie@truelongmont.com — 2012-08-03T02:22:57+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/i-need-sleep.2725/post-42593>

So Jana - are you lying there awake with the "hamster wheel brain"? Thinking of something like a work prob, or some neurotic thoughts? I think lets look at this from a different angle. . what is your body doing instead of sleep? Might provide some clues on which direction to go.

Also, have you ever done any deliberate music therapy? Music at a specific/steady beat actually can sync up neuron firing to get it more rhythmic and less chaotic, and can allow for metabolic processes to work in tangent . . ..

### vkiernan — 2012-08-03T03:10:32+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/i-need-sleep.2725/post-42603>

> **janagram;42793 wrote:** Destiny, I've had the same experience many times....better sleep, then down into insomnia again....but I am doing some different things.
>
> Thank you, Vk!!! A ticket to sleep! I have for the last 2 wks been taking 7 keto dhea, progesterone cream, and the really new one, estriol cream! And I stick to the LR diet, am quasi-epi-paleo, (eating much more fish) ......I am super hopeful!
>

I found it takes time for it to work, but it does. At least in my case and I "think" abetterme has had success with it too. After you start sleeping for a while, it seems that you worry less about waking up in the middle of the night cuz you have learned you will go right back to sleep MOST nights. I have relaxed a lot now since I know I sleep so much better. Sometimes I think when you wake and it is the NORM, you automatically go into overdrive of oh crap here we go again and actually make yourself wake up more.

### kathylu — 2012-08-03T17:06:41+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/i-need-sleep.2725/post-42678>

Ok. My sleep was really getting better, I was having nights where I got 8 whole, entire hours! Wow, it makes a difference. However, I started cycloset and metformin 3 days ago (on Jack's suggestion) to bring my FBG under control and bang! Bad sleep again. I have gone to bed by 10:30 the last 3 nights and every night like clockwork, I wake up at 2:30 am. The worst part is that I am not able to get back to sleep at all. So, 4 hours per night for the last 3 nights. Can the cycloset do this? Has anyone else had this problem? I already take 3 mg of melatonin at night, but I'm thinking a unisom sounds really good tonight too! I need to stay asleep!

### janagram — 2012-08-03T20:10:55+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/i-need-sleep.2725/post-42724>

cavemam, I like the music therapy idea!You should put out a CD!

I could just get up but I don't cuz that doesn't seem right...if I were told that 4 hrs of sleep is fine, I would be ok, but I know its not fine ...

> **vkiernan;42744 wrote:** Can't remember, are you taking DHEA or had it tested yet? That has been my ticket to sleep.

### kathylu — 2012-09-03T17:23:56+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/i-need-sleep.2725/post-49183>

> **hope2conceive;49463 wrote:** vkiernan, would you mind sharing how DHEA has been your ticket to sleep? Did your MD help you figure out the right dose? Mine just said "try with 10 or 20mg" based on my ASI....not too helpful.

I'm taking 100 mg DHEA and 50 mg 7-keto, 100 mg progesterone (transdermal), 5 mg melatonin, no caffeine, use blue blockers and I still can't sleep. I go to bed by 11 pm and I wake up about 4.5 hours later (and I mean wide-awake!). It seems like about every 3rd night I get 6 hours of sleep. I occasionally resort to unisom, which only works partially, meaning I'll get maybe 7 hours of sleep. I still think it's the cycloset I'm taking, that's when my sleep quality really degraded. I'm trying to wait it out, to see if I can adjust to the meds. I have my next salivary cortisol next week...maybe that will show what's going on.

### Destiny — 2012-09-04T00:16:42+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/i-need-sleep.2725/post-49253>

After you start sleeping for a while, it seems that you worry less about waking up in the middle of the night cuz you have learned you will go right back to sleep MOST nights. I have relaxed a lot now since I know I sleep so much better. Sometimes I think when you wake and it is the NORM, you automatically go into overdrive of oh crap here we go again and actually make yourself wake up more.

I slept from 9:30 PM to 6 AM last night. Even the previous nights were better than what I was used too. I hope it lasts!

### janagram — 2012-09-10T23:46:14+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/i-need-sleep.2725/post-50795>

sounds awesome, Destiny! ! I lost my sleepmask at a hotel...gotta go get another one!

### Destiny — 2012-09-11T01:02:42+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/i-need-sleep.2725/post-50831>

> **janagram;51353 wrote:** sounds awesome, Destiny! ! I lost my sleepmask at a hotel...gotta go get another one!

Hi janagram! It did not last but I feel that my sleep is better quality now even with the interruption once a night. I need to find a better sleepmask too because my eyes get too hot and sweaty under it.

### vkiernan — 2012-09-11T03:42:21+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/i-need-sleep.2725/post-50882>

> **hope2conceive;49463 wrote:** vkiernan, would you mind sharing how DHEA has been your ticket to sleep? Did your MD help you figure out the right dose? Mine just said "try with 10 or 20mg" based on my ASI....not too helpful.

When I got tested, I was a 1 with a range from 3-10 so it was very low. I actually started self-dosing and then was told by a doc that I could take more. It didn't take but a month or two for my levels to climb over the range. I still take 7-keto dhea 50 mg a day and reg. dhea on occasion for good measure. I also put the reg. dhea in my face cream with some co and I love it. I also use the face cream if I've been in the sun too long. Never peel. Always turns into a tan.

If you use reg. DHEA watch for hairloss and/or acne. The acne tells me everytime if I am using too much. I never use to get it so I have to believe it is from that. When I back off, it goes away too. I noticed I also feel much more awake in the day when I start using it consistently. I couldn't figure out why a couple of weeks ago I was feeling crappy again and then realized I stopped the dhea altogether due to my lab results.

I would start at a low dose and stay there for a week or two and see what it gets you unless you were really dificient. Then you could probably take 50 a day or more until your levels start to build. Do get retested if you think you can feel something from it. You don't want male tendencies from it converting to T. I needed some T so I didn't mind initially. But, I know I have to watch it. I swear this is what has helped my sleep!

### vkiernan — 2012-09-11T03:52:35+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/i-need-sleep.2725/post-50884>

> **kathylu;49686 wrote:** I'm taking 100 mg DHEA and 50 mg 7-keto, 100 mg progesterone (transdermal), 5 mg melatonin, no caffeine, use blue blockers and I still can't sleep. I go to bed by 11 pm and I wake up about 4.5 hours later (and I mean wide-awake!). It seems like about every 3rd night I get 6 hours of sleep. I occasionally resort to unisom, which only works partially, meaning I'll get maybe 7 hours of sleep. I still think it's the cycloset I'm taking, that's when my sleep quality really degraded. I'm trying to wait it out, to see if I can adjust to the meds. I have my next salivary cortisol next week...maybe that will show what's going on.

I really feel for you. Sounds like you were sleeping and now your not. I have no answers and don't really know much about the cycloset except what you all are posting. When are you taking the dhea? Only in the am right? Maybe try backing off the dhea to see if that really impacts it or not. It might take a week of an n-1 though to see a real result. Have to wonder if it is combining with the level of dhea and creating an effect. No clue here just thinking out loud. When is the last time you were tested for your dhea level? It probably is due to the cycloset but doesn't hurt to think about other options too.

So you aren't tired by 9 or 10pm? I am usually ready by 8. That sucks too. No night life for this ole' gal! Glad you are doing another asi. That usually answers a lot but if it is the cycloset what can you do besides wait it out? I am assuming you are already following all the c rhythm rules.

### janagram — 2012-09-11T03:58:34+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/i-need-sleep.2725/post-50886>

this is so crazy: I lost my sleep mask and kept waking up at around 5 when the sky began to lighten just a bit....so I grabbed my blublocker goggles and pulled a black sock over them (threaded the goggles into the sock and had wraparound sleep goggles.) Loved them! but after a few days they began poking my head cuz they are , you know, hard! So I still need a new sleep mask.

**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/i-need-sleep.2725/post-50991>

In my consult Dr. K suggested I increase my 200mg of prometrium(progesterone) to 400mg(until sleeping well) and take it about 2 hours before sleep..that has helped. I wear the blue blockers--my problem is I fall asleep within 10 to 15 min of putting them on--most of the time way too early.

I also added the mag L-threonate with 3mg. of melatonin.

I've had awful sleep for years and now I'm getting 7-8 hours and only waking up once or twice in a week.

### CarolMorris — 2012-10-12T19:28:23+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/i-need-sleep.2725/post-57801>

> **janagram;58972 wrote:** CarolM,
>
> Thanks (belated) for posting your experience! Sleep's important. I am sleeping well now...amazing! I have only orange light in my bedroom...I have a bucky blinks mask (I put it on when needed, not always), it's quite cold now and I think that is helping me sleep too. We haven't turned our heater on yet. (duh, it's california)...but it is pretty cold at night.
>
> Oh yeah, I take (before bed) mag malate, melatonin (3 mg) and GABA, 750 mg. Working...! So happy. Am I reset? I think so.

So happy for you! Sleep...the last frontier(at least as far as the leptin rx) it's what I have been waiting for. Sounds like the same for you??

# Thread: Saliva test results - opinions?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/saliva-test-results-opinions.2726/>
**Subforum:** Optimal Labs
**Posts:** 5
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Bernadette — 2012-07-27T22:40:06+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/saliva-test-results-opinions.2726/post-41582>

I got my saliva cortisol test and DHEA results back today. I have to say it appears to me to be "good news" but was wondering if anyone had any further insight?

Cortisol AM (6:15a) 7.5 ng/ml (3.7-9.5)

Cortisol noon (12:00) 2 ng/ml (1.2-3.0)

Cortisol Evening (16:15) 1.3 ng/ml (0.6-1.9)

Cortisol Night (11:30p) 0.7 ng/ml (0.4-1.0)

DHEAS 9.7 ng/ml (2-23)

I'd say the results are pretty accurate to what I expected based off how I'm feeling... the slightly higher cortisol in the evening (11:30p) is consistent with the occasional difficulty I have getting to sleep around my normal bedtime (9p)

### tweety — 2012-07-28T19:43:49+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/saliva-test-results-opinions.2726/post-41690>

I agree, your saliva test looks fairly good! Is this your first saliva test or have you done them previously? Are you using blue blocker glasses or at least turning lights off when the sun goes down? That and sunlight first thing in the am will help you further regulate your cortisol. But I would love those numbers!

I don't know enough about DHEA S to comment on that. I do know Dr. Kruse generally aims for the upper quartile of hormones, but that may be different based on the individual.

### Bernadette — 2012-07-29T17:57:02+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/saliva-test-results-opinions.2726/post-41796>

I'm not paying as much attention as I should be to the lights issue. I think lights off after sunset is something I'll shoot for next. Thanks for your input!

### Patty Cakes — 2012-07-29T20:44:42+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/saliva-test-results-opinions.2726/post-41831>

I think you may want to really pay attn to those light cycles. I don't want to burst your bubble but I would want that first reading of the day to be close to the top of the range, the noon looks pretty good, your evening result is probably OK for 4:15pm but I wonder what it would be at if you took it at 6pm, and the last result is high as you mentioned. You want that bedtime one to be close to the bottom range to allow you to sleep. You are starting to see some of the beginning stages of slight adrenal dysfunction. The night high reading is something you may want to address right now. Watch the morning for now and in a few months when you retest you can see if it's going down or not. I hope this makes sense. I've personally gone w/o much sleep in the last 48 hours due to airplane issues but I still wanted to weigh in on this for you.

### melrito — 2012-07-29T23:58:22+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/saliva-test-results-opinions.2726/post-41867>

dr. Kruse wants DHEA-S in the top quartile, so it's definitely low

# Thread: wisdom of the body
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/wisdom-of-the-body.2731/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 2
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Michael — 2012-07-28T10:18:38+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/wisdom-of-the-body.2731/post-41643>

Jimmy Moore had a recent guest - the man who had groups of people on four different diets (Atkins, Ornish, Zone, Learn) to see which was most effective.

There was perhaps little new in the discussion. What did interest me, though, was that after talking a lot about "compliance" the researcher suggested that it may be that some people knew (unconsiously) **not** to be compliant with a counter-productive diet. I thought that pretty sophisticated thinking on his part, and that he was probably right.

In particular he suggested that people who are severely insulin-resistant probably won't be "compliant" with a low-fat diet, because at some level they know, although not consciously, that the more compliant they are, the worse they'll feel. Fascinating stuff - and deeply plausible.

### Joann — 2012-07-29T05:08:22+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/wisdom-of-the-body.2731/post-41754>

I think you are correct, Michael. I was on the Dukan diet, which was real easy and did work to lose weight but I knew something was off. my hands had a weird dryness between fingers, my teeth ached. wasn't long before I "sneaked" some fat into my mouth. lol

# Thread: Webinar dropping out words and phrases
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/webinar-dropping-out-words-and-phrases.2733/>
**Subforum:** Feedback/Suggestions
**Posts:** 10
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Martin — 2012-07-28T17:53:47+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/webinar-dropping-out-words-and-phrases.2733/post-41669>

I missed a number of key words and concepts because the sound would get all electronic-sounding and modulated. Sounded like the character on the old tv show, "Max Headroom".

Is it on my end, or did others experience this?

I'm going to try and piece in some words and phrases on my recording in GarageBand so I can re-listen and learn stuff better, but sometimes too much was distorted and I may have to dig into the blog posts for answers to fill in.

Do-able, but frustrating, and time-consuming.

### KiwiLauren — 2012-07-29T03:19:38+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/webinar-dropping-out-words-and-phrases.2733/post-41745>

I downloaded it and listened to it without interference from any noise. I play it through Quicktime, in case that helps. It could be the program you're using to listen?

### ealachan — 2012-07-29T03:33:58+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/webinar-dropping-out-words-and-phrases.2733/post-41746>

> **Martin;41893 wrote:** I missed a number of key words and concepts because the sound would get all electronic-sounding and modulated. Sounded like the character on the old tv show, "Max Headroom".
>
>
>
> Is it on my end, or did others experience this?
>
>
>
> I'm going to try and piece in some words and phrases on my recording in GarageBand so I can re-listen and learn stuff better, but sometimes too much was distorted and I may have to dig into the blog posts for answers to fill in.
>
>
>
> Do-able, but frustrating, and time-consuming.

I had the same problem in parts as well, but I can't go back and re-listen, which is kind of a bummer.

### shilohman — 2012-07-29T03:47:37+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/webinar-dropping-out-words-and-phrases.2733/post-41749>

> **Martin;41893 wrote:** I missed a number of key words and concepts because the sound would get all electronic-sounding and modulated. Sounded like the character on the old tv show, "Max Headroom".
>
>
>
> Is it on my end, or did others experience this?
>
>
>
> I'm going to try and piece in some words and phrases on my recording in GarageBand so I can re-listen and learn stuff better, but sometimes too much was distorted and I may have to dig into the blog posts for answers to fill in.
>
>
>
> Do-able, but frustrating, and time-consuming.

Understand the frustration, but it probably is on your end. What browser are you using? I use Safari 6.0, but it also worked the same in 5.7.

Apparently the browser you are using is streaming the webinar file. On my Mac it downloads the whole file and then launches Quicktime to play the file. KiwiLauren's system works the same as mine, so we just need to figure what is different with your system.

### Souldanzer — 2012-07-29T03:53:30+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/webinar-dropping-out-words-and-phrases.2733/post-41750>

It sounds like Martin purchased a webinar ticket and is not downloading from the website. Martin, did you check your settings on GarageBand while recording? It's best if it is set to "voice". Maybe it was set to some sort of special effect? I've used GarageBand to do things like this and it's worked wonderfully.

My original and the download is fine. Except for when Dr K needs to go get more tannins, he fades out for a while... no complaint  answering our endless questions...

### shilohman — 2012-07-29T18:39:35+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/webinar-dropping-out-words-and-phrases.2733/post-41805>

Martin, Dr. K posted the blog, BRAIN GUT 6: EPI-PALEO Rx which seems to be taken from the webinar. You should be able to fill in what you missed by reading the blog.

### Martin — 2012-07-29T23:33:50+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/webinar-dropping-out-words-and-phrases.2733/post-41863>

I have a Macbook, Safari 5.1.7, and bought a webinar ticket. I recorded it on a Q3 Zoom recorder in front of my Bose speakers. Decent, but I would like to know how to record thru my computer without a mic'ed set-up. I even mic'ed it with a AKG condenser and an ancient 1/4" TEAC reel-to-reel! Back up!

But I digress. I had a feeling it was due to my computer just not up to the speed of streaming these days.

Model Name: MacBook 2007 (late)

Model Identifier: MacBook2,1

Processor Name: Intel Core 2 Duo

Processor Speed: 1.83 GHz

Number Of Processors: 1

Total Number Of Cores: 2

L2 Cache: 2 MB

Memory: 4 GB

Bus Speed: 667 MHz

I'm intrigued with the G'Band recording! And how did you set you Mac to download it, shilohman? How you do dat?!

I must figure that out. I looked into recording webinars beforehand and it sounded too complex and nothing was mentioned about Quicktime, G'Band and such.

Yes, I can plug in using BG6, but many of the goodies were in the intro and questions. Gold!

thanks a million, guys!

### Souldanzer — 2012-07-30T01:03:35+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/webinar-dropping-out-words-and-phrases.2733/post-41872>

Martin, I just tried this and it works beautifully.

You just start your GarageBand recording before the webinar starts, make sure it is set to podcasting or voice, no special effects. Then you listen to the webinar on your browser. Don't make any noise while recording! It will record that, too  Once the webinar is done you stop the recording and save the file. Super easy and very good quality (as long as you are quiet during the recording). Also make sure the volume on both your computer and GarageBand is set properly.

I'm sure there is amore sophisticated way to do it but this works for my many uses to save me from my bad brain

### shilohman — 2012-07-30T04:00:25+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/webinar-dropping-out-words-and-phrases.2733/post-41891>

GarageBand is probably the easiest way to capture the audio. There is also Audacity, which is a free audio application, but try out GarageBand, it should work as Souldanzer described.

### Martin — 2012-07-30T15:35:09+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/webinar-dropping-out-words-and-phrases.2733/post-41927>

Beautiful. Thanks for the great tip, you guys!

I'm going to use it for my consult with Doc on Friday. It is a BIGGIE!

# Thread: First Webinar I've Missed.  Are yams in or out on epi-paleo?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/first-webinar-ive-missed-are-yams-in-or-out-on-epi-paleo.2734/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 8
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### curves64 — 2012-07-28T18:16:11+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/first-webinar-ive-missed-are-yams-in-or-out-on-epi-paleo.2734/post-41671>

I see in Dr. K's blog that sweet potatoes are in because they are in the Marigold family. What about yams?

### vlynnb — 2012-07-28T19:40:01+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/first-webinar-ive-missed-are-yams-in-or-out-on-epi-paleo.2734/post-41689>

I could be wrong but I understand that all sweet potatoes and yams sold in the U.S. are really the same, that we don't have true yams in our stores, just a different variety of sweet potatoes that we call yams here. I sure hope so because I don't want to give them up.

### Shijin13 — 2012-07-28T20:05:14+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/first-webinar-ive-missed-are-yams-in-or-out-on-epi-paleo.2734/post-41698>

> **vlynnb;41915 wrote:** I could be wrong but I understand that all sweet potatoes and yams sold in the U.S. are really the same, that we don't have true yams in our stores, just a different variety of sweet potatoes that we call yams here. I sure hope so because I don't want to give them up.

This is correct. Sweet potatoes in the US are also labeled as yams but they are not yams. Yams are from Africa and have a completely different texture flavor.

Here's a link for sweet potatoes [http://en.m.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sweet\_potato](http://http://en.m.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sweet_potato)

Here's what yams look like -note they are native to west Africa. So if you live there and they are in season. Eat away

[http://en.m.wikipedia.org/wiki/Yam\_(vegetable)](http://http://en.m.wikipedia.org/wiki/Yam_\(vegetable\))

### curves64 — 2012-07-28T20:48:18+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/first-webinar-ive-missed-are-yams-in-or-out-on-epi-paleo.2734/post-41702>

> **Shijin13;41924 wrote:** This is correct. Sweet potatoes in the US are also labeled as yams but they are not yams. Yams are from Africa and have a completely different texture flavor.
>
>
>
> Here's a link for sweet potatoes [http://en.m.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sweet\_potato](http://http://en.m.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sweet_potato)
>
>
>
> Here's what yams look like -note they are native to west Africa. So if you live there and they are in season. Eat away
>
> [http://en.m.wikipedia.org/wiki/Yam\_(vegetable)](http://http://en.m.wikipedia.org/wiki/Yam_\(vegetable\))

The yams here in San Francisco look like the yams in the picture and not the sweet potatoes. I prefer yams to sweet potatoes. Boo hoo! I was hoping not to have to give them up!

### janagram — 2012-07-28T22:25:49+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/first-webinar-ive-missed-are-yams-in-or-out-on-epi-paleo.2734/post-41706>

there are "hannah" sweet potatoes that are pale yellow and even whitish japanese sweet potatoes (very sweet) but they still aren't yams, so maybe you are eating sweet potatoes?

### curves64 — 2012-07-28T23:20:20+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/first-webinar-ive-missed-are-yams-in-or-out-on-epi-paleo.2734/post-41712>

> **janagram;41933 wrote:** there are "hannah" sweet potatoes that are pale yellow and even whitish japanese sweet potatoes (very sweet) but they still aren't yams, so maybe you are eating sweet potatoes?

Can I just pretend they're sweet potatoes?  Really, I'd love to know. I get them at my local farmers market and they are pale yellow inside. Is there any way to definitively know?

### KiwiLauren — 2012-07-29T01:29:09+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/first-webinar-ive-missed-are-yams-in-or-out-on-epi-paleo.2734/post-41729>

Yam confusion is rampant... this is 'yams' in New Zealand

[http://en.m.wikipedia.org/wiki/Oxalis\_tuberosa](http://http://en.m.wikipedia.org/wiki/Oxalis_tuberosa)

and there is NOTHING called a sweet potato here... only the indigenous sweet potato, called kumara, which comes in orange, white and purple.  Wouldn't have a clue if they are optimal or not.

### curves64 — 2012-07-29T03:37:34+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/first-webinar-ive-missed-are-yams-in-or-out-on-epi-paleo.2734/post-41747>

> **KiwiLauren;41956 wrote:** Yam confusion is rampant... this is 'yams' in New Zealand
>
> [http://en.m.wikipedia.org/wiki/Oxalis\_tuberosa](http://http://en.m.wikipedia.org/wiki/Oxalis_tuberosa)
>
> and there is NOTHING called a sweet potato here... only the indigenous sweet potato, called kumara, which comes in orange, white and purple.  Wouldn't have a clue if they are optimal or not.

Thank you Kiwi. We don't have _anything_ that looks like that in San Francisco. So maybe what I've been eating are in fact sweet potatoes. I hope so cause they are really really tasty!!

# Thread: BAB: Your Assistance, Please
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bab-your-assistance-please.2735/>
**Subforum:** The Leptin Rx
**Posts:** 8
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Tizawee — 2012-07-28T18:41:10+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bab-your-assistance-please.2735/post-41674>

**BOTTOM LINE: I began LR in Feb 2012. I've always been good about BAB until I had to cut out eggs, although, between bacon, cod and salmon, I've managed quite well... until recently.

Because of an abscess, my mouth has been in treatment for the past 4 weeks. I had major oral surgery two weeks ago today. Now that I'm feeling better, I just can't seem to get back to a BAB. I'm not only not hungry but am slightly queasy in the mornings and don't feel like even trying to get anything down. Just wondering if anyone has any suggestions as to a workaround so I can get back to my BAB. I miss it.

On the days I force myself to eat something, I'm sure it's not going to stay down. It always does, though. Plus I can usually only get about 1/3 - 1/2 down before I have to quit.

Today, I've been up since 5am (always an early riser), it's 11:30am and I'm just now beginning to think I could eat something.

ANY assistance will be greatly appreciated.

HISTORY** (because this may have something to do with the queasiness):

Abscess: 06/24/12. Got penicillin\* & vicodin. Found out I'm allergic to the penicillin (wheezing). So he gave me another week of amoxicillin\*.

Surgery: 07/07/12. He took seven teeth. Conscious sedation + plenty of novocaine. Another week of amoxicillin\* + a week of vicodin. Went home with partials in.

Stitches out: 07/14/12. Everything is healing nicely. Partials fit well, no issues. Still on soft foods but eating well, at least in the evenings.

Thinking it might have something to do with gut flora, I got some probiotics. They just give me the runs.

\* It's been 25+ years since I've been given penicillin, but I've never had a bad reaction before. I've had amoxicillin over the intervening years and never had a reaction that I'm aware of, so I asked for that rather than try a new antibiotic. (I was given a fluoroquinolone several years back for something-or-other, wound up trying to throw up everything I'd ever eaten in my entire life and had to go to the ER.) I now wear a Medic Alert bracelet.

### tweety — 2012-07-28T19:48:49+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bab-your-assistance-please.2735/post-41693>

Lots of fermented veggies at the beginning of meals? Bone broth too to help rebuild health. I've also been told that we should try different types of probiotics, since there are so many strains.

As far as antibiotics, I think intolerance can happen at any point once exposed. I took them often as a child, and as a teen had to use them for an injury. From that point on I now have a reaction to any form of antibiotic. I avoid them as much as I possibly can, and take benadryl with them if I ever have to use them. I have no idea if that is because of weak gut health, and whether it could possibly change. I would hope so, but am not counting on it myself.

### Tizawee — 2012-07-28T23:40:03+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bab-your-assistance-please.2735/post-41713>

Thanks for the reply, tweety. I didn't even think of bone broth. I just fired it up in the slow cooker. I am making sauerkraut but it won't be ready for a couple of weeks yet.

Yeah, I know I'm supposed to avoid antibiotics but this was unavoidable. I wasn't aware you could just become allergic to them.

e

### tweety — 2012-07-29T00:22:32+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bab-your-assistance-please.2735/post-41716>

Don't forget to add a sheet of kombu in your broth. Its too hot here to cook broth, let alone eat it. Not to wish the summer away. but I am looking forward to fall already! Cold baths and broth.

I didn't mean to imply you should have avoided them! It seems like that was definitely a situation where you wouldn't have much choice. Sorry if it came out that way. I just meant that the above was my personal experience. I had used them without incident for years, and then one day I was just done. I have used them three or four times since, and like I said I take benadryl the whole time with each dose. I just pray I never need them more than a short period of time. I do however a discussion of high dose vitamin C being used in place of antibiotics, especially with regards to dentistry. MamaGrok, maybe?

### Tizawee — 2012-07-29T04:12:48+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bab-your-assistance-please.2735/post-41751>

Kombu? Nori? Okay. I have to confess I'm new at bone broth. I'm willing to try anything. I saw somewhere to make the broth, let it cool, then pour it into ice cube trays or muffins tins and freeze it for individual servings. If it's good, it doesn't have to be hot.

And I didn't take it that way. No harm, no foul. I have used high dose Vit C for non-emergent illnesses. But when your face looks like it's got a baseball in it, well... I didn't want to take chances. LOL! Big chicken here! Even though I lurk more than post, I read just about everything. I don't remember the Vit C + dentistry post but will look for it.

### Patty Cakes — 2012-07-29T04:58:05+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bab-your-assistance-please.2735/post-41753>

After my oral surgery in June, nothing quite as extensive as yours (just a wisdome tooth and cyst), I ate a ton of broth. I'd heat it up and add some ghee to it, whirl it around with a stick blender for a bit and add some salt. Yummy! I also did the ice cube gig. It works great and is handy for drinking or using it to cook with.

### tweety — 2012-07-29T16:22:13+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bab-your-assistance-please.2735/post-41782>

I can't eat mine any way but hot! It's too gelatinous. A bite or two ok, but not a whole serving.

Kombu is super easy. It comes in sheets. You rinse it under water, then throw it in whatever liquid you are cooking. No real flavor, just benefits. We've been using it for a couple of years, but mostly in the winter.

### Tizawee — 2012-07-29T17:20:27+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bab-your-assistance-please.2735/post-41794>

Thanks, Patty. I had a huge bowl for breakfast (got it down w/out problems). Gonna try the ghee in the morning. I think I'm going to stick w/ bone broth until I actually wake up hungry. Surgeon's office said it could be 3 months until I'm all healed. Better safe than hungry.

I always have nori in the house, tweety. Mine doesn't say kombu, but it's seaweed sheets so I guess it'll do. I can probably eat the bone broth cold because my Mom couldn't cook. I ate burned toast almost every day when I lived at home. She also used to come up with some... er, odd... food combinations. We kids didn't know any better. Once, and only once, her concoction was so bad my Dad couldn't eat it: she'd cleaned out the freezer, found some old (!) walnuts and added them to the meatloaf for dinner. Everyone tried a bite. Then Dad went & got pizza for our dinner.

# Thread: sodium  and aldosterone can switch Abnormal Activation of a Protein May Explain De
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sodium-and-aldosterone-can-switch-abnormal-activation-of-a-protein-may-explain-de.2736/>
**Subforum:** Adrenal Rx and Leaky Gut Rx
**Posts:** 2
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### chocolate — 2012-07-28T18:56:52+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sodium-and-aldosterone-can-switch-abnormal-activation-of-a-protein-may-explain-de.2736/post-41678>

[http://www.sciencedaily.com/releases/2011/09/110919104802.htm](http://http://www.sciencedaily.com/releases/2011/09/110919104802.htm)

Abnormal Activation of a Protein May Explain Deadly Link Between High Salt Intake and Obesity

> ScienceDaily (Sep. 19, 2011) â€” Dietary salt intake and obesity are two important risk factors in the development of high blood pressure. Each packs its own punch, but when combined, they deliver more damage to the heart and kidneys than the sum of their individual contributions. Discovering the molecular mechanisms behind this lethal synergy has presented a challenge to scientists, but research led by Toshiro Fujita, MD, professor and chairman of the Department of Internal Medicine and chief of the Department of Nephrology and Endocrinology at the University of Tokyo, suggests that high dietary salt intake and obesity work together to trigger an abnormal activation of a cellular protein called Rac1.
>
>
>
> How Obesity and a High-Salt Diet Team Up
>
>
>
> Dr. Fujita's team studied the effects of a high-salt diet in rats bred to have high blood pressure and different levels of blood pressure sensitivity to salt. When obese "salt-sensitive" rats were fed a high-salt diet, the team found that Rac1 activated the mineralocorticoid receptor (MR) on the rats' kidney cells. This receptor is normally activated by the hormone aldosterone. When turned on, MR leads to the expression of a protein called epithelial sodium channel (ENaC) and an enzyme called the sodium pump. Both of these substances promote the reabsorption of salt, which causes the body to retain fluid and results in high blood pressure. This is the first time scientists have seen Rac1 usurp aldosterone's role in activating MR in the regulation of blood pressure. The protein's usual duties entail regulating an array of cellular events such as cell growth.
>
>
>
> The team made the discovery when attempting to treat the obese, hypertensive rats with drugs designed to block MR activation and inhibit Rac1. When Rac1 inhibitors were successful in lowering the rats' blood pressure, the team knew they had discovered a mechanism by which obesity and a high-salt diet team up to wreak havoc on blood pressure and According to Dr. Fujita, the team's findings carry important implications for the treatment of hypertension. "Our data indicate that the Rac1-mediated pathway in the kidneys can be an alternative therapeutic target for salt-sensitive hypertension and salt-mediated kidney injury," he said. "Based upon our results, we can speculate that Rac1 in the kidneys regulates salt susceptibility of blood pressure, and that Rac1 inhibitors, as well as MR antagonists, may be effective in the treatment of salt-sensitive hypertension."

A presentation is mention in the rest of the article.

### chocolate — 2012-07-28T19:00:59+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sodium-and-aldosterone-can-switch-abnormal-activation-of-a-protein-may-explain-de.2736/post-41679>

[http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/19267423](http://http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/19267423)

> J Neurosci Res. 2009 Jul;87(9):2105-14.
>
> Rac1 inhibition negatively regulates transcriptional activity of the amyloid precursor protein gene.
>
> Wang PL, Niidome T, Akaike A, Kihara T, Sugimoto H.
>
> Source
>
>
>
> Department of Neuroscience for Drug Discovery, Graduate School of Pharmaceutical Sciences, Kyoto University, Sakyo-ku, Kyoto, Japan.
>
> Abstract
>
>
>
> Rac1, a member of the Rho family GTPases, participates in a variety of cellular functions including lamellipodia formation, actin cytoskeleton organization, cell growth, apoptosis, and neuronal development. Recent studies have implicated Rac1 in cytoskeletal abnormalities, production of reactive oxygen species, and generation of the amyloid beta-peptide (Abeta) observed in Alzheimer's disease. In this study, we examined the relationship between Rac1 and amyloid precursor protein (APP), because the abnormal proteolytic processing of APP is a pathologic feature of Alzheimer's disease. In primary hippocampal neurons, the Rac1-specific inhibitor NSC23766 decreased both Rac1 activity and APP protein levels in a concentration-dependent manner. To elucidate how NSC23766 decreases APP protein levels, we examined the effects of NSC23766 on APP processing, degradation, and biosynthesis. NSC23766 did not increase the levels of the proteolytic products of APP, sAPPalpha, Abeta40, and Abeta42. The proteasome inhibitor lactacystin did not reverse the NSC23766-induced decrease in APP protein levels. NSC23766 did, however, decrease the levels of both APP mRNA and APP protein. Decreased levels of APP mRNA and protein were also observed when HEK293 cells were transfected with an expression vector containing a dominant-negative Rac1 mutant or with siRNA targeting Rac1. By overexpressing progressively deleted fragments of the APP promoter in HEK293 cells, we identified a Rac1 response site at positions -233 to -41 bp in the APP promoter. Taken together, our results suggest that Rac1 regulates transcription of the APP gene in primary hippocampal neurons.

# Thread: HIT swimming
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hit-swimming.2739/>
**Subforum:** Optimal Fitness
**Posts:** 13
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Raiken3712 — 2012-07-28T20:16:24+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hit-swimming.2739/post-41701>

I have a 22,000 gallon pool and was going to try some hit on and off but I don't really know the specifics of it. I know you go all out for a certain amount of time and than rest, but I don't know how much time for either. Also should I go all out for a certain time and than stop? Or just swim around slowly during the breaks...

Thanks for any and all advice.

### Chrysanthemama — 2012-08-01T02:07:49+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hit-swimming.2739/post-42169>

> **Raiken3712;41927 wrote:** I have a 22,000 gallon pool and was going to try some hit on and off but I don't really know the specifics of it. I know you go all out for a certain amount of time and than rest, but I don't know how much time for either. Also should I go all out for a certain time and than stop? Or just swim around slowly during the breaks...
>
>
>
> Thanks for any and all advice.

My understanding of HIIT workouts are that they go like this:

1\. Maximum intensity for 30 seconds.

2\. Recovery for 90-120 seconds (only rest as long as you have to). Recovery meaning moving around gently but not stopping movement. You could tread water, do gentle strokes, etc.

\-Repeat this cycle 6-10 times, depending on your level of fitness (starting out at maybe 3 to 5 times for the first several workouts). it’s best to start with a 5 minute warm up at a comfortable intensity. After the workout, finish with a 5 minute cool-down period before you stop moving completely.

**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hit-swimming.2739/post-56650>

### Shijin13 — 2012-10-07T14:49:21+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hit-swimming.2739/post-56689>

When the kids have swimming lessons - I take the opportunity to do some laps. after I warm up I do 8x25Meters for as fast as I can on a 30second interval. I'm finishing the 25M in just under 20seconds - so I'm getting about 10seconds rest - very similar to a tabatta sprint. of 8 cycles of 20seconds fast all out w/10seconds rest. I'm hoping to increase my sprints by another 4x25M in the next week or two...

best work out ever

**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 1

### Raiken3712 — 2012-07-30T05:19:59+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/optimal-water-kefir.2756/post-41901>

So far from what I've read the longer you ferment the less sugar the final product has...but nothing on actual amounts. Also someone said that the longer you do it the less beneficial bacteria...not sure I get this? They start dying off after awhile because they don't have any sugar to feed on?...That makes some sense I guess that's it.

At some point it tastes like vinegar with very little sugar...is that the point where it doesn't have very much beneficial bacteria either? I'm trying to figure out the sweet spot for fermentation time. I also read that times past 36 hours can weaken the grains...I suppose when I have excess grains this isn't an issue...just starting at the moment at 8 tomorrow will be 48 hours.

I've also read second ferment can be anywhere from 3-10 days the fermentation step taken after you remove grains and add flavor. No waiting time needed for juice but for fresh or dries fruit you have to wait to infuse flavor? Also I read to change out fresh fruit every day....is this really necessary or is 1 day enough for it to go bad and grow mold? Is that why you change it out daily? I'm guessing so.

Any and all thoughts and experiences are greatly welcome. Thanks
