# Thread: low estrogen means magnesium loss?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/low-estrogen-means-magnesium-loss.2757/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 2
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### chocolate — 2012-07-30T05:27:58+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/low-estrogen-means-magnesium-loss.2757/post-41902>

[http://chronicfatigueandnutrition.com/minerals/pregnancy-magnesium/](http://http://chronicfatigueandnutrition.com/minerals/pregnancy-magnesium/)

Magnesium â€“ Deficiency

Here, we will discuss some conditions and how they create specific needs for magnesium and how ignoring the depletion of magnesium can put us in harms way. Armed with this knowledge, you will be able to begin to restore the mineral balance so crucial to proper metabolic function.

The elderly:

The elderly tend to eat less, drink less water, eat more processed foods and take diuretics for blood pressure regulation. This puts them at peculiar risk for deficiencies in total body magnesium. It is also a known fact that the elderly eat less magnesium containing foods. Is it no wonder why constipation and cardiovascular deaths, and leg cramps are epidemic among the elderly?

Pregnancy and magnesium

Pregnancy increases the motherâ€™s need for magnesium and calcium. Having multiple pregnancies increases the womanâ€™s risk for osteoporosis and hyperparathyroidism of pregnancy. Toxemia in pregnancy, and postpartum heart problems increases oneâ€™ chances of blood clotting because of magnesium deficiency. The stress of childbirth and the presence of preeclampsia increases the motherâ€™s draw of magnesium. Magnesium infusions were found to decrease the incidence of stroke and other thrombolytic events associated with these complications from pregnancy.

Magnesium deficiency and â€œsticky bloodâ€

Sticky blood is a common term for having red blood cells that tend to stick together, called rouleau. Sticky blood is not a good thing. Oxygen transport suffers as does nutrient transport. However, it dramatically increases oneâ€™s chances for stroke, because the blood forms mini clots in the body.

What causes sticky blood? Eating foods that we have become immunologically sensitized to, or have developed an allergy to,

or foods that react negatively to receptor sites on our red bloods cells now classified as incompatible with our genetic genotypeâ„¢ will accelerate the formation of sticky blood. Having insufficient digestive enzymes to digest oneâ€™s food, and individuals challenged by sugar metabolism issues, having trouble breaking down their carbohydrates, or problems with insulin resistance will tend to have sticky blood. Individuals with fatty livers, high cholesterol, and atherosclerosis also suffer from sticky blood. Women taking hormones, whether on birth control pills or for menopause, are at higher risk for sticky blood.

Magnesium deficiency accelerates the rate in which red blood cells tend to stick together, making all the above conditions worse!

Insulin resistance, Syndrome X

Insulin resistance, Syndrome X, having any type of sugar sensitivity or sugar metabolism problem such as reactive hypoglycemia, or low blood sugar, or low cortisol or adrenal fatigue causes the same problems of burning more magnesium at a faster rate to accommodate for the problems with sugar metabolism. The body needs magnesium to use insulin properly, helping it convert glycogen to glucose! Magnesium deficiency is prevalent among diabetics. A poll conducted by the American Diabetes Association found that 99% had not been advised by their health care practitioner about magnesium deficiency when over half were taking diuretics for blood pressure control and had a history of heart disease in the family!

Diabetes and magnesium:

Osteoporosis is a common complication of insulin dependent diabetes! Low serum magnesium levels are common in juvenile diabetes. Elevated glucose increases magnesium excretion in the urine. Insulin is necessary for the cellular uptake of magnesium. Insulin resistance makes proper magnesium uptake ineffective, contributing to the acceleration of osteoporosis among diabetics.

Menopause and magnesium:

Estrogen enhances the uptake of magnesium into soft tissue and bones. This can be one more reason why women of childbearing age are more protected from heart disease and osteoporosis. However, if estrogen gets out of balance, elevated estrogen levels coupled with low dietary intake of magnesium causes the bodyâ€™s biochemistry to shift to increase the calcium ratio over the magnesium. The elevate calcium causes increased fibrin formation leading to the development of clots, and the wasting of bone seen in osteoporosis. Taking calcium when one does not know if they have elevated estrogen will increase their risk for:

stroke

blood clots

kidney stones

bone spurs

osteoporosis even more

In addition, there is much to suggest that taking magnesium regularly is protective against the formation of clots and arteriospasms that can induce heart attack! Therefore, taking additional or even high dose calcium when you do not know your level of intracellular magnesium and you do not know what your ratio of calcium to magnesium can accelerate osteoporosis, and leading you quicker to stroke.

Osteoporosis and magnesium:

Estrogen deficiency, because of the loss of progesterone in menopausal women, and the ratio of estrogen to progesterone in the body (when estrogen remains elevated in ratio) results in reduced magnesium absorption. Insufficient magnesium in turn affects osteocytes (depressing the Mg-dependent ATP H+K+ pump) causing reduced bone formation and is a leading mechanism of osteoporosis in menopausal women! Interesting to note is that irrespective of the type of osteoporosis (senile osteoporosis, diabetic osteoporosis, alcohol associated osteoporosis and non-alcoholic cirrhosis induced osteoporosis) all forms showed low magnesium in the bone cortex. This resulted in an excessive acid state of bone extracellular fluid which in turn inhibited bone osteocytes from proper bone formation.

Parathyroid, imbalances in calcium and magnesium ratios:

**Unfortunately, with all the focus on taking calcium, little focus is on the consequences of having an imbalance in oneâ€™s calcium to magnesium ratio! Taking more calcium than magnesium interferes with magnesium absorption because calcium and magnesium share the same intestinal absorption pathway!

Three possible causes can exist for low calcium aka hypocalcemia which would prompt oneâ€™ doctor at first glance to simply recommend higher doses of calcium. Hypocalcemia can be due to the impaired release of parathyroid hormone (PTH). Hypocalcemia can also result from resistance from bone and kidney to PTH and/or defects in the vitamin D synthesis pathway.

It has been found that reversing hypocalcemia happens NOT with increasing your calcium but with magnesium therapy! Magnesium therapy has also been shown to increase oneâ€™s responsiveness to vitamin D and PTH! Calcium on the other hand, would continue to tip this out of balance and push the patient into osteoporosis because it is not properly balanced with sufficient magnesium.**

Estrogen keeps the parathyroid hormone in check. With loss of estrogen and this can occur without symptoms until itâ€™s advanced, parathyroid hormoneâ€™s action goes unchecked leading to increased bone resorption, postmenopausal hyperparathyroidism and osteoporosis. The mechanism is believed to involve magnesium. Low estrogen means low absorption of magnesium which in turn increases increases calcium loss. Restoring magnesium reverses parathyroid hyperplasia and bone loss.

lots to put together

### chocolate — 2012-08-18T06:14:48+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/low-estrogen-means-magnesium-loss.2757/post-45500>

Premenstrual syndrome (PMS)

Scientific studies suggest that magnesium supplements may help relieve symptoms associated with PMS, particularly bloating, insomnia, leg swelling, weight gain, and breast tenderness. One study suggests that a combination of magnesium and vitamin B6 may work better than either one alone.

Read more: [http://www.umm.edu/altmed/articles/magnesium-000313.htm#ixzz23rcGsN3f](http://http://www.umm.edu/altmed/articles/magnesium-000313.htm#ixzz23rcGsN3f)

# Thread: FOS Fructo Oligosaccharides
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fos-fructo-oligosaccharides.2760/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 13
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Jasmin — 2012-07-30T09:36:25+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fos-fructo-oligosaccharides.2760/post-41911>

Does anyone know if FOS is allright to use as a sweetner ?

I have AI and been addicted to the sweet taste. I started Leptin reset in january 2012 but used stevia. The last 10 weeks I have not added any sweetner and has no cravings at all. I am invited for dinner and was told to bring my own dessert, so do you thing that FOS could be a good choice?

### Michael — 2012-07-30T12:42:04+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fos-fructo-oligosaccharides.2760/post-41918>

It's used as a sweetener, because, unlike glucose, you can't use it for energy.

However, some gut bacteria can use it - hence it is also used as a prebiotic:

[http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fructooligosaccharide](http://http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fructooligosaccharide)

That _might_ possibly be a bad thing, depending on the state of your gut, inasmuch as prebiotics can also feed bacteria present in gut dysbiosis.

So I don't know but I think that might be a possible issue - but perhaps not if it's just a little in a dessert.

### villjamur_stevenson — 2012-07-30T15:18:06+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fos-fructo-oligosaccharides.2760/post-41923>

FOS is junk science in my opinion - something cheap they can add to yogurts and sell to the public. But the reality is, if any microbe in your gut can ferment it, all the microbes can. I would stay far away from it.

### janagram — 2012-07-30T17:06:32+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fos-fructo-oligosaccharides.2760/post-41938>

if the sweet taste of stevia is addicting why do you think FOS will not be? (I would prefer stevia)

### Jasmin — 2012-07-31T09:29:45+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fos-fructo-oligosaccharides.2760/post-42024>

> **Michael;42157 wrote:**
>
>
>
> That _might_ possibly be a bad thing, depending on the stat of your gut, inasmuch as prebiotics can also feed bacteria present in gut dysbiosis.

Thank you Michael

I was looking for something that would feed the good bacterias in my gut - didnt think for a second that it would also feed the bad bacterias. My mind is not optimal yet

### Jasmin — 2012-07-31T09:31:55+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fos-fructo-oligosaccharides.2760/post-42025>

> **villjamur\_stevenson;42163 wrote:** FOS is junk science in my opinion - something cheap they can add to yogurts and sell to the public. But the reality is, if any microbe in your gut can ferment it, all the microbes can. I would stay far away from it.

villjamur\_stevenson I certainly will stay away from FOS - thank you for your help

### Jasmin — 2012-07-31T09:34:50+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fos-fructo-oligosaccharides.2760/post-42026>

> **janagram;42179 wrote:** if the sweet taste of stevia is addicting why do you think FOS will not be? (I would prefer stevia)

It was ment as an alternative to stevia and I thought that I at the same time could do something good for the good bacterias in my gut. I will stay away from FOS - thank you for your help

### Michael — 2012-07-31T11:25:11+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fos-fructo-oligosaccharides.2760/post-42027>

> **Jasmin;42274 wrote:** Thank you Michael
>
> I was looking for something that would feed the good bacterias in my gut - didnt think for a second that it would also feed the bad bacterias. My mind is not optimal yet

Well, I was trying to be a bit tentative. I don't know that there's definite proof that that particular type of soluble fiber will do that. It's just I know some of these people who have experience with regimens like the SCD feel that extra soluble fiber, such as inulin, has proved unhelpful for some sufferers. Depends on what you've got going in your gut I should think. Moreover, I don't know how much you would use just to sweeten some dish on a one-off basis.

### villjamur_stevenson — 2012-07-31T21:39:35+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fos-fructo-oligosaccharides.2760/post-42096>

I used to belong to an IBD forum where we had a guy join the group - trying to sell inulin to everyone.

The group did a lot of research into it and concluded that it wasn't something that seemed sensible.

But, even after this conclusion a few people fell for the sales pitch and bought it. It made them a lot worse and resulted in flares.

The microbes in our gut work together. So even if one of them can digest the inulin, it is making byproducts that will feed the others.

So it certainly isn't the magic bullet that the sellers/resellers are pushing it to be.

### PaleoCowgirl — 2012-08-01T00:02:59+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fos-fructo-oligosaccharides.2760/post-42139>

Why not just bring a bowl of fresh fruit for dessert? Or grill up peaches or pineapples with a little cinnamon. No artificial sweeteners needed.

### Glen PDQ — 2012-08-03T21:59:12+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fos-fructo-oligosaccharides.2760/post-42750>

> **PaleoCowgirl;42391 wrote:** Why not just bring a bowl of fresh fruit for dessert? Or grill up peaches or pineapples with a little cinnamon. No artificial sweeteners needed.

Aren't peaches a goitrogen? Should peaches be avoided on the LR diet other than the fact they have fair amounts of sugar?

### Jasmin — 2012-08-11T09:34:10+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fos-fructo-oligosaccharides.2760/post-44289>

I have just re-read the blog "The leaky gut prescription" where Jack recommend FOS when treating a leaky gut - I am a bit confused now

### Glen PDQ — 2012-09-02T05:16:23+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fos-fructo-oligosaccharides.2760/post-48887>

After doing much reading of the blogs here it seems peaches or anything "in season" is acceptable for someone who is leptin sensitive, but not for someone who is leptin resistant. Observing the circadian rhythm and avoiding artificial light after sundown and before dawn would be a critical part of that.

Cooking appears to destroy the goitrogenic substances. Grilled peaches with a bit of balsamic vinegar anyone?

I have no clue about the FOS.

# Thread: Does CT affect cortisol results?

**Posts:** 5
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Eve — 2012-07-30T16:31:39+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/does-ct-affect-cortisol-results.2764/post-41931>

I just ordered a Dirunal Cortisol Saliva test kit...Do I have to stop CTing beforehand? Or is that just for sex hormones?

### Patty Cakes — 2012-07-31T04:36:03+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/does-ct-affect-cortisol-results.2764/post-42012>

I wish I knew the right answer for you but I suspect it will affect it since CTing is supposed to be real good for the adrenals (speaking as an adrenally challenged person). Hopefully someone else will chime in here and correct me if I am wrong.

### Souldanzer — 2012-07-31T04:57:12+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/does-ct-affect-cortisol-results.2764/post-42015>

Dr K has answered this before..... salivary cortisol tests your cortisol over the last few months and it shouldn't be affected by an acute rise in cortisol due to CT.

That said, there's plenty of people on this forum who chose to not CT the day of salivary cortisol testing despite.

### bigknitwit — 2012-07-31T14:09:39+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/does-ct-affect-cortisol-results.2764/post-42030>

>
>
>
> That said, there's plenty of people on this forum who chose to not CT the day of salivary cortisol testing despite.

Cortisol levels over the last few months? Really? So when I did mine back in March, I had weaned off the HC and all adaptogens for 2 weeks prior. But in reality it was measuring my average levels over the months prior? That would explain why my ASIlooked so reasonable compared to the reality of my symptoms off the HC...

### vkiernan — 2012-07-31T14:19:04+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/does-ct-affect-cortisol-results.2764/post-42032>

> **bigknitwit;42280 wrote:** Cortisol levels over the last few months? Really? So when I did mine back in March, I had weaned off the HC and all adaptogens for 2 weeks prior. But in reality it was measuring my average levels over the months prior? That would explain why my ASIlooked so reasonable compared to the reality of my symptoms off the HC...

Dr. K answered my question about being off HC before retesting and he said a few days. I would think you would be ok. You should retest in a couple of months I would think anyway just to make sure you are on the right path. I think he mentioned somewhere about 1 test isn't enough.

# Thread: Breast Feeding promotion = Nanny State?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/breast-feeding-promotion-nanny-state.2769/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 10
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Raiken3712 — 2012-07-30T22:50:07+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/breast-feeding-promotion-nanny-state.2769/post-41965>

[http://www.foxnews.com/politics/2012/07/29/nyc-mayor-wants-hospitals-to-lock-up-baby-formula-to-encourage-breast-feeding/?test=latestnews](http://http://www.foxnews.com/politics/2012/07/29/nyc-mayor-wants-hospitals-to-lock-up-baby-formula-to-encourage-breast-feeding/?test=latestnews)

I personally don't see it as all that bad....they compare it to Bloomberg outlawing sodas over a certain size and yet here we are talking about just putting the formula out of sight...they can still get it if they want it. Their reasons for encouraging breast feeding over formula are probably not as thorough as mine or Dr. Mercola's or anyone else who knows why Formula at least standard formula is so bad, but I think its a positive overall to let people know of the benefits.

Banning things is not the same as saying one thing has benefits but still allowing you to get the other.

Thoughts?

### Shijin13 — 2012-07-30T23:10:33+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/breast-feeding-promotion-nanny-state.2769/post-41967>

This is being pushed for nationwide - by moms who didn't receive appropriate breastfeeding support while in the hospital -

With both kids I was given a "Breastfeeding support" bag sponsored by similac - each back had formula samples. WFT? If I'm bfing why would I want a bag w/formula samples.

Formula companies offer lactation support counseling on 800# - and they are staffed by formula companies employees who are not trained IBCLCs! They regularly recommend supplementing instead of nursing.

WHO and CDC both recommend exclusive breastfeeding for the first 6mths (Doesn't matter if its nursing, eping, or via donor milk). However of the two only the WHO recommends nursing until 2yr of age for all children.

I remembered when I was 1st pregnant - I kept getting formula samples - I actually wrote on them return to sender - they stopped showing up. Unfortunately w/dd she was unable to nurse and I was unable to produce enough milk through expression to exclusively feed her breastmilk - so she got formula - this was before HumanMilk4HumanBabies got going - if it had been around I would have feed her that over formula - I did have friends who donated to me as well... the problem w/formula - is so many practitioners recommend it to help mom recover, give her a chance to rest or to give dad an opportunity to feed the baby - all that does is make exclusive breastfeeding that much harder.

Breastfeeding is the hardest thing I've ever done. I have a love hate relationship with my pump. I love nursing my son, and I wished dd could have nursed as well. only about 1% of women has a condition called IGT - where they cannot produce milk at all, the rest have chosen lazy route when they've been educated on the benefits, but choose formula. Its heart wrenching for those who do more than try to exclusively breastfeed not be able to do that - you can feel like a failure - even when you're not. Formula is a good thing in those instances where there are no other options.

But there are other options - milk from other women, for thousands of years this is how we supported infants who's mothers died or couldn't produce milk. Its how mothers who had a loss of a child at birth recovered from their grief.

When you look at the recommendations from the WHO the first choice is the milk from the mother of a child, 2nd is milk via direct nursing from another woman, 3rd is donor milk from multiple women, 4th is a WAP formula made w/goats milk. and 5th is Formula

That should say something but the marketing pushed by the food and drug mfrs who make this stuff - have sold americans on the idea that breasts aren't for nurturing offspring...

I could go on for hours about this....

when hospitals remove formula from their maternity wards - it is b/c the women using the facility are requesting it. some hospitals require you to bring your own if you don't plan on breastfeeding.

### Huck — 2012-07-30T23:24:30+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/breast-feeding-promotion-nanny-state.2769/post-41968>

Just because the government happens to get it right every once in a while, doesn't mean they should involve themselves like this. Seems like Bloomberg wants to be health commissioner, not the Mayor.

### Shijin13 — 2012-07-30T23:28:14+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/breast-feeding-promotion-nanny-state.2769/post-41969>

> **Huck;42212 wrote:** Just because the government happens to get it right every once in a while, doesn't mean they should involve themselves like this. Seems like Bloomberg wants to be health commissioner, not the Mayor.

maybe he's auditioning for the cabinet position of HHS...

### kathylu — 2012-07-30T23:49:04+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/breast-feeding-promotion-nanny-state.2769/post-41971>

Well, I do agree with the intention but I think the implementation is off base. Education is the answer, but ultimately the decision rests with the mother.

I was only able to breastfeed for 3 months, and only partially at that. I just didn't produce enough milk, too old I guess. Fortunately I had a girlfriend with a new baby who generously donated breast milk for almost 6 months. Between the two of us, my baby did well. Then I switched to the WAP formula (Fallon formula). My son really thrived on it, so all is well.

I think the use of bf first, then milk donation options then the Fallon formula would be the best path. No commercial formula unless there is no other option to keep the baby from starving.

### nonchalant — 2012-07-31T00:04:26+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/breast-feeding-promotion-nanny-state.2769/post-41973>

The government should have no place in this.

### KiwiLauren — 2012-07-31T01:07:50+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/breast-feeding-promotion-nanny-state.2769/post-41991>

In an Australian hospital I consulted with, formula was treated as medicine because it has so many known side effects. The hospital gave it to any mother who requested it, but she had to sign a consent saying this was what she wanted. Personal choice cannot be interfered with; however neither can informed consent. If you look at some of the recent research, a day's worth of soy formula has as much aluminum as a year's worth of vaccines. This cannot be taken lightly. (As well as issues with gut permeability from birth, leptin in breastmilk, etc.) However, the problem also cannot rest solely in the mother's hands. A mother with a newborn is exquisitely vulnerable. If she chooses not to breastfeed (or cannot...), then finding human milk banks/donations and/or using a homemade recipe like kathylu mentioned is obviously the next best step... but mothers need help receiving this information and accessing these things.

### Raiken3712 — 2012-07-31T01:47:14+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/breast-feeding-promotion-nanny-state.2769/post-41999>

> **Nonchalant;42217 wrote:** The government should have no place in this.

This one is hard for me...on the one hand I generally don't like government being involved...but if the choice is government involvement = more breast feeding or no government involvement = more formula use...

Ideally the hospitals would know whats best and be doing it without government even talking about it...

Sigh rghglllghghlghgl

### kathylu — 2012-07-31T01:47:42+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/breast-feeding-promotion-nanny-state.2769/post-42000>

This is an aside, but I never told any health care provider (and not much of anyone else either) that I was making my own baby formula from 6 months onward. I was really afraid someone would call Protective Services and report it and then I might have legal issues, or possibly even have the baby taken into custody for his own protection. Most people would think I was deliberately malnourishing my child. Then of course, there is the "raw milk is illegal" issue too. Stupidity abounds.

### Raiken3712 — 2012-07-31T17:26:57+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/breast-feeding-promotion-nanny-state.2769/post-42048>

yeah...telling people you feed your baby raw milk...it might actually happen that way...sad

I've seen some absurd stories about babies being taken away ....for some really stupid stuff I wouldn't put it past them.

[http://www.openculture.com/free\_textbooks](http://http://www.openculture.com/free_textbooks) A sampling of titles

Biology

Biology Pages, John W. Kimball, Harvard University

Botany Online: The Internet Hypertextbook by multiple scholars.

Cells: Molecules and Mechanisms. Published by the Axolotl Academic Publishing Co.

CK-12 Biology I (Grades 9-12) edited by Douglas Wilkin, Chair, Dept of Science, Los Angeles Unified School District

# Thread: Choline  Epigenetics
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/choline-epigenetics.2783/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 12
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### AKMan — 2012-07-31T19:57:25+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/choline-epigenetics.2783/post-42085>

Choline found mainly in eggs, offal, and seafood!

[http://medicalxpress.com/news/2012-07-sick-stress-blame-mom-epigenetics.html](http://http://medicalxpress.com/news/2012-07-sick-stress-blame-mom-epigenetics.html)

If you're sick from stress, a new research report appearing in the August 2012 issue of The FASEB Journal suggests that what your mother ateâ€”or didn't eatâ€”may be part of the cause. The report shows that choline intake that is higher than what is generally recommended during pregnancy may improve how a child responds to stress. These improvements are the result of epigenetic changes that ultimately lead to lower cortisol levels. Epigenetic changes affect how a gene functions, even if the gene itself is not changed. Lowering cortisol is important as high levels of cortisol are linked to a wide range of problems ranging from mental health to metabolic and cardiovascular disorders.

### AKMan — 2012-08-01T20:52:54+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/choline-epigenetics.2783/post-42315>

The salmon I get aren't like what you buy in the grocery store, they've swam hundreds of miles to spawn, their skin is red and has bad patches. They get a big hump of fat on their backs. These are real salmon like our ancestors ate, not caught out in the ocean where their skin is silver. We fillet, then and dry or smoke them. They are usually bristling with eggs ready to be laid--the chickens don't get those! The meat is so oily, you can burn it like a candle when it's dried. I love living in Alaska!

# Thread: leaky gut... leaky brain... leaky skin?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/leaky-gut-leaky-brain-leaky-skin.2785/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 4
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### hellojtm — 2012-07-31T21:55:15+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/leaky-gut-leaky-brain-leaky-skin.2785/post-42098>

Hey All,

Just posting some spurious thoughts I have had in the last couple of weeks. I have only been able to glance at the blog in passing and not read anything in detail for a couple of months. However I did get the drift from Dr. Kruse that leaky gut potentially is a positive adaptation from a previous stressor that is now being triggered in advertently. I was also thinking about sweating and the fact that I bought thin outerwear that covers my arms and neck so that I wouldn't have to use sunblock while hiking. There were some other random trivial thoughts around like when I jumped in the ocean a couple weeks ago that maybe the salt water was a signal to my body or even being absorbed.

So what about leaky skin? Is this something that humans have, i.e. hormone replacement can be applied as a lotion, that earlier primates don't have? Along with our ability to sweat did we develop the ability to absorb? Or is absorbance the earlier state and other animals have developed a thicker skin barrier?

Here is an article that ultimately likens the skin to the intestines, which triggered me to finally make this post:

[The Skin's Secret Surveillance System](http://http://www.nature.com/news/the-skin-s-secret-surveillance-system-1.11075)

jesse

### nonchalant — 2012-07-31T22:30:21+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/leaky-gut-leaky-brain-leaky-skin.2785/post-42109>

Interesting article, Jesse.

I know that since I've been avoiding low-quality seed oils, I don't burn or peel as easily.

Also since eating seafood daily, my skin quality has changed dramatically. More dense and smooth. I would guess less leaky maybe.

### hellojtm — 2012-09-18T00:10:24+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/leaky-gut-leaky-brain-leaky-skin.2785/post-52221>

Is Chris Kresser reading my posts!?

from:

[http://www.westonaprice.org/2012-conference/2012-conference-presentations](http://http://www.westonaprice.org/2012-conference/2012-conference-presentations)

**Chris Kresser, L Ac The Gut-Brain-Skin** Axis: New Perspectives on an Old Idea

The connection between skin and mental health disorders has been a topic of growing interest in medicine over the past two decades, as evidenced by the rise of new disciplines like psychodermatology and neurodermatology. More recently, researchers are turning their attention to the connection between the skin and the gut. For example, studies have shown that those with acne and other skin problems are more likely to experience digestive issues like constipation, acid reflux and IBS - and vice versa. In this presentation, we'll discuss the relationship between digestive, mental/behavioral and skin disorders, and explore nutritional strategies to preventing and treating them.

### hellojtm — 2012-10-02T20:29:31+0300

**Posts:** 5

Of course, we're all ahead of the curve here, but it's an interesting study nonetheless. [http://www.dovepress.com/comparison-with-ancestral-diets-suggests-dense-acellular-carbohydrates-peer-reviewed-article-DMSO](http://http://www.dovepress.com/comparison-with-ancestral-diets-suggests-dense-acellular-carbohydrates-peer-reviewed-article-DMSO)

### nonchalant — 2012-08-01T00:18:11+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/study-supports-brain-gut-axis-as-critical-to-leptin-sensitivity.2786/post-42142>

I need to find that coconut flour (in the chest freezer somewhere) and toss it.

### nate.reik@yahoo.com — 2012-08-01T01:51:13+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/study-supports-brain-gut-axis-as-critical-to-leptin-sensitivity.2786/post-42167>

Is that not just a hypothesis, as in, no experiment has been performed yet to support this? (Don't want the title to mislead people.)

### AKMan — 2012-08-01T02:24:54+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/study-supports-brain-gut-axis-as-critical-to-leptin-sensitivity.2786/post-42170>

> **Nate R;42419 wrote:** Is that not just a hypothesis, as in, no experiment has been performed yet to support this? (Don't want the title to mislead people.)

Every geek needs to read the full study (only 15 pages), here's the pdf: [http://www.dovepress.com/getfile.php?fileID=13214](http://http://www.dovepress.com/getfile.php?fileID=13214)

It perfectly sums up why paleo 'works'. Also explains why those almond flour pancakes and coconut flour cookies are doing more harm than good. Tie this to the epi-paleo rx and you have a recipe for success!

Here's the conclusion, in case you can't open the link:

"Conclusion

The increased storage life and convenience of some of our oldest agricultural products may come with a hitherto unrecognized metabolic cost. The foods eaten by hunter-gatherers, non-cereal horticulturalists, and those following a modern Paleolithic or â€œprimalâ€￾ diet are sharply delineated from modern foods by their lower carbohydrate densities. Consumption of exclusively low-density carbohydrates is suggested to produce a less inflammatory GI microbiota, and may explain the apparent absence of overweight and metabolic disease in two of these groups, and the promising early data from the third. This hypothesis may also explain (1) why obesity incidence scales with refined food intake, but has such confusing correlatory patterns with macronutrients; (2) why calorie-controlled diets of Westernized foods require a perpetual fight with homeostatic correction mechanisms; (3) the link between periodontal disease and systemic atherosclerotic

disease and obesity; (4) why the benefits of a diet of fruit and vegetables have not been replicated by supplements of the constituent antioxidants, vitamins, minerals, and fiber alongside a Western diet; (5) why low-carbohydrate diets produce ad libitum weight loss, but low-fat diets do not; and (6) the relative resistance of European people to obesity and diabetes from Westernized diets.

We should not settle for the meager improvements attainable

from the consensus dietary advice when it is already clear that so much more might be achieved. Our sights should be set high, to see how close we can move levels of industrialized metabolic health toward those enjoyed by non-Westernized populations. While many will resist making

dietary changes of such magnitude, official advice must nonetheless point in the correct direction, allowing individuals

to make informed decisions.

The social and financial burden of the epidemic of obesity and metabolic syndrome threatens the long-term viability of our health-care systems and perniciously undermines the other benefits of modern civilization, including redirection of scarce financial resources. If this dietary pattern is confirmed to work, practical policy solutions must be sought. Some grain cultivars may be found to produce less inflammation, sprouting techniques may be found to be of benefit, or grain production may be replaced with root-vegetable cultivation where practical. Over time, the interplay between market forces, attainable agricultural yields, and the practicalities of food-distribution networks may allow shifting demand from a newly informed populace to reshape global agribusiness.

A dietary pattern with carbohydrates exclusively from cellular low-density sources may remove the root cause of a range of our most prevalent diseases. The potential savings

in health-care costs should be borne in mind, and the hypothesis tested."

### nonchalant — 2012-08-02T00:47:01+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/study-supports-brain-gut-axis-as-critical-to-leptin-sensitivity.2786/post-42364>

Thanks for the link, AKMan.

So I am doing my first set of labs for a consultation. I am taking 10 mg DHEA and 100mg 7-keto DHEA each day. I started the 100mg based on Jack's post and knowing my DHEA-S is about 45. In looking at my instructions for the ASI, they say to note if taking DHEA as it can affect the results. I am wondering if I should continue to take it or if I should stop it. If I stop it, how many days ahead do I need to stop?

Jane

### James — 2012-08-02T17:02:29+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/stopping-dhea-for-baseline-asi.2791/post-42499>

# Thread: Interesting Study - Confirms what we already know!

**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 1
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Jim — 2012-08-01T06:42:11+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/interesting-study-confirms-what-we-already-know.2794/post-42217>

Interesting study that confirms what we already know!

[Mice lacking δ-opioid receptors resist the development of diet-induced obesity](http://http://www.fasebj.org/content/26/8/3483.abstract)

Abstract

Pharmacological manipulation of opioid receptors alters feeding behavior. However, the individual contributions of each opioid receptor subtype on energy balance remain largely unknown. Herein, we investigated whether genetic disruption of the δ-opioid receptor (DOR) also controls energy homeostasis. Mice lacking DOR and wild-type mice were fed with standard diet and high-energy diet (HED). Mice were analyzed in vivo with the indirect calorimetry system, and tissues were analyzed by real-time PCR and Western blot analysis. DOR-knockout (KO) mice gained less weight (P

> â€œYouâ€™re going to be hearing from my attorney,â€￾ Brian Egan told his boss on his last day of work, nearly a year ago. Last week, Egan filed a class-action lawsuit that accuses Telomerase Activation Sciences (TA Sciences) in New York of engaging in deceptive business practices in promoting a proprietary herbal extract intended to reverse the effects of ageing.
>
>
>
> The lawsuit threatens to put the science of telomeres â€” repetitive nucleotide sequences that protect the ends of chromosomes during DNA replication â€” on trial. But Noel Patton, president of TA Sciences, denies all of the allegations. â€œWe stand by what we say,â€￾ he adds.
>
>
>
> The connection between cellular ageing and telomere length is rooted in solid research. Telomeres become shorter every time a cell divides, and when they are lost cells can no longer reproduce. The enzyme telomerase can lengthen telomeres, possibly slowing or reversing degenerative diseases. In one study1, mice genetically engineered to lack functional telomerase showed brain degeneration and shrunken testes, but those effects were reversed when the enzyme was reactivated.

.

# Thread: Trying to decide, Leptin Reset or HCG +Leptin Rx
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/trying-to-decide-leptin-reset-or-hcg-leptin-rx.2802/>
**Subforum:** HCG Protocol
**Posts:** 15
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### rottzilla — 2012-08-01T17:51:54+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/trying-to-decide-leptin-reset-or-hcg-leptin-rx.2802/post-42271>

Hi, I'm new. To both the forums and Jack Kruse. I've done leptin diets successfully in the past, but this is my first introduction to Jack and CT. (Done Rosedale previously)

I lost 50 pounds on hcg, and have kept it off for 2 years.

I recently gained 10 pounds back, but have been maintaining.

I had 30 to lose, but since gaining 10 now have 40 to lose to reach my goal weight.

Maybe 45, but I'm being nice to myself.

I am currently trying to get pregnant, and found that I have dangerously low iron levels. Ferritin is at 7.

I'm 40 so I don't have a lot of time to play around to get pregnant in. However, I'm mostly healthy fertility wise from what my doctors can tell.

I also lost hair on round 2 of hcg, and it took a long time to grow it back.

I know I won't be ready to have a baby anytime soon - at least 3 months - because I have to increase my nutrient stores, including my iron, before I'm ready.

I really want to spend these 3 months preparing my body in the best way possible for my future baby. That includes both raising my nutrient levels AND losing as much weight as possible in that time. Eating nutrient dense foods are obviously on my list, as is juicing to get in as many greens as possible.

I can't decide if I want to do hcg one more time - I have two vials of 2000 hucog sitting at home.

I really want to do what's best for my body and future baby first, and that includes losing weight, but only if it's done healthily.

Does anyone have any thoughts to help me?

I'll be reading today to see what people are eating on the Leptin Rx AND hcg to gain a better idea, but I know sometimes forums are slow, especially in the summer. So forgive my ignorance, but I wanted to post to get out there prior to reading in the interest of time.

### jeanie@truelongmont.com — 2012-08-01T17:55:31+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/trying-to-decide-leptin-reset-or-hcg-leptin-rx.2802/post-42272>

Read all the epi paleo posts. No way are you going to build up nutrient stores while on hcg. Thr overfat isnt that much of a prob for the pregnancy. If you are eating clean epi paleo your baby will be a rockstar.

### MartiD — 2012-08-01T18:00:52+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/trying-to-decide-leptin-reset-or-hcg-leptin-rx.2802/post-42273>

I agree, concentrate on getting your body optimal with Leptin RX, 100% paleo diet with lots of seafood. Even if you don't lose the weight you think you want to lose, you will be in a much better place to get pregnant. HCG is great for weight loss but does mess up your hormones and system for a short period of time so not necessarily good in your situation. Good luck!

### rottzilla — 2012-08-01T18:01:37+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/trying-to-decide-leptin-reset-or-hcg-leptin-rx.2802/post-42274>

Thank you Cavemam! That's my feeling as well, and usually I go with my gut, but hcg is like an addiction. Seeing that fast movement on the scale is enticing and I'm so sick of being overfat, as you put it.

I want to lose quickly because of back issues, and a recent sciatic issue. Any added weight from the baby, I hope to be counterbalanced by losing the fat.

### rottzilla — 2012-08-01T18:02:16+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/trying-to-decide-leptin-reset-or-hcg-leptin-rx.2802/post-42275>

MartiD, also, thank you for the quick reply. I appreciate the time you took to read and reply.

### jeanie@truelongmont.com — 2012-08-01T18:33:58+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/trying-to-decide-leptin-reset-or-hcg-leptin-rx.2802/post-42282>

> **rottzilla;42526 wrote:** Thank you Cavemam! That's my feeling as well, and usually I go with my gut, but hcg is like an addiction. Seeing that fast movement on the scale is enticing and I'm so sick of being overfat, as you put it.
>
>
>
> I want to lose quickly because of back issues, and a recent sciatic issue. Any added weight from the baby, I hope to be counterbalanced by losing the fat.

Also all the "problems" one has with a fat-pregnancy are usually stemming from all the normal stuff caused by the SAD diet. I was a "high risk" pregnancy, I weighed 260 ish and I was 39! Not a problem in the world with the pregnancy. If you have got the eating dialed in, and are eating to provide baby with the right things - then that's your best bet. It will also help AFTER the baby because you baby will know to mind his/her circadian cycles and you will BOTH get more sleep. He/she will be rocking the BAT and you won't over warm him/her.

Also, I missed this whole thing - but the daily circadian cycles and light and sun and dark are of UTMOST importance. No doctor will tell you this. You are way better off being fat and full of nutrition and minding the light than some skinny-minny that drank diet sodas and exercised the cortisol high.

Losing weight IS a total stressor and is reflected in high numbers that will make your baby much much more difficult in the first year. READ HE/SHE WON'T SLEEP AND WILL CRY A LOT.

Relax, eat good food, and do some muscle stuff to get strong so your back doesn't go out!

### quelsen — 2012-08-01T19:34:39+0300

### colleencoble — 2012-08-01T23:19:19+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/trying-to-decide-leptin-reset-or-hcg-leptin-rx.2802/post-42347>

### kathylu — 2012-08-02T04:54:11+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/trying-to-decide-leptin-reset-or-hcg-leptin-rx.2802/post-42430>

rottzilla, if you are 40 you literally have no time left. Your top priority should be getting pregnant, shelve everything else. Worry about weight loss after the baby. I also had a very healthy baby while I was obese, so you can do it. Just eat lots of seafood, fish oil, low carb paleo and you will have a big brained paleo perfect baby!

### terryfischer39@yahoo.com — 2012-08-02T06:00:55+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/trying-to-decide-leptin-reset-or-hcg-leptin-rx.2802/post-42445>

### rottzilla — 2012-08-07T16:43:59+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/trying-to-decide-leptin-reset-or-hcg-leptin-rx.2802/post-43417>

Wow, you guys are all wonderful, thank you! What great responses. As for being out of time, I'm lucky in that my body (eggs) haven't aged like a 40 year old. So, yes, I'm having difficulty, but my body age is less than my chronological age, if that makes any sense?

I'll look into the SSS tonic. My doctor has me taking 325 mg of Ferrous Sulfate 3x a day at this point. As far as I know, my thyroid has been tested and been fine but it's been a few years.

I'm having a really hard time losing. I've been trying but failing at this already. UGH. I'm going to post what I've been eating over at the Leptin RX to see if I can get some input and help.

### jeanie@truelongmont.com — 2012-08-07T16:51:10+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/trying-to-decide-leptin-reset-or-hcg-leptin-rx.2802/post-43419>

This one real hard for me to "get" cognitive dissonance and all that - but 40 is O L D in human lady terms. Nature wants us to die now. I'm serious. We are made to make babies, raise them - then leave the rest for the next gen. All of this anti-aging stuff is tricking that. It's hard to accept - but it starts to make sense, it's why when you get to a certain age, no matter how perfect and awesome your lifestyle is, your hormones are going to try and kill you. It "starts" even as young as 30 - although most don't start to ID it until mid 40's.

Longstoryshort, no matter what you do - your hormones are going to work against you. You have to test and rein those bitches in and fight mother nature.

I know other things happen such as the eggs get tougher to penetrate by sperm, etc. And my fallopian tube was blocked and just unblocked when I had my HSG test... but all these years of eating nutrient dense, whole locally grown organic and biodynamic foods have paid off in that my body is very "young" according to the labs ... but the past two years of screwing around with diets and trying to lose the weight by any means possible have provided me with both weight loss and loss of nutrients - such as iron - so that now I'm nutrient depleted. I should have tried other means to lose weight, but the hcg was so attractive. And it did work well for me.

# Thread: Stockpiling Fiji 1 liters for Ice bath questions
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/stockpiling-fiji-1-liters-for-ice-bath-questions.2818/>
**Subforum:** Cold Thermogenesis
**Posts:** 11
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Raiken3712 — 2012-08-02T20:03:47+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/stockpiling-fiji-1-liters-for-ice-bath-questions.2818/post-42530>

So I've stockpiled a decent number of Fiji 1 liter bottles to use for an Ice bath and I have a few questions. How much alcohol do I need to use for lower freezing temp...? Rubbing alcohol will work right? Also do I put the frozen bottles in and let them melt and take them out or just put them in and get in will it be colder letting them melt or is it fine just putting them in the water.

Also how much room do I need to leave for expansion.

Thanks

### kathylu — 2012-08-03T18:26:32+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/stockpiling-fiji-1-liters-for-ice-bath-questions.2818/post-42690>

Leave 1.5 inches at the top for expansion. I just use water and it works well. Put them in the bath and wait for them to almost melt before getting in. The bath will be colder that way.

### Cowboy — 2012-08-03T20:34:41+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/stockpiling-fiji-1-liters-for-ice-bath-questions.2818/post-42730>

I hope someone with more physical science knowledge can explain the science, behind this, but I am going to take a shot at the practical aspect. If you use pure water it will freeze at 32 degrees. If you take this frozen water and keep it in a -10 degree freezer, the water will now be frozen, but it will also be -10 degrees.

If you add alcohol to the water, it will now freeze at a lower temperature depending upon how much alcohol you use, lets say 25% will now lower the freezing point of water to 25 degrees. If you take that frozen mixture of water and alchohol and put it in a -10 degree freezer you will now have frozen bottles of water and alchohol that are -10 degrees.

Now if you want to get below 32 degrees and maintain a liquid consistancy, mix water and alcohol. That will work, but to get a tub of water cold just use straight water.

Now if I am wrong I hope someone can expalin why and how.

### Huck — 2012-08-03T20:38:07+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/stockpiling-fiji-1-liters-for-ice-bath-questions.2818/post-42734>

I use just water because I didn't want to take a chance on the bottles leaking onto food in the freezer.

### Raiken3712 — 2012-08-03T20:41:35+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/stockpiling-fiji-1-liters-for-ice-bath-questions.2818/post-42735>

You are basically saying alcohol is pointless as the temperature of the water is the same regardless of alcohol or not....? Am I reading that right....it actually makes some sense the alcohol only lets the water/alc mixture stay liquid longer before freezing it doesn't actually get any colder?

If that's true though why are people using alcohol and water....missing something here or are people doing this mistakenly? Its just a waste of money if that's right lol.

How many 1 liter bottles does it take you to lower temp by 10 degrees?

### kathylu — 2012-08-03T21:54:33+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/stockpiling-fiji-1-liters-for-ice-bath-questions.2818/post-42748>

> **Raiken3712;43014 wrote:** You are basically saying alcohol is pointless as the temperature of the water is the same regardless of alcohol or not....? Am I reading that right....it actually makes some sense the alcohol only lets the water/alc mixture stay liquid longer before freezing it doesn't actually get any colder?
>
>
>
> If that's true though why are people using alcohol and water....missing something here or are people doing this mistakenly? Its just a waste of money if that's right lol.
>
>
>
> How many 1 liter bottles does it take you to lower temp by 10 degrees?

It takes me about 14 one liter bottles, but I have a large soaking tub.

### Cowboy — 2012-08-04T00:22:27+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/stockpiling-fiji-1-liters-for-ice-bath-questions.2818/post-42770>

alcohol is pointless if you want to freeze the bottles. Alcohol only makes a difference in the temperature at which the water/alcohol mix changes phase from liquid to solid. Once the mix is solid it can get colder and colder depending upon your freezer, at least to absolute zero (approx -480 if I remember right)

### Raiken3712 — 2012-08-04T08:02:32+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/stockpiling-fiji-1-liters-for-ice-bath-questions.2818/post-42816>

That is what I was thinking based on what you said...was just confused because I remember reading about people using alcohol for this purpose....I guess others didn't think about it being useless just like I didn't.

### pthomaslandry@gmail.com — 2012-08-04T14:45:25+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/stockpiling-fiji-1-liters-for-ice-bath-questions.2818/post-42824>

The heat capacities of ethanol and water are approximately the same at -10. However, the heat capacity of water almost doubles at room temperature. the heat capacity of water is pretty high, anything you add with a smaller heat capacity (salt, alcohol, a mackerel head) will decrease the overall heat capacity.

A good way to think of it is that it water can hold more energy at room temperature than alcohol. That is, it takes a larger quantity of energy to change the temperature of water than alcohol.

For cooling a substance the biggest heat capacity wins. You steal the energy from the tub water to heat up the ice bottles. The more energy the frozen bottles take away, the colder the water gets. So yes, wait till they melt.

Anyway.... Just water is better, and enough.

### terryfischer39@yahoo.com — 2012-08-04T19:20:27+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/stockpiling-fiji-1-liters-for-ice-bath-questions.2818/post-42866>

The reason I use alcohol and water is to keep the ice packs I use on my torso from getting hard. They then conform to my body and seem to hold the cold longer. I am glad to read this as I was wondering if I should add alcohol to frozen ice bottles to keep them cold longer. I bought a case of 8 oz water and will freeze them in my chest freezer to use in the water. It will be a while before I can use a bag of ice in the tub anyway. Thanks.

### shilohman — 2012-08-04T20:01:14+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/stockpiling-fiji-1-liters-for-ice-bath-questions.2818/post-42876>

The Ozarka 3 liter bottles work great. You fill them 80% full of water.

# Thread: Best reheating methods
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/best-reheating-methods.2820/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 4
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Raiken3712 — 2012-08-02T22:10:18+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/best-reheating-methods.2820/post-42554>

So I've read a bit on how bad microwaves are....still not sure what to think...from the extreme ...all nutrition is zapped...highly doubt that to the less extreme you lose some nutrition and its altered.

Basically making it more like a processed food...not as healthy...anyone based on my reading...I'm trying to reheat using other methods but I haven't figured out whats best.

Broiling on high is fast...but if I'm not careful it burns the food which isn't good. I think with a set short amount of time on each side of whatever food I'm cooking broiling is a good option...if I don't go off and forget it or something. I have done this several times with good effect but haven't worked out good timing yet.

Baking it would take a longer time...flipping it required most likely for getting good heat throughout.

Any recommendations?

Thanks

### Michael — 2012-08-02T22:27:29+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/best-reheating-methods.2820/post-42557>

> **Raiken3712;42821 wrote:** So I've read a bit on how bad microwaves are....still not sure what to think...from the extreme ...all nutrition is zapped...highly doubt that to the less extreme you lose some nutrition and its altered.
>
>
> I understand they can be quite destructive of vitamin content. Besides they tend to dry food out. And if you don't maintain them, so that they get leaky, you stand to get a dose of microwaves if you stand near while they're running. All in all, they don't seem to have a lot going for them other than "labor-saving". Some chef once, trying to find something for which they could possibly be useful invented a pudding that was hot inside and cold outside -- like a Baked Alaska in reverse. I guess that might be interesting for the novelty value ... if you ate puddings, which Paleo folks tend not to.
>
>
>
> Basically making it more like a processed food...not as healthy...anyone based on my reading...I'm trying to reheat using other methods but I haven't figured out whats best.

Go gently. Too much heat will damage heat-labile nutrients, degrade proteins, and so on. So put the food in a saucepan over a heat that's not too extreme and stir it regularly with a wooden spoon, so that the heat comes up gradually and the food is warmed through evenly.

I'm sometimes surprised at how much heat people throw at food. My niece said to me that she couldn't eat eggs at work, because of the smell. I said that they didn't smell if you did them properly. We _say_ "boiled egg" but actually you should simmer them. I said to her to start them in cold water, to turn them down immediately they boil, to hold them at a simmer for about 6 minutes, and then to plunge them in cold water in the sink to stop the cooking. She said she'd try.

### Patty Cakes — 2012-08-03T04:18:14+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/best-reheating-methods.2820/post-42622>

I will sometimes steam certain items on a low heat just to get them to warm up. I also use a toaster over for meat at 200 degrees again just to warm up. For me it depends on the food I am working with. Growing up w/o a microwave or toaster over we learned many ways to heat things up or we also just ate things cold!

### Inger — 2012-08-03T10:00:39+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/best-reheating-methods.2820/post-42643>

I never re-heat foods. Anything re-heated are very non-optimal to me and makes me feel not good. Clear case to me, avoid it!

I try to make everything fresh, only the amount I will eat, or I eat it plain raw. Way better for your health. At work I take with me something easy to eat, like minced elk-meat, I eat it plain and raw and cold or with a splash of balsamic vinegar. No need to keep it cool, it survives perfectly well a day in your bag in room temperatur, I never got issues from this (I know, CW tells you otherwise..) Or I take shrimps, I eat them whole with heads and all and as they are, cold. They taste great. No need to heat anything.


══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# Thread: I have a blocked ear
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/i-have-a-blocked-ear.2821/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 8
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Mery — 2012-08-02T23:08:39+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/i-have-a-blocked-ear.2821/post-42560>

My left ear has been blocked for nearly a week I went to see the Dr and he said I was producing too much wax and he mention to put Otex to dissolve the wax, however after 3 days of putting Otex in my ear, it is still blocked and I can not hear with that side.

Any suggestion on how to get my hearing back, thanks

### PrimalPam — 2012-08-03T00:10:43+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/i-have-a-blocked-ear.2821/post-42576>

It may take up to a week. I'd schedule another appointment if, after about 7 days, it is still not clear for you.

### Joann — 2012-08-03T01:02:20+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/i-have-a-blocked-ear.2821/post-42577>

I have heard Ginko Biloba helps with hearing issues

### jeanie@truelongmont.com — 2012-08-03T02:33:58+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/i-have-a-blocked-ear.2821/post-42595>

I had very good luck for me and someone else by making drops with olive oil (the good stuff) and crushed garlic. Steep the garlic in warmed oil for an hour or so - then drip the oil (not garlic) into the ear canal and let it get all over.

### KiwiLauren — 2012-08-03T04:21:26+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/i-have-a-blocked-ear.2821/post-42623>

If you have significant wax in your ear, you'll need an ear professional to remove it (my husband used to have this problem all the time - at home treatments don't work sufficiently). It's simple and easy for them to do.

### Mery — 2012-08-04T16:46:07+0300

# Thread: VLC *stomps* on hunger
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/vlc-stomps-on-hunger.2831/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 2
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Michael — 2012-08-03T15:23:20+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/vlc-stomps-on-hunger.2831/post-42656>

It still surprises me.

I've eaten low carb for quite a while, but not often very low carb. I have been experimenting with it recently again, though.

Today I had a high fat, moderate protein, low carb breakfast at around 07:00. At about 11:30 I ate about 100g/3.5 oz. of beef jerky -- wasn't hungry but it was there and it seemed convenient to start lunch early, as I had other things to do in lunch hour. So come 12:30 I got a bowl of salad with olive oil and vinegar, as the rest of my lunch, and I just did not want it. I did eat it anyway.

It is amazing how hunger _just goes away_. The mainstream has simply no idea. "A calorie is a calorie" ... pffft. If I'd added a couple of slices of toast to that breakfast, and had some dried mango with the dried meat, different story.

### Melinda — 2012-08-03T16:07:13+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/vlc-stomps-on-hunger.2831/post-42661>

I totally agree with you Michael. I love having hours and hours of not thinking about food.

### Shijin13 — 2012-08-03T17:46:30+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/pull-ups-success.2833/post-42683>

Whoot! so I was getting diapers going in the wash, and while I was waiting for the previous cycle to finish I hopped up on the pull up bar and banged out 2 pull ups.

I couldn't do a single pull up last year at this time. Couldn't do one back in January. Took some time off from pull ups... and just focused on body weight work, CT, and eating clean...

Suddenly I can do two successive pull ups.

after I got the diapers loaded I jumped up and did two more - this is no fluke!

success!

### Souldanzer — 2012-08-03T18:12:36+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/pull-ups-success.2833/post-42689>

argh, now I want a pull-up bar  congrats

### ealachan — 2012-08-03T18:26:55+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/pull-ups-success.2833/post-42691>

> **Shijin13;42966 wrote:** I haven't been able to bang out pull ups in any shape or form un-assisted since basic training over 20yrs ago! I'm so stoked... I don't care that some of my pants don't fit right now... I can do pull ups!

I've NEVER been able to do a pull-up. You're my idol. Seriously.

### bigknitwit — 2012-08-03T18:29:25+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/pull-ups-success.2833/post-42693>

Awesome! You cant' "fake" pullups!

### Shijin13 — 2012-08-03T18:38:59+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/pull-ups-success.2833/post-42697>

Al Kravado has a great site about using your body to get in shape - here's his blog on how to do a pull up. I've employed his techniques over the past 2yrs to get here....

[http://www.alkavadlo.com/2010/09/learning-to-do-a-pull-up/](http://http://www.alkavadlo.com/2010/09/learning-to-do-a-pull-up/)

### tercay@yahoo.com — 2012-08-03T20:34:44+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/pull-ups-success.2833/post-42731>

Wonderful!!!!!! Congratulations!

### AKMan — 2012-08-06T23:36:27+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/pull-ups-success.2833/post-43285>

Al K got me into pullups, too. I went from zero to being able to do 30 (good form, in a row). I did a lot of what Al calls 'greasing the groove'. Basically, do a couple pullups several times throughout the day, every day. Eventually you will find you can do enough to turn it into a meaningful workout. Nowadays, I do superslow pullups, like 4 or 5 until I can not physically do another, one day a week--then none until the next week. An important concept with pullups is "Time Under Load", way more important than number of reps. Focus on doing every pullup as perfectly as possible with as much range as possible.

It's a great feeling when you can finally do a couple! Keep up the good work!

# Thread: Water Retention
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/water-retention.2835/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 7
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### bigknitwit — 2012-08-03T18:38:07+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/water-retention.2835/post-42696>

Can we discuss this a bit? What makes some foods suddenly cause a ton of water retention. I get it that a sudden amount of carbs will do this due to glycogen storage. But what about other foods, like, for example _bacon_? Why can't I eat bacon (or ground pork, or ground chicken, or ham), without serious water retention? I'm assuming it's water, because I don't think one can store 3 pounds of fat overnight. Inflammation from leaky gut? Why do certain foods that should be "epi-paleo friendly" cause this?

### Shijin13 — 2012-08-03T18:40:35+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/water-retention.2835/post-42698>

bacon - is it salted? some times over salted food will do this to me... IDK could be something else...

### bigknitwit — 2012-08-03T18:49:48+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/water-retention.2835/post-42700>

It's not a salt problem - I eat a ton of salt... I think it has something to do with the food being more exposed to air, but I really don't understand why.

### zoebarry@yahoo.com — 2012-08-03T20:19:25+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/water-retention.2835/post-42729>

> **bigknitwit;42975 wrote:** Can we discuss this a bit? What makes some foods suddenly cause a ton of water retention. I get it that a sudden amount of carbs will do this due to glycogen storage. But what about other foods, like, for example _bacon_? Why can't I eat bacon (or ground pork, or ground chicken, or ham), without serious water retention? I'm assuming it's water, because I don't think one can store 3 pounds of fat overnight. Inflammation from leaky gut? Why do certain foods that should be "epi-paleo friendly" cause this?

If you have an intolerance to some food, it will cause some water retention until it moves out of your system. That has been my experience anyway. For example, an allergy test revealed that I am intolerant to eggs. I can bet money on the scale being up the next day. Same with yogurt (test said I was intolerant to casein) and a few other foods.

### ealachan — 2012-08-03T20:35:56+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/water-retention.2835/post-42732>

> **bigknitwit;42975 wrote:** Can we discuss this a bit? What makes some foods suddenly cause a ton of water retention. I get it that a sudden amount of carbs will do this due to glycogen storage. But what about other foods, like, for example _bacon_? Why can't I eat bacon (or ground pork, or ground chicken, or ham), without serious water retention? I'm assuming it's water, because I don't think one can store 3 pounds of fat overnight. Inflammation from leaky gut? Why do certain foods that should be "epi-paleo friendly" cause this?

You listed three pork products and a chicken product. Aren't pork and chicken both higher in Omega-6's than beef or fish? Maybe it's something to do with that?

I have no clue...just spit-ballin'...

### Shijin13 — 2012-08-03T23:35:03+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/water-retention.2835/post-42763>

> **ealachan;43011 wrote:** You listed three pork products and a chicken product. Aren't pork and chicken both higher in Omega-6's than beef or fish? Maybe it's something to do with that?
>
>
>
> I have no clue...just spit-ballin'...

This could be a big thing..

I had grassfed/finished pork last night for dinner - got all bloated etc - felt miserable... had it for lunch the same thing - must have been how DH made it... I'm guessing it was the yougurt based seasoning he made from scratch.

### bigknitwit — 2012-08-04T01:12:28+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/water-retention.2835/post-42778>

I've been wondering about this too. I can't touch any seeds/nuts either without the same problem. The thing is, if I were to eat pork today, and then almonds tomorrow, I would see 1-3 pounds the first morning, and then 1-3 pounds the second morning. It accumulates. I accumulated 15 pounds in 3 weeks before I stopped all food except turkey and added things in slowly. In the past, I could stop the accumulation by simply going low carb, but not anymore.

But there's got to be something more. Most people are walking around with horrendous Omega 3 status, and they're not ballooning...

> **Shijin13;43042 wrote:** This could be a big thing..
>
>
>
> I had grassfed/finished pork last night for dinner - got all bloated etc - felt miserable... had it for lunch the same thing - must have been how DH made it... I'm guessing it was the yougurt based seasoning he made from scratch.

# Thread: HELP--I'm not sure what to do...need wisdom!
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/help-im-not-sure-what-to-do-need-wisdom.2837/>
**Subforum:** HCG Protocol
**Posts:** 5
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### perdita — 2012-08-03T19:56:36+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/help-im-not-sure-what-to-do-need-wisdom.2837/post-42721>

Hi everyone, I am in need of wisdom on what to do.

I did Round 1 in Dec 2010/Jan 2011 and lost 18 lbs (from 142 to 124-ish). I'm petite, so this was a size 4. I was very happy!

Maintained for a while, then my Dad was diagnosed with cancer...I helped take care of him, and due to the stress, exhaustion, lack of time, I ate poorly and gained 8 lbs back.

Did Round 2 in Oct/Nov 2011 and lost the weight I gained. For phase 3 of that round, I did Leptin Reset and lost 6 more lbs. (See my old signature.) I was at my high school weight and very thrilled.

Then Dad died, we had another family crisis, and we moved twice. All in less than a year. I gained a lot of weight back.

It's now Aug 2012. I'm not even sure how much I weigh because I'm afraid to step on the scale, but I know I've gained at least 2-3 sizes. I have rolls of fat around my middle and can't zip up my jeans easily. I'm so sad and frustrated when I look in the mirror, because I know how good I looked less than a year ago.

I don't know whether I should do a Round 3 or just do the Leptin Reset or do both like Round 2. I have one vial of hcg in my fridge, but I'd need to order more. I've heard that it's harder to get hcg now due to all the fda press.

If I do Round 3, will it be just as effective as Round 1 and 2? Or should I go another direction? How do I stop this vicious cycle of losing and gaining?

### jeanie@truelongmont.com — 2012-08-03T20:01:43+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/help-im-not-sure-what-to-do-need-wisdom.2837/post-42723>

Don't do another round. You need to get healthy and re-nutrient from all the stress and think about body comp and health vs weight. You don't sound that overfat, do some testing find out what you need to optimize your health. Get that in line, THEN if you've maintained health for 6 months and evaluate then what needs to be done. You must get healthy to think clearly.

### perdita — 2012-08-06T06:30:25+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/help-im-not-sure-what-to-do-need-wisdom.2837/post-43163>

Thank you, Cavemam. I didn't really think about being depleted in nutrients due to stress, but you've got a real valid point.

I have limited funds to do medical testing. If you had to choose ONE test to start with, what would you recommend?

### MartiD — 2012-08-06T15:30:13+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/help-im-not-sure-what-to-do-need-wisdom.2837/post-43186>

I agree that another round isn't going to fix what's broken. You need to figure out why in a stressful setting you lose control of your eating habits. A quick fix isn't going to cure that issue...the cycle will continue. Try the reset, get tested, learn what works for you and move forward. Good luck and keep us posted.

### Fredricked — 2012-09-29T08:49:11+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/help-im-not-sure-what-to-do-need-wisdom.2837/post-55172>

THEN if you've maintained health for 6 months and evaluate then what needs to be done. You must get healthy to think clearly.

\-----------------------------

TVs making the ideal into reality --- [One Tree Hill Season 9 DVD](http://http://www.wholesaledvdsales.co.uk/One-Tree-Hill-Season-9-DVD-Boxset-FREE-SHIPPIING-1759.html), What We Need Is Persist.

### nonchalant — 2012-08-03T21:35:29+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sea-salts.2839/post-42744>

I finally found some Fleur De Sel salt today. It is so good! I think it's the texture that sets it apart. Instead of hard grains, it is an aggregation of tiny bits of salt. Like the difference between an ice cube and shaved ice (analogy for us CT types.)

And the flavor is lovely as well.

I'll have to do a taste test, beside my himalayan, celtic, Redmond, and various other sea salts I have at home.

### Patty Cakes — 2012-08-04T01:20:38+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sea-salts.2839/post-42781>

I believe Fleur de Sel is supposed to be the best salt around.....for taste. It's a favorite among chefs. I have some to and love it but use my Redmond coarse grind for everyday use.

### SeaHorse — 2012-08-04T01:40:12+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sea-salts.2839/post-42784>

There is a lovely soft crystal salt from Maldon in Essex England...it's my new favourite...very flakey and easy to crunch with your fingers.

### Patty Cakes — 2012-08-04T02:07:59+0300

# Thread: Celiac labs
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/celiac-labs.2840/>
**Subforum:** Optimal Labs
**Posts:** 8
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### trishalinn@gmail.com — 2012-08-03T21:45:25+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/celiac-labs.2840/post-42746>

So... My doctor ordered a bunch of labs including the blood tets for celiac disease. I have been mostly gluten free since January and religiously gluten free the past 5 week. I was, however, reading that for accurate labs you need to be eating gluten. Does anyone know or how long, or how much? I want to get the labs done soon, but want them to be accurate.

### ATL_Paleo — 2012-08-03T22:44:29+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/celiac-labs.2840/post-42757>

According to the Mayo Clinic, "No treatment can cure celiac disease. However, you can effectively manage celiac disease by changing your diet." ... so why not skip the celiac test, and just eat a clean Epi-paleo diet?

[http://www.mayoclinic.com/health/celiac-disease/DS00319](http://http://www.mayoclinic.com/health/celiac-disease/DS00319)

### colleencoble — 2012-08-03T23:57:46+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/celiac-labs.2840/post-42766>

That blood test is notoriously poor at telling you if you have it. A better test is a stool test from enterolab.

### nonchalant — 2012-08-03T23:59:28+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/celiac-labs.2840/post-42768>

Good question, Trisha. I'm curious as well about the answer to your question.

ATL\_Paleo, the results from a test will give weight to her avoidance of gluten, and it can also have great importance to other family members.

### Destiny — 2012-08-04T01:11:49+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/celiac-labs.2840/post-42777>

> **ATL\_Paleo;43036 wrote:** According to the Mayo Clinic, "No treatment can cure celiac disease. However, you can effectively manage celiac disease by changing your diet." ... so why not skip the celiac test, and just eat a clean Epi-paleo diet?
>
> [http://www.mayoclinic.com/health/celiac-disease/DS00319](http://http://www.mayoclinic.com/health/celiac-disease/DS00319)

That is what I thought as well. We know that gluten is not good for us so a positive or negative test cannot change that.

### Patty Cakes — 2012-08-04T01:16:12+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/celiac-labs.2840/post-42780>

I agree with Colleen. There are many false negatives with blood. I was told by a doc that I need to eat gluten for 4-5 days a week for two weeks before taking a test. With that said, I had the GI fx test (stool) recently for gut health and it showed antibodies for gluten and I have been seriously gluten free for well over a year. So somehow it has gotten in me anyway! I recommend a stool test!

### trishalinn@gmail.com — 2012-08-04T01:45:00+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/celiac-labs.2840/post-42785>

> **Nonchalant;43047 wrote:** Good question, Trisha. I'm curious as well about the answer to your question.
>
> ATL\_Paleo, the results from a test will give weight to her avoidance of gluten, and it can also have great importance to other family members.

Thanks, these are some of the reasons exactly! Even if it comes back false i plan on continuung to be gluten free anyhow.

This is a test that dr kruse recommended i get! If it comes back negative he suggested the cyrex test.

If anyone has any input on the original question, i would appreciate the answers.

### bigknitwit — 2012-08-04T01:50:33+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/celiac-labs.2840/post-42786>

4 years ago, I had to put my son back on gluten for 6 weeks prior to getting an endoscopy. He needed to eat a slice or two of bread each day. But that's for the endoscopy. Also, I was told that in the past, doctors would repeat the endoscopy on individuals found to be celiac 2 months later, to verify diet compliance. So in theory, it takes less than 2 months gluten free for the villi to regrow. It would also depend upon how much gluten you were eating prior to 5 weeks ago...
