# Thread: One week of trying and failing to do Leptin Rx
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/one-week-of-trying-and-failing-to-do-leptin-rx.2904/>
**Subforum:** The Leptin Rx
**Posts:** 8
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### rottzilla — 2012-08-07T17:24:59+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/one-week-of-trying-and-failing-to-do-leptin-rx.2904/post-43428>

Hi everyone! Iâ€™m new. I posted over on hcg forum. I am 40, 35-40 lbs overweight (lost 50 and kept it off for more than 2 years through hcg dieting and some Rosedale dieting.) Iâ€™m also low iron and trying to get pregnant. My doc has me taking iron supplements and I get retested in 2 months.

So, there is a lot going on for me. On the one hand, as a WAP member and paleo believer, I am excited by Dr. Kruseâ€™s protocol. On the other, itâ€™s very important I have certain things I need to do and eat for my iron levels and also for my pregnancy chances and to get my body in tip top shape for a baby. First and foremost, as people on here helped me realize, is getting myself baby ready. Losing weight is second, but itâ€™s very important to me as I have a bad back and sciatic issue that really kicked me in the pants recently. I really need to lose the weight and stay in shape for my health. Iâ€™m only 40 but some days I feel 70.

One thing I really feel I need to eat daily is raw milk. Harvard studies have shown fertility increases by 80% in women that consume 2 servings of dairy daily. Since Iâ€™m tying to do ketogenic paleo FTW (ha ha as people on here have said â€“ love that! But milk is high carbâ€¦ so Iâ€™m using my raw pastured milk and turning it into kefir. According to Domâ€™s site, kefir only has 11 g of carb per cup, so that seems OK. However, it changes the nutritional profile of the fatâ€¦ so Iâ€™m skimming the fat, fermenting it, and then adding the cream back when I drink it. So, each day I drink 1.5 cups of kefir and .5cups of cream with my BAB. I also eat 2 eggs, chicken sausage, fermented beets and red cabbage (for the iron and Vit C). Iâ€™m getting my 50 g of protein and about 26 g of carb (20 net) and 683 calories. For lunch, Iâ€™ll have some red meat and green juice (one cuke, half zucchini, lettuce, 2 celery, and cilantro) and half an avocado. Now Iâ€™m up to 45 g carb (27 net) and 91 g protein, 1100 calories. For dinner, I am just not hungry at all. I may force myself to eat, but sometimes I just go to bed. When I eat, Iâ€™ll have another bit of steak or something, maybe coconut oil or a few nuts.

Help me out here people, Iâ€™m not losing an ounce. What do I need to do? How about the CT? I am preparing by taking cold baths, but only from the tap, no ice yet, or cold showers. I do have to exercise for my recovery but most days I just walk. Iâ€™ve built up over 2 months to 45 min walking now, at a slow pace. Iâ€™ll be doing some yoga and stretching also soon.

Itâ€™s hard because itâ€™s physical therapy recovery from sciatica, preparing for baby, and losing weight. Theyâ€™re not in direct opposition to each other, but they are somewhat (exercise â€“ JK says no â€“ eating milk high carb â€“ and low carb ketogenic)

Maybe I should move my milk to later in the day before I exercise to burn off the carbs? Thoughts? JK says not to exercise first thing in the am, but also says to eat most of your carbs with breakfast.

### jeanie@truelongmont.com — 2012-08-07T17:42:10+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/one-week-of-trying-and-failing-to-do-leptin-rx.2904/post-43430>

One thing to think about. . ..

The Harvard Study you are referencing - did you go look at the how the study was designed and what the subjects were eating compared to the control group? Question everything!

The increase in fertility was for SAD eaters that added in the prescribed amount of CAFO dairy. I'm way more pro-dairy (raw, right cows, local and humane conditions etc) than most on here. But by no means do I think it necessary. Also milk itself can be an issue due to carbs and other things for someone inflamed (stubborn fat is inflamed somewhere) . . .you can get lots of the good milky vits and benefits with the raw Heavy Whipping Cream and the butter (fav on here is kerrygold). As far as getting ready for baby, I think the appropriate dairy fats to provide the k2 and A and all that isn't a terrible way to go.

Making Kefir with the raw milk, can also be a great fermented/good bacteria option. But, if you are sensitive enough to the the dairy proteins and they inflame you, then it's a no-no to you.

First off, stop counting calories. Provide your body with nutrients and you body will adjust to the calories. . .you can't possibly adjust your calories to your body on any given day. There is simply no way to judge that! It's kind of magical that way.

Oh, yes, CT CT CT! It's nice that we are heading to the end of summer - gives you an opportunity to work in slowly then hit it as the weather changes!

### rottzilla — 2012-08-07T17:56:49+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/one-week-of-trying-and-failing-to-do-leptin-rx.2904/post-43432>

Thanks for the thoughtful reply… in the Harvard study, the women who consumed full fat dairy had 80% increase in fertility as opposed to those who did not consume dairy, and oddly enough, (I’m forgetting exact number) but something like a 70% decrease in fertility (due to ovulatory disorder) for women consuming reduced fat dairy. It came out of the nurse’s health study.

I hadn’t thought of the inflammation component, which is odd because I’m a big proponent of inflammation being a marker of all disease. I do eat a fermented CLO and about to add liver for the Vit A and D. Raw cream is my preference but I can’t get it out here. Mass doesn’t allow farmers to sell their raw cream unless it’s to a producer. My farmer used to have us sign contracts saying we were not the end users and their cream was being put to manufacture in something else, but now they are just tired of the games and won’t sell cream at all. I’ve found pastured cream, but it’s not raw.

OK, I’ll move faster on the CT and also stop worrying about calories. I’ll really have to start to pay attention to how the dairy is affecting me and maybe stop it for a week or two and see my results and then compare to when I do drink it.

**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/one-week-of-trying-and-failing-to-do-leptin-rx.2904/post-43437>

Also, just for the record - I commiserate on how annoying and frustrating it is to "do all this" and really put the effort into a lifestyle that is supposed to "fix" things, and still be stuck with the weight! Sounds like you should focus on training and therapy for your back pain issue. . . as you don't want to be in pain the entire time - that can't be good for either of you (think inflamation!) and it sounds like you are way above the crowd on eating, too - I bet this works out

I was the last person in line as a candidate for parenthood, so before getting preggers I did nothing for fertility or preparing body to make a good babe. . but since then have learned alot trying to have a 2nd (that one didn't happen, irony - thanks nature) One thing you never see in all the fertility stuff is the timing of your orgasm while sperms are all up in there! Summary: He should go first, and you right after! Internal contractions of the lady parts pull the swimmers up.

Hi Rottzilla,

it's great to read your post! I am in your same boat (I am AMA and also trying to get pregnant, well, get ready for it as my hormones are all screwed up and I am in AF, low thyroid etc). I don't actually do dairy because I also have to recover from leaky gut except butter (Kerrygold) though I hope to introduce some raw goat cheese (to test).....so that is great you can do dairy. I love raw cream!

Do you know about this Amish Farmer in PA? They will ship to you, if you become a member, for I think $25 a year....it's not cheap for overnight shipping but they have raw cream....in fact they have a ton of stuff and it's all grass fed. They are wonderful people. I saw they have a co-op in Concord Mass, not sure how far that is for you. You might want to check it out.  I highly recommend their stuff and have used them on and off for a few years, especially for soy-free, pastured eggs which are so hard to find....

Here is the link: [http://millersorganicfarm.com/millers-co-ops-in-us/](http://http://millersorganicfarm.com/millers-co-ops-in-us/)

Good luck. And by the way I would love to connect with you offline....please keep posting here with your experience.

### Shijin13 — 2012-09-02T18:41:40+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/one-week-of-trying-and-failing-to-do-leptin-rx.2904/post-48973>

Ditch the dairy for one month see what happens. Yes you'll probably crave it but it's possible you gut flora is so crazy that is driving your need. Also dairy acts just like an opiate and attached to the same receptors. We don't need dairy. You'll find as you increase your seafood consumption you won't want the dairy.

# Thread: Great Meatball Trick!!
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/great-meatball-trick.2907/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 26
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### jeanie@truelongmont.com — 2012-08-07T18:41:02+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/great-meatball-trick.2907/post-43440>

I made the best meatloaf/meat ball stuff last night. Had a few tricks up my sleeve - got the idea from this months cooks illustrated.

2 lbds of meat (I used ground pork and ground bison)

1 onion finely chopped

Boatload of garlic finely chopped (I never feel there is enough, do as much as you can stand)

3 tbs coconut flakes

about 4-6 anchovies chopped fine and rinsed of olive oil.

1/2 cup bonebroth

1 packet of unflavored gelatin

seasonings to your liking. (I did italian as we were having spaghetti squash)

Saute onion on lower temps till starts to brown. I used red palm oil because I had some. This step takes longer than one thinks it should.

Add garlic and anchovies once onion starts to turn brown, keep sauting until garlic is cooked

Warm broth, dissolve gelatin in it.

In a bowl mix: meat, coconut, sauted veggies, broth/gelatin and season to taste!

I baked at 350 in little meatballs and a few meatloafts. AWESOME!!!! These are so juicy and moist and savory tasting!

### ealachan — 2012-08-08T19:09:12+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/great-meatball-trick.2907/post-43686>

> **Souldanzer;43974 wrote: I asked that question and Dr K said bone broth doesn't have glutamate...... I have been reacting to bone broth since my brain injury from my skiing accident. Can't have it. Bone broth = un-imaginable headache, brain fog and day spent horizontal with ice packs in bed.

**Bone broth not having glutamates does not jive, though. Glutamic acid is a building block of protein, so it occurs in anything that contains protein.

I'm not arguing that MSG is bad \*\*\*\*, btw...I'm just trying to understand how we're connecting the dots between gelatin and MSG. It seems to me that, if one is not over-sensitive to glutamates, that the naturally** occurring glutamates in bone broth and the resultant gelatins would not be a bad thing, but rather quite beneficial.

### Souldanzer — 2012-08-08T19:12:25+0300

any meat you eat has glutamate.... I think it's a concentration issue. Bone broth is good, I believe that. The long cooking process probably concentrates the glutamate contained and therefore, someone like me with lots of brain issues and sensitivities, might react.

Gelatin is commercially processed at probably high temps or something like that..... WAPF has the answer why it should be avoided but my brain is leaving me here

### jeanie@truelongmont.com — 2012-08-08T19:44:38+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/great-meatball-trick.2907/post-43690>

on another tangent, the reason for adding the gelatin in the first place. . .

The best meatloafs are usually made with a panade, which is a milk/bread paste that helps create a lighter juicier loaf. With NO panade what happens is liquids are not trapped, and the ground meat proteins binds tightly, squeezing out the moisture. This is made worse the more you "work" the meat and roll it and squeeze it.

They used mushrooms and gelatin (I used coconut flakes as an experiment cause I didn't have mushrooms on hand). THe sponge like mushrooms and gelatin trap moisture and lubricate the fibers - supposedly distributing flavors and juicyness.

My N-1 was that the meat balls made this way - with gelatin, were WAAAAY moister, fluffier and tastier than without fwiw. I did mix mine with actual bone broth too.

I am going to "reduce" some bone broth to create that natural gelatine like goop and use that with the finely chopped mushrooms on the next batch.

### jeanie@truelongmont.com — 2012-08-08T19:49:21+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/great-meatball-trick.2907/post-43691>

Has anybody made lots of fish stock? Do you get the same jello-goo from that if you cook it down?

### Glamazon — 2012-08-09T16:35:41+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/great-meatball-trick.2907/post-43892>

> **ealachan;43958 wrote:** But home-made bone broth often has gelatin in it from the bones being cooked so long. In fact, it's considered a desirable trait in bone broth, is it not?

My bone broth is really stock. I don't care for the taste of plain bones. I take something like oxtails, cover in a liquid in a crockpot with a splash of apple cider vinegar and cook for 8-12 hours. The tendons and cartilage break down into yummy jelly. I cook it so long I end up throwing away the meat because it tastes like cardboard. When it cools you can cut it like custard. I add spoonfuls of it to when I am cooking or I make "beef tea" for sipping.

If I eat a cup a day of it, like I do for long stretches in the winter, my body temp gets very warm and I get really horny. This is why I don't eat it in the spring because it raises my body temp till I sweat for hours. I use non hormone meat and mostly grassfed all the time from WFs or I order it. In Chinese medicine this kind of broth is meant to build jing chi. Jing is body energy that is most likely related to what westerners call hormones. It is given to the sick and the elderly for rejuvenation. Sometimes a knob of ginger is added because Ginger is considered an anti aging tonic. In China and here in the US, shaving fresh ginger into boiling water, let cook for about 5 minutes, then strain and drink it as considered a daily longevity tonic. A lot of the chinese elderly drink it.

This bone stock has a lot of Methylsulfonylmethane (MSM) and natural cartilage in it.

I have no bad reaction to this stuff at all.

### jeanie@truelongmont.com — 2012-08-11T17:46:52+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/great-meatball-trick.2907/post-44333>

> **Anna K.;44637 wrote:** It would be great to know, because according to great lakes gelatin's link, if I understand it correctly, normally processed gelatin has no MSG. It doesn't look like they are using any special methods to be msg free.
>
>
>
> so I also don't see how it's different from bone broth.

I looked through WAPF pretty hard and didn't see anything that would conflict with what the Great Lakes site has. I am wondering still on bone broth vs gelatin.

### janagram — 2012-08-11T18:29:59+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/great-meatball-trick.2907/post-44345>

Glam, what did you mean when you said you make stock because you don't care for bone broth? (always wondered what the difference was)

### Glamazon — 2012-08-13T04:00:13+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/great-meatball-trick.2907/post-44617>

> **janagram;44653 wrote:** Glam, what did you mean when you said you make stock because you don't care for bone broth? (always wondered what the difference was)

It depends on the region and culture. Some use the term interchangeably. I once ordered bone broth from grass fed meat supplier. It tasted like bones. Basically, the bones are just used. In stock, there is more than bones used, like carcasses and veggies. Broth is usually strained and clear. It can gel up but I find the way I make stock is much more a richer and thicker result. The gel cuts like custard. I have yet to make fish stock so I cant speak to that. Boiling pig parts like hocks makes a rich stock very quick, faster than a beef or chicken stock. I can put some hocks on and simmer for three hours and have something that takes 8-12 hours for beef. I cook the beef for so long everything dissolves if I include veggies, especially onions. Restaurant quality stock or broth is clear. I eat it without straining it.

An interesting side note, chitin, which comes from shrimp shells (or any crustacean) is used in surgery and procedures to close up wounds because it is absorb-able and tough and resilient. It can also help accelerate wound healing when applied.

### AKMan — 2012-08-08T02:10:32+0300

This guy's enthusiasm (and message) reminds me so much of Jack Kruse.

[http://www.youtube.com/watch?feature=player\_embedded&v=LJVEPB\_l8FU\[/video\]\]\[video=youtube\_share;LJVEPB\_l8FU\]http://www.youtube.com/watch?feature=player\_embedded&v=LJVEPB\_l8FU\[/video\]](http://[video=youtube_share;LJVEPB_l8FU)

### jib — 2012-08-08T02:53:54+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/jack-lalanne.2910/post-43539>

ah yah......I think after he passed dr eades did a short on him either via his blog or twitter and he figured that for part of his life Lalanne did some steak (meat) in there somewhere before the "infomercial" juicing thing came about. Maybe the juicing shortened his life, I don't really remember what the media said about this. But he sure made a lot of difference in some of the people who followed him on the TV. I don't remember him talking too much about nutrition "back in the day", but it seems his message then was to do "something" to engage your muscles. Oh yeah, lose some weight too!!

### AKMan — 2012-08-08T03:08:47+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/jack-lalanne.2910/post-43545>

Two of his often-quoted sayings were: "If man made it--don't eat it" and "If it tastes good, spit it out".

He is living proof that a simple diet and exercise will get you at least to 97. He has a heart valve replacement at 93 and died 4 years later of complications with pneumonia. Amazing what a guy like him accomplished without the internet or access to all the science we have now, then again, maybe we could all learn from that.

I firmly believe that epi-paleo, CT, and strict adherence to circadian cycles will get people easily into the 100's.

### chocolate — 2012-08-08T03:15:47+0300

### kathylu — 2012-08-08T15:36:32+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/jack-lalanne.2910/post-43640>

I have always been impressed with Jack LaLanne. He was just so far ahead of everyone else. I remember hearing him in an interview at age 92, talking about his daily routine. "Up with the sun, swimming in a cold pool followed by a high protein breakfast of eggs, a little fruit and green juice. Gives me all the energy I need and to spare!" I'm paraphrasing of course. I also liked that he said it was important to always have big goals in life and that he had his goals mapped out until he reached 115...then maybe he would consider "retirement".

### AKMan — 2012-08-08T20:19:09+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/jack-lalanne.2910/post-43699>

I posted this on MDA this morning, enjoy...

This was an awesome thread, thanks to whoever started it. I spent all day yesterday and this morning reading and watching Jack Lalanne videos--he is truly an inspiration. Here's a great interview he did in 2003.

[http://www.shareguide.com/LaLanne.html](http://http://www.shareguide.com/LaLanne.html)

Best quotes:

"That's what I am saying. You have to take care of your 640 muscles, and the number one thing is exercise. You can eat perfectly but if you don't exercise, you cannot get by. There are so many health food nuts out there that eat nothing but natural foods but they don't exercise and they look terrible. Then there are other people who exercise like a son-of-a-gun but eat a lot of junk. **They look pretty good because the exercise is king. Nutrition is queen. Put them together and you've got a kingdom!"

"Share Guide: Many people who go to the gym only go three days a week rather than six or seven. What do you think about this?

Jack LaLanne: Three days is enough if it's vigorous.

Share Guide: I go to the gym in the morning before work, around 7 am.

Jack LaLanne: Do you work with the weights?

Share Guide: Sometimes. I start with 30 minutes on the treadmill or stairmaster.

Jack LaLanne: Twelve to seventeen minutes is plenty on the treadmill--if it's done fast. That's all you need for cardiovascular benefit. You don't need to spend that extra time unless you are over weight and you need to burn off extra calories.

Share Guide: How fast do I need to go on the treadmill?

Jack LaLanne: Do it vigorously, like somebody is chasing you. You've got to do it hard. Otherwise, if you just take it easy and do it longer, you are spending all that time when you don't need it. Use that extra time with your weights instead. Focus on your problem zones, your strength, your energy, your flexibility and all the rest. Maybe your chest is flabby or your hips or waist need toning. Also, you should change your program every thirty days. That's the key."

"Jack LaLanne: I say if you enjoy it, do it. Let me ask this: when you are at the gym, how long do you rest before you do the next exercise?

Share Guide: Generally I do two sets of 10-12 reps with a minute in between.

Jack LaLanne: That's too long. I rest ten seconds between sets.

Share Guide: Two sets per exercise?

Jack LaLanne: That is the very minimum. I do 20 sets for deltoids, 20 sets for pecs, and 20 sets for lats. It's important to change things around. Maybe for 30 days I might do ten reps with high weight, but the tenth rep should be to failure."

"Share Guide: Why did you stop being a strict vegetarian?

Jack LaLanne: In those days everybody was saying that you had to eat meat to build muscle, so I went on a meat thing for awhile. NowI only eat fish--no chicken, no turkey, just fish. **I get all my protein from fish and egg whites.**

Share Guide: That is what my favorite healing authority says, Dr. Andrew Weil. He believes in eating fish and dairy on top of the vegetarian diet--the vegaquarian diet.

Jack LaLanne: I use no milk of any kind. Anything that comes from a cow I don't eat.**

Share Guide: You aren't eating nonfat yogurt with your fruit anymore?

Jack LaLanne: I may take one bite. Or a little skim milk once in a while is not going to hurt you. It isn't what you do once in a while that's a problem; it's what you do all the time. Look at the average American diet: ice cream, butter, cheese, whole milk, all this fat. People don't realize how much of this stuff you get by the end of the day. High blood pressure is from all this high-fat eating. Do you know how many calories are in butter and cheese and ice cream? Would you get your dog up in the morning for a cup of coffee and a donut? Probably millions of Americans got up this morning with a cup of coffee, a cigarette and a donut. No wonder they are sick and fouled up."

"Jack LaLanne: It's a bunch of bull. How do you know what's really organic? Today, there's all these impurities in the water and the air. The water for the fruits and vegetables has junk in it. If you get enough vitamins and minerals out of normal food and whole grains, and you get enough proteins and exercise (that's the key) then nature builds up a tolerance to all of these things. It's survival of the fittest. You can't have everything perfect, that's impossible, but the fit survive. The fit can handle the impurities in the air and in the water, but the poor people who are sick, it really affects them more."

"Jack LaLanne: That's right, don't kid yourself. You have a mirror, right? And you know what you can do. One thing that you have to remember is SCALES LIE! The biggest liars we have are the scales. I always ask in my lectures, "What did you weigh when you were 20 years old?" Someone will say, "Oh, I weighed 170 lbs." But then they played football and basketball, and were in good shape. Now they guy is 50 years old and he says, " Jack, I haven't gained a pound! I weigh 170 just like I did when I was 20!" I'll say "Really, that's wonderful! How big was your waist when you were 20?" "Oh, 30 inches" "How big is it today?" "Oh, 36." The sands of time have shifted. The guy put five or six inches on his waist. You figure about every inch is five pounds approximately. That guy has gained about 30 pounds of fat and lost 30 pounds of muscle and the scale says he weighs the same.

Share Guide: So it's what you are made of not how much you weigh.

Jack LaLanne: Yes. What you need to do is get that tape measure out, and start measuring that gut. Then you start working out and you start eating properly till that gut gets down close to it was when you were in your 20's. Then you'll find out what your weight should be.

Share Guide: Sounds like a lot of sit-ups to me!

Jack LaLanne: It's not sit-ups, it's everything! In fact, if you've got a big gut and you start doing sit-ups, you are going to get bigger because you build up the muscle. You've got to get rid of that fat! How do you get rid of fat? By changing your diet. You can't get rid of it with exercise alone. You can do the most vigorous exercise and only burn up 300 calories in an hour. If you've got fat on your body, the exercise firms and tones the muscles. But when you use that tape measure, what makes it bigger? It's the fat! A two or three inch layer of fat. The only way you get that fat off is to eat less and exercise more.

Share Guide: Is that is what you mean by saying "your waistline is your lifeline?"

Jack LaLanne: Yes. Getting out of shape is like a thief in the night that sneaks up on you. I always tell people that it is never too late. I tell them about the folks in their 90's that doubled their strength and endurance. In the old days the doctors would say: "Slow down Jack, you're going to have a heart attack; you won't be able to get an erection; you'll make the women look like men." They thought that athletes that worked out with my system wouldn't be able to throw a ball because they'd be too muscle bound. Those are the misconceptions I had to go through for about 40 years. Now the medical profession is saying something different. Every one of the doctors without exception is saying, "Build muscle." Things really changed after I went on television and got the message out. That's when I starting reaching millions of people. In fact, I just made The Guinness Book of World Records for being on television 34 years non-stop (20 years in Europe)."

"Jack LaLanne: You have to be pretty stupid to think there isn't a Supreme Being to put all this together. Do you think that man could ever make a heart that is indestructible? Do you think that man could make a calculator like your brain? Do you think that man could ever make a machine that the only way you could hurt it is don't use it? Could man make a machine that in every 90 days practically every cell changes? Think about this. Look at all the creatures, the snakes, the worms and the birds, and the entire universe and everything that goes on. Don't you think there is a universal power, that there is some Supreme Being that put this all together? You don't have to call it God or Jesus. That's religious humbug to a lot of people, but you've gotta believe that nature and spiritual things surround us. That is what put us here! I thank the universe for that every day of my life. We don't know all the answers. If we knew all the answers we'd be bored, wouldn't we? We keep looking, searching, trying to get more knowledge. I'm just trying to help people with my lectures. I say, "You are out on the sea and drowning yourselves. Here is a life preserver. Grab it. I can save your life. Help yourself!"

# Thread: 23andme mutations
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/23andme-mutations.2928/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 3
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### jeanie@truelongmont.com — 2012-08-09T04:29:50+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/23andme-mutations.2928/post-43802>

I've seen a couple of you on here mention your 23andme mutations. Can you direct me to where to find those and start researching what they mean? I've not seen this! Perhaps I'm not a mutant?

### freshveggies — 2012-08-09T17:47:50+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/23andme-mutations.2928/post-43899>

Here is a table someone has made to convert the 23 and me snips to the Yasko snips that she uses to make her methylation protocol.

[https://docs.google.com/spreadsheet/ccc?key=0Ar76dNWyEQLIdExKZUhfXzFUWHpkWnZObk1TM1dxX0E&hl=en#gid=0](http://https://docs.google.com/spreadsheet/ccc?key=0Ar76dNWyEQLIdExKZUhfXzFUWHpkWnZObk1TM1dxX0E&hl=en#gid=0)

Use the raw data browser at the upper right corner of the 23 and me and then copy and paste each snip of line 2 of the table in the the raw data brower and it will give you a gene altell. ( I think that is what it is called.)

Then you look at the 3rd line of the gene you just took from the table and read what it says about + & - A=T & C=G

Under D it says for gene CBS C699T +A-G---if you are GG you would be -/- If you are AG you would be +/- if it said TT you would be +/+, and so on. A is not always + and Gis not alwasy -. You need to look at the table to determine which each means. If you have any questions, feel free to post them.

this graph was also helpful.

\# rsid gene snp genotype positive genotype negative

rs1801181 CBS A360A AT GC

rs234706 CBS Y233Y (C699T) AT GC

rs4633 COMT H62H AT GC

rs4680 COMT V158M AT GC

rs769224 COMT 61 P199P AT GC

rs6323 MAO A R297R AT GC

rs1801133 MTHFR A222V (C677T) AT GC

rs1801131 MTHFR E429A (A1298C) GC AT

rs2066470 MTHFR 03 P39P AT GC

rs1805087 MTR A919G (A2756G) GC AT

rs10380 MTRR H595Y AT GC

rs162036 MTRR K350A GC AT

rs2287780 MTRR R415T AT GC

rs1801394 MTRR A919G (A66G) GC AT

rs1802059 MTRR 11 A664A AT GC

rs1544410 VDR BSM/TAQ AT GC

rs3741049 ACAT1 0 N/A AT GC

rs819147 AHCY 01 N/A GC AT

rs819171 AHCY 19 N/A GC AT

rs567754 BHMT 02 N/A AT GC

rs617219 BHMT 04 N/A GC AT

rs651852 BHMT 08 N/A AT GC

rs1979277 SHMT C1420T AT GC

I cant keep this graph lined up properly when paste it in.

# Thread: Take a look at my labs please
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/take-a-look-at-my-labs-please.2941/>
**Subforum:** Optimal Labs
**Posts:** 5
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Eve — 2012-08-10T03:19:20+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/take-a-look-at-my-labs-please.2941/post-44032>

These are some of my recent labs:

I'm in Canada but converted the results to conventional values, so I could use the conventional ranges given by LEF and other resources.

These were taken on Day 4 of my cycle.

Jul 30/12

Estradiol 40.04 pg/mL LOW

lef follicular \[12.5 - 166.0 pg/mL\]; below 90 is a deficiency (but not sure if is for any time during cycle)

earlymenopause.com \[25- 75 pg/mL\]; below 50 is low

Progesterone 0.38 ng/mL LOW

lef range follicular \[0.2 - 1.5\]

I calculated by hand my P:E ratio and it was 8.22:1......If I did that right, that's really low!

DHEA-S 275 ug/dL OPTIMAL (BARELY)

lef's optimal range 275-400ug/dL

Testosterone 37.46 ng/dL (couldn't find the conventional range for this one)

standard value 1.3 nmol/L \[0.5 - 2.6\]

Jul 10/12

HDL 78mg/dL

LDL 137 mg/dL

Vitamin D 59 (up from 17 in January)

TSH 2.42

free T3 and free T4 were just below range...I had switched to Erfa natural thyroid 60mg in May approx. I didn't increase fast enough I think, so I became hypo again. After increasing 30mg at the end of July, my hypo symptoms went away. (Because my TSH was in the normal range, my doctor tested for prolactinoma but my prolactin was normal. She suspected the TSH was normal because natural thyroid contains varying doses of thyroid hormones. She is against me taking it and handed me papers on the Boxed Recall of natural thyroid in the States). Retesting thyroid in September.

May? 2012

HS-CRP 1.6

Thoughts:

I am surprised by DHEA is so good, considering my super low estrogen. I also was suspecting low DHEA cause I have a really bad leaky gut...?

The low progesterone I was expecting cause that is associated with leaky gut and I was born premature (by ~5 weeks).

Btw, I am only 26. When I started GAPS last Feb, I gained 20-25lbs in 4 weeks but I also came off a calorie restrictive diet (although gluten-free) and allowed myself fats and went binge crazy on almond butter (which my leaky gut can't handle). I have been 175-185 (5'7) the past year and can't lose weight at all.

My next steps:

I am having a consult at the compounding pharmacy next week and am hoping to get connected to a doc who does BHRT. Hopefully, I will find one taking patients.

Tomorrow I am doing my diurnal cortisol kit.

All comments appreciated

Thanks for reading

### KiwiLauren — 2012-08-10T07:41:25+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/take-a-look-at-my-labs-please.2941/post-44082>

I'm not so good with hormones, but having T3 and T4 at the low end/below range is problematic. You don't have a RT3 test or cortisol testing, but do you have any concern about cortisol rhythms/levels? I see you're doing the test, so that should be helpful I'd think, to the overall picture. How is your energy? Are you following light cycles strictly?

Your hsCRP is too high (should be as close to zero as possible) which means you've still got inflammation on board (me, too!). Slow weight loss can be linked to the poor hormone panel and, ultimately, the inflammation. As long as your gut is leaky, you'll have problems righting your hormone levels/balance. If you didn't hear the BHRT webinar, I'm guessing you'd find it very helpful.

In the webinar, Dr K said he wants DHEA in the top quartile, FYI.

How long have you been doing the Leptin Rx? You may need to give yourself some time to reset things. Sounds like you've already ditched nuts and dairy, but going hard with epi paleo diet and Leptin Rx should help. And of course CT.

I'm sure others with more expertise will chime in.

### Patty Cakes — 2012-08-10T17:05:17+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/take-a-look-at-my-labs-please.2941/post-44128>

You should also test your thyroid antibodies if you haven't done that already: TPO and TgAB. This will test for Hashimotos. Also agree with Kiwi (on everything she said), you should check your rT3 and at the same time recheck FT3 as it should be in the top of the range. And it appears your hormones are trashed and so is your gut. Not a hormone expert but you definitely need help and probably need a pregnenolone test too. Hopefully you can find a doc to work with both your leaky gut and hormones.

### Eve — 2012-08-10T18:04:01+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/take-a-look-at-my-labs-please.2941/post-44141>

Thanks guys for the comments.

In Canada, they don't have a RT3 test (as much as I want one). I am seeing an Internal Medicine Doctor in October(! after a 4 month wait), so maybe he will order one for me from somewhere.

In 2008 when I was first diagnosed with hypothyroid, my doc tested for Hashi's and said I didn't have it. Unfortunately, I don't have a copy of the results. I think I was tested again for it by a different doc later as well in 2009.

I have been doing the Leptin Reset since January...yes, going on 8 months now. I would like to do the Epi-Paleo and load up on fish...unfortunately I am still on the hunt for fish that I don't react to...I was eating fish for a while and then realized I was reacting to the sodium phosphate in it. I tried organic salmon and had a reaction to that too...Also, I have a corn sensitivity and I read that all fish as soon as it's caught is stored in ice that has citric acid in it. Maybe local will be better...I'll try that next.

I don't think they have a pregnenolone test at least in my province. I looked at the online Lab catelogue online and it wasn't listed. But once again, perhaps the internal medicine doc can order it for me.

For the DHEA, is that upper quartile for even the "optimal" range? Or is it upper quartile for just the standard ranges?

After I get my cortisol test back, I'm also considering booking a consult with Dr. K.

### Patty Cakes — 2012-08-10T18:32:34+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/take-a-look-at-my-labs-please.2941/post-44145>

Yes DHEA s/b in the top quartile of the optimal range. You could raise yours a little, I would say. Keep doing what you are doing as it sounds like you are working at finding the right solutions for your situation. I hope you are able to get the tests you need.

# Thread: Coconut Oil how much is too much?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/coconut-oil-how-much-is-too-much.2943/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 3
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Raiken3712 — 2012-08-10T05:44:28+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/coconut-oil-how-much-is-too-much.2943/post-44068>

For awhile I was using coconut in my hair and on my skin daily...and nothing seemed bad about it. I was wondering though if coconut oil too much or too often could be bad though because it will kill good bacteria as well? I've heard about things ...maybe it was coconut oil that for some reason don't destroy the good bacteria but only the bad..not sure how that would work..can't remember what it was.

Anyway so I'm making Water Kefir and I'm not supposed to make it with honey because Kefir uses a bacteria/yeast...can't think of name... so was wondering if I should limit use because of this. There are supposed to be beneficial bacteria on both skin, in hair, in the stomach..pretty much everywhere in the body so will coconut oil kill these?

Thanks

### Michael — 2012-08-10T13:48:29+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/coconut-oil-how-much-is-too-much.2943/post-44092>

I think you can rest easy on that score.

There are people who've been annointing their skin with coconut oil (as a kind of sun-block among other things) for centuries, so if it killed the bacteria that, so to speak, belong there they'd have run into problems and learnt to stop doing that a long time ago.

Similar considerations would apply to food use. Recently CO has even been used with AIDS patients. Reports are it's quite good for their troubled systems. If it killed off beneficial bacteria in the gut, then it would create a niche for harmful ones, which would then run riot, os that the patients would get worse not better.

### Raiken3712 — 2012-08-11T00:10:15+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/coconut-oil-how-much-is-too-much.2943/post-44202>

> **Michael;44390 wrote:** I think you can rest easy on that score.
>
>
>
> There are people who've been annointing their skin with coconut oil (as a kind of sun-block among other things) for centuries, so if it killed the bacteria that, so to speak, belong there they'd have run into problems and learnt to stop doing that a long time ago.
>
>
>
> Similar considerations would apply to food use. Recently CO has even been used with AIDS patients. Reports are it's quite good for their troubled systems. If it killed off beneficial bacteria in the gut, then it would create a niche for harmful ones, which would then run riot, os that the patients would get worse not better.

You're right....makes quite a bit of sense....does that mean coconut oil ignores all beneficial bacteria though...and if so now I"m curious about the mechanism.

# Thread: New species
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/new-species.2945/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 1
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### jeanie@truelongmont.com — 2012-08-10T07:00:16+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/new-species.2945/post-44075>

Did yaaaal see this one?

# Thread: Hcg yes or no?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hcg-yes-or-no.2950/>
**Subforum:** HCG Protocol
**Posts:** 13
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### jeanie@truelongmont.com — 2012-08-10T15:34:55+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hcg-yes-or-no.2950/post-44109>

At a webinar last night Jack said something regarding who should not take HCG (i used all my tough love yesterday so i won't repeat his colorful insults) .

Summary is if you have hpa axis issues (eating disorders do) then its a bad idea.

I had not heard this reason yet and found it useful.

### finnite@dccnet.com — 2012-08-10T22:25:48+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hcg-yes-or-no.2950/post-44195>

on most hcg forums it's generally suggested that folks with eating disorders deal with tht first before trying hcg. few listen but it's a pretty standard recommendation. hcg definitely will not fix an ED and may worsen it (food restrictions etc)

### cantweight — 2012-09-21T15:41:26+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hcg-yes-or-no.2950/post-53153>

I did multiple rounds of HCG and lost a substantial amount of weight, however I gained it back within weeks of stopping and major hormonal fallout after each round. Perhaps I could have negated some of that with activated charcoal to help clean out whatever scary stuff all that fat released. I have low progesterone...another factor that to my understanding works against one using HCG. And my most recent finding is that my liver is not functioning as well as it should so while my estrogen is showing up very low in blood work, it is not low, my liver is processing it and storing in fat cells. I just smile and nod as I hear all these things.

Doing the leptin RX combined with CT gave me much better full body results than HCG. The improvement in skin, mood, hair, sleep, body shape etc, made up for the slower number decline on the scale. I actually looked better, felt better, and ate bacon!

### caroline — 2012-09-28T01:55:27+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hcg-yes-or-no.2950/post-54896>

> **cantweight;53788 wrote:** I did multiple rounds of HCG and lost a substantial amount of weight, however I gained it back within weeks of stopping and major hormonal fallout after each round. Perhaps I could have negated some of that with activated charcoal to help clean out whatever scary stuff all that fat released. I have low progesterone...another factor that to my understanding works against one using HCG. And my most recent finding is that my liver is not functioning as well as it should so while my estrogen is showing up very low in blood work, it is not low, my liver is processing it and storing in fat cells. I just smile and nod as I hear all these things.
>
>
>
> Doing the leptin RX combined with CT gave me much better full body results than HCG. The improvement in skin, mood, hair, sleep, body shape etc, made up for the slower number decline on the scale. I actually looked better, felt better, and ate bacon!

I so agree - after HHCG - I had a very hard time maintaining but since doing the leptin RX and CT I am down 2 sizes. That might not sound like a big deal - but I am not very big - 60K - so 2 sizes is huge. I didn't realize it was happening even tho my clothes were falling off me. I was also pretty bummed that I went up 2K on the leptin RX and didn't realize my body comp was changing. Sounds dopey not to realize - but when you have been dieting most of your life - things like that don't happen - not to me anyway! Dr. K. says women focus too much on the scale - I am shocked at how right he is! who knew.....

### Zorica Vuletic — 2013-01-29T21:30:58+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hcg-yes-or-no.2950/post-73200>

> [caroline said:](/goto/post?id=54896)
>
> I so agree - after HHCG - I had a very hard time maintaining but since doing the leptin RX and CT I am down 2 sizes. That might not sound like a big deal - but I am not very big - 60K - so 2 sizes is huge. I didn't realize it was happening even tho my clothes were falling off me. I was also pretty bummed that I went up 2K on the leptin RX and didn't realize my body comp was changing. Sounds dopey not to realize - but when you have been dieting most of your life - things like that don't happen - not to me anyway! Dr. K. says women focus too much on the scale - I am shocked at how right he is! who knew.....

I have always obsessed about body comp and never weight. Luckily b/c I always knew what made sense best. I figured my clothes would tell me one way or anther...So even since a teen I knew it was in the body comp not the 'size' per se. Also I would compare and observe people who are quite 'large' but I just looked at them and they had a better body comp (particularly very nice legs). I knew then also that it's the composition not the size.

### Charlotte — 2013-01-29T21:40:53+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hcg-yes-or-no.2950/post-73203>

I do not quite agree to that Caroline and Zorica. I can be 5 kg up and my pants still fit. The scale is a much finer tuned instrument to see a tendency. What is not good is obsessing. But what speaks against getting on the scale in the morning to evaluate where you are right now with weight. Is the tendency up or down or stable? What did you eat last day? If you can use that simply to evaluate instead of getting emotionally involved I think the scale is a perfect instrument.

### diane — 2013-01-29T22:07:12+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hcg-yes-or-no.2950/post-73212>

Charlotte I'm so envious if you can use it without emotional involvement!  That's one reason why I'm trying to find other ways right now. But I also think that more than just food can cause weight fluctuations. Perhaps that knowledge keeps you from getting emotionally involved.

For me, I read a lot about HCG, and I almost did it (even ordered some, but can only get the homeopathic kind here). But reading some of these forums, I saw how obsessed that people can get, and knowing my personality, I also feared that's what I would do. I also saw a lot of people that have to do it multiple times because they gain the weight back - and that's always my problem as well. I also just had a feeling that somehow I would be creating bigger problems for myself down the line. No evidence, but sometimes I have to go on my gut. But I'm happy for the people that it works for!

### caroline — 2013-01-29T23:17:47+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hcg-yes-or-no.2950/post-73227>

For me - weighing every day is just something I need to do.....but I realize that so many things effect it. if I didn't drink enough water - If I had a terrible sleep my weight will always be up. All my overweight life \[pre JK\] I would avoid the scales like the plague if I knew I was gaining ..... and that is the worst case scenario because it gets out of hand so fast. If you have gained 5 lbs - you can just take care of it ...but when you have gained 15-20lbs it is a major deal to get rid of it. Not getting on the scales when I knew I was gaining was way, way more stressful for me ..... and when I just went by how my clothes were fitting I was in big trouble because I could gain 10 -12 lbs and my clothes still fit fine - it just got distributed differently. When I weight everyday it makes me feel like I am in control of this journey for the first time in my life and I am not going to let anything derail me - so I just suck it up and do it ...and sometimes I get a nice surprise! my 2 cents.....

### Charlotte — 2013-01-29T23:24:33+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hcg-yes-or-no.2950/post-73228>

yes Diane, I agree. Most important is to know how you react, what you can take and what not. I also gain weight or stall when I stay up over night, as I had to do last August, when I worked three days and three nights straight without sleep. No loss at 500 kcal. Then, doing too much in terms of heavy lifting (not at the fitness studio, but at work), or having stress, not drinking enough, inflammation, something I ate that did not go down well and so on. And I think it is interesting to get on the scale in morning, and remember and then not get upset because you have not lost, but simply assess the situation. Its learning by doing for me.

### Charlotte — 2013-01-29T23:28:04+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hcg-yes-or-no.2950/post-73229>

exactly my experience too, Caroline. For me, getting on the scale is staying in control. (maybe we are just control freaks )

### caroline — 2013-01-29T23:49:47+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hcg-yes-or-no.2950/post-73235>

Charlotte - I really don't think we are control freaks - but after a lifetime of trying to starve my body into submission - all of this that JK is teaching us ...is working - but we need to dig in ... be 100% in. If we want to be successful we can't be afraid of the numbers on the scale - I need to know to keep on track .... just the same as looking at my labs - I need to know what's up and where I am going . JK's flashlight can only show me so much - I need to get in there and do....
I suppose this overweight thing isn't the same for everyone ..... I used to look in the mirror and feel like a thin person on the inside - but could never understand how/why all this flab had attached itself to me ....I always hear about obese people who lose weight and then they gain it back because they can't relate to the new thin person ....that was never me ...I was a thin person regardless and inspite of what the mirror was telling me .....

### diane — 2013-01-29T23:51:08+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hcg-yes-or-no.2950/post-73236>

I like this conversation - so much to think about. Thanks ladies!

### caroline — 2013-01-30T00:10:48+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hcg-yes-or-no.2950/post-73240>

Diane - I think - in the long run - that is what JK has taught us....think for ourselves and question everything...I am not a sheep - never have been and never will be...but I think - at some point - you do have to trust someone/something and go for it. All of this is falling into place for me after years of searching for answers ... that doesn't make me a sheep - it means I have finally found something that makes so much sense and seems so right. I suppose I could better evaluate if I had a medical degree - but then again - I have known Doctors who are pretty lame ....

# Thread: Can we cause gluten intolerance in our kids?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/can-we-cause-gluten-intolerance-in-our-kids.2960/>
**Subforum:** Optimal Kids
**Posts:** 11
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### trishalinn@gmail.com — 2012-08-10T20:03:39+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/can-we-cause-gluten-intolerance-in-our-kids.2960/post-44162>

So we have some celiac disease in my family, both my grandma and uncle have it. I have in the last few months cut my kids who are 8 and 3 off of gluten and sugar. More changes are in the works, but we are cutting things gradually - those are pretty huge changes for my boys anyhow. My twin 14 month olds I'm not allowing any gluten either and this is causing one of my aunts great distress. She has apparently read that not allowing them to eat gluten from a lung age will cause gluten intolerance. Now, I don't know whether or not this is true and it really doesn't matter that much because I am still resolved to cut this out of my families diet. I am curious whether it is true though, just so I k ow how to respond. I have already told her, I don't believe it is a healthy substance for people to eat EVEN if they aren't gluten intolerant. She recently had the blood tests done for celiac disease and she is quite excited that she is all clear to keep eating gluten and I guess she doesn't want my kids to miss out on cake and pie and pizza and Mac and cheese. You know, the healthy American way of eating (ha!).

### Shijin13 — 2012-08-10T20:34:26+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/can-we-cause-gluten-intolerance-in-our-kids.2960/post-44166>

eating gluten causes gluten intolerance. If you don't eat it - no problems. as for cookies, cakes, pie, pizza etc - they're your kids, she needs to respect how your raising them.

If she keeps pushing the issue give her a copy of the paleo answer, ask her to read it - she'll get the picture, as cordain explains why grains, legumes, soy etc are bad for us.

the real deal is - these are you kids - rasie them how you want them raised, they are not your aunts childeren - in the end they'll be healtier than their peers.

ETA: if you have to give treats/desserts - find things that are complementary to how you eat.

fresh berries/fruit in season w/HWC

kids don't need candy or cake - last weekend we went to a 4yo bday party - neither of our kids had cake. Our frend did make macarones for them - and they each had 1. While not ideal - at least they participated. The 4yo now ask if something has wheat, and attempts to avoid eating it.

### KiwiLauren — 2012-08-10T21:18:15+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/can-we-cause-gluten-intolerance-in-our-kids.2960/post-44182>

> **Shijin13;44470 wrote:** eating gluten causes gluten intolerance.

+1000 This is how we flipped that epigenetic switch isn't it?

and +1000 to what Gretchen said about these being YOUR children. When my children don't like a choice I've made for them, I simply tell them (after explaining why I made the choice) that I am responsible for their health and I MUST do what I believe is right, regardless of what others say. When they become adults they can make their own choices.

### bigknitwit — 2012-08-10T22:06:01+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/can-we-cause-gluten-intolerance-in-our-kids.2960/post-44192>

After my 2nd was diagnosed celiac at age 5 (while I was pregnant with #4), I didn't allow #4 to eat any gluten, except she had exposure through breastmilk until I cut gluten completely myself when she was 9 months old. He had allready been diagnosed with Hashi's at age 3.5. She obviously has not developped celiac, however, she did develop Hashi's at the same age he did (3). I'm pretty confident full blown celiac is in the cards for her should she start eating gluten. In fact I'm nervous because I'm not as rigid about cross-contamination issues with her or my other two as with her brother.

That all said, my in-laws used to be really distressed for the other children in my house having to endure eating gluten free all the time (the house is gluten free) just because one child has an issue... They blamed me of course, for imposing such a henous rule. But, 4 years have passed, and they have really come around. At least they respect the reason for the rule, not that any of them ever stopped eating gluten or anything lol!! My kid ws the 1st on either side of the family to get the diagnosis. Since then, I have a cousin and his mother (my aunt) diagnosed. But still nobody else wants to see the writing on the wall. Not my sister or my brother are concerned for their children... sigh...

### Souldanzer — 2012-08-11T01:24:35+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/can-we-cause-gluten-intolerance-in-our-kids.2960/post-44212>

I'd say the human species is gluten intolerant. We don't become it. We are. No matter if you eat it or not.

### NWgirl — 2012-08-11T02:12:07+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/can-we-cause-gluten-intolerance-in-our-kids.2960/post-44221>

Both my daughters have the celiac gene. One has just one of the genes and the other (they are fraternal twins) has BOTH of the genes. However the twin that has only the one celiac gene actually developed celiac disease at 15 years old. The other twin (with two genes) does not have it (yet).

My point is, the genes don't always switch on and sometimes it takes an illness or stress to turn them on. The girl that got celiac had mono and we suspect that illness switched on the gene.

### KiwiLauren — 2012-08-11T03:01:42+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/can-we-cause-gluten-intolerance-in-our-kids.2960/post-44231>

> **Souldanzer;44519 wrote:** I'd say the human species is gluten intolerant. We don't become it. We are. No matter if you eat it or not.

If you read Bill Davis (Wheatbelly) or many others in paleo land, they'd agree with that. And Davis points out repeatedly that our current testing for celiac is only ONE way to pick up major issues with gluten. He has worked with people who had completely incapacitating symptoms that resolved with gluten elimination but tested negative for celiac. I think it's pretty clear that not consuming gluten is required for optimal growth and development for all human beings.

### kathylu — 2012-08-11T03:15:02+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/can-we-cause-gluten-intolerance-in-our-kids.2960/post-44236>

> **KiwiLauren;44539 wrote:** If you read Bill Davis (Wheatbelly) or many others in paleo land, they'd agree with that. And Davis points out repeatedly that our current testing for celiac is only ONE way to pick up major issues with gluten. He has worked with people who had completely incapacitating symptoms that resolved with gluten elimination but tested negative for celiac. I think it's pretty clear that not consuming gluten is required for optimal growth and development for all human beings.

I tested negative for celiac, several times. However, I developed debilitating arthritis that cleared up completely with grain elimination. So, I'm certainly gluten/gliadin intolerant no matter what the blood tests show. Just sayin'.

### ColdBren — 2012-08-11T21:46:40+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/can-we-cause-gluten-intolerance-in-our-kids.2960/post-44377>

There is a subtlety of the question that doesn't seem to be addressed directly. Most of us agree gluten is bad overall. The question is does avoiding it completely at a young age create a greater sensitivity. I would like to know this too. It doesn't mean I will let my kid et it more, I just want to know th truth.

### KiwiLauren — 2012-08-12T00:04:32+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/can-we-cause-gluten-intolerance-in-our-kids.2960/post-44409>

> **ColdBren;44685 wrote:** There is a subtlety of the question that doesn't seem to be addressed directly. Most of us agree gluten is bad overall. The question is does avoiding it completely at a young age create a greater sensitivity. I would like to know this too. It doesn't mean I will let my kid et it more, I just want to know th truth.

My guess would be that avoiding it completely in childhood would mean that a person would not develop the symptoms (many of which become subtle, underground and not-apparently-linked-to-food) that go along with gluten consumption. In other words, creating a stronger, more optimal foundation of health. However, if that person started to eat gluten later in life, they may actually appear more 'sensitive' to it because the symptoms hadn't gone underground. So given what it does to gut health (make it more leaky), someone who consumes gluten earlier in life will have a longer time under their belt creating gut leakiness. They may not show symptoms, but the more years you pound your body with bad stuff, the more likely you are going to reap that later. But that's just my theory.

I also think it's pretty clear that some people are, because of their own genetics/epigenetics, more sensitive than others and will experience a much more obvious and greater negative effect immediately and in the long term. The problem is, I don't think we can easily tell where we/our children fall along that spectrum.

### Joann — 2012-08-16T10:27:31+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/can-we-cause-gluten-intolerance-in-our-kids.2960/post-45173>

I think the bottom line with gluten is that it causes inflammation whether or not a person gets gut symptoms.

**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/elderly-dog-in-pain-seeks-cold-water-news-article.2964/post-44198>

**Man cradles arthritic, sleeping dog in Lake Superior to ease his â€˜best friendâ€™sâ€™ pain**

John Unger holds his 19-year-old dog in cold water as he sleeps, to help cope with the dog's arthritis.

After 18 years of loyal companionship, a Wisconsin man is trying to comfort a dear friend through his final days of life.

A photo of John Unger holding his shepherd mix in a lake as the dog sleeps has gone viral, the image portraying the incredible bond between man and dog.

# Thread: Anyone ever heard or done this form of 'mini CT'?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/anyone-ever-heard-or-done-this-form-of-mini-ct.2974/>
**Subforum:** Cold Thermogenesis
**Posts:** 3
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### NWgirl — 2012-08-11T09:02:52+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/anyone-ever-heard-or-done-this-form-of-mini-ct.2974/post-44287>

My naturopath told me that on nights that I don't do a CT bath to try this method: wet a pair of cotton socks until soaking and wring them out, but just enough so they're not dripping. Put on the wet socks and then put another pair of dry socks over the wet socks, then go to bed.

I did this last night, not sure what it's doing but I like the idea of doing mini CT while sleeping.

### Raiken3712 — 2012-08-11T18:15:13+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/anyone-ever-heard-or-done-this-form-of-mini-ct.2974/post-44338>

Never heard of it but I can see how this might be helpful. If the socks are cold enough to elicit a response from your body than your body will try to warm your hands.

Since its just your hands it may not do much but it couldn't hurt. Or at least it seems fine to me...not sure how useful that is though.

### NWgirl — 2012-08-11T19:09:16+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/anyone-ever-heard-or-done-this-form-of-mini-ct.2974/post-44354>

The funny thing is the socks are completely dry by about 3am even though they're covered up. I didn't expect that!

# Thread: Fermented sauerkraut  dill pickles...questions

**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet

### PrimalPam — 2012-08-11T23:18:03+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fermented-sauerkraut-dill-pickles-questions.2987/post-44396>

Sauerkraut...good, I know. But is Claussen's from the grocery store ok? Or is there a brand/type to look for?

Dill pickles...same question.

### NWgirl — 2012-08-11T23:40:01+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fermented-sauerkraut-dill-pickles-questions.2987/post-44404>

The only brand of real sauerkraut (raw) with good bacteria that I know of is Bubbie's. It's pretty good but I prefer making my own as its inexpensive.

I have the recipe on one of my blogs: [http://thenourishingcook.com/sauerkraut-winters-miracle-vegetable-salad/](http://http://thenourishingcook.com/sauerkraut-winters-miracle-vegetable-salad/)

Let me know if you have questions, I have been fermenting veggies for awhile now.

### Patty Cakes — 2012-08-12T00:02:35+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fermented-sauerkraut-dill-pickles-questions.2987/post-44408>

Don't get anything that has vinegar in it for pickles. You want it fermented not vinegared! Bubbies pickles I;m pretty sure are made without vinegar.

### PrimalPam — 2012-08-12T00:06:17+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fermented-sauerkraut-dill-pickles-questions.2987/post-44410>

Oh thanks...Bubbies it is!

### Raiken3712 — 2012-08-12T02:39:19+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fermented-sauerkraut-dill-pickles-questions.2987/post-44431>

Are they pasteurized by law? Also if they are does it get rid of most of the good bacteria or just some of it? I have some Bubbies I bought from Sprouts Farmer's Market in Lewisville...doesn't say pasteurized on the bottle...not sure.

How can you tell if something still has beneficial bacteria intact? If you start to digest better? Any easier way to know?

### jeanie@truelongmont.com — 2012-08-12T04:04:54+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fermented-sauerkraut-dill-pickles-questions.2987/post-44444>

> **NWgirl;44712 wrote:** The only brand of real sauerkraut (raw) with good bacteria that I know of is Bubbie's. It's pretty good but I prefer making my own as its inexpensive.
>
>
>
> I have the recipe on one of my blogs: [http://thenourishingcook.com/sauerkraut-winters-miracle-vegetable-salad/](http://http://thenourishingcook.com/sauerkraut-winters-miracle-vegetable-salad/)
>
>

### jeanie@truelongmont.com — 2012-08-12T05:08:38+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fermented-sauerkraut-dill-pickles-questions.2987/post-44462>

### Souldanzer — 2012-08-12T07:59:49+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fermented-sauerkraut-dill-pickles-questions.2987/post-44489>

> **Patty Cakes;44716 wrote:** Don't get anything that has vinegar in it for pickles. You want it fermented not vinegared! Bubbies pickles I;m pretty sure are made without vinegar.
>
>
> Without vinegar but pasteurized or flash pasteurized. I wrote to them a while ago and they had to start flash pasteurizing b/c the jars were bubbling over and the stores complained about messes. Here's the answer:
>
>
>
>
>
>
>
> Thank you very much for writing in to Bubbies, we are always pleased to hear from one of our customers and we would be delighted to tell you more about our food products in regards to whether they are raw, heated, or pasteurized.
>
>
>
> Our Pure Kosher Dills, Dill Relish, Pickled Green Tomatoes and Sauerkraut are all naturally fermented and cured in salt water brine using a lacto-fermentation process. These products contain live cultures and the enzymes that form from a natural fermentation.
>
>
>
> The Pure Kosher Dills, Dill Relish and Pickled Green Tomatoes are 100% raw; the Sauerkraut in the jars has been flash heated but not pasteurized. This means that the sauerkraut is neither pasteurized nor raw. We were forced to begin heating our jarred Sauerkraut to calm the cultures inside because they were causing the kraut to continue to ferment too much in turn causing a buildup of gas that then results in brine leaking all over our distributor's and retailer's equipment and shelving.
>
>
>
> When we heat our jarred Sauerkraut, it is quickly raised to about 135-140 degrees and then sealed in the jars. The goal here is not to eliminate all the beneficial cultures, but rather to stifle them so they won't cause the jars to leak. While the heating we do for our Sauerkraut is only intended to calm the gas producing nature of the product with the specific goal in mind not to eliminate the beneficial cultures. We do not claim that this product is raw for these reasons, but it still does have live bacteria. From our testing, it is above 140 degrees that you really begin to eliminate the cultures present in our products on a massive scale.
>
>
>
> It is important to note that our Sauerkraut is very crisp. This is because there are still vegetable fibers left intact in the cabbage which are the complex carbohydrates that break down into the simpler food that the lacto bacillus cultures feed on during the fermentation process.

### kathylu — 2012-08-12T14:33:32+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fermented-sauerkraut-dill-pickles-questions.2987/post-44500>

The salt in Bubbie's is way too high for me. I make my own kraut with 1 tbsp of celtic sea salt per 1/2 gallon, much less salty and tastier I think! I did start my original batch using 1/4 cup of whey (from home made yogurt), but since then I just start a new batch with 1/4 cup of juice from the previous batch. You can also experiment with adding other veggies to the mix, shredded carrot, sweet or hot peppers, garlic, green onion, a little pickling spice, cubed daikon, jicama, cauliflower, broccoli etc. It's all good.

I also pour a glass of kraut juice occasionally and just drink it! It's cloudy with yummy good bacteria. Just top off the kraut with some filtered water (and a little salt if you like, but I don't) and pop it back in the fridge.

### PrimalPam — 2012-08-12T16:10:11+0300

And thanks for the kraut recipes NW Girl and kathylu. One of these days, when I get adventurous...of course, this coming from someone who spent decades microwaving Lean Cuisines.

### zzzcjr@comcast.net — 2012-09-03T21:54:08+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fermented-sauerkraut-dill-pickles-questions.2987/post-49218>

Do I have to put homemade fermented veggies in the fridge after a few days and once the jar is open? Can I take some out and still leave them on the counter until they're gone?

### NWgirl — 2012-09-03T23:03:31+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fermented-sauerkraut-dill-pickles-questions.2987/post-49234>

> **Kath;49722 wrote:** Do I have to put homemade fermented veggies in the fridge after a few days and once the jar is open? Can I take some out and still leave them on the counter until they're gone?

After the initial fermentation they should be kept in the fridge. They will over ferment on the counter pretty quickly after that.

### zzzcjr@comcast.net — 2012-09-03T23:06:04+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fermented-sauerkraut-dill-pickles-questions.2987/post-49235>

Good to know-thanks.

Thx.

### BJK77 — 2012-08-13T20:37:03+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fish-jerky.2989/post-44733>

I made salmon jerky before using Red Boat fish sauce - [http://redboatfishsauce.com/faq.html](http://http://redboatfishsauce.com/faq.html). It's the real deal. Good stuff!

### Michael — 2012-08-13T21:03:39+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fish-jerky.2989/post-44740>

> **Souldanzer;44726 wrote:** I've been making fish jerky

Dried fish? That's stockfish.

[http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Stockfish](http://http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Stockfish)

I think it used to be quite common in Scandinavia.

Dried salt-cod was also much used in Southern Europe -- i.e. in Roman Catholic countries -- in places like Portugal, as "fasting" or Lenten food. I believe sometimes nunneries would put the fish in a running stream for hours at a time in order to wash the salt out before cooking it. You could probably find recipes for using something like that under the name bacalao or bacalhau or morue.

Soaked dried salted cod is also used in traditional West-indian dishes. Tried to find an example by googling and came up with this, which is stated here to be Jamaica's national dish:

[http://eatjamaican.com/recipes/ackeeandsaltfish-recipe.html](http://http://eatjamaican.com/recipes/ackeeandsaltfish-recipe.html)

I've no idea what ackee is.

### Qodesh — 2012-08-16T19:45:24+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fish-jerky.2989/post-45224>

Stockfish is unsalted fish, especially cod, dried by cold air and wind on wooden racks on the foreshore, called "hjell". The drying of food is the world's oldest known preservation method, and dried fish has a storage life of several years. The method is cheap and effective in suitable climates; the work can be done by the fisherman and family, and the resulting product is easily transported to market.

Stockfish is cured in a process called fermentation where cold adapted bacteria matures the fish, similar to the maturing process of cheese.

ah, thanks!

### Souldanzer — 2012-08-16T20:03:57+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fish-jerky.2989/post-45227>

Thx everybody. I don't use salt to preserve my fish though before drying it. It works well without it though but tastes better with some spicing.

I'm going to venture into low temp dried squid today.... Wish me luck lol. The stuff at the Asian store looks yummy but has lots of MSG added. I've also only dried salmon and smelts so far... Going to try some other fish with this batch as well. Maybe cod? Or tuna?

Dried fish, especially the salmon bc its fatty appears to make great ready food and food on the go if needed.

# Thread: Opinions on gradual increase schedule of hormones
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/opinions-on-gradual-increase-schedule-of-hormones.2990/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 2
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### tweety — 2012-08-12T01:50:24+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/opinions-on-gradual-increase-schedule-of-hormones.2990/post-44421>

So I am currently on 50mg pregnenolone, 50mg DHEA and 60mg thyroid. I have orders from my doc to increase my NDT by 30g every two weeks. I would also like to increase DHEA and pregnenolone. But do you have ideas or recommendations on how to space out the increments? I'm thinking in the weeks I am not increasing thyroid I could increase either the DHEA or pregnenolone. Is that too much too fast? Should I wait until I am finished increasing thyroid until I tinker w the other two? Also, which would you increase first- DHEA or pregnenolone? And why? I'm horribly low in all!

Thanks

### tweety — 2012-08-12T03:09:45+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/opinions-on-gradual-increase-schedule-of-hormones.2990/post-44435>

Well, my doc wanted me on 5mg DHEA. I told her I was already on 50mg. She did recommend 50mg pregnenolone, but I have a long way to go.

I know what levels I want to increase too, just curious about how slowly to increase. I want to give each time to work, and not get myself in too deep too fast.

# Thread: Bone broth websites links wanted
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bone-broth-websites-links-wanted.2992/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 8
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Raiken3712 — 2012-08-12T02:44:55+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bone-broth-websites-links-wanted.2992/post-44433>

So there are tons of websites out there with bone broth recipes...anyone have any recommendations on good ones? Also I plan to get more but only have one pound of bone at the moment...is this enough to make some broth?

I've heard vegetables and other things to put in the broth...would it not be too bad if I just used ACV and Real Salt? That's all you need right? All the veggies and such are optional?

Thanks

### jeanie@truelongmont.com — 2012-08-12T04:02:41+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bone-broth-websites-links-wanted.2992/post-44443>

> **Raiken3712;44741 wrote:** So there are tons of websites out there with bone broth recipes...anyone have any recommendations on good ones? Also I plan to get more but only have one pound of bone at the moment...is this enough to make some broth?
>
>
>
> I've heard vegetables and other things to put in the broth...would it not be too bad if I just used ACV and Real Salt? That's all you need right? All the veggies and such are optional?
>
>
>
>
>
>
>
> Thanks

Yup! I'm not sure the veggies add any nutrients. . I'm not even sure their flavor stays after boiling for days.

I've made some with just ACV and bones. Don't sting on the legnth of boil. With just bones go for 72 hrs minimum.

In the olden days, they used to keep a pot of bones boiling constantly, just adding new water and bones now and then.

Stick it all in the crockpot, add water as needed, and keep it going!

### Raiken3712 — 2012-08-12T05:12:43+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bone-broth-websites-links-wanted.2992/post-44464>
