# Thread: how much food ?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-much-food.2994/>
**Subforum:** The Leptin Rx
**Posts:** 11
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### somewhatchubby — 2012-08-12T10:04:50+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-much-food.2994/post-44491>

morning all, after surprsingly getting my husband on board on the leptin thingy. I am amazed at how much he can eat at the crack of dawn. This morning ,humungous pork chop , three large rashers back bacon and three eggs. I would have popped a gasket after that much ,is he taking it too far or is it ok for a big guy to consume so much.? I havnt tracked it yet but I would guess easily 70 gms protein.

This morning I only managed to eat 39 gms , often I get over 50 gms but I feel sliglthy nauseated afterwards ,although this passes after an hour or so. Any other ladies struggling with this amount of food in the am ?

Also ,any ideas apart from eggs and bacon, canned fish patties. Sometimes I really need a change. Is full fat cottage cheese ok ? I find I can eat any amout of that.

thanks

### ealachan — 2012-08-12T12:33:25+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-much-food.2994/post-44494>

> **somewhatchubby;44799 wrote:** morning all, after surprsingly getting my husband on board on the leptin thingy. I am amazed at how much he can eat at the crack of dawn. This morning ,humungous pork chop , three large rashers back bacon and three eggs. I would have popped a gasket after that much ,is he taking it too far or is it ok for a big guy to consume so much.? I havnt tracked it yet but I would guess easily 70 gms protein.
>
> This morning I only managed to eat 39 gms , often I get over 50 gms but I feel sliglthy nauseated afterwards ,although this passes after an hour or so. Any other ladies struggling with this amount of food in the am ?
>
> Also ,any ideas apart from eggs and bacon, canned fish patties. Sometimes I really need a change. Is full fat cottage cheese ok ? I find I can eat any amout of that.
>
> thanks

The Rx guidelines say to eat at least 50g (and up to 70g if you're big / hungry) of protein every morning. If you add it all up, I bet your husband is falling within that range. One of the things I found surprising when I started the Rx was the sheer amount of food I needed to make up 50g of protein. Bacon is lovely, but it doesn't have a whole lot of protein per slice (although UK bacon has more than US bacon because yours isn't "streaky" like ours is. I miss UK bacon!). Eggs are 6-7 grams protein each. The pork chop is hard to tell without weighing it.

I've never had a problem feeling nauseated after a big protein breakfast, but I used to get wicked heartburn and/or just feel totally stuffed and sluggish. That partially wore off with time, and it also helped me to start taking an enzyme and an ox bile supplement. I don't have a gallbladder anymore, so things weren't working correctly in my guts to begin with, and the enzyme and bile supps really helped a lot. Might be worth giving them a try?

In terms of what to eat when you need a change, I like to do steak sometimes, chicken or pork sausage (make sure it's not full of sugar and/or MSG), canned salmon mashed up and scrambled with eggs, or anything I might have left over from dinner the night before (shrimp curry is fabulous after spending the night in the fridge). I would be careful with eating large amounts of cottage cheese, as it's a lot of dairy all at once and may possibly do bad things to your blood sugar.

Hope that helps!

### kathiebu@hotmail.com — 2012-08-12T16:30:44+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-much-food.2994/post-44521>

It's very diffeent from a person to another. I started the reset to stop the cravings and the binging, and I had to have 70 to 80g of protein! If I had "only" 50g, I would be hungry at night and you want to avoid that. You might have to try different amount of protein and see how you feel later that day.

I, too, was feeling a little nauseous when I started, but it quickly went away. Make sure you have plenty of fat with your BAB. To me, fat is more filling than protein. Make sure you have good quality bacon, CO, butter or coconut milk.

### Souldanzer — 2012-08-13T00:04:15+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-much-food.2994/post-44583>

Once I finally let go of my fear of food in the morning I went beyond 80grams lots of days..... I was hungry so I ate til done. My tummy sometimes wasn't so happy about it.

### bigknitwit — 2012-08-13T01:14:43+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-much-food.2994/post-44595>

I still don't ever feel like not eating anymore (but do feel like my stomach is full), often after 100+ grams of protein and fat (1500+ calories, tracked it once because I was curious). Often I finish my gigantic meal (1/2 pound fish, 1/4 burger too some mornings, plus the 1/4 cup or more coconut oil), and feel completely disappointed that I need to be done eating... Obviously my hormone satiety signalling is completely broken...

### Souldanzer — 2012-08-13T01:46:26+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-much-food.2994/post-44604>

> **bigknitwit;44904 wrote:** I still don't ever feel like not eating anymore (but do feel like my stomach is full), often after 100+ grams of protein and fat (1500+ calories, tracked it once because I was curious). Often I finish my gigantic meal (1/2 pound fish, 1/4 burger too some mornings, plus the 1/4 cup or more coconut oil), and feel completely disappointed that I need to be done eating... Obviously my hormone satiety signalling is completely broken...

I had exactly that for the longest time during re-feeding. Still have it sometimes. It's improving for me I think.

### bigknitwit — 2012-08-13T01:58:55+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-much-food.2994/post-44605>

I still shudder to think of the amount of food I packed in last Thanksgiving (before finding JK info), while I was intermittent fasting. I had fasted for 1.5 days before the big meal (made by me, all my favorite Thanksgiving foods). I was sooo stuffed, and yet everything still looked so appetizing. I am looking forward to making progress such that I can eat and food gradually loses its appeal as the meal progresses and finishes.

> **Souldanzer;44913 wrote:** I had exactly that for the longest time during re-feeding. Still have it sometimes. It's improving for me I think.

### primalminx — 2012-08-13T21:08:35+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-much-food.2994/post-44741>

I have found that if I eat in the morning, I am basically starved all day and can eat all day, this is not a good thing at all. Is doing a big protein shake ok?

### somewhatchubby — 2012-08-16T21:19:37+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-much-food.2994/post-45237>

Hi Ealachan, yes, that helps very much, thanks. I generally get over 5ogms but sometimes I feel less hungry. I ate at 6am this morning and didnt eat again until nearly 3 pm ,my stomach was growling a bit by then but I only ate a little.Tonight I could barely finish my dinner, which was shepherds pie, cauiflower mash and some broccoli. I ate the meat first !

I dont seem to have any carb cravings at all. I didnt eat a lot of them beforer I adopted this WOE.

Its true we're all different.

I was frustrated yesterday at the weekly weigh in that for two consecuative weeks I have put on 1 lb and now am back up to 11 1/2 stone where I started. I'm pressing on as I have felt much better, more energy and clearer headed.I'd really like to lose the 14lbs I feel is superfluous though.

### somewhatchubby — 2012-08-16T21:23:11+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-much-food.2994/post-45239>

AbetterME ,somtimes its the look of the food ,after I start to eat I am usually ok until I'm nearly finished. Sometimes I feel nauseaous for the best part of an hour and then it fades.

I have a lifetime of eating breakfast around 10.30 after the cows were milked. its a hard habit to break.

### somewhatchubby — 2012-08-16T21:25:06+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-much-food.2994/post-45241>

bigknitwig ,the less I eat the smaller my appetite gets. I suppose thats good ?

I'm generally around 1500 cals and I think someone is going to tell me its not enough ?

# Thread: Thyroid and BP
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/thyroid-and-bp.3003/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 25
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Destiny — 2012-08-13T04:00:48+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/thyroid-and-bp.3003/post-44618>

I have a question in regards to Cytomel and BP. I started Cytomel a week ago. My evening BP goes to 104/64 in last couple of days. I do feel a bit dizzy. I expected my BP to go up not down. Is this normal? Any ideas?

### bigknitwit — 2012-08-13T16:59:27+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/thyroid-and-bp.3003/post-44683>

Ughh - I've just been through literally a few months of this, and I can tell you it was really really difficult. Although my thyroid labs looked reasonable, my blood pressure just crashed and my heartrate went through the roof, and yes, there was lots of dizziness. Eventually I became almost non-functional - feeling like there wasn't enough blood getting into my brain. My worst symptoms were in the mornings, when BP would drop to 90ish/58ish, and heartrate would be above 100 resting. I felt very unwell. CT helped dramatically, but I found myself living in the cold to maintain some level of functionality (raised BP, dropped the heartrate). I had been adjusting my Armour dose upwards and wasn't tolerating the T3 very well, probably due to adrenal issues, or inadequate ferritin (at least that's what I'm hypothesizing). Finally, last week, I gave up, and returned to T4 only therapy. It took about 4 days for things start improving, but now, 6 days in, I am stabilizing. I will try again with T3 once I feel I've fully stabilized.

When do you take the T3? How much? Do you take T4 as well? Have you taken T3 before? How is your adrenal function, and iron levels? You may need to drop the dose lower and ease in slowly. It sounds like your adrenals are not tolerating the increased metabolic rate brought on by the T3 boost, but I'll be very interested to hear what others have to say, having just gone through this at the extreme.

### chocolate — 2012-08-13T20:58:21+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/thyroid-and-bp.3003/post-44738>

[http://www.healthboards.com/boards/thyroid-disorders/596343-critically-low-potassium-blood-pressure-drop.html](http://http://www.healthboards.com/boards/thyroid-disorders/596343-critically-low-potassium-blood-pressure-drop.html)

My daughter and I are hyper t and get those symptoms when we are low in potassium. Some doctors think you are high in sodium, when really you are low in potassium.

### Patty Cakes — 2012-08-14T03:00:54+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/thyroid-and-bp.3003/post-44805>

I learned when I started on T3 only meds that I need to take my BP and HR every hour to monitor what my body is doing. In the beginning my experience was that it increased my HR and BP about an hour after taking it and then it slowly lowered. I recently changed brands and it had a slightly different effect with lowered BP and normal HR. Fillers maybe? I heard people do have different reactions with different brands or generic versions.That is probably why it was recommended to me that I take vitals often. The BP will start to go up slightly when I need a dose. I have been on T3 only for 4-1/2 months now and still learning my optimal dose based on symptoms and what my body is doing. I raised the dose about every 7-14 days. I only have low adrenals and it sounds like you have a couple of things working against you. You will have to raise slowwwwwly. It's a process and takes time but you will make it through it.

**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/thyroid-and-bp.3003/post-44844>

> **Patty Cakes;45117 wrote:** I learned when I started on T3 only meds that I need to take my BP and HR every hour to monitor what my body is doing. I heard people do have different reactions with different brands or generic versions.That is probably why it was recommended to me that I take vitals often. The BP will start to go up slightly when I need a dose. I have been on T3 only for 4-1/2 months now and still learning my optimal dose based on symptoms and what my body is doing. I raised the dose about every 7-14 days. I only have low adrenals and it sounds like you have a couple of things working against you. You will have to raise slowwwwwly. It's a process and takes time but you will make it through it.

Patty, I have a bunch of questions if you don't mind. You have been on T3 for a while. Is there any limit for staying on it? What is the average time for you between doses? How many doses a day do you take? What was your starting daily dosage. What is your dosage now?

I read on thyromadness that Reverse T3 might get cleared up in 8 weeks. Then if the T3/RT3 ratio is good, is it a good idea to switch to natural des. thyroid?

### Patty Cakes — 2012-08-14T05:27:46+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/thyroid-and-bp.3003/post-44850>

Is there any limit for staying on it?

My understanding is that some people are on it for life. Some people cannot tolerate T4.

What is the average time for you between doses?

I take a dose at 3:30am (a slightly bigger dose), then one at 10, one at 3pm and I try to get another one in the evening or I just die at night! Some people, due to reasons I cannot remember, take a dose every three hours but I think that is rare.

What was your starting daily dosage? I was on 150 mg of Natural Thyroid when I switched to T3 so my starting dose will be different from yours. I started with 12.5 in the morning and two other doses of 6.25 each.

What is your dosage now? Now I take 72 mcg.

I believe it can take 8-12 weeks for rT3 to clear. Mine is clear now and my ratio is 44. Woo-hoo!!! So excited! I think the choice of T3 vs NDT is an individual one. I actually feel better on T3 in some ways and in other ways better on NDT. I'm still not sure what to do.

### Destiny — 2012-08-14T13:38:07+0300

Your ratio is amazing, Patty!! Time to celebrate. Thank you for all the details. It really helps to hear what others are doing. Especially from you with so much experience and knowledge.

It will be difficult to decide what to do next especially when you are doing good and feel good. It just sounds so good to give your body T1, T2, T3 and T4.

### bigknitwit — 2012-08-14T15:15:26+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/thyroid-and-bp.3003/post-44878>

May I jump in and ask a question? What is the reason to move to T3 only meds? To clear out elevated reverse T3? How do you go about figuring out why your got the elevated RT3?

I read somewhere that the hyper-like symptoms I was experiencing are more likely due to unsufficient cortisol levels. My hypothesis is that the body shunts everything it's got into making cortisol, and then falls short in trying to make other hormones, like aldosterone (which regulates electrolytes and blood pressure). Also, I read that supplementing sea salt helps because the body has a hard time holding onto it. I know I can't seem to eat enough salt.

### Patty Cakes — 2012-08-14T18:01:53+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/thyroid-and-bp.3003/post-44907>

> **bigknitwit;45200 wrote:** May I jump in and ask a question? What is the reason to move to T3 only meds? To clear out elevated reverse T3? How do you go about figuring out why your got the elevated RT3?
>
>
>
> I read somewhere that the hyper-like symptoms I was experiencing are more likely due to unsufficient cortisol levels. My hypothesis is that the body shunts everything it's got into making cortisol, and then falls short in trying to make other hormones, like aldosterone (which regulates electrolytes and blood pressure). Also, I read that supplementing sea salt helps because the body has a hard time holding onto it. I know I can't seem to eat enough salt.

T3 is the med of choice for clearing out the rT3. That is not the only reason to take it though. Some people cannot tolerate T4. Or some docs like to script T3 + T4, all synthetic. I'm sure there are other reasons for it too!

Testing is the best way to know if your aldosterone and renin need help. I believe that sea salt water does help with the aldosterone but I'm not positive. This is where the ASI test comes in. If you are on thyroid meds you can get hyper or hypo symptoms from either thyroid or from the cortisol. Always test to know for sure. I've seen people get the shakes with such low cortisol that the body couldn't handle the thyroid meds even though they were on a very low dose. Adrenals should always be fixed first....I learned this too late!

### Lyndra — 2012-08-14T23:49:56+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/thyroid-and-bp.3003/post-44951>

> **bigknitwit;45200 wrote:**
>
>
>
> I read somewhere that the hyper-like symptoms I was experiencing are more likely due to unsufficient cortisol levels. My hypothesis is that the body shunts everything it's got into making cortisol, and then falls short in trying to make other hormones, like aldosterone (which regulates electrolytes and blood pressure). Also, I read that supplementing sea salt helps because the body has a hard time holding onto it. I know I can't seem to eat enough salt.

I can say that after about a month of drinking salt water in the morning (1/2 tsp sea salt in 8 ounces water followed by another 12-16 oz of water), my crazy, non-stop thirst has abated. Used to be I would drink a full glass of water and feel just as thirsty as before I drank it. Also, my lightheadedness when I stand up is getting better. I haven't had my aldosterone retested but I suspect it has improved greatly. I've also made some other changes (started on low-dose Armour and increased iodine) so I can't way for sure the salt has done it, but I suspect it helped.

### Destiny — 2012-08-15T13:59:20+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/thyroid-and-bp.3003/post-45002>

I am back and doing really well on the Cytomel dosage of 6.25mcg twice a day. Is there a good way to tell WHEN I need to increase to 3 times a day?

### Patty Cakes — 2012-08-15T18:13:55+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/thyroid-and-bp.3003/post-45031>

I think you may feel hypo symptoms again. I took my vitals like a crazy person and found out for me that when my BP would start to go up I needed a dose. An hour after the dose my BP would go back down. I don't think that is normal but it is how my body reacted. Also my temp initially went up but then if I needed to up a dose, temp would get a little low. I started with three doses initially and have pretty much stuck with that but sometimes I add a smidge at night.

### Destiny — 2012-08-18T02:23:39+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/thyroid-and-bp.3003/post-45470>

> **Patty Cakes;45361 wrote:** I think you may feel hypo symptoms again. I took my vitals like a crazy person and found out for me that when my BP would start to go up I needed a dose. An hour after the dose my BP would go back down. I don't think that is normal but it is how my body reacted. Also my temp initially went up but then if I needed to up a dose, temp would get a little low. I started with three doses initially and have pretty much stuck with that but sometimes I add a smidge at night.

I think I am getting a hang of it! I take one dose at 6 AM and another between 2-3 PM. Today I felt a bit hypo since this morning. I decide to take a dose at noon. I also expected that I'll need another one in the afternoon but I didn't.

When I got home from work, I cleaned up the whole house! That is unheard of for Friday!! I used to just collapse to bed on Fridays.

### Patty Cakes — 2012-08-22T07:08:43+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/thyroid-and-bp.3003/post-46244>

So how are you doing now Destiny? Can you play with your dosing or is your doc micromanaging your T3?

### Destiny — 2012-08-22T13:30:26+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/thyroid-and-bp.3003/post-46257>

> **Patty Cakes;46629 wrote:** So how are you doing now Destiny? Can you play with your dosing or is your doc micromanaging your T3?

I certainly could play! My doctor gave me one dose of 25 mcg per day. The instruction on the bottle was to take it once a day which I did not. I knew better! So I still can go up another 12.5 mcg but still don't feel that I need it.

I just started my 3rd week on it, isn't it strange that I am still at 2 doses of 6.25mcg per day?

### Patty Cakes — 2012-08-22T19:31:26+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/thyroid-and-bp.3003/post-46364>

That is a very low dose for sure. But you need all kinds of internal support structures to be right before you can tolerate some of the other higher doses, like your iron and adrenals. Also right now you are using up any T4 that is there and may be converting to FT3. Once you clear the rT3 (you did have high rT3 didn't you?) many people find they need to lower the dose because their cells are no longer clogged with rT3 any more and can take up the T3 they couldn't before. I never experienced that. But I was on a T3 in the beginning that I realized after three months was weaker, had a filler that didn't agree with me or whatever, it just didn't work as good as the next batch (different brand). New batch has helped lower my HR which is great!

Good luck and remember use others advice as guideline not hard and fast rules. I try to take the lowest dose possible without sacrificing my total body and thyroid health.

# Thread: CT in the morning
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-in-the-morning.3006/>
**Subforum:** Cold Thermogenesis
**Posts:** 9
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### NWgirl — 2012-08-13T06:20:11+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-in-the-morning.3006/post-44640>

Does anyone else have problems doing CT in the morning or middle of the day? I've done this about 3 times after a BAB, it feels fine doing it but I get so tired after that it prevents me from doing things during the day. Yesterday I CT'ed for almost one hour then took a cold shower to wash. Afterward I warmed up in the sun. Sounds nice, right? But I felt kind of achy and had to take a nap. This has happened 3 times.

I guess when I CT after dinner and then go to bed I'm sleeping through this phase. Anyone know what this could be?

### ealachan — 2012-08-13T12:28:10+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-in-the-morning.3006/post-44656>

I believe it's because CT ramps up your body's DHEA production, which can make you sleepy? I think I remember reading that, anyway...if someone calls bollocks on it, then nevermind me...

### x_danny_x — 2012-08-13T17:28:08+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-in-the-morning.3006/post-44688>

CT helps you sleep. It is good to have a good night rest. If you going to do CT in the morning, I will advise you to work out a little bit to help keep you from sleeping. Or just do CT at night.

I put some music or bring laptop to watch shows to help pass the time. Or bring a radio to listen to whatever you like. Make it as enjoyable as possible.

### MartiD — 2012-08-13T18:17:22+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-in-the-morning.3006/post-44694>

I have the opposite effect. I always do a cold bath in the morning after a cold shower and have lots of energy for about an hour or two afterwards. Still sleep solid at night too!

### Qodesh — 2012-08-13T20:34:03+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-in-the-morning.3006/post-44731>

I just did my first morning CT. My tub is on the back screen porch here in South Florida. I had fatty beef bacon breakfast, took about a 5 minute soak in 50 degrees, then sat in the warm morning sunshine with my coffee.

It was beautiful and made the morning heat bearable.

Not tired so far and that was about 5 hours ago

### PrimalPam — 2012-08-13T21:20:01+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-in-the-morning.3006/post-44743>

Happens to me too, so I'm wondering if increased DHEA is the mechanism.

CT makes me sleep really well; on the days I do CT, I sleep 8 hours straight. Other days, I will wake up in the middle of the night and fall back asleep. But on CT days, sleep straight through.

If I do CT earlier than usual then yes, it seems to make me more tired earlier.

### ealachan — 2012-08-13T21:37:08+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-in-the-morning.3006/post-44749>

I take a 20-minute cold shower every morning that makes me feel almost hyper, but if I take one at night right before bed, it will definitely make me sleepy. Interesting.

### villjamur_stevenson — 2012-08-13T22:25:00+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-in-the-morning.3006/post-44760>

chalk me up in the energized category. I can just feel that norepinephrine getting released into my bloodstream.

My morning CT is usually the first thing I do - directly from bed to shower or bath.

### NWgirl — 2012-08-14T03:12:28+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-in-the-morning.3006/post-44811>

Wow it looks like about 50/50! I will have to attribute it to the DHEA, thanks for all of your replies!

# Thread: Too Much Calcium Could Cause Kidney, Heart Problems, Researchers Say
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/too-much-calcium-could-cause-kidney-heart-problems-researchers-say.3014/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 2
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### chocolate — 2012-08-13T19:50:55+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/too-much-calcium-could-cause-kidney-heart-problems-researchers-say.3014/post-44715>

[http://www.npr.org/blogs/health/2012/08/13/158506960/too-much-calcium-could-cause-kidney-heart-problems-researchers-say?](http://http://www.npr.org/blogs/health/2012/08/13/158506960/too-much-calcium-could-cause-kidney-heart-problems-researchers-say?)

> When it comes to a healthy diet â€” especially for women, and especially after menopause â€” nutritionists, doctors, everybody it seems, will tell you: calcium, calcium, calcium.
>
>
>
> Federal health officials recommend that women and men younger than 50 consume 1,000 milligrams of calcium per day. The recommendation goes up to 1,200 milligrams after age 70 for men and after menopause for women, when a major drop in estrogen causes bone loss.
>
>
>
> So, in a culture that often considers "more" to be "better," one might ask, if 1,200 milligrams of calcium is good, is 2,000 mg of calcium better?
>
>
>
>
>
> No, says Dr. Ethel C. Siris, director of the Toni Stabile Osteoporosis Center at Columbia University Medical Center in New York. "You need enough; you don't need extra," she says.
>
> "Extra calcium does you no good, and there is a small risk that if you take too much you might get a kidney stone," says Dr. Siris. That's because the body can only handle 600 milligrams of calcium at once. Extra calcium can build up in the bloodstream and, when excreted through kidneys in urine, it can cause a kidney stone.
>
>
>
> That's been known for a while. But recently, a few studies raised concern that excess calcium may also calcify coronary arteries in susceptible individuals and even precipitate heart attack.
>
>
>
>
>
> Robert Eckel, a cardiologist and endocrinologist at the University of Colorado, is a past president of the American Heart Association. While these studies are far from conclusive, and far more research needs to be done, he says they do raise the question about whether there's potentially some danger in over-the-counter calcium supplements that go beyond our usual dietary intake of calcium.
>
>
>
> "At this point in time, there's a bit of a signal" that should raise caution but remains highly controversial. "I don't think anyone has stepped up to say calcium supplements should be abandoned," says Eckel.
>
>
>
> That's particularly because calcium is so critical for bone health. The best plan of action, says Siris: Eat more calcium-rich foods.
>
>
>
> "If you're somebody who has a glass of milk with breakfast, that's 300 milligrams of calcium; a container of yogurt will give you another 200 to 300 milligrams; a couple of ounces of cheese will give you 200 to 300 milligrams," she says. For most healthy adults younger than 50, that's about all you need for bone health.
>
>
>
> If you don't eat dairy, Siris says there are plenty of other foods that also contain calcium. These include vegetables (like broccoli, bok choy and turnip greens), oranges, figs, salmon and sardines. Cereals and soy milk often have added calcium, along with added vitamin D, which is essential to help the body absorb calcium.
>
>
>
> So, estimate your daily intake of calcium from food, says Siris, and then calculate whether you need to take an extra supplement. You may just need 300 or 600 milligrams of calcium extra, and you may not even need that every day.

### Joann — 2012-08-13T23:55:47+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/too-much-calcium-could-cause-kidney-heart-problems-researchers-say.3014/post-44773>

Finally! Its scary how the mainstream push calcium alone.

# Thread: Reverse Osmosis Water
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/reverse-osmosis-water.3031/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 8
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### trishalinn@gmail.com — 2012-08-14T06:37:28+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/reverse-osmosis-water.3031/post-44857>

I'm not sure this is the right forum for this, but here I go!

My mom was referred to a Physical Therapist by her doctor, but more because the physical therapist is big into natural health and really good apparently at working with people to help their overall well-being. THe doctor was able to make the referral based on my mom's shoulder and elbow problems she's been having, but told her this PT would be great to work with surrounding a number of issues including nutrition etc.

The PT it sounds like had a LOT of great things to say to my mom. Eat real foods, get rid of chemicals, etc. One of the thing he asked about was where she lived and when she told him, he shook his head and said that the area has some of the worst water and that it has something in it that is causing a lot of sickness and problems for people in the area. I don't know if he specified something in particular, or just said "something" but he was very emphatic that if my mom only made one change as a result of their appointment it was that she stop drinking the local water.

He recommended getting an RO water system. Thoughts on this? I've never thought much about my water (probably a mistake), but I am now since I live in the same area. I'm kinda wondering what poison lurks in the water my kids and I drink every day! Anyhow, what do you think about RO water? Does it not strip all of the minerals from the water? If you use RO water do you remineralize it? If so, how? Do you have a system you recommend?

### KiwiLauren — 2012-08-14T07:42:36+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/reverse-osmosis-water.3031/post-44860>

Hi Tricia. Here is a very helpful podcast to listen to. Good luck. Lauren

[http://www.blogtalkradio.com/undergroundwellness/2010/08/05/whats-in-your-water-with-david-getoff](http://http://www.blogtalkradio.com/undergroundwellness/2010/08/05/whats-in-your-water-with-david-getoff)

### kathylu — 2012-08-14T13:41:32+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/reverse-osmosis-water.3031/post-44866>

Yes! It's very, very important to detoxify your water. I have a whole house water filter, which removes about 95% of voc's (volatile organic chemicals), chlorine and flourine. That's because a lot of chlorine ingestion occurs in the shower or bath by breathing in water vapor, so I really wanted to get rid of it. Then I have a reverse osmosis filter under the kitchen sink to filter the water we use for drinking and cooking. Yes, it does essentially completely demineralize your water, so you can remineralize with a pinch of celtic sea salt per glass. I actually don't worry about it much, because I use a lot of sea salt in cooking each day. A nice side benefit is that my water tastes great! You don't realize how bad those chemical contaminants make the water taste until you remove them.

### Michael — 2012-08-14T15:20:18+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/reverse-osmosis-water.3031/post-44879>

> **Trisha;45171 wrote:** He recommended getting an RO water system. Thoughts on this?
>
>
> AFAIK, pretty wasteful.
>
>
>
> My understanding was that you put as much water down the drain as you used with these. But it seems I may have been underestimnating the wastage. I did a quick search online to try to quantify that and found a Candian company that claims theirs _only_ wastes as much water as you use (!) and others are far worse:
>
>
>
> ... Every R.O. System will produce waste water. Where other R.O. systems have a waste water to purified water ratio of 7:1 (7 parts waste to 1 part purified water) Aquasafe’s ratio’s ...

[http://www.aquasafecanada.com/f-a-q-s/#Waste%20Water%20produced%20by%20R.O%20Systems](http://http://www.aquasafecanada.com/f-a-q-s/#Waste%20Water%20produced%20by%20R.O%20Systems)

I guess that's not really ecologically sound. And potentially costly, too, if you're doing that to water that you wash your clothes and floors and so on with (that is, if your water company meters consumption).

The lady from New Zealand links a podcast by David Getoff. IIRC, he has a darn great hopper of activated charcoal attached to the side of his house and lets the water drip through that. It's apparently highly effective -- doesn't sound practicable for most people, though. I think David has a range of suggested solutions you can look at, depending on what your aims are and how much you're prepared to spend, and that podcast would be very much worth listening to. He's a fan of removable carbon filters that can clip over a shower head -- they come pretty cheap and you can carry one around with you, if you're going to be staying in a hotel or something.

I'd research the different solutions out there for a while and try to cost the different options, including the necessary periodic maintenance and so on.

### Patty Cakes — 2012-08-14T18:13:21+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/reverse-osmosis-water.3031/post-44910>

> **kathylu;45181 wrote:** Yes! It's very, very important to detoxify your water. I have a whole house water filter, which removes about 95% of voc's (volatile organic chemicals), chlorine and flourine. That's because a lot of chlorine ingestion occurs in the shower or bath by breathing in water vapor, so I really wanted to get rid of it. Then I have a reverse osmosis filter under the kitchen sink to filter the water we use for drinking and cooking. Yes, it does essentially completely demineralize your water, so you can remineralize with a pinch of celtic sea salt per glass. I actually don't worry about it much, because I use a lot of sea salt in cooking each day. A nice side benefit is that my water tastes great! You don't realize how bad those chemical contaminants make the water taste until you remove them.

I'm with you and have the same gig set up in our house. The water tastes great considering our water here tastes like dirt; IMO, completely undrinkable. In the old house we had just a filter and the water still tasted like dirt so the RO system was a must. I do add a drop of minerals back into the water and my micronutrient test came back great in all the minerals tested so I'm glad I remineralize a little. For me the question was "Is the water doing harm to me in its current state" and if the answer was yes then something had to change.

### trishalinn@gmail.com — 2012-08-14T18:21:40+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/reverse-osmosis-water.3031/post-44912>

Any good recommendations on an RO system? Looked at consumer reports and it appears they all have major problems - leaking, no indicator for when the filter needs changed, costly frequent service calls. I ont mind the initial investment, but I want a quality system.

### Michael — 2012-08-16T11:08:00+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/reverse-osmosis-water.3031/post-45175>

I guess any piece of equipment for any purpose is only as good as it should be for as long as its properly maintained. So the regular maintenance is necessary; otherwise, one might as well not bother having the equipment

I suppose you can change a filter on a time basis or the basis of how much passes through it, which you can calculate. So I can't see that not having a built-in indicator is a big one. The rest of it's a poser.

I get the impression from listening to David Getoff talk that cleaning your water is very much a case of _caveat emptor. If you listen to the salesman, a system will remove this that and the other. As soon as you ask for measurements_ to show that -- let alone independent lab results -- you're likely to draw a blank. It's not perhaps that they mean to lie, but it's a case of someone told someone told someone that a system could do such and such, and that's the basis they're often going on, and selling is really their main interest.

IIRC, Getoff says that in truth only really high-quality burnt bone will get out some things. (He actually in-lines several different filters, depending on what he wants the water for, to deal with several different types of pollutants himself.) It seems to come down to how far do you want to go and how much are you prepared to pay?

I'm also kind of reminded of Jack's attitude to supplements -- you can't really know what's going on unless you take some measurements. It'd be interesting to know what is in your own particular water supply; so you know what you might want to remove. And again, it would be interesting to take measurements on the water after it's been through a system, so you know if it in fact did. Of course, you're limited to knowing about what the lab has thought to measure or is set up to measure ...

One of Sean Croxton's guests said recently that she will usually only drink good quality mineral water. (Note, AFAIK, the term "spring water" , which you find on much bottled water, is not controlled at law and means nothing at all.) I guess if it's bottled in glass and has slowly percolated down through several hundred feet of rock, that's probably as good as anything.

I guess you pays your money and you takes your choice.

### kathylu — 2012-08-16T13:42:58+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/reverse-osmosis-water.3031/post-45181>

> **Trisha;45234 wrote:** Any good recommendations on an RO system? Looked at consumer reports and it appears they all have major problems - leaking, no indicator for when the filter needs changed, costly frequent service calls. I ont mind the initial investment, but I want a quality system.

I use Star Water Systems reverse osmosis system. We are satisfied with it. It really doesn't require much maintenance. I change the filter at the suggested intervals and the RO membrane lasts several years. Every so often you will need to aid air to the tank to keep the pressure up so the water will flow easily (I use a bicycle pump). Also, if you are handy at all, the system is not difficult to install yourself.

I have seen reverse osmosis filter systems available for as little as $275. Minor cost for better water in my opinion.


══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# Thread: Short follicular phase (8-11 days); 14 day luteal phase, trying to conceive
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/short-follicular-phase-8-11-days-14-day-luteal-phase-trying-to-conceive.3034/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 3
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### thyme — 2012-08-14T16:38:53+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/short-follicular-phase-8-11-days-14-day-luteal-phase-trying-to-conceive.3034/post-44895>

We started the baby-making attempt thing in the beginning of this year, which means we made a few changes in January/February:

1) I had my copper IUD removed in January.

2) My partner had his vasectomy reversed in January.

3) I started cold thermogenesis, etc. in February; circadian, optimal, etc followed. Had already been eating Paleo for a year.

My cycles with the IUD were extremely regular, lunar 28 day cycles. My first two cycles after the IUD was removed were crazy; after that, I settled into progressively shorter 25-21 day cycles. I'm taking daily BBT and CM readings, and so I know that I'm losing time from my follicular phase and that my luteal phase is a constant, healthy 14 days.

I can't find much information about short follicular phases on the web, or in Taking Charge of Your Fertility, or in any of the other books I've consulted, but I don't think it's great for fertility. My very light menstrual period supports the notion that my uterus isn't getting much time to develop a lining before ovulation.

On the Women BBT thread, Mamagrok said her period had gotten lighter, with days lost from the follicular end as well. Has anyone else had this happen?

And does anyone have any suggestions for a) what's going on, and b) what, if anything, to do?

### L8F — 2012-08-19T03:41:12+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/short-follicular-phase-8-11-days-14-day-luteal-phase-trying-to-conceive.3034/post-45648>

How long have you been CTing? I think that it can really mess with the cycle.

### kathylu — 2012-08-19T07:47:17+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/short-follicular-phase-8-11-days-14-day-luteal-phase-trying-to-conceive.3034/post-45664>

Yup. Take 2 mg of oral estradiol per day starting on day 3 of your cycle. By ovulation at day 14-15 you will have a lush, cushy lining. You'll have to get your doc on board for this...you'll need a script.

# Thread: Raised Testosterone and Vitamin D (Male)
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/raised-testosterone-and-vitamin-d-male.3040/>
**Subforum:** Success Stories
**Posts:** 5
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Huck — 2012-08-14T22:53:04+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/raised-testosterone-and-vitamin-d-male.3040/post-44947>

I posted this originally in the thread [Sunbathing ups mens testosterone](http://http://forum.jackkruse.com/showthread.php?2329-Sunbathing-ups-mens-testosterone), but I think it is important enough to post here.

Guys, if you are having trouble raising your testosterone, get out in the sun while there is still time this year.

Starting around July 4, on sunny days, I tried to spend 1-2 hours in the sun, most days without a shirt. On some days, I just put the top down on the car and went for a long drive. I never burned and actually got where I craved being in the sun during the hottest part of the day. I just spent 4 hours in the sun today.

In the past, I was unable to raise testosterone or vitamin d with just the sun, but figured it was worth trying again. The results were absolutely amazing.

Results:

\[April 2012 . . . . . Aug 2012

Testosterone Serum . . . . 497 . . . . . . . . 714 (348-1197) (No dhea, 7-keto, pregnenolone, or testosterone during July)

Vitamin D . . . . . . . . . . . 68.6 . . . . . . . . 111 (30-100) (supplemented with 15,000 IU vit d)

I have been taking 15,000 iu of vit d for a couple of years and could only get vit d up to 68.6. Just 1 month of sunshine maxed it out.

I think this shows 2 things. The 1st is the most important - my body is starting to work again. HPA axis seems to be working. The 2nd is that sunshine is extremely important for more than just circadian rhythm. My IGF-1 was also at top of range and I wonder if the sun affected that as well. First time I tested IGF-1, so have no idea what it was in the past.

Twice in my life I have lived at the beach and I think I need to move there one more time where I can walk in the sand and sun, swim in the water, and eat fresh seafood every day.

### fitness@home — 2012-08-15T03:50:39+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/raised-testosterone-and-vitamin-d-male.3040/post-44985>

Huck,

That is a significant increase in both! Good for you.

My husband is currently on a testosterone, HCG, and anastrozole regimen for his "Low T". I am going to ask him to read your post. He definitely doesn't spend enough time outside in the sun.

Thanks for posting your results...

### shilohman — 2012-08-15T03:59:05+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/raised-testosterone-and-vitamin-d-male.3040/post-44989>

Woot, way to go Huck.

### Destiny — 2012-08-15T13:37:05+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/raised-testosterone-and-vitamin-d-male.3040/post-44999>

This is great, Huck. Alerting my husband to this right now!

### Martin — 2012-08-31T23:08:32+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/raised-testosterone-and-vitamin-d-male.3040/post-48632>

Great inspiration, Huck. I just dropped everything and jumped in the tub.

My T (free and bound) is in the crapper and need to get on it.

Go for my D update soon. I didn't spend nearly enough time down in the garden/sun this year. This test will be revealing.

I checked my IGF-1 and it is very low. Good to hear your's ain't!!!

### JanSz — 2012-08-24T16:52:07+0300

> **Sem;47045 wrote:** Yes, except I also asked for anti thyroid antibodies but I don't think they did that?

Testing thyroid always check antibodies, specially now, knowing that ANA is positive.

\=================

DHEA 188.24. Range 34.50-568.92

Estradiol 12.0 post menopausal is 0-32.3

FSH is 62.3 and post menopausal is 23.0-116.3

Progesterone e with SLE have a positive ANA test result. If someone also has symptoms of SLE, such as arthritis, a rash, and autoimmune thrombocytopenia, then she probably has SLE. In cases such as these, a positive ANA result can be useful to support SLE diagnosis. Two subset tests for specific types of autoantibodies, such as anti-dsDNA and anti-SM, may be ordered (often as an ENA panel) to help confirm that the condition is SLE.

A positive ANA can also mean that the person has drug-induced lupus. This condition is associated with the development of autoantibodies to histones, which are water-soluble proteins rich in the amino acids lysine and arginine. An anti-histone test may be ordered to support the diagnosis of drug-induced lupus.

Other conditions in which a positive ANA test result may be seen include:

SjÃ¶gren syndrome: Between 40% and 70% of those with this condition have a positive ANA test result. While this finding supports the diagnosis, a negative result does not rule it out. The doctor may want to test for two subsets of ANA: Anti-SS-A (Ro) and Anti-SS-B (La). About 90% or more of people with SjÃ¶gren syndrome have autoantibodies to SSA.

Scleroderma (systemic sclerosis): About 60% to 90% of those with scleroderma have a positive ANA finding. In people who may have this condition, ANA subset tests can help distinguished two forms of the disease, limited versus diffuse. The diffuse form is more severe. Limited disease is most closely associated with the anticentromere pattern of ANA staining (and the anticentromere test), while the diffuse form is associated with autoantibodies to the anti–Scl-70.

A positive result on the ANA also may show up in people with Raynaud's disease, rheumatoid arthritis, dermatomyositis or polymyosis, mixed connective tissue disease, and other autoimmune conditions. For more on these conditions, visit the American Autoimmune Related Diseases Association patient information page.

A doctor must rely on test results, clinical symptoms, and the person's history for diagnosis. Because symptoms may come and go, it may take months or years to show a pattern that might suggest SLE or any of the other autoimmune diseases.

A negative ANA result makes SLE an unlikely diagnosis. It usually is not necessary to immediately repeat a negative ANA test; however, due to the episodic nature of autoimmune diseases, it may be worthwhile to repeat the ANA test at a future date if symptoms persist.

Aside from rare cases, further autoantibody (subset) testing is not necessary if a person has a negative ANA result.

# Thread: Name that schrooom!
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/name-that-schrooom.3123/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 15
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### jeanie@truelongmont.com — 2012-08-20T19:30:42+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/name-that-schrooom.3123/post-45868>

I dared to venture to my lame attempt at a compost pile today. Found a boatload of these - any ideas? Seems like mushrooms could be something one just grows. Anybody done this? I want to - but as Lyndra can attest I'm fairly garden clueless.

{ "lightbox\_close": "Close", "lightbox\_next": "Next", "lightbox\_previous": "Previous", "lightbox\_error": "The requested content cannot be loaded. Please try again later.", "lightbox\_start\_slideshow": "Start slideshow", "lightbox\_stop\_slideshow": "Stop slideshow", "lightbox\_full\_screen": "Full screen", "lightbox\_thumbnails": "Thumbnails", "lightbox\_download": "Download", "lightbox\_share": "Share", "lightbox\_zoom": "Zoom", "lightbox\_new\_window": "New window", "lightbox\_toggle\_sidebar": "Toggle sidebar" }

### Souldanzer — 2012-08-20T19:45:50+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/name-that-schrooom.3123/post-45877>

I have no idea about mushrooms and it's one of those things where i believe erring on the side of caution is best.

These look a lot like the kind that a friend of mine regularly picks all over the place - they grow in moist places like recently heavily watered lawns. She will pick them as we walk (our dogs) and then either eat them right away or cook them at home or dry them for winter use.

Don't try eating them b/c I say they might be edible..... my friend grew up in eastern Europe and that's how they got part of their food so she knows what she's doing since she was a little child.

### vkiernan — 2012-08-20T20:32:39+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/name-that-schrooom.3123/post-45889>

Never eat a wild mushroom that "you" did not start growing unless you are sure what it is. The are that poisonous as far as I know. At least that is the fear my parents put into us kids back then. You can buy kits to grow your own if you'd like to start. The only wild schroom I eat is a morel from my back woods. Everything else comes from the grocery store.

### Lyndra — 2012-08-20T20:51:21+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/name-that-schrooom.3123/post-45893>

> **Cavemam;46252 wrote:** I dared to venture to my lame attempt at a compost pile today. Found a boatload of these - any ideas? Seems like mushrooms could be something one just grows. Anybody done this? I want to - but as Lyndra can attest I'm fairly garden clueless.

You can definitely grow your own but as vkiernan said, you need to start out with known specimens. I have a friend who recently collected 10 pounds of wild mushrooms but she knows what she's doing. I would never eat a mushroom that I just found somewhere; too much risk. A friend's dog was poisoned by eating backyard mushrooms (and sadly, died).

### Shijin13 — 2012-08-22T17:29:53+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/name-that-schrooom.3123/post-46311>

I remember going mushroom picking w/my biotony teacher at USAFA - he was a fungiologist by trade - he did the actual picking - we didn't I clearly remember he said never to eat wild mushrooms, unless you're with an expert such as himself. HE also did provide fair warning that at time even experts can get some shrooms confused. stick w/what you can buy in the store - or what you can grow you're self from a "Starter kit"

### jeanie@truelongmont.com — 2012-08-22T17:50:47+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/name-that-schrooom.3123/post-46316>

Well, Y'all scared me enough to not eat them. But they look so lovely!!! And how cool would it be to have my own mushroom patch. I wonder if there is a way to "test" or an expert I can consult. Hmmmmmmm.

I am thinking of planting a blueberry bush in that spot - although I'm not sure what would grow. It's only $10, so what the hell. This spot is where I tossed "compostable" stuff for a couple of years with no idea wtf I was doing. It turned into a big moldy pile that none of us dare to approach. I will also need to buy a big sturdy steel rake and a shovel.

### Souldanzer — 2012-08-22T18:22:04+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/name-that-schrooom.3123/post-46331>

Just FYI, blueberries don't grow in our neck of woods unless you get very lucky to have an unusually acid spot of dirt. We're really alkaline in CO..... blueberries like it acidic. YOu can create such a spot in your garden if you desire but it does take some upkeep b/c the soil will keep getting too alkaline for blueberries to thrive.

### SeaHorse — 2012-08-22T19:29:58+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/name-that-schrooom.3123/post-46362>

If you can get pine needles...they will acidify the soil, they are used as mulch for blueberries.

We have a lot of woods on our property and this fall I'm getting a mushroom expert to come and forage. He will get most of the mushrooms this year to sell, but I will get a free education and the knowledge to be safe. This guy is amazing, he sells mushrooms at the farmer's market that he harvests for free....no tricky ones though...only things like Hen of the Woods, Morels, Oyster, Chanterelles etc.

Cavemam...you could take your mushrooms to the nearest Botany dept at a University...they would love to id them for you.

### Lyndra — 2012-08-22T19:41:18+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/name-that-schrooom.3123/post-46371>

> **Cavemam;46705 wrote:** Well, Y'all scared me enough to not eat them. But they look so lovely!!! And how cool would it be to have my own mushroom patch. I wonder if there is a way to "test" or an expert I can consult. Hmmmmmmm.
>
>
>
> I am thinking of planting a blueberry bush in that spot - although I'm not sure what would grow. It's only $10, so what the hell. This spot is where I tossed "compostable" stuff for a couple of years with no idea wtf I was doing. It turned into a big moldy pile that none of us dare to approach. I will also need to buy a big sturdy steel rake and a shovel.

Step away from the shrooms. Seriously. If you want to grow them yourself, buy a kit. I've done it and it's quite fun.

Regarding blueberries, Soul is right, even with adding stuff to acidify the soil, they aren't likely to grow or thrive because of the alkaline soil. You may have noticed a number of trees, especially maples, that are chlorotic (green veins with yellow leaves). The alkaline soil prevents them from taking up iron and there's just no way to effectively change the soil. Grow something that does well here - blackberries or raspberries. It's more in tune with nature, anyway.

### Michael — 2012-08-22T19:47:56+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/name-that-schrooom.3123/post-46375>

I think the best guide is likely to be this one:

[http://www.amazon.com/Mushrooms-Other-Fungi-North-America/dp/155407651X/](http://http://www.amazon.com/Mushrooms-Other-Fungi-North-America/dp/155407651X/)

A lot of the older guides have watercolours, not always very well executed, and they could be just about anything. Roger Phillips uses photographs and does them well.

They look rather like the pictures of _Russala_ in his guide for Great Britain and Europe, which I have, though what's around in North America I don't know. There are dozens of Russala, some of which are edible and some not.

### Kiera2 — 2012-08-23T09:55:08+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/name-that-schrooom.3123/post-46497>

I agree, if you want to pick your own mushrooms, then grow your own from a kit.

[http://www.abc.net.au/news/2012-06-08/woman-dies-after-eating-death-cap-mushrooms/4061254](http://http://www.abc.net.au/news/2012-06-08/woman-dies-after-eating-death-cap-mushrooms/4061254)

### Dali Dula — 2012-08-23T15:01:41+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/name-that-schrooom.3123/post-46512>

There are some easily recognized mushrooms that are not easily confused with toxics. Chanterelles,coprinus. Most that grow on wood are safe. Don't mess with LBMs Little brown mushroms. They are difficult to identify and there are so many different kinds. There are some good field guides available as apps for ipod. Don't eat anything unless you are 100% sure what it is. If you are really interested there is probably a mushroom hunting club near you. These folks organize forays and probably know their stuff. The kits usually work well and are fun.

### caroline — 2012-08-24T01:53:06+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/name-that-schrooom.3123/post-46620>

> **Coriander;46895 wrote:** I agree, if you want to pick your own mushrooms, then grow your own from a kit.
>
>
>
> [http://www.abc.net.au/news/2012-06-08/woman-dies-after-eating-death-cap-mushrooms/4061254](http://http://www.abc.net.au/news/2012-06-08/woman-dies-after-eating-death-cap-mushrooms/4061254)

Sadly - didn't some folks die from mushrooms they had picked last Christmas/New years? - in your area?

### Kiera2 — 2012-08-24T02:55:43+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/name-that-schrooom.3123/post-46625>

In Canberra, a New Year's Eve party. Two of four people who ate the mushrooms died. Apparently the deathcap resembles a traditional chinese mushroom.

### jeanie@truelongmont.com — 2012-08-24T04:25:39+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/name-that-schrooom.3123/post-46641>

# Thread: Comparing Nautropaths
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/comparing-nautropaths.3128/>
**Subforum:** Educating Doctors
**Posts:** 3
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### stephaniesherie — 2012-08-20T21:44:23+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/comparing-nautropaths.3128/post-45906>

So I love my Nautropathic Dr.'s! They rock.

I noticed a MAJOR difference in philosophie and would love some feedback other than what is in my own head.

1)Relies on doing the right testing (salivia, blood, etc.) and supports it w/muscle testing. Gives more nutritional supplements vs. herbs

2) uses this ZYTO bio feedback maching - which gives different results from blood tests (at least it did for me) and is used to suggest supplements based on the results of the biofeedback scan.

Any thoughts on these two approaches? I can't ask the Dr.'s cuze each thinks they are right and the other is wrong.

### Zophie — 2012-08-21T18:54:08+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/comparing-nautropaths.3128/post-46061>

My doctor is definitely in the first category. He also starts off by finding the miasim and takes that into account when suggesting herbs and supplements. I really love how I can muscle test something and then have the blood work show the exact same thing..... Amazing!

Bio feedback doesn't work on me either.

### stephaniesherie — 2012-08-21T19:41:52+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/comparing-nautropaths.3128/post-46079>

> **Zophie;46446 wrote:** My doctor is definitely in the first category. He also starts off by finding the miasim and takes that into account when suggesting herbs and supplements. I really love how I can muscle test something and then have the blood work show the exact same thing..... Amazing!
>
>
>
> Bio feedback doesn't work on me either.

HI Zophie,

Yes the bio feedback was WAY off - suggesting take supplements that contain the very thing the blood test showed me as SEVERE intolerant less than two weeks prior. SCARY! I felt like it was a way to push supplements unethically.

# Thread: Struggling with T3
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/struggling-with-t3.3137/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 20
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### BJK77 — 2012-08-21T03:06:56+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/struggling-with-t3.3137/post-45965>

Both my current doctor (Dr. K said to consider) in my last consult have said I need T3. I started it several weeks ago, but messed up and added it with my Naturethroid instead of replacing the NT. I had labs run and ended up HYPER with very high T3 levels. I ended up stopping the T3 and just staying on NT because I was feeling pretty lousy, had major cold sensitivity and insomnia. Found out later that it was totally my fault because I was taking them together.

I've read the dosing info on Stop The Thyroid Madness website, but still feel unsure about how to proceed. I did start the T3 again a few days ago (without the NT) and have to say I'm already feeling like going back to the NT. I actually had been feeling pretty decent on it, but my labs still sucked. On the T3 I'm getting the major fatigue back along with more cold sensitivity, brain fog and just a general rage/irritable feeling.

Any tips/suggestions on figuring out the T3 doses? My doctor gave me no guidelines at all - just said start T3! Not much help! I was taking 25 mcg when I wake (around 6 AM usually) and another 25 mcg before bed, but after reading STTM I'm trying to take smaller doses when I feel the fatigue/brain fog kicking in. Today I took the normal 25 mcg when I woke and then 12.5 mcg around 1 PM and another 12.5 mcg around 5 PM and 3 hours later I still feel absolutely exhausted. I haven't been able to get my butt off the couch since I got home from work  The last few weeks prior to starting T3 I've had more energy than I had in the last few years. I'm sure this is what I need and I just need to figure out the right dose it just stinks that I have to go backwards first.

### shilohman — 2012-08-21T04:39:35+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/struggling-with-t3.3137/post-45991>

Sounds like your T3 is being converted to rT3. What is your cortisol level? Anything going on that is causing you to be stressed?

### finnite@dccnet.com — 2012-08-21T04:49:23+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/struggling-with-t3.3137/post-45995>

generally 1 grain of NDT = 25 mg of T3 in terms of effectiveness so if you were taking 2 grains of NDT you would look at taking 50 mg of T3. how much NDT were you on?

when you take the T3 as you are right now how is your pulse etc? if you are not displaying hyper symptoms and you doc is okay with the overall level of dosing you can consider adding in another 12.5 mg at the 5 pm mark.

alternatively there are those such as Owl who take both NDT and T3 but the amount of NDT is smaller than previous such as 1/2 grain NDT and 12.5 mg of T3 upon rising then T3 for the rest of the day dosing every 4-5 hours until bedtime. (this isn\`t Owl\`s dosing btw but rather a reflection of things I\`ve read)

a good spot for learning about thyroid dosing are thyroid forums such as realthyroidhelp dot com.

### BJK77 — 2012-08-21T18:20:27+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/struggling-with-t3.3137/post-46053>

> **shilohman;46375 wrote: Sounds like your T3 is being converted to rT3. What is your cortisol level? Anything going on that is causing you to be stressed?
>
>
> When I last tested my rT3 when I was taking both the NT and Cytomel (and was HYPER), my rT3 was low so that wasn't the case then. I'll have it re-tested in about 2 weeks to see what's happening now with just the Cytomel.
>
>
>
> My cortisol is off a bit, but not as bad as I had expected. Somewhat low in the morning, I guess, but I've been very good lately about lights out at sunset and early to bed, early to rise. I just had it tested last month:
>
>
>
> Cortisol Morning: 6.1 (Range 3.7-9.5)
>
> Cortisol Noon: 3.0 (Range 1.2-3.0)
>
> Cortisol Evening: 1.5 (Range 0.6-1.9)
>
> Cortisol Night: 0.4 (Range 0.4-1.0)
>
>
>
> ColleenM;46379 wrote:** generally 1 grain of NDT = 25 mg of T3 in terms of effectiveness so if you were taking 2 grains of NDT you would look at taking 50 mg of T3. how much NDT were you on?
>
>
>
> when you take the T3 as you are right now how is your pulse etc? if you are not displaying hyper symptoms and you doc is okay with the overall level of dosing you can consider adding in another 12.5 mg at the 5 pm mark.
>
>
>
> alternatively there are those such as Owl who take both NDT and T3 but the amount of NDT is smaller than previous such as 1/2 grain NDT and 12.5 mg of T3 upon rising then T3 for the rest of the day dosing every 4-5 hours until bedtime. (this isn\`t Owl\`s dosing btw but rather a reflection of things I\`ve read)
>
>
>
> a good spot for learning about thyroid dosing are thyroid forums such as realthyroidhelp dot com.

I was taking 1 grain of NT. I read on the STTM site that there's only 9 mcg of T3 in one grain of NT, so I'm assuming that's all I was getting. I don't seem to notice any changes in my pulse either way - with/without the extra T3, even when my labs showed my hyper.

I was feeling better with the combo of NT + T3, but it scares me that I went hyper. And during the last webinar I asked Dr K about that and he said it's not a good idea to mix the two. I have another consult with him in about a week and a half so I'm sure I'll get some clear answers then . . . the question is whether I'll manage until then! I just feel unbelievably drained of all life. I have so much to do and just can't get moving. Thank goodness I don't have to work today, but the rest of the week is super busy and I'm not sure I'll be able to function!

### Patty Cakes — 2012-08-21T19:31:45+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/struggling-with-t3.3137/post-46076>

This is just my personal experience with transitioning from NDT to T3. I wanted to start out slow. There are 9 mcg of T3 in each 1 grain NDT pill (plus the T4). I was on 2.5 grains so I started with approximately that amount of T3 (2.5 x 9 = 22mcg). My first dose was 12.5mcg in the morning then another dose of 6.25 and then another at 6.25 for a daily total of 25. I took my heart rate, BP, and temp almost every hour in the first couple of weeks to check dosage and watch for hyper symptoms. If you are clearing rT3 you definitely do not want to go hyper. Remember you still have some T4 in your system for approx 6-8 weeks due to its half life that may convert to T3.

I slowly raised to a dose of 85 mcg over 4 months. You raise based on the statistics of your vitals. I tried to stay lower in the beginning to clear as much of the rT3 I could and get the T4 converted (hopefully) to T3. I now have a rT3 ratio of 41 so I have accomplished the goal of ridding my body of excess rT3. I will go back to NDT after I get my ASI test and if my adrenals are OK.

I have heard many people mixing the NDT + T3 with good results although personally I am not sure that this is a good idea. Most do it with T3 in the morning and then NDT in the afternoon. I don't think anyone I've heard of has mixed the two in one dose. Maybe that was what Dr. K was referring to.

### finnite@dccnet.com — 2012-08-21T19:32:19+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/struggling-with-t3.3137/post-46077>

hyper based on labs and not symptoms sounds like an RT3 issue, in other words you are showing hyper because the exogenous t3 is not being used. if you havent already go to rt3-thyroid.com and review the information there on treating RT3 with T3. the site is fairly comprehensive and covers symptoms, dosing etc

### freshveggies — 2012-08-21T19:35:30+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/struggling-with-t3.3137/post-46078>

> **Patty Cakes;46461 wrote:** This is just my personal experience with transitioning from NDT to T3. I wanted to start out slow. There are 9 mcg of T3 in each 1 grain NDT pill (plus the T4). I was on 2.5 grains so I started with approximately that amount of T3 (2.5 x 9 = 22mcg). My first dose was 12.5mcg in the morning then another dose of 6.25 and then another at 6.25 for a daily total of 25. I took my heart rate, BP, and temp almost every hour in the first couple of weeks to check dosage and watch for hyper symptoms. If you are clearing rT3 you definitely do not want to go hyper. Remember you still have some T4 in your system for approx 6-8 weeks due to its half life that may convert to T3.
>
>
>
> I slowly raised to a dose of 85 mcg over 4 months. You raise based on the statistics of your vitals. I tried to stay lower in the beginning to clear as much of the rT3 I could and get the T4 converted (hopefully) to T3. I now have a rT3 ratio of 41 so I have accomplished the goal of ridding my body of excess rT3. I will go back to NDT after I get my ASI test and if my adrenals are OK.
>
>
>
> I have heard many people mixing the NDT + T3 with good results although personally I am not sure that this is a good idea. Most do it with T3 in the morning and then NDT in the afternoon. I don't think anyone I've heard of has mixed the two in one dose. Maybe that was what Dr. K was referring to.

When you test for FT3 and Rt3 did you take the meds in the am then test? I used to do my labs first thing in am then take my T3 now Dzugan has his patients taking meds 1-2 before labs.

### Patty Cakes — 2012-08-21T19:53:39+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/struggling-with-t3.3137/post-46081>

I actually did NOT take the meds before I tested. This is my first T3 only lab test so I wanted to see what it would read with no morning meds. I came out low T3 as expected since I didn't have anything in my system since 3pm the day before. My next test will be with my morning meds taken. It should be higher on the scale. I have seen over the top range of T3 after taking meds and results like mine where the reading is low if you haven't taken meds. Dzugan probably wants to see the max you are reaching w/ the dose since t3 peaks usually 1-2 hours after ingestion. Obviously, it will be a very different reading, but keeping all that in mind you can interpret the labs better knowing when you took the meds.

### vkiernan — 2012-08-21T20:09:36+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/struggling-with-t3.3137/post-46087>

I took my Armour about 2-3 hrs before testing, does that mean they are not accurate? Is that why my FT3 was over the range?

### finnite@dccnet.com — 2012-08-21T20:38:14+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/struggling-with-t3.3137/post-46095>

> **vkiernan;46472 wrote:** I took my Armour about 2-3 hrs before testing, does that mean they are not accurate? Is that why my FT3 was over the range?

yes

T3 peaks in effectiveness at the 4-5 hour mark. if you want to know how the meds/doses are doing take a half dose 4.5 hours prior to testing. test before that 4.5 hour mark and you will show excess T3 in the blood.

if you want to know in general how your body is using the T3 take last meds 12 hours before testing.

T4 doesn't matter as the half life of this one is about 6 weeks which is why docs wait for 6 weeks to test once you are on NDT

### vkiernan — 2012-08-21T20:42:22+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/struggling-with-t3.3137/post-46098>

> **ColleenM;46480 wrote:** yes
>
>
>
> T3 peaks in effectiveness at the 4-5 hour mark. if you want to know how the meds/doses are doing take a half dose 4.5 hours prior to testing. test before that 4.5 hour mark and you will show excess T3 in the blood.
>
>
>
> if you want to know in general how your body is using the T3 take last meds 12 hours before testing.
>
>
>
> T4 doesn't matter as the half life of this one is about 6 weeks which is why docs wait for 6 weeks to test once you are on NDT

Thanks Colleen. They tried to tell me I was hyper and there is no way I was. So the new doc said to take t4 and drop the Armour. This really is a big mistake then. Dropping the t4 today. Another weird thing, before I found I was hypo my left arm would fall asleep every night on me. When I started the t4 and dropping back on the Armour, my arm started falling asleep on me again. Makes me wonder since I haven't had that problem in a year.

### Shijin13 — 2012-08-21T20:45:38+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/struggling-with-t3.3137/post-46099>

> **vkiernan;46483 wrote:** Thanks Colleen. They tried to tell me I was hyper and there is no way I was. So the new doc said to take t4 and drop the Armour. This really is a big mistake then. Dropping the t4 today. Another weird thing, before I found I was hypo my left arm would fall asleep every night on me. When I started the t4 and dropping back on the Armour, my arm started falling asleep on me again. Makes me wonder since I haven't had that problem in a year.

interesting about the arm... I have that problem too at night.

### finnite@dccnet.com — 2012-08-21T20:52:21+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/struggling-with-t3.3137/post-46102>

on thyroid forums there is a big difference made between showing hyper on labs and hyper by symptoms.

hyper by labs can be a simple dosing issue or an indication of a big RT3 issue. hyper by symptoms is waay more useful as you try to find your sweet spot.

the other thing to remember is when you take exogenous thyroid meds, in particular when you add in T3 if you didn't have it before the body at first more or less ignores the exogenous T3 and you get both the incoming and what you produce. then in a couple of days or weeks (a YMMV situation) the body realizes that there is sufficient incoming T3 and drops it's own production/conversion thus giving the classic: I felt wonderful and then all of a sudden I was hypo again which shows at the 2 week mark (or so).

back in the day docs used to Rx Armour (the original) they would start with a small dose say 1/2-1 grain and then begin to up that dose within a 2-3 week period (often half the dose again or doubling in harder cases depending on symptoms) with full testing at the 6 week mark to see if the dose is correct. this is how my thyroid doc did it and he is in his mid 70's so not a TSH worshipper.

these days it seems to be some arbitrary dose that is given and people are told to come back in 3 months. unfortunately the body doesn't work this way.

### WannaBeOptimal — 2012-08-21T21:52:58+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/struggling-with-t3.3137/post-46107>

Sorry for the dumb question, but what does NT and NDT stand for?

### finnite@dccnet.com — 2012-08-21T21:53:37+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/struggling-with-t3.3137/post-46108>

Naturethyroid and Natural dessicated thyroid

### Patty Cakes — 2012-08-21T22:41:39+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/struggling-with-t3.3137/post-46117>

> **vkiernan;46472 wrote:** I took my Armour about 2-3 hrs before testing, does that mean they are not accurate? Is that why my FT3 was over the range?

Agree with ColleenM and yes it probably is why. I was always told not to take the NDT before a morning test for any of the thyroid tests. Just take you last dose the day before and take the morning dose AFTER you have had the blood drawn.

### Souldanzer — 2012-08-21T23:12:33+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/struggling-with-t3.3137/post-46127>

> then in a couple of days or weeks (a YMMV situation) the body realizes that there is sufficient incoming T3 and drops it's own production/conversion thus giving the classic:

so how would a thyroid ever get better as we are optimizing if it downregulates with being exposed to exogenous T3?

### Souldanzer — 2012-08-21T23:53:27+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/struggling-with-t3.3137/post-46140>

> I have another consult with him in about a week and a half so I'm sure I'll get some clear answers then . . . the question is whether I'll manage until then!

Why don't you ask him if he can move it sooner for you if you're feeling that badly and think that he'll help you figure things out? I know he usually is pretty flexible - he's Mr Spontaneous after all  He got me in quickly both times I needed it.

### finnite@dccnet.com — 2012-08-22T01:06:35+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/struggling-with-t3.3137/post-46172>

> **Souldanzer;46512 wrote:** so how would a thyroid ever get better as we are optimizing if it downregulates with being exposed to exogenous T3?

get the underlying stuff fixed and then reverse the process. a year ago I was on 75mg of T3, I'm now on 37.5mg T3 and feel better than I did last year. I'm hoping to go down to 25 mg (split 2x)

If the thyroid hasn't been destroyed by radiation or antibodies it will revive and begin to produce T4 and T3 again. frankly body builders do it all the time when they cycle T3 for cutting. (not the group I would suggest getting thyroid advice from but they do this process succesfully with some regularlity) the key is of course getting the rest of the stuff optimized.

fwiw it's the same with the adrenals and folks who are put on hydrocortisone. after a certain point hydrocortisone shuts down the bodies own production of cortisol and as you wean off hc the body begins to produce it again provided there has been sufficient time, hormone regulation and nutrition to strengthen the adrenals. AF groups refer to the process as giving your adrenals a rest
