# Thread: Drink made from berry wine may provide tasty drug for diabetes
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/drink-made-from-berry-wine-may-provide-tasty-drug-for-diabetes.3146/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 1
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### chocolate — 2012-08-21T15:07:18+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/drink-made-from-berry-wine-may-provide-tasty-drug-for-diabetes.3146/post-46029>

[http://esciencenews.com/articles/2012/08/20/drink.made.berry.wine.may.provide.tasty.drug.diabetes?](http://http://esciencenews.com/articles/2012/08/20/drink.made.berry.wine.may.provide.tasty.drug.diabetes?)

> Drink made from berry wine may provide tasty drug for diabetes
>
> Published: Monday, August 20, 2012 - 13:34 in Health & Medicine
>
>
>
> In evaluating the bioactive compounds of Illinois blueberry and blackberry wines, University of Illinois scientists have found compounds that inhibit enzymes responsible for carbohydrate absorption and assimilation. And that could mean a tasty way to help people with diabetes decrease their blood sugar. "We're thinking about a dealcoholized fermented fruit beverage that would optimize the inhibition of the alpha-amylase and alpha-glucosidase enzymes and also make use of the wines' other healthful bioactive components," said Elvira de Mejia, a U of I professor of food chemistry and food toxicology.
>
>
>
> Graduate student Michelle Johnson evaluated the nutritional value of 19 Illinois wines, deciding on a blueberry-blackberry blend for maximum effectiveness.
>
>
>
> In the in vitro study, the scientists compared the anti-carb effects of the alpha-amylase and alpha-glucosidase enzymes with acarbose, an anti-diabetes drug. The carb-degrading enzymes were inhibited in a range of 91.8 percent for alpha-amylase compared to acarbose and 103.2 percent for alpha-glucosidase compared to acarbose, de Mejia said.
>
>
>
> The study is the first to assess the effect of berry fermentation at different temperatures on these carb-inhibiting enzymes. At both room and cold (4ÂºC) temperatures, berry wine retained the ability to degrade the enzymes, she said.
>
>
>
> In a second study, Johnson quantified the antioxidant, polyphenol, and anthocyanin content of blueberry and blackberry wines. Her proposed blend contains an abundance of these bioactive compounds, which add to its healthful properties.
>
>
>
> The researchers are particularly interested in the ability of anthocyanins to reduce inflammation, which contributes to the development of many chronic illnesses, including cancer, metabolic disease, and cardiovascular disease. To that end, they are experimenting with the berries' effects on inflammatory cells, and they have found that anthocyanins reduce markers associated with the inflammatory response.
>
>
>
> "Preliminary studies have indicated that anthocyanins may have a positive effect on cognition and overall brain health while protecting against some of the effects of aging, such as Alzheimer's disease and memory loss. These berries have some very intriguing components," de Mejia said.
>
>
>
> A food chemist, de Mejia would like to remove the alcohol from the wines, leaving the carb-degrading enzyme compounds, the inflammation-fighting anthocyanins, and other beneficial bioactive components in a functional and flavorful drink for diabetics and others.


══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

# Thread: Cruciferous Vegetables - who should eat, and who should avoid?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cruciferous-vegetables-who-should-eat-and-who-should-avoid.3148/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 13
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### bigknitwit — 2012-08-21T17:38:21+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cruciferous-vegetables-who-should-eat-and-who-should-avoid.3148/post-46044>

I am not fully dialed in to who should eat them, who should avoid them, and it seems there may be sub groups of the veggies too, to be aware of. So what are the markers that would make cruciferous veggies not an "optimal" food choice? Should cruciferous veggies always be eaten cooked? Are there sub-sets (of the cruciferous veggies) that can consume and others not? I am particularly interested in their use (or avoidance) for a person with Hashimoto's, but would love to know the full details about this food group!

### Shijin13 — 2012-08-21T18:30:09+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cruciferous-vegetables-who-should-eat-and-who-should-avoid.3148/post-46055>

I'm E-dominant w/o an AI issues, Cruciferous veggies have always been my preferred veggies of choice intuitively. I'm moving now more to fermented versions of these veggies when I can get them... unfortunately my first attempt at sauerkraut was a total failure.... back to the drawing board....

### bigknitwit — 2012-08-21T19:03:57+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cruciferous-vegetables-who-should-eat-and-who-should-avoid.3148/post-46065>

I've had a hard time making sauerkraut in the past too (and so now I don't!). Too many bits floating to the top and getting moldy with the long fermentation time. I find cucumbers much easier. Cucumbers, garlic, dill, and coriander if you have it. Pour salt water to cover, and place a bag full of salt water on top to help keep them submerged. In the summer, these are usually "ready" in about 48ish hours.

Should someone with an AI disease avoid cruciferous vegetables?

### Endless — 2012-08-21T21:23:28+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cruciferous-vegetables-who-should-eat-and-who-should-avoid.3148/post-46105>

I follow the recipes in Sally Fallon's 'Nourishing Traditions' book for all my fermented veggies and they seem to work great, but they use a small amount of liquid whey as a 'starter' so if you want to do salt only they would not be suitable. It's so much quicker than just salt!

### Michael — 2012-08-22T15:37:04+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cruciferous-vegetables-who-should-eat-and-who-should-avoid.3148/post-46288>

> **bigknitwit;46429 wrote:** ... cruciferous veggies ... I am particularly interested in their use (or avoidance) for a person with Hashimoto's ...

You can try googling something like "goitrogenic foods" to get a list of foods that might need to be approached with a certain amount of caution by anyone who's hypothyroid. And, yes, it does include cruciferous vegetables. But they are OK if cooked.

But probably a little raw isn't likely to be a problem--I don't think anyone would need to pick young spinach leaves out of a mixed green salad, for example. You just don't want to eat a lot of raw cruciferous vegetables on a regular basis. And presumably people like some vegans who liquidize whole cabbages and drink the raw juice could run into problems, if not warned by their doctor. (But I guess any technology that lets you consume foods in forms or in quantities in which you couldn't in nature is _potentially problematic.) However, strangely, I've never heard of a doctor mentioning goitrogenic foods to a hypothyroid patient.

I do know that hypothyroidism makes the conversion of beta-carotene to vitamin A (not as good as its often been assumed to be at the best of times) more problematic. I'd have thought that would mean that someone who's hypothyroid should take particular care to occasionally eat foods containing pre-formed vitamin A (liver for example). But, again, I've never heard of a doctor telling a patient that. In fact, I've never heard of one giving any_ dietary or lifestyle advice to a patient. It seems they just hand over the medication and leave it at that.

It's rather shocking to me.

### Michael — 2012-08-22T22:38:34+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cruciferous-vegetables-who-should-eat-and-who-should-avoid.3148/post-46408>

BTW, I just grabbed a minute to see if there was anything online about whether fermenting vegetables that were goitrogenic was helpful.

The references I could find indicated not. (I just did a quick google search on "thyroid sauerkraut" -- nothing very technical; wasn't looking for papers or anything, and don't know if there is any academic literature on this.)

Someone at Paleo Hacks said that the WAPF says fermenting raw cabbage does not reduce its goitrogenic activity -- I presume that's accurate. I haven't gone to the WAPF site and checked whether that's an official WAPF statement and what evidence they adduce for it.

The search brought up what looks like quite a nice practical little piece here:

[http://nourishedandnurtured.blogspot.co.uk/2011/05/thyroid-goitrogens-and-recipe-for.html](http://http://nourishedandnurtured.blogspot.co.uk/2011/05/thyroid-goitrogens-and-recipe-for.html)

This is just internet stuff thrown up by google-searching, but it does sound like you might need to go a little easy on the sauerkraut.

### Endless — 2012-08-23T03:49:37+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cruciferous-vegetables-who-should-eat-and-who-should-avoid.3148/post-46461>

So cooking brassicas makes them thyroid-safe, but fermenting them does not. darn....

So if one is avoiding nightshades, brassicas AND starch, what the heck kind of veggies can you eat?? Suggestions please!

### KiwiLauren — 2012-08-23T08:58:41+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cruciferous-vegetables-who-should-eat-and-who-should-avoid.3148/post-46495>

> **Endless;46856 wrote:** So cooking brassicas makes them thyroid-safe, but fermenting them does not. darn....
>
>
>
> So if one is avoiding nightshades, brassicas AND starch, what the heck kind of veggies can you eat?? Suggestions please!

I eat a lot of fresh herbs... cilantro, parsley, dill, rosemary, etc. And I eat garlic and sometimes onions. And lots of mushrooms.

### Dali Dula — 2012-08-23T13:50:46+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cruciferous-vegetables-who-should-eat-and-who-should-avoid.3148/post-46504>

I addition to what KiwiLauren mentioned there are greens like swiss chard, spinach, lettuces. red onion. These can be eaten raw, cooked, or lacto fermented.

### SeaHorse — 2012-08-23T14:52:11+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cruciferous-vegetables-who-should-eat-and-who-should-avoid.3148/post-46510>

Bok Choy...and some of the other asian greens too. Also, just to put another wrench in the works...some of these greens have very high oxalates...chard, spinach and some of the herbs mentioned. I have been avoiding these too...so bok choy starts to look really good after all these eliminations. I stopped feeling something was wrong about not eating vegetables earlier in the summer. I'm just going to wait for some more healing and then see how it goes....I think you have been down to very few foods too haven't you bigknitwit? Once the building blocks are being stacked up again, I think we will be able to branch out...but until then I'm just not going to worry about it.

### Michael — 2012-08-23T15:48:54+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cruciferous-vegetables-who-should-eat-and-who-should-avoid.3148/post-46519>

> **Endless;46856 wrote:** So cooking brassicas makes them thyroid-safe, but fermenting them does not. darn....
>
>
>
> So if one is avoiding nightshades, brassicas AND starch, what the heck kind of veggies can you eat?? Suggestions please!

Is it that much of a problem?

I mean I guess someone with thyroid issues might want to pass an eye on what's in a list of goitrogens. Those would one want to cook more often than not, although small amount raw sometimes would probably be OK.

The link I put up in post #7 is quite interesting. There's a suggestion there to have a little seaweed _with_ a (small) amount of sauerkraut. Sound like a good idea.

I don't think most of the stuff in question is usually traditionally eaten raw anyway ... And, of course, there would be reasons for that.

### Endless — 2012-08-23T20:40:00+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cruciferous-vegetables-who-should-eat-and-who-should-avoid.3148/post-46573>

Not raw, but I do like to make my own sauerkraut....

I like the idea of combining it with sea vegetables though, hmmm.

Can't say I've ever tried to lacto-ferment swiss chard or spinach, I'm not sure what that would even look like!

### bigknitwit — 2012-08-23T22:57:09+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cruciferous-vegetables-who-should-eat-and-who-should-avoid.3148/post-46595>

I remember reading somewhere ( I think something by Chris Masterjohn), that cooking the cruciferous veggies doesn't reduced their goitrogens significantly. He proposed including more iodine in the diet to counter the effect, if I remember correctly. I'll look it up and see if I can find the source.

I did lacto-fermented ginger carrots, but they turned out way too salty, and don't have any flavor except saltiness. I little tweaking (less salt, more innoculating brine, and it may get better results. My list of foods is small and dwindling, it seems.

# Thread: Fluoride damage permanent?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fluoride-damage-permanent.3151/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 5
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Raiken3712 — 2012-08-21T18:46:56+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fluoride-damage-permanent.3151/post-46059>

I was watching an anti-fluoride video and it quoted someone saying anyone drinking fluoridated water for a year will be forever changed...implying permanent brain damage...

I would hope this is untrue....and think it is. The human body is more resilient than people give it credit for. Not to mention the things you can do to improve the bodies ability to restore itself. There are obviously things that are permanent and no amount of good diet or good living can fix...not sure this is one of them. I suppose it would help to know what damage exactly fluoride causes and why. The root of the matter I suppose is can the body repair all of the damage done by fluoride once its removed...

Thoughts?

### chocolate — 2012-08-21T19:05:19+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fluoride-damage-permanent.3151/post-46066>

I read an article that said magnesium will displace fluoride. Here's hoping.

### Raiken3712 — 2012-08-21T19:11:13+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fluoride-damage-permanent.3151/post-46068>

> **chocolate;46451 wrote:** I read an article that said magnesium will displace fluoride. Here's hoping.

Haven't heard that one yet. I heard Iodine displaces fluoride. More reason to eat copious seafood. Wish it were cheaper for good quality fish/seafood.

### chocolate — 2012-08-22T00:01:38+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fluoride-damage-permanent.3151/post-46146>

[http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/21839622](http://http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/21839622)

> Biological effects of magnesium particles degradation on UMR-106 cell line: influence of fluoride treatments.
>
> Grillo CA, Alvarez F, de Mele MA.
>
> Source
>
>
>
> Instituto de Investigaciones FisicoquÃ­micas TeÃ³ricas y Aplicadas (INIFTA, CCT La Plata-CONICET), Facultad de Ciencias Exactas, Universidad Nacional de La Plata, La Plata, Argentina.
>
> Abstract
>
>
>
> Mg-based materials are promising for orthopedic, dental, and cardiovascular applications but their high degradation rate in vivo (release of Mg ions and debris particles) is cause of great concern. Protective treatments involving fluoride conversion coatings have been proposed in order to reduce corrosion rates. The aim of this study was to evaluate Mg debris biodegradation and its possible cytotoxic effects on osteoblastic cells in situ. Neutral Red dying and Acridine Orange staining techniques were used as endpoints to analyse the cytotoxic effects at 100-2000 μg/mL concentration range. Results showed a marked variation of Mg ion concentration in the culture medium after different exposure periods (1, 2, or 24h). Interestingly, the release rate of magnesium ions was dependent on the presence or absence fluoride treatment. Adverse effects induced by ≥1000 μg/mL MP doses and Mg ion concentrations higher than 480 μg/mL were observed on cells. Results showed significant differences between the concentration of Mg ions in the presence and absence of cells. This fact reveals a dynamic equilibrium mediated by Mg ion input and output in the cells that leads to the change in MP corrosion rates. **Fluoride release from conversion coatings did not show cytotoxic effects**

The article I read was older and kind of along the same lines. The magnesium released the fluoride somehow and when it did, there was no problem. I just don't remember how I searched it. I must have tripped over it. I almost may have misunderstood.

### chocolate — 2012-08-22T00:10:00+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fluoride-damage-permanent.3151/post-46157>

[http://jn.nutrition.org/content/116/6/1018.full.pdf](http://http://jn.nutrition.org/content/116/6/1018.full.pdf)

> ABSTRACT There is considerable uncertainty about the interrelated effects of
>
> calcium (Ca), magnesium (Mg) and fluoride (F) on hypocalcification of the skeleton
>
> and Ca accumulation in vital soft tissues. This paper describes experiments with rats
>
> fed a diet deficient in Ca and Mg, the latter deficiency being accentuated by a low
>
> potassium content. For different groups the drinking water was supplemented with
>
> Ca, Mg, F, Ca + F or Mg + F; Ca and F were supplied as chemically compatible
>
> compounds. At the end of the 54-d experiment, plasma Ca was strongly reduced in
>
> the Ca-deficient groups. **Plasma Mg was reduced particularly in the test groups sup
>
> plied with Ca, without any influence of F supply or varying plasma F. Ca accumula
>
> tion was much more pronounced in the kidneys than in the heart or aorta, but was
>
> **mitigated by both Mg and F supplementation. Bone mineralization disturbance due
>
> to the dietary imbalance was preventable only by Ca or Ca + F supplementation,
>
> and was less in alveolar bone than in femur. However, the Ca supplement was as
>
> sociated with the highest aorta and heart Ca contents, and Ca + F was associated
>
> with marked renal Ca accumulation; adequate Mg supply may be essential with**
>
> Ca + F administration against osteoporosis.** J. Nutr. 116: 1018-1027, 1986.

I think it can be manipulated, at least in rats.

# Thread: Coffee and Fat Make Blood Sugar Levels Spike
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/coffee-and-fat-make-blood-sugar-levels-spike.3153/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 9
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### chocolate — 2012-08-21T19:29:37+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/coffee-and-fat-make-blood-sugar-levels-spike.3153/post-46075>

[http://www.healthytheory.com/coffee-and-fat-make-blood-sugar-levels-spike](http://http://www.healthytheory.com/coffee-and-fat-make-blood-sugar-levels-spike)

Home

Coffee and Fat Make Blood Sugar Levels Spike

Posted Thu, 04/07/2011 - 11:12am by Camilla Cheung

Filed Under:

antwerpenR / Flickr.com

The next time you order that burger and fries, you may want to skip the coffee. A new study published in the Journal of Nutrition suggest that not only does eating a fatty meal make your blood sugar levels spike, but drinking coffee along with your fatty meal doubles the sugar level increase.

University of Guelph researchers gave a fatty lipid-rich beverage to healthy men, and six hours later gave them a sugary beverage. The men's blood sugar levels, when measured after they ingested the fatty beverage and the sugary beverage, were 32% higher than when they ingested the sugary drink alone, and not the fat-rich beverage. The fat the men had consumed appeared to interfere with the body's ability to produce insulin and clear the excess sugar from the bloodstream.

The results were even greater when the men consumed caffeinated coffee after drinking the fatty beverage. In this second study, the men were given the fatty beverage, then five hours later consumed the equivalent of 2 cups of coffee, and when their blood sugar was tested an hour later (six hours from the consumption of the fatty drink), blood sugar levels were 65% higher than when the subjects drank only the sugar drink and not the fat and coffee.

Other studies have shown that people suffering from Type 2 diabetes should cut their caffeine intake, switching instead to decaffeinated coffee or tea, as the excess caffeine can lead to spikes in blood glucose. However, this is the first time the effect of a high-fat meal and the consumption of caffeine have been studied simultaneously. The results suggest that the effect of a fatty meal can have repercussions on your health even hours after it is consumed, especially if you consume caffeine with your meal or later that day.

(On the flip side, some studies have shown that drinking coffee can have a small protective effect against diabetes, but these studies suggest that the protective effect may be due to antioxidants in the coffee rather than to caffeine content. Decaffeinated coffee had as much of a preventative effect against diabetes as caffeinated.)

Experts at the Mayo Clinic also say that caffeine can impair insulin action in people with Type 2 diabetes, leading to rises in blood glucose levels.

For people with diabetes or at risk of developing diabetes, or for people interested in eating healthily in general, it is a good idea to cut high-fat foods from your diet, especially the unhealthy saturated fats found in processed food and fast food meals. And if you do indulge in a fast-food meal once in a while, you may wish to skip the caffeinated soda or coffee that comes with your meal. Fat, sugar, and caffeine? Not an optimal combination.

I guess lots of people get trouble with coffee and fat.

### ealachan — 2012-08-21T20:08:17+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/coffee-and-fat-make-blood-sugar-levels-spike.3153/post-46086>

# Thread: Helping others become optimal
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/helping-others-become-optimal.3154/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 1
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Raiken3712 — 2012-08-21T20:19:31+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/helping-others-become-optimal.3154/post-46090>

This was posted by Peggy Holloway in the comments on [http://www.dietdoctor.com/a-new-toy-measuring-blood-ketones](http://http://www.dietdoctor.com/a-new-toy-measuring-blood-ketones)

If anyone has any thoughts maybe you could post a reply to her in the blog comments there. Sorry about the wall of text that's how it was in the comment section. Not sure how best to break it down so I'll just leave the formatting alone.

"This is a bit off subject, but I am desperate for some answers, so I'm posting on my favorite low-carb blogs. I have done this in the past, starting over a year ago and got some suggestions, but after a year, the situation has gotten worse instead of better. My sister was diagnosed as Type II (runs in the family - took the lives of our grandfather and father and all of us have carb intolerance) in 1999 after years of fighting weight issues with low-calorie, low-fat diets. She, my brother, and I all learned about low-carb diets about that time and all pursued low-carb lifestyles. My sister persevered despite reluctance from her physician to use diet to manage her blood sugar. She took Metformin from the start and other the years has been on Januvia. She has declined other medications and refused to take insulin. Her blood sugars have remained above normal and she has tried all sorts of "tweakings" of her low-carb diet. Last summer she went to see Mary Vernon (we live fairly close to her Kansas office) and had no success. The only recommendation Dr. Vernon and her associates made was a low-carb diet and they just couldn't understand that she'd been on a low-carb diet for 12 years! Jane also corresponded with Jackie Edelstein (spelling?) of the Atkins Society who suggested a yeast reducing diet, which Jane religiously followed. Not seeing great results, she, with my encouragement, has tried really upping her fat intake and lowering protein to replicate my ketogenic diet (which is the connection to this topic). Nevertheless, this past year, Jane's fasting blood sugars have risen exponentially, and her readings since attempting to go ketogenic are consistently between 180 and 250. Her last A1C was 10. This is really alarming and we have no idea what she should do. (My partner, who is also having great results from a ketogenic diet, losing 40 pounds in the last few months, is a retired Family Physician, so he has tried to help her since her own doctor is not sympathetic to the idea of treating with diet and avoiding medications. His only idea is to get her off her beta blocker which she claims she can't do without getting rapid heart palpatations and severe migraines.). Her doctor wants her to take Byetta and I'm sure will really start pushing insulin. Any ideas what is going on here? Thoughts about what she might do?"

# Thread: Foods to boost liver function
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/foods-to-boost-liver-function.3181/>
**Subforum:** Optimal Kids
**Posts:** 3
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### omlh@todmi.com — 2012-08-22T18:07:33+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/foods-to-boost-liver-function.3181/post-46322>

My youngest came up with some really nasty spontaneous bruising a week or so ago, and so our doctor ordered some blood tests. She's got to have another round of these, as her liver function test is abnormal (slightly raised ALTs), and I wondered if there were any specific foods to boost liver function. She eats well, a typical day would be coconut "cereal" (toasted coconut flakes) with coconut milk for breakfast, or eggs and bacon, soup, salad and cold meat or fish for lunch, with maybe some berries for pudding (usually with double cream, but no sugar) and meat or fish and vegetables for supper. She doesn't really snack, and she finds her asthma and eczema are more manageable if she is on the lower carb side of life (50 - 100 grams per day, and she's not underweight and is growing like a weed!). She occasionally like a mug of hot chocolate (made with unsweetened cocoa powder and coconut milk), and also occasionally like a couple of squares of 85% or higher chocolate, but in general avoids sugar like the plague. Is there anything I could be doing to help her bloods come back to normal?

### KiwiLauren — 2012-08-23T09:00:53+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/foods-to-boost-liver-function.3181/post-46496>

Olivia, I'm not sure I'm able to be helpful, but what about dandelion tea or a milk thistle supp? Also, have you read the latest blog? i'd worry more about her gut than just the liver. So minding light cycles big-time, eating epi-paleo, and working on gut flora (you could read the leaky gut Rx). Good luck.

### omlh@todmi.com — 2012-08-23T14:21:52+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/foods-to-boost-liver-function.3181/post-46508>

Haven't had enough time to really take in the latest blog posts, but I am most certainly mindful of circadian cycles for her. My biggest problem is that she returns to boarding school in two weeks (unless there is something major wrong), and whilst I'll make sure she's eating epi-paleo until then, I have no control once she returns to school ... milk thistle supplement might be a very good idea, and I'll get some whilst I'm out today! I doubt I could persuade her to drink dandelion tea, and I don't want her to really guess that there might be something seriously wrong with her unless we come to that point.

# Thread: Podcasts, Music, General Entertainment to occupy your mind

**Subforum:** The Cave

**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/podcasts-music-general-entertainment-to-occupy-your-mind.3182/post-46330>

Hi All,

I notice many of you are like me, and need mental stimulation all the time. ON that note, I find my podcast round up, as well as peppy music for HIIT training is getting mucho stale. I'd love you to say what podcasts, books, music you have that you would reccomend! I am always hooked up to the idevice and listening and I've run out of materials! Hoping you've got something good I've not heard of or tried.

I'll start:

Podcasts:

BTR Underground Wellness

Fatburning Man

ICU Rounds

Latest in Paleo

Livin la Vida Low Carb

Revolution Health

Ted stuff

Upgraded self

Non-health stuff just for fun:

Dave Ramsey

Clark Howard

Leo Laport

This American Life

### ealachan — 2012-08-22T18:48:33+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/podcasts-music-general-entertainment-to-occupy-your-mind.3182/post-46344>

I can't focus on work when people are talking in my ear (either co-workers or podcasts), but I routinely rock out to Spotify all day long...usually quite loud to drown out the co-worker drama that I want no part of. I have lots of different playlists because my musical tastes are pretty diverse, but I tend to gravitate mostly toward classic rock, hard rock, funk and hip-hop. I am also a huge sucker for 80's hair-band music (omg Whitesnake, SQUEEEE!) and have a whole playlist dedicated just to that. I also have a really neat white noise / meditation music playlist that helps chill me out when I'm stressed at work.

At home, I just like quiet. I need either a fan or the A/C unit running at night while I sleep, otherwise I hear every little mouse-scuttle and faucet-drip.

### bigknitwit — 2012-08-23T03:18:53+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/help-with-iron-labs.3190/post-46456>

In the context of low Free T3, low Free T4 levels, can anyone give insight into my iron labs?

Total iron 241 (40-175)

Iron binding capacity 275 (250 - 450)

%saturation 88 (15-50%)

Ferritin 33 (10 - 154)

I have not been supplementing iron, but I am having a horrendous time dealing with thyroid supplementation. Not sure if there's anything I need to "do" about the iron issue in the mean time though...

### Patty Cakes — 2012-08-23T04:15:39+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/help-with-iron-labs.3190/post-46470>

These labs are beyond me! This is what I see but don't know what it all means:

Total Iron looks very high

Sat% looks high (s/b 35-45%??)

Ferritin looks low

Have you checked your rT3? With those iron labs I would imagine that you would have a rT3 problem especially with a low FT3. Sorry not much help.

> Sat% looks high (s/b 35-45%??)

> Ferritin looks low

>
>

### bigknitwit — 2012-08-23T22:50:50+0300

Wow. I can't believe I'm looking at an iron overload condition, when what I really want is to improve my ferritin status. Just put in a call to my doctor about how to proceed forward with this. My iron levels in December were all in normal range (ferritin in the 20s though - not optimal). In May they were getting high (55% saturation, and total iron had climbed just out of range). This hasn't been a good week for lab news.

### bigknitwit — 2012-08-23T22:51:50+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/help-with-iron-labs.3190/post-46592>

Would having sky high iron levels be a reason for extremely poor absorption of thyroid meds, especially T4? I wonder.

### Destiny — 2012-08-24T02:43:11+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/help-with-iron-labs.3190/post-46621>

> **bigknitwit;46998 wrote:** Would having sky high iron levels be a reason for extremely poor absorption of thyroid meds, especially T4? I wonder.

I heard about low ferritin giving a trouble with thyroid meds absorption.What is ferritin? Ferritin is an iron-storage protein which keeps your iron in a dissolvable and usable state, making the iron non-toxic to cells around it. So when Ferritin is measured via a blood test, it is basically measuring the iron you have tucked away for safe use.

Why is measuring storage iron important? You can have so-called “normal” or even optimal serum iron and saturation levels, and even normal hemoglobin and hematocrit, yet a low Ferritin. And some patients report continued problems raising desiccated thyroid even with just low ferritin while the other labs look good. What causes low ferritin when other labs look great? It can point to bacteria and viruses feeding on iron. Even too much of certain herbs like Milk Thistle are said to lower ferritin.

# Thread: Optimal Vitamin D from the Sun
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/optimal-vitamin-d-from-the-sun.3203/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 6
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Raiken3712 — 2012-08-23T20:48:22+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/optimal-vitamin-d-from-the-sun.3203/post-46574>

So I was wondering if anyone has any links to websites which either list dates, times, locations for when the sun will be at a good angle for good Vitamin D production or have maps preferably. I would like the visual if possible.

Also if anyone has any advice on how to find tanning salons that use safe tanning beds or what questions to ask that would be great. I'd love to use tanning beds/natural light as much as possible. I've lately been thinking about doing tanning at a location for Vitamin D mainly but wouldn't mind a bit of darkening either. I also remember reading about someone saying that exposure all over could be beneficial....do any tanning places allow you to be naked?

If anyone has any more information regarding exposure to all areas that would be great. I guess at the very least I could buy a Speedo or something for almost complete exposure though I would put it on in privacy and switch back to something less embarrassing. I don't think I have the body for a Speedo but would like the benefits if there are any of full exposure.

My fence has wholes people can see through and my mom is not comfortable with me sun bathing naked in my back yard. At some point I hope to get a board on board type fence or something without the problem....not sure if I could convince my mom even with it because she would rather not see me either  but it might help.

### lioness7 — 2012-08-23T22:34:54+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/optimal-vitamin-d-from-the-sun.3203/post-46584>

[http://aa.usno.navy.mil/data/docs/AltAz.php](http://http://aa.usno.navy.mil/data/docs/AltAz.php)

This site will calculate your sun angle times. Add an hour for daylight savings. I have never been to a tanning salon nor plan on it...somehow just doesn't seem natural. Perhaps we don't need vit D exposure twelve mos of the year? I remember reading years ago that the front abdomen and back of knees has more vit d receptors...though I never followed up on this. The more naked the better I am sure. I have been tanning all summer, weather and housework permitting that is - 15 mins front, back and each side , half an hour before and after solar noon a few times a week. We have never used sunscreen. Can't do naked with 7 kids roaming around but I have learned how to be extremely discreet despite them. Also you can go here too

[http://nadir.nilu.no/~olaeng/fastrt/VitD\_quartMEDandMED\_v2.html](http://http://nadir.nilu.no/~olaeng/fastrt/VitD_quartMEDandMED_v2.html)

### Raiken3712 — 2012-08-23T22:39:46+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/optimal-vitamin-d-from-the-sun.3203/post-46585>

You are right about not needing it 12 months out of the year if your level is good. Actually I recently got a Vitamin D test so what I do or don't do might change based on the result. Tanning beds are not natural but I've heard they are the next best thing after the real sun if you aren't optimal in the optimal range.

There are tanning beds using electronic ballasts and ones using magnetic ballasts. Also they have varying levels of UVA/UVB. If the ratio is good and they use electronic ballasts they are probably good....Optimal would of course be the sun and if your level is optimal in the fall and winter using a tanning bed or supplements probably isn't necessary....depending on how fast it degrades.

This I'm not sure of...what level do you need to be able to last through the fall and winter.

I'm not aware of what values need to be for good Vitamin D production...and what do I need to set tabular value at?

### Raiken3712 — 2012-08-23T22:45:03+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/optimal-vitamin-d-from-the-sun.3203/post-46588>

On the second page they list 1000 IU every other day as ideal....I don't think this fits with Jack Kruse's or Dr. Mercola's ideal amounts.

### Souldanzer — 2012-08-23T22:45:12+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/optimal-vitamin-d-from-the-sun.3203/post-46589>

I think the only safe tanning bed is getting your omega 3 to 6 ratio in check before using any.

You can get quite a bit of vitamin D from seafood. Fish roe rocks for that purpose.

### Raiken3712 — 2012-08-23T22:46:44+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/optimal-vitamin-d-from-the-sun.3203/post-46590>

Can you get the activated form of Vitamin D without sun or safe tanning bed exposure though? The activated form is sulferated. Sulferated doesn't come up as a dictionary word....maybe I'm not spelling it right or that's the incorrect term.

On the Omega 3/6 point I actually did a bloodspot test...hoping I did it right and should be getting results today if all goes well.

# Thread: calf liver - help!
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/calf-liver-help.3224/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 7
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### ealachan — 2012-08-24T17:10:45+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/calf-liver-help.3224/post-46718>

I know it's not fish, but I bought a frozen grass-fed calf's liver from my local farm last night. I've never had liver before, outside of braunsweiger. I don't know what it tastes like, how to cook it, etc. Please to be giving me all your liver suggestions, tips and tricks. I don't have the ability to grind it up and mix it with ground beef currently, but I'm game for pretty much any other methods.

They also had kidney and heart in the deep freezer...any suggestions on those?

### Shijin13 — 2012-08-24T17:47:21+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/calf-liver-help.3224/post-46727>

here's one place to start [http://hillarystarbright.wordpress.com/2012/04/23/going-paleo-this-is-the-best-liver-recipe-ever/](http://http://hillarystarbright.wordpress.com/2012/04/23/going-paleo-this-is-the-best-liver-recipe-ever/)

another thing you can do - is drop some into a blender and puree it, then mix it w/ground beef/pork - and make burgers of it.

or you could make liver pate - in Jack's recipe book...

as for heart - I make jerky w/it. easy on the go snack...

### ealachan — 2012-08-24T17:52:11+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/calf-liver-help.3224/post-46729>

Do you marinate the heart at all before you dry it for jerky? I have a dehydrator that I NEVER use...lol

Thanks for the link, that looks perfect!

### Shijin13 — 2012-08-24T17:56:36+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/calf-liver-help.3224/post-46733>

> **ealachan;47139 wrote:** Do you marinate the heart at all before you dry it for jerky? I have a dehydrator that I NEVER use...lol
>
>
>
> Thanks for the link, that looks perfect!

yes... I take salt, and a mesquite seasoning (there's nothing offensive in it I think we get it at COSTCO), plus liquid smoke, and some balsamic vinegar and let it sit in that for 2-3days - then pop it in the dehydrator.

I recommend letting the heart defrost a little bit...its easier to cut when its still a slightly frozen.... easier to get thin slices when cutting by hand...

### SeaHorse — 2012-08-24T19:33:30+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/calf-liver-help.3224/post-46754>

The way I like to cook liver is to cut it REALLY thin while it is still semi frozen...then fried up with bacon (and onion if you can tolerate).

The super thin slices cook almost immediately and you don't get a lot of mushy big pieces...just nicely browned on both sides slices.

It's one of my favorite meals to order on the road in diners .... apart from the oil they use, it's all good.

### Destiny — 2012-08-25T00:41:58+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/calf-liver-help.3224/post-46853>

>
> It's one of my favorite meals to order on the road in diners .... apart from the oil they use, it's all good.

I do the same with a lot of onions. Love it!

### Souldanzer — 2012-08-25T01:24:22+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/calf-liver-help.3224/post-46860>

> **SeaHorse;47164 wrote:** The way I like to cook liver is to cut it REALLY thin while it is still semi frozen...then fried up with bacon (and onion if you can tolerate).

### Endless — 2012-08-24T18:18:16+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/oysters.3228/post-46738>

Does anybody know if the raw oysters sold shucked in the little plastic tubs are ok to eat raw? Or must you stick to live ones...

### SeaHorse — 2012-08-24T19:20:04+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/oysters.3228/post-46749>

I only feel comfortable eating oysters raw that have come out of their shells alive. They do live out of water for much longer than mussels and clams....up to a week or more so you can keep them in the fridge...I'm lucky to be able to buy live shellfish. I think I would cook any that were pre shucked, but not processed.

### Endless — 2012-08-24T19:33:02+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/oysters.3228/post-46753>

That's kinda what I was feeling too....I wish I had better access to live shellfish!

### bigknitwit — 2012-08-24T20:15:42+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/vitamin-k2-in-osteoporosis-dosages.3232/post-46766>

Is anyone doing this dose (45mg/day)? Where did you source your K2? Butter oil? Fermented cod liver oil? Some food source? My K2 supplement has 2mg per capsule!

### Souldanzer — 2012-08-24T21:02:28+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/vitamin-k2-in-osteoporosis-dosages.3232/post-46780>

Thorne Research K2 in MCT oil suspension 1mg/drop.

Lef.org K2 @ 15mg/pill.

I'm doing it and have been for 10months now. I got a DEXA scan this week.... waiting for results. Kinda nervous.

You can use pasteured butter or cod liver oil or organs as add ons but if you're seriously deficient, it probably won't be enough I think.

### ealachan — 2012-08-24T21:11:11+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/vitamin-k2-in-osteoporosis-dosages.3232/post-46784>

Man, here I thought I was hardcore taking 90mcg instead of the 45mcg recommended on the bottle of Jarrow that I got...lol

### nonchalant — 2012-08-24T21:20:31+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/vitamin-k2-in-osteoporosis-dosages.3232/post-46786>

I use Carlson's vitamin K2 Menatrenone 5g. I noticed after I started taking it that all these needle jabs aren't bruising me like before.

### Shijin13 — 2012-08-24T21:21:49+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/vitamin-k2-in-osteoporosis-dosages.3232/post-46787>

This is what I take... I take 45mg/day (max dose)

[http://www.vrp.com/bone-and-joint/ultra-k2](http://http://www.vrp.com/bone-and-joint/ultra-k2)

### Souldanzer — 2012-08-24T21:37:09+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/vitamin-k2-in-osteoporosis-dosages.3232/post-46792>

> **Nonchalant;47196 wrote:** I use Carlson's vitamin K2 Menatrenone 5g. I noticed after I started taking it that all these needle jabs aren't bruising me like before.

bruising actually is related to the amount of pressure they put on the vein after they pull out the needle... ask me how I know

The more pressure you put on and the longer, the less bruising tends to happen. You gotta do this yourself b/c the people who draw your blood usually have something else to do instead. But yeah, ever since a nurse told me that recently, I've stopped looking like a junkie finally.

### Jasmin — 2012-08-25T09:15:41+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/vitamin-k2-in-osteoporosis-dosages.3232/post-46970>

I was told by dr. K that I should consider taking 15mg K2 along with 10000IU D3 a day.

# Thread: Creme Bulgari
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/creme-bulgari.3234/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 3
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### jeanie@truelongmont.com — 2012-08-24T20:48:09+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/creme-bulgari.3234/post-46773>

Thinking of using the yogurt starter to make it with Heavy Whipping Cream, this is called creme bulgari. Do you think putting this in hot coffee would kill the good cooties?

### ealachan — 2012-08-24T20:54:09+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/creme-bulgari.3234/post-46775>

> **Cavemam;47183 wrote:** Thinking of using the yogurt starter to make it with Heavy Whipping Cream, this is called creme bulgari. Do you think putting this in hot coffee would kill the good cooties?

It depends on the strains of bacteria - some can tolerate heat better than others. Some die at 120 degrees, some hang on up to 140-160. I would check the starter you're using and see if you can Google up the heat tolerances of the specific bugs.

### kathylu — 2012-08-24T20:55:02+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/creme-bulgari.3234/post-46776>

As long as your coffee is not more than 125 degrees F (which is very hot to the touch), the little buggers should be fine.

# Thread: Hack my (non) sleep.....
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hack-my-non-sleep.3236/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 66
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Souldanzer — 2012-08-24T21:33:28+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hack-my-non-sleep.3236/post-46790>

I seriously need to fix my sleep. My cortisol is "okay", I take progesterone, I take DHEA, my lights never come on at night, my computer has f.lux, a dimmed screen and I wear blueblockers, my room has a blackout cover over the window at night and I sleep with ear plugs. I stop eating at least 4 hours before bedtime.

I thought those were the major offenders. I fall asleep fine though sometimes it takes a while. Most nights I wake at around 3am unable to get back to sleep until 6am or so. It ruins my day. It takes the little bit of brain function from me I might actually still have and it kicks off major headaches.

Is the only thing left that could be destroying my sleep that I am one of those "extremely sensitive" to light people and I need to turn my computer off at sunset? Or am I missing something?

### Pamela — 2012-08-24T21:38:26+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hack-my-non-sleep.3236/post-46793>

Have you tried L-Tryptophan or Melatonin before bed?

### Souldanzer — 2012-08-24T21:44:53+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hack-my-non-sleep.3236/post-46798>

> **Pamela;47203 wrote:** Have you tried L-Tryptophan or Melatonin before bed?

I've tried melatonin a few times but it won't prevent the awakenings. Melatonin also will prevent the prolactin surge from happening for women during the night so that's why Dr K doesn't recommend it for longterm use I think.

I'm on a neurotransmitter support program through Dr Kalish so I am getting tons of 5-htp and tyrosine with all the co-factors. I just got my results (still low) and have been ordered to add L-dopa to the mix. Not sure if that would mess with my sleep.

### Pamela — 2012-08-24T21:48:49+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hack-my-non-sleep.3236/post-46800>

Personally I am good also on falling asleep and wake up pretty regularly 5 hours later. What I have found works for me is to take a cal-mag capsule and about 3 drops of liquid melatonin (NOW brand) which is really very little. I usually fall back asleep within 10 minutes or so.

### Souldanzer — 2012-08-24T21:52:35+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hack-my-non-sleep.3236/post-46802>

Thanks, Pamela. My calcium is over the range, so no calcium for me. I could try to do some sublingual mg threonate maybe. My melatonin is sublingual, too, but I've tried that before and it just made me really groggy during the day b/c then I still didn't sleep.

### docwatt@gmail.com — 2012-08-24T22:16:39+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hack-my-non-sleep.3236/post-46810>

You might try an evening CT session. Personally I sleep much more deeply after CT.

### nonchalant — 2012-08-24T22:21:09+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hack-my-non-sleep.3236/post-46815>

I think you should try turning off the computer at dusk. I'll send you some paperbacks.

### jeanie@truelongmont.com — 2012-08-24T22:32:22+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hack-my-non-sleep.3236/post-46817>

> **Nonchalant;47225 wrote:** I think you should try turning off the computer at dusk. I'll send you some paperbacks.

hehe yea, we keep taking actual books to the goodwill, I'll start saving them for you.

Perhaps look in a different direction for help too - like in setting your own circadian things. . .look at what happens in the a.m., perhaps use an alarm for a bit and at the same time every day get up, get out and stare at that damn light. Maybe anchoring that side of things will help your body and brain sort out the night time side.

I know that means some days will be not enough sleep - just nap those days, but make sure at the same time each day (or put that smartypants brain of yours to use) and calculate for each day when the sun will be in the same relative location in the sky so you adjust wake up time based on sun location.

Once we started anchoring the a.m., nighttime just fell into place nicely. Of course being able to step out my back door in pj's, and having a child that gets me up helps.

One last thing, meant to say this when I saw a post last week. I saw a study that people who "Watch the sun set behind mountains or over the horizon" jump start the melatonin and get good and sleepy. Like we are wired to see the sun set behind those mountains and our body will respond.

I've really started to enjoy our evening time of sitting outside watching the sun go down.

Thanks, Cavemam. I've been doing the sunset watching and I do like it. I go for a walk or hike usually, sometimes I work in my garden and watch it. Or I'm on my bike. It does do something to my brain and feels like an "off" switch. I think it helps me fall asleep. It doesn't help me \*stay\* asleep.

I've been thinking about the wakeup time issue for a while now, too. I think my issue with that has been that I need around 10hrs of sleep, which means i would have to go to bed darn early! Which wouldn't be so bad actually..... b/c my brain goes on \*off\* at night anyways and I'm just doing mind numbing things. I think I might try it. But yeah, the tired days that come with that.... they s\*ck. Not that I'm not tired anyways if I don't sleep correctly so it might be a no-loss trial overall.

### Shijin13 — 2012-08-24T23:05:15+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hack-my-non-sleep.3236/post-46829>

> **Souldanzer;47200 wrote:** I s
>

I'm one of those people too... Jack suggested to me in my consult I should consider melatonin during the summer ... I'm doing 3mg right now...but maybe you need more? IDK, it seems like your circadian cycle is off... have you asked Jack in your consults if you might benefit from cycloset? I used to have crazy insomnia - and worked night shifts for several yrs... I'm finally seeing some normalization in sleep and waking cycles with the use of the cycloset. you probably need more dopamine, considering you're trying to "re-train your brain"

### Souldanzer — 2012-08-25T10:39:55+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hack-my-non-sleep.3236/post-46973>

> **Endless;47386 wrote:** How about audiobooks as an alternative to the computer?

I've tried the audio books after my TBI. The problem with those is that they are even harder than books for me I think.... b/c I check out and miss stuff without really being able to slow it down or go back frequently to re-read. With books, when my attention goes, I just stop reading or realize I didn't get that last paragraph. But the audio books... they just blabber away on me and I give up.

But technically, I think those are probably a great idea for the non-TBI affected.

### Souldanzer — 2012-08-25T10:41:32+0300

No sleep

Hate it. It's the weekend and I'd like to have some fun with some friends. Really. But at this rate, I'm going to be half asleep all day and waiting for it to be over. I guess I really need to lose the computer at night. But there's only so much day dreaming even I can do!!

### vkiernan — 2012-08-25T13:28:48+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hack-my-non-sleep.3236/post-46976>

Morning sunshine. You are suppose to be sleeping. I have to get up for our fiscal yr end inventory audit. Have you been still taking a lot of dhea? Just thinkin' out loud (yes, it always gets me in trouble) seems like if I go overboard my sleep is trashed. I wonder if there is an over limit and what it can do to you? If I get acne, I back off. I also know my labs were over the limit too though. You really seem to go in ups and downs. Maybe today for a project you could go thru this journal and chart when your sleep is good and when your sleep is trashed and see if anything correlates. If nothing else, maybe it will bore you into sleeping. Sometimes you are telling us you are sleeping 10 hrs or more and other times you can't sleep period. I realize it might just be that you aren't fixed enough yet too. Maybe go outside and play today and get some cold water and maybe you will sleep tonight. My personal experience is if I go out on the lake and play all day in and out of the water, I sleep great that night. Hoping to be doing that today after work. I hope you have a nice day despite this whole not sleeping issue. Give your brain a rest and quit worrying too. Go play!

### Souldanzer — 2012-08-25T13:41:44+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hack-my-non-sleep.3236/post-46977>

Hey V. Still no sleep  It's getting worse.

I do sleep in phases. Some nights a lot, some nights I have a lot of trouble. The trouble is more often than the lot. I was supposed to be sleeping better with the progesterone added but it only helped for a few days or so. I guess I can try skipping the DHEA today. Might be a good idea. But then I'm kinda due for a better nights sleep. Eventually I get so exhausted I sleep 11 hrs in one night.

This isn't the worst it's ever been. But bad enough. During AN there were times where I just plain wouldn't sleep for weeks/months at a time. They had to give me special consideration at school b/c of my insomnia.

I get what you're saying about getting outside and playing. Thing is, for me that doesn't help. Ski days/weekends used to be some of the worst sleep nights. Also when I get a big hike in or am otherwise really active. The best sleep I get when I haven't slept for a week... b/c I'm exhausted and my body just gives in at some point.

Tonight I have tried taking melatonin, using f.lux all day and no artificial light exposure at all, taking activated B6, listening to a webinar replay, boring myself to sleep with reading Hinz research papers.... I don't know  And lots of day dreaming. I figure if I'm awake I might as well manifest some things I'd like to see happen  But yeah, it's about 4 hrs later and still no sleep.

### nonchalant — 2012-08-25T16:39:42+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hack-my-non-sleep.3236/post-47003>

I think you need to wait a bit, if possible. When I was on the LR, my sleep settled down (eventually) beautifully. Then I added CT and yikes! My sleep was terrible! For a few weeks. Then it settled down, and it became even better than before. When you wake up during the night, don't think of it as a failure, just use the time to imagine hiking or viewing the sunset or whatever. Even if you are awake for the rest of the night. Stay in bed. Act like you're asleep.

### Souldanzer — 2012-08-25T21:04:14+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hack-my-non-sleep.3236/post-47088>

There is this place in Crestone, CO that I checked out a few years ago when I was at my breaking point with AN. It's a buddhist retreat center in the mountains. No acces by vehicle you have to hike in. I did when I checked it out. It is for people who are seeking solitude to deepen their practice. They have tiny little cabins far spread out over a huge property so you won't run into other's who are retreating in silence. Very affordable. You can stay as long as you like and they make sure to replenish your food supply so you don't have to leave ever.

I do think there is a food problem b/c they probably won't allow epi-paleo. They are vegetarian. And I'm not one to secretly break these sacred places' rules. Too bad.

### Richard — 2012-08-25T21:43:38+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hack-my-non-sleep.3236/post-47100>

Souldanzer, I sometimes have the same problem-waking around 3 am. My NP recommended Calcium Malate. It seems to help. Also, some nights I drink Sleepytime Tea, the blend with Valerian Root. Occasionally I eat a small protein snack right at bedtime. For some people early morning waking can be caused by blood sugar dropping. There are some iPhone apps that I listen to if I can't get back to sleep. My current favorite is SleepStream 2. Lastly, I read somewhere to think about something SMALL that you are grateful for when you are lying there. The last time I couldn't go back to sleep I thought about my toothbrush, how important it is, how many bristles it had, good brushing technique.......ZZZzz

### Stavity — 2012-08-26T01:15:42+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hack-my-non-sleep.3236/post-47146>

When my vitamin B's are up there, sleep is much better. I also associate internal organs with the time I wake, if too early. Liver is what I am dealing with and my wake up time, no matter what, is 4 am. I know when my liver is better sleep will be a breeze. I wake up, and need to stay up for about 45 min then fall back to sleep for about 3 hrs, but that doesn't cut it and is not optimal sleep for me.

Maybe try upping your B complex? Also, if I drink chamomile tea it might help but no more then 1 or it can have the opposite affect on me.

### Souldanzer — 2012-08-26T01:36:44+0300

I'm taking B-vitamins and now just started on activated B6 which I guess could make a difference. I think I might be attacking this from the wrong end. I'm trying to tell my body no to the adaptations it is making considering my environment (lights at night, noisy, etc).... I"m sure some people can live like that. My body has proven to me several times it does not thrive in this kind of environment. I don't know if there's anything I can do about this, right now, really. It's been a goal for years to change my environment.

But maybe I need to open up to new ideas about how it could look like.

### cjb — 2012-08-26T17:03:12+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hack-my-non-sleep.3236/post-47233>

I would try finding a guided meditation to use when you wake up and can't go back to sleep.

Another option would be to get a cd of ocean sounds or another sound that you find relaxing and play that when you wake up. Then you have to let your mind take you where it wants to go and don't stress about being awake and not able to sleep. Once you start thinking about not being able to sleep, I think it becomes a self fulfilling prophecy.

### Souldanzer — 2012-08-26T17:31:46+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hack-my-non-sleep.3236/post-47242>

Thanks, cjb. Tried the meditation and the nature sounds.... no go. What being awake during the night really does to me is it makes me afraid to go to sleep in the first place.... I stay up longer and longer b/c I'm afraid if I'm not tired enough I will wake up during the night and not be able to go back to sleep. Very. Very. Counterproductive. B/c usually I stay up using my laptop. Um.

BUT. Good News for a change  Slept last night. Not the best and deepest sleep ever but it was sleep anyways! Went out to see the sunset, came back, realized I had forgotten my blueblockers to navigate the very illuminated hallway, tried to get the keys into the door holding my eyes shut with the other hand (lol, I hope nobody saw me). Had a moment of \*aha\*.

Well, there's light shining under the door from the hallways. I knew that and I've covered that up before when forced to leave my bedroom door open for airconditioning purposes during the night. But I realized how little light it really takes to throw things off after listening to the webinar. That's really part of where my dissatisfaction with some of the world is coming from..... and why I'm longing for a more natural surroundings. It's just so hard to avoid some things if you happen to be "sensitive".

So covered up the door issue as best I could. I decided temperature was another issue and put the A/C on high for the night. Which then required a whole lot more of covering up light sources. See.... my bedroom is "safe". No light Period. But the rest of this place is a trap hole.

Next I doubled up my neurotransmitter support for the day at night. I figure, I don't got enough dopamine in my system yet I have a dopamine related sleep issue. Dopamine needs to come around first. Also took some melatonin.

Was in bed by 8:30pm, turned off the computer by 8:45pm, took a while to fall asleep, woke up a couple times during the night, and the next thing I know is the sun peeking through the little corner of my window that's left open for that purpose.

I woke with the sun this morning  Let's see if I can repeat this.

### Souldanzer — 2012-08-26T17:34:40+0300

### Zorica Vuletic — 2012-08-26T23:32:05+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hack-my-non-sleep.3236/post-47343>

Oops, nevermind...I figured it out. I just had to slide the little ball over to 'halogen'.

### Souldanzer — 2012-08-26T23:33:33+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hack-my-non-sleep.3236/post-47344>

> **Gladina;47783 wrote:** Btw, how do I set my f.lux to 24/7? I try putting the south pole...b/c it's 'winter' there now...doesn't compute. I don't see a setting to have it 'turned on for 24/7'.
>
>
>
> Thanks.

You click on the little symbol (top right if you have a mac, it looks like a little analog clock).

Choose "preferences"

There will be two little slide bars one for "daytime" and one for "nighttime". Slide the "Daytime" button all the way to the left until it says 2700, just like the nighttime one.

CLick "done" and you're good to go.... your F.lux will never turn off.

### Shijin13 — 2012-08-27T16:23:30+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hack-my-non-sleep.3236/post-47478>

> **Souldanzer;47784 wrote:** Shijin, how are you managing the extreme light sensitivity?

Since my last consult I've added melatonin back in to my night time routine 1hr before when I want to go to bed. Also, we've been pushing hard the no light at night - the only light we use are red head lamps for reading, as well as candles (the non flame time). orange glasses, as soon as it starts to get dark, limited computer time after 6pm (I do admit I do check FB in the evenings - but I put it to bed NLT 9pm but I need to get better w/that) Any reading is done w/my kindle touch or a regular book using the red head lamp... next week going to start studying for my professional exam in the evenings again... using the same stuff.

I also plan on buying orange bulbs (blue light blocking ones) to put into our lamps for this winter when It gets dark (DH hasn't fully committed to no lights at night - but I'm working on him.) This past week - we've started eating by candle light - working slowly towards preparing every on for the winter....

Add the cycloset to it... & the RX of orgasm before bed, and after sunrise.... things seem to be getting on track ;D

Soul, that's AWESOMEEEEEE!!!!!!!!! I hope this means improving sleep etc. for you.

So now you have T3 and seafood, and NT support...! I hope all is group effort fixing you! Still gonna go to the mountain in a tent?

Also, from the other thread, are you going to try oxytocin?

Damn, I know the going is slow on a day to day basis...but I suppose measure your success from when you first started Leptin Rx/AI protocol, to CT, to now epi and everything in between. Then imagine in the next 10 months...

One day it will be like this: Someone will ask your phenomenal health and they would NEVER know your history. And...you can have the hopes of living to a ripe old age in a much better health than for majority of your first part of your life. That is exciting and am excited for your hard work.

I get frustrated with (what seems) society's lack of appreciation for hard work. It almost seems they chastise it and de-value someones' success if they 'had to work hard' for it....

But I don't want to live in that frame-work. Trust me if I did...I would have called it quits (in my efforts) longgggg ago. It's actually worth it to try hard and to be cliche builds character. That means a lot to me...and no one can take that away from me. That is MY freedom.

### Glamazon — 2012-08-30T07:10:54+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hack-my-non-sleep.3236/post-48308>

> **Souldanzer;47759 wrote:** that must be new... don't remember that being there when I went to check it out

I stayed there a few years ago for a long weekend. You can drive up to it. Their food is organic; they grow much of their own. They are totally veggie. Before I knew how sensitive I really was to carbs I would pass out for an hour after each meal because it was so carb heavy. Very tasty though. I would bring cans of salmon and some pastured eggs (eat raw). They do have eggs and dairy for meals. It's probably about 10 minutes to drive back into Crestone which does have just a few places to eat. It is very quiet there at night. Just hear the animals roaming thru. They do go silent retreat for the winter. I went up to visit first in the spring when the monks were coming off a silent retreat. The monks were a bit pale, gaunt, and acting a little bit fried and strung out it seemed post silent time.

It is a beautiful, serene place. The dorms are nice too. The cabins are tiny. They also have a TV in the main lodge where the meals are served.

I met a few of the monks, some were silent some welcomed us as we entered the premises to the common house. What I remember most is less "pressure". I spent a whole long time just sitting next to the Stupa. I guess I feel funny about bringing non-vegetarian food onto the premises b/c they usually ask you not to. I know Shoshoni has a zero tolerance rule for that. I respect their beliefs so I don't break it. At Shoshoni, I used to just eat salad and some eggs, then eat once home.

### shilohman — 2012-08-30T17:35:48+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hack-my-non-sleep.3236/post-48374>

> **Souldanzer;47200 wrote:** I seriously need to fix my sleep. My cortisol is "okay", I take progesterone, I take DHEA, my lights never come on at night, my computer has f.lux, a dimmed screen and I wear blueblockers, my room has a blackout cover over the window at night and I sleep with ear plugs. I stop eating at least 4 hours before bedtime.
>
>
>
> I thought those were the major offenders. I fall asleep fine though sometimes it takes a while. Most nights I wake at around 3am unable to get back to sleep until 6am or so. It ruins my day. It takes the little bit of brain function from me I might actually still have and it kicks off major headaches.
>
>
>
> Is the only thing left that could be destroying my sleep that I am one of those "extremely sensitive" to light people and I need to turn my computer off at sunset? Or am I missing something?

My sleep use to be the same, wake up around 3am and not able to get back to sleep until around 6am. I did all the same things you have done. For me I did the following:

1) upped my DHEA intake in the morning - goal was to get it to the top quartile on testing

2) made sure I eat 50 grams of protein in the morning - this puts serotonin and dopamine in balance. Serotonin is converted to melatonin.

3) the biggest thing for me was to stop working out at night (very bright lights at the gym)

4) I sleep wearing my blue blocker glasses - this was the biggest as far as getting a full nights sleep. Its amazing how much light you get even though your room is dark.

### Souldanzer — 2012-08-30T17:47:02+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hack-my-non-sleep.3236/post-48376>

> **shilohman;48845 wrote:** My sleep use to be the same, wake up around 3am and not able to get back to sleep until around 6am. I did all the same things you have done. For me I did the following:

>
> 2) made sure I eat 50 grams of protein in the morning - this puts serotonin and dopamine in balance. Serotonin is converted to melatonin.

>
> 4) I sleep wearing my blue blocker glasses - this was the biggest as far as getting a full nights sleep. Its amazing how much light you get even though your room is dark.

Thanks shiloman. I do all of this and I still wasn't sleeping. I've been sleeping the past few nights though. I think the biggest change I made was to not expose myself to artificial light during the day anymore (well, minimize it) and to run f.lux on my computer at all times.

I've also doubled up on my NT support until I get my hands on some l-dopa b/c my serotonin/dopamine was still low despite supplementation and BAB. Once I get the l-dopa I will follow Dr Kalish's recommendations again and retest. But for now I'm self-medicating as I see need.

As the evening gets closer to sunset, I start to dim my computer screen, turn off any artificial lights that might be on (bathroom and kitchen) and avoid any other offenders even though the sun has not set yet. I figure there is a natural dimming of light even before sunset that I need to heed.

I added a bit of melatonin at night for now. Not decided what to do with that part yet.

### Souldanzer — 2012-09-01T16:36:44+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hack-my-non-sleep.3236/post-48718>

Night before last sucked, but holy fish did I get some SLEEP last night  I feel like new person today! (That, as I have now learned, means that I did get my pulsatile growth hormone release during 2-5am and autophagy happened and I'm not using yesterday's proteins today  new proteins for a new day, please!).

Took my melatonin last night a little after 8pm, by 8:45 I couldn't stay awake anymore, next thing I know is I open my eyes and the sun is poking in through the little open corner of my window cover. Got up a little after 7am. I think I really am one of those super light sensitive people and maybe a little additional melatonin until it gets dark earlier isn't so bad. I also now make things pitch dark around me once the sun sets if I happen to be home.... I used to wait for that until it got dark outside. And I avoid all artificial light as much as I can. I woke this AM remembering a ton of my dreams

### ColdBren — 2012-08-24T22:42:56+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/mercury-and-candida.3239/post-46818>

As I am trying to rid my daughter of a possible candida overgrowth leading to some tics. I came across more than one article saying.....Candida helps keep mercury less toxic in the body! Now this is very concerning to me since the other potential cause of her tics is mercury. Am I doing the right thing by trying to clear her of the candida, or am I making a mercury problem worse. (note: 2 heavy metals tests showed she doesn't seem to have high metal problem) Please advise! Thanks!

### chocolate — 2012-08-24T23:10:24+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/mercury-and-candida.3239/post-46831>

I keep reading that also. But you know what I think? I think that mercury is circadian, also. There is a natural circadian cycle for mercury and it chelates out with cysteine I think. Swamp fever that hits in spring will clear it. There is a study in from Canada one year showing that glucose extended the diarrhea. And glucose extends yeast. It think you just have to hit everything at once and know your sweet baby is going to have detox symptoms. The candida substitutes for our own immune system, I thought for its own benefit, but now I don't think so. Its supposed to be on the outside of things. Kind of the don't eat fruit with broken skin. Our daughters and myself are the fruit with broken skin. Now we have to lose the glucose and fructose.

Eating meat will chelate the mercury. I take carnitine for it and the ip-6.

### ColdBren — 2012-08-24T23:17:21+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/mercury-and-candida.3239/post-46833>

thanks! we will continue on epi-paleo. but, i have her on nystatin and am concerned maybe i shouldn't clear all the yeast??

....has she been tested for mercury? Have you? Do you or she have any amalgam fillings? I was full of them and had candida for years....and from my research, I have learned that the body won't get rid fully of the candida until the mercury is gone, or significantly reduced....

not sure that helps but good luck!

### shah78 — 2012-08-28T20:02:56+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/mercury-and-candida.3239/post-47816>

I just findihed my 25th round of Andy Cutler's Protocol for mercury chelation. I eat 100%Primal (with fish) 24/7/365. I average 7-10 cold therapy sessions per week. # Thread: Organic Cation Transporter Testing/ Neurotransmitter Support
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/organic-cation-transporter-testing-neurotransmitter-support.3249/>
**Subforum:** Optimal Labs
**Posts:** 17
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Souldanzer — 2012-08-25T12:44:14+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/organic-cation-transporter-testing-neurotransmitter-support.3249/post-46975>

Just throwing this out there.... anybody else doing this or working with Dr Kalish?

I was re-listening to the Epi-Paleo Rx webinar tonight and I was wondering about some things:

1) BAB is designed to refill our brains with missing amino acids, it then curbs appetite. I eat plenty of protein for BAB probably rather at the higher limit than the lower most days. I still eat a lot for dinner (too much). My last Cation Transporter test came back as still deficient in serotonin and dopamine. **_Is that part of the reason why I eat so much? B/c my brain is trying to refill my sub-optimal NT stores?

2) **_How high can I go with protein for BAB to attempt to help my brain fill up those NT stores? At what point am I just plain overeating on protein and not helping myself anymore?

3) My last test I understand says I am now in Phase 2 with my organic cation transporters. I'm still reading up on the Hinz papers I found posted on his research site... but I get that Phase 1 means I am deficient in NTs and I am wasting them through my kidneys b/c the gates that would allow them into my brain are closed. So I pee them out instead. Phase 2 implies that the gates are open (fully??) and I am refilling my brain with NTs yet there isn't enough NTs around so I am still deficient. This is the phase my body/brain is in right now. Phase 3 is the desired phase, with the gates fully open, NTs being sufficient in the body and brain, and the body excreting any extra NTs through the kidneys.

The part I don't understand quite yet is that I am still deficient in serotonin and dopamine, yet my NT support program only had l-dopa added, 5-htp and tyrosine I am advised to keep steady. I guess I'm a bit lost in **_how my serotonin will come up with l-dopa only added? Don't I need more of the other two as well to increase my serotonin? Back to my second question.... can I make up for this deficit by eating more protein for BAB? Or just plain more protein and less fat instead?_**

### Souldanzer — 2012-08-29T22:05:38+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/organic-cation-transporter-testing-neurotransmitter-support.3249/post-48171>

> **Recent studies have suggested that the circadian neuroendocrine rhythm mediated by dopamine and serotonin plays a large role in insulin sensitivity.** In people without diabetes, the postmeal state causes a decline in endogenous glucose production due to suppression of glucagon and inhibition of lipolysis. In individuals with T2D, a drop in dopamine levels is thought to lead to an inadequate hypothalamus response, resulting in elevated levels of blood glucose, free fatty acids (FFA), and triglycerides (TG), which contribute to insulin resistance, visceral adiposity, and beta-cell dysfunction. Clinical studies suggest that bromocriptine-QR administered in the morning (within 2 hours of waking) mimics the normal peak of dopamine in the central nervous system, consequently thought to reset this circadian rhythm. The main target organ of bromocriptine is the brain.

> Mucuna Pruriens 99% L Dopa is a 99% pure formulation of L-Dopa. Also called Levodopa, this is an amino acid precursor of dopamine which is able to cross the blood brain barrier and essential turn into dopamine.
>
>
>
> Extracted from the seeds of the Mucuna Pruriens plant, this is the purest form on the market. Most other formulations contain between 15 and 50%.
>
>
>
> Once L-Dopa crosses the blood brain barrier it acts as a precursor for dopamine and adrenaline.
>
>
>
> This leads to some rather incredible beneficial effects related to mood, cognition, disease treatment and even some sexual benefits. Dopamine is believed to help with mood, movement and sexuality.
>
>
>
> You will notice an effect on your mood and cognition. A general feeling of well-being ordinarily occurs, along with a positive mood and mental alertness. Proper brain functioning and enhanced cognition are supported.
>
>
>
> Since 1961, L-Dopa has been the most effective treatment for Parkinson's disease. It is most effective against nausea, rigidity and slowness these patients usually suffer.
>
>
>
> **Several studies have also indicated that this supplement may enhance the production of HGH (human growth hormone) and lead to improved sexual performance as well.**
>
>
>
> It is thought to increase testosterone production and protect sperm from oxidative damage.

> Cortisol is a stress hormone that is linked with dopamine production in the brain. Levels of cortisol in the bloodstream are highest during rising in the AM, which in turn leads to higher dopamine levels during daytime.** Dopamine, a neurotransmitter, is directly responsible for a number of important functions in the brain, including attention and learning when we rise.
