> Recent studies have suggested that the circadian neuroendocrine rhythm mediated by dopamine and serotonin plays a large role in insulin sensitivity. In people without diabetes, the postmeal state causes a decline in endogenous glucose production due to suppression of glucagon and inhibition of lipolysis. In individuals with T2D, a drop in dopamine levels is thought to lead to an inadequate hypothalamus response, resulting in elevated levels of blood glucose, free fatty acids (FFA), and triglycerides (TG), which contribute to insulin resistance, visceral adiposity, and beta-cell dysfunction. ### Souldanzer — 2012-09-06T21:24:29+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/organic-cation-transporter-testing-neurotransmitter-support.3249/post-49826>

[http://webcache.googleusercontent.com/search?q=cache:http://www2.marilia.unesp.br/revistas/index.php/reic/article/view/734/636](http://http://webcache.googleusercontent.com/search?q=cache:http://www2.marilia.unesp.br/revistas/index.php/reic/article/view/734/636)

> **MD \[major depression\] is strongly correlated with low glucose metabolism in the brain,leading to a diffuse reduction of cortical activity....there are other steps in the transport of energyfrom the blood-brain barrier to the neuron that may be defective.
>
> ....
>
> Second, the transport of glucose and its metabolites to neurons is madeby glucose transporters (GLUT-1; Abbott et al., 2006). In the interior of theneuron, the glucose reaches the mitochondria, where ATP is produced andbecomes available to neuronal metabolism. **Norepinephrine and serotoninsystems are involved in the control of brain glucose delivery, transport, anduptake. Low levels of glucose in the brain can be caused by downregulationof these systems. Failure of glucose uptake initially leads to glycolysis, thebreakdown of the stored mode of glucose. Serotonin reuptake inhibitors canimpact on the symptoms of depression, although they do not act directly onthe primary causes (those that determine low ATP production in neurons).
>
> Third, there is lactate transport from astrocytes to neurons (see Pellerinand Magistretti, 2004) controlled by glutamatergic systems. Glutamate,besides prompting lactate, also inhibits glucose transport to neurons (seePorras et al., 2004). **Among glucose metabolites, lactate plays a primary role as either direct or indirect (gluconeogenesis) energy source.** Currently, there isa debate on the role of the “lactate shuttle” to sustain neuronal activity (Aubertet al., 2005; Dienel and Cruz, 2006; Schurr, 2006)
>
> We hypothetize that under stress conditions neurons are more likely todepend on lactate; if the brain is under prolonged stress and both glucose andlactate transport are defective, MD begins. As a consequence of MD, there is adysfunction of the dopaminergic reward system, and then an increase inglucose consumption as an attempt to compensate.**
>
> .....
>
> Therefore, a failure in the activity of lactate-excited neurons would leadto the silencing of glucosensing neurons and then to an increase of glucoselevels in the blood (but not reaching the neurons), causing hyperglycemia. Thehyperglycemia would then cause the observed insulin resistance, possiblycombined with low ATP levels in cortical neurons.**

I'm supposed to be in phase 3 with my NTs now (not tested yet but on the amount of support Dr. Hinz/Neuroresearch calculated I need - no idea how they did that ). I'm still dealing with feeling hypoglycemic while actually being hyperglycemic though.

# Thread: head hurts when submerged.
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/head-hurts-when-submerged.3256/>
**Subforum:** Cold Thermogenesis
**Posts:** 6
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### growltgc — 2012-08-25T20:00:38+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/head-hurts-when-submerged.3256/post-47067>

I've been swimming in 53 degree water. It's called Arcata/Humboldt bay incase anyone is curious. located in northern california.

If I start out putting my head under too fast and for too long, then I get quite a bit of pain when I stop and return to upright position. why does this happen, and does it mean I should be doing the transition to slower? i.e accustom my head better to the cold by going slower?

usually I do start swimming with my head above water, but transition to in the water because it is easier to swim that way.there is a tolerance that builds up. what I mean by this is that as I keep my head submerged for longer lengths, the time extends I can put my head under without feel pain after coming back up.

What I notice, is I can get to the point, where I can do normal swimming without pain. but if I float on my back for a couple minutes with the back of my head completely submerged for that time period, that part of my head will hurt when I go back to upright position. It's only for a couple of seconds that I feel the pain, then is disappears.

### Souldanzer — 2012-08-25T20:03:37+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/head-hurts-when-submerged.3256/post-47068>

hmmm.... maybe that's the ice-cream-frozen-brain effect caused by low omega 3s? Honestly, I have no idea. I stick my head in ice water all the time and it sometimes hurts a lot but only when I have a real bad headache at the time and only while my head is in the water.

### ealachan — 2012-08-25T20:23:24+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/head-hurts-when-submerged.3256/post-47073>

I tend to get a "brain-freeze" sensation if I put my forehead right under the cold shower spray or dunk forehead-first into cold water, but it doesn't happen when I let cold water hit the top, sides or back of my head at all.

### growltgc — 2012-08-25T22:01:02+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/head-hurts-when-submerged.3256/post-47106>

I guess it could be comparable to brain freeze. kind of hard for me to consider, because I only remember getting brain-freeze once or twice in my life. At some theme park, when I had one of the cold ice drinks... well, the pain when I'm swimming, it's a pretty strong ache. I know that's not too descriptive. sometimes it's at the back of my neck as well.

I should add that previously I was swimming in water probably around 3-5 degrees warmer, about 5 hours south, and I got the pain a lot less when I submerged my head. It still was there to different extends though.

### growltgc — 2012-08-28T07:34:30+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/head-hurts-when-submerged.3256/post-47731>

I had a weird experience today. It's a slightly different topic, but is about me swimming in the bay.

I jumped in today and i didn't feel the shock that I expected too. It felt like maybe jumping into a 65 degree pool. this made me wonder if I would really feel so much pain in my head after submerging. And, I didn't. just a very mild feeling.

I really believe the water must have been warmer... but I swim at the same time I always do, the tide going to the same way. So I'm confused.

The last thing, is that I shivered less. I only really shivered for 5 minutes. and very small trembles every now and then for the next 15 minutes. which is different than before.

maybe my body is adapting in some way. I plan to swim most days this week, so I'll see how it goes.

### growltgc — 2012-08-30T00:13:50+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/head-hurts-when-submerged.3256/post-48201>

I looked at some temperature data from some site within weather.gov. the bay actually changes temperatures pretty drastically which the main influence is the tide I suspect. I'm kind of interested to graph some data which I already started doing to see what the trend is and figure out what the cause is.

when I swam on Sat. water temp = 56.6

Monday = 62.6

Tuesday =62.2

Each day I swam at aprox. 4:30pm

So that basically explains why I didn't feel the shock or shiver much the last two times. Anyways its pretty cool I can know the temperature each time I swim, so I can more accurately understand the response of my body to the water.

I l**ike spaghetti squash but wanted to let you know that the link in your post is broken, at least it is not working for me using Win7 and Brave browser.**

Been researching what 'veggies' I can have with all my food sensitivities esp gluten and nightshades ..so no tomato sauce for me. Also looking for good tastes for my picky family. **popcomsally** if you see this please check that breakfast recipe for me. and **Endless** if you have tips for growing Spaghetti Squash in central Virginia if you see this please post

**Hoping this squash is not too 'carby'... technically it is a fruit. in the melon family** I think? POO! Now going to have to find out 'fructose' content and net Net carbs. I eat very little fruit at all and when I do I still practice 'Natural Hygiene' Proper Food combining which teaches to eat fruits alone with other fruits but for certain _**Eat Melon ALL Alone or Don't !**_ Fruit digests much more quickly and their thinking is that it ferments on top of foods like meats that digest a lot longer using different enzymes to break things down. It is just a habit of mine left over for the 70's It got rid of my indigestion and acid refluxe back then and I rarely never had those since. The Book **Fit for Life** by Harvey Diamond and his wife introduced me to that thinking back then. I think I kept **Fit for Life II one of his later editions.**

At Is Spaghetti Squash A Winter Or Summer? Post author By [Elvira Bowen](https://sweetishhill.com/author/elvira-bowen/)
[https://sweetishhill.com/is-spaghetti-squash-a-winter-or-summer/](https://sweetishhill.com/is-spaghetti-squash-a-winter-or-summer/)
I read that it was good for constipation but when ever I hear that a plant or herb has a laxative effect... I always wonder if it is working in a good way or because it is toxic and the body is set to get it out of the body ASAP.

****So this Melon may not fly with bacon and eggs????**
Spaghetti Squash Anyone?**

# Thread: Optimal unflood service?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/optimal-unflood-service.3277/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 10
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Raiken3712 — 2012-08-26T23:17:28+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/optimal-unflood-service.3277/post-47333>

So I was a retard and left a bathtub with a an overflow stopper. I flooded my mom's whole bedroom and some of the living room. I need to find as cheap an option as possible without paying a fortune. Please advise this idiot....

I'm in Lewisville, TX

### Souldanzer — 2012-08-26T23:20:28+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/optimal-unflood-service.3277/post-47335>

lol.... eventually one of us had to do this

sorry not really helpful here

### Raiken3712 — 2012-08-26T23:21:48+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/optimal-unflood-service.3277/post-47337>

Somehow I'm not hysterical yet...diet related or just my brain hasn't gotten the message yet? Anyway anyone know anything?

### nonchalant — 2012-08-26T23:25:15+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/optimal-unflood-service.3277/post-47338>

A wet/dry vac would pick up the water, and you could use fans to complete the drying. You may be able to rent a wet/dry vac at Home Depot or a grocery store. Oh, and a carpet cleaning machine would also pick up water.

### Raiken3712 — 2012-08-26T23:31:20+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/optimal-unflood-service.3277/post-47342>

Anyone have opinions on [http://dallaswaterdamages.com/water-damage/lewisville-tx/](http://http://dallaswaterdamages.com/water-damage/lewisville-tx/)

Was planning on using Water restoration service. It appears they have free estimates...I'm hoping they don't charge a ton.

### Lyndra — 2012-08-26T23:33:58+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/optimal-unflood-service.3277/post-47345>

Was this a ground floor, second floor, etc? Can you pull up the flooring (carpeting?) to see if the underlayment has sustained damage or look from below from the basement or crawlspace? Flood clean-up companies will bring out industrial-sized fans that will dry things out much faster than any fan you're likely to have.

### Lyndra — 2012-08-26T23:35:22+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/optimal-unflood-service.3277/post-47346>

> **Raiken3712;47785 wrote:** Anyone have opinions on [http://dallaswaterdamages.com/water-damage/lewisville-tx/](http://http://dallaswaterdamages.com/water-damage/lewisville-tx/)
>
>
>
> Was planning on using Water restoration service. It appears they have free estimates...I'm hoping they don't charge a ton.

Get a couple of quotes, but whatever they charge is probably worth it. The sooner the water is cleaned up, the less you need to worry about long-term effects.

### Raiken3712 — 2012-08-26T23:50:01+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/optimal-unflood-service.3277/post-47351>

Its only a one floor house. Its two rooms...pretty much only one room because the other has already been dried was laminate floor. The other room is carpet.

### jeanie@truelongmont.com — 2012-08-27T04:52:40+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/optimal-unflood-service.3277/post-47419>

> **Raiken3712;47785 wrote:** Anyone have opinions on [http://dallaswaterdamages.com/water-damage/lewisville-tx/](http://http://dallaswaterdamages.com/water-damage/lewisville-tx/)
>
>
>
> Was planning on using Water restoration service. It appears they have free estimates...I'm hoping they don't charge a ton.

I'm in real estate and do plenty of renovations/building, and helping clients with stuff like this.

Unless you get it out and dry early, you may want to suck it up and go with the pro water restoration.

Consider the costs of that vs the cost of replacing carpets. . ..also, it can often be covered by insurance. Replacing carpets isn't as expensive as people think. BUT, specially down there in wet texas - do not leave wet carpet/carpet pad sitting for more than 24 hrs.

The walls, if you can pull the water out quickly will dry. Also, even wood floors that swell will "unswell" over time (3-6 months).

Most important thing is to get as much water out as quickly as possible. Wet/Dry vacs are cheap. GO NOW!

### Raiken3712 — 2012-08-27T05:01:37+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/optimal-unflood-service.3277/post-47422>

I went ahead and paid around 700 dollars to get them to remove the water and now fans are sitting on it to dry.....what an expensive mistake blah.

Oh well I'll deal. Thanks for the advice

# Thread: Glutathione, Melatonin
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/glutathione-melatonin.3281/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 1
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### chocolate — 2012-08-27T06:04:33+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/glutathione-melatonin.3281/post-47437>

[http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/20868358](http://http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/20868358)

> The glutathione system and its regulation by neurohormone melatonin in the central nervous system.
>
> LimÃ³n-Pacheco JH, Gonsebatt ME.
>
> Source
>
>
>
> Departamento de NeurobiologÃ­a Conductual y Cognitiva, Instituto de NeurobiologÃ­a, Universidad Nacional AutÃ³noma de MÃ©xico, Boulevard Juriquilla 3001, QuerÃ©taro, MÃ©xico.
>
> Abstract
>
>
>
> The glutathione system includes reduced (GSH) and oxidized (GSSG) forms of glutathione; the enzymes required for its synthesis and recycling, such as gamma-glutamate cysteine ligase (γ-GCL), glutathione synthetase (GS), glutathione reductase (GSR) and gamma glutamyl transpeptidase (γ-GGT); and the enzymes required for its use in metabolism and in mechanisms of defense against free radical-induced oxidative damage, such as glutathione s-transferases (GSTs) and glutathione peroxidases (GPxs). **Glutathione functions in the central nervous system (CNS) include maintenance of neurotransmitters, membrane protection, detoxification, metabolic regulation, and modulation of signal transduction. A common pathological hallmark in various neurodegenerative disorders, such as amyotrophic lateral sclerosis and Alzheimer's and Parkinson's diseases is the increase in oxidative stress and the failure of antioxidant systems, such as the decrease in the GSH content. The administration of exogenous neurohormone melatonin at pharmacological doses has been shown not only to be an effective scavenger of reactive oxygen and nitrogen species but also to enhance the levels of GSH and the expression and activities of the GSH-related enzymes including γ-GCL, GPxs, and GSR. The exact mechanisms by which melatonin regulates the glutathione system are not fully understood.** The main purpose of this short review is to discuss evidence relating to the potential common modulation signals between the glutathione system and melatonin in the CNS. The potential regulatory mechanisms and interactions between neurons and non-neuronal cells are also discussed.

Just what are pharmacological doses?


══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# Thread: OK to get a little of the bad stuff if you are getting a lot of good stuff?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ok-to-get-a-little-of-the-bad-stuff-if-you-are-getting-a-lot-of-good-stuff.3308/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 6
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### kjgraffin@yahoo.com — 2012-08-28T03:28:37+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ok-to-get-a-little-of-the-bad-stuff-if-you-are-getting-a-lot-of-good-stuff.3308/post-47656>

I'm trying out a new medical clinic that has me really excited. They have a lot of MD's & a couple of naturopaths. The MDs and labs are covered by my insurance. While waiting on the results of my comprehensive stool test, the naturopath has set me up with a supplement plan to help heal my gut & also to support my adrenals. It includes upping my L-Glutamine to 5g/day, switching to one of their probiotic supplements that include Immunoglobulins, using a cream for adrenal support & taking 2 scoops of their Foundation Gut Enhancement powder. The Gut Enhancement powder has a lot of good stuff that I need, but also a little "bad" stuff, namely Sunflower Oil, pea protein isolate, Rice Protein concentrate, Carageenan, evaporated cane juice & Brown Rice Syrup solids. A total of 16g carbs. I haven't decided if it is something I want to take, but I also don't want to throw out the baby with the bath water.

Any thoughts?

### Souldanzer — 2012-08-28T03:37:42+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ok-to-get-a-little-of-the-bad-stuff-if-you-are-getting-a-lot-of-good-stuff.3308/post-47660>

I wouldn't but that's just me.

### jeanie@truelongmont.com — 2012-08-28T21:19:57+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ok-to-get-a-little-of-the-bad-stuff-if-you-are-getting-a-lot-of-good-stuff.3308/post-47867>

Poison is in the dose?

If it were me, I might point out to them you are "uncomfortable" with all of those other ingredients and even ask if there is something you could do instead to get the good stuff without the bad.

Is it the kind of thing where that's a profit item for the clinic? If that is the case, they won't be open to discussions. . just pass.

### ealachan — 2012-08-28T21:31:40+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ok-to-get-a-little-of-the-bad-stuff-if-you-are-getting-a-lot-of-good-stuff.3308/post-47873>

> **kjgraffin;48107 wrote:** I'm trying out a new medical clinic that has me really excited. They have a lot of MD's & a couple of naturopaths. The MDs and labs are covered by my insurance. While waiting on the results of my comprehensive stool test, the naturopath has set me up with a supplement plan to help heal my gut & also to support my adrenals. It includes upping my L-Glutamine to 5g/day, switching to one of their probiotic supplements that include Immunoglobulins, using a cream for adrenal support & taking 2 scoops of their Foundation Gut Enhancement powder. The Gut Enhancement powder has a lot of good stuff that I need, but also a little "bad" stuff, namely Sunflower Oil, pea protein isolate, Rice Protein concentrate, Carageenan, evaporated cane juice & Brown Rice Syrup solids. A total of 16g carbs. I haven't decided if it is something I want to take, but I also don't want to throw out the baby with the bath water.
>
>
>
> Any thoughts?

It sounds to me like they're trying to get you to pay for and take stuff that makes them money. If that's the case, I'd pass.

### Shijin13 — 2012-08-28T21:41:10+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ok-to-get-a-little-of-the-bad-stuff-if-you-are-getting-a-lot-of-good-stuff.3308/post-47879>

> **ealachan;48324 wrote:** It sounds to me like they're trying to get you to pay for and take stuff that makes them money. If that's the case, I'd pass.

THIS.... just say no. my chrio tries to get me to buy stuff I don't need... I tell him I've got all the sups I need.... DH on the other hand...is buying the stuff chiro is selling him... but he's got to figure out what he needs/doesn't need...

### kjgraffin@yahoo.com — 2012-08-29T02:48:01+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ok-to-get-a-little-of-the-bad-stuff-if-you-are-getting-a-lot-of-good-stuff.3308/post-47996>

So I found a powder on Life Extension's website that has almost the exact same nutrients, but not any of the things I wanted to avoid in the powder I got from my doctor's office. There is a bit of a trade-off as the Life Extension product has maltodextrin derived from corn.

Acceptable alternative? (The clinic will take back the other powder since it is not opened.)

# Thread: luxury CT opportunity in Carmel.
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/luxury-ct-opportunity-in-carmel.3317/>

**Posts:** 4

### janagram — 2012-08-28T20:24:29+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/luxury-ct-opportunity-in-carmel.3317/post-47829>

At my recent retreat at Asilomar, a friend invited me to bop over to "The Refuge" in Carmel Valley for an afternoon of tub hopping! There among the /boulders and palms were a multitude of smallish pools of varying temps. The cold was verry cold and I could only walk thru it up to my bra-line...I stayed in it for a couple minutes at most. But it was kind of awesome...when you get out you feel warm...(cuz anything's warmer than that)

I confess I am a superwimp but I thought of you guys and how fun it would be for you. Then there's a huge dry sauna, and all the rest.

[http://www.refuge.com/the\_refuge\_experience\_two.html](http://http://www.refuge.com/the_refuge_experience_two.html)

# Thread: Weaned toddler needs + seasonality of macros
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/weaned-toddler-needs-seasonality-of-macros.3318/>
**Subforum:** Optimal Kids
**Posts:** 5
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### JoeBranca — 2012-08-28T21:44:53+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/weaned-toddler-needs-seasonality-of-macros.3318/post-47880>

We have a 19 month old toddler (mama stopped producing enough milk/toddler lost interest around 15-16 months) and for solid foods she's doing pretty good but I'm still not clear how much we should be watching what she gets as far as protein/fats/carbs. According to this recent post, it should mimic healthy breastmilk ratios:

[http://www.thepaleomom.com/2012/08/carbohydrate-recommendations-for-kids.html](http://http://www.thepaleomom.com/2012/08/carbohydrate-recommendations-for-kids.html)

And I wonder if that's sound advice.

As far as carbs, she gets raw pastured goat milk daily, but also lots and lots of fruit in addition to sweet potato at most meals. My confusion is if this is a good thing or suboptimal for toddler development, as it replaces what could be more caloric intake of brain developing fats, and perhaps in the long run encourages a preference for the sweets over other things if presented as choices.

Should I not worry about fruit intake for the rest of summer and just have mom titrate down the fruit into the fall and winter months, so that the majority of toddler's carbs are from the goat milk and veggies? Are sweet potatoes a good year round thing or should that be seasonal as well?

Well, to make the question simpler, for a young toddler do you tweak the seasonality principle according to context of their particular developmental needs? Should I instead forget seasonal macro ratios altogether and be thinking purely in terms of micro nutritional intake?

Am I overthinking this?

### Shijin13 — 2012-08-28T22:15:59+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/weaned-toddler-needs-seasonality-of-macros.3318/post-47893>

Here's what I do. I have a 2yo (still nursing 1x/day) & 4yo.

Breakfast always includes protein & FAT same for lunch & dinner

This summer we've done a lot of talking about seasonal fruits w/our 4yo. We no longer buy fruit out of season

Fruit is our desert and we only have it w/meals. Dd is almost able to handle 3 meals/day but not always so when she's hungry we offer protein/FAT combo

Ds who's 2 eats on instinct. Every am while making bkfst he asks for plane ghee before his meal. So I give it to him some days he'll eat 3-4 TBS of ghee. He's still eating more on a nursing schedule of an older toddler every 3-4 hrs, even though he's not nursing throughout the day

Dd in the winter naturally shuns veggies/carbs i no longer fight her on that. If all she wants is protein/fat that's what she eats. She knows this intuitively

With both kids we've really been pushing the epipaleo and tgey've embraced it. They love their shrimp, mussels, oysters and squid (too ds a couple of wks to get there but he now asks if he can have squid).

Give them unlimited access to the good stuff and that's what they'll ask for. Your dd will follow your lead what you eat she'll eat

### jeanie@truelongmont.com — 2012-08-28T22:37:19+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/weaned-toddler-needs-seasonality-of-macros.3318/post-47897>

> **Shijin13;48344 wrote:** Here's what I do. I have a 2yo (still nursing 1x/day) & 4yo.
>
>
>
> Breakfast always includes protein & FAT same for lunch & dinner
>
>
>
> This summer we've done a lot of talking about seasonal fruits w/our 4yo. We no longer buy fruit out of season
>
> Fruit is our desert and we only have it w/meals. Dd is almost able to handle 3 meals/day but not always so when she's hungry we offer protein/FAT combo
>
>
>
> Ds who's 2 eats on instinct. Every am while making bkfst he asks for plane ghee before his meal. So I give it to him some days he'll eat 3-4 TBS of ghee. He's still eating more on a nursing schedule of an older toddler every 3-4 hrs, even though he's not nursing throughout the day
>
>
>
> Dd in the winter naturally shuns veggies/carbs i no longer fight her on that. If all she wants is protein/fat that's what she eats. She knows this intuitively
>
>
>
> With both kids we've really been pushing the epipaleo and tgey've embraced it. They love their shrimp, mussels, oysters and squid (too ds a couple of wks to get there but he now asks if he can have squid).
>
>
>
> Give them unlimited access to the good stuff and that's what they'll ask for. Your dd will follow your lead what you eat she'll eat

Playdate? Arranged marriage?

### Shijin13 — 2012-08-28T22:58:39+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/weaned-toddler-needs-seasonality-of-macros.3318/post-47911>

> **Cavemam;48348 wrote:** Playdate? Arranged marriage?

LOL.... I think the epi-paleo kids will find their own kind.... w/o our interference...

### kathylu — 2012-08-29T06:39:05+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/weaned-toddler-needs-seasonality-of-macros.3318/post-48031>

I still struggle with this. My 5 year old eats well, but not as cleanly as I would like. We often have to bribe him with fruit..."if you eat your shrimp and steak, then you can have grapes", etc. I have gotten very tough about introducing foods though. He automatically rejects anything he hasn't seen before. So, I just state "this is dinner. If you don't want to eat, fine but nothing else tonight. And dinner will make a great breakfast tomorrow! We'll save it for you." He is slowly coming around. A big part of the problem is that we sent him to pre-k where they had terrible junky snacks and parties almost every day. I can't believe it, but parents would bake full size cupcakes and adorn them with icing and candies and give it to the kids with juice boxes. Then wonder why they were bouncing off the walls a few minutes later. I caved to peer pressure though...I let him eat these things thinking a few times per week wouldn't really hurt. Boy, did it ever! He would beg and cry for cereal in the mornings, because "everyone else eats it!" Well, we are homeschooling now and have almost broken the bad habits. Still a work in progress.

# Thread: Breast lump
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/breast-lump.3330/>
**Subforum:** Cold Thermogenesis
**Posts:** 12
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Mery — 2012-08-29T15:20:13+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/breast-lump.3330/post-48069>

Iâ€™d like to share my experience with a lump I developed on my right breast.

Iâ€™ve been putting ice on my breast and tummy as it was the only way to get some CT. I did this on and off, as it was not easy to begin with and then in the middle of July I started applying the ice daily. At the start, the longest I could manage was 15 minutes but I slowly increased the time, now I can last 35 to 40 minutes.

On July 27, I noticed a lump on my right breast on the lower part on the left side. The diameter was 2 inch by 1 inch, I asked my husband to check for me. This is when I started having pain on the lymphatic area under my armpit every time I lifted my arm and after a few days the pain went.

At this time I was having problem with my ears as I could not hear and I was feeling a bit down so I went to the doctor to have my ear checked. I mentioned to the doctor that my sister has a lump and the doctor said that she should have it checked. I said that she didnâ€™t like mammograms and the doctor said they donâ€™t do that anymore that instead they do a scan. I didnâ€™t say it was me because I knew they would send me letters.

After my ears were cleaned, I felt more positive, throughout all this stressful time I was putting ice on my breast and tummy and I noticed that the lump was getting smaller and on Friday I checked it and it was gone.

Iâ€™m so happy and being calm, not telling the GP has paid off. I still got my 2 beautiful ladies and now they are firm and can pass the pencil test.

Like Dr Jack said no toxin will survive CT, thanks Dr Jack

# Thread: How does Hashi's destroy your hormones?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-does-hashis-destroy-your-hormones.3332/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 7

### bigknitwit — 2012-08-29T17:34:01+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-does-hashis-destroy-your-hormones.3332/post-48088>

This was mentioned to me in my consult. Hashimoto's destroys thyroid hormones, then comes insulin resistance, food intolerances, poor Pg/E2 ratios.

First goes the thyroid hormones, and oral replacement is needed. What is the mechanism that makes the other hormones fall, and how do we lose hormone control at the brain level? Is this all thanks to high levels of inflammation? How does the insulin resistance come about? I am trying to figure out the full cascade of events to get it all clear in my head (going to the doctor tomorrow).

### MartiD — 2012-08-29T17:38:25+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-does-hashis-destroy-your-hormones.3332/post-48089>

I'd be interested in this as well as I just was diagnosed with hashi's and have an appt. in two weeks. I am hoping this new Dr. will be proactive instead of just wanting to wait around for the antibodies to destroy the thyroid and then treat with replacement.

### nonchalant — 2012-08-29T17:55:39+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-does-hashis-destroy-your-hormones.3332/post-48093>

Hi knit. I bet Dr. K mentioned that you should read the Hormone 101 blog. Your body converts the cholesterol in your LDL, using T3 from the thyroid and vitamin A, into the precursor hormone, pregnenolone. Then pregnenolone becomes cortisol, or it becomes DHEA, the various sex hormones, and vitamin D.

Hashi's attacks the thyroid, which lowers the T3 needed early in this process. So all those hormones suffer.

Inflammation comes into the picture by increasing stress. If the body is stressed, cortisol rises. So what little pregnenolone the body makes, is all used up by the more important function of keeping cortisol high to deal with stress. And the other hormones crash.

That was all from memory, Dr. K. How did I do?

### vkiernan — 2012-08-29T20:50:33+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-does-hashis-destroy-your-hormones.3332/post-48150>

> Originally Posted by Nonchalant
>
> Hi knit. I bet Dr. K mentioned that you should read the Hormone 101 blog. Your body converts the cholesterol in your LDL, using T3 from the thyroid and vitamin A, into the precursor hormone, pregnenolone. Then pregnenolone becomes cortisol, or it becomes DHEA, the various sex hormones, and vitamin D.

> **bigknitwit;48548 wrote:** Thank you!! This was fantastic!
>
> Now how does insulin resistance start cmoing into the picture?

The question is how does IR come into the picture, per the following you become LR first, then IR and then AR. The following blog: Hormones 101 - Clinical thoughts revealed answers all the questions. It is a great blog. Always remember, rising cortisol assists in the "get fat" dept. per the following

I want to now give you some perspective as to WHY this matters.

We have established that as one gets fat, Leptin levels rise. Once they get high enough (around a Body Mass Index (BMI) of 20-24), Tumor Necrosis Factor (TNF) rises in several tissues. TNF quickly destroys normal hepatic homeostasis, which sets the stage for fatty liver disease and type two diabetes over time. This rise in TNF also biochemically changes Leptin receptor signaling and changes its quantum properties by changing its â€œresonanceâ€ (think of a vibration like effect in the receptor) at the hypothalamus level. Once TNF rises, it causes the liver to make an acute phase inflammatory protein called highly sensitive CRP. HS-CRP is therefore a very early biomarker for cellular inflammation before any disease is established. We have already established in earlier blogs that inflammation causes Leptin resistance. Leptin controls all energy production in the body. Inflammation stops T4 to T3 conversion in the liver and abruptly turns off your thyroidâ€™s ability to function properly despite normal thyroid labs. As an analogy, your car engine no longer has a gas pedal to use. Simultaneously Vitamin D production caves as well because TNF takes that out too. Immunity fails and bad things commence for the cellular terroir. (Levee 1)

In Summary, once Leptin resistance occurs centrally in the brain, the liver soon follows and then the peripheral tissues become resistant too. This effects fecundity, bone metabolism, cardiac metabolism, the thyroid, and the immune system in that order. Are you with me?

RESULTANT HORMONE CASCADE

Leptin resistance occurs first. Then insulin resistance happens next. This eventually leads to adrenal resistance. Cortisol is the stress hormone that allows for fight or flight syndrome (life or death). This is the hormone that allows you to run away from a hungry lion fast and live. Evolution always makes sure cortisol production stays ready for action at the expense of the other hormones that also are made from the same precursors. That precursor is pregnenolone. Pregnenolone is made from cholesterol, and cholesterol is made from LDL. So anytime the body is stressed or inflamed, it up-regulates cholesterol production to make more lifesaving hormones. It requires T3 and vitamin A as cofactors to complete this step. Blocking cholesterol production will increase cellular stress. This is why biochemically, to me, no statin drugs have ever made any sense under any circumstance in medicine. Moreover, this is why there is a chronic association of cancer to low cholesterol levels in the literature. In times of infection or stress, LDL levels always need to rise to protect the cell.

A diet should provide a substrate of animal protein to sustain the hormone substrates. This is why a Paleo diet is ideal for hormone health because it provides ample substrate for all hormones. A Paleolithic diet is best for longevity in my opinion. It supports all of our hormones that are present in abundance in youth and health. Many other physicians, like Dr. Ron Rosedale, tell folks that protein stimulates the mTOR pathway which leads to death. The real issue I have there is that he gives no context to the statement. Here is my context. His belief is only true if the background cellular terroir has a high HS CRP back drop, and if your gut is leaky as shown by a low HDL level. If your HS CRP is treated aggressively with a Paleo diet, your HS CRP will be low and your HDL will be high, and then a moderate protein diet is protective. So I go hard after HS CRP in all of the patients I work with. It is a staple in my practice. Once solved, it is hard-core Paleolithic Robb Wolf style advice to affect the most important part of our biology.

HORMONES. So anytime cellular stress is high (high HS CRP), it also forces all the hormone backbone substrate called pregnenolone to be shunted to cortisol production. This is called pregnenolone steal syndrome. What exactly does this mean, Doc? DHEA, Androstenedione, Vitamin D, testosterone, estrogen, and aldosterone production all fall dramatically. These are all of the hormones that are made from a common precursor. Remember that chronic Leptin resistance leads to huge hypercortisolism all the time! NEVER FORGET THIS BIOLOGIC FACT! So that means we need to understand well what high cortisol does to the cellular terroir as well. (Levee 1)

This also means that Leptin resistance clinically will lead to low vitamin D levels. This completely explains the epidemic that John Cannell, MD is reporting about in the Vitamin D council newsletters. This in turn down regulates T regulator cells of the immune system, and it will decrease bone metabolism as well since vitamin D is a cofactor in bone metabolism. Since DHEA and Androstenedione are lowered too, the sex steroids are also lowered because they are made from DHEA and Andro. In younger humans this leads to early andropause, low libido, and early onset perimenopause any time stress is present. When this occurs in older humans, like postmenopausal women, it destroys libido and electrolyte balance (low aldosterone effects) and causes osteopenia and osteoporosis. Younger females lose control of fecundity, oocyte maturation, and get amenorrhea. Evolution does not favor these activities when your stress hormone is high. It favors survival over these other activities. This observation was mind shattering to my practice when I realized it. In men, longevity is tied to their optimized testosterone levels. Here is a link that confirms this. [http://www.nature.com/ijo/journal/v27/n7/fig\_tab/0802292f1.html](http://http://www.nature.com/ijo/journal/v27/n7/fig_tab/0802292f1.html)

The high cortisol also directly affects the Hypothalamic-pituitary-adrenal (HPA) axis too. Cortisol directly blocks 5′ deiodinase enzyme that converts T4 to T3 (this occurs in the liver). Cortisol Releasing Hormone (CRH) (seen elevated in high cortisol production states) directly blocks TSH. The implication is huge because Thyroid Stimulating Hormone (TSH) and T3, the active thyroid hormone, are inhibited quickly in this process. Immediately, any excess T4 is then shunted to reverse T3. Reverse T3 is a COMPETITIVE inhibitor to T3, the active thyroid hormone. This basically turns off the thyroid! (Youâ€™re welcome ladies.), This is a biological switch needed to shut metabolism off in starvation mode. This is precisely what happens in starvation or in anorexia. Once T3 is turned off, no fat burning can occur at the muscles with UCP3. Remember UCP3 activation requires T4, T3 and Leptin to be working well. With high cortisol it cannot. Shutting off these things could be good biologically, if youâ€™re badly starving or if youâ€™re morbidly obese. CRH also directly inhibits TSH. This is why TSH is a horrible marker for thyroid status. If you do not know the cortisol status you can infer zero information from a thyroid panel. This is reason number one for many thyroid misdiagnoses. The sad part is that most endocrinologists seem to have forgotten this vital biochemical fact. It is the source of most patient frustrations with their thyroid condition.

CRH directly blocks secretion of Growth Hormone secretion as well. This means you get sarcopenia and osteopenia together! Low GH levels (IGF1) increase your body fat, decrease your lean muscle mass and increase your osteopenia to great degree. It also causes the cardiac muscle to fail and decreases the stroke volume. Sarcopenia is the result of low IGF1 or GH level, and is a harbinger of ensuing death, especially with respect to the heart. There is now excellent data to support the use of GH and testosterone for cardiac health in aging because of these effects. High cortisol comes from stress. The causes of stress in humans are: psychological, traumatic, infectious, allergic, electromagnetic (EMF), xenobiotic and geopathic, as well as Leptin resistance and dysbiosis.

Key points about cortisol.

1\. Anything that causes a chronic elevation of cortisol causes chronic disease.

2\. Any chronic elevation of cortisol and insulin will lead to some kind of chronic disease and death.

3\. Signs and symptoms that you really have stress and a cortisol problem will cause most medicine docs to put you on one or more of these ten medications: 1. Statin 2. Premarin 3. Synthroid 4. Prilosec 5. Hydrocodone 6.Norvasc 7. glucophage/metaformin 8. Albuterol 9. Claritin 10. Prozac/sleeping pills.

I was just re-reading this blog, and the leap I'm struggling with is:

I'm feeling pretty slow at the moment. Leptin resistance turns off the thyroid and the active thyroid hormones. Hashimotos destroys these hormones before they're even made. # Thread: Leptin reset and raised blood pressure
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/leptin-reset-and-raised-blood-pressure.3335/>
**Subforum:** The Leptin Rx
**Posts:** 19
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### somewhatchubby — 2012-08-29T19:53:21+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/leptin-reset-and-raised-blood-pressure.3335/post-48127>

Has anyone experienced raised blood pressure from doing paleo or the Leptin reset.

I managed to get my husband to do the reset with me and today after rectal endoscopy (because his iron has been found to be low)after a prostate flare up. Nothing was found although they want to do the tests from the other end now.!! What they did find was that his usually normal blood pressure had risen. Of course the stress of the procedure could be a contributory factor .

He is three weeks into the diet.I seem to remember someone else saying there blood pressure had risen and was wondering if it became lowered again.

Also before going on the diet he used to eat a lot of antacids and Ive read somewhere that antacids can interupt iron absorbtion.

Since cutting carbs (apart from a beer) he hasnt needed to take antacids.

### ealachan — 2012-08-29T20:15:06+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/leptin-reset-and-raised-blood-pressure.3335/post-48133>

My blood pressure has improved on the reset, to the point where I'm working my way off of medication now.

Did they tell you to keep track of his blood pressure at home for a while? Seems like it would be pretty hard to deem someone as having chronic elevated blood pressure just from one reading which happened to be in a stressful situation. I have "White Coat Syndrome", wherein my blood pressure is always higher at the doctor's office because I find it stressful no matter how much I try to convince myself that I'm calm. This is why I have my own cuff at home and keep daily track of my blood pressure there. If my doctor went only by the readings she got in the office when I go in, I'd be on even more medications than just the one I take.

Also, tell him to knock it off with the beer. It's like drinking liquid bread. I had to learn this the hard way. I love beer, but it doesn't love me.

### Patty Cakes — 2012-08-29T20:15:33+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/leptin-reset-and-raised-blood-pressure.3335/post-48134>

My blood pressure went down within the first 30 days of doing the Leptin Rx. I was on a HBP medicine and now have been off for 7 months.

### somewhatchubby — 2012-08-29T20:24:34+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/leptin-reset-and-raised-blood-pressure.3335/post-48138>

Hi ealachan and patty cakes, blimey that was quick !Great to hear that both of you have been able to get off your meds. Hubby is 64 and isnt on any meds at all which is why its come as a surprise.

What worries me most is that once the docs start you never know where it will end. Although I'm a believer and I have told him about things I have read in 'Trick or Treat' by Barry Groves. However ,for him,in a docs surgery , is he likely to be swayed. ?

I have tried to get him off the beer ,trouble is if I push too hard he may give up. I figure giving up all else should help .He does like a drink at night and half a pint ,although I know its bad could be worse.

He has lost weight already.

So, we may just have to wait and see what happens.

Are BP cuffs very expensive ?

thanks for your responses.

### ealachan — 2012-08-29T20:29:31+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/leptin-reset-and-raised-blood-pressure.3335/post-48141>

> **somewhatchubby;48589 wrote:** Hi ealachan and patty cakes, blimey that was quick !Great to hear that both of you have been able to get off your meds. Hubby is 64 and isnt on any meds at all which is why its come as a surprise.
>
> What worries me most is that once the docs start you never know where it will end. Although I'm a believer and I have told him about things I have read in 'Trick or Treat' by Barry Groves. However ,for him,in a docs surgery , is he likely to be swayed. ?
>
> I have tried to get him off the beer ,trouble is if I push too hard he may give up. I figure giving up all else should help .He does like a drink at night and half a pint ,although I know its bad could be worse.
>
> He has lost weight already.
>
> So, we may just have to wait and see what happens.
>
> Are BP cuffs very expensive ?
>
> thanks for your responses.

Mine was $70, but I had to get a special sized cuff because I have extra-big upper arms. If you get one, make sure you measure your DH's upper arm and get the correct cuff size...it makes a difference.

Also, unless the doctor is talking about dragging your DH back in to put him on meds for the blood pressure, I wouldn't worry about it too much. Like I said - it's hard for them to claim he has a chronic problem from just one whacked-out reading. Blood pressure is not a static thing - it changes constantly all day long, depending on what you're doing.

### Patty Cakes — 2012-08-29T21:05:40+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/leptin-reset-and-raised-blood-pressure.3335/post-48155>

I use a wrist cuff by Omron. I've heard people say the wrist cuffs are not as reliable but I've tested mine against the drug store ones and its within a couple of points so mine is great. It's small enough to carry it anywhere, if I need to.

### somewhatchubby — 2012-08-29T22:00:47+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/leptin-reset-and-raised-blood-pressure.3335/post-48165>

hi ealachan, thanks for that. I will look into it.

His BP was 165 /? he cant remember the other reading. I personally think this has been coming on for a year or more. He ate alot of sweet stuff, lots of spuds,bread , cakes ,wine, biscuits potato crisps. just thinking about it turns me queazy. So you see how I have to be clever about how I proceed.?

I drink water but he wont ,he still needs some sweetness in there. I ponder over what I could get/make him to drink in the evening. Any ideas ?

### somewhatchubby — 2012-08-29T22:05:33+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/leptin-reset-and-raised-blood-pressure.3335/post-48170>

hi patty cakes, which model is that please ? There are several by the looks of things.

### ealachan — 2012-08-29T22:29:15+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/leptin-reset-and-raised-blood-pressure.3335/post-48177>

> **somewhatchubby;48618 wrote:** hi ealachan, thanks for that. I will look into it.
>
> His BP was 165 /? he cant remember the other reading. I personally think this has been coming on for a year or more. He ate alot of sweet stuff, lots of spuds,bread , cakes ,wine, biscuits potato crisps. just thinking about it turns me queazy. So you see how I have to be clever about how I proceed.?
>
> I drink water but he wont ,he still needs some sweetness in there. I ponder over what I could get/make him to drink in the evening. Any ideas ?

It's not going to sound fun, but in my opinion the easiest way to make the change needed is to do it like ripping off a band-aid: quickly and decisively. It will hurt (figuratively) for a few days, but it's far easier on the system than trying to "ease" into it and prolonging the mental pain...see what I mean?

I couldn't get my husband on board with the new way of eating until he was diagnosed with T2D. At that point, I had to sit him down and say "look, all these things that you're eating that you think are not so bad because you're only eating a little of them...they all add up, big-time. If you keep eating them, they will kill you. I don't want you to suffer like others in your family are doing from diabetic complications. This new way of eating will help you." He agreed, and while he was a bit wibbly over losing his beer and soda and sweets for a few days, he started to feel better almost immediately, and now we don't usually have an issue.

# Thread: Estimated macronutrient and fatty acid intakes from an East African Paleolithic diet
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/estimated-macronutrient-and-fatty-acid-intakes-from-an-east-african-paleolithic-diet.3336/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 1
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Michael — 2012-08-29T20:38:05+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/estimated-macronutrient-and-fatty-acid-intakes-from-an-east-african-paleolithic-diet.3336/post-48146>

This is interesting. This is an article to which both Remko S. Kuipers and Loren Cordain contributed.

[Estimated macronutrient and fatty acid intakes from an East African Paleolithic diet](http://http://journals.cambridge.org/download.php?file=%2F40019_F393045E162BC03BD0AFF6AC26AB2A7A_journals__BJN_BJN104_11_S0007114510002679a.pdf&cover=Y&code=cce1b7f1e41e422ed990e75cf703ee45)

I'd seen the [previous article from Cordain et al. from 2000](http://http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/10702160), -- it's often cited in the Paleosphere -- but this is ten years on.

You have to wonder how accurate a picture you're getting with all the assumptions that are imbedded in these models, but they are complex and deeply impressive models. 172 references for the paper.

# Thread: Glucose Tolerance test and meter issues
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/glucose-tolerance-test-and-meter-issues.3339/>
**Subforum:** Optimal Labs
**Posts:** 11
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### bigknitwit — 2012-08-29T23:51:33+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/glucose-tolerance-test-and-meter-issues.3339/post-48195>

I did a 2 hour oral glucose tolerance test last week, hoping to fail and convince my endocrinologist to give me cycloset. I didn't fail enough for that purpose, unfortunately. My results were:

A1C 5.1%

Fasting Glucose: 73

Fasting Insulin less than 2

1 hour: 134

2 hour: 80

I brought my meter with me, and had a similar reading at the 1 hour (130), but got 136 for the 2 hour, which is pretty different from 80. I've never seen my FBG as low as 73, or 70anything, or 80anything on that meter. I usually see a FBG of 89-93. Are the meters really that unreliable?

This is my second meter. I bought one last summer, and got am readings consistently in the 120s. I thought I had developped late onset type 1 diabetes. Did a serum test and got lower 80s as the result.

Are some meters more reliable than others?

I got home from the test 30minutes later, retested and got a reading of 84, then 30 minutes later started feeling pretty bad (low blood sugar feeling), and got a reading of 56. So the meter isn't completely out to lunch, but it doesn't seem like something reliable to work with.

### Patty Cakes — 2012-08-30T00:15:28+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/glucose-tolerance-test-and-meter-issues.3339/post-48204>

I really don't know about meters. Which one do you have? I occasionally use the True Test and it seems fairly i line with my fasting BG.

### bigknitwit — 2012-08-30T00:22:18+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/glucose-tolerance-test-and-meter-issues.3339/post-48205>

I have the Walmart one - ReliOn

### Patty Cakes — 2012-08-30T00:27:28+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/glucose-tolerance-test-and-meter-issues.3339/post-48206>

Hopefully others will chime in and help. The ReliOn is different although I think Walgreens and CVS sell one like the one I have under a different name.

### jerry.malone@q.com — 2012-09-02T02:55:06+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/glucose-tolerance-test-and-meter-issues.3339/post-48840>

I have had very bad luck with ReliOn meters! The numbers jump all over the damn place.

Try taking several readings in a row, from the same prick, one right after the other. You will be amazed at the variance you get. Take at least 5.

I got TrueTrack meter instead, and I'm quite happy with it. The TrueTrack has 4 electrodes in the strip, unlike the others, and it takes a full 10 seconds to analyze the sample. I took several readings in a row, and I still see variance, but it's considerably less. (Often, two readings in a row are within a few points of each other.)

The nominal accuracy of all of these meters is +/- 20% So if your actual BG is 100, you could get any random number between 80 and 120. That's not very useful! So if I want a really accurate reading, or I have reason to think my BG is out of whack, I take at least 3 readings and average them. Never trust a single reading!

### mambo — 2012-09-03T09:17:04+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/glucose-tolerance-test-and-meter-issues.3339/post-49149>

ReliOn is generally reliable but last year they had some bad batches of strips which they recalled. The most reliable meter on the market is thought to be Roche's Aviva. Dr. Bernstein's office recommends it.

> **bigknitwit;48648 wrote:** So the meter isn't completely out to lunch, but it doesn't seem like something reliable to work with.

### mambo — 2012-09-03T09:25:40+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/glucose-tolerance-test-and-meter-issues.3339/post-49151>

Your results look good for the most part, especially your fasting insulin. Now, what's a bit confounding is your 2h post-prandial number. Assuming you took the big fast-acting glucose carb they administer, it shouldn't really be at 136, right? But then your BG control really isn't impaired since your A1c is 5.1 My interpretation: you probably have something called "biphasic" glucose curve, where BG rises again (but not as much as the first spike) at the 1.5-2.5 hour mark. In other words, you probably dipped below 130 between 1.5 to 2 but your 2h reading caught the rebound. It's normal and quite a bit of people have this: in fact, many people with biphasic (rather than monophasic) BG readings seem to have better BG control overall (lower A1c, etc.) and become diabetic at a lower rate. You can confirm by measuring your BG at 15 minute intervals after ingesting 75g carbs. I did and confirmed through several trials that I indeed have a "second BG spike" -- though a lot smaller spike than the first spike. Anyway, that's called geeking out.

> **bigknitwit;48648 wrote:** My results were:
>
>
>
> A1C 5.1%
>
> Fasting Glucose: 73
>
> Fasting Insulin less than 2
>
> 1 hour: 134
>
> 2 hour: 80

### jeanie@truelongmont.com — 2012-09-03T19:21:55+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/glucose-tolerance-test-and-meter-issues.3339/post-49197>

Yuck - 20 off is material! I wonder if my bg isnt as awesome as i think!

### Souldanzer — 2012-09-03T20:42:31+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/glucose-tolerance-test-and-meter-issues.3339/post-49205>

> **Cavemam;49700 wrote:** Yuck - 20 off is material! I wonder if my bg isnt as awesome as i think!

20 off is the biggest error you \*could\* get but you rarely do. You can find out how accurate your own meter tends to be by retesting a few times and comparing numbers. That's why they tell you in the instructions to re-test before freaking out if you get a result that appears off. I have a Bayer Contour and if I do multiple tests they are usually within a few points. Have I had a 10 point difference happen... yep. It happens. I retest if it seems weird.

### bigknitwit — 2012-09-03T21:00:04+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/glucose-tolerance-test-and-meter-issues.3339/post-49207>

One of the readings was really off though. My 2 hours blood level measured 80, but on the meter it measured 136. Unfortunately I didn't take the fasting on the meter for another data point. My FBG's are consistenly in the high 80s, low 90s on that meter, but now I'm wondering since here my fasting was 73. Maybe I'm not as badly off as I thought... Very annoying to have these discrepancies!

### Souldanzer — 2012-09-03T21:18:16+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/glucose-tolerance-test-and-meter-issues.3339/post-49211>

> **bigknitwit;49710 wrote:** One of the readings was really off though. My 2 hours blood level measured 80, but on the meter it measured 136. Unfortunately I didn't take the fasting on the meter for another data point. My FBG's are consistenly in the high 80s, low 90s on that meter, but now I'm wondering since here my fasting was 73. Maybe I'm not as badly off as I thought... Very annoying to have these discrepancies!

You can buy a test solution to test your meter's accuracy. If I got a reading like that I'd retest but I'm assuming you didn't get the lab results until later.

Women 275-400 μg/dL

**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/salivary-cortisol.3354/post-49152>

I would note that his 147 Ferritin is a bit high -- perhaps RBCs not turning over quickly. I believe HS CRP is only for cardiac. His BG control is good and fasting insulin is excellent. Given this, could you really conclude that he's hypothyroid? His FT3 is just a bit low. Unless he has symptoms, he may not be hypothyroid. His problem seems to be really low T. Assuming that his TG is overstated because of whatever medication he's taking for Crohn.

> **Souldanzer;48857 wrote:** I agree with the leaky gut but his inflammation is actually kind of low? hs CRP at .6 isn't that terrible in my book. Low inflammation and crappy hormones point to a brain issue as per Dr K and my labs.

### mambo — 2012-09-03T19:03:10+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/salivary-cortisol.3354/post-49192>

I said his insulin is excellent, his BG control is good. I used to think that 4.5 was the optimal A1c. I no longer believe that. Most healthy people are actually closer to 5 than 4.5. 4.5 is really for T1 diabetics on a ketogenic diet. Healthy peopel eating moderate carbs should have A1c around 5.

### jeanie@truelongmont.com — 2012-08-30T18:39:32+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/a-few-weird-lab-results.3358/post-48393>

ok, so got back a few lab things today - and interpreting this stuff is all running together. Comments? Help?

I'm battling absorption issues due to gastric bypass.

Vit-D is 21 with a range of 30-150. This one annoys the poop out of me. I've spent the last two months in the sun in addition to supplimenting to the tune of 20K-40K iu a day.

Any suggestions on other ways to up this?

Testosterone - I may be a man!

Free - 1.4 with a range of 0.2-2.0

Total - 152 with a range of 7-46. . . is this is as bad as it sounds? I am having hair loss, but I chalked it up to the

25 lbd weight loss with HCG, but perhaps it's the Testosterone.

On the anti-thyroid ABS

Thyr Perox AB - 11.8, with a range of 0-35

Thyroglob AB -

### Patty Cakes — 2012-08-30T18:56:06+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/a-few-weird-lab-results.3358/post-48396>

Wow that testosterone is high! Maybe PCOS?

On the D, I am wondering if you need a different brand. Are you taking oil based or pills or what? Just thinking out loud here but maybe you need a different not just brand but makeup of the D itself. I think some absorb better than others....oil based.

OK, on the thyroid antibodies there are two schools of thought: 1) antibodies within range means no Hashis, 2) any antibodies means something is haywire and you should take the precautions as if you have Hashis. As someone who has Hashis I come from the second school of thought. I wish I had known sooner.

### jeanie@truelongmont.com — 2012-08-30T19:05:22+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/a-few-weird-lab-results.3358/post-48397>

Thanks PK!

On Vit-D, taking Carlsons oil based. I will switch that up - certainly couldn't get worse, this is same level I had back in March before sun and supplements.

On Testosterone - I have been taking 25-50 mgs of the DHEA, which I will cease immediately. I see no reason to stop 7-Keto tho right? I've only been doing that about 3 weeks prior to test - seems weird. Testosterone wasn't reported on any prior bloodwork so I have nothing to compare it with.

On Antibodies - The Thyroglob AB, just gives "

### Shijin13 — 2012-08-30T19:09:01+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/a-few-weird-lab-results.3358/post-48399>

PCOS.... w/those T levels... are you dominate for E2? (what's your Pg:E2 ratio look like)

FWIW PCOS can also start the cascade w/in the Thyroid for Hashis

looking at your Vit D3 - its the final hormone that's made in the hormone cascade. so what the low level tells me is you've got some hormone converstion problems - think pregnenolone steal (need labs to verify - VAP, full hormone pannel etc)

so what's happening is what hormones are shunting away from making Pg, and going to cortisol, Testosterone, and most likely going to E2 as well... how are your FBG levels? Additionally what does your DHEA-S look like?

PCOS is a wicked thing - and it can kick off other diseases... its a hormone disruption (high T and E2), insulin resistance, leptin resistance, - the combination of which will turn off your thyroid (think idling doing nothing)

go back and read my blog - specifically June2012, and July 2012 entries [http://sunvalleysoul.blogspot.com](http://http://sunvalleysoul.blogspot.com) or [http://sunvalleysoul.wordpress.com/2012/06/20/my-journey-to-optimal-health/](http://http://sunvalleysoul.wordpress.com/2012/06/20/my-journey-to-optimal-health/) (pick which ever blog platform you prefer)

I'm getting ready to give an updated - based upon tracking FBG for the past month - and discovering that my FBG Spikes the last 5days of my cycle and into TOTM - b/c of decrease of Pg.

STTM - has an explaination of how PCOS can induce hashi's [http://www.stopthethyroidmadness.com/2012/05/30/hypothyroidism-insulin-resistance-and-metformin/](http://http://www.stopthethyroidmadness.com/2012/05/30/hypothyroidism-insulin-resistance-and-metformin/)

to support Vitamin D3 conversion you need both K2 and A. I would look at the using the following product at the max dose 45mg/day [http://www.vrp.com/bone-and-joint/ultra-k2](http://http://www.vrp.com/bone-and-joint/ultra-k2), and increase your offal consumption, and up your seafood.

FWIW CT can raise T naturally.

### Patty Cakes — 2012-08-30T19:20:42+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/a-few-weird-lab-results.3358/post-48403>

Can I just say I learn so much about the interconnectedness of all these issues from you Shijin! I love to read your posts. Just wow!

Cavemam, as far as the antibodies goes if you are showing some in either category (TPO or TgAB) I would go to AI diet right now. You are like me (and most Hashis people) where you only show TPO and TgAB is really low.

I had always heard the Vit D oil based is better for absorption but I like Shijin's explanation above. Makes total sense also in my situation why my D has always been low. I also upped K2 to help with absorption.

I'm not sure about the DHEA but agree that if it were me I would continue with the 7-keto. Have you tested the DHEA? Sounds like you may need a total hormone panel so you can follow the cascade and see where the holes are. Plus an ASI test to see what is going on there.

### Shijin13 — 2012-08-30T19:47:03+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/a-few-weird-lab-results.3358/post-48407>

as for vitamin D3 - I've had great success w/Now brands - dirt freaking cheap too, I'm one of those who need additional VD3 support yr round - though now w/the BHRT on board - I'm hoping I can cut back... but looking at my history - D3 tanks after September, as does my T3. but when I get to winter I feel amazing - its my season.

in order to fix the problem.. you have to understand the underlying problem...

for those of us w/PCOS - did the PCOS come first (hormones) or did the LR IR come first? from my perspective it really is the perfect storm of what can go wrong will go wrong... I'm going to do as much as I can to learn how to combat this, so dd won't have these problems...

### colleencoble — 2012-08-31T01:10:55+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/a-few-weird-lab-results.3358/post-48460>

But your free testosterone is fine. I'm not sure it's anything to worry about.

### TribalSpice — 2012-08-31T01:16:27+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/a-few-weird-lab-results.3358/post-48462>

With the VitD are you getting sunshine before you wash all the natural oils off your skin? When we strip away the oils, even with products without SLS's etc it makes it much more difficult for the body to synthesize vit D. Could you try getting sunlight to as much exposed skin as possible before showering?

### KiwiLauren — 2012-08-31T02:46:08+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/a-few-weird-lab-results.3358/post-48492>

+1 on everything Shijin said (Dr Gretchen i am now going to call her!). I also know that my Thyroid antibodies were MUCH lower than yours (almost zero on both) and Dr K told me I could NOT rule out Hashis until I had 3 thyroid antibody tests in a row (over 18 months) that showed those minimal levels. So I'd think about retesting a few times, getting the Vitamin D co-factors Gretchen mentioned, and working on hormones, specifically getting that Pg:E ratio testing done. (Also, given Gretchen's theory about the hormone steal, have you had the 4-panel ASI test?)

### kathylu — 2012-08-31T04:07:34+0300

### jeanie@truelongmont.com — 2012-09-01T23:19:49+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/a-few-weird-lab-results.3358/post-48798>

> **TribalSpice;48937 wrote:** With the VitD are you getting sunshine before you wash all the natural oils off your skin? When we strip away the oils, even with products without SLS's etc it makes it much more difficult for the body to synthesize vit D. I was! I even went so far as to not shower when I could get away with it. I haven't used soap in forever. . but I have been slathering with CO oil mix, and I wonder if that interfered with either the production of skins natural oils or simply just blocked the process.

Low BP

Low HR

Blood Sugars fine/low, no signs of insulin resistance

On the other hand, the Thyroid antibodies could lead to something - doing more tests. . . obviously I need hormones done - expect to get that next week.

Right now my thyroid panel points to HYPER, not HYPO. . .but that too makes zero sense considering my symptoms (fat!).

I've read a lot that as things are starting to go wonky on the thyroid front, that it's not uncommon to see fluctuations - but to show as hyper?

He's now running the parathyroid tests (4 glands unrelated to thyroid). . .which sorta makes sense with my blood levels of calcium.

I just don't have the negative "symptoms" of either PCOS or Hyper/hypo thyroid - except my hair falling out, which still couldbe the testosterone jump from regular DHEA.

However, I did go through a hair falling out stage and was told it was thyroid. But my estrogen was also almost at zero so it could have been that. Iron may have something to do with hair too IIRC.

# Thread: Diverticulitis - need some help for a friend

**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread

### vkiernan — 2012-08-30T20:10:57+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/diverticulitis-need-some-help-for-a-friend.3360/post-48410>

My friend was just diagnosed with Diverticulitis yesterday. Her doc is no help and she is not supposed to go see him for 2 weeks. Her gut has been in pain for almost a month now. Anyone have any suggestions I can share with her? It is going in one end and coming right out the other. I feel really bad for her and sure wish I knew how to help. I have told her stop all grains but what else?

### Lyndra — 2012-08-30T20:35:16+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/diverticulitis-need-some-help-for-a-friend.3360/post-48417>

Consider the Specific Carbohydrate Diet. Worked for my husband, but much different circumstances (SIBO). It is a gut-healing protocol which I would think couldn't hurt. Start with lots of bone broth.

### KiwiLauren — 2012-08-31T02:49:41+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/diverticulitis-need-some-help-for-a-friend.3360/post-48493>

> **vkiernan;48883 wrote:** My friend was just diagnosed with Diverticulitis yesterday. Her doc is no help and she is not supposed to go see him for 2 weeks. Her gut has been in pain for almost a month now. Anyone have any suggestions I can share with her? It is going in one end and coming right out the other. I feel really bad for her and sure wish I knew how to help. I have told her stop all grains but what else?

How about reading the BG 9 gut post and listening to the webinar? No grains is for sure. But given what Dr K has taught us about gut, she's got 3 major things going on (likely): leaky gut, insufficient gut flora and insufficient gut variety. Not to mention potentially H Pylori and/or Candida. I'd be supplementing with the Leaky Gut supps (glutamine/bone broths, pro and prebiotics, DGL, aloe, glutathione, colostrum) and testing with a functional med doc. I'd cut all dairy and nightshades as well as the grains. It is a LOT to take on. Has she read any of Dr K? Maybe she could start by listening to his podcasts and/or the webinars?

### vkiernan — 2012-08-31T02:53:42+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/diverticulitis-need-some-help-for-a-friend.3360/post-48495>

Thanks Lauren and Lyndra. I think she is in shock for the most part. She already has lyme disease and she has read that blog. I will mention a func. med for sure. That would be a great place for her to start. I will also mention everything you guys have mentioned to start healing. Really wondering if her lyme's is playing a part in all of this?
