### steve@wity.net — 2012-09-02T19:23:08+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/help-looks-like-i-am-far-from-optimal.3407/post-48989>

Inger - the first thing needed is the ranges - without these the figures are difficult to interpret. Each laboratory is different.

Progesterone 2.4nmol/l converts to 0.75ng/ml - low but where should it be for day 11?

Testosterone 1.2nmol/l converts to 34.5ng/dl - low but where should it be for day 11?

Your Estradiol conversion to pg/ml looks fine.

I'm not an expert on P/E ratios so hopefully one of the experts will calculate and comment.

DHEA - I think this might be in ng/ml. You need to be in the top 25% of the range - so 14 plus to be optimal.

HS-CRP - you are correct - this needs to be as low as possible. My last labs came in at 0.38. Jack told me he wants me to be aiming at 0.03.

Glucose - I think the units should be mmol/l and i'm guessing the range is roughly from 4 to 6. Your figure is 5.3 and so converts to 95.5mg/dl. Anything over 99mg/dl is a definite no so your figure is quite high. Definitely a fasting blood test?

Body comp will improve as hormones improve but you look like you are well on the way and looking great with your eating and CT. Hormones may have suffered due to the stress you were/are under. Sex hormones are sacrificed to make more cortisol. A salivary cortisol would be interesting.

I wouldn't start taking out the odd treat at this point in my opinion - it mght stress you more!

### Inger — 2012-09-02T19:53:00+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/help-looks-like-i-am-far-from-optimal.3407/post-48994>

Thank you so much Corvus! I will do all to get HS CRP to 0,03! Wow that is really low! But I can see it doable, as I still see room for improvement with my foods! Like quitting dairy 100% (I have it occasionally, not often but it must be enough to mess up). I guess I should, when, only use this raw organic goatmilk that I can buy here, it is from a very old Finnish breed. No 85% black and green chocolate eather, or 90% Lindt. It is NOT good for me, I notice it on my skin fast!

Corvus what are you doing now to get it that low? I am curious! Do you have any advice for me except of what I mentioned above?

Here are the Hormone ranges that was on the lab-paper;

Estradiol 1,1 nmol/l (299,6 pg/ml) (1100 pmol)

Range;

Follikular 0.07-0.53 nmol/l

Ovulation 0.23-1.31 nmol/l

Luteal 0.20-0.79 nmol/l

Progesterone 2,4 nmol/l (755 pg/ml) (2400 pmol)

Range;

Follikular

### Souldanzer — 2012-09-02T22:44:17+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/help-looks-like-i-am-far-from-optimal.3407/post-49044>

Day 11 is most likely follicular phase, depending on your cycle.

FBG should be under 85 but I think you really want for it to be in the 70s.

I think your results just mean that you need to fix some more things. Don't despair. Knowing you used to have an ED - I think we are some of the more broken people. I think your body might need a winter... were you eating carbs during the summer? CT and epi-paleo rx will get your hs CRP down.... and fixing your hormones. Keep eating your seafood....

Are you taking magnesium? Good for the blood sugar. As is vitamin K.

### mambo — 2012-09-03T10:13:33+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/help-looks-like-i-am-far-from-optimal.3407/post-49155>

Fasting BG numbers are just a snapshot on that particular morning, so I wouldn't worry too much over a 96 reading; it will be high depending on what you ate the night before, your level of stress, liver dump, etc. If you want to conclude something from FBG, measure a weekly average and see where that falls. Under 90, good. Over 95 and closer to 125, closer to insulin resistance but there is such a thing as physiological insulin resistance if you're doing very low carb.

Also, 0.22 in HS CRP should be regarded as excellent. I don't know anyone with zero CRP. Some labs (LabCorp) may not even measure something in increments that small (as .03).

> **Inger;49406 wrote:**
>
> Glucose 5,3 nmol/l (90 mg/dl)
>
> HS CRP 0,22 mg/l And my HS CRP could be much closer to 0? Should it be 0 actually?

# Thread: Falling back asleep after breakfast
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/falling-back-asleep-after-breakfast.3413/>
**Subforum:** The Leptin Rx
**Posts:** 2
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### hope2conceive — 2012-09-02T18:33:57+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/falling-back-asleep-after-breakfast.3413/post-48968>

so I'm very early in my LRx, only day 5. I'm trying really hard to follow the circadian rhythm rules...well today, I woke up earlier than normal, and was famished. So I had breakfast earlier than normal (630am), within the 30 minutes of waking up....but afterwards I was still so tired, I fell back asleep for a couple hours. Is this bad? Will it screw up my circadian rhythm reset I'm trying to do?

I normally can NEVER go back to sleep in the morning...but maybe cause it's Sunday? I don't know.

I'm hoping someone can give me some feedback. Thanks much!

### Glamazon — 2012-09-02T20:36:04+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/falling-back-asleep-after-breakfast.3413/post-49004>

If your body needs sleep, get it. You can never sleep too much in this process. The reason you are tired is probably because you are running on yesterday's proteins that weren't autophaged during your sleep cycle. In most recent webinar, Dr. K. went into this in great detail. It is a must listen to for your journey to optimal. Also what is your most hormone panel, your vit D3 status, and your HS crp level. Other wise we are just peeing in the wind with narrowing down options.

For me, and this may not be you because I am have my own individual probs with my hormones and leaky gut, I slept constantly thru the day off and on for hours when I started the LRx. It took about a month before I quit doing so.

Before starting to eat paleo I noticed that about 3 days before I broke out with cold/flu symptoms I would get painful jolts of static electricity whenever I grabbed the door handle of the car. # Thread: When to get lab tests drawn
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/when-to-get-lab-tests-drawn.3420/>
**Subforum:** Optimal Labs
**Posts:** 3
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### popcornsally — 2012-09-03T00:10:45+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/when-to-get-lab-tests-drawn.3420/post-49062>

I'm planning on having lab tests drawn in the next couple of months for estradiol, progesterone, pregnenolone, testosterone free and total, DHEAs, cortisol AM, TSH, free T4 and free T3 (plus vit. b12, vit. d, CMP, CBC, and lipid panel). These are the tests my naturopathic doc is ordering. I did my own panel via mymedlab a couple months ago. I know the a.m. cortisol, TSH and some of the other things may not be that helpful, but as long as my insurance will pay for it, I'm not going to argue.

I am most interested in optimizing my cortisol, DHEA and sex hormones at this time. **My question is when is the REAL best time to draw the blood?** My doc said do it during 3rd week of menstrual cycle (43 y.o. pre-menopausal female). I've read here to do it on day 2-4 of cycle. Last fall my tests showed ALL hormones (including cortisol) were low. I am supplementing with pregnenolone and DHEA in addition to natural desiccated thyroid. Maybe I should do just a progesterone and estradiol level on day 2-4 and again with everything else around day 21? By the way, I will do a saliva adrenal stress index I ordered soon. Doctor sort of poo-poo'd that. Whatevs!

I don't really trust my doc to be in the driver's seat. She's nice and agreeable, but has not been very pro-active in helping me improve my health! I've figured out most of this stuff on my own by doing hours of reading on the internet and in books. It's so disappointing that my self-prescribed treatment has done much more for me than any doctor's ever has. I am a veterinarian so I do have some understanding of endocrinology. Just never thought I'd have to use it to treat myself! I feel sorry for people who don't have the gumption to do their own research and just continue to suffer.

Thanks for your help!

### KiwiLauren — 2012-09-03T03:13:59+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/when-to-get-lab-tests-drawn.3420/post-49086>

From my understanding, you want to draw the E and P and T (sex hormones) on day 21 of your cycle or day 3/4. I don't think this applies to DHEAs. Nor do I think it matters what day you do a thyroid panel or your vitamin levels or cholesterol. TSH is good to get as long as you get the free T3 and free T4. FYI, the cortisol and melatonin need to be saliva, and ideally a 4-panel test. I think that's what you mean - definitely get the melatonin not just the cortisol. Good luck.

### popcornsally — 2012-09-03T07:10:46+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/when-to-get-lab-tests-drawn.3420/post-49144>

Thanks, KiwiLauren. Yes, I do mean mainly the sex hormones. So you think it's either day 3/4 OR day 21? It seemed to me like it would be better to test estrogen early and progesterone later, but I'm not sure.

My latest rant:

[http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Food\_irradiation](http://http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Food_irradiation)

Food irradiation is the process of exposing food to ionizing radiation\[1\] to destroy microorganisms, bacteria, viruses, or insects that might be present in the food. Further applications include sprout inhibition, delay of ripening, increase of juice yield, and improvement of re-hydration. Irradiated food does not become radioactive, but in some cases there may be subtle chemical changes

### Stavity — 2012-09-26T21:27:00+0300

want to consider tightening up your Epi-paleo diet i.e. watch the carbs. That's my two cents.

### Patty Cakes — 2012-09-06T03:51:49+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/if-you-can-read-them-please-hack-thank-you.3443/post-49667>

I wonder if that was in "context" with regard to something else going on in the body and a different ratio is in order????? Just thinking out loud again.

### shilohman — 2012-09-06T03:53:08+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/if-you-can-read-them-please-hack-thank-you.3443/post-49668>

> **ColdBren;50197 wrote:** so how can mine be 4.8?

Here is the formula I got from the forum:

Progesterone in ng per ml x 3180 = converted progesterone. Estradiol in pg per ml x 3.671 = converted estrogen. The converted progesterone value divided by the converted estrogen value = the P/E ratio. For women a ratio between 20-170 is desirable. For men a ratio of around 60 is ideal. Women need to get their CRH/ACTH/cortisol fixed/healthy before getting their P/E healthy.

### ColdBren — 2012-09-06T03:57:54+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/if-you-can-read-them-please-hack-thank-you.3443/post-49671>

### JanSz — 2012-09-06T04:01:19+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/if-you-can-read-them-please-hack-thank-you.3443/post-49672>

> **ColdBren;49961 wrote:** Thanks!! My pregnenalone was 91 ng/dl. Is that sufficient?

Was that on the same blood draw as the list above (Taken around ovulation)?

Anyhow it looks low.

I think that you could easily take one or two 150mg MLM preg pills, with your current results.

That would be continuous dose. One pill at wakeup the other around noon.

If you want make a project out of it, consider making checks at day (2-4) and day (19-21) of your cycle.

Actually, better start with just day (2-4).

Figure out your MLM pregnenolone dose measuring:

draw blood at LabCorp, 7:30AM and noon.

Show up at lab 6:30AM, they are open at 7AM but often people are already waiting:

1 ------- Pregnenolone, Mass Spectrometry--Test Number: 140707 CPT Code: 84140

2 ------- Progesterone--Test Number: 004317 CPT Code: 84144

3 ------- Cortisol, AM & PM Test Number: 104000 CPT Code: 82533(x2) (done at 7:30AM & 12PM)

4 ------- Dehydroepiandrosterone (DHEA) Sulfate--Test Number: 004020 CPT Code: 82627

\--

It would be better if two weeks before blood draw you would take the preg tabs at 6:30AM and 11AM.

Adjust pregnenolone dose

trying to have rather high preg (priority #3)

trying to have progesterone=1.5 ng/mL (priority #2)

trying to have cortisol~20 (priority #1)

goal DHEAs(Women 275-400 μg/dL

depending on your responses, you may need to go into more details than just the minimum 4 items above.

...

If I take more than 3 mg of pregnenalone my heart goes crazy with palps.....I take about 2 mg per day. How can I fix this and if I take more, wont it just shunt it to cortisol anyway?

### shilohman — 2012-09-06T04:49:19+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/if-you-can-read-them-please-hack-thank-you.3443/post-49681>

Verified everything from [http://www.yourmenopausetype.com/menopausequestionsandanswers/06112000.html](http://http://www.yourmenopausetype.com/menopausequestionsandanswers/06112000.html)

Here is the math:

1) convert Progestertone of 1.4 ng/mL to picometers 1.4 x 1,000 = 1,400 pg/mL

2)convert to moles: prog 1,400 pg/mL x 3.18 = 4452 pMol/L

3)convert E2 to moles: 253 pg/mL x 3.671 = 929 pMol/L

4) divide Prog/E2: 4452/929 = 4.8 : 1

The above website says the fourth quartile is 133:1 to 170:1 ratio. So optimal would be 133:1 - 170:1.

The ratio range is given as 20:1-170:1

I figured P/E ratios:

day (2-4)

Progesterone in ng per ml= 1.5

Estradiol in pg per ml = 35

P/E=37

day (19-21)

Progesterone in ng per ml= 14

Estradiol in pg per ml = 130

P/E=93

\=======================================================

Again, this is much closer to

(20-160)

than

(400-500)(Kruse) or (200-300)(Lee)

\=======================================================

Clarification.

Assuming one is need.

I am not saying that dr K or dr Lee are wrong.

I would prefer to hear directly from dr K that he really wants such a high P/E (rather than getting it thru grape vine)

Even better, why does he wants it so high.

Note;

my calculations based on average menstruation chart are tacitly assuming healthy woman, likely young woman.

When something goes wrong and requires corrections,

even if those corrections have intentions to restore healthy young status,

they may need to emphasize one and de-emphasize other.

\=========================================================

Specially progesterone,

it is highly under discussed topic

and

it also have a bad name (specially among some docs who treat men).

at ovulation time

progesterone, low, only about 1/4 available, it should be much higher

estradiol, high, real close to twice of what it should be

To lower estradiol ask for script for Arimidex

one pill a day, for 90 days, 6 refills

but

use only one pill E3D

3 weeks after starting Arimidex draw blood,

preferably draw blood on day (2-4) of your cycle.

Adjust Arimidex dose.

Assume that E1 is also high.

Eat brocoli, kimchi, raw home made sourcraut (or Bubbies sourcraut)

At this time there is no justification for DIM.

\----

low progesterone

I am assuming that your pregnenolone is actually low (but it is not 91)

your reaction to Country life (minute amount) pregnenolone is unsettling.

Consider getting

Pregnenolone Micronized Lipid Matrix 150mg tabs scored, from Nutricology/Allergy Research

Chip off small portion of tab, report reaction.

If you can tolerate, go up to two tabs (300mg/day).

One tab at wakeup time (or 1-2 hrs before on a way to bathroom).

Next tab 3-4 hrs after wakeup.

If you are able to get on that program, stay on it for a one menstrual cycle, then at LabCorp

on day (2-4) draw blood for thise tests (below).

Adjust dose per test results (Arimidex & pregnenolone & DHEA).

After day (2-4) is in acceptable shape, pay attention to part of menstrual period that have a high levels, that usually is on day (19-21), but depends on length of your cycle.

1 ------- Pregnenolone, Mass Spectrometry--Test Number: 140707 CPT Code: 84140

2 ------- 17α-Hydroxypregnenolone by HPLC-MS/MS

3 ------- 17α-Hydroxyprogesterone by HPLC-MS/MS

4 ------- Progesterone--Test Number: 004317 CPT Code: 84144

5 ------- Cortisol, Free, Serum (Free Cortisol, Serum Cortisol, CBG)

6 ------- Cortisol, AM & PM Test Number: 104000 CPT Code: 82533(x2) (7:30AM, 12PM)

7 ------- Corticosteroid-binding Globulin (CBG), Transcortin

8 ------- Corticotropin Releasing Hormone (CRH)

9 ------- Dehydroepiandrosterone (DHEA) Sulfate--Test Number: 004020 CPT Code: 82627

10 ------- DHEA

11 ------- Androstenedione

12 ------- Testosterone, Free and Weakly Bound, With Total Testosterone, LC/MS-MS Test Number: 070282 CPT Code: 84402; 84403

13 ------- SHBG

14 ------- Dihydrotestosterone

15 ------- Androstanediol Glucuronide (Endocrine Sciences) Test Number: 500881 CPT Code: 82154

16 ------- Estrogens, Total; Test Number: 004549 CPT Code: 82672

17 ------- Estradiol, Sensitive Test Number: 140244 CPT Code: 82670

18 ------- Estrone, Serum Test Number: 004564 CPT Code: 82679

\---------

If you are not able to tolerate pregnenolone from Nutricilogy, consider visiting with dr Kruse.

You may still try workaround. This is second (reluctant) choice.

Get script for Prometrium or Microgest

they are 100mg gelcaps (can't divide them), contain micronized pregnenolone

If you are able to tolerate them, take them before bed. Take one gelcap/bed time

Do the blood testing as above.

Adjust dose per blood test.

Some women may need to use testosterone, androstenedione.

I would not be surprised if you would need both.

Well, stopping testosterone---->Estradiol conversion (using Arimidex)

and

androstenedione---->Estrone conversion (using Arimidex)

may eliminate that need.

\============================================================================================================

\============================================================================================================

continued on next post

### JanSz — 2012-09-07T19:00:05+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/if-you-can-read-them-please-hack-thank-you.3443/post-50054>

continued from previous post

\============================================================================================================

\============================================================================================================

Getting back to basics.

Get this tests done (that will take a while), PM me after you have posted scans of all pages of both tests.

Spectracell Comprehensive Nutritional Panel

[http://www.healthremedies.com/intracellular\_vitamin\_analysis\_spectracell.html](http://http://www.healthremedies.com/intracellular_vitamin_analysis_spectracell.html)

Essential & Metabolic Fatty Acids Analysis (EMFA) by Genova Diagnostics

[http://www.healthremedies.com/essential\_metabolic\_fatty\_acids\_analysis\_emfa\_genova\_diagnostics.html](http://http://www.healthremedies.com/essential_metabolic_fatty_acids_analysis_emfa_genova_diagnostics.html)

\---------

In the mean time do not eat any fish oils or krill oils or primrose oil or similar supplemental oils.

Oils that come with food are ok, I am concerned with mega sources of EPA, DHA, GLA, AA.

\------------------------------------------------

(EMFA) by Genova is really not the best test (others are less good), it would require re-interpretation of results.

I am assuming that you are strapped for $$

there is better test

Red Blood Cell Fatty Acid test from Kennedy Krieger Laboratory

but

this test costs more $$ plus you would have to hire doctor who would write you script for that test.

Go for it if you can.

Call 888-320-8338

bodybio.com

dr Patricia Kane

\-----------------------------------------------

While at bodybio

they have

[http://www.bodybio-wellness.com/report-blood-test.html](http://http://www.bodybio-wellness.com/report-blood-test.html)

BodyBio™ Nutrient Report

I have used input from that test to tell you about your low cholesterol (above).

It is two part test.

First you get your GP write you a script for blood test then you send results to BodyBio and they make their review.

That is usually less expensive way, because insurance should pay for blood testing.

If BodyBio would handle script for blood testing it would cost more.

I have looked at number of those tests an figured out portion of how they are made.

That information is actually posted on reports, so is not a big secret.

If you can come with this list I can take a look and tell you what came up.

Likely you have all that info already in your past tests.

Partial input willl also work (partially).

This list:

[http://www.musclechatroom.com/forum/showthread.php?20061-Dr.-Patricia-Kane&p=171949#post171949](http://http://www.musclechatroom.com/forum/showthread.php?20061-Dr.-Patricia-Kane&p=171949#post171949)

set to get Patricia Kane BodyBio™ Nutrient Report

• Comprehensive Metabolic Panel (14)\*

BLOOD CHEMISTRY PANEL:\*

Alanine Transaminase (ALT or SGPT)\*

Albumin\*

Alkaline Phosphatase\*

Asparate Transaminase (AST or SGOT)\*

Bilirubin, Total\*

BUN (Blood Urea Nitrogen)\*

Calcium\*

CO2 (Carbon Dioxide)\* br />
Chloride\*

Cholesterol, Total\*

Creatinine\*

Globulin\*

Glucose\*

HDL\*

LDL\*

Potassium\*

Protein, Total\*

Sodium\*

Triglycerides\*

• Individual Tests

o Gamma-Glutamyl Transpeptidase (GGT)

o Iron, Total\*

o Lactate Dehydrogenase (LDH)\*

o Phosphorus

o Uric Acid

• CBC with Differential and Platelet\*

COMPLETE BLOOD COUNT w/ MANUAL DIFFERENTIAL:\*

WBC\*

RBC\*

RDW\*

Hemoglobin\*

Hematocrit\*

MCV\*

MCH\*

MCHC\*

Mean Platelet Volume\*

Platelet Count\*

Neutrophils\*

Lymphocytes\*

Eosinophils\*

Monocytes\*

\==============================================

..

.

### shilohman — 2012-09-07T19:39:00+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/if-you-can-read-them-please-hack-thank-you.3443/post-50058>

> **ColdBren;50228 wrote:** Triiodothyronine,Free,Serum(FT3) 2.9 pg/mL 2.0-4.4 is that my t3? what can I do from here to determine the rest?

Sorry, I missed the Free T3. What do you do from here? I would get the following tests:

Thyroid antibodies test. lef.org has this test for members at $99

ASI panel from ZRTLabs.com - can't remember the cost, maybe $135

HS-CRP lef.org has this for $42

If you are not a member of lef.org, then join, you will save the $75 membership fee on tests in no time

Next you have got to find a doctor in your area that is a BIHT doctor. These are the only doctors that are

going to be willing to write prescriptions in a high enough dosage to fix you.

If you don't find a BIHT doc that you can afford, then look for a functional medicine/holistic doctor. Get a consult with Dr. Kruse, if you have the right tests, then on a consult he can give specific dosages for your medical context. Get your FM on board or shop for one until you find one that will dosage according to the cÃ¸nsult. This is exactly what my girlfiend has done. She got tested, had a consult with Dr. Kruse. We recorded the Skype consult on the computer and then transcribed it to a word document. That was all that was needed, her Dr. wrote the prescriptions she needed.

Finding a doctor/getting a consult can take time, so what do you di in the mean time. I would

1) tighten up my diet so it would be squeaky clean Epi-paleo. You would be amazed at how doing this will fix any leaky gut issues you

have. Remember, your problem is inflammation and inflammation enters through the gut.

2) tighten up your light sources as much as you possible, when I did this I was amazed at my improved sleep/reduced inflammation.

I know that all of this can be confusing, but you gotta have a game plan, what I listed above is what I would do.

### ColdBren — 2012-09-07T21:04:55+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/if-you-can-read-them-please-hack-thank-you.3443/post-50072>

wow, thanks so much. I am so overwhelmed, this is some amazing info and I want to really go through everything. My 7 year old daughter has some tics that looks like they might be coming from yeast. I started her on Nystatin and she is having major "die off" symptoms. Its all I can focus on right now as I am so worried for her. So, Jansz, I will definitely come back to this and go through all of your recs. And Shiloman thank you so much, I know you are right and I need to face my fears about biht and just do it.

# Thread: Omega6/Omega3 ratio per Kennedy Krieger Lab
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/omega6-omega3-ratio-per-kennedy-krieger-lab.3444/>
**Subforum:** Optimal Labs
**Posts:** 1
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### JanSz — 2012-09-04T18:03:28+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/omega6-omega3-ratio-per-kennedy-krieger-lab.3444/post-49358>

Ideal test at:

Red Blood Cell Fatty Acid test from Kennedy Krieger Laboratory

Ranges are from dr Patrica Kane's Detoxx Book, Page 231(1/24/2007)

All results of Omega6 and Omega3 are in mid range, see %Status in column R

Results are in column E

TotalO6/TotalO3 =**4.66**

LA(Omega6):ALA(Omega3) =92.95

Note that Krieger's numbers are little misaligned.

When LA & ALA are both in mid range, their ratio is not, (%Status of LA/ALA=29.16%

Similarly %Status of TotalO6/TotalO3=11.46%

Both are well within acceptable +-50%

Action is recommended when results fall outside the +-50%

[http://img825.imageshack.us/img825/4563/image251e.jpg](http://http://img825.imageshack.us/img825/4563/image251e.jpg)

The cod livers I ordered on Amazon got here a couple days ago. Half the cans were dented but otherwise fine. These things are delicious, creamy and not overpoweringly fishy. The taste has an extremely mild hint of tuna. I eat them straight up out of the can, but I saw a recipe for a cod liver salad using boiled eggs, minced shallot and the cod livers as main ingredients. Everything is mashed up with a fork.

### JanSz — 2012-09-06T01:14:33+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hack-my-labs.3448/post-49622>

> **Destiny;50014 wrote:** Jansz, could you elaborate on the following?
>
>
>
> To minimize negatives, newer use testosterone supplementation without HCG/Ovidrel.
>
>
>
> Why?

HCG is natural product derived from pregnant human female

Ovidrel is recombinant HCG, rHCG

Both act similarly at male testicles, they mimic activity of LH.

When testicles are stimulated with LH or FSH or both they may facilitate production of testosterone and sperm or not, but they stay in their original physical shape, size. Scrotum also exist.

When external testosterone is used, that testosterone is noted by pituitary, production of LH & FSH stop.

Lack of LH & FSH over time will result in testicles and scrotum atrophy, down to nothing.

This process takes time and its progress vary from person to person.

To prevent all this HCG or Ovidrel injections are used.

....

The recommended dose of CYCLOSET is 1.6 mg to 4.8 mg administered once daily

"within two hours after waking in the

morning. CYCLOSET should be taken with food to potentially reduce gastrointestinal side effects such as nausea. "

\----------------------------------------------------------------------

"Recommended Dosing

The recommended dose of CYCLOSET is 1.6 mg to 4.8 mg administered once daily within two hours after waking in the morning. CYCLOSET should be taken with food to potentially reduce gastrointestinal side effects such as nausea.

**Titration**

CYCLOSET should be initiated at one tablet (0.8 mg) and increased by one tablet per week until a maximum daily dose of 6 tablets (4.8 mg) or until the maximal tolerated number of tablets between 2 and 6 per day is reached."

////

///////////////////////////

Dr K mentioned

CYCLOSET Tablets contain bromocriptine mesylate.

CYCLOSET is a dopamine receptor agonist

Bromocriptine is used to lower prolactin.

\-------------

But there is also

Parlodel (Bromocriptine Mesylate)

Bromocriptine (Parlodel) is a dopamine agonist drug (meaning it acts like dopamine in the brain), primarily activating the dopamine D2 receptors.

\-------------

Above report nausea

but here I see:

[http://thinksteroids.com/steroid-profiles/dostinex/](http://http://thinksteroids.com/steroid-profiles/dostinex/)

Dostinex (Cabergoline) is a dopamine agonist.

Dostinex is the preferred treatment in terms of effectiveness as well as having very few undesirable side effects (3). It does this very well for both men and women, it should be noted…almost identically actually (4)

\=============================

Dr K

any comments here, please.

# Thread: Would you eat this?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/would-you-eat-this.3453/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 11
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### bigknitwit — 2012-09-04T20:32:22+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/would-you-eat-this.3453/post-49402>

I have a freezer in the basement that used to be pretty much dedicated to liver - calf liver, pork liver, lamb's liver, chicken/turkey liver. I've known for a long time the nutritional value of liver, and have always bought it frozen from local farmers raising pastured animals that I was also buying. Trouble is, I really dislike liver, so I didn't keep up with making it. The liver freezer expanded in varieties. I could probably get to optimal 3 times over with the quantities of high quality liver I've collected... Then I started using the liver freezer for my many many ice packs... On 2 separate occasions, the liver freezer got left ajar overnight. The many pounds of pastured liver defrosted, fully, twice.

No I am thinking about all that liver, and how much I need to be eating it. Question is, would you, given it's been fully defrosted on 2 separate occasions?

### ealachan — 2012-09-04T20:41:54+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/would-you-eat-this.3453/post-49404>

I wouldn't, personally...but what do I know?

edited to add: I wouldn't eat it because it had been thawed completely twice...assuming you don't know how long it had been thawed for, either. In terms of general food safety, I wouldn't touch it other than to throw it out...but I guess you never know unless you try.

### jeanie@truelongmont.com — 2012-09-04T21:04:49+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/would-you-eat-this.3453/post-49410>

Can't taste worse than Normal. Start mixing up meatloaf and balls that hide taste. iirc, nutrition should be intact.

### indigogirl — 2012-09-04T21:16:30+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/would-you-eat-this.3453/post-49413>

I wouldn't be worried about the taste, but about what's in the liver after it defrosted twice.

I wouldn't eat it. But that's me...I wash my fruits and veggies!

### bigknitwit — 2012-09-04T21:44:06+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/would-you-eat-this.3453/post-49420>

I'm not worried about the taste or the nutrition, just whether or not it's rotting. It tastes bad to me regardless!! Freezer was open for a maximum of 24 hours both times. It would have been many hours before the liver was fully defrosted, and when I got to it, it was still "cold."

### vlynnb — 2012-09-04T22:42:48+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/would-you-eat-this.3453/post-49428>

I've read that that if food has thawed it is not safe to refreeze it. Freezing slows down the growth of organisms in the food but does not totally stop the growth and if it is thawed more organisms begin to grow at that time and continue after refreezing. If there are still ice crystals after one thaw it is probably OK to refreeze if it is used a short time later but if there were no ice crystals left at all or it has thawed a second time I wouldn't take the chance, especially if you already have a leaky gut or compromised immune system. Even a healthy person would be taking an unnecessary risk to their health.

### Souldanzer — 2012-09-05T00:19:29+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/would-you-eat-this.3453/post-49452>

My local buffalo rancher has instructed me several time that it is perfectly okay to defrost meat, cut it into smaller portions, and then refreeze. The thing he told me to watch out for is to not let it get to room temperature. At that point, you need to either cook it or throw it out.

Some sort of raw dog feeder probably wouldn't worry even a bit about this.... so if you need a place to give it away, find a dog person who feeds raw.

Yeah, and the disclaimer is that I just had food poisoning, most likely. So don't listen to me. LOL

### bigknitwit — 2012-09-05T00:25:09+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/would-you-eat-this.3453/post-49453>

> **Souldanzer;49981 wrote:** My local buffalo rancher has instructed me several time that it is perfectly okay to defrost meat, cut it into smaller portions, and then refreeze. The thing he told me to watch out for is to not let it get to room temperature. At that point, you need to either cook it or throw it out.
>
>
>
> Some sort of raw dog feeder probably wouldn't worry even a bit about this.... so if you need a place to give it away, find a dog person who feeds raw.
>
>
>
> Yeah, and the disclaimer is that I just had food poisoning, most likely. So don't listen to me. LOL

Food poisoning... The closest thing I've experienced in life to death... Hope you are feeling better! I think I will just throw it all out, remembering the hours spent on the floor in the bathroom years ago.

### Souldanzer — 2012-09-05T00:27:35+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/would-you-eat-this.3453/post-49454>

> **bigknitwit;49982 wrote:** Food poisoning... The closest thing I've experienced in life to death... Hope you are feeling better! I think I will just throw it all out, remembering the hours spent on the floor in the bathroom years ago.

somehow I didn't come up with sleeping on the bathroom floor.... that would've been a good idea. Seriously

### nonchalant — 2012-09-05T00:33:53+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/would-you-eat-this.3453/post-49459>

I remember once DH and I got sick at the same time from food poisoning. Really bad. Heard the toddlers knock over the kitchen trash can, but didn't hear any glass break, and couldn't smell fire, so we didn't move to investigate. We were incapacitated for a good long while.

On the other hand, I once accidently left liver out overnight, and still the kitty ate it over the course of several days. She started balking after a while though, so I ended up tossing the rest. She's a pretty good judge of what's edible and what isn't.

### kathylu — 2012-09-06T03:55:51+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/would-you-eat-this.3453/post-49669>

If it really was still cold, you are probably ok. Why not cook the living daylights out of a bit of it and try it? I would, but then I think we are all too concerned about bacteria.

### Paul — 2012-09-04T21:03:36+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cytomel.3454/post-49409>

My most recent thyroid labs (8/7/12) were as follows:

TSH â€“ 2.85 uIU/mL (0.50-4.50)

T4, Free â€“ 1.4 ng/dL (0.8-1.8)

T3, Free â€“ 2.8 pg/mL (2.0-4.9)

Reverse T3 â€“ 22 ng/dL (11-32)

My doctor prescribed Cytomel, 5 mcg. 2 x daily.

Does this sound reasonable?

### Patty Cakes — 2012-09-05T00:58:16+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cytomel.3454/post-49476>

Sounds reasonable to me! I am only a Hashis patient. Your rT3 ratio ([http://www.stopthethyroidmadness.com/rt3-ratio/](http://http://www.stopthethyroidmadness.com/rt3-ratio/)) is 12.7 which means you have a rT3 issue going on which the Cytomel will help. Also you FT3 is way too low. It should be at the top of the range. But the big question you should ask yourself and your doc is what is causing this rT3 issue. Maybe adrenals dysfunction or iron out of whack or ????

The dose is small but is a good place to start. If you need more info read on the website that has the rT3 ratio calculator. Lots there based on patient experience.

I wonder how Paul is doing these days....

My thyroid numbers were similar and my doc just started me yesterday on 2.5mcg liothyronine which seems kind of of low, but like Patty Cakes says, _"The dose is small but is a good place to start."_ Actually when the doctor was talking to me she said Cytomel, but it was filled as liothyronine sodium 5mcg tablets---of which I am to split it in half and only take 2.5mcg.

These are my numbers:

TSH â€“ 1.78 uIU/mL (0.450-4.50)

T4, Free â€“ 1.2 ng/dL (0.82-1.77)

T3, Free â€“ 2.3 pg/mL (2.0-4.4)

Reverse T3 â€“ 25.5 ng/dL (13.5-34.2)

Curious to know how Paul is doing now.... maybe he'll see and respond to this post.

### indigogirl — 2012-09-04T21:29:30+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/soft-food-for-a-tooth-extraction.3457/post-49417>

I am having an infected root canal gone bad molar extracted tomorrow. Just learned today it is coming out tomorrow.

Not much time to plan and make soft food for the next few days.

I probably have candida and Hashis and am having a hard time figuring out what to prepare other than broths.

PurÃ©ed meat? Yuck!

Soft fish will be easier to chew (salmon, cod, etc.)

Any suggestions?

### Souldanzer — 2012-09-05T00:32:57+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/soft-food-for-a-tooth-extraction.3457/post-49457>

Sorry Indigo. Did this just go bad.... I remember you saying it was all over?

Soft foods..... maybe avocados blended? I like them with a bit of cinnamon and stevia.

Fish smoothies

I like pureed vegetable soups, I guess you could throw some meat in there if you wanted. Protein is hard to come by in soft forms.

### Patty Cakes — 2012-09-05T00:38:57+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/soft-food-for-a-tooth-extraction.3457/post-49463>

I just had oral surgery three months ago. Two weeks ago I somehow got an infection in that same hole. I could only eat soft unchewable foods. Tried a smoothie - made me sick to my stomach. Here are my ideas:

avocado

cubes of fish or small scallops and just swallow (did this a lot)

unflavored good quality gelatin (I use Great Lakes)

bullet-proof broth

avocado soup - just found a recipe online

cauliflower soup - same as above - just pureed veggies like Soul suggested with layers of flavors

### indigogirl — 2012-09-05T00:48:05+0300

Thanks Pattycakes and Soul!

Soul- he finished the root canal a week ago and it was infected again within 2 days (now on the other side of the gum). Another round of antibiotics later I decided I needed another pair of eyes to see it. Was referred to a periodontist this AM by my dentist and he felt there is a hairline fracture and the tooth has to go. I've had this infection now for about 6 weeks and who knows how much longer as a low grade infection so I am actually glad to be rid of the tooth. Next step implant.

### AKMan — 2012-09-06T03:02:06+0300

Indigo, I did so much reading on implants in the body after your Ask Jack post.... thank you so much for this. I had no idea. It was my intuition to not get the implant for my PFO and a bit of anecdotal evidence. I kept referencing my various allergies and I was always shot down with titanium is safe and crap..... I'm still not sure how to convince insurance to pay for heart surgery if they can fix it for a fraction of the cost with a stupid implant that will kill me down the road.... but I'm collecting evidence for my case. Thanks for bringing this to my attention.

I was talking to a friend the other day and he said that one of his friends went to Turkey to have a tooth regrown there from stem cells. I haven't followed up on it since I don't really need to regrow a tooth right now (lol, but I broke one years ago in an accident which is holding up so far but you never know so I keep my eyes and ears open) but I guess it was actually not that expensive compared to the work she would have had to pay for in the U.S. Just something to think about

# Thread: vaccines - educate me, please
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/vaccines-educate-me-please.3458/>
**Subforum:** Optimal Kids
**Posts:** 32
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### ealachan — 2012-09-04T21:34:58+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/vaccines-educate-me-please.3458/post-49418>

I'm posting this in the kids forum, because I figure the mamas in this group will have the most experience with this kind of thing. FWIW, I will preface by saying that I do not have kids, nor do I plan to have them.

So. What I would like to know is: what's the deal with vaccines? I've heard everything from "OMG, filled with heavy metals, will give your kids autism, don't vaccinate" to "OMG, people who don't vaccinate are idiots, diseases that were basically extinct before have made a come-back because people have stopped getting their kids vaccinated". I believe the one major study that linked vaccines with autism was debunked, right? So are vaccines still bad?

I apologize for my ignorance, but it's something I've never really had to read up on in the past, if you see what I mean.

### Shijin13 — 2012-09-04T22:12:36+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/vaccines-educate-me-please.3458/post-49422>

I've worked infectious diseases, from the perspective of CDC and DOD. so my opinion is probably a bit skewed. I've also had several adverse event reactions myself (most likely b/c my immune system was in the tank - can we say BAD GUT!)

any way - Vaccines have their place, they provide herd immunity to the entire population, even if there are those who don't choose to vaccinate. the big up roar w/vaccines was the shoddy study by a brit Doc - who lost his license that said vaccines caused ASD. No such link. That said, no vaccine is 100%. Nothing in life is. so yes adverse advents do happen, and can be triggers for problems if the epigenetic switches have already been turned on.

both kids received all their vaccines on a delayed schedule - but that's how our ped does it. he thinks kids get too many vaccines these days - current standard for MMR is 12mth. back when I was a kid - it was 18mth. our ped's office gives MMR at 18mths. We delayed chicken pox on dd to see if we could find it naturally but no such luck.

I understand why so many people are against vaccinations - we haven't seen a major disease epidemic in close to 40 yrs. none of our generation remember polio, or small pox. none of the diseases we face today - are as scary as facing life paralyzed from a virus, or a virus that has an 20% survival rate like small pox, w/o vaccination.

Initially I was against vaccination until I read the book Demon in the Freezer. its a big wake up call. it lays out why vaccines are important, and what benefits they bring to the table.

now - flu shots - I've never had luck w/them. after reading the BG series, as a family we're skipping them, and we'll continue to do so in the future.

### ealachan — 2012-09-04T22:34:18+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/vaccines-educate-me-please.3458/post-49427>

I remember hearing about the shoddy study that was debunked later on, yes.

I guess what I have a hard time with is people who view the situation in terms of absolute black and white. I'm guessing it's likely easier to see abosolutes (to a certain degree, anyway) when one is suddenly responsible for a child's life as well as one's own...but still. It just makes me wonder.

Incidentally, when I was a kid (which, I'm 32, so not THAT long ago), they didn't vaccinate for chicken pox...at least where I grew up, anyway. Aside from saving your kid from a week of relative discomfort, what's the benefit to vaccinating for chicken pox? It can't actually make you terribly ill unless you get it as an infant, correct? Polio, small pox, serious diseases I can certainly see the value in vaccinating for...but chicken pox?

### KiwiLauren — 2012-09-04T23:37:41+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/vaccines-educate-me-please.3458/post-49437>

I think there are several variables here and this is a very complex issue in which context is, again, important.

1) The biggest worry in vaccines isn't the idea or process of vaccination but the adjuvants that are added... heavy metals, formaldehyde, etc. These things preserve vaccination material but are not necessary (in other words other preservatives could be used). However they are highly toxic. Which dovetails with....

2) If you have a leaky gut already and you get these highly toxic materials into your body, you become at further risk for accumulation. If you are a child with a developing brain, it is worse. This also has to do with having sufficient methylation pathways on board, but these are often compromised in the same children/people who have leaky guts.

3) To further complicate things, vaccinations are apparently dispensed in tubes that contain several doses, so the nurse/doc who administers, withdraws the dose for one child at a time until the tube is empty. However, the heavy metals sink to the bottom. If the medical professional isn't aware of this and/or doesn't shake the tube vigourously each time, the last person to receive the vaccine gets the full amount of toxic preservatives.

4) I do believe that Wakefield's research has been totally debunked. And if you listen to Dr K on this he suggests that the autism link is more likely the mother's DHA levels and health that allows the growing fetus to build the correct brain/body structures.

5) Not only is there questionable benefit about vaccinating for things like chicken pox, but immunity lasts for a limited time, so for some vaccinations, immunity ends at a highly vulnerable time (early adulthood when pregnancy could be likely). Children who are at greatest risk of adverse consequences from things like whooping cough or chicken pox are also the ones who are the most immune compromised to begin with. So this points to bigger issues than just vaccination. And the decisions about vaccination may need to be made in context of this person's overall health and status.

6) The most vulnerable age, for example, for whooping cough is under 3-6 months when the child is too young to vaccinate. Hence, there is big overlap with things like breastfeeding and overall health.

7) There is a strong argument in 'for the greater good' (it's public health thinking). However, if you work in other ways to insure good immune function in you/your child, you not only reduce your own risk of contracting an illness, but you reduce the risk to the population in doing so. (There is also good population data that small pox and polio were actually waning - in other words, we'd created our own species immunity through infection - before the vaccine came on the scene, so no clear way of knowing if the vaccine is what stopped outbreaks or not.)

8) A GP friend told me that most people do not need the full amount of vaccination to confer immunity... the amount given and the numbers of injections are another public health issue (so it's an estimate of averages). She suggests testing the person after each round to see if they have immunity. But this is another out-of-the-box issue that most GPs won't believe or want to do I'd guess.

It is a very complex and personal issue. When it comes to parents making decisions about their children, it's very hard to get good information because both 'sides' are highly charged.

### vlynnb — 2012-09-04T23:47:53+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/vaccines-educate-me-please.3458/post-49442>

I know you have probably looked this subject up elsewhere and are now asking mom's what they have decided but I want to refer you to this site: [http://www.everydayhealth.com/kids-health/dr-mallikas-5-fast-facts-why-kids-need-the-chickenpox-vaccine.aspx](http://http://www.everydayhealth.com/kids-health/dr-mallikas-5-fast-facts-why-kids-need-the-chickenpox-vaccine.aspx)

This Dr. states very clearly what I have read elsewhere and has lead me to believe in the immunizations. One reason is statistically 1% of those vaccinated have serious complications of the disease while 10% of those who contract the disease come down with serious complications. I don't want to take the chance that one of mine will be very ill from the disease or that after contracting the disease themselves they could be responsible for serious illness in another. I have had one sister-in-law and one niece in very serious condition, hospitalized with pneumonia, as a complication of chicken pox. Don't want to go through that again. Another acquaintance almost died and has neurological problems after acquiring whooping cough as a two week old baby and being hospitalized for 3 months. All being exposed by people who decided that not getting the vaccines was OK for them, they didn't get very sick, just those around them who didn't know they had a compromised immune system of some sort.

### jeanie@truelongmont.com — 2012-09-04T23:54:52+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/vaccines-educate-me-please.3458/post-49444>

Since you were clearly about to ask. . .

I didn't want to do vaccines - but started to panic about it, so did them on a delayed schedule - plus the Docs in the family read us the riot act about it. I was so out of my depth with a baby (I had never even babysat before or held one) that I tended to freak out that I was going to kill him.

I'm all good and tiger mom now, so I don't know if I would do it again.

I think I would go a hybrid route and try and utilize family physicians to get preference and 1st dibs on a new bottle - and hope they felt strongly enough about it to humor me. . or I would look into see if there were options for kinder/gentler preservatives.

All the docs in the family are slathered in sunscreen and takin statins for the heart disease and god only knows what for their beeteees, so we are NOT on the same page with all this. It sure would make life easier if they were on board. . .

Hey XX I need a refill on my metformin

Hey XX I need a new hormone panel

The problem is they are good and well meaning people and would have to explain to us why we are the ones who are nuts.

Glad I got that off my chest.

I don't want to take the chance that one of mine will be very ill from the disease or that after contracting the disease themselves they could be responsible for serious illness in another. Another acquaintance almost died and has neurological problems after acquiring whooping cough as a two week old baby and being hospitalized for 3 months. ### Shijin13 — 2012-09-05T00:07:23+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/vaccines-educate-me-please.3458/post-49450>

Right now I have had almost as many vaccines as my kids have had... and that's from being deployed overseas with the military.

I'm also one of the few 70's babies that actually was vaccinated against small pox and I also received the old version of the TB vaccine when it used a live virus - every time I get tested for TB I test positive b/c of the vaccine - then I have to show my shot records etc... to keep from being put on treatment. I have received boosters since w/dead virus but hey - I had an old school ped was adamant about Small Pox and TB live virus vax  my SP Scar is on the inside of my left thigh (Which is one of the approved areas for vaccination of SP)

one of the reasons they're pushing chicken pox is b/c there are fewer and fewer of live cases going around, its a preventative measure so kids who weren't exposed as kids don't get really sick if a live case arises when they are teens/adults (CPx is much worse in adult hood than in kids, and far more deadly) The other thing the CPx vaccine is supposed to do is protect you from shingles... however, those who get the CPx vaccine will require boosters to stay current....

### Owl — 2012-09-05T00:28:53+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/vaccines-educate-me-please.3458/post-49455>

I am 53, and had chicken pox at about 6 years old. My younger sister had it at the same time. People back then wanted their kids to get it, because as Shijin said, it's much more dangerous to get as an adult. There was no vaccine for it then.

I also had the smallpox vaccine on my outer thigh, and had the polio sugar cube vaccine.

My DH is older, but has never had chicken pox, and will probably never catch it now, since he's not exposed to kids. That's a good thing, because he won't get shingles from it later. His mom suffered horribly from that when she was going through lung cancer treatment.

### kathylu — 2012-09-05T18:54:12+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/vaccines-educate-me-please.3458/post-49613>

I really go back and forth on the vaccine issue. In general I think they are helpful, certainly on a population wide level. However, if your child is the one that experiences an adverse event, what can you do? Nothing. So, I have been vaccinating my son on a very delayed schedule. No more than one vaccine per visit. Always the ones that have no mercury and the lowest level of aluminum or other adjuvants possible. By the way, aluminum from vaccines is deposited in brain and bone...scary. I'd certainly be relieved if he contracted "wild" chicken pox, but there is not a huge chance of that. I waited until he was 4 to give him MMR, because then a single injection will produce immunity instead of the required two doses. I won't give him the IPV unless we travel to an area where polio is an issue. Like that. Each vaccine is an individual decision. It's funny, I had the ped office nurse ask me why I was proceeding so slowly with this and I replied "maybe because I'm a scientist, I see potential for harm that others don't". Her reply? That "it is much easier being a parent if you are stupid"...sheesh!

### Jude — 2012-09-06T03:05:02+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/vaccines-educate-me-please.3458/post-49652>

quote" Her reply? That "it is much easier being a parent if you are stupid"...sheesh!"

I take that as a back handed compliment to you Kathylu! It's like she's saying that she recognises the/your problem but it's easier for her and parents just to submit to THE timetable despite the possibility of complications. The old saying "ignorance is bliss",to me, summarises her comment??

### TribalSpice — 2012-09-06T03:31:44+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/vaccines-educate-me-please.3458/post-49657>

I think vaccination is the hardest decision any parent has to make. We chose not to vaccinate our kids after a lot of agonizing. The reasons we came to this conclusion was first off that mercury (thimersal), aluminium, phenoxyethanol, and formaldehyde all cause demyelinisation, plus liver damage. Many also contain a number of antibiotics. These all have an effect on health in one way or another. The argument of "herd immunity" also didn't sit well, we aren't a herd. We don't eat the same food, from the same sources, we don't have the same close interactions that herds do. Therefore, just because my neighbour comes down with the flu, doesn't mean I will (actually, I doubt I will).

My eldest is a teenager now and has brilliant health, she would be completely drug-free except she busted her foot badly and had a single paracetamol to get to sleep one night. My other 2 are completely drug-free, happy healthy, active amazing kids. We have done everything to ensure they are healthy, great diet, good lifestyle, plenty of sunshine, chiro, lots of laughter, hugs, kisses.

We have had plenty of exposure to childhood diseases, including my DH coming down with "man-mumps" (he is vaccinated), a number of exposures to chicken pox and WC, but every time their immune systems kicked in full on and they stayed well.

One last point is with looking at all the information that Dr Jack gives here, there is a place for "disease" in evolution and improved health. There is a difference between coming in contact and genetic/epigentic changes occurring to increase the strength of the population and surcoming to sickness and TBH I am not willing to intentionally expose myself to HIV to see what changes occur, but these childhood diseases are ones that we have been surviving for millennia. We can become germaphobic and reach new levels of poor health or we can embrace health and that is where germs are no concern to us.

I think with understanding that living with optimal health, our bodies were designed to deal with all this stuff. It has been 9 years since I was last sick. I am getting close to optimal, and I want my kids to stay with me here.

### IceBarbie — 2012-09-06T09:45:21+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/vaccines-educate-me-please.3458/post-49697>

My kids, ages 18, 25 & 29 have not been vaccinated (with 1 exception) and we have never regretted our decision. My oldest son did receive one set of shots at 2 months of age back in 1983 but he had a bad reaction to them and this was before the number of vaccines grew to what they are now. He had a high fever, high-pitched scream then he fell asleep and was hard to wake up again. So we just said, "Well, that can't be good for him" and stopped vaccinating and never looked back. None of my kids has ADD, ADHD or Autism. They are all very healthy, creative and have high IQ's. Of course, they all nursed until they were at least 2 years old too. The oldest one nursed until he was 3 1/2.

I continue to keep up with vaccination news and I still haven't found a good reason to do it. By the way, Dr Wakefield's claim about the MMR vaccine causing Autism (which was completely related to gut damage) was duplicated by other researchers. But the Powers That Be don't want us to know about those studies. He rocked the "Big Pharma" boat too much with his recommendation that the MMR be divided and taken separately instead of clumped into one vaccine so he wasn't even against vaccines--just the MMR combo that was causing gut issues in his young patients. Here is one article that discusses this:

[http://www.naturalnews.com/031116\_Dr\_Andrew\_Wakefield\_British\_Medical\_Journal.html](http://http://www.naturalnews.com/031116_Dr_Andrew_Wakefield_British_Medical_Journal.html)

I'm also fascinated by info that discusses why so many kids are deathly allergic to peanuts these days. Turns out that Peanut Oil is used in many vaccines and that triggers an allergic response down the road. Same thing with latex allergies--they even stopped using latex in the vaccine bottle tops because so many people started having latex allergies which ironically affected a lot of health care workers who wear latex gloves in their profession. In other countries that use other types of oils instead of peanuts in vaccines, those places have allergy issues to THOSE oils instead of peanuts. And if you read the scientific info, you find that other foods have similar molecular weights as peanut oil so those severe allergies extend to certain fruits as well.

And, many of these dangerous diseases such as Whooping Cough are kept alive in society by vaccines. Whenever there's a breakout, it's mostly Vaccinated kids who catch it (since most vaccines don't really work as promised anyway). And the babies who are too young for the vaccines are at risk because they contract WC from newly vaccinated brothers and sisters. This happened to my friend's infant--she got WC from her older sister and nearly died. She didn't get it from an unvaccinated kid running around the neighborhood. I tried to keep my kids away from freshly vaxed kids because I saw them as toxic. I didn't rely on "herd immunity" by letting other kids take the vaccine risk. We all have choices and NOT injecting my kids with mercury or aborted fetus cells was MY choice and risk I was willing to take.

It's hard to think outside the box when everyone else around you only knows what mainstream medicine tells them to believe. My parents had a FIT that I nursed my kids! They thought I was a bad mother when I wouldn't shove a bottle of fluoridated water into their mouths all day long because "They Say" nursing babies need to drink water because there isn't enough water in breast milk. On and on it went fighting with relatives and so-called friends about so many issues including natural childbirth, no circumcision for my sons, nursing a baby older than 4 months old, no vaccines, no polyester flame-retardant sleepers (mostly cotton clothes), and a family bed to boot. Again, no regrets! I feel like we really dodged a lot of bullets going back to the basics with our kids even if it was against CW. Most of us are here on this forum now because we followed Conventional Wisdom and compromised our health.

### kathylu — 2012-09-06T17:27:01+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/vaccines-educate-me-please.3458/post-49749>

Well, I can't really argue with the decision not to vaccinate because I have had these thoughts as well. However, I want to bring up a slightly different point of view. First, the assumption that primitive man was supremely healthy and resistant to all viral and bacterial diseases is just false. Yes, perhaps immunity was better, but maybe not, we really can't answer that question. A few examples of populations where terrible epidemics have occurred are native Hawaiians and native Americans, supremely healthy populations who were almost wiped out by the introduction of measles virus into the population. Black plague (bacterial...Yersinia pestis) has repeatedly caused population decimation. Malaria, Dengue fever, smallpox, etc. Polio did not kill huge numbers, but paralyzed a certain percentage of people infected...that is almost totally prevented by vaccination now. To say that vaccines haven't reduced the incidence of many of these diseases is not true. And of course you are benefiting from "herd immunity". How could you not? If you are surrounded by a buffer of immune individuals, of course it lessens the possibility of spread of these diseases and therefore lessens your chance of contracting a disease. Viruses and bacteria are very good at what they do. They've been at it longer than we have been around, if they weren't very efficient at infecting a host, they would die out. Something to think about.

### TribalSpice — 2012-09-08T02:34:42+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/vaccines-educate-me-please.3458/post-50145>

> **kathylu;50290 wrote:** First, the assumption that primitive man was supremely healthy and resistant to all viral and bacterial diseases is just false. Yes, perhaps immunity was better, but maybe not, we really can't answer that question. A few examples of populations where terrible epidemics have occurred are native Hawaiians and native Americans, supremely healthy populations who were almost wiped out by the introduction of measles virus into the population. Black plague (bacterial...Yersinia pestis) has repeatedly caused population decimation. Malaria, Dengue fever, smallpox, etc. Polio did not kill huge numbers, but paralyzed a certain percentage of people infected...that is almost totally prevented by vaccination now. To say that vaccines haven't reduced the incidence of many of these diseases is not true. And of course you are benefiting from "herd immunity". How could you not? If you are surrounded by a buffer of immune individuals, of course it lessens the possibility of spread of these diseases and therefore lessens your chance of contracting a disease. Viruses and bacteria are very good at what they do. They've been at it longer than we have been around, if they weren't very efficient at infecting a host, they would die out. Something to think about.

Epidemics of measles did kill off large numbers of native peoples, but think about where we go measle from? It came with agriculture and living in close proximity to cattle. The Indigenous Americans, Hawaiians and Australians had no agriculture and no passive immunity passed on for generations, until those that survived such epidemics started having children. Having said that, there has not been a disease that has 100% death rate, even Ebola there are those in the population that survive. What is the difference between those that survive and those that don't?

Look also at how some of these diseases are communicated, Polio is oro-fecally transferred. So it requires water polluted with effluent, or some serious poor hygiene practices to transfer.

As to riding off the back of the immunity of others, it makes sense, but reality is different. Where I live we had a whooping cough epidemic last year. The vast majority of people that came down with it were fully vaccinated, in actual fact those that seemed much less likely to get it were the unvaccinated. I did a lot of visiting of friends whos families were effected (because I don't get sick) to help save their sanity while being quarantined. I got not so much as a sniffle. Herd immunity only works if a)we are in fact a herd and b)vaccines have 100% efficacy (which we all no they don't). Recently in Australia they have even cut back our whooping cough vaccination programs after they not only didn't reduce numbers of cases but have been suspected of altering wild whooping cough to make the vaccine completely useless.

Yep, viruses and bacteria are awesome at what they do, but the most successful ones are those that leave their hosts alive and well. It is hard to keep procreating when your food sources keep dying off.

### Chrysanthemama — 2012-09-08T05:27:12+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/vaccines-educate-me-please.3458/post-50191>

It would be wonderful if the majority of parents could do the appropriate research and determine which vaccines, if any, to choose for their children. There are many good resources out there for parents, but most simply follow the advice of their pediatrician, most of whom follow the advice of the current federal and state guidelines. My pediatrician literally spent hours with me offering up her current opinions about vaccines and then letting me make my informed choices. We elected to do certain vaccines, and did them on our chosen schedule and ages. My oldest had an adverse reaction to the MMR vaccine, and I elected to wait until my youngest was 5 to vaccinate her. We also had local outbreaks of measles, with very serious consequences for those who got sick, and that also influenced my decision, as well as expected overseas travel.

I think that every parent needs to make an informed decision, even when that decision means zero vaccination or full vaccination. In the end it's a personal choice, but unfortunately it's not usually an informed one for the majority.

### IceBarbie — 2012-09-11T23:01:14+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/vaccines-educate-me-please.3458/post-51059>

> **freshveggies;51625 wrote:** what about the tetnus shot. Any thoughts one way or the other?

We haven't done them. My son got a bad cut on his arm from a box cutter while cutting a shingle and when we took him to the ER, we were willing to let him have a tetanus shot but the doctor said it wouldn't really matter anyway to give him a shot or the booster so he didn't. And my son was fine. The article above did mention that you can give a tetanus shot immediately after an injury and it does work. And I've read that there currently are treatments available for lockjaw. It's pretty rare to contract because you need get it from sharing dirty needles or from stepping in affected horse manure with an open wound on your foot or from animal bites for example. If these are dangers for you or your kids, then by all means, vaccinate for tetanus.

Tetanus--According to Dr. Roy Dittman:

"The CDC reports that tetanus is not contagious and must be contracted from either an animal bite, shared needles from drug use, or other exposure. A tetanus shot can be obtained immediately after a suspected infection delivering a virtual 100% cure. You can check this one off your list." [http://www.naturalhealth365.com/vaccines-2/back-to-school.html](http://http://www.naturalhealth365.com/vaccines-2/back-to-school.html)

### TribalSpice — 2012-09-12T03:47:52+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/vaccines-educate-me-please.3458/post-51107>

Tetanus is a difficult one. It is less of a vaccine and more akin to an antivanine. You aren't building an immunity to the actual bacteria, but creating antibodies to the neurotoxin produced by the bacteria. It would be one I would consent to under certain circumstances. The issue we have in Australia is there is no individual vaccine for tetanus, you have to get the whole DTPa just for tetanus. Because of this we use peroxide as antiseptic on wounds which highly oxygenates the area.
