### Josh — 2014-03-28T05:52:07+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/vaccines-educate-me-please.3458/post-118421>

Decent Thimerosol Effects review by Dr. Mullan, she also has free calls on Tuesday nights if one signs up.

> **_Keep Methylation Central_**
>
>
> Any one would have problems with Thimerosal...
>
> Hi, I am Dr Nancy Mullan,
>
>
> I help people with chronic disease who are open to working with dietary change and genetics based nutritional supplementation, and who want to go back to developing their lives as opposed to dealing with illness.
>
> Thimerosal is the mercury-laden preservative that is used in flu shots and other vaccines. Thimerosal damages your genes in three ways:
>
> Number Three Kind of Damage:
>
> Thimerosal damages your genes by inhibiting key enzymes in the production pathway of methyl groups, which are central regulators of gene function, and which also repair gene structure.
>
> Thimerosal has the chemical structure and formulation described below:
>
> [](http://clicks.aweber.com/y/ct/?l=H.Ihk&m=42leCWo0Q9aPYn6&b=tGOqcHYSiRlafa8_l9oO5A)
> Thimerosal releases organic mercury into your body. Organic mercury is an old and familiar poison. The Mad Hatter from Lewis Carroll's Alice in Wonderland demonstrates just how long it has been common knowledge that mercury causes neurological degeneration.
>
> The Hatter was mad, out of his mind, because people who made hats were known to use mercury and suffer neurological damage in the process.
>
> Mercury in even nano gram amounts inhibits a key enzyme in the biochemical pathway that makes methyl groups, a one-carbon, three-hydrogen molecule, that is central to the regulation of gene function and the repair of gene structure.
>
> Methyl groups help to turn genes on and turn genes off. Methylation is a regulator of DNA function. It also edits DNA transcription and repairs breaks in the DNA structure.
>
> Methylation silences DNA: it acts to keep viral DNA static, to keep it from reproducing and becoming an infection.
>
> Methylation is the attachment of a methyl group on to another molecule. If you do not have enough methyl groups in your body, inadequate methylation is the result.
>
> You may not have enough methyl groups because of an enzyme problem like MTHFR+, or because you've been exposed to an enzyme poison such as mercury.
>
> Lack of methylation allows DNA that should be silent to be expressed. Viruses can insert their DNA into your own body's DNA. When you do not have enough methylation capacity, that viral DNA can no longer be silenced, and can grow, express itself, and proliferate.
>
> The MMR vaccine is a live virus vaccine. Two out of the three viruses in the MMR are retroviruses. A retrovirus is a kind of virus that can insert itself into your DNA.
>
> Someone with adequate methylation may tolerate this vaccine because the injected and proliferating virus can be kept quiet. Someone without adequate methylation is at risk from any vaccine containing virus.
>
> Number Two Kind of Damage:
>
> Thimerosal is very closely chemically related to a drug that is used to inhibit the growth of DNA.
>
> [](http://clicks.aweber.com/y/ct/?l=H.Ihk&m=42leCWo0Q9aPYn6&b=tGOqcHYSiRlafa8_l9oO5A)
> The figure above depicts o-mercaptobenzoic acid. O-mercaptobenzoic acid is a drug which is used to treat HIV.
>
> HIV is a retrovirus so it can insert its DNA into your DNA sequences, both reproducing and living in your cell as a host by doing this.
>
> Many drugs that are used to treat HIV and cancer work by inhibiting the enzymes in key pathways for the synthesis of DNA and RNA bases.
>
> DNA and RNA bases are purines and pyrimidines. They are required for the production of new cells. This drug is used for both aids and cancer, in which diseases you have rapidly growing cells that you want to stop from dividing.
>
> If you inhibit DNA synthesis, the cells can't divide. In the case of HIV or cancer, that's a benefit.
>
> In the case of a child whose body needs to develop, or anyone wanting to have normal gastrointestinal mucosa, healthy wound healing, or good immune function, it works against you.
>
> Looking at the chemical structure of thimerosal in Figure 2, you can see that it is very similar to that of o-mercaptobenzoic acid. Cleavage can occur at the sulfur-mercury bond, and the release of ethyl mercury happens.
>
> Many researchers and clinicians have been primarily concerned about the release of organic mercury from thimerosal. But if you look at what is left behind after mercury is cleaved off, you see a molecule that is very close to the chemical structure of o-mercaptobenzoic acid, a chemical that can inhibit the growth of a retrovirus by acting on its ability to make DNA.
>
> The biochemical pathway that MTHFR+ affects is the same one that makes DNA bases. If you have a problem with MTHFR and someone injects you with thimerosal, you are going to have double trouble trying to make new DNA. In particular, you will not be able to make thymidine because thymidine has to be methylated, and MTHFR+ inhibits your ability to make methyl groups.
>
> Number One Kind of Damage:
>
> Thimerosal conforms to the basic structure of DNA bases and may substitute in the DNA for some bases.
>
> [](http://clicks.aweber.com/y/ct/?l=H.Ihk&m=42leCWo0Q9aPYn6&b=tGOqcHYSiRlafa8_l9oO5A)
> The DNA bases are pyrimidines and purines, the structures on the right in the above figure. The chemical structure of thimerosal is very similar to the DNA bases themselves. Look at thimerosal drawn two different ways in Figure 3.
>
> DNA bases are also called nucleotides. It is known that mercurated nucleotides, nucleotides with a mercury molecule deliberately put on it, will incorporate into your DNA.
>
> Thimerosal can form up like a DNA base, incorporate into your DNA and get stuck there. Your DNA is not going to work properly with that foreign molecule there.
>
> When are you going to say **enough** to thimerosal administration in vaccines, any vaccine?
>
> As a consumer, you have a lot of power. Just as boycott is going to finish GMO foods and accomplish what legislation does not, boycott can wrap it up for thimerosal in vaccines.
>
> Drug companies will not make what people will not buy. You can vote with your feet...
>
> **You have got to figure** that if thimerosal is so deadly to contaminants in a vaccine, **it might be bad for you also...**
>
> I hope this helps you understand the many ways in which thimerosal can interfere with your biochemical functioning.
>
> I hope this helps you 'just say no' to vaccines that contain thimerosal, like the flu shot, which is recommended for the young, the sick, the pregnant and the elderly, just the people who are most vulnerable to the problems of this drug.
>
> My Tuesday night call is your chance to learn what you need to know to get important areas of your health and biochemistry understood.
>
> I take your questions on many topics, among them the impact of your personal genetics on your biochemistry. I discuss how to balance your biochemistry with nutritional supplements that bypass problem genetics.
>
> **It is up to _YOU_ to avoid the hazards that are put in the way of your health.**
>
> **You need to know about the role of optimizing both methyl group production and your capacity for methylation.**

### optimeyes — 2014-11-18T07:37:26+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/vaccines-educate-me-please.3458/post-147687>

Every \*body\* is different, and each parent needs to make up their own minds about their children.

First of all, Dr. Wakefield's license was taken away because he conducted his research in a supposedly distasteful manner. What he did was "pay" for his blood donations by giving candy and a dollar to children at a party, where he collected the blood, with the children and their parents' consent. So, because he did not collect the blood in a lab, and because he "bribed" his "volunteers," his methods were not considered valid. :-\\

Secondly, vaccines HAVE been tied to autism, AND so have wild viruses. Vaccines have been tied to other neurological issues, hence that little waiver you must sign when you receive a flu vaccine, stating you are aware that flu vaccine can cause Guillain Barre. Oh, here's the vaccine insert for one DPT shot that mentions autism: [http://www.fda.gov/downloads/BiologicsBloodVaccines/Vaccines/ApprovedProducts/UCM101580.pdf](http://www.fda.gov/downloads/BiologicsBloodVaccines/Vaccines/ApprovedProducts/UCM101580.pdf) Yep, that's on the FDA website.
And scroll to page 11 if you will to see:
"Adverse events reported during post-approval use of Tripedia vaccine include idiopathic thrombocytopenic purpura, SIDS,
anaphylactic reaction, cellulitis, autism, convulsion/grand mal convulsion, encephalopathy, hypotonia, neuropathy, somnolence
and apnea. Events were included in this list because of the seriousness or frequency of reporting. Because these events are
reported voluntarily from a population of uncertain size, it is not always possible to reliably estimate their frequencies or to
establish a causal relationship to components of Tripedia vaccine.

In other words, they know autism has been reported post-vaccination but are unable to reliably establish a link (for reasons I am about to mention in the following paragraph.) And if they cannot reliably establish a link, they cannot reliably prove no-link!

Third, no double blind w/ placebo trial will ever be conducted for vaccination. It would be considered unethical, therefore there will never be "undeniable proof" that vaccines cause autism. You'd have to not vaccinate a bunch of people, and then expose them to wild pathogens (the unethical part.) You'd have to have dozens of groups, with this group having one vaccine, and the paired group with the placebo of that one vaccine, then you'd need another group with a different vaccine, and its' placebo group. Then a group with two vaccines, and that matching placebo group, etc etc. Then you need the "known to be un-vaccinated" group, because you'd need that group to proof against all the placebo groups. And with all those groups, you'd also need to have subsets, where part of the group was exposed to pathogens and the other part just went about business as usual. Then, this study would have to go on over the course of a decade. So, who would pay for this massive-scale study? Usually the ones that pay for the studies are the drug companies. Do you see a reason they would want to find their products harmful or ineffective? Much easier to say they've done the testing, release the vaccine, and then "oopsy we've made a mistake" when there's an issue. I am not sure how many vaccines have been recalled, but it's been several in just the 10 years I've been researching vaccines.

Another point: no studies have been conducted to date to show the safety/effectiveness of the current vaccine schedule.

Finally, there are way too many medical personnel saying and doing things that just don't make sense. For instance, I was told twice (after two different pregnancies) that I should get my MMR shot since I was not immune to rubella, and that it was completely safe to receive this vaccine while breastfeeding. This is completely false. You cannot even have the MMR vaccine within 3 months of \*conception\* because it is considered unsafe. It is a live virus and it sheds. You would as much want to have MMR vaccine and breastfeed your baby as you would if you actually had the measles. I did not have the MMR after either pregnancy, and I thank goodness for that. (The rubella never takes in my immune system... I have had the MMR shot probably 6 times in my life - every time a doctor found me non-immune as a student attending public schools.)

My personal choice was to go slowly on our own schedule with the vaccines. My brother nearly died from a vaccination reaction (immediately in the office, right before the doctor's eyes) and therefore I already knew our family had sensitivities to them. My children have exemptions because I am against using the fetal components of aborted babies for vaccination, and these babies' DNA is in the live virus vaccines we use today. The babies were aborted \*because\* the moms contracted rubella while pregnant. Rubella in pregnancy can cause all sorts of problems \*including AUTISM!\* ( [http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/21592401](http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/21592401) ) According to this study approximately 16,000 ASD cases were prevented by vaccinating for MMR; however they didn't mention how many adverse reactions, including seizures and death, were recorded in this 10 yr period post MMR vaccine. I would like to see that data reported in the same paper.

**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/risky-cooling-therapy-saves-pregnant-mom-baby-after-cardiac-arrest.3459/post-49439>

Not surprised but a bit angry/sad. They know all this stuff yet they don't use it in so many more cases... especially the ER. When I was there after my skiing accident I had to \*hide\* my plastic bags filled with snow that I was packing my head in. I'm mean, seriously, they use hypothermia on super serious TBI's why not try to protect my not so (?) super serious TBI brain cells.

I'm sticking with it: ERs need mostly tubs of ice instead of beds/stretchers. Probably most of the medical system needs that. INstead, they kept telling me I am going to give myself frost bite and noted "repetitive" in my neuro assessment. Yes - give me back my damn ICE already!!! Who cares about frost bite if your brain cells are at stake. I mean. Really.

### indigogirl — 2012-09-05T00:04:02+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/risky-cooling-therapy-saves-pregnant-mom-baby-after-cardiac-arrest.3459/post-49447>

Thankfully her Dr. knew this procedure. How many don't?

### Shijin13 — 2012-09-07T15:53:54+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/risky-cooling-therapy-saves-pregnant-mom-baby-after-cardiac-arrest.3459/post-49973>

When I was there after my skiing accident I had to \*hide\* my plastic bags filled with snow that I was packing my head in. I'm mean, seriously, they use hypothermia on super serious TBI's why not try to protect my not so (?) super serious TBI brain cells.
>
>
>
> I'm sticking with it: ERs need mostly tubs of ice instead of beds/stretchers. Probably most of the medical system needs that. INstead, they kept telling me I am going to give myself frost bite and noted "repetitive" in my neuro assessment. Yes - give me back my damn ICE already!!! Who cares about frost bite if your brain cells are at stake. I mean. Really.

it seems that only the docs who regularly step outside the box embrace cold...look at the NFL player from the Buffalo Bills who was paralyzed on the first play of the season opener several years ago... what did the team physician and Physical therapists do? They packed him in ICE on a back board before transportation to the hospital... not only saved his life...but he's now walking...

I really think I need to update my Road ID - so the first line says - IF INJURED PACK IN ICE...then my emergency contact info....

### AKMan — 2012-09-07T18:52:38+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/risky-cooling-therapy-saves-pregnant-mom-baby-after-cardiac-arrest.3459/post-50047>

### Glamazon — 2012-09-08T06:14:05+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/risky-cooling-therapy-saves-pregnant-mom-baby-after-cardiac-arrest.3459/post-50197>

> **Shijin13;49959 wrote:** [http://todayhealth.today.com/\_news/2012/08/28/13529917-risky-cooling-therapy-saves-pregnant-mom-baby-after-cardiac-arrest?lite](http://http://todayhealth.today.com/_news/2012/08/28/13529917-risky-cooling-therapy-saves-pregnant-mom-baby-after-cardiac-arrest?lite)
>
>
>
>
>
> anyone here surprise?

It is pretty much the treatment of choice here in the local hospitals for cardiac arrest, especially those who arrest out in the field. Don't really consider it risky when you consider it is done only in ICUs.

I would be more surprised if I found a hospital that did not use the Arctic Sun and hypothermia protocols.

Nobody really will recommend cooling therapy in hospitals for pregnant women without studies. They are like this odd alien creature in pregnancy and so it is a big risk management issue. That would be more the risky part that would worry the staff.

# Thread: Math minds please apply
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/math-minds-please-apply.3464/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 9
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### jeanie@truelongmont.com — 2012-09-05T02:50:14+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/math-minds-please-apply.3464/post-49501>

I want a formula for quickly establishing value to range . . .like when we are looking for something "in the top quartile"

For example:

value of .88 with a range of .4 - 4.5

What would be the excel formula for expressing that as X percentage of value.

Example.

Value of 25, with a range of 1 - 100 would = 25% in the range.

I clearly need a MHS, I used to be able to do this kind of stuff on mah toes. I just need the math, I can do the excel part.

### docwatt@gmail.com — 2012-09-05T02:59:02+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/math-minds-please-apply.3464/post-49504>

You have to assume a distribution of the population of values in the range before you can establish quartiles. If the population is uniformly distributed over the range, the quartile divisions would be at the arithmetic quartiles of the range, if it is a normal distribution, the quartiles are approximately +/- .68 above and below the mean. This can be calculated from determining the area under the curve.

> **Cavemam;50030 wrote:** I want a formula for quickly establishing value to range . . .like when we are looking for something "in the top quartile"
>
>
>
> For example:
>
>
>
> value of .88 with a range of .4 - 4.5
>
>
>
> What would be the excel formula for expressing that as X percentage of value.
>
>
>
> Example.
>
>
>
> Value of 25, with a range of 1 - 100 would = 25% in the range.
>
>
>
> I clearly need a MHS, I used to be able to do this kind of stuff on mah toes. I just need the math, I can do the excel part.

### Shijin13 — 2012-09-05T03:04:59+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/math-minds-please-apply.3464/post-49508>

This is MamaG's speciality. I'll try to take a look when my brain isn't disconnected.

### Souldanzer — 2012-09-05T03:08:14+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/math-minds-please-apply.3464/post-49509>

.88 is about 21.5% of your given range?

Assuming you're just working with the even distribution. Forgive me if I'm wrong. Not the best brain today

Range is 4.1 divide by 100 for percentage points gives you .041. .88 divided by .041 gives you 21.5%. A little more than a fifth. At least that's the box for multiple choice I would check on this sick day

### jeanie@truelongmont.com — 2012-09-05T03:11:13+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/math-minds-please-apply.3464/post-49510>

These are lab reference ranges, I think we assume equal distribution within the range right? They decide L or H simply if the value falls out of the range.

I don't want quartiles, I didn't mean that - I want X% of range... It's on the tip of my neuron. I want the FORMULA, I can figure it out for any given range - but I want to set it up to pull in data and auto apply the forumula.

Make sense?

### jeanie@truelongmont.com — 2012-09-05T03:15:18+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/math-minds-please-apply.3464/post-49511>

And I do realize there will be things where bottom and top ranges don't apply - different plans for those. . .

I'm putting my 20 yrs of awesome spreadsheet goodness to some practical use! I'll share when done!

### Souldanzer — 2012-09-05T03:16:51+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/math-minds-please-apply.3464/post-49512>

x/((high value of range - low value of range)/100)= % of range ....... with x being your lab result......... I think. Still not what you're looking for?

### finnite@dccnet.com — 2012-09-05T04:50:43+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/math-minds-please-apply.3464/post-49534>

> **Cavemam;50030 wrote:** I want a formula for quickly establishing value to range . . .like when we are looking for something "in the top quartile"
>
>
>
> For example:
>
>
>
> value of .88 with a range of .4 - 4.5
>
>
>
> What would be the excel formula for expressing that as X percentage of value.
>
>
>
> Example.
>
>
>
> Value of 25, with a range of 1 - 100 would = 25% in the range.
>
>
>
> I clearly need a MHS, I used to be able to do this kind of stuff on mah toes. I just need the math, I can do the excel part.

here's how I would calculate it:

4.5-0.4 = 4.1

0.88-.4 = 0.48

(0.48/4.1)\*100=11.7%

top quartile would be 0.75\*4.1= 3.075 or higher

### Shijin13 — 2012-09-05T15:03:49+0300

# Thread: Rapeseed oil
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/rapeseed-oil.3466/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 5
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Jasmin — 2012-09-05T08:40:46+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/rapeseed-oil.3466/post-49560>

Is rapeseed oil something that I should avoid when eating epi-paleo? I think that it is but I just need to be sure

### Michael — 2012-09-05T09:24:01+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/rapeseed-oil.3466/post-49562>

I think it's pretty safe to say it would get a thumbs down.

AFAIK, it's modern use dates back to research in Canada aimed at trying to breed the plant so that it did not produce as much erucic acid. This is an unusual fatty acid present in the oil that seems to be linked with heart lesions.

Hence the oil has been sold in North America under the brand name Canola ("Can-" for Canadian).

I believe they weren't totally successful at getting that out. Besides, most of the oil will be produced at high temperature and pressure - because that's what the industry does with seed oils. That degrades them and makes them reactive even before they leave the factory. Even if that weren't so, I think you'd find that the omega 6mega 3 ratio was unhelpful, and that the total amount of PUFAs was excessive.

Googling around I find that Mary Enig, the lipids expert at the WAPF, has written an article on Canola/Rapeseed oil:

[www.westonaprice.org/know-your-fats/the-great-con-ola](http://http://www.westonaprice.org/know-your-fats/the-great-con-ola)

However, the site's down at the moment for maintenance.

### nonchalant — 2012-09-06T01:19:44+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/rapeseed-oil.3466/post-49623>

I believe they partially hydrogenate the oil to eliminate the foul odor. Since the purpose of the hydrogenation is not to make the oil solid, the USDA or whoever deems it unnecessary for them to mention the hydrogenation on the label. Pity our bodies don't understand our good intentions.

### shilohman — 2012-09-06T03:02:26+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/rapeseed-oil.3466/post-49649>

Not sure what perceived health benefit you see in rapeseed oil, but the omega 3 is mostly ALA. Your body/brain needs DHA mostly and then EPA. My guess is that the erucic acid would be inflammatory at the gut level. Don't buy into the rapeseed industry's propaganda, rapeseed is sub-sub optimal.

### Jasmin — 2012-09-06T17:07:25+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/rapeseed-oil.3466/post-49742>

THANK YOU SO MUCH there is no doubt im my mind now - no rapeseed for me

# Thread: Injecting BAT into abdominal fat
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/injecting-bat-into-abdominal-fat.3470/>
**Subforum:** Cold Thermogenesis
**Posts:** 3
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### AKMan — 2012-09-06T01:47:42+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/injecting-bat-into-abdominal-fat.3470/post-49631>

Boy, theyare trying hard to figure out how to make this BAT thing profitable. Here is an article that describes how they injected BAT into mice and it worked in reducing the white fat. Incredible.

[http://medicalxpress.com/news/2012-09-mice-heat-generating-cells-belly-fat.html](http://http://medicalxpress.com/news/2012-09-mice-heat-generating-cells-belly-fat.html)

From the article:

Researchers conducting the study were surprised to see that the injected cells even acted like "missionaries," converting existing belly fat cells into so-called thermogenic cells, which use fat to generate heat.

The scientists took advantage of the heat-generating properties of a so-called good fat in the body, brown fat, to cut back on the white fat cells that compose the visceral fat that tends to accumulate in the belly.

A closer look at exactly what was going on in the animals' cells showed that the injected cells produced high levels of a protein called Ucp1, which burns fat, suggesting that this protein assisted in the visceral fat reduction.

Because that creation of heat could be uncomfortable inside a human body, the researchers analyzed the treated mice further to see if the thermogenesis in the belly would produce effects similar to hot flashes.

"Heat production was higher in injected animals, but it was not dramatically higher. So there is some kind of response, but it seems not to be at a magnitude impairing a patient's well-being," Ziouzenkova said. "The animals were also moving less than noninjected animals, but in spite of that, they were still able to lose visceral fat. Their glucose tolerance improved, as well, which is probably related to reduced visceral fat."

Ziouzenkova said she hopes to design additional capsules to target a variety of diseases beyond obesity.

### nonchalant — 2012-09-06T01:57:11+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/injecting-bat-into-abdominal-fat.3470/post-49635>

I'm glad. At least they are researching BAT, instead of new ways to reduce normal lipids, etc.

### PrimalPam — 2012-09-06T05:04:56+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/injecting-bat-into-abdominal-fat.3470/post-49686>

Jay Leno did a joke the other night about "beige" fat and how it helps with the rest of your fat. (joke was something like, we're not fat enough, we need more fat of different colors?).

You know if Jay's joking about it, it's becoming "acceptable" to discuss.

# Thread: High Early Morning Adrenal Result
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/high-early-morning-adrenal-result.3473/>
**Subforum:** Optimal Labs
**Posts:** 9
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Patty Cakes — 2012-09-06T02:55:07+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/high-early-morning-adrenal-result.3473/post-49645>

I have been taking T3 only for five months. Last month I tested my FT3/rT3 ratio and it was 41. Woo-hoo, after a 14.3 ratio, I'm pleased. This month I tested the adrenals via a cortisol saliva kit from ZRT. The top range came back high at 13.6 (3.7-9.5), while all the others were in the perfect place in their respective ranges. Last year's early morning result was at the very bottom of the range so this is a huge jump.

I take my first T3 dose 2.5-3 hours before I get up and two other times during the day. I sure hope I didn't make a mistake with taking it that way before my first saliva sample although I'm not sure how this would make a difference since none of the other times appeared skewed and I had my other two T3 doses as usual. I'm not really having stress and slept good that night. Does anyone know any reason why the high reading would be so high?

### freshveggies — 2012-09-06T03:03:19+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/high-early-morning-adrenal-result.3473/post-49650>

Last year's early morning result was at the very bottom of the range so this is a huge jump.
>
>
>
> I take my first T3 dose 2.5-3 hours before I get up and two other times during the day. I sure hope I didn't make a mistake with taking it that way before my first saliva sample although I'm not sure how this would make a difference since none of the other times appeared skewed and I had my other two T3 doses as usual. I'm not really having stress and slept good that night. Does anyone know any reason why the high reading would be so high?

I read the T3 circadian book. If your cotrisol is too high in the am wouldn't it be better to take the fist dose later --maybe 1 hour before getting up so it doesn't give the adrenals such a big boost?

### Destiny — 2012-09-06T03:19:59+0300

This month I tested the adrenals via a cortisol saliva kit from ZRT. The top range came back high at 13.6 (3.7-9.5), while all the others were in the perfect place in their respective ranges. Last year's early morning result was at the very bottom of the range so this is a huge jump.

>
>
> I take my first T3 dose 2.5-3 hours before I get up and two other times during the day. I sure hope I didn't make a mistake with taking it that way before my first saliva sample although I'm not sure how this would make a difference since none of the other times appeared skewed and I had my other two T3 doses as usual. I'm not really having stress and slept good that night. Does anyone know any reason why the high reading would be so high?

First, congratulations on the ratio! It also looks that you fixed your AF. Great!

Fresheveggies might have a point in regards to the morning cortisol level. It might make sense to try taking your first dose when you wake up?

### Patty Cakes — 2012-09-06T03:36:16+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/high-early-morning-adrenal-result.3473/post-49659>

Thank you both. I just got my results back today so I haven't made any changes yet. Just wondering why it would go so high so I may change what I've been doing. I will most likely end up changing the time of the first dose to when I wake up but I thought it may be something else. Maybe I need to go back and check the book again.

### Patty Cakes — 2012-09-06T04:09:51+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/high-early-morning-adrenal-result.3473/post-49677>

I have also been taking 200 mg pregnenolone divided into two doses. I didn't want to spend the money to test that yet but I would think that may have helped with the pregnenolone steal I had going on. Oh dear ....

### Destiny — 2012-09-06T13:24:07+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/high-early-morning-adrenal-result.3473/post-49703>

I am no expert but I would think with this overall positive change, you should be able to tweak it.

I am not sure about pregnenolone either. I tested and it did not help me to know that it was very low. I still could not take even a very small amount of it. I think your reactions and symptoms are very important to follow with hormones not just your numbers.

### Patty Cakes — 2012-09-06T18:30:02+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/high-early-morning-adrenal-result.3473/post-49774>

Most mornings I feel OK but not like I have that high of a cortisol reading...seriously. I fell hiking the other day (they kicked me out of dancing school when I was 7 so I am not the most graceful bunny around) and its hard not to lie on my deeply scraped up knee/leg so I'm not sleeping as well as I should be. But that should get better once the pain subsides and I can sleep straight through the night.

My BP, temp, and HR are all normal in the morning. I have felt a little wired feeling around 10ish a few times but not on a regular basis and this high reading was from my 6:30 am result. I think I may just keep doing what I'm doing and do a little more investigation to see what may need to change. It's also time for another consult with the doc!

### indigogirl — 2012-09-06T20:11:09+0300

My labs were low in B12 so my Dr. has prescribed B12 shots 1000 mcg. once a month.

I was just on Dr. Mercola's website and he has a B12 spray that delivers 1000 mcg. a day.

My Dr. is CW and now I am wondering how much I should be getting daily and in what form?

I'd appreciate any info on what others are doing!

### KiwiLauren — 2012-09-07T00:44:07+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/b12-how-much.3490/post-49867>

I was taking a sublingual B12 because it was really low (I was a vegetarian for a long time!). As soon as it got into the top quartile fo the range I stopped. I think you need to take whatever form feels right and then just test quarterly if possible. Good luck!

### kjgraffin@yahoo.com — 2012-09-07T02:44:14+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/labs-after-3-months-of-bhrt.3496/post-49889>

These are my results after being on BHRT for 3 months. I want to talk to my doc about making adjustments. I'm thinking that progesterone definitely needs to be increased. Do I need more estrogen? I think I need more testosterone, but am waiting til my PG:E2 ratio is better. It looks like it is 14? Any thoughts?

42 yo female, blood drawn on day 21 of cycle.

Using Estrogen Biest cream .5g (1.5mg/gm) 2x/day

Progesterone is oral at 100mg/day taken at night

DHEA - Was taking 20mg at the time of testing - have switched to 25mg 7ketoDHEA due to breakouts on chest & head

Pregnenolone - Was taking 200mg at the time of testing. Have dropped back to 50mg after high cortisol test results

Vitamin D - 15,000iu/day

DHEAs: 396.4 (60.9-337.0) Optimal 250-380?

Pregenolone: 184 (

### Shijin13 — 2012-09-07T04:14:16+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/labs-after-3-months-of-bhrt.3496/post-49912>

You need some serious Pg! Where your PG is on cd 21 is where it should be at the lowest point at the beginning of your cycle. It should be at the top range on cd21.

As for estrogen. What good is it doing for you if it's still keeping your Pg:E2 ration upside down?

### colleencoble — 2012-09-07T04:56:21+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/labs-after-3-months-of-bhrt.3496/post-49918>

Just remember though, these levels rise SLOWLY. progesterone especially took me a while to get up to optimal.

### colleencoble — 2012-09-07T04:58:55+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/labs-after-3-months-of-bhrt.3496/post-49919>

### Lyndra — 2012-09-08T00:30:40+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/labs-after-3-months-of-bhrt.3496/post-50123>

I take progesterone at night the 2nd half of my cycle, but I am not sure if helps the 1st half yet.

Please try again later.", "lightbox\_start\_slideshow": "Start slideshow", "lightbox\_stop\_slideshow": "Stop slideshow", "lightbox\_full\_screen": "Full screen", "lightbox\_thumbnails": "Thumbnails", "lightbox\_download": "Download", "lightbox\_share": "Share", "lightbox\_zoom": "Zoom", "lightbox\_new\_window": "New window", "lightbox\_toggle\_sidebar": "Toggle sidebar" }

### colleencoble — 2012-09-08T03:10:44+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/labs-after-3-months-of-bhrt.3496/post-50157>

freshveggies, my doc added the estrogen at night because my migraines weren't gone. I needed a more constant stream of hormones. I take the bulk in the morning (.8 cc) and .4 cc at night. So if you don't have migraines, I'm not sure you would notice signs. That was my only sign.

### kjgraffin@yahoo.com — 2012-09-08T03:44:07+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/labs-after-3-months-of-bhrt.3496/post-50165>

JanSz,

The estradiol is pg/ml. The red numbers are from a post that I copied that you did a while back. I don't have the original link.

I do feel like it is like being on a merry-go-round. While trying to correct my dhea & pregnenolone, I caused my cortisol to rise. It is possible the cortisol test was off and I will re-do in another month. In the meantime, I am asking my doc to increase my progesterone. I'm thinking I will also add a daily dose of Maca powder.

I've been doing a lot of work on my circadian rythm. No artificial light after 7pm without blue blocker glasses and then it is rare. I do get up before sunrise around 4am but there isn't much I can do about that right now. I have started supplementing with PS. I started on it just after this blood test was done. I've been taking zinc for about 6 weeks along with selenium.

I'm also waiting on some other lab tests to come back. I did a Comprehensive Stool Test & just had blood drawn for a fatty acid profile. I should have the fatty acid profile back in a couple of weeks.

# Thread: Waking up in the middle of the night
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/waking-up-in-the-middle-of-the-night.3499/>
**Subforum:** The Leptin Rx
**Posts:** 22
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Milton — 2012-09-07T11:41:14+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/waking-up-in-the-middle-of-the-night.3499/post-49944>

Hi everyone!

Since I've started living according to the circadian rythm (meaning not using any artificial light and going to sleep together with the sun) I started waking up around 1-3 AM for about 1-2 hours, then falling asleep again and waking up around dawn. I wake up feeling strong, rested and full of energy but it's becoming quite annoying as I am spending WHOLE night in bed

It has been an issue for about 2 months now and it happens regardless my diet (no, I usually don't eat within 4 hours prior to bedtime) and activity. Any clues?

Milton

### caroline — 2012-09-07T13:33:27+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/waking-up-in-the-middle-of-the-night.3499/post-49948>

I am doing the same thing for about the last 6 weeks - love some clues too.

### Shijin13 — 2012-09-07T15:59:44+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/waking-up-in-the-middle-of-the-night.3499/post-49975>

Liver function needs support with what ever Every time I start waking up btwn 1-3am I know my Liver needs detox support... might want to look into that to see if you need it...

### Milton — 2012-09-07T16:10:24+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/waking-up-in-the-middle-of-the-night.3499/post-49980>

But I had never had such issues before I started paying attention to light cycles and going to sleep much earlier, even in the times of my liver being in much much poorer condition.

### indigogirl — 2012-09-07T16:58:06+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/waking-up-in-the-middle-of-the-night.3499/post-49998>

If I remember correctly (but I don't know where I read this...one of Jack's blogs, a newspaper article?) when people go to sleep with the sun and wake up with the sun 12 hours is too long to sleep. We naturally wake up during this time for an hour or two and then go back to sleep.

### Milton — 2012-09-07T17:20:49+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/waking-up-in-the-middle-of-the-night.3499/post-50008>

But maybe we can somehow decide where to place these 2 hours of staying awake? If not by waking up earlier (and I mean waking up 'naturally', without alarm clocks) than mayby by going to sleep later... but resisting sleep cravings (and that's the case with me when the darkness begins) is even more unnatural...

We're doomed!

### Glen PDQ — 2012-09-07T18:05:04+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/waking-up-in-the-middle-of-the-night.3499/post-50025>

3 PM falls right after the period of autophagy, apoptosis, and protein recycling according to Jack. If I had to grab an idea why out of the air, perhaps it would be that the brain needs to come awake to finish rebooting repaired (or new) brain/nerve circuits. This sounds really cool if it were true. I've experienced the 1pm-3pm wakefullness, too, and it can be annoying.

Do you come awake at this time out of deep sleep or from dreaming? If it is from a lucid dream you can stop from waking up as soon as you feel the dream beginning to break apart by rubbing your dream hands briskly together as if trying to warm them up. The dream will stabilize around you. Another trick is to spin quickly in place. I've tried both methods with success. These virtual actions task the brain to create dream sensations instead of waking up.

### Stavity — 2012-09-07T18:13:20+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/waking-up-in-the-middle-of-the-night.3499/post-50031>

In Traditional Chinese medicine waking up from 1-3 means liver Qi stagnation and stress. I wake up between 3-5 which means lung meridian issues and they are considered to hold the emotion of grief. If this happens frequently again, I am going to write a journal in order to explore any unresolved grief issues I may have, past or present. You may want to get tested for liver function. Last night after eating something bad yesterday, I couldn't sleep between 11 - 1 and that means my gallbladder was acting up. Eat bad food = no sleep til after 1 am.

Here is the Chinese clock. [http://healthmad.com/conditions-and-diseases/tcm-body-clock-is-there-a-right-or-wrong-time-for-everyday-activities/](http://http://healthmad.com/conditions-and-diseases/tcm-body-clock-is-there-a-right-or-wrong-time-for-everyday-activities/)

### nonchalant — 2012-09-07T18:13:31+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/waking-up-in-the-middle-of-the-night.3499/post-50032>

> **Glen PDQ;50570 wrote:** Do you come awake at this time out of deep sleep or from dreaming? If it is from a lucid dream you can stop from waking up as soon as you feel the dream beginning to break apart by rubbing your dream hands briskly together as if trying to warm them up. The dream will stabilize around you. Another trick is to spin quickly in place. I've tried both methods with success. These virtual actions task the brain to create dream sensations instead of waking up.

Interesting, Glen!

I have held onto dreams before, long ago, but didn't know these tricks.

### Stavity — 2012-09-07T18:15:07+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/waking-up-in-the-middle-of-the-night.3499/post-50034>

> **Glen PDQ;50570 wrote:** 3 PM falls right after the period of autophagy, apoptosis, and protein recycling according to Jack. If I had to grab an idea why out of the air, perhaps it would be that the brain needs to come awake to finish rebooting repaired (or new) brain/nerve circuits. >

Keep talkin peeps.

### Huck — 2012-09-07T18:36:19+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/waking-up-in-the-middle-of-the-night.3499/post-50044>

**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/waking-up-in-the-middle-of-the-night.3499/post-50078>

### Milton — 2012-09-07T21:46:57+0300

### Lyndra — 2012-09-07T22:39:35+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/waking-up-in-the-middle-of-the-night.3499/post-50104>

According to Dr. K, it's not abnormal to wake at night - see the video he recommended. [http://www.ted.com/talks/jessa\_gamble\_how\_to\_sleep.html](http://http://www.ted.com/talks/jessa_gamble_how_to_sleep.html). The good part starts at around 2:45 in.

### Primal Dream — 2012-09-07T23:31:03+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/waking-up-in-the-middle-of-the-night.3499/post-50112>

I've only recently (re) started the Leptin Rx, and I have a lot of characteristics that Dr. K mentioned in BG 11 (post menopausal, wonky BG issues, hormone issues etc.). So I expect it will be a while before my sleep issues even out.

I bought a fitbit over the summer, which measures basically one's activity during sleep. I looked over the last several days, and I often wake between 2:30 and 3:30 and/or between 4 and 5. At those times I am often awake for an hour, give or take. Last night I was in bed for something like 9 hours and awoke several times, according to fitbit. I was basically awake between 3 and 4 or so.

This is all so fascinating. I really look forward to better and deeper sleep. I'm not sure I've ever been a deep sleeper. I liked what Jessa Gamble in the ted talk said about the subjects of the study on the human body clock without light cues: they reported being wakeful during the day like they never had experienced before. I long for that day (but want to work with the light cues to get my body on track).

Priscilla

### maggie — 2012-09-08T01:21:17+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/waking-up-in-the-middle-of-the-night.3499/post-50131>

> **Stavity;50579 wrote:** I think Dr Kruse explanation is probably more on the money, however I don't understand it.

### Stavity — 2012-09-08T03:41:52+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/waking-up-in-the-middle-of-the-night.3499/post-50163>

> **Maggie;50678 wrote:** @Stavity: Can you point me to Dr. K' explanation? I don't recall seeing one for this condition. Thanks!

I meant what Glen explained sounded right to me. Read what he said. Most of these people here have far more understanding then I do.

### diane — 2012-09-09T02:46:37+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/waking-up-in-the-middle-of-the-night.3499/post-50357>

A very similar thing has happened to me. Before I did a consult with Dr. K, I was feeling really tired and foggy all day, and he said my circadian rhythms are off, and that I'm tired because of my sleep quality. Since then, I've noticed that I also wake up, but I think it's around 4 or 5 (I don't look at the clock). I wear a sleep mask, which helps because then I don't focus on the fact that I'm awake.

The funny thing is that I've felt better during the day - definitely not as tired and foggy. So I'm not sure if I'm just getting better quality sleep when I am sleeping, or maybe I'm just more conscious of the fact that I'm not sleeping. I'm definitely interested in seeing how everyone works through their sleep issues!

### Milton — 2012-09-10T11:54:39+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/waking-up-in-the-middle-of-the-night.3499/post-50673>

“They slept for a lot longer than usual, but in the middle they woke up and had two hours of quiet contemplation in bed,” says Gamble. “During that time, they had a surge of prolactin – a hormone we use in lactation and sex, but that doesn’t surge during an unbroken eight-hour sleep. People who experienced that ‘anti-nap’ during the night said that the next day, they experienced true wakefulness for the first time.”

Source: [http://www.magazine.utoronto.ca/feature/jessa-gamble-circadian-rhythm-chronobiology-seasonal-affective-disorder-polyphasic-sleep/](http://http://www.magazine.utoronto.ca/feature/jessa-gamble-circadian-rhythm-chronobiology-seasonal-affective-disorder-polyphasic-sleep/)

It indeed feels like being truly awake for the first time in your life. This week, due to my afternoon shift at work, I'll be going to sleep at 11:30 PM at the earliest but able to stay in bed for as long as I desire. We'll see if those 'two hours of quiet contemplation' occur.

### Milton — 2012-09-13T20:32:17+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/waking-up-in-the-middle-of-the-night.3499/post-51479>

UPDATE: I am still waking up in the middle of the night but for not more than 10-15 minutes as far as I'm right (I'm less aware of myself, I'm just "not that awake'). All that means 1-2 extra hours for me but nothing for free - I indeed don't experience the 'true wakefulness' during the day

By the way, check this out! [http://healthysleep.med.harvard.edu/interactive/circadian](http://http://healthysleep.med.harvard.edu/interactive/circadian)

### ATL_Paleo — 2012-09-07T18:14:28+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/gunnar-computer-eyewear.3505/post-50033>

I have used Gunnar eyewear for several years while using my computer. Definitely cuts down on the glare, but does not block blue light the way my blue blockers do. Gunnar is sold at Best Buy and Frys if you want to see them in person. I recommend Gunnars but with the understanding they are not blue blockers.

### cuivie@me.com — 2012-10-13T05:47:22+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/gunnar-computer-eyewear.3505/post-57932>

> **ATL\_Paleo;50578 wrote:** I have used Gunnar eyewear for several years while using my computer. Definitely cuts down on the glare, but does not block blue light the way my blue blockers do. Gunnar is sold at Best Buy and Frys if you want to see them in person. I recommend Gunnars but with the understanding they are not blue blockers.

Do the amber versions of their lenses block blue light? Is it just the clear/crystal that don't? Or do neither?

### ATL_Paleo — 2012-10-13T17:17:53+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/gunnar-computer-eyewear.3505/post-57957>

Using the CD disk test, my Gunnars do not block blue light. On the other hand, the strain on my eyes is most definitely reduced by wearing them while using my computer. .... In summary, I use my Gunnars during the day, and my blue blockers after sunset.

### cuivie@me.com — 2012-10-17T05:50:13+0300

Yeah, went to Best Buy to try them out and they definitely didn't block out all blue light. But supposedly they do block out the excess blue light, so I may get some in my prescription for daytime use and continue with the real blue blockers after sunset as you suggest.

# Thread: Tough Mudder?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/tough-mudder.3521/>
**Subforum:** Optimal Fitness
**Posts:** 8
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### TribalSpice — 2012-09-08T10:33:49+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/tough-mudder.3521/post-50212>

Has anyone here done one? I am doing one in 2 weeks time, just wondering what you ate, how you went etc.

We are down to start around 9:30-10am. I am trying to decide if getting up at 4:30 to eat something is a good idea or not. I normally just have butter coffee first thing in the morning and eat between 11 and 12:30.

Any suggestions would be greatly appreciated.

### Chrysanthemama — 2012-09-08T17:29:27+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/tough-mudder.3521/post-50257>

MDA has a blog post about fueling for a Tough Mudder:

[http://www.marksdailyapple.com/how-to-fuel-ultra-distances-the-worlds-toughest-mudder/#axzz25t9TRX6t](http://http://www.marksdailyapple.com/how-to-fuel-ultra-distances-the-worlds-toughest-mudder/#axzz25t9TRX6t)

### Danco3636 — 2012-09-08T17:29:46+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/tough-mudder.3521/post-50258>

I did one last month. Went into it fasted.... About 16 hour fast and then didn't eat anything afterwards for about 2-3 hours. After that my meal consisted of bacon, seafood and kelp. I was just fine but I fast and eat mostly low carb daily. Some sweet potatoes and berries post lifting workouts. So it may all depend on your overall health, your past eating schedule, foods and how fat adapt you are.......

I think you will need to acces where you are currently. Worst case keep it like how you do your meals based around your current workouts.

Have a fun time......! I had a blast..... And my wife got to help hose me down with some CT afterwards.

\- Dan

### TribalSpice — 2012-09-09T02:11:44+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/tough-mudder.3521/post-50342>

Thanks. I am quite well keto adapted I think I will just listen to my body on the day. I am thinking of making my own chocolate coated coffee beans with raw cacoa, coconut oil, glucose, cocoa butter etc. as well as the coconut water molasses etc hydration drink on the Marks daily apple page. Thanks for that link, it is great.

I am pretty excited about it. We have an awesome team. I should get through it no problems.

I am leaving DH and the kids at home, so someone else will have to hose me down after.

### TribalSpice — 2012-09-10T03:44:01+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/tough-mudder.3521/post-50619>

Hmmm maybe not as well keto adapted than I thought. I fasted from lunch yesterday (oysters, pastured homegrown beef, a little homemade coleslaw, spinach salad). I got up and had a coffee with butter and then went for a run after dropping the kids at school (18hrs since lunch yesterday) I felt awful running. I did some chin ups and monkey bars stuff and then walked about 2km. The walking was fine, the strength stuff not too bad (not optimal though) but the run was totally \*\*\*\*house. I felt foggy in the brain and weak in the muscles and damn hot! It is currently 21C, so not even that hot.

Ok, I have less than 2 weeks to get this sorted. Help!

### Danco3636 — 2012-09-10T16:22:24+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/tough-mudder.3521/post-50706>

Have a meal closer to the event. Perhaps protein and fats in the morning 3-4 hours before. Or eat dinner around 7-8pm so you don't have such a long fast. Cut the fast to 14-16 hours. Try doing different things during training but do what works on event day.

### TribalSpice — 2012-09-25T11:49:22+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/tough-mudder.3521/post-54075>

So, the day before I ate as normal except I had a baked sweet potato with bolognase for dinner. Plus a few Litres of coconut water

In the morning got up had coffee, a can of oysters, some chia pudding (chia, cacao, coconut cream and water) and some home made coffee bean and goji chocolate with coconut oil.

Well, I could have run all day. It was so much fun and there was not a single moment where I felt drained of energy. I am planning on running back to back mudders (both the Saturday and the Sunday) next year.

### Endless — 2012-09-30T19:00:58+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/tough-mudder.3521/post-55415>

Congratulations, and thanks for sharing! I am thinking of doing one in June, if I'm ready.

# Thread: Lights out after dark with a family...
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/lights-out-after-dark-with-a-family.3532/>
**Subforum:** Optimal Kids
**Posts:** 19
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### trishalinn@gmail.com — 2012-09-08T23:03:31+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/lights-out-after-dark-with-a-family.3532/post-50310>

So, I had a consult with Dr kruse and he really stressed this for me. I've been doing the diet and some ct, but this is a piece I haven't implemented yet.

How are ou implementing this with kids? I'm afraid my kids are going to seriously protest at no nightlights.

Another issue I have is my twin 15 month olds still wake a number of times a night. Sometimes to nurse, but sometimes... I don't know why. They just have not ever been good sleepers. It makes it difficult for me to get much sleep which I'm sure isn't helping anything. \*sigh\* any great suggestions on getting one year olds to sleep through the night?

### KiwiLauren — 2012-09-08T23:33:42+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/lights-out-after-dark-with-a-family.3532/post-50314>

Trisha, I've said this in other places before, but we've just been through an entire winter (plus a few months before) with no artificial lights. It got dark here by 4:30/5:00pm. My children were 7 and 11 when we began. So older than yours, to be sure. But I really think the worry about how much children will protest is unfounded. Sure, they will find it strange at first. But they adapt really quickly. We didn't get the blue-blocking glasses, just no lights/screens of any kind from dusk. Basically, we just never turned on the lights, so as it darkened outside we started to light candles. My kids play, do homework, cuddle, talk, make up games, etc all by candlelight. They have zero issues going to bed or falling asleep. And their sleep has improved.

If your little ones are having trouble in the night, can you not put them in a bed in your room? That way you don't need lights on. I remember listening to a TS Wiley podcast and her being asked what to do when someone was afraid of the dark. Her answer was simple: sleep with them until they aren't anymore. Especially if they are waking to nurse, I'd think just being able to 'find' them without lights would be a simple solution.

My guess is that once you get on the circadian cycles you'll find their sleep (and yours) will even out. You can't expect uninterrupted sleep at any age if you're getting artificial light input right up until you go to bed... and during!

Last thought... you could get a salt lamp and put it on the lowest setting in the hallway. It should cast enough light so that it's not pitch black but it would be orange/pink light.

We've had huge changes as a result of following the light cycles and I really recommend it. I know it's hard with the age your children are. But I really think you can do it... and see some pretty good changes right away. Good luck!!

### BJK77 — 2012-09-09T00:37:06+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/lights-out-after-dark-with-a-family.3532/post-50327>

I probably don't have much to add beyond what Lauren has already said. Her responses on other posts helped me get my kids on board with the whole light issue so far!

We've only had a "no-fake light" policy only for a little while now, but the kids are totally embracing it. They think the candlelight is cool and the last few nights they've been asking earlier and earlier to light the candles - before it's even dark! They're 12 and 8, so old enough for me to explain why we're doing this and have them at least somewhat understand.

As far as nightlights in their rooms, I gave them both a flashlight with a red lens that they can sleep with in case they wake at night and MUST get up for something. So far it neither has had to use it, but it makes them feel a bit more secure I think.

### trishalinn@gmail.com — 2012-09-09T01:16:32+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/lights-out-after-dark-with-a-family.3532/post-50335>

Thanks! I have the two 15 month olds, a 4 year old, and an 8 year old. I'm not worried about the 8 year old, but my 4 year old thinks he MUST have a night light. The biggest problem with having the 4 year old sleep with us is tht my husband and I are up and down all night long with the babies and it would be very disruptive to him.

Is there a type of light/ lightbulb we could use in their room as a night light?

I'm also not worried about the babies, I'm sure the darkness will only help them sleep. They have are the sweetest babies with the best dispositions, but sleep horribly! They get probably 7 hours of sleep in a 24 hour period. It's crazy.

### KiwiLauren — 2012-09-09T02:00:08+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/lights-out-after-dark-with-a-family.3532/post-50338>

Trisha, all I can suggest is that you take an experimental attitude ("I wonder what this will be like?"... "Let's try this and see what happens") versus one of certainty ("My four year old must have a night light."). This is totally new and, as a result, can yield very different results. My son used to be very afraid of the dark. He wouldn't even go to the toilet on his own at night, and that was with night lights. Now he happily wanders the house. Like BJK, he has a torch to use if he needs it, but he rarely does. In fact, both of my kids are addicted to evening face dunks. They fill the sink with ice water and do face dunking by candlelight. They say it feels great and helps them sleep! I didn't ask them to do this. They started on their own after watching me. So you may want to experiment with different things and see what works. I'm sure you'll figure it out.

### kathylu — 2012-09-09T02:07:54+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/lights-out-after-dark-with-a-family.3532/post-50340>

Trisha, consider trying this: [http://www.amazon.com/Cloud-Twilight-Constellation-Night-Light/dp/B000VZKGYY](http://http://www.amazon.com/Cloud-Twilight-Constellation-Night-Light/dp/B000VZKGYY) . It worked for my 4 (now 5) year old!

Very low light (we use red only) that projects stars and moons on the ceiling and it turns itself off after 45 minutes. Seemed like a good compromise on the night light issue.

### Shijin13 — 2012-09-09T02:44:55+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/lights-out-after-dark-with-a-family.3532/post-50356>

> **Trisha;50889 wrote:** Thanks! I have the two 15 month olds, a 4 year old, and an 8 year old. I'm not worried about the 8 year old, but my 4 year old thinks he MUST have a night light. The biggest problem with having the 4 year old sleep with us is tht my husband and I are up and down all night long with the babies and it would be very disruptive to him..

Just make it happen. Eventually the 4yo will tell you to turn off your red light b/c it's too bright.

### kjgraffin@yahoo.com — 2012-09-09T15:27:10+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/lights-out-after-dark-with-a-family.3532/post-50464>

I have a 9 year old & 16 year old. My 9 year old is transitioning very well. I struggle with the 16 year old because I can't come up with alternative things for him to do other than be on the computer. I was able to convince them we don't need TV but he has found his favorite shows on the internet to watch. His circadian rythm is really off and I know getting off the computer and turning off the lights would be very good for him. I was an avid reader as a teenager so I can remember that is what I did at night, but he hates reading since it required so much at school. Any ideas on how to occupy a teen at night?

### Shijin13 — 2012-09-09T16:45:14+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/lights-out-after-dark-with-a-family.3532/post-50477>

REading for school is one thing... reading for pleasure is entirely another... ONe of the generas I loved growing up was sci-fi - Has he read HGW Time machine? or War of the worlds? great books... Also anything by robert heinline is awesome too Stranger in a strange land was a trippy cool read in highschool still love it later in life... also philip k dick has great short stories... has he seen blade runner? if so ask him if he'd be interested in reading the short story that inspired it.

These are classics - yet most of them won't be read in school - trust me there are great books out there that he'll love. it only takes one really good one to snag them... even w/all the reading in school... plus the great thing about sci-fi is it usually tackles the things they cover in school from a perspective of other worlds...

other options... if he has not desire to read at all... knitting... eye hand coordination at night in the dark w/red light... talk about a skill builder... betting if you give him that or reading...he'll take the reading

eta I picked up the fountainhead and atlas shrugged in 7th grade - made a serious impact on me then (even if I didn't really grasp everything in both books )

### Jennie747 — 2012-09-09T17:50:59+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/lights-out-after-dark-with-a-family.3532/post-50496>

I just put red bulbs in their night lights. The ones I had in there before were blue LEDs! Ack! (they were cool looking though...)

My daughter woke up every night to nurse until I weaned her at 18mos so I feel your pain on that one!

### freesia — 2012-09-09T20:37:08+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/lights-out-after-dark-with-a-family.3532/post-50535>

16 - yes that would be difficult! LOL about him finding his shows on the internet. Resourceful kid - WILL get his fix.  How about audio books? I don't know what's available in that department but it might be worth a look. Public libraries sometimes have good selections. You could also see if he would get used to followig a guided relaxation CD just before sleep. They can be quite addictive...in a good way. Wish I had more ideas for you!

> **kjgraffin;51019 wrote:** I have a 9 year old & 16 year old. My 9 year old is transitioning very well. I struggle with the 16 year old because I can't come up with alternative things for him to do other than be on the computer. I was able to convince them we don't need TV but he has found his favorite shows on the internet to watch. His circadian rythm is really off and I know getting off the computer and turning off the lights would be very good for him. I was an avid reader as a teenager so I can remember that is what I did at night, but he hates reading since it required so much at school. Any ideas on how to occupy a teen at night?

### trishalinn@gmail.com — 2012-09-10T05:49:36+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/lights-out-after-dark-with-a-family.3532/post-50655>

Alrighty, so we're working on the no artificial lights after dark thing. We're not quite all the way there yet, but it was only day 2 so I'm sure we'll get there soon!

Yesterday, we didn't get lights out quite before dusk. My husband and I discussed we just need to maybe try to encourage the kids to keep lights out as much as possible even during the day. We need to make sure the shades are open for optimal sunlight in the house. I'll sometimes need light in the kitchen for meal prep and in the basement for laundry, etc.

We did really good tonight! We had all of the lights out before dusk. The older boys both thought the candles were really cool (especially my 8 year old). We have, for now, let them keep their small night light in their room, but they've been told that it will be going away soon. I actually may try doing what Jennie suggested and swapping the bulb out for a red light. The boys have always liked us to keep the hall way light on, that is OUT... and amazingly with no fussing!

A couple glitches we've run into and things we need to do:

\- Get a different bulb for the refrigerator - opened the door to get a drink of water tonight and got blinded by the refrigerator light!

\- It would be nice to have a reading light/lamp of some sort besides candle light. Maybe we can just get a bulb for our bedside lights?

\- We live in a subdivision and have outside lights all around us. Our neighbor in the back has a huge flood light (terribly irritating even prior to this). Anyhow, we already put light-blocking shades in our bedrooms. I was noticing last night (as I was up and down ALL night long with babies). That there is still a little bit of light that gets in between the edge of the shade and the windows. Do I need to be concerned about this, or will this little bit of light not cause a problem? If I need to be concerned about this, any good suggestions?

\- Any recommendations on good red/amber blue-blocking bulbs to use? I see there are bulbs on LowBlueLight.com, but they are quite pricey. Are the cheaper red/amber lights you can buy on Amazon or in our local store just as good? Is there something in particular I should be looking for?

### KiwiLauren — 2012-09-10T06:16:57+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/lights-out-after-dark-with-a-family.3532/post-50658>

> **Trisha;51210 wrote:** Alrighty, so we're working on the no artificial lights after dark thing. We're not quite all the way there yet, but it was only day 2 so I'm sure we'll get there soon!

That there is still a little bit of light that gets in between the edge of the shade and the windows. Do I need to be concerned about this, or will this little bit of light not cause a problem? If I need to be concerned about this, any good suggestions?
>
> \- Any recommendations on good red/amber blue-blocking bulbs to use? I see there are bulbs on LowBlueLight.com, but they are quite pricey. Are the cheaper red/amber lights you can buy on Amazon or in our local store just as good? Is there something in particular I should be looking for?

Yea for you! You're doing so great... so much progress in such a short time.

I hear you re: neighbours. It sucks. We literally have rooms we don't go into after dusk because I can't block out all the street lights and neighbour's lights in all the rooms. I have, however, worried about the light you've referred to in the bedrooms coming in through the edges. Our budget is super tight so I dealt with this by going to the local opportunity shop and getting a bunch of old curtains and blankets for $2 each and I stuff them above the curtains wherever light seeps in. I also use clips to keep the curtains back against the wall.

Oh... and the fridge! I know. It's amazingly bright. I try to never open ours after dusk. I'm thinking of just taking the bulb out.

### KiwiLauren — 2012-09-10T06:28:46+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/lights-out-after-dark-with-a-family.3532/post-50660>

> **kjgraffin;51019 wrote:** I have a 9 year old & 16 year old. My 9 year old is transitioning very well. I struggle with the 16 year old because I can't come up with alternative things for him to do other than be on the computer. I was able to convince them we don't need TV but he has found his favorite shows on the internet to watch. His circadian rythm is really off and I know getting off the computer and turning off the lights would be very good for him. I was an avid reader as a teenager so I can remember that is what I did at night, but he hates reading since it required so much at school. Any ideas on how to occupy a teen at night?

I used to work with teens (though have yet to live with my own, so all advice with a big grain of salt). I'd be engaging your 16 year old in some deep learning about WHY you are doing what you're doing. Listening to podcasts with JK, reading Lights Out and the blog, etc. The other thing is trying to get a teen to live in a counter-cultural way. They want to live counter to mainstream/parental culture, but not to teen culture. And teen culture is all about the computer and technology, so it's hard. In the long run, I don't think it's going to work if you have to occupy him. But you could do family things. We invested in a whole bunch of card and board games over the past year and we play them most every night. Maybe also get him to do an ASI panel... if he sees his altered circadian cycle on paper he may be more motivated and it may be more personal for him. Last thing: as he weans himself off the computer at night, you could get him blue blockers and make sure f.lux is installed. Good luck... not an easy task with a teen!!

### Jennie747 — 2012-09-10T15:07:33+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/lights-out-after-dark-with-a-family.3532/post-50689>

I went to the hardware store and got some amber flame looking lights for the dining room chandelier and a bunch of yellow bug lights. They had the red flame lights on clearance so I got the last four of those, might have to look at another store for more of those as long as they're on clearance (Lowe's). In my bedside lamp the bug light was too bright but the amber flame one too dark for reading so need to figure something out there. I think I'll put an amber one in the frig. I'm going to look for some orange cellophane from the craft store and see about covering lights from clocks, TVs, cable boxes, etc. with that. The stove light is another one I need to deal with. I also have a neighbor with a super bright light on at night, but it comes in the bathroom window which is covered pretty well.

My kids are pretty young (6 and 9) and they go to bed early so they haven't been too difficult about it.

### kjgraffin@yahoo.com — 2012-09-10T15:33:24+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/lights-out-after-dark-with-a-family.3532/post-50694>

I was able to coax my 16 year old into playing UNO with me & my 9 year old last night. I asked him to just play 2 hands and then he could do what he wanted. He ended up playing until bedtime! It was great. He did promptly go upstairs and turn on his light though to get ready for bed. But at least he knows all lights have to be out before I come upstairs to say good night.

He is having an ASI done and I am hoping this will help. I've been talking about circadian rythm with him for months now. We both know that his is backwards, but sometimes seeing it in black and white causes it to hit home a bit harder. He keeps telling people we've gone Amish!

### BJK77 — 2012-09-10T19:44:51+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/lights-out-after-dark-with-a-family.3532/post-50750>

Wow Trisha! Sounds like you are well on your way  We ran into the blinding light from the fridge too . . . such a pain. We were just getting water from the outside and it's still a super bright light. I've just been trying to remember to fill up our water bottles before we go to candlelight. Then there's no excuse to open the fridge or use the water dispenser.

I'm getting into more of a routine with all of this now. I go around and light the candles and DH's propane lantern, fill the water bottles, make sure the kids have brushed their teeth (so much easier before it gets too dark!) and make sure everything is in its place for the next day. After a little over a week it just feels normal now

### omlh@todmi.com — 2012-09-16T18:39:33+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/lights-out-after-dark-with-a-family.3532/post-51936>

For bedtime reading, have you thought about candle sconces on the wall above the bed? We have a double one high above each of our pillows and, being high up, the light they produce is more than sufficient to see by. You can also buy sconces with mirrors behind where the candles go to magnify the amount of light produced.

### enyaw — 2018-09-25T15:41:21+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/lights-out-after-dark-with-a-family.3532/post-251666>

# Thread: Pastured Bacon: So good, but so elusive.
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/pastured-bacon-so-good-but-so-elusive.3537/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 12
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### RespectTheProcess — 2012-09-09T03:22:13+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/pastured-bacon-so-good-but-so-elusive.3537/post-50369>

Hi all,

Many recent blog posts hail bacon to be combined with seafood. Well, no one has to tell me that twice! However, here is my conundrum....

Where do you all buy pastured, completely GRAIN-FREE bacon? Despite hours spent on EatWild and researching local farms, all of them supplement their pastured hogs with some grain (per them, it's not "forced" but it's there and the hogs do supplement with it). I can taste the difference between local farms bacon and the "pastured bacon" at Whole Foods, so I believe nutrient-wise, the local stuff is better. I'm thinking that this could be considered "okay" bacon, but not "optimal."

Also, I have read some papers that hogs (who have a single chamber stomach, unlike the multiples of cows) can't fully digest all the fiber in the greens from the pasture and need some grain to supplement. I need to do more research, but what are other's opinions about this?

I'm co-eccentric to MD, PA, VA, WV, and D.C. so if anyone has a local supplier, please pass them along! I'm willing to buy in bulk and drive a bit to get it.

Thanks!

### nate.reik@yahoo.com — 2012-09-09T04:01:14+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/pastured-bacon-so-good-but-so-elusive.3537/post-50377>

I don't know of ANYONE feeding their hogs grain-free around me.
