### ColdBren — 2012-09-11T23:02:57+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/mercury-yeast-advice-please.3595/post-51060>

My 7 year old daughter is having tics and they are getting very aggressive, my heart is breaking for her. I suspect yeast is part of the problem and she is taking Nystatin (and having horrible die-off symptoms) I just got back her Mercury numbers (from blood) and they are 20 (elevated) I guess 10 or 15 is normal. I feed her packed tuna many times a week. Would seafood be enough to cause these or is it most likely from her vaccinations 3 years ago? SHould I take her off seafood? Anyone have advice on chelating while getting rid of yeast? I am a mess trying to find the right answers. Thank you

### jeanie@truelongmont.com — 2012-09-11T23:52:14+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/mercury-yeast-advice-please.3595/post-51065>

Bren,

I'm so sorry you and your daughter are going through this. If it were me, I would totally cut the tuna - yes we all know seafood is good, but she's eating it now and that doesn't seem to be helping her. I have zero experience in all of this, except a friend who's son has tics (he spins and grabs his crotch). They went paleo for awhile, cutting grains - and it seemed to lessen, but came back with stress.

I would take special care with any chemicals etc (food dye, shampoos etc) and start talking to different professionals and see if you can find one that has treated these with success.

Have you done a GAPS type thing with her yet? Might be something you can do NOW while you look for medical help.

So, why do you think this is mercury?

### nonchalant — 2012-09-11T23:58:14+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/mercury-yeast-advice-please.3595/post-51067>

Most canned tuna has soy in it. Starkist, Chicken of the Sea, etc. Also tuna is relatively high in mercury.

Maybe try sardines or salmon?

To rid the body of mercury, add selenium (brazil nuts) to bind with mercury and cilantro to eliminate the bound mercury.

[http://www.washingtonpost.com/national/health-science/2012/04/03/gIQABd16sS\_graphic.html?tid=sm\_gplus\_wap](http://http://www.washingtonpost.com/national/health-science/2012/04/03/gIQABd16sS_graphic.html?tid=sm_gplus_wap)

### TribalSpice — 2012-09-12T03:37:18+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/mercury-yeast-advice-please.3595/post-51101>

That sounds really hard for both of you.

You can make great pesto with Brazil nuts and cilantro to help remove heavy metals. Also gelatinous foods like aloe Vera and chia seeds are a great help in heavy metal detoxing.

Is she getting enough magnesium? Have you also considered Chiro?

### nosnhojjennifer@yahoo.com — 2012-09-12T03:38:49+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/mercury-yeast-advice-please.3595/post-51104>

Can you feed her smaller fish? Shellfish? Bigger fish have more mercury. Also try to stay away from cans if you can - BPA.

I buy the salmon in a pouch. Dress is up with mustard, avocado, lime juice, olive oil.

Good luck!

### KiwiLauren — 2012-09-12T05:19:56+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/mercury-yeast-advice-please.3595/post-51137>

Bren, I'm dealing with my own Hg issues and +1000 on ditching the canned tuna. (Though I'm guessing it's harder to face one's own toxicity issues than those of your child!!!) Not only is it canned (so full of BPA etc) and a big fish, but it's cooked at such high temps before canned that she's probably getting very little benefit from it. Find something with cilantro she loves (like the pesto TS suggested) and get her to eat heaps. And yes, make sure she's getting selenium from Brazil nuts. Also, lots of seaweed or iodine, too. I'd also be working hard on her gut - you can't get rid of toxins if you re-absorb them. Supplement with B vitamins, too, to help the methylation pathways. (You can also test for how well these detox pathways are working.)

In the meantime, try not to stress and take things one step at a time. Easier said than done, I know. But if you stress a lot about it, she will too and this will worsen things for her health.

Hang in there. XO

### quelsen — 2012-09-12T16:24:43+0300

NAC

MSM

other sulphur agents.

# Thread: Sarcolipin
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sarcolipin.3596/>
**Subforum:** Cold Thermogenesis
**Posts:** 2
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### AKMan — 2012-09-12T01:36:54+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sarcolipin.3596/post-51081>

Don't recall anyone talking about sarcolipin before, but maybe another path to CT.

[http://www.newscientist.com/article/dn22250-muscles-that-do-nothing-can-keep-you-warm-and-thin.html](http://http://www.newscientist.com/article/dn22250-muscles-that-do-nothing-can-keep-you-warm-and-thin.html)

Muscles that burn energy without contracting have yielded new clues about how the body retains a constant temperature â€“ and they may provide new targets for combating obesity.

Traditionally, the body's main thermostat was thought to be brown fat. It raids the body's white fat stores in cold conditions to burn energy and keep the body warm.

Muscles also play a role in keeping the body warm by contracting and triggering the shiver response â€“ but this is only a short-term fix because prolonged shivering damages muscles. Now it seems that muscles have another way to turn up the heat.

"Our findings demonstrate for the first time that muscle, which accounts for 40 per cent of body weight in humans, can generate heat independent of shivering," says Muthu Periasamy of Ohio State University in Columbus.

Surviving the chill

Through experiments on mice that had their usual thermostat â€“ brown fat â€“ surgically removed, Periasamy and his colleagues proved that a protein called sarcolipin helps muscle cells keep the body warm by burning energy, almost like an idling motor car, even if the muscles do not contract.

All of the mice had their brown fat removed, but some of them had been genetically engineered to lack sarcolipin too. These rodents could not survive when held at 4 Â°C, and died of hypothermia within 10 hours. By contrast, mice that could make sarcolipin were able to survive the chilly temperatures and maintained their core body temperature â€“ despite having no brown fat.

Periasamy also showed that an inability to make sarcolipin made mice 33 per cent heavier than normal when fed a high-fat diet. This suggests that idling muscles might also help combat obesity by burning off excess energy. The search is now on for drugs that perform the same role, triggering idling muscles to burn off excess fat.

"The most interesting finding is that mice unable to make sarcolipin are more susceptible to obesity," says Andy Whittle of the University of Cambridge, who is testing spicy dietary treatments to ramp up the fat-burning activity of brown fat. "The research demonstrates that muscle is an important component even in mice, which have comparatively more brown fat than humans. In humans, burning fat in muscle is likely to be even more important for proper energy balance."

### WannaBeOptimal — 2012-09-12T03:40:00+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sarcolipin.3596/post-51106>

Very interesting, yet predictable response to the findings...produce another drug!

Why not just get cold?

# Thread: Looks like someone else is starting to recognize circadian rhythms in Obesity..
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/looks-like-someone-else-is-starting-to-recognize-circadian-rhythms-in-obesity.3600/>
**Subforum:** The Leptin Rx
**Posts:** 4
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### BenG — 2012-09-12T10:14:01+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/looks-like-someone-else-is-starting-to-recognize-circadian-rhythms-in-obesity.3600/post-51146>

[http://www.fasebj.org/content/early/2012/05/16/fj.12-208868](http://http://www.fasebj.org/content/early/2012/05/16/fj.12-208868)

Timed high-fat diet resets circadian metabolism and prevents obesity

Hadas Sherman, Yoni Genzer, Rotem Cohen, Nava Chapnik, Zecharia Madar and Oren Froy2

\+ Author Affiliations

Institute of Biochemistry, Food Science, and Nutrition, Robert H. Smith Faculty of Agriculture, Food, and Environment, The Hebrew University of Jerusalem, Rehovot, Israel

↵2 Correspondence: Institute of Biochemistry, Food Science, and Nutrition, Robert H. Smith Faculty of Agriculture, Food, and Environment, The Hebrew University of Jerusalem, Rehovot 76100, Israel. E-mail: [froy@agri.huji.ac.il](mailto:froy@agri.huji.ac.il)

Abstract

Disruption of circadian rhythms leads to obesity and metabolic disorders. Timed restricted feeding (RF) provides a time cue and resets the circadian clock, leading to better health. In contrast, a high-fat (HF) diet leads to disrupted circadian expression of metabolic factors and obesity. We tested whether long-term (18 wk) clock resetting by RF can attenuate the disruptive effects of diet-induced obesity. Analyses included liver clock gene expression, locomotor activity, blood glucose, metabolic markers, lipids, and hormones around the circadian cycle for a more accurate assessment. Compared with mice fed the HF diet ad libitum, the timed HF diet restored the expression phase of the clock genes Clock and Cry1 and phase-advanced Per1, Per2, Cry2, Bmal1, Rorα, and Rev-erbα. Although timed HF-diet-fed mice consumed the same amount of calories as ad libitum low-fat diet-fed mice, they showed 12% reduced body weight, 21% reduced cholesterol levels, and 1.4-fold increased insulin sensitivity. Compared with the HF diet ad libitum, the timed HF diet led to 18% lower body weight, 30% decreased cholesterol levels, 10% reduced TNF-α levels, and 3.7-fold improved insulin sensitivity. Timed HF-diet-fed mice exhibited a better satiated and less stressed phenotype of 25% lower ghrelin and 53% lower corticosterone levels compared with mice fed the timed low-fat diet. Taken together, our findings suggest that timing can prevent obesity and rectify the harmful effects of a HF diet.â€”Sherman, H., Genzer, Y., Cohen, R., Chapnik, N., Madar, Z., Froy, O. Timed high-fat diet resets circadian metabolism and prevents obesity.

### Destiny — 2012-09-12T13:26:15+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/looks-like-someone-else-is-starting-to-recognize-circadian-rhythms-in-obesity.3600/post-51151>

> **BenG;51725 wrote:** [http://www.fasebj.org/content/early/2012/05/16/fj.12-208868](http://http://www.fasebj.org/content/early/2012/05/16/fj.12-208868)
>
>
>
> Timed high-fat diet resets circadian metabolism and prevents obesity
>
> Hadas Sherman, Yoni Genzer, Rotem Cohen, Nava Chapnik, Zecharia Madar and Oren Froy2
>
> \+ Author Affiliations
>
>
>
> Institute of Biochemistry, Food Science, and Nutrition, Robert H. Smith Faculty of Agriculture, Food, and Environment, The Hebrew University of Jerusalem, Rehovot, Israel
>
> ↵2 Correspondence: Institute of Biochemistry, Food Science, and Nutrition, Robert H. Smith Faculty of Agriculture, Food, and Environment, The Hebrew University of Jerusalem, Rehovot 76100, Israel. E-mail: [froy@agri.huji.ac.il](mailto:froy@agri.huji.ac.il)
>
> Abstract
>
>
>
> Disruption of circadian rhythms leads to obesity and metabolic disorders. Timed restricted feeding (RF) provides a time cue and resets the circadian clock, leading to better health. In contrast, a high-fat (HF) diet leads to disrupted circadian expression of metabolic factors and obesity. We tested whether long-term (18 wk) clock resetting by RF can attenuate the disruptive effects of diet-induced obesity. Analyses included liver clock gene expression, locomotor activity, blood glucose, metabolic markers, lipids, and hormones around the circadian cycle for a more accurate assessment. Compared with mice fed the HF diet ad libitum, the timed HF diet restored the expression phase of the clock genes Clock and Cry1 and phase-advanced Per1, Per2, Cry2, Bmal1, Rorα, and Rev-erbα. Although timed HF-diet-fed mice consumed the same amount of calories as ad libitum low-fat diet-fed mice, they showed 12% reduced body weight, 21% reduced cholesterol levels, and 1.4-fold increased insulin sensitivity. Compared with the HF diet ad libitum, the timed HF diet led to 18% lower body weight, 30% decreased cholesterol levels, 10% reduced TNF-α levels, and 3.7-fold improved insulin sensitivity. Timed HF-diet-fed mice exhibited a better satiated and less stressed phenotype of 25% lower ghrelin and 53% lower corticosterone levels compared with mice fed the timed low-fat diet. Taken together, our findings suggest that timing can prevent obesity and rectify the harmful effects of a HF diet.â€”Sherman, H., Genzer, Y., Cohen, R., Chapnik, N., Madar, Z., Froy, O. Timed high-fat diet resets circadian metabolism and prevents obesity.

Dr. Kruse has better study subjects ... people! It nice to see that it works for mice too !

### vkiernan — 2012-09-12T14:43:33+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fat-malabsorption-and-ox-bile-salts.3602/post-51166>

I recently found out I have fat malabsorption and ox bile salts were mentioned to assist with that. My question is what will I notice when it is working and how will I know if I am taking enough or not. Any info on this or fat mal. is great appreciated. I did a lot of searching the last few nights and there is a lot more I'd like to know. The web ties this to celiac in almost every article I've read which makes sense. I have also read that as I get celiac under control (let my body deflame), my fat mal. will go away.

Thank you all for any input!

### Destiny — 2012-09-12T15:23:24+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fat-malabsorption-and-ox-bile-salts.3602/post-51172>

> **vkiernan;51748 wrote:** I recently found out I have fat malabsorption and ox bile salts were mentioned to assist with that. My question is what will I notice when it is working and how will I know if I am taking enough or not. Any info on this or fat mal. is great appreciated. I did a lot of searching the last few nights and there is a lot more I'd like to know. The web ties this to celiac in almost every article I've read which makes sense. I have also read that as I get celiac under control (let my body deflame), my fat mal. will go away.
>
>
>
> Thank you all for any input!

I started taking ox bile salts when I started Leptin RX simply because of the change to the fatty diet last November. Since I had no gallbladder, I had many fat intolerance symptoms. At one point I thought, I will never tolerate more than a small amount of fat. The ox bile did not work right away even though I was taking it 3 times a day. I carried on and eventually realized that I started tolerating fats much much better. Now, I am down to 1 capsule in the morning and doing great. I have no symptoms of fat intolerance anymore. I can eat any fat!

This is what I have been using:

[http://www.amazon.com/Jarrow-Formulas-Bile-Factors-Capsules/dp/B0013OSHHK/ref=sr\_1\_5?s=hpc&ie=UTF8&qid=1347452487&sr=1-5&keywords=ox+bile](http://http://www.amazon.com/Jarrow-Formulas-Bile-Factors-Capsules/dp/B0013OSHHK/ref=sr_1_5?s=hpc&ie=UTF8&qid=1347452487&sr=1-5&keywords=ox+bile)

Glad to hear it didn't work right away(ykwim) cuz I have not noticed anything yet. I have the same one that you recommended. I am taking a couple with each meal and more if it is fattier.

### ealachan — 2012-09-12T16:37:37+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fat-malabsorption-and-ox-bile-salts.3602/post-51186>

> **vkiernan;51760 wrote:** Thanks Destiny. I think you are saying you would get D. I have the opposite problem from a bad colon (like Jack says, everything in context right) and I still have my gallbladder. Although, most of my family doesn't. This prompts me to work harder at it. So, I am trying to figure out what will happen. TMI, but I think it will no longer float. But that is all I can figure out. Glad to hear it didn't work right away(ykwim) cuz I have not noticed anything yet. I have the same one that you recommended. I am taking a couple with each meal and more if it is fattier.

Major TMI ahead - you have been warned:

Floating poo is a big sign of fat malabsorbtion, I believe. I have no gallbladder and was getting urgent runs right after eating a fat-heavy meal, and a combo of digestive enzymes and ox bile helped a LOT. I used to sometimes get floaters as well (on the rare occassion my poo was actually solid). Now I don't usually get runs, I don't have floaters, and my poo is usually well-formed rather than loose / ragged.

### Patty Cakes — 2012-09-12T18:42:08+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fat-malabsorption-and-ox-bile-salts.3602/post-51213>

Hi V! I got the same results on my Metametrix GI fx test. My doc said to start taking ox bile with meals and use a high poptency one. Well I got a 500 mg one and it was toooooo much and gave me the big D every time I took it. So I went down to the 125mg and adjusted from there. I usually take 4 HCL 15 min before BAB and then w/meal take 250mg ox bile+digestive enzyme+lipase (to help w/fat), lunch (if I eat anything I just take the enzyme and then dinner I take 3 HCL+250 mg ox bile+one enzyme.

I think you should start and play around with the dosages until you find what works. I like the 125mg ox bile because I can adjust on the fly. When I travel I look like a card carrying pill pusher. I have little plastic bags (1/3 snack baggie size) that I use for my 3x day supps and then I have all these digestive aids I have to bring with me. I laugh when my bags go through the xray (I decline xray, of course) and wonder what those guys looking at the contents of the bags are thinking!

### Jude — 2012-09-13T04:14:07+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fat-malabsorption-and-ox-bile-salts.3602/post-51355>

> **Destiny;51922 wrote:** I had to stop beatine hcl after a few days only. It gave me a serous stomach pain like when I had a gastritis. I am fine just on ox bile.

Same happened to me. Have been through the D from too much -(500mgms). The interesting thing that I have found is that I've really been able to reduce the amt to 25mgms daily ox bile in the past year. No gall bladder and absolutely no toleration for fat for 10 yrs-gave me such gut pains 2 hrs after eating and such bad burping etc.

What I've found is that now I can tolerate a BAB with no ox bile supplement. No ill effects,-and everything comes out alright! Presume that must have sufficient bile for breakfast. Only take it if meal looks extra fatty--and hasn't been that often lately- especially since I've been eating epi paleo.--what a change.

### Patty Cakes — 2012-09-13T05:32:34+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fat-malabsorption-and-ox-bile-salts.3602/post-51378>

Forgot to answer this question. Mine was 7.4 and marked high. Which is weird to me since I hadn't eaten gluten in ten months. I must have gotten glutened somewhere. We hardly eat out any more because it's just too hard. Sushi is our go-to place and we both love it. Anyway, I can't remember if you posted your antigliandin score but what was it?

I also had teh cyrex test for cross-reactivity of foods that act like gluten in our system. I have a few things I cannot eat due to that but most of them are grains anyway. Thank goodness coffee and chocolate weren't on my list! Coffee is a treat to me these days though. I stopped it a year ago to help heal my adrenals.

Congrats on being able to have coffee and chocolate. I am trying to convince myself to give them up and I am struggling. That would be my only treat too. As for the gluten, it can show up for a year or two. That is why that test is good and also it shows up a lot sooner in your stool than blood, etc. I was doing a lot of reading and if you read on Enterolabs, they give very good explanations. I am probably going to do their test that consists of the gluten (2nd opinion) but also quite a few foods like dairy so I know exactly what I shouldn't be eating. If I can add back to the menu, that would be a happy day.

Do you think you have celiac? Everything I have read points hashis's to celiac and fat mal. to celiac. Sounds like all 3 are working off each other.

### Patty Cakes — 2012-09-13T06:37:23+0300

I've wondered about the Cleiac myself and have heard many connections but I figured at this point it is mute for me since gluten is definitely a thing of the past. I don't cheat at all when it comes to gluten and I am quite cautious. Even eating sushi we go to this one place 90% of the time and the sushi chef/owner knows we can't have any rice or flour so he makes everything special for us. Lots of sashimi and rolls with no rice.

Are there other things you have read about that we Hashis/Fal Mal/Maybe celiac should be concerned about? Are you going to take any other special precautions if you are diagnosed? I researched celiac a couple years ago and since I didn't have any symptoms I stopped researching. My head has become filled with hormones and other stuff these days!!

### vkiernan — 2012-09-13T14:44:14+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fat-malabsorption-and-ox-bile-salts.3602/post-51404>

> **Patty Cakes;51973 wrote:** I've wondered about the Cleiac myself and have heard many connections but I figured at this point it is mute for me since gluten is definitely a thing of the past. I don't cheat at all when it comes to gluten and I am quite cautious. Even eating sushi we go to this one place 90% of the time and the sushi chef/owner knows we can't have any rice or flour so he makes everything special for us. Lots of sashimi and rolls with no rice.
>
>
>
> Are there other things you have read about that we Hashis/Fal Mal/Maybe celiac should be concerned about? Are you going to take any other special precautions if you are diagnosed? I researched celiac a couple years ago and since I didn't have any symptoms I stopped researching. My head has become filled with hormones and other stuff these days!!

Ok, this is my own bad thinking here and I'm sure someone is going to hammer on me for it. For myself, if I have celiac, I know I am eating poison. Otoh, if I only have a gluten sensitivity, then I don't feel like I am eating poison just something not very good. Perhaps I need to read more on the diff. TMI warning, I DO know without a doubt that I have glut sens. as everyday after breakfast (yes, good ole' whole grains) I would bloat up so damn bad that my pants were ridicuously tight and eventually the gas would come out that was created from it and I would start a new day the next day. This went on for years. I had no clue as this was my life and the only way I knew it. It took me years to realize I was lactose intolerant. If I made a roast, carrots and potatoes for a meal along with a glass of milk, within 15 min. of dinner my stomach went into overdrive and the gas was bad and non-controllable. It took me a long time to make the connection. I also couldn't eat cheap ice cream. Same effect. Eventually I put it all together and quit the milk. I did not have that problem with cheese, custard, etc. Cream was ok but milk no way. I have learned that my poor health history goes way back. All this time docs have been telling me I am sooo healthy when in fact I was slowly killing myself right under their noses. If your blood pressure is fine (actually mine was always low 80/50 but that didn't matter) and you don't have a weight problem, you are HEALTHY!

I'm pretty sure I am kidding myself about the diff. between celiac and glut sens. I have probably even stirred my dh in that direction as he is under the impression that I can eat some gluten if I am only sens. to it. I am going to have to do some serious reading on the diff. I think that is why labeling me celiac carries a lot more weight for me for him to get it. I know he has to be struggling with all of this. I work for him a couple evenings a week and we would usually grab something to eat out. This can't happen anymore and he thinks it only can't happen if I have celiac.

I have a new friend that has been going thru all of this for a long time and she and I basically have the same stuff going on. Diff. is she has no money worries and is actually currently in India for 3 weeks getting some special ayurvedic treatments 2-4 times a day. But, she is very intelligent and insightful and is really helping me take a different stance on all of this. She also owns a gluten free restaurant and her dh is a chef. She, like Jack, is looking at all of this for health and doesn't take the approach like I do as in just a little on occasion please!!! Yes, I always want to be healthy but I want that little cheat every now and then. I'm not talking even monthly; I think when you tell me I cannot do or have something, I fight all the harder. Mind thing for sure. That is an honest response and I know it sucks and my thinking needs to change or I will never get to level of health that I'd like. She even recommended a few docs in the Chicago area and I might just try the one out. Sounds like she has had everything we have and then some and managed to heal herself. Good person to have in my back pocket.

I think we are all very trained to having "treats" and when you say no more "treats" it is sort of penalizing in a way like you've done something wrong. Like a kid who didn't clean their room and can't have a cookie. I have to get over this thinking!

### Patty Cakes — 2012-09-13T18:55:27+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fat-malabsorption-and-ox-bile-salts.3602/post-51452>

You are so lucky to have a friend who is in the same place as you are. And someone who is a researcher and looks for ways to get healthy. I wish I had someone local who is like that. I look to my forum friends for that because most people think I am a freak! But if sounds like you definitely may find a good new doc in Chicago.

I have one close g/friend who has Hashis and another on thyroid meds who I think has Hashis but she has never been tested. One g/friend has had a double mastectomy due to breast cancer, one has had radiation due to breast cancer (twice) and two who "seem" to be healthy but are just now getting into the peri-meno phase of life. Looking at that I see a group of gals who are not healthy. My Hashis friend listens to me but it takes her about 6 months to start something. She is now paleo and completely gluten free. She just recently has been doing this detox thing from her naturopath and she now has her antibodies so low they are within range. I'm not totally sure what that means as far as Hashis goes, but at least she isn't in an active attack all the time. I hope she will heed the light cycles by the first of the year. MHS probably will probably never happen!

Maybe treats could be something else for you. A healthy treat..... I grew up with a health nut mother and that was her philosophy. We could have healthy treats, those things with no sugar. Most kids would get real yummy treats coming home from school. Not me. I got a bowl of raisins and sunflower seeds to eat or a piece of fruit. Change the thinking to make the "good for you" item your treat. Whatever that is for you. I'm not sure if I'm supposed to eat chocolate but I found this fabulous 91% smooth dark with no soy lecithin in it and one square is all I need to feel like I had something treat-like. Can you have chocolate? Maye I'm enabling you by suggesting other treat ideas. Oh boy.....I may not be helping here!!!

### Lyndra — 2012-09-13T20:08:13+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fat-malabsorption-and-ox-bile-salts.3602/post-51474>

I have a friend with celiac disease (and lupus and too many others to count). She is extremely sensitive to gluten in any form. She can't be in a house where someone is baking because the flour in the air affects her. She had to switch to shampoo without gluten and that was very hard for her to find. I wonder if you're being exposed to gluten in ways that you hadn't imagined?

### vkiernan — 2012-09-16T04:26:23+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fat-malabsorption-and-ox-bile-salts.3602/post-51891>

> **Patty Cakes;51796 wrote:** Hi V! I got the same results on my Metametrix GI fx test. My doc said to start taking ox bile with meals and use a high poptency one. Well I got a 500 mg one and it was toooooo much and gave me the big D every time I took it. So I went down to the 125mg and adjusted from there. I usually take 4 HCL 15 min before BAB and then w/meal take 250mg ox bile+digestive enzyme+lipase (to help w/fat), lunch (if I eat anything I just take the enzyme and then dinner I take 3 HCL+250 mg ox bile+one enzyme.
>
>
>
> I think you should start and play around with the dosages until you find what works. I like the 125mg ox bile because I can adjust on the fly. When I travel I look like a card carrying pill pusher. I have little plastic bags (1/3 snack baggie size) that I use for my 3x day supps and then I have all these digestive aids I have to bring with me. I laugh when my bags go through the xray (I decline xray, of course) and wonder what those guys looking at the contents of the bags are thinking!

I have backed way off and am going to stop for a day or two. I have D every am pretty bad this past whole week since taking it. I think I need something way less strong like you were talking. It even hit me this afternoon. I don't know where it is all coming from actually. Guess I will have to go shopping for something different. Perhaps I don't have fat m too bad for the ox bile to be having this effect on me. I just ordered my mag citrate and do not need that anymore!

Patty, How long have you been on the t3 only protocol? After speaking to the compounding pharmacist, I need to e-mail my doc about titrating the dose up. I looked for the book tonight in the book store but they didn't have it. I was hoping to read some stuff that I could pass off to her about it. She is giving me about 40 mcg and I don't think that will go very far as I was up to 60 before my old doc made me stop. Do you have any info off the top of your head that I could pass off to her? You are feeling better on it right? I feel I can ask you since we both have the same things going on. Thanks!

# Thread: Making Fish Head Soup
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/making-fish-head-soup.3604/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 4
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### indigogirl — 2012-09-12T15:59:13+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/making-fish-head-soup.3604/post-51179>

I've been cooking fish head soup in my crockpot on low for 18 hours and some of the bones, skin, and eyes haven't totally dissolved. Will they ever? How long should I cook for them to dissolve?

This is a leap for me. I do not like fishy tasting fish : (

I added clam juice, carrots, celery, onion, kombu, fresh parsley, thyme,3 red snapper heads and water to cover. Anything I could have added to make it taste less fishy? I hope to cultivate a taste for fishy tasting fish...

### Michael — 2012-09-12T16:51:44+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/making-fish-head-soup.3604/post-51191>

The eyes you should probably eat raw.

The bones and skin are unlikely to dissolve as far as I know, although maybe they'd get soft enough to chew. In practice you probably wouldn't cook them for long enough.

Fish broth is traditionally cooked for far less time than meat broth. I think something like a couple of hours is usual. I don't know the reasons - probably based on flavour. Traditional practice is probably a good guide unless one's sound information to indicate not.

I've made meat stock a few times but rarely fish. With the former you include a tablespoon or so of cider vinegar to help pull the minerals from the bone. Probably the same deal with the fish.

Sally Fallon will have directions in "Nourishing Traditions". There she quotes a South Anerican proverb to the effect that good fish broth can even "raise the dead"! But someone is bound to have put the instructions online, so try googling something like:

"sally fallon fish stock"

Or try a more general search - but she is pretty reliable. Or have a look at some of the WAPF or paleo sites. I think there's one called "The Nourishing Gourmet".

### indigogirl — 2012-09-12T22:33:17+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/making-fish-head-soup.3604/post-51248>

Thanks, Michael.

I probably cooked everything good out of it cooking it as long as I did!

### Michael — 2012-09-13T08:24:38+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/making-fish-head-soup.3604/post-51392>

> **indigogirl;51831 wrote:** Thanks, Michael.
>
> I probably cooked everything good out of it cooking it as long as I did!

I expect so. The WAPF is saying 4 to 24 hours. I'd understood chefs generally cooked fish stock for quite a bit less than meat stock, but maybe I was mistaken.

[http://www.westonaprice.org/food-features/broth-is-beautiful](http://http://www.westonaprice.org/food-features/broth-is-beautiful)

# Thread: A convenient way to get your CT on and get lots of other stuff done too!
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/a-convenient-way-to-get-your-ct-on-and-get-lots-of-other-stuff-done-too.3607/>
**Subforum:** Cold Thermogenesis
**Posts:** 7
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### trishalinn@gmail.com — 2012-09-12T17:18:47+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/a-convenient-way-to-get-your-ct-on-and-get-lots-of-other-stuff-done-too.3607/post-51201>

Hey guys, I just wanted to share something I was introduced to recently- Cool Fat Burner. My husband and I have started experimenting with it and are going to document our experience on our blog. The thing I like best about it is that I don't have to figure out creative ways to keep ice packs in place. I'm very active throughout the day with 4 boys, so it can be tricky. I've even found I can nurse my twinsies while I am CT'ing with this. I'm sure this can't take the place of complete submersion, but I think it's a fantastic option for those who have been trying to do spot CT throughout the day.

Here is the blog post: [http://intoxicatedonlife.com/2012/09/12/gettin-our-ct-on-with-the-cool-fat-burner/](http://http://intoxicatedonlife.com/2012/09/12/gettin-our-ct-on-with-the-cool-fat-burner/)

Hope this helps someone out

**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/a-convenient-way-to-get-your-ct-on-and-get-lots-of-other-stuff-done-too.3607/post-51294>

# Thread: Question about Free vs Total Testosteron in Ladies
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/question-about-free-vs-total-testosteron-in-ladies.3609/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 2
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### jeanie@truelongmont.com — 2012-09-12T18:25:44+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/question-about-free-vs-total-testosteron-in-ladies.3609/post-51210>

Ok, so got some hormones back a few weeks ago. . .

Testosterone was 152, with a range of 7-46. So 3X the high of range.

Free Testosterone was 1.4, with a range of .2-2, so in range.

I stopped all DHEA supps, thinking that was effecting testosterone. . . and we re ran it 3 weeks later.

Docs office just called and said my testosterone didn't go down, it DOUBLED! Panic Panic Panic. Said other hormones were in range (for what that means)

I'm going in to talk to him in a few hrs. . .can anybody point me to the relevant blog posts or information on what I need to know about Free vs. Total in ladies - or anything else that might be relevant I need to brush up on?

I have none of the other PCOS signs, in fact have ruled that out based on other stuff. I will hopefully get the numbers on the rest of my hormone panel when I'm there today.

The other major symptom I'm having is hair loss - it's falling out. Could have been the 25 lbd weight loss, but this testosterone thing is a bugger.

### Shijin13 — 2012-09-12T19:36:02+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/question-about-free-vs-total-testosteron-in-ladies.3609/post-51218>

> **Cavemam;51793 wrote:** Ok, so got some hormones back a few weeks ago. . .
>
>
>
> Testosterone was 152, with a range of 7-46. So 3X the high of range.
>
> Free Testosterone was 1.4, with a range of .2-2, so in range.
>
>
>
> I stopped all DHEA supps, thinking that was effecting testosterone. . . and we re ran it 3 weeks later.
>
>
>
> Docs office just called and said my testosterone didn't go down, it DOUBLED! Panic Panic Panic. Said other hormones were in range (for what that means)
>
>
>
> I'm going in to talk to him in a few hrs. . .can anybody point me to the relevant blog posts or information on what I need to know about Free vs. Total in ladies - or anything else that might be relevant I need to brush up on?
>
>
>
> I have none of the other PCOS signs, in fact have ruled that out based on other stuff. I will hopefully get the numbers on the rest of my hormone panel when I'm there today.
>
>
>
> The other major symptom I'm having is hair loss - it's falling out. Could have been the 25 lbd weight loss, but this testosterone thing is a bugger.

FWIW you don't have to have all the PCOS symptoms to be dx. my experience was that in general my concerns about my issues were pushed to the back of the pile... until I paid for a full hormone lab and included my both free and total T. my VA PCP looked at it last September and said WHOA - why haven't you been Dx'd w/ PCOS before this? especially after we discussed my symptoms. Some PCOS symptoms can be confused w/thyroid. You can have PCOS and not be infertile btw.

not saying this is you... don't forget CT can also raise T.

They weren't completely numb, just starting to feel that way. Same with the fingertips. ### KiwiLauren — 2012-09-14T03:23:00+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/do-i-have-diabetes-type-i-ii-other.3613/post-51571>

Do a quick experiment, maybe. Stop taking Cycloset for a week and see what happens. (Keep monitoring blood glucose and the feeling in your fingers and feet.) I think that could be a quick first step maybe.

### mambo — 2012-09-15T09:01:01+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/do-i-have-diabetes-type-i-ii-other.3613/post-51774>

Those diabetic #s look good, especially fasting insulin. I don't think it's diabetic neuropathy. Did your neuro consider whether you may have autoimmunity? Lupus, Sjogren's, RA could all cause neuropathy. I would get ANA tested and see if you're positive for any autoimmune diseases.

> **bigknitwit;51804 wrote:** A1c 5.1
>
> fasting Insulin < 2
>
>
>
> Glucose Tolerance Test
>
> Fasting 73
>
> 1Hour 134
>
> 2Hour 80

### maggie — 2012-09-15T23:55:57+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/do-i-have-diabetes-type-i-ii-other.3613/post-51872>

Your A1c looks great. There seems to be a wide range of opinion about what's acceptable for post prandial readings. I'd get upset about a post prandial number of 129 and would take a serious look at what I ate for lunch. I think the neuropathies start at much lower readings than generally thought.

### bigknitwit — 2012-09-16T13:53:12+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/do-i-have-diabetes-type-i-ii-other.3613/post-51910>

I didn't like that post-pranial either. I had my regular lunch - fish sauteed in tallow, scallops, sauteed cabbage and carrots. Maybe I ate too many carrots. Or maybe my meter was giving inaccurate results.

Hmm, are you sure you meant Estrogen, not progesterone? Usually, what I have seen in the past is herbs to help with estrogen but not the real thing. online I have seen Estriol....very low dose. but not Estradiol.

I would opt for compounded over the drug companies brand name,then you could specify a dose for your own needs.

# Thread: Study Shows Light From Electronic Devices Suppresses Melatonin
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/study-shows-light-from-electronic-devices-suppresses-melatonin.3629/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 1
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### indigogirl — 2012-09-13T04:36:47+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/study-shows-light-from-electronic-devices-suppresses-melatonin.3629/post-51371>

ScienceDaily (Aug. 27, 2012) â€” A new study from the Lighting Research Center (LRC) at Rensselaer Polytechnic Institute shows that a two-hour exposure to electronic devices with self-luminous "backlit" displays causes melatonin suppression, which might lead to delayed bedtimes, especially in teens.

Share This:

147

See Also:

Health & Medicine

Insomnia Research

Sleep Disorder Research

Mind & Brain

Insomnia

Sleep Disorders

Computers & Math

Spintronics Research

Computer Modeling

Living Well

Reference

Circadian rhythm sleep disorder

Circadian rhythm

Jet lag

Delayed sleep phase syndrome

The research team, led by Mariana Figueiro, associate professor at Rensselaer and director of the LRC's Light and Health Program, tested the effects of self-luminous tablets on melatonin suppression. In order to simulate typical usage of these devices, 13 individuals used self-luminous tablets to read, play games, and watch movies.

"Our study shows that a two-hour exposure to light from self-luminous electronic displays can suppress melatonin by about 22 percent. Stimulating the human circadian system to this level may affect sleep in those using the devices prior to bedtime," said Figueiro.

The actual melatonin suppression values after 60 minutes were very similar to those estimated using a predictive model of human circadian phototransduction for one-hour light exposures. "Based on these results, display manufacturers can use our model to determine how their products could affect circadian system regulation," said Figueiro.

The results of this study, together with the LRC predictive model of human circadian phototransduction, could urge manufacturers to design more "circadian-friendly" electronic devices that could either increase or decrease circadian stimulation depending on the time of day -- reducing circadian stimulation in the evening for a better night's sleep, and increasing in the morning to encourage alertness. In the future, manufacturers might be able to use data and predictive models to design tablets for tailored daytime light exposures that minimize symptoms of seasonal affective disorder, and sleep disorders in seniors. Individuals would be able to receive light treatments while playing games or watching movies, making light therapy much more enjoyable than just sitting in front of a light box.

Along with Figueiro, co-authors of the study are LRC Director and Professor Mark S. Rea, LRC Research Specialist Brittany Wood, and LRC Research Nurse Barbara Plitnick.

Melatonin is a hormone produced by the pineal gland at night and under conditions of darkness in both diurnal and nocturnal species. It is a "timing messenger," signaling nighttime information throughout the body. Exposure to light at night, especially short-wavelength light, can slow or even cease nocturnal melatonin production. Suppression of melatonin by light at night resulting in circadian disruption has been implicated in sleep disturbances, increased risk for diabetes and obesity, as well as increased risk for more serious diseases, such as breast cancer, if circadian disruption occurs for many consecutive years, such as in nightshift workers.

"Technology developments have led to bigger and brighter televisions, computer screens, and cell phones," said Wood, who used the study as the basis for her master's thesis. "To produce white light, these electronic devices must emit light at short wavelengths, which makes them potential sources for suppressing or delaying the onset of melatonin in the evening, reducing sleep duration and disrupting sleep. This is particularly worrisome in populations such as young adults and adolescents, who already tend to be night owls."

In the study, the participants were divided into three groups. The first group viewed their tablets through a pair of clear goggles fitted with 470-nm (blue) light from light emitting diodes (LEDs). This was a "true positive" condition because the blue light is known to be a strong stimulus for suppressing melatonin. The second group viewed their tablets through orange-tinted glasses, capable of filtering out the short-wavelength radiation that can suppress melatonin; this was the "dark control" condition. The third group did not wear glasses or goggles. Each tablet was set to full brightness.

In order to accurately record personal light exposures during the experiment, each subject wore a Dimesimeter close to the eye. The Dimesimeter is a small calibrated light meter device developed by the LRC that continuously records circadian light and activity levels."

The research team established that duration of exposure and the distance between the eye and the display, which determines the amount of light reaching the back of the eye, affects melatonin levels. Melatonin suppression after a one-hour exposure to the tablet was not significantly affected. However, after a two-hour exposure there was significant suppression.

The type of task being performed on the tablets also determines how much light is delivered to the cornea and, therefore, the impact on evening melatonin levels. As shown by the team's Dimesimeter measurements, the range of photopic illuminance levels at the cornea from the tablets alone varied from 5 lux, which is not likely to affect melatonin, to over 50 lux, which would result in measurable melatonin suppression after a two-hour exposure. Therefore, before any generalizations can be made, it is important to measure how much light one is receiving from these self-luminous devices.

Until manufacturers develop more "circadian-friendly" electronic devices that increase or decrease light exposure based on time of day, Figueiro has several recommendations to reduce their effects on sleep. "We recommended dimming these devices at night as much as possible in order to minimize melatonin suppression, and limiting the amount of time spent using these devices prior to bedtime."

You are definitely looking hypo to me too! Your FT3 is going down and it was too low six months ago. That reading, your bio-available hormone, should be near the top of the range. The FT4 should be mid to top of range. I would guess you are tired throughout the day especially around 3pm-ish. Have you checked your adrenals lately? Oftentimes when FT3 is low the body looks to the adrenals for energy. This is OK for a short time but after a while the adrenals get too taxed. The big question is why is your FT3 so low?

Also ditto to what Shijin said about the hormone Pg/E2 ratio. Based on the range you do look like you are kinda high though, but I am not a hormone expert.

# Thread: Can dark Chocolate be eaten? Also about Bone Broth
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/can-dark-chocolate-be-eaten-also-about-bone-broth.3649/>
**Subforum:** Beginners Area
**Posts:** 23
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### x_danny_x — 2012-09-14T04:01:31+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/can-dark-chocolate-be-eaten-also-about-bone-broth.3649/post-51591>

I heard some dark chocolate can be eaten and not full of oxidation material that can contribute to a leaky gut. Some of these chocolates are eaten during the winter too since it is very low on carbs???

Also where can you buy Bone Broth to help heal a leaky gut? I am not too sure if I have one but would like to no more about it.

### Michael — 2012-09-14T15:51:30+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/can-dark-chocolate-be-eaten-also-about-bone-broth.3649/post-51632>

Dark chocolate should be OK - for most people at any rate. The higher the cocoa content the better. There has been at least one thread on chocolate on the board before, which you can probably locate with the search box in the top right.

Bone broth you would probably have to make. Most commercial stock/broth/consomme will have undesirable additives like MSG. But there are some smaller producers around who do sell real additive-free stock made from scratch. If you say where you are, it's possible someone may be able to point to something that's available where you are.

### Patty Cakes — 2012-09-14T17:26:56+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/can-dark-chocolate-be-eaten-also-about-bone-broth.3649/post-51642>

I haven't bought this myself (I make my own) but you can try US Wellness for broth. [http://www.grasslandbeef.com/Categories.bok](http://http://www.grasslandbeef.com/Categories.bok)

### jeanie@truelongmont.com — 2012-09-14T20:06:48+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/can-dark-chocolate-be-eaten-also-about-bone-broth.3649/post-51662>

Michael,

I'm a gal who likes easy, and I have a healthy budget - but buying bone broth elsewhere just doesn't cut it. Crockpot + bones does the trick.

I've tried everything as far as what other veggies to add to it to make it stocky etc. . .and I find I do LIKE the flavors, it's so subtle and the broth seems to have the same effects no matter how I use it. . .just adding salt and bones.

Turn crockpot on on low, toss in bones, let it cook for 2-3 days. Strain and freeze. You can go lots of places from here - as far as what to add etc, but JUST the bones gets you there like 99%.

I make beef bones and chicken bones and feet. I like the flavors of some meat left on the bones. Longer you let it cook, the more minerals you get from the bones. Back in the day, they used to just leave a pot on a slow simmer all through the winter and toss any bones in as they got them. . .and just pulled out the broth as needed.

Dark Chocolate is great all around. I got used to the 99% and I have zero BG issue from it.

### x_danny_x — 2012-09-14T20:26:59+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/can-dark-chocolate-be-eaten-also-about-bone-broth.3649/post-51666>

I am in Middletown, NY...about 1 hour upstate from New York City. Good thing to know that dark chocolate is okay.

But damn, I have no clue on how to create a good bone broth. Crap, im out of luck on that one.

### jeanie@truelongmont.com — 2012-09-14T21:13:18+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/can-dark-chocolate-be-eaten-also-about-bone-broth.3649/post-51670>

> **x\_danny\_x;52264 wrote:** I am in Middletown, NY...about 1 hour upstate from New York City. Good thing to know that dark chocolate is okay.
>
>
>
> But damn, I have no clue on how to create a good bone broth. Crap, im out of luck on that one.

Plug in crockpot, fill with water, throw in bones. Wait 72 hrs. Cool. Voila, bone broth.

### x_danny_x — 2012-09-14T23:33:45+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/can-dark-chocolate-be-eaten-also-about-bone-broth.3649/post-51693>

that is all, but damn 72 hours, lol.

**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/can-dark-chocolate-be-eaten-also-about-bone-broth.3649/post-51894>

**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/can-dark-chocolate-be-eaten-also-about-bone-broth.3649/post-58686>

> **Cavemam;52268 wrote:** Plug in crockpot, fill with water, throw in bones. Wait 72 hrs. Cool. Voila, bone broth.

Hi Cavemam, I don't have a crockpot as we are off the grid and run our house on a generator for a few hours in the evening only. (working on turning it off earlier after finding out about circadian rythyms!)  So I have a woodfired cooker - I am guessing that I could make the bone broth and just let it cook and then cool as I have the cooker fired up and cooled down?

### Patty Cakes — 2012-10-17T19:38:20+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/can-dark-chocolate-be-eaten-also-about-bone-broth.3649/post-58732>

I think that would work fine Amber! Do it and let us know. I've made my broth on the stove top and I know it can be made on a wood burning stove. Mom did that.

### KiwiLauren — 2012-10-18T00:32:11+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/can-dark-chocolate-be-eaten-also-about-bone-broth.3649/post-58828>

> **Amber Tyrrell;60222 wrote:** Hi Cavemam, I don't have a crockpot as we are off the grid and run our house on a generator for a few hours in the evening only. (working on turning it off earlier after finding out about circadian rythyms!)  So I have a woodfired cooker - I am guessing that I could make the bone broth and just let it cook and then cool as I have the cooker fired up and cooled down?

Amber, I think the main issue would be temperature control (and that it needs to cook for a minimum of 12 hours). You don't want it to get too hot. What about your mum? Is she off the grid? You could set up a crock pot at her place and come back 2 days later to collect it!

### amber_tyrrell@hotmail.com — 2012-10-18T12:12:42+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/can-dark-chocolate-be-eaten-also-about-bone-broth.3649/post-58892>

> **Patty Cakes;60268 wrote:** I think that would work fine Amber! Do it and let us know. I've made my broth on the stove top and I know it can be made on a wood burning stove. Mom did that.

Thanks I'll give it a go and let you know how it turns out.

### amber_tyrrell@hotmail.com — 2012-10-18T12:14:03+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/can-dark-chocolate-be-eaten-also-about-bone-broth.3649/post-58893>

> **KiwiLauren;60365 wrote:** Amber, I think the main issue would be temperature control (and that it needs to cook for a minimum of 12 hours). You don't want it to get too hot. What about your mum? Is she off the grid? You could set up a crock pot at her place and come back 2 days later to collect it!

Yes Mum is on the grid and she has a crockpot so I'll set up one there and I'm going to do one on my cooker as well to see how it turns out. How often should you drink it? And do you add veges to the broth or is it just bones?

### Kiera2 — 2012-10-18T13:58:40+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/can-dark-chocolate-be-eaten-also-about-bone-broth.3649/post-58895>

Amber, you could set your pot on a trivet once it has come to the boil. That should keep it at a simmer. But I guess the problem is that you don't have the fire going all day? How long would the pot be cold, before you start the fire again? Maybe a haybox would keep your pot warm enough in the interim?

### KiwiLauren — 2012-10-19T00:43:42+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/can-dark-chocolate-be-eaten-also-about-bone-broth.3649/post-59056>

> **Amber Tyrrell;60432 wrote:** Yes Mum is on the grid and she has a crockpot so I'll set up one there and I'm going to do one on my cooker as well to see how it turns out. How often should you drink it? And do you add veges to the broth or is it just bones?

I don't know what others do, but here is what I do. I put a bunch of bones (usually cow shin bones) in the crock pot with a bit of apple cider vinegar and a handful of seaweed and fill it with water. I put it on low and leave it for 48 hours. Then I strain and put it in the fridge. When it cools, the fat will be on the surface (I remove it) and the broth will be gelatin-like. Then I use it as a base for soups and sauces. So, if you're making a veggie soup, add this instead of stock. I eat it daily with my breakfast. I make a crock pot per week roughly.

### edwardfries@netcommander.com — 2012-10-24T23:45:40+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/can-dark-chocolate-be-eaten-also-about-bone-broth.3649/post-60038>

Ahh kiwilauren you are the first person to mention using an acid to leech the minerals out of the bones better, I was wondering if anyone was using an acid like Apple Cider Vinegar. Also do any of you use reverse osmosis distilled water to pull even more minerals off the bones and do you do any second cookings with the bones?

And I would be curious as to why you throw out the fat? Is it because you cook it on low for more than 24 to 36 hours?

### KiwiLauren — 2012-10-25T23:16:20+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/can-dark-chocolate-be-eaten-also-about-bone-broth.3649/post-60191>

> **MrFreeze;61632 wrote:** Ahh kiwilauren you are the first person to mention using an acid to leech the minerals out of the bones better, I was wondering if anyone was using an acid like Apple Cider Vinegar. Also do any of you use reverse osmosis distilled water to pull even more minerals off the bones and do you do any second cookings with the bones?
>
> And I would be curious as to why you throw out the fat? Is it because you cook it on low for more than 24 to 36 hours?

I use the water from my water filter (a ceramic & charcoal one). I always use a bit of ACV. I don't do second cookings, but that's because I haven't a clue about how to do that! When I'm done with mine (usually 48 hours) the bones will sometimes be close to crumbling and can be snapped with your hands. Can you use them again?

I don't always throw away the fat. I sometimes use it for cooking. I just don't keep it with the broth. I find it gives it a stronger flavour that I don't really like.

### edwardfries@netcommander.com — 2012-10-30T18:09:38+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/can-dark-chocolate-be-eaten-also-about-bone-broth.3649/post-60952>

I use the bones twice. Once you've strained off the broth, I add the same mixture of water and some apple cider vinegar and cook it another 24 hours to 36. By this time the bones mush when I press in on them

### x_danny_x — 2012-11-02T19:26:26+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/can-dark-chocolate-be-eaten-also-about-bone-broth.3649/post-61578>

**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/can-dark-chocolate-be-eaten-also-about-bone-broth.3649/post-61619>

Hey danny - I wouldn't worry about that. I just got my hydro bill, and between keeping my place colder and turning off the lights at night, my hydro usage is almost half of the same time last year! And that's using my crockpot a few times a week lol. I don't think it takes that much energy.

**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/can-dark-chocolate-be-eaten-also-about-bone-broth.3649/post-61718>

**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/somewhat-off-topic-ping-on-philosophy-of-children-and-stories.3651/>

# Thread: iPhone 5
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/iphone-5.3654/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 2
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Michael — 2012-09-14T15:59:37+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/iphone-5.3654/post-51633>

Someone just told me this is 4G. can anyone confirm that?

IIRC, 4G is what the Isreali Government has said they won't allow in their country after their science experts assessed the dangers.

If 4G is very much more powerful than 3G, the thought of all those new handsets against people's heads or nestling in trouser pockets against their owner's gonads is somewhat concerning.

In broad terms I'm inclined to see this as something else that illustrates that we are able to manipulate our environment to a very great extent, but don't yet understand, or even much think about, what the effects of doing so are upon ourselves.

### steve@wity.net — 2012-09-14T21:11:39+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/iphone-5.3654/post-51669>

Well the one coming to the UK is. I've edited this from the BBC website.

"The iPhone 5 is the first of Apple's handsets to support 4G, but it has been optimised for just three spectrum bands - 1800MHz, 850MHz and2.1GHz"

# Thread: ok... please hack my smoothie!
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ok-please-hack-my-smoothie.3656/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 10
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### granitereno@gmail.com — 2012-09-14T16:43:25+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ok-please-hack-my-smoothie.3656/post-51638>

Finding myself without the Inger classic formula ingredients (mackerel head and lemon) I have done the following for the last couple of days:

\*2 whole frozen portuguese sardines (6oz)

\*4-5 stalks of frozen asparagus

\*2-3 pickles OR c 1tbsp capers

\*a splash of apple cider vinegar or pickle "juice"

\*a splash of olive oil

\*1/2 cup or so of water

This all goes in the ninja until the chunks abate.

Then, I get to choke this down.

I follow this with a chaser of a 1+ cups of cold shrimp and wakame salad and then floss the scales and whatnot out of my teeth

verdict: I've kept it down... but i didn't want to.

Here is my dilemma:

I REALLY enjoy seafood, but this I approach with the gusto of eating pickled puppy face or the like.

I can't see this being sustainable for me.

Is there something that I get here that I won't in another way?

I am trying to determine my ideal juice vs. squeeze ratio.

Thoughts?

### Inger — 2012-09-14T17:09:36+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ok-please-hack-my-smoothie.3656/post-51641>

I would not use frozen sardines.. because they are so fatty and they get rancid fast. Even if frozen. IF you get any other fish, buy fresh. Fresh is best for sure, you can use any fish heads. If you do want to use the frozen sardines, check that they are as fresh as possible, and I would not puree the whole fish, why? Purreed fish is yucky.. lol. Why use the delish parts too in the smoothie? Too much yucky stuff to drink..lol.

I would puree only the part that is hard to eat or I do not like otherways. The meat / filet I always turn into something very yummy raw, like tartar. I wrap the tartar in nori sheets and it is so very yummy. For spices I use organic white wine vingegar (or lime), wild raw honey, fresh organic ginger and seasalt and chopped onion. I only puree the heads and liver and roe.

I do not care how my smoothie tastes like, I never add anything. It wan't get yummy anyways I guess... so who cares. I always drink the smoothie first and then I get to enjoy the rest of the fish as a delish dish. Works for me very well!

### granitereno@gmail.com — 2012-09-14T18:03:20+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ok-please-hack-my-smoothie.3656/post-51645>

This makes a lot of sense.

Thank you, Inger!

### KiwiLauren — 2012-09-14T21:40:46+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ok-please-hack-my-smoothie.3656/post-51674>

Re: the smoothie, I too would recommend using just the heads (though I looked up fish anatomy and figured out where the liver was so I could cut just below there - it's sort of the top 1/3 of the fish). And I would add as little extras as possible. I just squeeze in 2 lemons and enough water to make it into slush. The more extras you add (esp stuff like pickles and asparagus) the bigger that smoothie gets. Mine fills just a small/medium size glass. By the way, I also use sardines (frozen, caught fresh) all the time and they don't cause me any problem. Good luck!

### caroline — 2012-09-15T00:19:20+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ok-please-hack-my-smoothie.3656/post-51704>

> **KiwiLauren;52272 wrote:** Re: the smoothie, I too would recommend using just the heads (though I looked up fish anatomy and figured out where the liver was so I could cut just below there - it's sort of the top 1/3 of the fish). And I would add as little extras as possible. I just squeeze in 2 lemons and enough water to make it into slush. The more extras you add (esp stuff like pickles and asparagus) the bigger that smoothie gets. Mine fills just a small/medium size glass. By the way, I also use sardines (frozen, caught fresh) all the time and they don't cause me any problem. Good luck!

I bought frozen "small yellow croaker" at my Asian store. Do you think they would be okay? I thought I would just use 1 of them per smoothie. Anyway - I thought - even if we bought fresh - we should freeze them first. waddayathink???

### KiwiLauren — 2012-09-15T02:13:45+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ok-please-hack-my-smoothie.3656/post-51732>

> **caroline;52303 wrote:** I bought frozen "small yellow croaker" at my Asian store. Do you think they would be okay? I thought I would just use 1 of them per smoothie. Anyway - I thought - even if we bought fresh - we should freeze them first. waddayathink???

I do always use frozen when doing the smoothies. I'd guess what you bought would be fine, but I'm not a a fish expert. I thaw mine on the counter for a few minutes (otherwise my blender has fits). You can start with just the heads to make it easier. Good luck!! And don't forget your lemons!

### SeaHorse — 2012-09-15T14:23:56+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ok-please-hack-my-smoothie.3656/post-51782>

I had my first smoothie this morning!! I did use 3 whole fresh sardines from portugal which where pretty big. I put four lemons and a bunch of cilantro, celtic salt, ice and a bit of water. It was actually very tasty...it just looks so awful you have to keep your eyes away.

I can see how you might want to concentrate on heads and save the flesh for other more elegant eating, but this way I figured it wouldn't put me off if my first smoothie was tasty.

I was going to try it with a giant cod head I have from a 7 pound fish...one look at the bones and I knew my cuisinart wouldn't hold up, so it's on the stove turning into soup.

We've been trying to catch trout in our pond for the last few weeks, but no luck. They just aren't biting at the flies, lures, worms ....whatever we are using. There are a lot of late bug hatchings going on, so we'll keep trying. Fresh heads would be great and my husband can have the bodies...(that sounds kind of strange doesn't it?)

### Zorica Vuletic — 2012-09-15T19:16:25+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ok-please-hack-my-smoothie.3656/post-51825>

When I can get a Vitamix...I will try a fish head smoothie. Seems like a quick and easy on the go 'meal replacement' which is not some gross 'protein powder'. LOL

I am satisfied with soups though in the meantime...hehe.

### Zorica Vuletic — 2012-09-15T19:25:44+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ok-please-hack-my-smoothie.3656/post-51826>

Btw, I don't use protein powders at all. I do some integrated exercise, as in I swim in a lake, walk, and bike. I don't do any other 'intended' exercise.

I eat so much seafood (like everyone here). People always ask if I 'work out'. I say no. They don't believe me. It's true, when the body is able to repair itself during the night (sleep) then it can maintain a decent musculature on it's own. I am not wasting away or anything.

Also, I feel like I don't need to eat that much. I get satisfied from eggs, fish, avocadoes, bacon, coconut oil, seaweeds, etc. (I like sweet potatoes, sqaush, pumpkin during this season). I suppose if I wanted to train Olympic level I'd have to eat more but I am not an athlete so I just eat what I feel like.

I think I am 'chemically-reduced' (as Jack refers to).

### Zorica Vuletic — 2012-09-15T20:31:43+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ok-please-hack-my-smoothie.3656/post-51834>

Does my signature show?

# Thread: I have 2 mackerels . . . what to do?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/i-have-2-mackerels-what-to-do.3669/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 7
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### BJK77 — 2012-09-15T01:53:30+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/i-have-2-mackerels-what-to-do.3669/post-51724>

My local farmer's market has just added a seafood stand and this is the first time I've seen whole mackerel, so I felt compelled to buy some . . . now I'm wondering what the heck I was thinking! On the drive home I figured I'd make some fish broth, but I just looked in my Nourishing Traditions book and see they don't recommend using a fatty fish for the broth.

These were fresh and never frozen. I know I could probably go ahead and do the MHS, but I don't quite think I'm there yet. Plus, if and when I do that I think I'll want to have the fish frozen first for the whole parasite issue.

So, can I make broth with this? Any other ideas? I suppose I could just grill it whole, but again I don't know if I'm at the point where I can actually eat the eyes and 'stuff' yet!

### Souldanzer — 2012-09-15T02:53:14+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/i-have-2-mackerels-what-to-do.3669/post-51742>

Cut off the fillets and freeze the heads for when you're ready

### Shijin13 — 2012-09-15T02:59:11+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/i-have-2-mackerels-what-to-do.3669/post-51745>

> **Souldanzer;52341 wrote:** Cut off the fillets and freeze the heads for when you're ready

This. Then take the rest of the fish butterfly it (see Julia child's cook book or joy of cooking for how to)

Then prepare your grill. Get some smoker chips soak them in water then place them on your coals (or tinfoil if you have gas grill) put fish over top of smoker chips Season w/salt pepper avocado oil. Nom nom nom

### Inger — 2012-09-15T08:07:58+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/i-have-2-mackerels-what-to-do.3669/post-51772>

Just do it.

If you want to get healthy, _what are you waiting for...!!!?_

Don't wish, just do.

This is my motto.

You can do it. Everyone can. \*\*\*\*hugs\*\*\*\*

(parasite-issue.. when you open the mackerel you will soon see if there are worms, they are small rolled worms, anisakia-worms. If nothing, they have no worms. I have been eating unfrozen mackerelk with these worms without issue, I just pick the worms I can see away. These worms cannot live longer hat 3 weeks in human anyways. They can give some digestive issues though, according to my research. Soem are allergic to them to and they suffer more. I had no issues. But read it up yourself that is the best. We all have to know our risks and do he research.)

### JoeBranca — 2012-09-15T09:01:04+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/i-have-2-mackerels-what-to-do.3669/post-51775>

after a surfing attempt this evening, i had fish. including a raw, small cut up japanese mackerel, sashimi style from a local japanese market. they included the head, bones, and tail, which are usually set aside for deep frying. i decided to consume a whole raw fish as is, those extra parts just took longer to chew. the head was tasty, actually. who would have thought

### SeaHorse — 2012-09-15T14:26:40+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/i-have-2-mackerels-what-to-do.3669/post-51783>

Was that your first? Way to go!

### JoeBranca — 2012-09-16T08:28:27+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/i-have-2-mackerels-what-to-do.3669/post-51909>

had whole sardines before but they were still partially frozen, which felt like a safety net cuz it takes some edge off the experience lol. this was first time pure naked raw to every last bit.

there is a particular organ in the guts that's way harsh bitter. its at the bottom just behind the head/gills... maybe the gall bladder? best to skip that whatever it is

# Thread: Dr Who's Newby Questions
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/dr-whos-newby-questions.3672/>
**Subforum:** Cold Thermogenesis
**Posts:** 1
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### AKMan — 2012-09-15T07:03:25+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/dr-whos-newby-questions.3672/post-51766>

Not sure why it got closed, but it was a question that keeps popping up. Look here for some more info, easy to miss: [http://forum.jackkruse.com/showthread.php?816-Quickstart-guide-to-Cold-Thermogenesis](http://http://forum.jackkruse.com/showthread.php?816-Quickstart-guide-to-Cold-Thermogenesis)

I recommend the face dunking for newcomers to CT. It invokes the mammalian dive response and preps the body for more serious CT. This is especially good for people who live in warm climes and never really get cold.

Once you can comfortably keep your face in icy water while holding your breath for about a minute, you are good to go for cold showers and baths. I think we discussed using a snorkle, but decided that defeats the MDR which is about hypoxia.

Start out with cool showers if you like, don't sweat the temp at first, just get way below your comfort zone. Eventually you'll be able to shower at the lowest setting you can get. If that turns out to be 50-55 deg F, you are good to go. If it's only 60+ degrees, you may want to try the ice bath with ice added to get the magical 55 degrees. 55 degrees is the temp where skin cold receptors are maxed out and full activation of BAT and uncoupling protein-1 (UCP-1) is fully working. People on here have workied their way down to 32 degree water for 45 minutes or more. If you go that extreme, make sure you have a spotter and watch for hypothermia.

The extremes you go to should be dictated by your individual circumstances and goals, not just mimicing others here. If you just have a few pounds to lose and are otherwise healthy, an ice bath after a hard workout or a workout wearing an ice-pack vest may do wonders for you. If you are morbidly obese or metabolically a wreck, you may want to work your way down to 'deep CT' as we like to say.

good luck, post about your experiences. I will be posting when I start CT again in another month.

### Caroline Cooper — 2012-09-15T18:02:11+0300

**World Rivers Day is on September 29, 2012. Some of my friends from **350.org** and the Thompson Environmental Network** want to meet down at the Thompson River for a garbage clean-up day. I really wanted to go pick plastic bags and garbage out of the river but I have to work on Saturdays. I still had the urge to help. It got me thinking about how the garbage gets into the river in the first place.

I realized I didn’t need to go anywhere...

[http://eatkamloops.org/solidarity-of-a-sort/](http://http://eatkamloops.org/solidarity-of-a-sort/)

I invite you to clean the rivers of the world with me without leaving your neighborhood. If you would like, please post pictures of your neighborhood garbage. I am wondering if the garbage will look the same everywhere or if it is different.

# Thread: Hack my Labs - Input Please!
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hack-my-labs-input-please.3683/>
**Subforum:** Optimal Labs
**Posts:** 7
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### angieonthegogo — 2012-09-15T21:27:38+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hack-my-labs-input-please.3683/post-51851>

TSH 2.220 uIU/mL 0.450-4.500

T4 9.4 ug/dL 4.5-12.0

FT3 2.8 pg/mL 2.0-4.4

FT4 1.25 ng/dL 0.82-1.77

rT3 42.1 ng/dL 13.5-34.2 HIGH

TPO Ab 11 IU/mL 0-34

Antithy Ab 39

LDL 103 mg/dL 0-99 HIGH

Free Testosterone 0.4 pg/mL 0.0-2.2

Progesterone 4.4 ng/mL

Estradiol 98.7 pg/mL

All labs done on approx CD 22 of 30. 33 yo F. In looking at these I obviously have a T4-T3 conversion problem right? rT3 is high, LDL is high but believe it or not it has come down from 128 last Oct. HDL is still low but up from 38. My TC is down from 193 and Trigs are way down from 139. Not sure why my TC dropped so much...don't I need that higher to help with the hormonies?

I'm thinking my iron defficiency anemia and leaky gut are wreaking havoc on my rT3. My blood sugar is normally in the 95 area which is still high but is way high on here. I'm not sure what came first...the chicken or the egg? I'm guessing the first things to address are the Iron and the leaky gut. Then the Insulin resistance? I'm thinking the leaky gut is throwing off the cholesterol and thyroid. What to do first?

I'm confused about the Antibodies. I've read some conflicting things and other than the rT3 the others seem to be within normal functional ranges but what specifically is considered optimal? I've been to STTM and my head is kind of spinning.

Any help would be much appreciated!

ETA: Oh ya DHEA & Sex hormones are in the TOILET!  But what is optimal?
