# Thread: I'm kind of a baby, so I need some help please.
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/im-kind-of-a-baby-so-i-need-some-help-please.3777/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 13
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### cookworm — 2012-09-20T05:37:57+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/im-kind-of-a-baby-so-i-need-some-help-please.3777/post-52864>

I love salmon, but can't always afford the fresh stuff, so I am willing to go with wild canned salmon to make my diet more epi-paleo. I don't have a problem with bones, but, I admit, the skin grosses me out. So, I have a three part question:

\- What are some different ways to enjoy canned salmon? and

\- Is there a way to enjoy the salmon with the skin and bones, carefully finding a way to disguise the skin? and

\- Does mashing the skin with the bones and fish make the salmon itself taste different/unpleasant?

Thanks!

### Melinda — 2012-09-20T06:12:46+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/im-kind-of-a-baby-so-i-need-some-help-please.3777/post-52865>

I use Trader Joe's wild canned 1 lb cans to make a salmon loaf type dish. I don't have a set recipe but the bones and skin disappear as long as I add some celery. It's better than using expensive salmon meat without the skin and bones. Mash fish, add a few eggs, celery, onion, sun dried tomatoes, dill, maybe some rice bread crumbs, shredded cheese, diced pre-cooked bacon, diced black olives etc...mix all and cook. I pat it into a fry pan, lots of ghee and fry it, flip it onto a plate and slide back into the pan to cook the other side or leave it in the pan, add more cheese and broil it until firm/cooked.

### caroline — 2012-09-20T14:03:40+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/im-kind-of-a-baby-so-i-need-some-help-please.3777/post-52876>

> **cookworm;53496 wrote:** I love salmon, but can't always afford the fresh stuff, so I am willing to go with wild canned salmon to make my diet more epi-paleo. I don't have a problem with bones, but, I admit, the skin grosses me out. So, I have a three part question:
>
>
>
> \- What are some different ways to enjoy canned salmon? and
>
> \- Is there a way to enjoy the salmon with the skin and bones, carefully finding a way to disguise the skin? and
>
> \- Does mashing the skin with the bones and fish make the salmon itself taste different/unpleasant?
>
>
>
> Thanks!

The skin and bones used to gross me out too- but now I just mash it all up and add green onions and it is good to go! You really can't tell - doesn't change the taste - at least I don't think so. Lately - food just seems to be fuel to me - so I just eat it and move on.

### WannaBeOptimal — 2012-09-20T16:19:05+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/im-kind-of-a-baby-so-i-need-some-help-please.3777/post-52894>

Recommend that you switch to Wild Planet brand...they have BPA free cans. I eat both the pink and the sockeye. TJ's is supposed to be working on BPA free cans.

### ealachan — 2012-09-20T17:06:33+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/im-kind-of-a-baby-so-i-need-some-help-please.3777/post-52897>

> **cookworm;53496 wrote:** I love salmon, but can't always afford the fresh stuff, so I am willing to go with wild canned salmon to make my diet more epi-paleo. I don't have a problem with bones, but, I admit, the skin grosses me out. So, I have a three part question:
>
>
>
> \- What are some different ways to enjoy canned salmon? and
>
> \- Is there a way to enjoy the salmon with the skin and bones, carefully finding a way to disguise the skin? and
>
> \- Does mashing the skin with the bones and fish make the salmon itself taste different/unpleasant?
>
>
>
> Thanks!

Just mash it all up. The skin and bones break right down and don't change the flavor of the salmon at all. My favorite way to use canned salmon is to fry a couple pieces of bacon, take them out of the pan, then add in a can of mashed-up salmon. Saute it in the bacon fat a little bit to warm through, then scoop it onto a plate and add the strips of bacon, chopped up. It's good with sauteed kale and a couple runny eggs on top, too...yum!

### WannaBeOptimal — 2012-09-20T17:45:16+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/im-kind-of-a-baby-so-i-need-some-help-please.3777/post-52905>

What ealachan said...done the same myself.

### Patty Cakes — 2012-09-20T18:16:03+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/im-kind-of-a-baby-so-i-need-some-help-please.3777/post-52913>

When we were kids and my mom was making salmon patties after she dumped the blob of salmon out of the can she would ask us "who wants the bones?" and all three of us would run to munch and crunch on the bones. We were taught they were a treat like batter from the blender beaters when making cookies.

I still make salmon patties and this is what I eat when I travel. I mash the salmon with some freshly grated (or bottled) horseradish, caramelized onions, good salt, and a sprinkling of walnut meal to hold them together. I fry in a little olive oil (used to use coconut oil but no more). I have also made little salmon balls this same way just a different shape. I also have a variety of organic dried herbs that I use to add a different flavor if I'm in the mood.

### ealachan — 2012-09-20T18:22:05+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/im-kind-of-a-baby-so-i-need-some-help-please.3777/post-52914>

> **Patty Cakes;53548 wrote:** When we were kids and my mom was making salmon patties after she dumped the blob of salmon out of the can she would ask us "who wants the bones?" and all three of us would run to munch and crunch on the bones. We were taught they were a treat like batter from the blender beaters when making cookies.
>
>
>
> I still make salmon patties and this is what I eat when I travel. I mash the salmon with some freshly grated (or bottled) horseradish, caramelized onions, good salt, and a sprinkling of walnut meal to hold them together. I fry in a little olive oil (used to use coconut oil but no more). I have also made little salmon balls this same way just a different shape. I also have a variety of organic dried herbs that I use to add a different flavor if I'm in the mood.

Oooh, I love horseradish. Now I need to make some more salmon cakes and try them with horseradish in!

Your talking of crunching the bones made me laugh. I actually don't mind the texture of them anymore, but they seem to kind of stick to my teeth, which drives me bonkers.

### Patty Cakes — 2012-09-20T18:24:35+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/im-kind-of-a-baby-so-i-need-some-help-please.3777/post-52916>

> **ealachan;53549 wrote:** Oooh, I love horseradish. Now I need to make some more salmon cakes and try them with horseradish in!

You must try freshly grated horseradish! So much better than the prepared bottled stuff. I am assuming here that it is an epi-paleo approved food since it is a root. I can't have peppers so this is pretty much my only ZING!

### ealachan — 2012-09-20T18:32:15+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/im-kind-of-a-baby-so-i-need-some-help-please.3777/post-52923>

> **Patty Cakes;53551 wrote:** You must try freshly grated horseradish! So much better than the prepared bottled stuff. I am assuming here that it is an epi-paleo approved food since it is a root. I can't have peppers so this is pretty much my only ZING!

Oh yes, my dad grows it, so he's always got some hanging around. Once I had the actual ground root with just a little vinegar in it to preserve it, I never went back to the bottled stuff. I used to work with an old-timer who kept a bottle of his own home-grown horseradish root in the fridge. When one of our crew got a sore throat or a stuffy nose, out would come the horseradish and he'd make us take a dose straight-up. That stuff would knock a cold out of you like nobody's business!

### Patty Cakes — 2012-09-20T18:40:36+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/im-kind-of-a-baby-so-i-need-some-help-please.3777/post-52927>

OMG!!! Horseradish up the nose! I'm getting a burning sense up there just thinking about it. Add a raw garlic sandwich to that and you'd be good to go!

### cookworm — 2012-09-20T19:36:57+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/im-kind-of-a-baby-so-i-need-some-help-please.3777/post-52939>

ealachan--hey you Raveler!  This sounds delicious (anything with bacon is better!)

These are some GREAT ideas--thank you everybody! I'm not opposed to trying most things...I just can't seem to think outside the box mostly.

### BJK77 — 2012-09-20T22:17:46+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/im-kind-of-a-baby-so-i-need-some-help-please.3777/post-52981>

My kids had a really hard time accepting the skin and bones as part of their "meal" for a while, but I think I finally found a way to make it work. I do salmon patties like someone previously mentioned. Just mash up the salmon, bones, skin and all - add to a beaten egg with some sea salt, fresh/dried dill. I used to always coat the patties in pecan meal, but we're trying to really limit nuts these days so now I chop up bacon and mix that into the patties . . . the kids love it! I fry the bacon in a cast iron skillet and leave the grease in their so I can just fry the patties in it to soak up the extra goodness. Works like a charm . . . the bacon flavor / color totally overpowers any skin/bone issues

# Thread: Bpa
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bpa.3778/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 15
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Endless — 2012-09-20T08:58:02+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bpa.3778/post-52869>

I'd love to see this as a sticky, so people could check out their canned fish! If anyone has determined through research or other means that a particular brand of canned fish/whatever is BPA free, please post it here!

[http://www.naturalnews.com/037241\_BPA\_nervous\_system\_plastics\_chemical.html](http://http://www.naturalnews.com/037241_BPA_nervous_system_plastics_chemical.html)

### WannaBeOptimal — 2012-09-20T17:27:40+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bpa.3778/post-52901>

Wild Planet...available at Whole Foods and Sweetbay Supermarket. Price varies on the type of salmon and ranges from 3 sumtin to 4 sumtin (6 oz).

### Patty Cakes — 2012-09-20T18:22:22+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bpa.3778/post-52915>

_"Trader Joe's Tells Little, Does More

. It is sometimes hard to know whether to be infuriated or pleased with the Trader Joe's approach to food retailing. Stories abound about some of TJ's practices - clear information at the company's web site does not. But a call to TJ's customer relations reveals that canned corn, canned beans, canned fish, canned poultry, and canned beef at Trader Joe's are all packaged in BPA free cans. However, any other products that contain corn or beans or fish, etc. and other items - for example in soups or chilis - are not in BPA free cans, the company said. No labeling here, either."_

From: [http://www.treehugger.com/green-food/7-companies-you-can-trust-to-use-bpa-free-cans.html](http://http://www.treehugger.com/green-food/7-companies-you-can-trust-to-use-bpa-free-cans.html)

But how do we know they haven't substituted BPS for the BPA?

### WannaBeOptimal — 2012-09-20T19:40:33+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bpa.3778/post-52942>

That article is over 2 years old.

Someone here contacted TJ's in the last few months, and was told that their fish is NOT in BPA free cans yet.

### WannaBeOptimal — 2012-09-20T19:51:42+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bpa.3778/post-52949>

Just emailed Trader Joe's about their cans. Will update upon reply.

### Patty Cakes — 2012-09-20T20:22:27+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bpa.3778/post-52959>

> **WannaBeOptimal;53584 wrote:** Just emailed Trader Joe's about their cans. Will update upon reply.

Thanks WannaBeOptimal! I've called TJs on other things and received different answers from different people so really never sure what to believe. I like shopping there for some things too!

### WannaBeOptimal — 2012-09-20T22:07:12+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bpa.3778/post-52980>

> **Patty Cakes;53594 wrote:** Thanks WannaBeOptimal! I've called TJs on other things and received different answers from different people so really never sure what to believe. I like shopping there for some things too!

### nonchalant — 2012-09-20T22:39:38+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bpa.3778/post-52984>

Natural Value tuna is BPA free. $1.99 for a can of wild caught yellowfin tuna at my locally owned HerbMart.

[http://naturalvalue.com/](http://http://naturalvalue.com/)

### WannaBeOptimal — 2012-10-09T03:32:42+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bpa.3778/post-57026>

Here you go...email reply from Trader Joe's Customer relations folks:

Thank you for contacting us. Here is the deal with BPA. First, regarding Tetra, all Tetra Pak is BPA-free.

Second, every glass jar item has a metal lid. All metal lids do have a layer of BPA coating. However, there is another coating put on after that. There is no direct contact of BPA to food. We have multiple supplier testing results showing there is no BPA detected from metal lids.

All our canned fish (and our canned chicken and beef too) are now in BPA-free cans EXCEPT: Sardines, Crab, Cherrystone Clams & Oysters (our suppliers are working for a 2012 solution).

All our canned fruits, beans and vegetables (including tomatoes, and the Organic Canned Pumpkin when it returns this Fall) are in BPA-free cans EXCEPT: Mandarins, Hatch Chilies, Artichokes, Organic Baked Beans (expecting transition next Fall).

All of our canned Soups and Stews (and including Joe's Os) are in cans that DO have BPA. Some of our suppliers are expecting they will be able to make transition next year.

Lastly, Coconut Milk and Coconut Cream is in a BPA-free can.

Regards,

Nikki

Customer Relations

### skline@peak.org — 2012-10-09T03:41:34+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bpa.3778/post-57027>

I've been buying brisling sardines in olive oil, source Poland -- hoping that since BPA is outlawed in

Europe, maybe it's outlawed in Poland as well?

or not ...

> Lastly, Coconut Milk and Coconut Cream is in a BPA-free can.
>
>
>
> Regards,
>
> Nikki
>
> Customer Relations

Great post...thanks so much!

### Mery — 2012-09-20T15:16:09+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/gain-muscle.3779/post-52884>

LR started 19-3-2012. Iâ€™ve been complaining about gaining weight even though I did not realised it that Iâ€™ve gained muscles and bones.

Iâ€™m not very observant like my husband said. I did notice before that my tummy was much firmer even though I was so obsessed about not being able to fit in my clothes that I forgot to look more closely to my arms and legs. My skin is not longer loose. No more bingo wings on my arms or flabby legs.

Iâ€™ve always had weak and flaccid muscles, no matter how much exercise I did, they didnâ€™t get firm, but now without any exercises my whole body is muscular.

It is nice to feel this way, my husband wants to do the same as he has got weak muscle like I used to have.

### ealachan — 2012-09-20T18:28:51+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/gain-muscle.3779/post-52920>

+1 to this! I'm still waiting on my bingo wings to melt off, but I have MUSCLES now. My calves used to be squishy, now they're hard. My thighs used to be like pudding. Now they're like...a layer of pudding over rock. LOL I can even feel abs if I poke deep enough into my Pillsbury Dough Boy belly, and...this is going to sound so silly, but...I can make my boobs bounce by flexing my pectoral muscles. I do it in front of the mirror almost every morning and crack myself up (because I'm way too easily amused, clearly).

All this and I haven't lifted a weight yet since I started this process. It's pretty rad.

### Destiny — 2012-09-26T13:32:16+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/gain-muscle.3779/post-54386>

The 10 Top Tips When Buying And Using Body Fat Scales

1\. Use them to measure your progress only. Don't compare your body fat percentage to tables or to your friends score. It is probably inaccurate no matter what the manufacturers say.

2\. Choose body fat scales that have the right profile for you. If your children are going to use them make sure they can be calibrated for children. If you're an athlete (+10 hrs of strenuous exercise a week) same thing applies. This is important. It makes a big difference.

3\. Measure out some water one hour before you test yourself. Make sure you always drink the same amount of water one hour before you test yourself.

4\. Measure yourself at the same time of day for each test.

5\. Your skin temperature affects the electrical current used by the scales. It's difficult but try to test yourself in a similar room temperature each time.

6\. Don't test yourself after exercising. When you exercise you sweat and when you sweat you lose water. This affects your hydration levels and hence... the results.

7\. Thoroughly clean the foot pads, preferably with alcohol and then dry them off each time you test.

8\. Buy the most expensive body fat scales you can afford. Accuracy will almost certainly increase with price.

9\. If ever you get the chance, have your body composition measured by a professional using skin fold calipers. It will give you a frame of reference as to how accurate your scales are.

10\. Read the page on body fat calipers for an alternative you could consider.

# Thread: Optimal Education
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/optimal-education.3781/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 21
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### jeanie@truelongmont.com — 2012-09-20T16:59:25+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/optimal-education.3781/post-52896>

I thought I would start a discussion about education.

Last night my son's school showed the film "Race to Nowhere" and it was very difficult to watch and keep my mouth shut.

I found it both correct in a sense and completely misguided to the reasons behind the stories featured.

The messages were so jumbled, and they did not offer much research or alternate theories etc etc, it was a pull the heartstrings kind of thing with the death of a child.

Opening the conversation about how bad our education is today, is a good thing, so at least it accomplished that - but I feel it was like vegetarians praching to vegetarians about CAFO.

The gist was we are ruining all these kids by teaching to the test and giving them 3-6 hrs of homework and then they threw in the "must get into top college" craze and how harmful that is.

While they were trying to place blame on the education system - these kids they shadowed, were sitting there with their cokes and chips, and then sitting in front of their computers for 4 hrs a night until 11-12 and getting up early for school the next day. No matter how you assign blame to try and understand "the reason why", all of it is clearly contributing.

I am ready for some good discussion on how we can balance the reality of the social structure we live in vs raising our kids the best way we can.

I've totally considered homeschooling, and if I had had a little introvert copy of myself - I'd be there. . . but I ended up getting a sports minded social butterfly, who is an only child - and for him I think being around others more often is better for his overall being.

That, and I'm grooming him to be ruler of the free world

Discuss!!!!!

### Lyndra — 2012-09-20T18:26:05+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/optimal-education.3781/post-52917>

> **Cavemam;53531 wrote:**
>
> I've totally considered homeschooling, and if I had had a little introvert copy of myself - I'd be there. . . but I ended up getting a sports minded social butterfly, who is an only child - and for him I think being around others more often is better for his overall being.
>
>
>
> That, and I'm grooming him to be ruler of the free world
>
>
>
> Discuss!!!!!

Would you hurry up on that last part! We could use somebody good at the top who gets "optimal."

As far as homeschooling, I know a few who do it and they have their kids involved in group activities - 4H, boy/girl scouts, church, karate, music, dance, etc. I know once the kids are high school age, they can play on high school teams. I would think you could find plenty of activities for him so he'd have interaction with other kids. The question I have for you - do you have the time to devote to home schooling? Figure several hours a day. One of the upsides, of course, is spending time with him and doing field trips that are both fun and educational. But it's a big commitment on your time. I could refer you to a friend who is home schooling her son who is probably around 8 now if you want to talk to someone.

### jeanie@truelongmont.com — 2012-09-20T18:47:57+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/optimal-education.3781/post-52930>

> **Lyndra;53552 wrote:** Would you hurry up on that last part! We could use somebody good at the top who gets "optimal."
>
>
>
> As far as homeschooling, I know a few who do it and they have their kids involved in group activities - 4H, boy/girl scouts, church, karate, music, dance, etc. I know once the kids are high school age, they can play on high school teams. I would think you could find plenty of activities for him so he'd have interaction with other kids. The question I have for you - do you have the time to devote to home schooling? Figure several hours a day. One of the upsides, of course, is spending time with him and doing field trips that are both fun and educational. But it's a big commitment on your time. I could refer you to a friend who is home schooling her son who is probably around 8 now if you want to talk to someone.

Actually. . . I (we) have given this considerable thought. When we moved here, it was with the intention of HSing. We got involved with the HS groups when he was 3 (moms group who planned to HS).

I still may do this - it was what I wanted. What I had to accept was that my little bugger just seemed to do better and learn better in a group setting. Also, the being around groups of other kids on a regular basis seemed a very big positive for him.

The problem lies with Moi - I can be at home all week and never leave the house and be perfectly happy with that, and I didn't know if I had it in me to MAKE SURE I got him out and about in the world on a daily basis. That life experience with the world is needed by him, and I wasn't outgoing enough to provide it. I noticed the successful homeschooling moms were always planning something, always happy to hang out with other moms etc. . . .and I'm not there yet.

We went to a year of flagstaff for kindergarten. . . I think it's an awesome school, but man it did not gel with mama. All those rules and doing things because it's procedure and system and needed by the state etc was not something I could do very well.

SO this year, we are repeating Kindergarten but only 3 hrs a day at Montessori. . .and I'm doing the learning part at home in a natural way (for example, we learned the chemical structure of Boron at bfast today).

Also, he starts at 12:30, so we don't have crazy schedules. . .I'm slowly adjusting work for me. I've gone from crazy mom trying to teach him chinese at age 2 to laid back "ahhh, he will learn all that on his own".

I need to find the right path for him for moving on in the world after us. As morbid as it is, we have no other family and he's all alone in the world when we are gone. I must prepare him to go out of this beautiful nest (and hopefully find an optimal wife and make me lots of grandkids quick!  without the security of our little 3 some - and kicking him out to be with others seems like the right 1st step for that. I hate it though, but that's selfish.

### Shijin13 — 2012-09-20T19:16:40+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/optimal-education.3781/post-52936>

Ahh the challenges... I would love to homeschool - I think my kids would enjoy it - however financially that's not feasible. Like I've said previously I've consider a career change to teaching - but not entirely sure how we'd swing that b/c I'd have to go back to school FT and by doing that we'd loose my cheap/good health insurance.

If we could afford it I'd send both Chaos and Havoc to the Waldorf school - they spend all day out side (Rain, sleet, snow or sun), they focus is on math and science and the natural world and its secular based. but at $20K/yr/ kid - that's just not feasible.

So, we take advantage of where we are w/all the free museums & we try to hit at least one every couple of months. Thus far we've only made it to the natural history museum (3x no less) b/c dd is a HUGE dinosaur fan. She's 4 she's already doing her addition/subtraction, she's writing her name, and she's starting to teach herself how to read. ds is still behind on talking, but ahead in motorskills - he's almost mastered the rock wall on the swing set at 2&1/2....dd didn't master it until she was almost 4.

One thing we do is when we go on walks, or work out side, we stop and explore learning moments. Last night - the moon was out, and dd noticed it wasn't full and had a shadow - she asked where the other part was - so we talked about how the moon reflects the sun's light which is why we see the moon at night. we also talked about how the earth's shadow is what gives the appearance of a moon with different sizes... she thought that was cool, then started asking about venus. Last weekend when we pulled up the garden - we had a ton of bugs - we brought the kids over and talked about why bugs are good for the environment - even talked about the nasty ol' mosquito.

One thing I've been considering is doing a MOVNAT class - and learning how to incorporate those movements into the kids and our daily movements. Last night at the end of our walk - we all got on our hands and feet and walked the last 30 ft that way - just to do something different...the kids had a blast doing that!

I realize that in PS that Chaos and Havoc are going to be taught different things - specifically being taught how to test well - that's ok. I've already started teaching them how to think critically, and solve problems - I don't help them every time they ask for it. I make them try to solve the issue they are facing. they've got to know how to look at a problem - and figure out how to fix it by themselves.

Right now we're battling the idea that dd has to wake up and take a shower every morning and wash her hair (dcp talks about this) dd takes a bath each night - and we wash hair 1x/week - doesn't need to be done any more than that... but b/c other kids do different things she wants to be like them - b/c she thinks the way others do things is right. we're trying to convince her that's not true. Since she's so adamant that she needs to shower/wash her hair ect - I told her tomorrow she could get in the shower w/me tomorrow morning - and that means getting up at 530 to make that happen. I'm betting by Wednesday next week she's over it.

We've told both kids from the start at 18 they're out of the house. they have to have a job, go to college or go into the military but they have to learn to become self sufficient, and figure out how to manage the demands of life w/o mom and dad on a daily basis. That doesn't mean if they go to college, and want to come back home to pay off student loans, after getting job they can't do that - they will have to pay rent, and contribute to meals etc. but once the loans are paid off...back out on their own they go.

maybe we should just start a commune....

### kathylu — 2012-09-20T19:37:39+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/optimal-education.3781/post-52940>

I'm struggling with the same question. My son is an only and to compound the issue, there are teenagers in our neighborhood but no 5 year olds! So he was getting almost all of his play and social time in connection with his pre-k program. It was 3 hours, 3 days per week which was perfect for him! I don't know how it is in Colorado, but here in Maryland kindergarten is a full time program. So, it seemed the switch from 9 hours to per week to 35 hours per week would just be brutal. So, for that and other reasons, we opted for homeschooling this year. There are pros and cons of course. Academically, it's great. He's already working at a 2nd grade level (WAPF diet, no grains ever, and fish oil since birth made him a little superman-einstein). What really made our decision to homeschool was when I asked the public school what accomodations they had in place for gifted children and the answer was "None, until 2nd grade...he will just have to wait for the other children to catch up to where he is." Lovely. So, homeschooling it is.

To handle the social aspect, we are enrolling him in swim lessons, soccer, gymnastics and yoga and I have 2 active playgroups that meet once per week. We are also considering joining a homeschool co-op or two. He seems to be having a great time so far, but you are right...it is hard on me. I am constantly taking him somewhere and waiting while he has an activity or plays.

I gave up the "supermom" syndrome a while ago, though I must admit he constantly surprises me with his abilities! When I really asked myself what my goals are for him, they are really pretty simple. I want him to be happy. I want him to be able to provide for himself and his future family by doing work he loves. I want him to be healthy. That about covers it. Anything else he will have to write...

### Huck — 2012-09-20T19:42:05+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/optimal-education.3781/post-52945>

Are you all familiar with [Kahn Academy](http://http://www.khanacademy.org/)? It is a free Internet school. It can be used by an individual, a home schooling parent and child, any student for remedial work, etc.

It is also being used by some school systems. What they do is flip the way normal school works. Kids use Kahn Academy after school to learn the material and then school time is used for exercises and one on one help.

My favorite book about learning is [On Intelligence](http://http://www.onintelligence.org/reviews.php). While the book is not about learning per se, it does present a theory on how the neocortex works and I found it very helpful in helping understand how our brains process and "learn" information. I've found the info I learned in On Intelligence to be helpful when Jack waxes poetically about the neocortex. One of the few times I actually completely understand what he is talking about.

[radio interview](http://http://www.rni.org/hawkins/Jeff_Hawkins_On_Intelligence.mp3) with author on Intelligence and how the brain works.

### nonchalant — 2012-09-20T20:44:22+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/optimal-education.3781/post-52967>

My sister is homeschooling her two children all the way through school. One child is very outgoing, the other less so. The oldest will be finished with high school soon, and is enrolled in some community college courses. They are active in church, sports, & music. I am constantly amazed by her kids. They are so intelligent, and can get up and speak easily to groups of strangers, as I witnessed at a bridal shower where they only knew a few people there.

Not sure about their current curriculum, but several years ago, when I asked, sis was only spending about 4 hours per day on studies, with the rest of the time spent on sports, music, whatever. I don't know about homework, I believe that was included in the 4 hours.

It's amazing how much time you have when you don't teach all the agenda-laden stuff...

### trishalinn@gmail.com — 2012-09-24T07:31:15+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/optimal-education.3781/post-53758>

>

I also have a little social butterfly. That being said, I knew when deciding to homeschool that we would need to be intentional with keeping him involved. In my area there are some excellent homeschool groups and other extracurricular opportunities available to homeschoolers.

We are involved in a fantastic classical education co-op once a week. There is another homeschool group in the area who does mainly social fun events that we occadionaly attend also. My son is also playing on the elementary homeschool basketball team (practice 3 days a week plus games) and will probably also swim on the swim team(through the public school). There are so many other things he could do... A homeschool music group with band, choir, and orchestra, 4h... The list goes on and on!

The point being, if you want to homeschool I think there are more opportunities popping up all the time for homeschooled kids!

### Michael — 2012-09-24T16:57:26+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/optimal-education.3781/post-53784>

> **Cavemam;53531 wrote:** I thought I would start a discussion about education.
>
>
>
>
> I'm not a parent. I did go through post-grad teacher training for what that's worth.
>
>
>
> I think what Jack said recently in another context is also relevant here:
>
>
>
> The world of modern medicine can be a dangerous place to visit for any human; not because of the physicians, hospitals, corporations, and government rules that are inherently sinister. It is this way because of the people in those groups who refuse to do anything about altering it. They just are not aware of what they do not know and they refuse to look for a better way.

What you get going on in schools inevitably comes to have more to do with what suits the way the school is set up, what's easy to administer, what's habitually done, whether the school needs to hit some artificial target set by government, what the teacher's capabilities and anxieties are with regards to "crowd control" and a host of other things like that rather than simply what's best for children.

And, simply, "They just are not aware of what they do not know and they refuse to look for a better way."

I'll throw in one thing that would be a really hard sell in Britain or the U.S.

I think children start "forced learning" too early. In most of the Scandinavian countries and some other parts of Europe that doesn't begin till seven. IMO, younger than that they simply oughtn't to be sitting in chairs for protracted periods doing activities like writing. In Britain the kids start in the year in which they will be five. So they're four and a few months when they actually go and begin rather (not totally but beginning to be so) formal schooling.

There are plenty of Scandinavians on this board. Ask them whether the children over there are any further behind by the time they get to eleven, let alone 18.

They should be outside, getting some sun, and running around. They could be doing exploratory things, like digging in sandpits and finding out what you can do with water. Simply being around and talking to people and observing, they're learning all the time anyway.

Then they could be doing things that improve their gross motor skills. So they should be able to play catch with beanbags, and later balls, with adults and other children, and all that sort of thing - climbing and jumping.

At the age at which they're sat at a table and told to write in Britain many of them don't even have the hand-eye coordination and level of strength that would enable them to do it. It's too early for many of them. So if you walk around a class of children and watch them in a writing-practice lesson, you'll see some really odd cortortions that will be harmful for the spine, and some really strange pen-holds, that also arise from this. They screw their bodies up and round in these ways, because the activity is, as yet, too difficult for them. They probably shouldn't be given pencils either, but fatter crayons that would be easier for them to hold, but the teacher should check they're holding them with fingers loosely down them, not clutched in a fist. And actually, both the tables and chairs in schools are usual unsuitable for the purpose, difficult as it is already. The older desks with sloped lids were better. But did you ever hear of a school that provided writing slopes? The chairs are poor, too. For writing the seat should probably be able to slope forward, whereas many tilt backwards which rotates the pelvis backwads the wrong way, so that the child can't lean forward naturally from the hips.

Best of all would be one of thsoe continuously adjustable chairs. I think they're Swedish - Trip-trap or something?? That way the child's height and leg-length could be catered to.

Schools haven't even begun to think about these things. "They just are not aware of what they do not know".

### johnnyb — 2012-09-24T17:30:35+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/optimal-education.3781/post-53787>

How awesome would it be if PE teachers could pass on MovNat skills.

### Shijin13 — 2012-09-24T17:35:17+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/optimal-education.3781/post-53788>

### Michael — 2012-09-24T20:04:35+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/optimal-education.3781/post-53843>

Here, BTW, in case it's of interest to anyone are the Tripp Trapp chairs. There are probably other companies doing similar things:

[http://www.stokke.com/en-gb/highchair/tripp-trapp-product-concept.aspx](http://http://www.stokke.com/en-gb/highchair/tripp-trapp-product-concept.aspx)

One of the interesting things is the footrest.

You get this problem that the chair has to, as it were, fit both the table and the child. So if the table's relatively high, you can raise the child up to a comfortable level where he can work on a table (or eat at it or whatever) ... which then means the child's legs are dangling.

That can get tiring, because none of the weight is being taken through the big muscles in the legs, which are effectively taken out of action.

### zzzcjr@comcast.net — 2012-09-24T20:40:56+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/optimal-education.3781/post-53863>

I'm starting my 17th, and last, year homeschooling.

There are so many options out there, you'll probably find one to fit you and your son.

We were heavily involved in enrichment classes at a megachurch, where up to 1000 kids K-12 attended twice a week. LOTS of socializing going on, but exhausting and I became a much better mommy when we stopped going. Sounds like you would hate it.

Kahn academy is wonderful, but didn't come out until our kids were past that point. Also, while he tries to be objective, some topics make it difficult i.e. he's clearly a fan of Keynesian economics and I'm not at all. You just need to watch where politics can enter in. Both sides aren't always evenly portrayed. Math is pretty safe.

If you have good charter schools, they usually offer a part-time program specifically for homeschool kids. Some have half-day programs and some a couple full days a week. Then if you decide to send him full time, it's an easy transition. They can take care of the basic essentials and you can do the fun stuff. You'd be surprised how much you can get done in a fraction of the time public school kids take.

In retrospect, reading LOTS good books taught them more than anything. Also, including them in everything I was doing, while explaining the whys of everything.

My kids are 22, 20, and 16. All 3 tested college ready in Math & English by age 16. I was anal with the first one and really on top of everything and I'm not sure I taught the last one anything. I was tired and not all that "present" at that point. They both learned just fine so I don't get credit or blame. Once they can read, they can learn, though some need more prodding than others.

The point that I took too long to make is, there's an option out there that'll be just right. Schools can be very accommodating, especially charters.

# Thread: Tooth Demineralization
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/tooth-demineralization.3782/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 6
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Michael — 2012-09-20T20:21:51+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/tooth-demineralization.3782/post-52958>

This is in vegans. The study was recently tweeted by Dr. Eades, who commented that what's true for the teeth is likely true for the bones.

[http://www.minervamedica.it/en/journals/minerva-stomatologica/article.php?cod=R18Y2010N12A0583](http://http://www.minervamedica.it/en/journals/minerva-stomatologica/article.php?cod=R18Y2010N12A0583)

I don't think studies that show this kind of thing are grounds for laughing at vegans or something. On the other hand, I think it's well to be aware of the damage these diets cause - among other things, because the diets most people eat are sort-of heading that way, just not quite so bad. If you eat a diet low in minerals and fat-soluble vitamins and high in carbohydrate and wholegrains you'd seem to be risking your teeth even if you do have a little meat as well.

There's a rather silly "meme" now going around paleo whereby people say that whatever diet you go on, as long as it's not the average diet that most people are eating, it's a good thing. I can't imagine where such a ridiculous idea came from. Sure meat and potatoes, pasta with bolognese sauce, rice and curry, burgers in buns, cereal, fruit juice and rest of it, including in some cases doubtless more than a little pizza, beer, and coke, is far from ideal. It's hardly the case that bulgar wheat and soy would be better.

Loren Cordain, to his credit, has said precisely the opposite and Chapter 4 of _The Paleo Solution is one of the most powerful and best-researched chapters in the book. The meta-studies from Oxford University quoted by the professor on page 50 of his book show that a vegetarian (let alone a vegan) diet is not_ better than the average diet.

Cordain virtually arraigns the USDA for deception. The implication is that it implies vegetarian and vegan diets are more harmless than it must know them to be and than the data shows them to be. One it assumes it does this either because it wants a quite life and vegetarian activists can be noisy or simply because such diets don't get in the way of the economic interests the USDA serves, so that it couldn't care less.

### KiwiLauren — 2012-09-21T00:03:06+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/tooth-demineralization.3782/post-53021>

Money talks with the USDA. Nothing else. Certainly not health. It is a political body and operates on a political basis. Vegetarian 'activists' will be funded precisely because they serve the economic interests of the USDA/USA big agra etc. And therefore everything will be kept quiet, no matter how much we know. Having been a vegetarian for many years I can say that there is as big a span of vegetarianism as there is mainstream dieting. I know many vegetarians who are gluten and sugar free because they know the problems with that. I also know many who are soy-free. This would clearly be steps in the right direction. However, the issue will be what they are deficient in that simply can't be obtained by avoiding offending foods. And the maker of Fat Head proved that you can eat fast food in a way that is healthier and doesn't clearly do harm (though I'm not sure he or anyone else would argue it's optimal, only that there is a range of health across SAD, too).

Self-empowerment seems to be the only real way forward. I don't take a paranoid position of assuming they're all out to make me sick, but I DO assume the mainstream docs/writers are uninformed.

### Souldanzer — 2012-09-21T00:08:09+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/tooth-demineralization.3782/post-53025>

I used to be around raw vegans and they do get a lot of problems with their teeth. Most raw food gurus say this is b/c people don't eat enough greens and therefore minerals. So that's how green smoothies came about. I was pounding the greens when I was a raw foodie and vegan. It does help. Thing is, I don't think it's the minerals (maybe also) but the super high K1 that people are getting when they eat lbs and lbs of greens that heals their teeth problems.

The other problem is that a lot of vegans are chocolate chip cookie vegans. They eat crap, not real food. Not saying veganism is healthy... just pointing out that it can lead to very very bad choices amongst the choices they do have b/c of all their restrictions.

### jeanie@truelongmont.com — 2012-09-21T03:39:27+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/tooth-demineralization.3782/post-53083>

You know, not much difference between a good vegetarian and a pescatarian. . . .just sayin, we are not all that far off.

### Souldanzer — 2012-09-21T04:59:17+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/tooth-demineralization.3782/post-53110>

> **Cavemam;53718 wrote:** You know, not much difference between a good vegetarian and a pescatarian. . . .just sayin, we are not all that far off.

yeah but a lot of difference between a cookie vegetarian and an epi-paleo person......

### Michael — 2012-09-21T08:07:08+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/tooth-demineralization.3782/post-53128>

> **Cavemam;53718 wrote:** You know, not much difference between a good vegetarian and a pescatarian. . . .just sayin, we are not all that far off.

If someone were eating a lot of eggs and really good dairy produce - and taking the right supplements, because that still wouldn't get them there - then they'd probably be OK. (Cordain is actually crunchingly specific about which nutrients you're going to be low in on a vegetarian diet and how fast it happens - within _days_ for some nutrients.)

The people in this study weren't lacto-ovo vegetarian. They were vegan. If you go that route, then to get enough protein you have to eat quite a lot of a mix of cereal grains and pulses, to get the full complement of amino acids. But if you eat a lot of wholegrains and legumes, then it's going to interfere with the uptake of minerals. Notice also that the fat-soluble vitamins, such as vitamin A, seem to play a role here. True vitamin A you can only get from animal sources.

I don't think we should be surprised that these people were demineralizing their teeth.

# Thread: My labs show sub-optimal, but nothing *screams* out at me as a problem!
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/my-labs-show-sub-optimal-but-nothing-screams-out-at-me-as-a-problem.3784/>
**Subforum:** Optimal Labs
**Posts:** 9
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### smithangels@comcast.net — 2012-09-20T22:24:30+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/my-labs-show-sub-optimal-but-nothing-screams-out-at-me-as-a-problem.3784/post-52983>

I have suspected that I have either adrenal/cortisol issues or thyroid issues for a long time. Just got some thyroid tests back (waiting to save up $$ for the adrenal tests!) and simply don't know what to make of them. I was \*sure\* there was going to be something wrong with them (I \*feel\* hypothyroid), but I simply cannot see anything out of kilter.

If anybody that knows more than me can take a look and let me know if you notice something, please let me know! I would be grateful

TSH 1.800 uIU/mL (range: 0.450-4.500)

T4 6.6 ug/dL (range: 4.5-12.0)

T4,Free(Direct) 1.25 ng/dL (range: 0.82-1.77)

T3, Free,Serum 3.4 pg/mL (range: 2.0-4.4)

Reverse T3, Serum 21.5 ng/dL (range: 13.5-34.2)

Testosterone, Serum 30 ng/dL (range: 8-48)

Free Testosterone 0.6 pg/mL (range: 0.0-2.2)

Other test results (in case they are helpful), DHEA, cholesterol and a liver test are high, Vit D is low, it seems:

DHEA-Sulfate 254.1 ug/dL (range: 35.4-256.0) (I've been supplementing 7-keto until a week b4 labs)

Estradiol 110.6 (taken on day 21 of my cycle. Luteal phase range: 43.8 - 211.0)

Vitamin D, 25-Hyd. 56.6 ng/mL (range: 30.0-100.0) (and I've been supplementing 5-10,000 daily for a year now!!!)

C-Reactive Protein 1.29 mg/L (range: 0.00-3.00)

Progesterone 5.9 ng/mL (taken on day 21 of my cycle. Luteal phase 1.7 - 27.0)

Cholesterol, Total 241 mg/dL (range: 100-199) \*SUPER HIGH!

LDL Cholesterol Calc 172 mg/dL (range: 0-99)

VLDL Cholesterol Cal 20 mg/dL (range: 5-40)

HDL Cholesterol 49 mg/dL (range: >39)

T. Chol/HDL Ratio 4.9 (range: 0.0-4.4)

Lastly, this liver "test" was high - even after Googling it, I am unsure what it means

ALT (SGPT) 54 IU/L (range: 0-40)

Thanks for any insight you have so I can improve myself - I am an "older" mom (45 years old, my girls are 6 and 7) and I want to stay around with my children for a while!

Regards,

PaleoBarbie

### Lyndra — 2012-09-21T00:37:32+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/my-labs-show-sub-optimal-but-nothing-screams-out-at-me-as-a-problem.3784/post-53038>

You look hypothyroid. Jack recommends Free T3 and Free T4 be in the top quartile of the range and TSH below 1. You also have too much reverse T3; ratio is 15 and should be at least 20 ([http://www.stopthethyroidmadness.com/rt3-ratio/](http://http://www.stopthethyroidmadness.com/rt3-ratio/)).

Your hsCRP is elevated, showing inflammation of some sort. Total cholesterol is not bad at all (mortality rates are actually lower for people with total cholesterol over 200). However, HDL is a bit low and LDL is high. Probably a case of pregnenolone steal going on here. You don't have enough T3 to join with LDL to create pregnenolone and its downstream hormones. Vitamin D isn't bad but should be higher. You don't give results for thyroid antibodies; that's something that should be checked for possible Hashimoto's. Pay attention to your diet and circadian rhythms.

I'll let someone else figure out your Pg/E2 ratio and advise on the liver.

### mambo — 2012-09-21T03:14:53+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/my-labs-show-sub-optimal-but-nothing-screams-out-at-me-as-a-problem.3784/post-53067>

Your ALT is high, in the fatty liver territory. How's your AST? The liver enzymes track your triglycerides, which is 100. So if you recently lowered your trigs from some higher number to 100, it will take some more time to lower your high ALT. Usually, if you lower your trigs under 100, you do not have fatty liver. It may take some time. Just make sure you don't have any hepatitis. You have moderate inflammation so it's understandable that your liver enzymes would be high or high-normal.

As for your thyroid, it seems that your high RT3 and TSH may be enough to give you hypothyroid symptoms. But it could also be your antibody positivity. So test your TPO and TG. Also, how are your WBCs? Approaching the low end of the Reference Range or steadily decreasing? If so, that would be likely indication of Hashimoto's.

> **PaleoBarbie;53618 wrote:** ALT (SGPT) 54 IU/L (range: 0-40)

### mambo — 2012-09-21T04:03:39+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/my-labs-show-sub-optimal-but-nothing-screams-out-at-me-as-a-problem.3784/post-53095>

WBC's tend to be low-normal, may not be out of the range but usually lower than 6 and into 5s or 4s if you have other autoimmune diseases or had Hashi for a while, then into 4s. The midpoint reference range for WBCs is around 7.3 for LabCorp. They may respond to treatment, however, so for untreated autoimmune conditions only, I believe, which Hashi is. If you have reports going back years, check and see if your WBC has been steadily falling.

> **Cavemam;53711 wrote:** Oooh, I've not heard this one before. . .how does the WBC relate to hashi's? (that does mean white blood cell right?) Also, if you care to share what ALT is. . .

Thanks mambo for the info.

### mambo — 2012-09-21T07:08:28+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/my-labs-show-sub-optimal-but-nothing-screams-out-at-me-as-a-problem.3784/post-53121>

Platelets could be affected, too (but more for other autoimmune conditios than Hashi's). I used to have them in the 300s. My latest test shows 190. Still within the reference range but approaching the low end of the range. If you notice it's taking an awful long time for paper cuts to heal, it could be your low platelets.

Another thing you wanna track is lymphs, absolute count. Many people with autoimmunity (esp. lupus) approach lymphocytopenia at

### Huck — 2012-09-20T22:57:52+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/blue-light-for-energy-anyone-using.3786/post-52989>

Since the days are getting shorter, I decided to order a blue light to see if it helps with my energy and mood. Here's what I ordered. [http://www.amazon.com/Philips-Hf3321-60-Golite-White/dp/B003XN9ZQQ/ref=sr\_1\_1?s=hpc&ie=UTF8&qid=1348170906&sr=1-1&keywords=Philips+Hf3321%2F60+Golite%2C+White+%2F+Blue](http://http://www.amazon.com/Philips-Hf3321-60-Golite-White/dp/B003XN9ZQQ/ref=sr_1_1?s=hpc&ie=UTF8&qid=1348170906&sr=1-1&keywords=Philips+Hf3321%2F60+Golite%2C+White+%2F+Blue)

Has anyone tried a blue light and did you get good results? I'm hoping I can use it 1st thing in the morning to simulate the sun.

### Souldanzer — 2012-09-21T00:37:11+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/blue-light-for-energy-anyone-using.3786/post-53036>

Dr K recently stated that basically all blue/artificial light is bad for us.

### kathylu — 2012-09-21T02:04:41+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/blue-light-for-energy-anyone-using.3786/post-53050>

Huck, I have one of these. I tried it last winter, 15 minutes first thing in the morning on dark days. I certainly felt better using it, but I had my first cortisol test during this period and found that my morning cortisol was waay high. It was 33.4 (range 9-22). I stopped using it and my morning cortisol has been around 16 since then. So, it certainly had an effect.

### Souldanzer — 2012-09-21T02:06:03+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/blue-light-for-energy-anyone-using.3786/post-53051>

That's interesting, Kathy. Another lesson what blue light does to us.... at night. I've noticed the tiniest bit will wake me up.

### Huck — 2012-09-21T02:18:16+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/blue-light-for-energy-anyone-using.3786/post-53054>

I certainly felt better using it, but I had my first cortisol test during this period and found that my morning cortisol was waay high. It was 33.4 (range 9-22). I stopped using it and my morning cortisol has been around 16 since then. So, it certainly had an effect.

My cortisol is low in the morning, so that is the main reason I am trying the light. I need lots of sun to feel good. Even an overcast day seems to deflate me.

### kathylu — 2012-09-21T02:46:27+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/blue-light-for-energy-anyone-using.3786/post-53061>

> **Huck;53689 wrote:** My cortisol is low in the morning, so that is the main reason I am trying the light. I need lots of sun to feel good. Even an overcast day seems to deflate me.

# Thread: Mammalian reflex saved a life
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/mammalian-reflex-saved-a-life.3789/>
**Subforum:** Cold Thermogenesis
**Posts:** 2
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### PrimalPam — 2012-09-20T23:16:42+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/mammalian-reflex-saved-a-life.3789/post-53002>

[http://www.mandatory.com/2012/09/20/10-unbelievable-medical-miracles/?icid=maing-grid7%7Cmain5%7Cdl2%7Csec1\_lnk2%26pLid%3D208345#photo=10](http://http://www.mandatory.com/2012/09/20/10-unbelievable-medical-miracles/?icid=maing-grid7%7Cmain5%7Cdl2%7Csec1_lnk2%26pLid%3D208345#photo=10)

Marine Boy

On December 4th, 1987, 11 year-old Alvaro Garza fell through some ice into the bone chilling waters of the Red River in North Dakota. Garza was underwater for 45 minutes before rescuers pulled him out. His survival is attributed to the actions of the paramedics and doctors who treated him and a phenomenon known as the mammalian reflex. Triggered by extreme cold, the mammalian reflex is an oxygen conserving response that shuts down all the bodyâ€™s non-vital functions preventing swelling in the brain and increasing the chances for a full recovery. Garza, now 36, lives with his wife and four children in Texas.

### nonchalant — 2012-09-21T00:17:19+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/mammalian-reflex-saved-a-life.3789/post-53028>

45 minutes, wow! Amazing. Sounds like if you have to drown, be sure to do it in icy water.

# Thread: Easy shrimp recipes, anyone?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/easy-shrimp-recipes-anyone.3791/>
**Subforum:** The Leptin Rx
**Posts:** 20
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### hope2conceive — 2012-09-21T00:16:10+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/easy-shrimp-recipes-anyone.3791/post-53027>

Anyone have any favorite shrimp recipes to share? I'm getting tired of my way, which is just shrimp and old bay seasoning......thanks in advance!

### Huck — 2012-09-21T00:43:24+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/easy-shrimp-recipes-anyone.3791/post-53040>

This is a real simple one I use for a change.

Lightly oil a frying pan. Salt and heavy pepper the shrimp. Place in frying pan, turn after a minute or 2. Eat. I usually dip in low carb ketchup/horseradish sauce.

### Shijin13 — 2012-09-21T00:52:34+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/easy-shrimp-recipes-anyone.3791/post-53043>

pan, shrimp, avocado oil, salt, garlic - saute together sever w/guacamole for dipping.. nom, nom, nom

or just cook the shrimp in xtra bacon fat - w/salt and garlic - drink the fat, after you've eaten all the shrimp

### debbiemikus — 2012-09-21T00:58:59+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/easy-shrimp-recipes-anyone.3791/post-53048>

How about a Thai style shrimp in coconut milk with red or green curry sauce? Very tasty!

### kathylu — 2012-09-21T01:59:18+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/easy-shrimp-recipes-anyone.3791/post-53049>

This is good. A little trouble, but has a crunchy coating, yum.

Coconut Shrimp

This sugar-free version of coconut shrimp can be used as an appetizer, party food, or main course. There is a little sweetener in the coating, because the popular restaurant versions are sweet, but this is optional. You may find that the Spicy Sweet Dipping Sauce has enough sweetness.

Yield: 3 Servings

Ingredients:

1 pound large raw shrimp, peeled and deveined (thaw if frozen)

1/3 cup coconut flour

1/4 teaspoon cayenne pepper, or 1 teaspoon ancho pepper

1/4 teaspoon black pepper

1 teaspoon salt

2 eggs

2 tablespoons water

Optional sugar substitute equal to one tablespoon sugar

1/2 cup shredded coconut - unsweetened

cooking oil of your choice

Preparation:

1\. Mix coconut flour with red and black peppers, and salt.

2\. Whisk the eggs with a fork in a small dish, and mix with the 2 tb water. Add sweetener if desired.

3\. Put shredded coconut in a separate dish.

4\. Put oil in a large skillet to about 3/4 inch depth. Heat to 350 to 360 degrees, or until the end of a wooden spoon handle dipped into the oil collects bubbles around it.

5\. Holding shrimp by the tail, roll in coconut flour, and shake to get most of it off - you just want a thin coating. Then dip in egg, again shaking off the excess. Finally, roll in coconut.

6\. Fry the shrimp until golden on each side, about 2 minutes per side. I usually put each in the pan as I prepare them, but you have to watch the ones in the pan closely if you do it this way. An alternative is to bread a few at once and then put them all in the pan at the same time. Don't crowd the pan, which will lower the temperature of the oil - this makes them absorb more oil and end up heavy and greasy. Tongs are the best tool for turning and removing the shrimp.

7\. Remove from the pan to a paper towel or cooling rack. Serve with sweet and spicy dipping sauce, if desired.

### diane — 2012-09-21T05:27:11+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/easy-shrimp-recipes-anyone.3791/post-53113>

I like a ginger lemon shrimp:

Heat bacon grease or olive oil in a pan.

Add ginger (or garlic), grated lemon rind from a 1/2 lemon. Squeeze lemon juice from the lemon into the pan.

Add shrimp and sautÃ© until cooked through.

### Rosekelly — 2012-09-21T07:11:02+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/easy-shrimp-recipes-anyone.3791/post-53122>

Avocado Shrimp Salad: I just made this today because I had a ripe avocado and wondered what interesting thing I might do with it, and I also bought some fresh jumbo shrimp and thought the same thing. So I boiled the shrimp for 3-5 minutes, then rinsed them in cold water and removed the shells, then cut the shrimp meat up and mixed it in a bowl with the diced avocado, along with some olive oil, salt and pepper. YUMMMM!!!

### Shijin13 — 2012-09-23T03:54:01+0300

> **luvmyzoe;54154 wrote:** hahahaahh! DRINK THE FAT!!! HAHAHAH! I love you, Shinjin!!

### hope2conceive — 2012-09-23T15:57:31+0300

Wow these all sound great. And Shijin13 - that is awesome about the FB posts...those are the best. Make people think  especially those afraid of heart attacks!

### Rick — 2012-09-23T16:43:14+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/easy-shrimp-recipes-anyone.3791/post-53588>

My 2 favorite shrimp recipes:

Thai style shrimp appetizer: marinate bite sized cooked shrimp pieces with olive oil, limejuice, galangal (ginger), hot Thai chiles if desired and Thai basil (cilantro) and fish sauce for salt.

Make a salad of diced avocado, orange and or grapefruit segments and congealed coconut milk (coco cream sort of).

Then spoon the "salad" into large chilled martini glasses, spoon the marinated shrimp over the top and garnish with more basil or cilantro.

Italian style shrimp dinner: Saute lots of sliced garlic in lard until fragrant add some cleaned shrimp and crushed red chiles if desired. When the shrimp are nearly done, raise the heat and quickly deglaze with a little OJ, add some sea salt and stir in some fresh chopped tarragon and mint. Plate the finished dish and drizzle with some frutata EVOO...

Chef Rick

### hope2conceive — 2012-09-23T16:46:32+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/easy-shrimp-recipes-anyone.3791/post-53589>

Hmm...that all sounds yummy too, Rick! Love it - thanks to you - and all - so much!! I am SO thankful for this....and everything else on here

### hope2conceive — 2012-09-23T16:47:24+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/easy-shrimp-recipes-anyone.3791/post-53590>

PS. Rick, what is frutata EVOO? (the frutata part) - where do I get it, or how do I make it?

### Endless — 2012-09-23T18:52:32+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/easy-shrimp-recipes-anyone.3791/post-53610>

Hmmm...the Thai basil I use is definitely not cilantro. But either would be tasty!

### Rick — 2012-09-23T21:23:32+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/easy-shrimp-recipes-anyone.3791/post-53670>

Hi guys, frutata just means extra-fruity low acid EVOO that you would NOT use for cooking but only for dipping and garnishing and the other ingredients in parens were meant as a substitute in case some folks don't have access to Thai ingredients u can sub ginger for galangal and cilantro for Thai basil....sorry 4 the confusion!

### hope2conceive — 2012-11-10T02:02:46+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/easy-shrimp-recipes-anyone.3791/post-63249>

Hmmmm....that horseradish sauce is great. Do you make it yourself?

### LinD — 2012-11-10T02:59:27+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/easy-shrimp-recipes-anyone.3791/post-63258>

Subscribing to this thread!

Great ideas. Thank you.

### Endless — 2012-11-10T04:55:49+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/easy-shrimp-recipes-anyone.3791/post-63293>

I like the large (16-20 per pound) pacific white shrimp. I will heat avocado oil and/or bacon grease, add the shrimp and sort of 'sear' it with garlic, fresh ground pepper, turmeric and fresh garam masala. They get a nice spice crust on the outside and a gorgeous colour from the turmeric!

### PrimalPam — 2012-11-10T05:34:08+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/easy-shrimp-recipes-anyone.3791/post-63299>

I make this about 3 times a week:

Salt & pepper the shrimp

Heat olive oil or CO oil (CO is better for cooking)

Saute tons of garlic, basil, oregano, etc.

Add shrimp

Cut an avocado in half, slice into pieces. Add to plate: Sauerkraut, sliced pickles. Eat all up.

Edit: Oh, I re-read the thread, and I wasn't the only one with this recipe. Must be good.

# Thread: Flux question
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/flux-question.3795/>
**Subforum:** Beginners Area
**Posts:** 13
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### hope2conceive — 2012-09-21T02:35:05+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/flux-question.3795/post-53057>

Hello...any of you flux users, ever had a problem with the software? Mine doesn't turn colors, and I can't uninstall or delete the app because it's telling me the app is OPEN when it's not working. Ugh!

Has this ever happened to anyone else? I can't seem to get an answer from the company....

### mafixit — 2012-09-21T03:14:17+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/flux-question.3795/post-53066>

Mine seems to be working fine.

### jeanie@truelongmont.com — 2012-09-21T03:32:13+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/flux-question.3795/post-53077>

I had that on an old computer - I think since it is running all the time it can suck down too much mem or speed and won't go right.

### Michael — 2012-09-21T08:49:03+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/flux-question.3795/post-53134>

> **hope2conceive;53692 wrote:** Hello...any of you flux users, ever had a problem with the software? Mine doesn't turn colors, and I can't uninstall or delete the app because it's telling me the app is OPEN when it's not working. Ugh!
>
>
>
> Has this ever happened to anyone else? I can't seem to get an answer from the company....

Yeah, it would have to be running on start-up. So it will always be going in the background: I guess it would never be _not_ working, in that sense, although it might not be doing much most of the time.

And because of that you couldn't uninstall it until you explicitly quit it.

I have it on my Mac. I guess you'll be using Windows. You could perhaps try re-booting. Alternatively, you could call up a list of the running processes, see if you can identify the process and kill it from there. Then you could re-start the program manually.

Control + Alt + Delete to call up the Task Manager, isn't it? Someone here will probably know the name of the process, although it may have an obvious enough name.

### x_danny_x — 2012-09-21T10:39:53+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/flux-question.3795/post-53137>

go to device manager or go to the start up menu by typing msconfig and just disable it from starting at the beginning. Afterwards then delete/uninstall it. This is assuming you have a Windows computer and not a MAC.

### Optimalbound — 2012-09-23T20:29:17+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/flux-question.3795/post-53656>

Has anybody gotten f.lux to work properly on a dual-screen Linux computer? It only works on one of my two screens.

I know this is a very long shot, but I thought I'd ask, anyway.

### Michael — 2012-09-24T10:30:47+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/flux-question.3795/post-53762>

I don't know the answer, but by golly that's an impressive question.

### Optimalbound — 2012-09-24T22:33:53+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/flux-question.3795/post-53892>

### Michael — 2012-09-25T08:18:27+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/flux-question.3795/post-54064>

> **Optimalbound;54563 wrote:** You're being sarcastic, right?

### Optimalbound — 2012-09-25T09:29:19+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/flux-question.3795/post-54068>

### KetoWarrior — 2012-11-19T08:24:59+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/flux-question.3795/post-65287>

> **Optimalbound;54298 wrote:** Has anybody gotten f.lux to work properly on a dual-screen Linux computer? It only works on one of my two screens.
>
>
>
> I know this is a very long shot, but I thought I'd ask, anyway.

After two months, I understand I'm reanimating a zombie thread and apologize but thought the usefulness of this info might outweigh the breech of forum etiquette.

I run dual-screen setup on Debian testing with XFCE and KDE window environments. I could not get F.Lux to work properly but have had great results with [Redshift](http://http://lifehacker.com/5567172/redshift-makes-your-screen-easier-on-the-eyes-at-night) which does the same thing and is available for a number of distributions. See [http://jonls.dk/redshift/](http://http://jonls.dk/redshift/)

### Optimalbound — 2012-11-19T11:47:31+0200

# Thread: Who's Taking Alpha Lipoic Acid and How Much?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/whos-taking-alpha-lipoic-acid-and-how-much.3796/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 7
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### mambo — 2012-09-21T03:54:25+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/whos-taking-alpha-lipoic-acid-and-how-much.3796/post-53092>

I've been taking 600mg of Alpha Lipoic Acid (ALA) daily for 2 years for my neuropathy (diabetes-related and suspected carpal tunnel syndrome). My neurologist at the time suggested that I could take more, if I wanted to. I also read about a clinical trial in Germany where neuropathy was rolled back somewhat after administering mega doses (don't know how much) of ALA intravenously.

After recent flare-ups, I upped my dose to 900mg for a week and then tried to go up to 1,200mg. Then I came upon an interview with Dr. Burton Berkson, who pioneered the use of Low Dose Naltrexone (LDN) and ALA in cancer treatment. [Dr. Berkson says that at very high doses of ALA, monkeys have died because their mitochondria "fell apart". He says 90 mg/kg could kill a primate. As for humans, he's never gone above 15mg per kilogram.](http://http://www.prescription2000.com/Interview-Transcripts/2010-01-22-burt-berkson-lipoic-acid-naltrexone-transcript.html)

So if you weigh 160 lbs., your upper limit is 160lbs. = 72.6kg; 15 x 72.6 = 1,089mg. That's less than 1,200mg! If you are female and weigh 110 lbs., the upper limit is 750mg. As for the lethal dose that could wreck one's mitochondria, it's 4,500mg.

Now, that seems very hard to believe. ALA comes in capsules of either 300 or 600mg per pill. If true, many people are dosing above what the Father of ALA says is the ceiling.

So what then is the optimal dose if you want to treat neuropathy? Cancer? Autoimmune diseases? Anyone gone above 1,200mg and seen any side effects?

### Souldanzer — 2012-09-21T04:55:52+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/whos-taking-alpha-lipoic-acid-and-how-much.3796/post-53108>

Well I had no idea about this.... I think I take 500mg twice a day. For months now.

### JerryB6768 — 2012-09-25T23:31:36+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/whos-taking-alpha-lipoic-acid-and-how-much.3796/post-54250>

It depends on the dosage form of the ALA product. With tablets and powder-filled capsules, the ALA is poorly absorbed and taking above 600 mg is probably not a concern. I personally use a solubilzed product called ThioGel that has a 200-300% higher bioavailability than powdered products.

### shah78 — 2012-09-25T23:38:42+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/whos-taking-alpha-lipoic-acid-and-how-much.3796/post-54251>

Question for jerry and Mambo? Do you have amalgam fillings? I'm chelating mercury out of my brain (Cutler Protocol) and I can't take more than 50 mg per day and I have to divide the dose into 6 mg. servings every three hours or I'll get wicked side effects. How do you take such massive amounts.I'm utterly amazed!

### mambo — 2012-10-11T01:24:19+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/whos-taking-alpha-lipoic-acid-and-how-much.3796/post-57467>

Go to Swanson or iHerb and any ALA you see come in 300mg or 600mg per capsule. Where do you purchase 50mg doses?

> **shah78;54957 wrote:** Question for jerry and Mambo? Do you have amalgam fillings? I'm chelating mercury out of my brain (Cutler Protocol) and I can't take more than 50 mg per day and I have to divide the dose into 6 mg. servings every three hours or I'll get wicked side effects. How do you take such massive amounts.I'm utterly amazed!

### shah78 — 2012-10-11T01:39:24+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/whos-taking-alpha-lipoic-acid-and-how-much.3796/post-57468>

@MAMBO: 21st century pills from swanson.

### mambo — 2012-10-11T07:08:37+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/whos-taking-alpha-lipoic-acid-and-how-much.3796/post-57533>

I guess I don't know the difference between regular Alpha Lipoic Acid and R-Fraction Alpha Lipoic Acid. I always took the regular one ranging from 300 to 600mg. I'm now on 300mg. Did it help my neuropathy? Don't think so. But then supplements are supposed to let things not get worse, not better. So it's preventive, not ameliorative. By that standard, I really can't judge the effectiveness of Alpha Lipoic Acid. I only recently heard about its use for cancer patients and the combo therapy with LDN.

> **shah78;58778 wrote:** @MAMBO: 21st century pills from swanson.

# Thread: Cold Tub options
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cold-tub-options.3800/>
**Subforum:** Cold Thermogenesis
**Posts:** 3
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### edwardfries@netcommander.com — 2012-09-21T08:35:52+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cold-tub-options.3800/post-53133>

I'm new here and I really want to get started on the cold thermogenesis. I tried to search for a topic like this so I'm sorry if this was already answered because I didn't think of the right key words to try. The house I'm in has showers with things that look like tubs, but are not really tubs since to lay flat your chest is at the upper end of the sides on the supposed tubs so immersion of the torso is not possible in either shower. I guess this is from cheaper installs in houses where people mostly shower these days. At the coldest the water never hits less than 70 degrees so a cold shower is not an option either. I would gladly by an outdoor cold tub but I found only one on the internet that was specifically designed to be a cold tub.

[http://www.coldtub.com/](http://http://www.coldtub.com/)

It looks incredible and I was thinking if it was maybe 5 thousand I would do it, 10 thousand would be hurting but possible. It was twice that expensive so I don't know how easy it may be to make a jacuzzi into a cold tub or what other options you guys may have but I would love to hear them

PS I live in Texas so it doesn't get real cold here except maybe 2 weeks out of the year, that's why I was going for something that could cool the water instead of buying lots and lots of ice daily.

### Ray — 2012-09-21T19:46:23+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cold-tub-options.3800/post-53230>

People are buying the Rubbermaid stock tanks and using ice from their freezer to bring down the temperature as necessary. Some have rigged up refrigeration units, but it seems like just throwing some ice in would be cheaper and not too hard.

Here is a thread on this site with some info: [http://forum.jackkruse.com/showthread.php?990-Stock-tank-tips-amp-tricks](http://http://forum.jackkruse.com/showthread.php?990-Stock-tank-tips-amp-tricks)

### Patty Cakes — 2012-09-21T20:14:36+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cold-tub-options.3800/post-53237>

I like this tub. It's like a hot tub for the cold....a very large ice chest in the shape of a circle. I want one. I'm still struggling to get my whole body in the tub. It won't fit! SO I alternate baths one day it's my torso and upper legs and the next day it's legs and lower torso. Not happy about this method...
