# Thread: Question about oysters
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/question-about-oysters.3802/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 23
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### BJK77 — 2012-09-21T15:29:48+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/question-about-oysters.3802/post-53150>

Oysters have never been my thing before. I have always loved seafood, but I stuck mostly to fish, shrimp, crab, etc. I know I desperately need the ugly little oysters, but have a few questions about eating them.

Since raw is so much better, is it ok to eat already shucked oysters raw? I'm thinking of these: [http://www.ilovebluesea.com/shuck-oysters-tub?acc=a5771bce93e200c36f7cd9dfd0e5deaa](http://http://www.ilovebluesea.com/shuck-oysters-tub?acc=a5771bce93e200c36f7cd9dfd0e5deaa)

I ordered them before and they seemed to be of good quality (based on my limited knowledge), but I was lightly cooking most of them. I think I did eat a few raw, but then I read something on here about not eating frozen oysters raw. These were never frozen though, so I just want some opinions on what all you seafood experts think.

I do plan on freezing these for later use though as it's a good bit cheaper to order 3 containers and get free shipping. So, what is the opinion then on whether or not they can be eaten raw after I freeze them? Last time I put about 10-20 in a small baggie, froze them and then just thawed and ate throughout a day or two.

### jeanie@truelongmont.com — 2012-09-21T17:19:33+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/question-about-oysters.3802/post-53176>

My advice is to "start" your oyster journey with freshly shucked SMALL ones. I think this time of year you can get littler ones. The big once can be overwhelming in the "omg I'm eating snot"department. The little ones, easy to suck down from the shell without chewing.

My fish guy told me with lots of emphasis. . .DONT EAT FROZEN RAW. I didn't ask if it was just a foodie thing, or what - just took that advice to heart.

### MamaGrok — 2012-09-21T17:31:37+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/question-about-oysters.3802/post-53184>

Is that a safety thing? Or texture? Because most of the time, freezing is recommended for eating other foods raw, to destroy parasites and such.

I have ZERO access to fresh oysters, that I am aware of.

### ealachan — 2012-09-21T17:31:41+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/question-about-oysters.3802/post-53185>

Yeah, not a good idea to eat pre-shucked previously frozen oysters raw. The idea is that raw ones you shuck yourself have their own liquor in them and are super fresh, where as the frozen ones have been sitting around intermingling with a bunch of other oysters' liquor and just aren't the freshest thing ever. It's kind of akin to eating fresh home-made beef tartar or carpaccio (really good, not likely to make you sick) vs. eating raw ground beef that came from a processing place 3 states away (gods only know what's actually in it, not a good idea to eat raw).

### ealachan — 2012-09-21T17:33:41+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/question-about-oysters.3802/post-53186>

> **MamaGrok;53819 wrote:** Is that a safety thing? Or texture? Because most of the time, freezing is recommended for eating other foods raw, to destroy parasites and such.
>
>
>
> I have ZERO access to fresh oysters, that I am aware of.

Safety. You can tell a sick / dead oyster when you shuck it. If it was a machine or a person who maybe didn't care so much when they were shucking a whole bunch of oysters to meet a daily quota, you never know quite what you might be getting.

### Souldanzer — 2012-09-21T17:37:49+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/question-about-oysters.3802/post-53189>

I've been eating oysters like crazy for a few months and my spectracell just showed a very good level of zinc!! No zinc supps at all at any time

### MamaGrok — 2012-09-21T17:44:46+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/question-about-oysters.3802/post-53194>

So if the idea is never to eat an oyster raw that you don't shuck yourself, I suppose that's the same as saying never to eat them raw in a restaurant?

I suppose having a good source (like the equivalent of US Wellness Meats) would be an exception, then?

I don't avoid beef carpaccio or raw oysters in restaurants, and never get sick.

### jeanie@truelongmont.com — 2012-09-21T17:46:49+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/question-about-oysters.3802/post-53195>

> **MamaGrok;53829 wrote:** So if the idea is never to eat an oyster raw that you don't shuck yourself, I suppose that's the same as saying never to eat them raw in a restaurant?
>
>
>
> I suppose having a good source (like the equivalent of US Wellness Meats) would be an exception, then?
>
>
>
> I don't avoid beef carpaccio or raw oysters in restaurants, and never get sick.

I have never had an oyster sickness - and I eat a ton of oysters, always have. In restaurants I order when they shuck them there.

I have learned that stores like WF and even seafood vendors have to have the damn things flown in. I think the idea is as long as they are alive you are good to go.

### ealachan — 2012-09-21T17:52:38+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/question-about-oysters.3802/post-53199>

> **MamaGrok;53829 wrote:** So if the idea is never to eat an oyster raw that you don't shuck yourself, I suppose that's the same as saying never to eat them raw in a restaurant?
>
>
>
> I suppose having a good source (like the equivalent of US Wellness Meats) would be an exception, then?
>
>
>
> I don't avoid beef carpaccio or raw oysters in restaurants, and never get sick.

Well, no. The idea is that, in a restaurant, you trust that they know what they're doing and they're preparing things correctly. I eat sashimi and raw oysters in restaurants and have never had a problem, but I'm theoretically paying extra to have someone who knows what they're doing preparing them for me.

### MamaGrok — 2012-09-21T18:06:35+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/question-about-oysters.3802/post-53203>

> I think the idea is as long as they are alive you are good to go.

Aha! Now I understand! Thank you! So if you eat raw oysters, they should be alive shortly before prepared and eaten?

Smoked oysters in a can would be fine, then, since I assume they're not raw?

### jeanie@truelongmont.com — 2012-09-21T18:16:40+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/question-about-oysters.3802/post-53206>

yups! ....

### kathylu — 2012-09-21T20:15:39+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/question-about-oysters.3802/post-53238>

Oysters need to be freshly harvested and shucked and kept very cold (on ice) to eat them raw. Never from the jars of previously shucked raw oysters! I'll only eat them raw from a restaurant I trust and on the few occasions when we dredge for oysters ourselves in the winter (I live near the Chesapeake Bay...great oyster beds!).

### cuivie@me.com — 2012-09-21T20:36:53+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/question-about-oysters.3802/post-53245>

I buy the Ekone brand shucked (but not frozen) oysters from Whole Foods and eat at least 30oz (3 jars) of them a week--always raw. I also eat 3 lbs/week farmed rainbow trout or wild Alaskan salmon from Sprouts sashimi-style raw, even after days of open air dry-aging in my refrigerator right next to my grassfed chuck steak that gets the same treatment (and gets eaten the same way--raw). I have never gotten sick from eating all this raw seafood (or beef or offal).

I have, however, gotten violently ill from well-cooked supermarket ground beef before.

Google "raw paleo" and check out the forums of people who have eaten raw or raw paleo for decades and never get sick. It's not about the presence of germs or viruses, it's about the state of your body and gut flora.

Perhaps I've just gotten extremely lucky with my raw seafood (and raw beef and raw offal) up to now, perhaps my gut flora is much healthier since the ground beef episode in my pre-Kruse days (blech.....I will never touch ground beef again, ever...\*vomit\*), or maybe my zonulin-powered leaky gut is absorbing ocean viruses as we speak to give me and my future progeny a wider library of epigenetic programs to choose from to adapt to coming environments.

### Souldanzer — 2012-09-21T21:18:24+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/question-about-oysters.3802/post-53257>

>

I recently had food poisoning. I don't know what item was to blame. I had a gag reaction to everything basically that was protein afterwards. But I'm noticing that the thing I'm having the most avoidance right now is not the fish, raw fish, raw frozen oysters that I ate that day...... it's the cooked liver I had for dinner.

### Endless — 2012-09-22T06:21:58+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/question-about-oysters.3802/post-53365>

I will stick to eating oysters raw only if they are alive...not going to risk the pre-shucked ones. That would have been the only way our predecessors ate them. Not like a mammoth, that you'd have to store and eat more slowly ;-)

### cuivie@me.com — 2012-09-22T08:37:10+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/question-about-oysters.3802/post-53369>

Your concern is understandable, and certainly prudent. Though as many raw foodists would point out, at the time period Epi-Paleo is targeting, our ancestors wouldn't have eaten cooked anything, ever.

If whole, unshucked oysters weren't cost prohibitive at the amounts we eat, I would certainly be eating them instead. All I financially have access to is the Ekone brand canned (plastic jar, actually), but they seem very fresh. Ekone is a small company with less than 50 employees, so I trust their QC far more than a bigger, higher-volume producer. I'm lucky with that, living in SoCal and the oysters getting here overnight from Washington state, and never frozen. If you live further inland, then getting shucked oysters fresh might not be possible.

### SeaHorse — 2012-09-22T15:05:55+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/question-about-oysters.3802/post-53379>

Just checked their website and they only ship smoked produce if you order on line...lots of other ingredients in the smoked oysters so it won't work for me. You ARE lucky living so close to them.

I'm finding that the cheapest oysters I can buy fresh are 1.25 each. But it adds up if you really want to chow down on them 2 or 3 times a week. Still, Vermont is in a pretty good spot as far as seafood with Maine just over the mountains, so I won't complain.

### ealachan — 2012-09-22T16:40:05+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/question-about-oysters.3802/post-53397>

> **CuiviÃ«;54004 wrote:** Your concern is understandable, and certainly prudent. Though as many raw foodists would point out, at the time period Epi-Paleo is targeting, our ancestors wouldn't have eaten cooked anything, ever.

True, but our ancestors would have been cracking their own oysters open right on the beach and eating them straight from the shells, not getting them pre-shucked from plastic containers that had sat around under undetermined conditions for an undetermined amount of time.

### Endless — 2012-09-22T19:44:31+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/question-about-oysters.3802/post-53431>

I wish I had better access to live oysters....I would love to eat them every day. I do buy frozen oysters and eat them very lightly steamed as often as possible.

I'm lucky with that, living in SoCal and the oysters getting here overnight from Washington state, and never frozen. ### cuivie@me.com — 2012-09-24T19:05:40+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/question-about-oysters.3802/post-53822>

> **Patty Cakes;54078 wrote:** Have you tried their smoked oysters?

No, haven't. My whole all-raw kick was about concern over AGEs and other carcinogen by-products of cooking, at up until recently I was eating 1-2lbs of grilled/seared steak a day. So now that I have gone raw I have avoided anything smoked.

It sounds delicious, though. I LOVE the taste of seared and smoked meat. Alas, for now I need to avoid such things. Perhaps every now and then as a treat.

### cuivie@me.com — 2012-09-24T19:42:22+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/question-about-oysters.3802/post-53834>

> **ealachan;54036 wrote:** True, but our ancestors would have been cracking their own oysters open right on the beach and eating them straight from the shells, not getting them pre-shucked from plastic containers that had sat around under undetermined conditions for an undetermined amount of time.

Again, good point. But this is the only financially un-insane access to regular amounts of oysters, and given that raw seafood is best for promoting the gut flora response we want for Epi-Paleo/EPCOTx (as well as the fact I love raw), I'm willing to take the risk to eat them raw. Whole Foods has good storage/handling practices (or so they claim) and I always buy the jars that are the most covered in ice and avoid any that look oxidized (turned pinkish instead of the usual grey/white).

Raw is still more evolutionarily appropriate, but like you said, with modern food access realities, there are added elements that we can't control. It will have to be a personal decision.

Personally, I have never gotten sick since going raw, nor since eating both steak and seafood seared/blue from quality sources. I have gotten very sick from well-cooked food from conventional sources (traditional supermarkets, restaurants). FWIW, I wouldn't dare eat anything raw--seafood or otherwise--from a conventional grocery store.

Pre-existing gut health may also be a factor. I was strict paleo for nearly 2 years before I went rare, 3 years with lots of pro-biotic usage before going all-raw.

I had some cooked wild Alaskan salmon last night and got really nauseated, borderline having to vomit. Never once, ever, got sick eating it raw. I fear the heat oxidized the PUFAs (including our sought-after DHA) and made it rancid.

Eating this particular brand from this particular store raw happens to work for me. YMMV with different brands, store practices, geographic location, or gut health.

### ealachan — 2012-09-24T20:00:19+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/question-about-oysters.3802/post-53839>

Sure, I was just playing devil's advocate, mostly. It's an annoying habit I picked up from my husband...

FWIW, I've never gotten sick from eating anything raw either (knock on wood). I routinely munch on pieces of raw (supermarket) beef while I'm prepping steaks for dinner, and I always have my steak blue in the middle as well. I also eat raw uncured bacon sometimes (weird I know, but I like it for some strange reason, always have), and have no problem tasting my meatloaf, meatball or sausage mix raw to make sure I like the seasoning, as well. Aside from undercooked salmon and rare tuna steak, I've yet to do raw fish at home, but I have no issues with it in a restaurant setting and would happily make my own sashimi at home if I had half a clue what I was doing!


══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# Thread: Food allergies, desensitization, reactivity
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/food-allergies-desensitization-reactivity.3803/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 3
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### bigknitwit — 2012-09-21T15:46:44+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/food-allergies-desensitization-reactivity.3803/post-53155>

I thought I'd post an interesting story and see what people say about it.

A friend of mine's son is allergic to peanuts, and she is very strict about keeping him (and her house, and his classroom) nut free. He also has significant environmental allergies, and other lower level food allergies (egg, tree nuts, maybe more). The boy eats an "upgraded" SAD. Cereal for breakfast, regular lunch/dinners, although she does her best to buy organically, and most of their meats come from pastured animals.

1.5 years ago, she began the boy on a peanut desensitization trial program. Every day, he consumed an increasing (dose increases every 2 weeks, symptom dependant) amount of peanut, from 3mg/day all the way up to 3 peanuts/day. The goal was never to have him eating peanuts again, but to be able to handle living in a non-nut-free environment - handle cross contamination with reduced risk of anaphylactic reaction. He did pretty well on the protocol, but was never able to get beyond 2 peanuts/day without having reactions after each dose (small reactions, but reactions none the less). The protocol had him eating tons of foods (starches) before the dose, and stay still and quiet for 2 hours afterwards.

I have been thinking about this a great deal. I think they want the kids still and quiet to keep cortisol levels from increasing. Doesn't increasing cortisol increase gut leakiness? She found that even playing video games was too much during this time and would lead to more reactivity. What is the rationale behind having them eat so much starchy food beforehand? She said the straches seem to work like a barrier in the stomach, buffering the dose from the gut.

How much would an epi-paleo diet improve the success rate for these kinds of kids? I suppose all other foods put the cells in a more inflammatory path, leading to chronic oxidation. This boy was not able to move past 2 peanuts, and has stayed that dose for 6ish months. Another boy had been doing the maintenance dose for many months but had an anaphylactic reaction one evening from the dose. He had sustained an injury while wrestling earlier in the day, and had taken ibuprofen. NSAIDs also increase gut leakiness, I think, which would lead to more peanut getting through the brush border..? I wonder if having good HDL levels would also be a predictor of better tolerance? Vitamin D levels would also be important, but I'm not sure I exactly know why, except that it's an immune system modulator.

I'm just trying to put the biology pieces together here to have a solid hypothesis about what's going on, things the parents could do to help the situation, and why those things would help.

### Souldanzer — 2012-09-21T17:19:51+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/food-allergies-desensitization-reactivity.3803/post-53177>

Not much input here but wanted to say that playing video games imo is one of the worse things to do for managing cortisol so I"m not really surprised he didn't do well with that. People \*think\* they are sitting still and resting, but they don't take into account the stuff that happens not so obviously while playing these things. Lots of cortisol and adrenaline flowing from the game and wanting to win and stuff and those stress hormones have nowhere to go b/c your sitting still, a very unnatural combination for our bodies.... we're supposed to move when stress hormones are up.

The other thing is that if the boy had an injury that required pain med treatment that also means his cortisol/adrenaline was already elevated that day, so the peanut dose could have pushed things over the limit, pain meds or not.

### bigknitwit — 2012-09-21T19:11:16+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/food-allergies-desensitization-reactivity.3803/post-53218>

> **Souldanzer;53812 wrote:** Not much input here but wanted to say that playing video games imo is one of the worse things to do for managing cortisol so I"m not really surprised he didn't do well with that. People \*think\* they are sitting still and resting, but they don't take into account the stuff that happens not so obviously while playing these things. Lots of cortisol and adrenaline flowing from the game and wanting to win and stuff and those stress hormones have nowhere to go b/c your sitting still, a very unnatural combination for our bodies.... we're supposed to move when stress hormones are up.
>
>
>
> The other thing is that if the boy had an injury that required pain med treatment that also means his cortisol/adrenaline was already elevated that day, so the peanut dose could have pushed things over the limit, pain meds or not.

I fully agree with you here about the exercise injury already elevating his cortisol and therefore increasing his gut leakiness.

What I wonder about is how helpful these kinds of desensitization treatments are. They have the potential to improve quality of life (child can now fly on an airplane, etc), but I wonder about the biological cost. Is the child now living "even more" in the oxidation pathway, with increased inflammation and pregnenelone being shunted to cortisol, etc? Or after a time, does the immune system learn to accept what it used to attack so ferociously? What other markers could be used to screen a child as a good (or not appropriate) candidate for this kind of protocol? What supplements (or foods) would increase the likelihood of success in this kind of a program. Nobody wants to feed their child 2 peanuts everyday to improve quality of life now, only to find out later on that bigger problems have been the "side effect." It's a difficult place to be as a parent.

At the same time, this mother has said she would feed her son fish everyday, and that at one point he was eating 2 cans of sardines/day, and now he refuses fish of all kind. With an allergic kid, this is very difficult. I have a child who refuses a lot of different foods (mostly fruits/veggies), and for years I would push on it (somewhat). Later, intradermal allergy testing showed he reacted to a lot of them. He is very sensitive, and intuitively knows what he should eat, and what he shouldn't (also gravitates heavily to fish and beef, not oysters, sadly). I try to respect what he himself knows, but it would be much more difficult it the foods he was refusing were the very ones I thought might stand a chance to heal him (the seafoods).


══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# Thread: Tell me about your liver.   Not the one you eat (I hope).

### jeanie@truelongmont.com — 2012-09-21T17:26:32+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/tell-me-about-your-liver-not-the-one-you-eat-i-hope.3805/post-53182>

I've seen posts about monitoring liver function. I'm clueless on this. Discuss?

### finnite@dccnet.com — 2012-09-21T17:39:31+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/tell-me-about-your-liver-not-the-one-you-eat-i-hope.3805/post-53190>

AST and ALT are serum tests that can help you monitor your liver function. elevated levels can be signs of non-alcoholic fatty liver disease or simply show the liver is not functioning at optimum levels.

### Patty Cakes — 2012-09-21T20:22:18+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/tell-me-about-your-liver-not-the-one-you-eat-i-hope.3805/post-53242>

There is also the liver detox phase 1 & 2 testing to dtermine liver functionality in detoxification. I know nothing about this aspect either. Anyone please share.

### AKMan — 2012-09-21T21:48:24+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/tell-me-about-your-liver-not-the-one-you-eat-i-hope.3805/post-53264>

ALT and AST seem to be a standard lab. Back in my SAD days, these were both elevated slightly out of range. Ranges for ALT are 30-65 and AST are 50-136.

As I progressed further into metabolic syndrome, gaining weight, my triglycerides went up to 400's, TSH was 45, ALT was 100, AST was 150.

I went for an ultrasound of my liver, and they said I had non-alcoholic liver disease (NALD). I was on meds for trigs, chol, bp, hypothyroid at the time. Nothing changed until I started eating paleo--all this went away within a year of ditching grains, sugar, veg oil. ALT now 48, AST now 20.

### jeanie@truelongmont.com — 2012-09-21T21:50:26+0300

> **Cavemam;53900 wrote:** Woah! Now that is some amazing info, so nice to see success!

I knew I had a liver problem because my liver actually hurt. I could feel it when I was sitting, and I couldn't sleep on my stomach or right side. I figured it was liver cancer or something, so I just ignored it since I didn't want chemo...crazy, I know, but that's how men think. Glad it was just diet-related and easily reversed.

### docwatt@gmail.com — 2012-09-22T01:46:24+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/tell-me-about-your-liver-not-the-one-you-eat-i-hope.3805/post-53330>

Actually AST and ALT are not measures of liver function. They are enzymes AST (aspartate amino transferase) and ALT (alanine amino transferase) normally in the liver cells. Cell damage leads to leakage so the serum levels rise. Liver function can be arrived at by looking at levels of serum proteins, ammonia level, or a bromosulphalein excretion test.

### Joann — 2012-09-22T03:07:11+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/tell-me-about-your-liver-not-the-one-you-eat-i-hope.3805/post-53345>

alkaline phosphatase is another enzyme looked at for liver

### Rosekelly — 2012-09-24T19:19:29+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/tell-me-about-your-liver-not-the-one-you-eat-i-hope.3805/post-53827>

> **Patty Cakes;53877 wrote:** There is also the liver detox phase 1 & 2 testing to dtermine liver functionality in detoxification. I know nothing about this aspect either. Anyone please share.

I had this done recently, and just went over the results with my doctor last week. It's a bit overwhelming, due to the scientific lingo and terms, and I'm still absorbing it. It's an expensive test but only needs to be done once since genes don't change over time. However, as we all know here from Dr. Kruse, the genes' _expression_ can change. I proudly offered the word "epigenetics" when my doc began to explain how we can compensate for a defective gene.

So it turns out I'm missing one gene entirely: GSTM1, a glutathione gene. Glutathione is the body's strongest anti-oxidant and protects against cancer. This is very helpful to know, as I've had cancer twice.

I also have a few defective genes, two of which, CYP1B1 and COMT, signify impaired estrogen metabolism. This means my doc will not prescribe BHRT for me, although if I lose more weight she may consider it, since right now my fat cells are over-producing estrogen, and it's not being properly metabolized. This is also a cancer risk.

I also have defects in NAT2 (cancer risk), GSTP1 (also glutathione-related), and SOD2 (mitochondria)

For each defect, the health risks and ways to minimize risk are included. For example, for me, smoking is strongly discouraged. It says I'm at much higher risk than most people from smoking. So it's good I quit! But I should also avoid charbroiled food and eat tons of cruciferous veggies (so strongly recommended throughout the test that my doc suggests I take a good broccoli seed supplement to really flood my system with all that veggie goodness).

She also told me to take NAC, sulforaphane, and consider some type of glutathione supplementation, depending on my ability to afford it, as there are several types, such as foot creams that are compounded by a pharmacy and even something you breathe into your lungs every day. I believe I read on the forum that CT improves glutathione production, which I hope is true. If so, I've already made progress on upping my glutathione.

So I consider the test very worthwhile. It cost $470 plus $50 for the blood draw. It took me a while to come up with the money, but I'm glad I did.

### Patty Cakes — 2012-09-24T20:01:07+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/tell-me-about-your-liver-not-the-one-you-eat-i-hope.3805/post-53841>

Thank you RoseKelly. That's a lot of info in that test. Thanks for putting it down here for all of us. I wonder if the genes would show up on the raw data of the 23andme test. I have that one already done.

You may want to look into DIM for some "veggie goodness"! It helps with metabolizing of estrogen as well.

### Rosekelly — 2012-09-24T20:13:38+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/tell-me-about-your-liver-not-the-one-you-eat-i-hope.3805/post-53848>

Thanks, Patty. My doc mentioned DIM but recommended sulforaphane as even stronger. I was just about to order it a few minutes ago, and I discovered it actually is the broccoli sprout extract I should be taking. I thought they were two different things. Not.

But yeah, DIM helps estrogen metabolism too, so why not take that too?

Now to budget all this!

> **Patty Cakes;54504 wrote:** Thank you RoseKelly. That's a lot of info in that test. Thanks for putting it down here for all of us. I wonder if the genes would show up on the raw data of the 23andme test. I have that one already done.

### chewingthefat — 2012-09-21T19:30:07+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/crazy-fatty-acid-profile.3807/post-53223>

Just got back my fatty acids profile. Not quite sure of what to make of it. My ND thinks I should stop the krill oil (taken only when I eat non grass fed beef-I used to take it 2/day a few months ago) and the fermented cod liver oil (used on the days I do not eat liverworst/braunschwieger which I eat 2 times a week). I do take Evening Primrose oil as it has helped me hormonally. She didn't think I should stop it? I've been gluten-free for more than 2 years, paleo for most of it, and have been eating sea food once at day (mostly) for 3 months. I can't believe the palmitic acid is low as I eat a ton of it daily? And saturated fat is low?!? I only cook with CO and uncured bacon grease and use olive oil for salad dressing? Lots of fats on my menu...I do admit to occasionally (once every couple of weeks) eating a rotisserie chicken with nice crunchy skin...I craved it when my gut was healing from celiac. I'm a bit on the lean side.

The other items were normal range, but these were out-of-range:

Palmitic acid 1252 L 1480-3730 nmol/mL

Lineoleic acid 4147 H 2270-3850 nmol/mL

EPA, C20:5W3 365 H 14-100 nmol/mL

Arachidonic ACI 1636 H 520-1490 nmol/mL

DHA, C22:6W3 1206 H 30-250 nmol/mL

Triene Tetranene 0.0008 L 0.010-0.038

total Monsatur 1.7 1.3-5.9 mmol/L

Total W3 1.8 H 0.2-0.5 mmol/L

total W6 6.0 H 3.0-5.4 mmol/L

Total Fatty Acid 11.7

21 ---- Saturated Fatty Acids(Meat, Diary, coconuts,palm oils

22 ---- C15:0 Pentadecanoic

23 ---- C16:0 Palmitic

24 ---- C17:0 Margaric (Heptadecanoic)

25 ---- C18:0 Stearic

26 ---- C20:0 Arachidic

27 ---- C22:0 Behenic

28 ---- C23:0 Tricosanoic

I don't know if the correlation is causation here, but since I reduced my Evening Primrose oil intake a couple weeks ago (after getting back the labs) my hormones have been taking a nose dive. Haven't had hot flashes for 6 mos.--now they are back. I'd like to think that with the overall 3:6 ratio in line (am i right that it is between 1:3-1:4?), that I might be OK with going on as I have been, just decrease the EPO--maybe every other day, but change up the types of DHA with the suggestions you made?

> All that it tells you is that
>
> Evening Primrose oil (EPO) is affecting your levels of Steroid Hormones.
>
> No wonder, EPO contains GLA -----> it highly influences 5Ar---->which in turn influences progesterone and DHT levels.
>
> I had these done at Quest. I had a full work-up, but left off the ones that were in normal range.

You may get better input if you scan and post exact copy of your complete Fatty Acid report, all pages, however insignificant they may look.

Would save lots of guessing.

Even better if you would add all available tests.

There is a lots of variables.

///

# Thread: Help me catch up!
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/help-me-catch-up.3811/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 10
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### MamaGrok — 2012-09-22T01:00:43+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/help-me-catch-up.3811/post-53313>

I just had my first consultation, and Jack's on with someone else right now, so he can't come here and make fun of me yet.

Educational consult revealed to me: Hashi's. Apparently responsible for everything, from the cravings to the diarrhea, fatigue to ... everything else.

Well, now I need to catch up. I've been mostly off the forum all summer, living my life and enjoying the increase in energy I've had. And I missed a few things. So any of you out there who have 5 minutes to sum up for me, I'd really appreciate it! I plan to read a whole bunch of back blog posts, and already hit most of BG12, but here's what I think I'm missing out on:

\- Epi-paleo - does this just mean paleo with a higher percentage of seafood than most do? How high? How much raw? How much shellfish vs fish fish? We don't care about the contamination, or what?

\- Iodine is king. Are we just eating more seafood, or seaweed, or supplementing? Are we concerned with selenium status first?

\- What's the consensus these days on best everyday CT? I have a great river very close that's 50 degrees year-round, but can't go without hubby, and he's not always available. AKman's brown fat burner thing? MS cooling vests? What can I use during my daily life around the house? Looking normal while doing so is a big bonus.

\- Um, what is ASI?

Hrm, that's all for now. I'm really beat up right now. But I guess I should be seeing the light at the end of the tunnel does exist ... just a lot further away than I had thought it was.

Thank you for your help! I will be catching up reading in due time, but I need a jump start.

### nonchalant — 2012-09-22T01:13:32+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/help-me-catch-up.3811/post-53319>

MamaGrok!!! Good to see you again!

Sorry about the Hashi's. \*hug\*

I have to run, but you might want to start eating some sort of seafood everyday. You will find that you will come to like it more than you used to.

Don't worry about mercury for now, but yes, taking selenium or eating a brazil nut every other day would be good.

### Patty Cakes — 2012-09-22T01:31:54+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/help-me-catch-up.3811/post-53326>

Hashis isn't the end of the world although it's not the best news. Been dealing with it for five years now and Jack's advice seems to be the best. Never felt better.

ASI is Adrenal Stress Index. Obviously something you need to get??? Stressed adrenals go hand in hand with Thyroid issues. Are you still taking the ThyroGold or have you moved onto something else.

Also selenium helps with the hashis attacks on your thyroid. I rotate now 200/400mg every other day to help with that.

Seafood every day all day is great. I'm able to get lunch (if I have it) and dinner as seafood (preferably shellfish, crustaceans). I think ocean fish then fresh water fish come next in order of importance. Oysters are the best most nutrient dense. Also include ocean veggies.

That's just a little snippet but I'm sure others will add to that. Good to see you back on here!

### vkiernan — 2012-09-22T02:04:25+0300

MG,

there was this thing about eating dirty vegetables from the garden.. to get good bacterias in...

I guess something raw every day is great. No need to be all raw. Fish eyes and brain and skin and all will give us the perfect nutrition. Like oysters and shrimps eaten with head and skin.

MG welcome back to the fold!

I can only do one meal/day for budgetary reasons but I can surely feel a difference if I skip a day. Pay special mind to the light cycles. Blue blockers on when sun goes down, minimize/eliminate artificial lights sources especially at that time. Get AM sun first thing in the morning if possible, just a few mins can get things going.

I'm considering ice pack thingy AKMan posted about. Seems in the long run to be an economical choice while still allowing for mobility.

### Inger — 2012-09-22T21:15:51+0300

Angie,

I use all kind of seaweed. Then I eat the seaweed when all the water is gone.

### KiwiLauren — 2012-09-22T22:15:01+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/help-me-catch-up.3811/post-53467>

> **MamaGrok;53948 wrote:** I just had my first consultation, and Jack's on with someone else right now, so he can't come here and make fun of me yet.
>
>
>
> Educational consult revealed to me: Hashi's. Apparently responsible for everything, from the cravings to the diarrhea, fatigue to ... everything else.
>
>
>
> Well, now I need to catch up. I've been mostly off the forum all summer, living my life and enjoying the increase in energy I've had. And I missed a few things. So any of you out there who have 5 minutes to sum up for me, I'd really appreciate it! I plan to read a whole bunch of back blog posts, and already hit most of BG12, but here's what I think I'm missing out on:
>
>
>
> \- Epi-paleo - does this just mean paleo with a higher percentage of seafood than most do? How high? How much raw? How much shellfish vs fish fish? We don't care about the contamination, or what?
>
>
>
> \- Iodine is king. Are we just eating more seafood, or seaweed, or supplementing? Are we concerned with selenium status first?
>
>
>
> \- What's the consensus these days on best everyday CT? I have a great river very close that's 50 degrees year-round, but can't go without hubby, and he's not always available. AKman's brown fat burner thing? MS cooling vests? What can I use during my daily life around the house? Looking normal while doing so is a big bonus.
>
>
>
> \- Um, what is ASI?
>
>
>
> Hrm, that's all for now. I'm really beat up right now. But I guess I should be seeing the light at the end of the tunnel does exist ... just a lot further away than I had thought it was.
>
>
>
> Thank you for your help! I will be catching up reading in due time, but I need a jump start.

I've missed you!

Lots of us dealing with Hashi's (or potential Hashi's, not ruled out yet), so welcome back. I'm sure he told you to keep testing antibodies (how were your levels?) as it's a fluid disease that can be managed.

Re: your questions, I don't think there are 'rules' but I'll give you my general take-home messages:

Epi-Paleo means as much seafood as you can eat. There is a whole blog about it with the JK Epi Paleo food pyramid. Most of us, I'd say, eat at least one big seafood meal a day. Ideal, have something raw every day. The more raw the more DHA. If you're cooking it all, eat more. The now-famous MHS (mackerel head smoothies) are a cheap and easy way to get raw seafood in you. Also, don't put saturated fat with your seafood. Stick with mac oil or olive oil (or even bacon which is mostly MUFA) to eat with it. If you need/like/want sat fat (as I do) eat it at another, separate meal.

Iodine is best received from food (and there is a list of all the seaweeds in the epi paleo blog post - I think BG5). There are some who have wondered about supplementing until deficiency status goes away, but I know JK prefers directly from food (and there are risks with supplementing.)

Dr K has repeatedly suggested to 'pre-load' with selenium before doing the iodine, so I'm guessing this means making sure you've got good Se levels, too. You can supplement a la Brownstein protocol or you can eat Brazil nuts (1 per day - I do this even though I'm AI, not sure all would though). There is, of course, a lot of Se in seafood, so ideally this sorts the problem.

As far as CT, I think anything is good. Whatever you can do, whenever you can do it. Bathtubs, ice packs, cold rivers, cold vests. I know there is a thread by Trisha on the cooling vest she uses and has found really great.

You've already been told about ASI.

Hang in there. And so lovely to see you back.

Lauren

# Thread: Autumn Starts Tomorrow (9/22), 6-Week CT Experiment
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/autumn-starts-tomorrow-9-22-6-week-ct-experiment.3813/>
**Subforum:** Cold Thermogenesis
**Posts:** 11
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### KA24 — 2012-09-22T01:49:13+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/autumn-starts-tomorrow-9-22-6-week-ct-experiment.3813/post-53332>

I stopped CT in June and have only done it twice in the last 3 months. I've focused a lot more on training and other stuff since then, and have also been incredibly busy with work and building a new house. However, I've been planning on this self-experiment with CT for awhile and wanted to get everything going on the first day of fall. I've purchased a 100-gallon drum/tank for cold soaking and put it in my garage gym. Keep in mind, I'm going to jump right into it, without any re-adaptation time. Here is what I plan on measuring.

**Controls

_CT Sessions - 3 times per week for 6 weeks, or 18 total sessions

_Water temperature - I can usually get the water down to around 50 degrees with ice. I have 2 thermometers and will make sure the water is similar for each session.

_Length of soak - Each soak will be 30 minutes long of complete submersion (except for the head and two hands)

**Variables

_CT Time of day_ - I will try to control this as each session will be in the evening, but it's not feasible to do it the exact same time each day

Pre-CT meal/supplements**_ **- I am going to test different pre-CT meals weekly. I am not sure exactly how detailed I will get with this, but it definitely won't be 100% consistent session to session.

**Measurements******

Body measurements**_ **- this will basically include different body measurements affected by the CT.

Blood sugar**_ **- I plan on taking pre-CT and post-CT blood sugars 5-minutes before and after each session. I am a former Type 2 Diabetic who has a very good record of previous blood sugars.

Strength levels** - this is more of an observational thing, but I want to see if certain strength elements show significant improvement as the CT goes on

Hypothesis**

I think it's pretty safe to say that everything mentioned has been shown to be affected by CT. I want some concrete numbers for myself. I believe it will improve Glucose levels, body composition and strength gains.

Any recommendations for someone wanting to track this stuff? Anything I'm not accounting for? What to add, subtract, tweak, etc?

### villjamur_stevenson — 2012-09-22T02:08:21+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/autumn-starts-tomorrow-9-22-6-week-ct-experiment.3813/post-53334>

i'm not sure if you have the time to do this, but the literature suggests two session per day, of 1/2 time is better than one full time CT exposure. At least in mice.

### KA24 — 2012-09-22T02:19:56+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/autumn-starts-tomorrow-9-22-6-week-ct-experiment.3813/post-53335>

How far apart for each session? I think it's an interesting thought. Do you have any links or studies that reference this? I'd be interested in the margin for benefits and how they are measured on two sessions per day...

### freesia — 2012-09-22T06:49:46+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/autumn-starts-tomorrow-9-22-6-week-ct-experiment.3813/post-53366>

> **villjamur\_stevenson;53969 wrote:** i'm not sure if you have the time to do this, but the literature suggests two session per day, of 1/2 time is better than one full time CT exposure. At least in mice.

Would love to see the study you read. Looking forward to tweaking my CT for next Autumn and Winter. Benefits from 1 x per day have been great but I'm happy to improve on it

Thx

### KA24 — 2012-09-25T04:44:50+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/autumn-starts-tomorrow-9-22-6-week-ct-experiment.3813/post-54018>

I think I may incorporate the twice a day thing. I'm still wondering how much time in-between would give max benefits.

Tonight, I did an abbreviated session after doing some chest and back lifting in the garage. Went 15 minutes and it felt great. I was definitely sore. Got cold enough outside last night that I kept the water at 59 degrees, makes it great to have the 100-gallon drum in the garage (no need for ice).

After 15 minutes, I got out and despite being pretty exhausted from the lifting session, I was able to do 30 straight Kettlebell snatches with a 75 lb Kettlebell. Generally, 35-40 is my max effort with this weight, and that is usually with some pre-workout supplement and it's usually a max set. Although I felt "numb" from the cold, I was really felt some additional strength capacity. I know I started another thread about this before and plan to really do some testing later in the winter on cold training, but it was cool to get a glimpse of. I really do think there is some adaptation that can be made for additional strength gains in the "numbness window" right after CT.

### villjamur_stevenson — 2012-09-25T15:41:29+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/autumn-starts-tomorrow-9-22-6-week-ct-experiment.3813/post-54103>

sorry, I posted that and then went away. Let me find the studies and I'll post them here.

### nonchalant — 2012-09-25T17:01:32+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/autumn-starts-tomorrow-9-22-6-week-ct-experiment.3813/post-54117>

Thanks for the reminder about Autumn, KA24. I sure didn't miss the first day of Spring, but I forgot about 9/22. Time to get serious.

Are you going to drink icewater before doing the CT?

### villjamur_stevenson — 2012-09-25T17:03:23+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/autumn-starts-tomorrow-9-22-6-week-ct-experiment.3813/post-54119>

i'd like to say I'm normally more organized than this - lol. But this happens more than I'd like to admit.

I've been searching through my horde of papers and this is all I'm able to find at the moment:

[http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/2155691](http://http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/2155691)

> Two 2-h cold exposures daily were sufficient to increase basal metabolic rate and nonshivering thermogenesis to the same level as continuous cold exposure, whereas one 4-h cold period per day did not result in a significant increase of both parameters.

### KA24 — 2012-09-25T19:35:23+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/autumn-starts-tomorrow-9-22-6-week-ct-experiment.3813/post-54156>

> **Nonchalant;54821 wrote:** Thanks for the reminder about Autumn, KA24. I sure didn't miss the first day of Spring, but I forgot about 9/22. Time to get serious.
>
> Are you going to drink icewater before doing the CT?

Experimenting with different pre-CT foods/supplements. I have some data about these foods and effect on blood glucose, and will compare the effect they have while doing the CT. I generally don't use the ice water as I haven't noticed much of a difference. I've actually tried some experimenting with drinking a hot beverage both before and during CT.

### KA24 — 2012-09-25T19:37:33+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/autumn-starts-tomorrow-9-22-6-week-ct-experiment.3813/post-54158>

> **villjamur\_stevenson;54823 wrote:** i'd like to say I'm normally more organized than this - lol. But this happens more than I'd like to admit.
>
> I've been searching through my horde of papers and this is all I'm able to find at the moment:
>
> [http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/2155691](http://http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/2155691)

Interesting. I'm going to print this one out. Interesting that it's two 2-hour exposures though. I wonder if the benefit decreases reverse-exponentially as the time goes down.

For instance, 4 hours is of greater benefit compared to 1 hour. However, i wonder if two 30-minute sessions would be better than 1 hour. Thanks for the link.

### philerup — 2012-09-26T04:19:37+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/autumn-starts-tomorrow-9-22-6-week-ct-experiment.3813/post-54332>

Yes, thanks Villjamur for the link. I have been doing sort of a 2x per day cold exposure using gel packs on the BAT areas. I do about 1 hour each time. It seems to at least keep my body composition stable as I haven't done much exercise, eat the same amount (or often more), and perhaps even got leaner.

Good luck with your experiment KA24. I'm interested in seeing how you do.

# Thread: Stomach Pain - Help!

### vkiernan — 2012-09-22T03:47:44+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/stomach-pain-help.3815/post-53354>

Here we go again. I have had a pain in my gut (beneath the ribs to the navel and to the left and to the right) all day. It was finally subsiding this evening and then I ate dinner consisting of blue gills fried in bacon grease with salt and pepper. Now my gut is hurting all over again. What is going on? Almost seems a little better to lay on my left side. If I can't even eat fish right now, what the hell can I eat?

Thanks for any input.

### vkiernan — 2012-09-22T15:00:04+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/stomach-pain-help.3815/post-53378>

No takers on what's going on? Last night was pretty bad. Woke up every 2 hours with pain. Wondering if I have gastritis? I had this about 7 years ago. Anyone know anything about gastritis? If I do, what do I eat? I am eating burger right now and we'll see how things fare. Pretty scared as food seems to make it flare up.

### Destiny — 2012-09-22T15:42:48+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/stomach-pain-help.3815/post-53387>

> **vkiernan;53989 wrote:** Here we go again. I have had a pain in my gut (beneath the ribs to the navel and to the left and to the right) all day. It was finally subsiding this evening and then I ate dinner consisting of blue gills fried in bacon grease with salt and pepper. Now my gut is hurting all over again. What is going on? Almost seems a little better to lay on my left side. If I can't even eat fish right now, what the hell can I eat?
>
>
>
> Thanks for any input.

That sounds like my reaction to Betaine HCL. I had to stop taking it after a week on it. After a few days of stopping, I was good.

Since then, I have been taking Ox bile, no problem with that.

### Patty Cakes — 2012-09-22T20:36:02+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/stomach-pain-help.3815/post-53437>

I'm just here to give you support. I know nothing about gastritis. Big hug!!!

### vkiernan — 2012-09-22T21:19:41+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/stomach-pain-help.3815/post-53449>

I am a lot better today stomach wise. Yesterday was bad and kinda scary not really knowing what was up. Must have been a touch of stomach flu or something. I slept a lot yesterday and last night and am really drained today. Going to lay low and take a nap soon.

# Thread: Can't decide if I should do it again or keep trying the hard way!
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cant-decide-if-i-should-do-it-again-or-keep-trying-the-hard-way.3816/>
**Subforum:** HCG Protocol
**Posts:** 5
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### LSWood@eoni.com — 2012-09-22T03:52:24+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cant-decide-if-i-should-do-it-again-or-keep-trying-the-hard-way.3816/post-53356>

A year ago I did a successful round of the Bodyshapers (real HCG, sublingual, which gets a "positive" on pregnancy tests). I am female, 60+ and had no other weight-loss success despite multiple other attempts, so I was thrilled, not to mention that the HCG Diet Info Forum (and Colleen Coble! THANKS!) introduced me to Jack Kruse, the Leptin reset and Paleo. Here it is a year later, I've only regained about 5 of the 15 lbs, but it seems like it's all fat around my middle (I'm about 147-148 now, 160 when I started last year). Part of me wants to keep plugging away eating Paleo, etc. (the hard way) but I'm really tempted to go another round of the Bodyshapers HCG (the easy way). I did CT for a while last spring but didn't get great results and didn't really do it 100% with ice packs in the tub - my immersions were only about 60 degrees. I know not to attempt HCG and CT at the same time, according to Colleen's N=1.

I liked doing it in the fall - lots of fresh veggies and strawberries at the farmer's market and it set me up for eating more ketogenic in the winter.

Any thoughts/advice? Especially regarding using the Bodyshapers again - I see it's a different company but since it worked so well and will get here quickly I'd prefer using them again unless anyone knows any reasons I should be concerned about using them again.

I still have some sleep issues and not great energy, and I've been following all the threads regarding those problems, so just concerned regarding the potential impact of HCG. They were pre-existing before HCG so I doubt that should actually be of concern but thought I'd mention them while I was at it.

Thank you all!

### tellmisty — 2012-09-26T07:17:12+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cant-decide-if-i-should-do-it-again-or-keep-trying-the-hard-way.3816/post-54374>

Consider that your PG-E2 ratio is off, making you estrogen dominant, which is causing excess estrogen to be stored in fat cells in your belly. Detoxing estrogen, in this case, is what will strip your middle of the bulge. You need labs and the support of a good functional medicine doctor to tackle this. It's the first thing that's ever successfully removed stubborn belly fat for me, and as I learned about it, I was surprised at how common it was and how little it's discussed for women ...

### caroline — 2012-09-27T12:36:44+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cant-decide-if-i-should-do-it-again-or-keep-trying-the-hard-way.3816/post-54712>

> **tellmisty;55107 wrote:** Consider that your PG-E2 ratio is off, making you estrogen dominant, which is causing excess estrogen to be stored in fat cells in your belly. Detoxing estrogen, in this case, is what will strip your middle of the bulge. You need labs and the support of a good functional medicine doctor to tackle this. It's the first thing that's ever successfully removed stubborn belly fat for me, and as I learned about it, I was surprised at how common it was and how little it's discussed for women ...

Thanks Misty for talking about this. I have just discovered that I am down 2 sizes but my weight has not changed - in fact I am up 2 K. All of this really does work. No one could be more surprised than me - I have been on every diet know to mankind. They usually worked when I was younger but as I got older the middle bulge just kept getting bigger. Now I know it is my PG-E2 ratio etc. Thanks Dr. K.

### AdrianaDr. Radler-Pohl — 2012-09-27T13:04:46+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cant-decide-if-i-should-do-it-again-or-keep-trying-the-hard-way.3816/post-54716>

I think that this Pg/E2 ratio thing is what most women need. Esp those who are not optimal with their bodycomp. The off-bodycomp ist the body talking to us. Still I find it not easy to get things adjusted. here in Germany it is so difficult to find a Doc who is familiar with the whole hormone stuff. and you need often prescriptions - so I find myself in a situation I want to change but it takes so long to find the right help ..... Sigh!

### caroline — 2012-09-28T01:45:48+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cant-decide-if-i-should-do-it-again-or-keep-trying-the-hard-way.3816/post-54892>

> **ARP;55537 wrote:** I think that this Pg/E2 ratio thing is what most women need. Esp those who are not optimal with their bodycomp. The off-bodycomp ist the body talking to us. Still I find it not easy to get things adjusted. here in Germany it is so difficult to find a Doc who is familiar with the whole hormone stuff. and you need often prescriptions - so I find myself in a situation I want to change but it takes so long to find the right help ..... Sigh!

I so absolutely agree - My bulging middle is what started me on this journey. I really thought that something was the matter but when my Docs would test they would say I was fine. Have you done a consult with Dr. K? I got lucky yesterday and went to a new Doc. He was very interested and watched my Skype consult with Dr. Kruse. He agreed to do scripts for what Dr. K recommended and said he will do further research and work with a compounding pharmacy. At Least this is a start for me.

It is hard in Australia as well. I was able to order tests from ZRT Labs and this new Doc showed me an on line pharmacy where I could order the progesterone that Dr.K recommended. I got the name of this new Doc from a compounding pharmacy so I would suggest you give that a go. It is a huge step forward to do an email consult with Dr. K about what labs you need and then do a skype consult. I found that it really helped to show the new Doc the skype consult because then he knew I wasn't dealing with some online Whacko. If you want the name of the online pharmacy - PM me. Good luck!

# Thread: Brighton catches up

**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 1
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Michael — 2012-09-22T09:38:47+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/brighton-catches-up.3820/post-53373>

Just down the coast here there's the town of Brighton. The names's a contraction of Brighthelmstone, and this was a tiny fishing village till the Prince Regent decided it would make a suitable place for the seabathing cure in the early 19th century and built a Moghul-cum-Chinese palace at the cost of what would now be a medium-sized country's national debt. I suppose it kept some craftsmen in work. I don't know whether paddling in the sea cured the royal gout. It seems to have had less effect on the regal girth than an epi-paleoRx would have done, judging by some of the Gillray cartoons:

[http://www.britishmuseum.org/explore/highlights/highlight\_objects/pd/j/james\_gillray,\_a\_voluptuary\_un.aspx](http://http://www.britishmuseum.org/explore/highlights/highlight_objects/pd/j/james_gillray,_a_voluptuary_un.aspx)

Anyway, for the past half-dozen or so years Brighton has been allowed to refer to itself as a "city".

And it's catching up fast. Yesterday a man died in front of his son after crashing his van in the city. While a woman who was passing "struggled to save his life" with artificial respiration her purse was stolen.


══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# Thread: Seaweed Scone recipe
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/seaweed-scone-recipe.3824/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 5
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### jeanie@truelongmont.com — 2012-09-22T18:08:38+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/seaweed-scone-recipe.3824/post-53407>

Here is the Scone recipe that was asked after. . . .

It's the basic recipe, and I tried it with added dried seaweed and chopped dark chocolate - awesome, lots of seaweed and you would never know it was there.

2 cups almond meal

Â¾ tsp baking soda

Â½ tsp sale

3 tbs maple syrup

1-egg

Mix up all the ingredients, and pat out into a round, bake at 350 till done.

Variations:

I like adding cinnamon or pumpkin spices.

You can add drier additions like crushed seaweed â€“ if adding dry stuff I would increase egg to 2.

Â½ cup of additions like larger chunks of toasted pecans or chopped dark chocolate or dried fruit always works well.

This isnâ€™t chemistry like regular baking, so you probably wonâ€™t mess it up by experiementing!

### jeanie@truelongmont.com — 2012-09-22T18:50:32+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/seaweed-scone-recipe.3824/post-53417>

Yes, I do realize it's not optimal optimal. N=1 it baby!

[http://www.groupepolyphenols.com/index.php?option=com\_content&view=article&id=53&Itemid=59&b528026c36a38313c3bc0e90a25fbe0c=de83a81432badb0913a5a4741596b426](http://http://www.groupepolyphenols.com/index.php?option=com_content&view=article&id=53&Itemid=59&b528026c36a38313c3bc0e90a25fbe0c=de83a81432badb0913a5a4741596b426)

### AKMan — 2012-09-22T19:50:42+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/older-polyphenol-article.3826/post-53433>

Loved the last paragraph!

I would like to end this article by a short anecdote that will perhaps contribute to convince the most sceptical scientists among you of the value of polyphenols. In 1934, DÃ©sirÃ© Cordier (1858-1940), founder of the world famous Cordier wine trading house, organized the first “longevity festival” in Saint-Julien-Beychevelle (MÃ©doc, France), after having noted that life expectancy in this wine-producing area near Bordeaux was 45% higher than the national average. All the centenarians from MÃ©doc came to celebrate wine as the “elixir of life”… A votre santÃ© !

### chocolate — 2012-09-22T20:20:01+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/older-polyphenol-article.3826/post-53434>

> Loved the last paragraph!
>
> am not entirely convinced that I will never be able to digest a glass of wine again. I just have a mess to clean up, first.
>
>
>
> [http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/19930765](http://http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/19930765)Abstract
>
>
>
> Sucrose increases postprandial blood glucose concentrations, and diets with a high glycaemic response may be associated with increased risk of obesity, type 2 diabetes and CVD. Previous studies have suggested that polyphenols may influence carbohydrate digestion and absorption and thereby postprandial glycaemia. Berries are rich sources of various polyphenols and berry products are typically consumed with sucrose. We investigated the glycaemic effect of a berry purÃ©e made of bilberries, blackcurrants, cranberries and strawberries, and sweetened with sucrose, in comparison to sucrose with adjustment of available carbohydrates. A total of twelve healthy subjects (eleven women and one man, aged 25-69 years) with normal fasting plasma glucose ingested 150 g of the berry purÃ©e with 35 g sucrose or a control sucrose load in a randomised, controlled cross-over design. After consumption of the berry meal, the plasma glucose concentrations were significantly lower at 15 and 30 min (P < 0.05, P < 0.01, respectively) and significantly higher at 150 min (P < 0.05) compared with the control meal. The peak glucose concentration was reached at 45 min after the berry meal and at 30 min after the control meal. The peak increase from the baseline was 1.0 mmol/l smaller (P = 0.002) after ingestion of the berry meal. There was no statistically significant difference in the 3 h area under the glucose response curve. These results show that berries rich in polyphenols decrease the postprandial glucose response of sucrose in healthy subjects. The delayed and attenuated glycaemic response indicates reduced digestion and/or absorption of sucrose from the berry meal.

This study included the sucrose. I want the berries without sucrose. I wish I knew what to think. I think they can only help.


══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# Thread: Pls hack my thyroid labs...
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/pls-hack-my-thyroid-labs.3831/>
**Subforum:** Optimal Labs
**Posts:** 8
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### DarleenMB — 2012-09-23T00:14:08+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/pls-hack-my-thyroid-labs.3831/post-53494>

Just got back from a trip to Colorado to find my lab results in my inbox. Any thoughts?

TSH 1.460 uIU/ml **Reference Interval (RI)** = 0.450=4.5000

T4 7.5 ug/dL ; RI= 4.5-12.0

T3 uptake 31%; RI = 24-39

Free Thyroxine INdex 2.3; RI 1.2-4.9

T4, Free (direct) 1.11 ng/dL; RI = 0.82-1.77

Reverse T3, serum 24.6 ng/dL, RI = 13.5-34.2

T3 95 ng/dL; RI = 71-180

T3, Free, Serum 2.4 pg/mL; RI = 2.0-4.4

Now according to one site I looked at my free T3 "should" be 3.5 to 4.2 and my RT3 should be less than 16.

But I look at those reference intervals and am scratching my head. My cortisol results were:

AM 6.84 (ref range 18-35)

PM 0.44 (ref range 0.9-9.2)

And those numbers are all I have at the moment via phone from my compounding pharmacist who got them in an email from the lab. He did say there was a note the values are LOW (really?!) but that in '07 they were lower than that.

### vkiernan — 2012-09-23T00:25:50+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/pls-hack-my-thyroid-labs.3831/post-53501>

Your ft3 to rt3 ratio is 10 and needs to be over 20; this means no t3 in your cells. Your ft4 is kinda low too. Looks like you have a low cortisol issue to deal with. Need to see that morning sun as soon as it rises and soak it up and let your body know it is am.

### DarleenMB — 2012-09-23T00:45:55+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/pls-hack-my-thyroid-labs.3831/post-53503>

Where did you get this info because I"m going to have to have some proof to hand to my doc. And I've been gazing "at" the sun in the mornings ... I know that will help but there has got to be more I can do.

### vkiernan — 2012-09-23T02:23:22+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/pls-hack-my-thyroid-labs.3831/post-53520>

Jack's blogs mainly. Go to StopTheThyroidMadness.com and read, read and read some more. [http://www.stopthethyroidmadness.com/rt3-ratio/](http://http://www.stopthethyroidmadness.com/rt3-ratio/) You can put your own numbers in and get your ratio. Make sure you choose the correct u/m otherwise your ratio won't be correct. This would be a great place for you to learn a lot about the thyroid. Also read Jack's Hormone 101 blog. [http://jackkruse.com/hormone-cascade-101/](http://http://jackkruse.com/hormone-cascade-101/)

Glad to hear you are getting the morning sun. Jack says most adrenal issues are in the brain and not in the adrenals themselves.

### vkiernan — 2012-09-23T02:49:57+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/pls-hack-my-thyroid-labs.3831/post-53529>

Darleen, the following link is from a recent "ask Jack" post and it tells a lot and is easy to understand. It talks about using t3 only to clear the rt3. Also talks about adequate amounts of Vit. D and how it is necessary to push the t3 into the cells. Jack agreed with the vit. D. I think you will enjoy the read.

[http://www.hairlossclinic.com.au/blog/2012/03/14/reverse-t3-when-the-body-shoots-blanks/](http://http://www.hairlossclinic.com.au/blog/2012/03/14/reverse-t3-when-the-body-shoots-blanks/)

> And those numbers are all I have at the moment via phone from my compounding pharmacist who got them in an email from the lab. He did say there was a note the values are LOW (really?!) but that in '07 they were lower than that.

I am not a doctor.

My view is that at this time, you should leave your thyroid hormones be, and work on improvement to your steroid hormone panel.

Among most doctors and people frequenting boards you will find more (moral) support to directly increasing your poor cortisol levels. (I (mostly) do not support this approach, because the is much better way.)

You already doing a lot by minding what dr J preaches,

that is

circadian rhythm via light/dark management and proper eating,

but

you are still missing on hormone testing and corrections.

I do not recall your age, but you already have Medicare.

That solves many financial obstacles for you (until this election) and possibly after elections (if you vote right).

There is more, but you can have very good start if your doc will write for you a series of scripts as below.

Results from those tests plus proper supplementation to incrementally correct deficiencies should make lots of improvement to your Steroid Hormone Panel.

To be done at LabCorp

1 ------- Pregnenolone, Mass Spectrometry--Test Number: 140707 CPT Code: 84140

2 ------- 17α-Hydroxypregnenolone by HPLC-MS/MS

3 ------- 17α-Hydroxyprogesterone by HPLC-MS/MS

4 ------- Progesterone--Test Number: 004317 CPT Code: 84144

5 ------- Cortisol, Free, Serum (Free Cortisol, Serum Cortisol, CBG)

6 ------- Cortisol, AM & PM Test Number: 104000 CPT Code: 82533(x2) (7:30AM, 12PM)

7 ------- Corticosteroid-binding Globulin (CBG), Transcortin

8 ------- Corticotropin Releasing Hormone (CRH)

9 ------- Dehydroepiandrosterone (DHEA) Sulfate--Test Number: 004020 CPT Code: 82627

10 ------- Dehydroepiandrosterone (DHEA)--Test Number: 004100 CPT Code: 82626

11 ------- Androstenedione, LCMS--Test Number: 004705 CPT Code: 82157

12 ------- Testosterone, Total, Serum Test Number: 004226 CPT Code: 84403

13 ------- Testosterone, Free and Weakly Bound--Test Number: 143255 CPT Code: 84402; 84403

14 ------- SHBG

15 ------- Dihydrotestosterone (DHT)--Test Number: 500142 CPT Code: 82651

16 ------- Androstanediol Glucuronide (Endocrine Sciences) Test Number: 500881 CPT Code: 82154

17 ------- Estrogens, Total; Test Number: 004549 CPT Code: 82672

18 ------- Estradiol, Sensitive Test Number: 140244 CPT Code: 82670

19 ------- Estrone, Serum Test Number: 004564 CPT Code: 82679

............................................................

### DarleenMB — 2012-09-23T16:11:21+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/pls-hack-my-thyroid-labs.3831/post-53583>

vkiernan, thanks for the input. I will go read, again (LOL), those posts from Jack. He makes me feel like a blockhead. thunk thunk. I found the "stop the thyroid madness" site ages ago. Also, Dr. Wilson's site about adrenal fatigue.

JanSz, man I thought I had annoyed EVERYbody already with my posts about hormones. I have been on the Wiley Protocol for over a year. My parts got yanked in 1997 and if things weren't already bad enough that sent them straight down the potty.

My progest/estradiol has recently been checked although I neglected to get the actual numbers from the PA, testosterone is flatlined so I'm now on THAT replacement.

I'll print out your list and give to my doc/PA in the hopes they will order them, although going to labcorp is a 2 hour drive from here. Maybe I can convince them to order the tests locally. And yes, I am on Medicare FINALLY and thank goodness. We had good insurance when DH was working (he was a conductor for the BNSF railroad == paid a lot better than teaching and gave us a GREAT retirement). When he was forcibly retired because of injuries he received on the job my insurance ran out and we had 4 years of paying BCBS about $400month for basically NOTHING. I hope the CEO enjoyed the fact I was putting the gas in his hummer. Do NOT get me started. Our problem is not "health care" our problem is the greed mongers who infiltrated the insurance field when a certain president and his minions DEregulated everything about 30 years ago. see. I started myself.

gah.

So. Yes I know I need more tests. The question is now that I'm homing in on my problem what the HECK do I do about it?

So the multi-biliion dollar sports industry uses what since the can't use steroids? Sleep/Cold. We need to sell them some epi-paleo lessons.

[http://msn.foxsports.com/nfl/story/new-york-jets-rex-ryan-sleep-specialists-to-help-performance-092212](http://http://msn.foxsports.com/nfl/story/new-york-jets-rex-ryan-sleep-specialists-to-help-performance-092212)

"We've had sleep people come in, talking about how important sleep is, how important it is to get eight hours of it," Ryan said Friday. "We bring in these specialists for a reason. I just think it's wise to do that, listening to people with a lot more knowledge than myself about it.

"If we can gain a little advantage, then we're going to look for it." The players received handouts earlier in the week about how sleep helps performance. Meetings and practices were shortened a bit, and the change in the schedule was welcomed by the players."

### cramernh — 2012-09-25T17:07:49+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/music.3850/post-54121>

Im actually a musician. I play Bassoon (chief), clarinet, bari sax, piano, flute and mallet percussion. I perform with the SNHU Orchestra here in southern NH.

At work I do advanced medical billing, coding and appeals and a very large component requires the use of music to concentrate on each section of my responsibilities. If I am composing an appeal (mostly on my largest claims: neuro, GI, Endo, Derma, etc), I rely on classical music to focus on reading the clinical documentation. Thankfully my clinic experience really does help (Im the only person from school who scored 100% in A&P, hehehehe) with the appeals but often I need classical compositions to analyze and compose the appeals letter. Its very rare that I need an LMN (letter of medical necessity) unless the insurance asks for one.

The easier components of my position are certain genres...

I even have music compiled for when I am not feeling well. Once per month I have a power migraine that 1000mg of advil just numbs it a bit... so Ill listen to meditative music and it truly does help me to remain productive.

On the crazy side, everyone knows me for my 70s and 80s BIG time! LOL!

### nonchalant — 2012-09-25T17:22:45+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/music.3850/post-54124>

> **cramernh;54825 wrote:** At work I do advanced medical billing, coding and appeals and a very large component requires the use of music to concentrate on each section of my responsibilities. If I am composing an appeal (mostly on my largest claims: neuro, GI, Endo, Derma, etc), I rely on classical music to focus on reading the clinical documentation. Thankfully my clinic experience really does help (Im the only person from school who scored 100% in A&P, hehehehe) with the appeals but often I need classical compositions to analyze and compose the appeals letter. Its very rare that I need an LMN (letter of medical necessity) unless the insurance asks for one.
>
>
>
> The easier components of my position are certain genres...
>
>
>
> I even have music compiled for when I am not feeling well. Once per month I have a power migraine that 1000mg of advil just numbs it a bit... so Ill listen to meditative music and it truly does help me to remain productive.

Interesting. The classical music seems to give you greater access to your analytical and concentration abilities. Maybe there is some sort of association cortex between the auditory system and whatever else is needed in the brain? Allows you to use more of your brain for the task?

### cramernh — 2012-09-25T17:34:34+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/music.3850/post-54128>

> **Nonchalant;54828 wrote:** Interesting. The classical music seems to give you greater access to your analytical and concentration abilities. Maybe there is some sort of association cortex between the auditory system and whatever else is needed in the brain? Allows you to use more of your brain for the task?

When I started playing music (we are talking 1984 mind you), learning became so much easier for me. Moving forward from there, listening to music was was a requirement to be capable of studying for my tests/quizes, reading books and pages of lessons, etc. I could not learn anything without it. I could not retain the information and my 'off days', teachers could tell whether or not I studied without the music. But, by incorporating music, I retained materials so much better, that often I did not have to refer back to the books.... When I went for Medical Assisting for both clinical and administrative, I never once referred to my books... took a few notes here and there but was able to retain the information so much faster when there was music in the background playing.... Very weird for me..... but not so much when my daughter also developed the same traits as well. She had a 3.95gpa in high school thanks to using music during her study times, and even was in the band during her freshman year of high school (She had my music director also)...

Right now, I could still recite the last appeal on a pt's account that I did 30 days ago as if I had just composed it this morning... the reasons it originally denied and the departments I had to speak with pertaining to Synvisc-1 and the ramifications BCBS-Massachusetts has with coverage criteria... all because of using music as a tool to get the job done...

Its a really powerful tool

### Michael — 2012-09-25T19:47:52+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/music.3850/post-54170>

How about some music from the eccentric Australian pianist and composer Percy Grainger?

I'm something of a Grainger fan: there often seems to be an irrepressible mirth and sense of fun in his music. He was once seen walking along a London street, every so often nipping off the pavement and up and down the basement-stairs of the houses in time to a tune that was obviously running in his head.

Besides how could we not honour him here?

> Grainger would sleep naked in the winter with his windows open ... However, this is just the beginning of his sadomasochism.

Here he is playing his own arrangement of the March-Jig from Stanford's Four Irish Dances.

\[video\]\[video=youtube\_share;B5d53hnXvmA\]http://www.youtube.com/watch?v=B5d53hnXvmA\[/video\]\[/video\]
