>
>
>
> [http://www.meandmydiabetes.com/2011/02/24/medicine-for-magdalena-checkup-with-ron-rosedale/](http://http://www.meandmydiabetes.com/2011/02/24/medicine-for-magdalena-checkup-with-ron-rosedale/)
>
>
>
> How many prescribed drugs was Ozzie Osbourne on when he fell off that motorized quadricycle thing? Doctors said afterwards it was surprising he could function at all, even sit upright.

This has been my experience with doctors, too. Even when seeking out the most holistic and open-minded, they still think in terms of symptoms. But it's all Indra's Net. And we are left to sort out the complexities. You can't treat it like falling dominos.

The T3-iodine/seaweed-meds issue is one that shows this. From what I gather, Terry Wahls isn't going for an epi-paleo template. So for that reason alone, adding seaweed without the seafood could be problematic. It's hard to imagine that humans living by the sea would have eaten a lot of seaweed and not a lot of seafood, too. Seafood contains the DHA as well as the selenium that Dr K suggests we 'pre-load' with before increasing iodine consumption. And of course, each of us has the individual issue of sorting out where in this chain of events we are sitting.

It seems pretty clear that one of the main drivers of all these 'broken thyroids' is the move away from an epi-paleo template of eating. Add to that not following circadian cycles and my guess is we all get a thyroid that struggles to some degree. If we boost our T3 with carbs, then other bits of us crash... which eventually impacts our thyroids anyway.

### Michael — 2012-10-03T08:34:40+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-much-sea-veggies.4039/post-56030>

> **KiwiLauren;57175 wrote:** It's hard to imagine that humans living by the sea would have eaten a lot of seaweed and not a lot of seafood, too. Seafood contains the DHA as well as the selenium that Dr K suggests we 'pre-load' with before increasing iodine consumption.
>
>
> Now you mention it, I think I do recall the pre-loading with selenium, although that's not mentioned [here](http://http://jackkruse.com/brain-gut-6-epi-paleo-rx/) where seaweed is extensively discussed. Perhaps possible low selenium in people is why Dr. Wahls has this caution. Who knows, if she won't say?
>
>
>
>
> > It seems pretty clear that one of the main drivers of all these 'broken thyroids' is the move away from an epi-paleo template of eating.
> >
> >
> > One would suppose so.
> >
> >
> >
> > Add to that not following circadian cycles and my guess is we all get a thyroid that struggles to some degree.
>
>
>
> That sounds all very likely to me, too
>
>
>
> If we boost our T3 with carbs, then other bits of us crash... which eventually impacts our thyroids anyway.

I expect that's true, too. Hang on ... how could "safe starches" hurt us? LOL

I do like fish and have always eaten it fairly regularly. Now I suspect nowhere near regularly enough - and shellfish less often. It seems to me that the French are instructive in more ways than one. I was flicking through an old book of mine on Norman cookery and noticed that after soups and appetizers and eggs it moved onto fish and shellfish ... page after page after page ... before a chapter on "meat and game" and then poultry (much of which was duck recipes). Back in the eggs section, some of the egg dishes contained shellfish and/or fish.

I may also have damaged mine with off circadian cycles. The problems light can cause out of time is something I had little notion of until about five years ago. And likely with excessive carbohydrate and (another common cultural failing) too much alcohol.

I guess there's a possibility of autoimmunity, which seems to be the most common cause of thyroid problems in the U.S. from what I gather. Not something the NHS in the UK is going to test for. Here's one drawback of "socialized medicine": you don't pay at the point of purchase but you get what you're given.

The U.S. seems to have a bewildering array of tests on offer for different things. I don't think some of that exists anywhere else in the world.

### Michael — 2012-10-03T11:58:32+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-much-sea-veggies.4039/post-56041>

> **KiwiLauren;57175 wrote:** This has been my experience with doctors, too. Even when seeking out the most holistic and open-minded, they still think in terms of symptoms.

It's interesting how the more you hear, the more you see patterns emerging.

So this morning I've been listening to Jimmy Moore's podcast, and he's talking to a midwife. It said the episode was on women's health, but I listened anyway. I came away with the impression that women are perhaps getting an even rawer deal out of the current medical set-up than people in general. She says many women suffer PCOS, because of high-carbohydrate diets. They then go to their doctor, who prescribes the birth-control pill, which kind of addresses the symptom, but does nothing for the cause. Moreover, that can often worsen their insulin-resistance. She says that babies are born too big, because of gestational diabetes, owing to high-carb diets (actually recommended for them, of course), and that's driving caesarean sections, but the doctors don't ask why C-sections are going up, they just gaily go ahead and book them.

Here's what I think. I think they could find themselves in a sticky place, because of the internet - which spreads a lot of information very fast, much of it junk to be sure but some that isn't. I think doctors have got to raise their game or risk people losing all confidence in them.

### nonchalant — 2012-10-03T18:19:13+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-much-sea-veggies.4039/post-56069>

> **Michael;57252 wrote:** The U.S. seems to have a bewildering array of tests on offer for different things. I don't think some of that exists anywhere else in the world.

Don't worry, we're working hard to rectify that situation here in the States.

# Thread: Fish for a baby
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fish-for-a-baby.4040/>
**Subforum:** Optimal Kids
**Posts:** 8
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Destiny — 2012-10-01T02:46:39+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fish-for-a-baby.4040/post-55510>

I would like to find out when a baby can start eating fish. Also what kind?

### Shijin13 — 2012-10-01T03:21:51+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fish-for-a-baby.4040/post-55514>

> **Destiny;56581 wrote:** I would like to find out when a baby can start eating fish. Also what kind?

When they start reaching do food off your plate. Go ahead and let them have some just make sure it's cut up good or ore chew it befor giving it to your little one

### Destiny — 2012-10-02T03:20:01+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fish-for-a-baby.4040/post-55733>

> **Shijin13;56585 wrote:** When they start reaching do food off your plate. Go ahead and let them have some just make sure it's cut up good or ore chew it befor giving it to your little one

Shijin, thanks! What kind of fish is best to start with?

### Shijin13 — 2012-10-02T03:26:26+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fish-for-a-baby.4040/post-55736>

> **Destiny;56893 wrote:** Shijin, thanks! What kind of fish is best to start with?

What ever your eating! Ours started out on salmon, tuna, sardines & oysters

### kathylu — 2012-10-02T03:31:42+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fish-for-a-baby.4040/post-55738>

We always fed our baby what ever we were eating (before spices of course), just pureed, mashed or cut into appropriate sized pieces for the developmental stage. As for fish, just mash well or puree with bone broth for less than 1 year old, older babies could pick up pieces and chew it themselves since cooked fish is rather soft.

One great thing I did (I'm still patting myself on the back) is that I NEVER fed my child any grains. Until he was about 3 anyway...then it gets a little harder, since they see other kids eating crackers and bread, etc. Then we went to gluten free products and only the minimum we could get away with.

### Destiny — 2012-10-02T13:17:45+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fish-for-a-baby.4040/post-55782>

Thank you, ladies, for the great advice.

The baby is 6 months old. Should we start now or wait for another month?

### Shijin13 — 2012-10-02T14:43:07+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fish-for-a-baby.4040/post-55789>

> **Destiny;56954 wrote:** Thank you, ladies, for the great advice.
>
>
>
> The baby is 6 months old. Should we start now or wait for another month?

Is she trying to take food off your plate? If the answer to that question is no wait. Solids before 1yr is really about teaching culture. A 6mth old doesn't need solids at all. Just breast milk

Check out babyled weaning - also known as babyled solids its a way of still focusing on breast feeding while introducing solids off your plate when they show signs of readiness: loss of tongue thrust, sitting up unassisted, reaching & taking food off your plate when you introduce solids always nurse first then give then appropriate sized foods they can practice their pincher grasp and imitate your feeding skills (fingers, utensils to mouth). Expect a mess and lots of food being spit out as they're learning new skills, tastes & textures

### digital — 2012-10-08T17:03:22+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fish-for-a-baby.4040/post-56893>

We started feeding our baby Salmon, Salmon Roe, Oysters and Mussels between 6-7 months. He loves seafood over grass-fed beef/lamb/bison.

> **Destiny;56581 wrote:** I would like to find out when a baby can start eating fish. Also what kind?

# Thread: Hey squid eaters.....
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hey-squid-eaters.4045/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 20
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Endless — 2012-10-01T17:23:48+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hey-squid-eaters.4045/post-55572>

The squid I bought are whole. They appear to contain some guts and the hard thing that runs along the back. I'm scraping out those things and cutting the head off....do you eat the head/eyes too, or discard? Any hints or favorites would be welcome, this is a new experience.

### Endless — 2012-10-01T18:09:31+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hey-squid-eaters.4045/post-55585>

Ok, no takers so far...here's my experience. I decided to cut the tentacles off and discard the head because I was unsure about the 'beak' part in there....

I threw all the good stuff in a pan and sauteed it in it's own juice for 5-7 min....it turned a lovely pinky-purple color and I \*\*\*\* you not, smelled exactly like Kraft macaroni and cheese! Bizarre. Eating with a squeeze of lemon and some salt. I'd love some more elaborate preparation ideas though.

### nonchalant — 2012-10-01T18:15:39+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hey-squid-eaters.4045/post-55588>

You go, girl!

Isn't experimentation great?!

Sorry, I can't help you about squid.

### kathylu — 2012-10-01T19:31:14+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hey-squid-eaters.4045/post-55607>

[http://homecooking.about.com/od/seafood/ss/cleansquidsbs.htm](http://http://homecooking.about.com/od/seafood/ss/cleansquidsbs.htm)

How to clean squid, with photos!

### Dali Dula — 2012-10-01T20:07:02+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hey-squid-eaters.4045/post-55615>

Squid is my new pasta. You should eat everything but the beak. clean the tentacles, skin and head and set aside for a different meal. now take the hollow body and slice it crosswise as thin as you can this creates rings. Now slice all your rings in half by cutting lengthwise one cut. You now have squid noodles. Saute in fav sauce or toss in soup broth. Squid does not need to cook long. Delicious chewy noodles! I have been making a sauce with lobster roe, olive oil and curry. Drizzle lemon or lime. Oh yeah.

I get frozen already clean squid bodies at the asian market for 2.99lb

### Shijin13 — 2012-10-01T20:37:43+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hey-squid-eaters.4045/post-55625>

squid or octopus smoked on the grill! yum. the kids throw fits when we have fish and don't have squid/octopus.

we buy them frozen and cleaned....though our international mkt sells them whole, and will remove the beak if we ask.

### Endless — 2012-10-01T20:40:00+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hey-squid-eaters.4045/post-55627>

What about the eyes? I couldn't figure out how to save the head without getting the beak part, but I'll bet the eyes are pretty nutritious.

### Shijin13 — 2012-10-01T20:43:07+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hey-squid-eaters.4045/post-55629>

> **Endless;56783 wrote:** What about the eyes? I couldn't figure out how to save the head without getting the beak part, but I'll bet the eyes are pretty nutritious.

we eat the whole things we get... not sure how they get the beak out...but we eat everything... the kids think the eyes and tentacles are cool!

### Endless — 2012-10-01T20:43:16+0300

> How to clean squid, with photos!

Fantastic link, thanks!

### KiwiLauren — 2012-10-01T21:02:23+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hey-squid-eaters.4045/post-55635>

Endless, I had to use the step-by-step photos, too. In the end, after you do it once it's pretty easy. That cuttle bone was amazing to remove! As for the eyes, I swallowed them raw. My zinc for the day

### freesia — 2012-10-01T22:11:55+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hey-squid-eaters.4045/post-55651>

I could not believe how positively my kids reacted to the tentacles either!  they count them as they eat them!

We get them guts and beak removed, ready to cook. I slice them and stir fry them w ginger mushrooms and bacon in macadamia oil. Kids and husband had leek and watercress soup with their surf & turf stirfry, and I had a handful of seaweed.

Happy days.

### Integral_D — 2012-10-09T19:41:23+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hey-squid-eaters.4045/post-57120>

Long ago I lived in Monterey, CA and several of the restaurant/diners there served "squid and eggs" for breakfast. I haven't seen a recipe, but basically the rings were breaded (in something with a lot of herbs) and fried, served along with eggs and biscuits, just like bacon or sausage. I've never seen it in any other place, but man was it good! It may go back to the Greek and Portugese fishermen that worked that area. My favorite joint was the Fat Cat Cafe in Pacific Grove. Located in the corner of a trailer park (right at the entrance to 17-Mile Drive no less), no two chairs were alike, there was a jar of homemade blackberry jam on every table, and a picture of Willie Nelson on the wall over the stove. Squid omelets too.... You could get squid&eggs at other places like at some of the humbler diners down on Fisherman's Wharf (this was before the Aquarium even opened) but none could match the Fat Cat. There is a world full of yummy things to do with squid, from nearly any culture that had anything to do with the sea.

**Squid Cleaning**: This page is a treasure trove of how-to: [http://www.foodandwine.com/articles/how-to-clean-fish-and-other-seafood](http://http://www.foodandwine.com/articles/how-to-clean-fish-and-other-seafood)

Jacques Pepin is a great teacher of how to prepare stuff starting with a whole critter, and he really is good at showing how to do the basics. I basically learned how to cook (use a knife, cook an egg) by watching his old PBS shows. If you want to eat further down the food chain, he's got great recipes for fish, shellfish, darned near anything. He's French, though, so I'm guessing the raw mackerel head smoothie is not really his thing....

### janagram — 2012-10-09T21:02:30+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hey-squid-eaters.4045/post-57130>

they say the french will eat anything....

# Thread: Swinging TSH problem
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/swinging-tsh-problem.4058/>
**Subforum:** Optimal Labs
**Posts:** 3
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Eve — 2012-10-02T05:17:08+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/swinging-tsh-problem.4058/post-55760>

Since I switched from levothyroxine to NDT, I've been having problems with my TSH. It's been swinging from very low to mid-range.

Some history:

Diagnosed hypo Feb 2008. Started 50mcg Eltroxin. Retested approx April 2008 and July 2008 (results from memory)

TSH ~1.4

free t4 and t3 in range....I think t4 was mid or upper quartile, and t3 was mid or low-normal

Hashi's - no

After starting meds, still hair loss, fatigue but not the "hypo" fatigue

Jan 2012 (and very similar results in Mar 2012)

taking 50mcg Eltroxin & 40mg Allergy Research Group Natural Thyroid since Dec 2011

TSH 0.09 (0.35 - 5.50)

free t4 16.2 (10 - 19)

free t3 4.0 (3.5 - 6.5)

Hair loss decreased, some fatigue but not "hypo" kind

March 2012 switched completely to NDT Erfa brand on my request - 60mg

Jul 09/12

TSH 2.42

free t4 9.1 (10 - 19)

free t3 3.0 (3.5 - 6.5)

Tested for prolactinoma (hypopituary prob) - no

Feeling very hypo, hair loss again, major "hypo" fatigue --> upped NDT to 90mg. Felt better initially for about ~3-4 wks then started feeling hypo again

Sept 19/12

TSH 0.27 (.35 - 5.50)

free t4 11.0

free t3 3.8

Antibody Peroxidase Antibody

### Patty Cakes — 2012-10-02T05:23:27+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/swinging-tsh-problem.4058/post-55762>

60 mg is a very small dose in my opinion. I think you could probably add 30 mg in the afternoon. Before I switched to T3 only I was on 150 gm (two doses) of NDT. Make sure you iron and adrenals are good in order to handle the increase. Also I didn't see the other Hashis test TgAB. I don't know if you can get that in Canada but it is another marker for Hashis even with negative results on the TPO antibodies.

### finnite@dccnet.com — 2012-10-02T18:23:04+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/swinging-tsh-problem.4058/post-55844>

TSH can swing up to 3 points during the day, were all your labs taken at the same time of the day?

If you are in Canada and on NDT I'm guessing you are taking ERFA? if that is the case there have been issues with the amount of T3 in the ERFA and there is a subset of the population that cannot absorb the ERFA properly because of the fillers.

You may want to try a compounding pharmacy, your doctor will give you an Rx that simply says "thyroid" and then you take it to the compounder and they will ensure that the filler used is something you can abosorb.

ERFA has cornstarch and talc as fillers

# Thread: Why the High fat/Protein Meal before CT sessions
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/why-the-high-fat-protein-meal-before-ct-sessions.4068/>
**Subforum:** Cold Thermogenesis
**Posts:** 9
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Robbie Bourke — 2012-10-02T17:53:52+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/why-the-high-fat-protein-meal-before-ct-sessions.4068/post-55837>

Hi guys I know this probably has been covered somewhere before, but could someone tell why Jack recommends the meal before CT?

### QuantumRadical — 2012-10-02T21:03:54+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/why-the-high-fat-protein-meal-before-ct-sessions.4068/post-55902>

> **Robbie Bourke;57028 wrote:** Hi guys I know this probably has been covered somewhere before, but could someone tell why Jack recommends the meal before CT?

Fuel for the furnace. The whole idea of CT is to get the brain to tell the rest of the body to generate heat to maintain body temperature--with all the subsequent health benefits therefrom. You need fuel in the furnace to generate the heat. Eating a high fat meal before CT helps this along.

### villjamur_stevenson — 2012-10-02T22:01:15+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/why-the-high-fat-protein-meal-before-ct-sessions.4068/post-55924>

From reading the literature, high fat meals get UCP1 going. I'm not sure what the significance of high eating protein is in relation to BAT.

### nonchalant — 2012-10-02T22:37:24+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/why-the-high-fat-protein-meal-before-ct-sessions.4068/post-55940>

I think it might be an electron-counting thing. Your body doesn't have enough time to digest the meal and use the fats for fuel in time for the CT session, but it will know that the nutrients are on the way. So it doesn't have to worry about burning a few thousand calories as heat.

### QuantumRadical — 2012-10-04T19:16:16+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/why-the-high-fat-protein-meal-before-ct-sessions.4068/post-56251>

> **Nonchalant;57153 wrote:** I think it might be an electron-counting thing. Your body doesn't have enough time to digest the meal and use the fats for fuel in time for the CT session, but it will know that the nutrients are on the way. So it doesn't have to worry about burning a few thousand calories as heat.

I like this. Thinking about it, probably several benefits derive from eating the right stuff before getting COLD. Depending on the length of time in CT, the benefits are probably spread out over time. My experience with longer CT sessions is a slight case of heartburn, so something is going on in the gut during the CT. I suspect the gut flora are at war over energy expenditure and distribution, and the brain is desperately signaling the forces to maintain core temperature. Hopefully Jack might chime in and provide a more scientific basis for the pre-CT high-quality meal.

### cuivie@me.com — 2012-10-07T06:20:30+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/why-the-high-fat-protein-meal-before-ct-sessions.4068/post-56675>

When I'm keto-adapted (carnivore diet, daily 19-24 hour fasts), I don't notice a difference whether I eat before CT or not. If I try to eat upon rising, my satiety system and ability to go long periods without eating gets royally screwed up and I have to eat something before CT if I want to not shiver badly after only 5 min.

Needless to say, I'm back to daily IFing and do CT whenever it is convenient for my schedule.

### x_danny_x — 2012-10-08T00:09:09+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/why-the-high-fat-protein-meal-before-ct-sessions.4068/post-56816>

I usually only wait 15 to 20 minutes for the food to go down before jumping in the shower. Man I take showers and not baths. they are brutal but I just have no patience. I want to start right away.

**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/why-the-high-fat-protein-meal-before-ct-sessions.4068/post-57293>

# Thread: Viking Dishes
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/viking-dishes.4072/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 7
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Michael — 2012-10-02T20:44:13+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/viking-dishes.4072/post-55894>

> Putrefied shark meat. Sheep heads and testicles. Some of Iceland's traditional delicacies might challenge a few palates, but if you're looking for a little edible adventure, eating like a Viking just might be the way to go. ...
>
>
>
> "Either you love this or hate it," \[Halldor Sigurdsson of Reykjavik\] said of his stewed fish meal. "Imagine a rotten fish, and multiply that smell and taste by 97, and that is the smell and taste of this strange food."
>
>
> [http://eatocracy.cnn.com/2011/09/09/eat-like-a-viking-in-iceland/](http://http://eatocracy.cnn.com/2011/09/09/eat-like-a-viking-in-iceland/)
>
>
>
> Inger would be right at home. ;-)
>
>
>
>
>
>
>
> Seafood in Iceland back in Viking times is mentioned in this passage from _Egils saga_:
>
>
>
> Skallagrim was also a great shipwright. There was plenty of driftwood to be had west of Myrar, so he built and ran another farm at Alftaness and from there his men went out fishing and seal-hunting, and collecting the eggs of wild fowl, for there was plenty of everything. They also fetched in his driftwood. Whales often got stranded, and you could shoot anything you wanted, for none of the wildlife was used to man and just stood around quietly.

[http://www.vikinganswerlady.com/food.shtml](http://http://www.vikinganswerlady.com/food.shtml)

The same source has this on "Food from the Sea, Rivers and Lakes":

Food from the Sea, Rivers and Lakes: **It is estimated by scholars that up to 25% of the calories in the diet of coastal Norwegians would have come from fish in normal years. The fish resources in the Atlantic off the western coasts of Scandinavia were (and continue to be) extremely rich, providing **cod and coalfish, and freshwater would have been a source of **salmon. Even Norwegians who lived in the interior had access to high proportions of fish in their diets, since coastal people would have traded fish for timber and other goods. Shrimp were also eaten.

In Eastern Scandinavia as well fish was an important part of the diet, with **herring being caught in BohuslÃ¤n, off Denmark, and in the Baltic, and salmon in the rivers and lakes. Other saltwater fish known to have been eaten include **haddock, flat-fish, ling, horse mackerel, smelt, and saithe.

There is also evidence that a variety of freshwater and estuarine fish and shellfish** were eaten. Most of the evidence for freshwater fish consumption comes from JorvÃ­k (modern York) and the Danelaw. Freshwater fish included roach, rudd, and bream, with perch and pike** being the most commonly found freshwater fish at archaeological sites. We have evidence for estuarine fish from both England and the Viking holdings in Dublin, including oysters, cockles, mussels, winkles, smelt, eels, salmon, and scallops**.

In northern Scandinavia, the dry, cold conditions allowed fish to be preserved almost indefinitely by drying. The fish (mostly cod) was strung up and hung it from a rod or "stock" and allowed to dry. This produced "stockfish"**, called skreiÃ° ("sharp-fish") in Old Norse. During the Viking Age, the rock-hard skreiÃ° was prepared for eating by being beaten and pounded to break up the fibers, and served with butter. ...

Whales** were also an important food resource during the Viking Age. The sagas frequently mention complex conflicts that arose because of disputes over the legal rights of a landowner to the meat, blubber, and bone from beached whales. It was probably extremely rare that ships went out and harpooned whales, and probably only Iceland and the Faroe Islands used this method of whaling. Whales were also trapped in inlets and bays with narrow openings, where they were frightened and driven aground from boats, or shot with poisoned arrows. The Old English Orosius tells how the chieftain Ã“ttar (Ohthere, in Old English) of northern Norway hunted whales ...

(And in his own land is the best whale-hunting: they are 48 ells long (180 feet), and the largest are 50 ells long. There he said that he and five others slew sixty in two days.)

If the number of whales killed here is correct, then Ã“ttar and his five men must have driven a pod of small whales such as pilot whales onto shore, and there killed them using knives and spears.

**Porpoises and seals were also hunted. The most important seal product was blubber, which was eaten in place of butter or used for frying. Apparently seal meat was not a particularly prized food, but was eaten by peasants because other meat was scarce.

In addition, various sea birds and their eggs** would have been consumed.

### Shijin13 — 2012-10-04T02:55:35+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hey-punkin.4073/post-56136>

This inspired me today when making egg cups for breakfast tomorrow. Other than eggs I was out of my usual ingredients so I worked w/what I had

6eggs, vanilla extract, 1/4-1/2c (depending on consistency) coconut flour, ghee (1/4c), cream (1/2c), liquid stevia, and some cooked butternut squash, nutmeg & cinnamon. Blended it all together, poured it into muffin tins, popped it in the oven @350

We'll see tomorrow if they're any good

### kathylu — 2012-10-04T03:15:04+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hey-punkin.4073/post-56140>

Made zucchini latkes for the little one tonight. Grate 3 zucchini, sprinkle lightly with salt, mix, wait 15 minutes. Then wring dry in a clean dishtowel. Mix squash with 2 eggs, 1 tsp each vanilla and cinnamon, 1 tbsp coconut flour. Drop a dollop in hot coconut oil, fry until brown on both sides. Serve with applesauce and/or sour cream on top. Pretty yummy!

# Thread: So, if I wake before dawn....
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/so-if-i-wake-before-dawn.4074/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 7
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Momma28 — 2012-10-02T21:31:14+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/so-if-i-wake-before-dawn.4074/post-55913>

Should we use artificial lights in the bathroom or check our email before the sun is up and we've had a chance to look at it? Dr. K., did u mean on the seasonal thread post that our cortisol levels in the morning, which are what wakes us up...are reduced with artificial lights? I'm just below you in Mississippi and it's still pitch black out at 5:30 in the morn when I naturally rise, well rested, and ready to start my day. I go down early so I can rise early to have time for myself before all the kids get up with the sun and would really like to continue this practice.

### ealachan — 2012-10-02T21:35:13+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/so-if-i-wake-before-dawn.4074/post-55915>

I have to get up at 4 to 4:15am anywhere from two to four days a week, depending on my husband's work schedule. On days when I have to be up before the sun rises, I put my blue-blocker glasses on as soon as I wake up, and I try to limit my exposure to bright lights even when I have the glasses on. For me, this means dimmed overhead lighting and no computer screens.

### nonchalant — 2012-10-02T22:41:51+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/so-if-i-wake-before-dawn.4074/post-55942>

What ealachan said.

I usually wait until the sun has been up for a while to turn on the computer. But with Winter coming on, that is going to be more and more inconvenient. Guess I'll have to adjust. But that's what we humans do best.

### Shijin13 — 2012-10-02T22:52:10+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/so-if-i-wake-before-dawn.4074/post-55944>

I'm optimal at night, but I'm not as optimal during the weekday mornings (what with getting ready for work, getting the kids up and all that's entailed with getting us out the house on time to get to work)

that said, I do wear blue blockers in the office b/c I'm stuck in Florescent hell in a windowless office

### freesia — 2012-10-02T23:12:29+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/so-if-i-wake-before-dawn.4074/post-55948>

> **ealachan;57128 wrote:** I have to get up at 4 to 4:15am anywhere from two to four days a week, depending on my husband's work schedule. On days when I have to be up before the sun rises, I put my blue-blocker glasses on as soon as I wake up, and I try to limit my exposure to bright lights even when I have the glasses on. For me, this means dimmed overhead lighting and no computer screens.

Same as ealachan. I use low artificial light and blue blockers. I wake at 5am and work for an hour (handwork that doesn't require good light). Now that I'm post leptin reset I don't eat until 6.30am.

I'd be really happy to sleep an extra hour in the am, but CT at night knocks me out cold  and I'm asleep by 9.30pm so getting enough sleep overall.

### Momma28 — 2012-10-04T04:01:58+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/so-if-i-wake-before-dawn.4074/post-56156>

OK...thinking out loud here. Wouldn't we WANT the body to believe it is morning even if its just 4:15? That is our normal, daily wake up time. Wouldn't we WANT the body to start its cortisol release and brain signaling no matter what time we started our day? Paleoman probably wouldn't have started his day in pitch blackness, but is it important that our hours in darkness versus hours in daylight exactly match the season we are in if the variation is only an hour or two? Does this throw off the season recognition mechanism in the brain?

If I go to bed at 8:30 and am in absolute darkness, get my 8 hours and am ready to wake, fully rested...I don't see what the problem is with artificial light for an hour or two, provided I am getting natural sunlight throughout the rest of my day.

Anybody else feel this way? I mean...is there such a thing as taking it too far?

### ealachan — 2012-10-04T16:52:04+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/so-if-i-wake-before-dawn.4074/post-56217>

> **Momma28;57409 wrote:** OK...thinking out loud here. Wouldn't we WANT the body to believe it is morning even if its just 4:15? That is our normal, daily wake up time. Wouldn't we WANT the body to start its cortisol release and brain signaling no matter what time we started our day? Paleoman probably wouldn't have started his day in pitch blackness, but is it important that our hours in darkness versus hours in daylight exactly match the season we are in if the variation is only an hour or two? Does this throw off the season recognition mechanism in the brain?
>
>
>
> If I go to bed at 8:30 and am in absolute darkness, get my 8 hours and am ready to wake, fully rested...I don't see what the problem is with artificial light for an hour or two, provided I am getting natural sunlight throughout the rest of my day.
>
>
>
> Anybody else feel this way? I mean...is there such a thing as taking it too far?

My understanding is that it's not that you don't want your body to know it's morning, it's that artificial light creates TOO much of a cortisol response vs. natural light. This, over time, can cause chronic issues which then lead to inflammation. Also, there are a lot of systems in the body that are tied to circadian rhythm. If your rhythm is whacked out, those systems will be whacked out as well. Best way to heal your own internal clock is to mind the sun's rhythm very closely.

That's my quick-and-dirty version, anyway. You should re-read the blog posts for more / better info.


══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# Thread: Nuts and inflammation

Just thought maybe someone knew what was in them.

My understanding with AI is that you get varying symptoms (including inflammatory foods that cause no obvious symptoms). It may be that some nuts are simply less inflammatory for you, too.

### Inger — 2012-10-03T09:39:07+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/nuts-and-inflammation.4075/post-56034>

### nonchalant — 2012-10-03T18:14:19+0300

I suppose I should find my nutcracker.

# Thread: Paleo Salad Dressing
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/paleo-salad-dressing.4076/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 14
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Raiken3712 — 2012-10-02T21:46:25+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/paleo-salad-dressing.4076/post-55918>

So I was wanting to make a basic salad dressing for using with Spinach and maybe some other leafy greens.

[**This**](http://http://paleodietlifestyle.com/salad-dressing-and-vinaigrettes/) is confusing me........

It says a good rule of thumb is to use 1 part Vinegar and 3 parts oil...and than all the recipes call for things completely different than the rule of thumb. For example the Balsamic vinaigrette says 3/4 cup of Vinegar and Oil both...that's 50/50 not 25/75 which is what 1 part vinegar 3 parts oil would be.

What am I missing? Also have any recommendations for a good site to bookmark? I don't have many ingredients though I'd be willing to buy more for later. I was going to try one with Apple Cider Vinegar, Olive oil, coconut oil possibly, mustard, black pepper, and garlic.

Does that sound like it would be ok? Also since I'm confused about the whole recipe thing what amounts of each thing do I need and is a blender the ideal way to make it?

Thanks

### ealachan — 2012-10-02T22:03:36+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/paleo-salad-dressing.4076/post-55925>

The beauty of a vinaigrette is that it's infinitely customizeable. There's really no right or wrong way to do it. Do what tastes good. I tend to do 1 part vinegar to 2 parts olive oil if I'm using something like ACV or red wine vinegar. If I'm using balsamic, I'll use a bit less because the sweetness can be overwhelming. Start out with whatever ratio you think sounds good, and taste it. If it doesn't taste good, add something and taste again. You'll eventually hit on something you like.

Minced (or grated) raw garlic in a vinaigrette is fabulous, but be prepared for heat / pungency, and dragon-breath after the fact.  If raw garlic isn't quite your thing, try minced shallots instead...a little bit more mild. A little squirt of prepared Dijon mustard is also good in a vinaigrette and will help keep it emulsified for longer. Also, don't forget the salt. Greens need salt.

You don't need a blender at all...just a whisk and a bowl. Dump ingredients in bowl, whisk, toss with greens. Easy peasy. That recipe you linked to is WAYYYYYYY overcomplicating things. You don't need to drizzle anything in slowly. That's only important if you're making aioli or mayo and you're worried about incorporating air. Salad dressing is not about air. At least, not at my house. I don't even keep dressing prepped ahead of time - I make it right in the salad bowl, right before we eat.

### janagram — 2012-10-02T22:28:47+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/paleo-salad-dressing.4076/post-55933>

I just made a great dressing for an asian style salad...with almond butter, apple cider vinegar and mac nut oil, in about equal proportions, or you could cut back a bit on the vinegar....plus minced garlic, ginger, and some tamari or salt to taste!

### Shijin13 — 2012-10-02T23:10:06+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/paleo-salad-dressing.4076/post-55947>

here's one of my favorites:

bacon fat, apple cider vinegar, 1Tb of mustard... heat in pan and serve over veggies... nom, nom,nom

### ealachan — 2012-10-02T23:36:24+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/paleo-salad-dressing.4076/post-55958>

> **Shijin13;57160 wrote:** here's one of my favorites:
>
>
>
> bacon fat, apple cider vinegar, 1Tb of mustard... heat in pan and serve over veggies... nom, nom,nom

So good over potatoes, if you happen to eat them...LOL

### jeanie@truelongmont.com — 2012-10-02T23:39:24+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/paleo-salad-dressing.4076/post-55959>

taste taste taste. I like a very vinegar heavy one, but that changes too. I put my in a jar with lid and just shake it up right before pouring. Less dishes! A little mustard added makes everything stay mixed up.

Balsamic is my fav, I think it goes with everything.

This isn't bakin, you can't go wrong because you can always add more of one if needed to your taste. Don't forget to add a little salt

### jeanie@truelongmont.com — 2012-10-02T23:40:12+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/paleo-salad-dressing.4076/post-55960>

> **janagram;57146 wrote:** I just made a great dressing for an asian style salad...with almond butter, apple cider vinegar and mac nut oil, in about equal proportions, or you could cut back a bit on the vinegar....plus minced garlic, ginger, and some tamari or salt to taste!

Girl, that sounds awesome! I'm puttin this on the list!

### janagram — 2012-10-02T23:52:47+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/paleo-salad-dressing.4076/post-55961>

thanks...to be honest I think it could be better with balsamic (for the sweetness)

### fitness@home — 2012-10-03T00:32:43+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/paleo-salad-dressing.4076/post-55965>

I make this Pesto Vinaigrette in my Magic Bullet:

1C fresh basil leaves

2 TBL olive oil

1 TBL vinegar

1 clove garlic

Pinch of salt

Fresh ground pepper

I have some basil growing in the backyard. Will have to make some of this soon...

### diane — 2012-10-03T00:34:47+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/paleo-salad-dressing.4076/post-55966>

I have an awesome dressing that has ginger, garlic, cilantro leaves, olive oil, rice vinegar (which can be substituted) with a bit of wishabi. So delicious!

### Raiken3712 — 2012-10-03T06:59:44+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/paleo-salad-dressing.4076/post-56024>

Thanks for all the ideas and tips. I'll see about making some tomorrow as I went to work early today so no time. How much dressing do you usually use for just yourself? Half-cup, cup, perhaps depending on how big of a salad you're eating?

### ealachan — 2012-10-03T17:50:32+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/paleo-salad-dressing.4076/post-56060>

> **Raiken3712;57246 wrote:** Thanks for all the ideas and tips. I'll see about making some tomorrow as I went to work early today so no time. How much dressing do you usually use for just yourself? Half-cup, cup, perhaps depending on how big of a salad you're eating?

Nowhere near that, for me. I use maybe 3 tbsp, tops. This is part of why I mix the dressing up in the salad bowl - if you put the dressing in the bowl and then toss the greens in it, you get a little dressing on all the greens, rather than getting a whole bunch of dressing on some parts and none on others.

### Raiken3712 — 2012-10-03T18:22:04+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/paleo-salad-dressing.4076/post-56071>

> **ealachan;57286 wrote:** Nowhere near that, for me. I use maybe 3 tbsp, tops. This is part of why I mix the dressing up in the salad bowl - if you put the dressing in the bowl and then toss the greens in it, you get a little dressing on all the greens, rather than getting a whole bunch of dressing on some parts and none on others.

I was just throwing amounts out there...if I would have thought about it more I would have used tablespoons not cups . Not that it matters but thanks for the input.

So if you were going to do 1 part vinegar 3 parts oil with ACV, Coconut Oil, EVOO, Mustard, and Spices how much would you put of each for 1 serving of salad. It would be an easy conversion if making more salad.

Since you said you put the salad in the bowl you mixed. I'm guessing that means you only make enough for the salad you are eating.

Perhaps 1 tablespoon Vinegar 2 tablespoons Evoo, and 1 tablespoon coconut oil with 1 tsp mustard? I'll figure it out but like to have a good plan before starting. I like to have some idea with new things.

Thanks

### kcorcoran@comcast.net — 2012-10-04T01:53:03+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/paleo-salad-dressing.4076/post-56131>

I would start with one part vinegar to one part oil or fat of your choice, (bacon fat is awesome) add salt, pepper, parsley, thyme, 1/2 tsp dijon mustard, to taste. Just make up enough for what you'll immediately use, no need to make up a bunch ahead of time.

# Thread: Help with latest thyroid labs
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/help-with-latest-thyroid-labs.4077/>
**Subforum:** Optimal Labs
**Posts:** 10
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### bigknitwit — 2012-10-02T21:46:40+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/help-with-latest-thyroid-labs.4077/post-55919>

TSH 1.85

Total T4 3.2 (4.5 - 12)

FT4 1.1 (didn't grab range over phone, but I'm below range)

FT3 1.7 (2.3-4.2)

HS-CRP 0.5

She won't run RT3, since Mayo Clinic says they can't find a kit to give reporduceable results.

Sigh... These labs were run at 9am, and I hadn't taken any of my cytomel beforehand (normally take 10 throughout the day).

She wants to run a binding globulin lab and repeat all of the above tomorrow morning, and then possibly do a pituitary MRI. All this and I've been feeling significantly better lately... Is there anything more I should request? She will run an 8am cortisol in 10 days (couldn't schedule it sooner, so I opted for 2 draws).

I take 150mcg T4 (tirosint - it's a gel capsule to help with absorption), and 10mg cytomel. I don't seem to absorb much T4, no matter what form I take it in (at least that's how I'm interpreting things as of late). On very high Armour doses (4.5 grains), I was only barely getting my T4 numbers in range. I absorb vitamin D pretty well -enough to shift the numbers on blood draws as one could expect.

I should add that I have Hashimoto's, and need full replacement doses of thyroid hormone

### Shijin13 — 2012-10-05T15:57:15+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/help-with-latest-thyroid-labs.4077/post-56467>

Go to private MD labs [http://www.privatemdlabs.com/](http://http://www.privatemdlabs.com/) and order the RT3 - and have your Rt3 run on the same day as your next labs -

the following coupon code will work to take 15% off h9d6ls4 (it will only work 3xs) at least then you can get your RT3.

### Patty Cakes — 2012-10-05T18:45:10+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/help-with-latest-thyroid-labs.4077/post-56495>

Maybe you are a candidate for T3 only treatment. If the T4 isn't doing much for you maybe you can slowly increase the Cytomel. I'm on T3 only and ran labs prior to taking my morning T3 dose and my T3 is low. IIRC it only lasts 8 hrs in the body so there isn't anything left at 9am for you to measure. If your adrenals/iron are OK you should be able to tolerate a replacement dosage of T3 w/o the use of T4.

### bigknitwit — 2012-10-05T19:58:13+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/help-with-latest-thyroid-labs.4077/post-56503>

I am thinking about T3 only as well, since that's all the seems to be showing up in my blood.

However, this past summer, I was doing Armour (and only absorbing a minimum of the T4), and it was awful. I was probably taking way more than I was ready for. Got my FT3 into the top quartile, and could barely function (meanwhile, T4 was below range). High heartrates and low blood pressures, especially in the mornings. I felt like I wasn't getting enough blood into my brain. Orthostatic hypotension. Often it was a struggle to be standing. I was up to 4.5 grains of Armour at the maximum (which is almost 40mg T3).

My ferritin is always a problem. Last checked it was 34 (up from 24). Meanwhile my % saturation went to 88%... I don't think my adrenals are okay...

Now, on my new thyroid dose, my weight is climbing, and there's not a lot I can do about it (up 13 pounds since the beginning of August. Still eating properly, etc). My weight seems to correlate pretty decently with the amount of T3 in my system. In the summer, with great T3 levels, my weight dropped down to my optimal setpoint. I need to gently push my T3 up.

### Patty Cakes — 2012-10-05T22:34:47+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/help-with-latest-thyroid-labs.4077/post-56518>

I have a feeling you won't be able to handle the doses you need of T3 until you get those adrenals fixed. The iron is probably a problem but maybe you can get iron shots to get more in your system faster??? Or if your ferritin high as well it could be a sign of inflammation while still not having enough iron.

Maybe Paul Robinson's book on using T3 to help the adrenals would be helpful: [http://www.amazon.com/Recovering-T3-Journey-Hypothyroidism-Thyroid/dp/0957099304/ref=sr\_1\_1?ie=UTF8&qid=1349465653&sr=8-1&keywords=paul+robinson+t3](http://http://www.amazon.com/Recovering-T3-Journey-Hypothyroidism-Thyroid/dp/0957099304/ref=sr_1_1?ie=UTF8&qid=1349465653&sr=8-1&keywords=paul+robinson+t3)

### bigknitwit — 2012-10-05T22:55:12+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/help-with-latest-thyroid-labs.4077/post-56522>

> **Patty Cakes;57779 wrote:** I have a feeling you won't be able to handle the doses you need of T3 until you get those adrenals fixed. The iron is probably a problem but maybe you can get iron shots to get more in your system faster??? Or if your ferritin high as well it could be a sign of inflammation while still not having enough iron.
>
>
>
> Maybe Paul Robinson's book on using T3 to help the adrenals would be helpful: [http://www.amazon.com/Recovering-T3-Journey-Hypothyroidism-Thyroid/dp/0957099304/ref=sr\_1\_1?ie=UTF8&qid=1349465653&sr=8-1&keywords=paul+robinson+t3](http://http://www.amazon.com/Recovering-T3-Journey-Hypothyroidism-Thyroid/dp/0957099304/ref=sr_1_1?ie=UTF8&qid=1349465653&sr=8-1&keywords=paul+robinson+t3)

Yeah - that's what this summer showed me. After that catastrophe, I dropped to T4/low T3. I had been hoping to reset my adrenal function using cycloset. But within 2 weeks I was having numbness issues in the fingers and feet. Since I've discontinued it, the numbness has gone away. I might consider trying again once my thyroid hormones are a little more improved. Meanwhile, I'm being careful about light, trying to get first morning light on my eyes, etc. The hypotension symptoms have cleared, and overall I am feeling reasonably energetic. Not spectacular, but reasonable. No foggy brain either... I'm starting to wonder if there was a lab error. I am really curious what my new labs (done 2 days ago) will show. I did them with 2.5 mgs of T3 taken 3 hours beforehand.

### JanSz — 2012-10-06T19:22:53+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/help-with-latest-thyroid-labs.4077/post-56610>

Post your steroid hormones labs or do this list at LabCorp, and post it:

1 ------- Pregnenolone, Mass Spectrometry--Test Number: 140707 CPT Code: 84140

2 ------- 17α-Hydroxypregnenolone by HPLC-MS/MS

3 ------- 17α-Hydroxyprogesterone by HPLC-MS/MS

4 ------- Progesterone--Test Number: 004317 CPT Code: 84144

5 ------- Cortisol, Free, Serum (Free Cortisol, Serum Cortisol, CBG)

6 ------- Cortisol, AM & PM Test Number: 104000 CPT Code: 82533(x2) (7:30AM, 12PM)

7 ------- Corticosteroid-binding Globulin (CBG), Transcortin

8 ------- Corticotropin Releasing Hormone (CRH)

9 ------- Dehydroepiandrosterone (DHEA) Sulfate--Test Number: 004020 CPT Code: 82627

10 ------- Dehydroepiandrosterone (DHEA)--Test Number: 004100 CPT Code: 82626

11 ------- Androstenedione, LCMS--Test Number: 004705 CPT Code: 82157

12 ------- Testosterone, Total, Serum Test Number: 004226 CPT Code: 84403

13 ------- Testosterone, Free and Weakly Bound--Test Number: 143255 CPT Code: 84402; 84403

14 ------- SHBG

15 ------- Dihydrotestosterone (DHT)--Test Number: 500142 CPT Code: 82651

16 ------- Androstanediol Glucuronide (Endocrine Sciences) Test Number: 500881 CPT Code: 82154

17 ------- Estrogens, Total; Test Number: 004549 CPT Code: 82672

18 ------- Estradiol, Sensitive Test Number: 140244 CPT Code: 82670

19 ------- Estrone, Serum Test Number: 004564 CPT Code: 82679

\-----------------

Eat selenium and iodine/iodide

Study and supplement your miscronutrients, fatty acids

do not eat fish oils and/or krill oils

...

### bigknitwit — 2012-10-06T20:36:48+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/help-with-latest-thyroid-labs.4077/post-56616>

I spoke with my endocrinologist last week, and talked to her a little about doing more labs. Cholesterol, pregnenelone, DHEA, etc, etc. She said that she hesitates to run labs when she doesn't know what she's going to do with the results. I told her a low cortisol would tell me I'm living life oxidizing, low pregnenelone would tell me there's very little mother hormone to make everything downstream. She said that even still, if I am oxidizing away, there's very little she can do with this data.

I know, it's time to start doctor hunting (again). But I want to give her one last try, because she does mean well, she does listen, and she is open minded, at least somewhat. Somehow when I am speaking to her, I am embarrased to say that I can't articulate a bottom line reason to run this kind of thorough lab panel. I can say that low pregnenelone means I have little available resources to make other hormones. When all the other hormones are low as a result, bad things start happening, depending on how the cookie crumbles. It would give me better direction on how much to supplement precurser hormones (like pregnenelone and DHEA). But really, I am blanking on the higher view of why I need hormones running in the top quartile that really hits it home. There's so much spinning in my head these days. I'm sure I had the higher view before, it's just cloudy now. I'm off to re-read hormones 101.

### JanSz — 2012-10-07T02:08:27+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/help-with-latest-thyroid-labs.4077/post-56647>

### bigknitwit — 2012-10-15T19:24:51+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/help-with-latest-thyroid-labs.4077/post-58341>

I have new labs drawn 8 days after these old ones, and I took 2.5mg cytomel 3 hours before the draw. I find it interesting that my TSH really nose-dived in that time frame, as the new HRT got to the 6 week point. At least my pituitary is doing something...

TSH 5.45

Total T4 4.3 (4.5-12)

Free T3 2.2 (2.3 - 4.2)

Total T3 33 (76-181)

Free T4 1.1 (0.8 - 2.7)

Current HRT has me taking 150mcg T4, plus 10mcg T3. Doctor agreed to increase my T3 to 15mg. I will do the other hormone testing once I have my thyroid labs in a better place. For me the thyroid is huge because I don't have any functioning gland left. Once my thyroid numbers approach better territory, I think I will be better positioned to evaluate what's going on with all the other hormones. Meanwhile, I am supping DHEA (4mg - had it compounded, and this dose was based on saliva labs, from ASI), 7-Keto DHEA 10mg (i prefer to go lightly, given what others are experiencing with high T), and 30mg pregnenelone (I think I will push this to 60, based on my labs).

I don't understand why I don't absorb T4 very well. This tells me something about my gut (it's badly off), but I would like to figure out more specifics here. The same thing happened with Armour over the summer - great T3 absorption, very little T4 absorption.

# Thread: Armour Dosing
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/armour-dosing.4083/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 5
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### AKMan — 2012-10-03T02:25:52+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/armour-dosing.4083/post-55982>

I finally got my doc to put me on Armour. I stopped my Synthroid for 4 months and my TSH went to 5.23 (ref .34 - 4.82) and T4 went to .97 (.59 - 1.61). They didn't test T3. I am having some hypo symptoms, mainly cold fingers and toes.

I started Armour 30mg on 1 Sep. Last week my TSH was 3.05 (.34 - 4.82) and FT4 was 1.01 (.59 - 1.61).

I talked them into increasing my Armour to 60mg/day to see if we can get my TSH toward the bottom and T4 at the top. They just ordered labs in 6 weeks for TSH, FT4, and T3.

What do you think? Should I be happy with where I'm at or should I try to get my TSH as low as possible?

I'm the first patient this doc has ever put on Armour.

### Shijin13 — 2012-10-03T02:55:39+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/armour-dosing.4083/post-55987>

Colleen Coble is the Thyroid expert here...

PattyCakes is another thyroid expert here as well!

I know JAck likes to see TSH as low as possible.... hopefully some of the experts will chime in

I'm still on 1/2 grain ~30mg of armour - its going to be interesting to see where everything is.. I feel like my Thryoid is out side of the park guess we'll see what my labs say!

### trishalinn@gmail.com — 2012-10-03T03:12:52+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/armour-dosing.4083/post-55989>

> **AKMan;57195 wrote:** I finally got my doc to put me on Armour. I stopped my Synthroid for 4 months and my TSH went to 5.23 (ref .34 - 4.82) and T4 went to .97 (.59 - 1.61). They didn't test T3. I am having some hypo symptoms, mainly cold fingers and toes.
>
>
>
> I started Armour 30mg on 1 Sep. Last week my TSH was 3.05 (.34 - 4.82) and FT4 was 1.01 (.59 - 1.61).
>
>
>
> I talked them into increasing my Armour to 60mg/day to see if we can get my TSH toward the bottom and T4 at the top. They just ordered labs in 6 weeks for TSH, FT4, and T3.
>
>
>
> What do you think? Should I be happy with where I'm at or should I try to get my TSH as low as possible?
>
>
>
> I'm the first patient this doc has ever put on Armour.

You really need to have them check your free t3. It's important it's at the top of the range.

I'm currently on 135 mg. I was at 180 prior to my last pregnancy, but my thyroid function improves while pregnant and I was down to 45 while pregnant. Still not where I need to be because my last labs showed my free t3 not even in range despite a tsh that was mid range. Gradually my doc is increasing my dosage, just not as quickly as I would like.

Best of luck...thyroid issues are a PAIN!

### AKMan — 2012-10-03T03:25:10+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/armour-dosing.4083/post-55993>

> **Trisha;57202 wrote:** Best of luck...thyroid issues are a PAIN!

Yes they are! Last Winter, I was toying with Synthroid and CT. When my T4 was low, it made CT unbearable. I can usually judge my thyroid function pretty close with body temp. Right now I'm just getting up to 97 waking, 98 evening. I would love to see 98.6 in the afternoon before I start CT'ing this winter.

On the plus side, at least I know my thyroid is still somewhat alive. No antibodies and in range, although low, T4 with no meds. I want to get into a cycle of no thyroid meds in Summer and then just enough to get my temp up in Winter.

### Patty Cakes — 2012-10-03T03:50:54+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/armour-dosing.4083/post-55996>

AKMan go to stopthethyroidmadness.com and read into how to increase your dosage and why to do it that way. The testing you want done is FT3. This gives you the amount of bioavailable hormone for your body to use. The T4 is your storage hormone and coverts to FT3 in the liver and gut. You probably knew all of that. You want the FT3 in the high end of the range and the FT4 in the high-middle range to high end of the range. The TSH can go very low when someone is on Armour. My last TSH reading before I switched to T3 only was .08 and my FT3 was in the top of the range. My doc was not at all worried but she is very familiar with thyroid issues as she has graves and now is hypo from having her thyroid radiated. Anyway that was more of a warning that many docs freak out when they see a very low TSH.

# Thread: segmented sleep!!
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/segmented-sleep.4085/>
**Subforum:** The Leptin Rx
**Posts:** 6
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### gemstar — 2012-10-03T08:31:36+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/segmented-sleep.4085/post-56029>

[http://fullcomment.nationalpost.com/2012/09/17/book-excerpt-how-the-lightbulb-transformed-the-way-we-sleep/](http://http://fullcomment.nationalpost.com/2012/09/17/book-excerpt-how-the-lightbulb-transformed-the-way-we-sleep/)

### cuivie@me.com — 2012-10-03T08:51:10+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/segmented-sleep.4085/post-56031>

Excellent article. Ever since reading Mark Sisson's article on bi-phasic sleeping I've come to view a monolithic, single-phase sleep as biologically abnormal. All the research literature where I'm guessing Dr. Kruse gets his info on circadian cycles of hormone release is probably based on modern, artificial-light induced mono-phasic sleep. We (as producers and students of science today) probably don't really know what the cycles really ought to be.

[http://www.marksdailyapple.com/biphasic-sleep/#axzz28DCmjZv8](http://http://www.marksdailyapple.com/biphasic-sleep/#axzz28DCmjZv8)

### steve@wity.net — 2012-10-03T10:58:21+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/segmented-sleep.4085/post-56039>

Very interesting. This works perfectly when you are down on the equator (Rift Valley) because you have equal hours of night and day and so you go to sleep at 8.00pm - wake for an hour or so just after midnight and then go back to sleep to be woken by the sun coming up.

### nonchalant — 2012-10-03T19:00:46+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/segmented-sleep.4085/post-56081>

Further away from the equator you would have short nights in the summer, long in the winter.

So polyphasic sleep might be more common during the longer nights.

But what was normal for people in industrial societies, even 200 years ago, may not be optimal, however.

### Michael — 2012-10-03T20:51:23+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/segmented-sleep.4085/post-56098>

> **gemstar;57251 wrote:** [http://fullcomment.nationalpost.com/2012/09/17/book-excerpt-how-the-lightbulb-transformed-the-way-we-sleep/](http://http://fullcomment.nationalpost.com/2012/09/17/book-excerpt-how-the-lightbulb-transformed-the-way-we-sleep/)

I've got Ekrich's book - interesting stuff.

Have you seen the TED video that Jack links from this article?

[http://jackkruse.com/how-does-the-leptin-rx-work/](http://http://jackkruse.com/how-does-the-leptin-rx-work/)

Around three minutes in, she starts talking about the waking period during the night that was often experienced by people going to bed with the sun. You get a massive surge of prolactin during that time, she says.

### WannaBeOptimal — 2012-10-04T00:39:16+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/segmented-sleep.4085/post-56129>

Very interesting...when I started observing circadian daily cycles (blue blockers, less artificial light, flux, etc), I started waking up in the middle of the night...anywhere between 1 am and 3 am. Previously, I would sleep about 7 hours straight on a regular basis.

When I wake, I am VERY awake for a couple of hours sometimes.


══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# Thread: Multinodular Goiter Shrinking
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/multinodular-goiter-shrinking.4086/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 3
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Gracelind — 2012-10-03T09:32:22+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/multinodular-goiter-shrinking.4086/post-56033>

Early last spring in running my fingers over my neck I discovered a marble sized and shaped lump on the right side over my thyroid gland. The left side of the gland felt a bit lumpy, too. I'd just had an exam by my internist a month before in which he specifically felt my neck but made no comment so I don't know when it appeared.

A thyroid scan and then iodine uptake test revealed 5 or 6 nodules which did not show any signs of being malignant. I'm scheduled to see an endocrinologist at UCSF in November. My docs felt there was really no further treatment needed. All summer it remained the same size.

Two weeks ago while a friend was telling me of her wonderful physical changes after hypothyroid was discovered and treated (by an MD who practices naturopathic medicine after the other docs dismissed her labs as normal), I again ran my fingers over my neck and ... the lump is nearly gone!

The only change I made was eating filet of sole two or three times a week with maybe a couple of scallops and/or prawns. I have to work my way into fish from the mildest, gradually getting more adventurous. The night before noticing that the lump was melting I'd eaten in a restaurant a dish of salmon, scallops, shrimp, and mussels--so large that I took it home and had it three meals in a row. I wonder if all that seafood made the difference that my thyroid needed to shrink the nodules?

A CT several years ago had an incidental finding of a calcified thyroid nodule, but none of my docs got interested in that and it wasn't mentioned in the scan.

Tonight's dinner was a strip of salmon baked, but with the center still sushi-like, seaweed salad and tobiko (tiny fish eggs). I may be canceling that UCSF appt after all.

### nonchalant — 2012-10-03T18:27:30+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/multinodular-goiter-shrinking.4086/post-56075>

Nice, Gracelind, so glad your throat is doing better! Was your throat uncomfortable last Spring? Or did it feel fine, except when you touched it with your fingers?

### Gracelind — 2012-10-03T19:40:01+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/multinodular-goiter-shrinking.4086/post-56087>

Thanks, Nonchalant.

My throat is always sensitive to any external pressure. Can't wear high neck clothes, especially turtlenecks. But I was totally unaware of the lump until palpating it. Shocked me because once I felt it, it was obvious in the mirror. Then, of course, it seemed even more sensitive because I was aware.

I have an autoimmune disease called myositis that gives me dysphagia--trouble swallowing. Hard to say if the goiter was interfering, in addition to the muscle weakness. As I understand it, multi-nodular goiter is different from a "regular" goiter.

# Thread: Hydro-static body fat analysis
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hydro-static-body-fat-analysis.4089/>
**Subforum:** Cold Thermogenesis
**Posts:** 1
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### msclman — 2012-10-03T18:45:32+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hydro-static-body-fat-analysis.4089/post-56078>

Hi all just wanted to throw this out there. I found a guy that has a mobile lab that does hydro weighing (supposed to be the gold standard), Resting metabolic rate, and vo2 max. Only issue is we need 20 people to sign up for him to drive the truck down. If anyone in the tri state are or is willing to drive to brooklyn is interested let me know. I have used him once before and it is AWESOME!! turns out the calipers and bio-impedance were both about 5% high so really i am 15% not 20% which i am pretty psyched about. the RMR put me at 2189 cal per day while the online calculators have me at 1987! Anyway, i think the info is crucial for anyone watching their weight or creating a diet tailor made for them.

# Thread: Why is this salmon making me sick?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/why-is-this-salmon-making-me-sick.4092/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 6
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### BJK77 — 2012-10-03T20:12:02+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/why-is-this-salmon-making-me-sick.4092/post-56091>

I need some detective work here! I eat sashimi regularly and lots of other cooked seafood. Yesterday and today I had salmon (wild alaskan), supposed to be very high quality (wildforsalmon.com) and I very lightly broiled it so the middle was still partially raw.

I didn't realize yesterday that it was the salmon that made me sick, but now that it's happening again after lunch today I'm sure it is. It's making me extremely nauseous and very fatigued with a headache.

What do ya think? Bad fish or something else going on?!?!

### Shijin13 — 2012-10-03T20:17:30+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/why-is-this-salmon-making-me-sick.4092/post-56093>

Costco recently had a recall for some if their salmon. Might be worth contacting the supplier

### BJK77 — 2012-10-04T19:44:35+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/why-is-this-salmon-making-me-sick.4092/post-56264>

Amazing. This salmon totally kicked my butt yesterday. I came home for lunch and ended up crashing hard. I can't remember the last time I napped. I usually can't even fall asleep during the day no matter how tired I am. Anyway, I called the company and they said they aren't aware of any issues. No one else has had a problem. Bizarre!

### MonteD — 2013-10-23T16:39:33+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/why-is-this-salmon-making-me-sick.4092/post-98727>

> Amazing. This salmon totally kicked my butt yesterday. I came home for lunch and ended up crashing hard. I can't remember the last time I napped. I usually can't even fall asleep during the day no matter how tired I am. Anyway, I called the company and they said they aren't aware of any issues. No one else has had a problem. Bizarre!

The same thing happens to me with FCLO!!! I crash HARD an hour or two after eating it. Once it felt like my mind was melting and I had to go to sleep immediately. I then had dreams that I couldn't move... I've never had anything but FCLO do this to me. Wierd.

### caroline — 2013-10-23T23:08:29+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/why-is-this-salmon-making-me-sick.4092/post-98762>

Sorry ....what is FCLO?

### Gracelind — 2013-10-23T23:56:10+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/why-is-this-salmon-making-me-sick.4092/post-98774>

A dream where you can't move may be sleep paralysis. See: [http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sleep\_Paralysis](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sleep_Paralysis)

# Thread: Lewis and Clark
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/lewis-and-clark.4104/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 1
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Michael — 2012-10-04T11:06:08+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/lewis-and-clark.4104/post-56179>

Someone in the comments on Jack's blog asserts that most Native Americans lived inland and not on the coasts.

I'm not at all sure this is correct. I haven't got population estimates for different periods pre- and post-contact to hand, but one thing that did come into my mind was having read that Lewis and Clark at one point travelled for months in the interior without seeing a living soul.

I've no reference from the _Journal_ but did come up wuith this:

> The Expedition met no Indians from the time they left the Mandan and Hidatsa villages until they made contact with Sacagaweaâ€™s people in mid-August 1805, near present-day Lemhi Pass on the Continental Divide. The captains kept a keen lookout for any sign of Native peoples during their long trip up the Missouri, but they saw no Indians and few signs of camps or activity.

[http://www.ohs.org/the-oregon-history-project/narratives/lewis-and-clark/exploring-foreign-place/across-the-plains.cfm](http://http://www.ohs.org/the-oregon-history-project/narratives/lewis-and-clark/exploring-foreign-place/across-the-plains.cfm)

# Thread: how long did it take to see sleep improvements and how did you ramp up your times?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-long-did-it-take-to-see-sleep-improvements-and-how-did-you-ramp-up-your-times.4117/>
**Subforum:** Cold Thermogenesis
**Posts:** 2
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### e_h — 2012-10-04T18:09:10+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-long-did-it-take-to-see-sleep-improvements-and-how-did-you-ramp-up-your-times.4117/post-56241>

I am curious when folks started to see improvements in their sleep? I have been having issues waking up super early and not being able to fall back asleep.

I did 1 week of face dunking and then for the last week and a half have been doing full immersion in my bath tub (51-55 degrees). I started with 10 minutes in the bath and am up to 23 minutes (5 mins sitting / 18 mins full immersion). I feel really good while doing it but am pretty cold after for an hour or so.

I plan to work up to 45 minutes- wondering how others ramped up their times?

Yesterday I started doing a bath in AM and one in PM.

Thanks,

Ethan

### Endless — 2012-10-05T20:52:49+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-long-did-it-take-to-see-sleep-improvements-and-how-did-you-ramp-up-your-times.4117/post-56508>

I am sleeping better after about a month...but I have not tried CT in the evening, I always do it in the morning. I've read a lot of others say that they sleep like a rock after doing it at night though.

I extended my times depending on how it was feeling....some days I don't have time to spend so will get out at 25-30 mins, other times I will stay in for 40 and try for an extra 5 if I'm feeling good!

# Thread: DIY Resveratrol
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/diy-resveratrol.4128/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 18
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### nonchalant — 2012-10-04T23:24:56+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/diy-resveratrol.4128/post-56323>

I have access to dewberries (wild blackberries).

In the past, I have squashed them to get juice for jam, which left a LOT of skins and seeds.

The crop this year was poor, because of heat & drought the year before, but next spring I'm expecting a good crop.

I think I just might try my hand at homemade resveratrol. I probably won't do well at the oxygen-poor processing environment, but hey, it's free. Maybe just freeze small blocks of it. That would involve a lot less oxygen than drying it.

### Endless — 2012-10-04T23:55:02+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/diy-resveratrol.4128/post-56336>

I wonder of elderberries have much reservatrol, they are mostly skin! There are tons of those around here right now .

### nonchalant — 2012-10-05T00:04:13+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/diy-resveratrol.4128/post-56337>

How about red sumac? It's certainly the right color. Makes a very nice citrus-tasting tea.

### Caroline Cooper — 2012-10-06T19:28:27+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/diy-resveratrol.4128/post-56612>

> **Nonchalant;57579 wrote: I have access to dewberries (wild blackberries).
>
> In the past, I have squashed them to get juice for jam, which left a LOT of skins and seeds.
>
> The crop this year was poor, because of heat & drought the year before, but next spring I'm expecting a good crop.
>
>
>
> I think I just might try my hand at homemade resveratrol. I probably won't do well at the oxygen-poor processing environment, but hey, it's free. Maybe just freeze small blocks of it. That would involve a lot less oxygen than drying it.**

**

Hi Nonchalant,

I am doing it right now. I have a problem with many of the additives that are put in commercial prepared supplements. I also don't like the inflated cost. It makes me angry that nutraceutical companies take the "waste products" from industrial food processing and repackage and market these products and make a fortune. I do not have problems with them using a waste product, actually that's wonderful. I get angry that they charge a fortune for these "value added products". Maybe that is the "America way" but it's not very ethical, nor the right thing to do. As a population, if we continue to put our financial benefit first, we are lost.

**Dr Mercola suggests that **muscadine grapes are the best choice due to very high levels of resveratrol. Also, they are native to south-eastern US:

[http://products.mercola.com/purple-defense/](http://http://products.mercola.com/purple-defense/)

I have a number of grapes that have been growing in very poor soil and low water levels. These are perfect conditions for the plant to make resveratrol according to Dr Kruse.** I needed to pick them this week because it is starting to frost in my area at nights. Here are some pictures about my processing to make poor man's resverstrol**. My juicer could not handle the grapes very well and kept on clogging up with fiber. I decided to try drying the whole grape instead:

1\. Pick grapes and clean out stems. My grapes are tiny. The average is less than 1/4 inch. The flavor is intense and very sweet.

2\. Juice the whole grapes.

3\. Dehydrating poor man's resveratrol.

4\. Use grape juice in making wine or in this case flavoring water kefir.

5\. Dry the whole grape for winter sublingual treatment**. I mean suck on them before swallowing.

[](http://http://www.jackkruse.com/forum/convert_files/attachments/57912=497-picked-grapes-1.jpg)

[](http://http://www.jackkruse.com/forum/convert_files/attachments/57912=498-juice-grapes-2.jpg)

[](http://http://www.jackkruse.com/forum/convert_files/attachments/57912=499-poor-mans-resveratrol-3.jpg)

[](http://http://www.jackkruse.com/forum/convert_files/attachments/57912=500-grape-kefir-4.jpg)

[](http://http://www.jackkruse.com/forum/convert_files/attachments/57912=501-drying-whole-grapes-5.jpg)

### Caroline Cooper — 2012-10-10T00:49:32+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/diy-resveratrol.4128/post-57191>

Just an update on the DIY resveratrol. I have tried both types of **poor man's resveratrol**. The ground up seeds and skins from the juicer tastes really good and crunchy. The whole dried grape are very sweet and it is easy to spit out the seeds. (Some kinds of grapes have resveratrol in the seeds. Mine don't, just in the skins.) I could see eating 5gm a day would be easy to do. Actually, it would be a tasty "dessert".

As I was sampling each type I wondered which would be better considering a person's existing conditions:

1\. I think if someone had diabetes removing the juice and letting someone else drink it would be a good idea. The juicer will remove at least some of the sugars, thus the ground up seeds and skins would be better for someone with blood sugar issues.

2\. At the same time people that have gut issue will find the ground up seeds can be difficult to digest. So someone like me, without blood sugar issues would be better to eat the whole dried grape and spit out the seeds.

### Jonathin — 2012-10-10T04:24:29+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/diy-resveratrol.4128/post-57240>

Another options for processing berries and herbs for their medicinal value is to lacto-ferment them with an inoculating culture such as EM. This eliminates most of the sugars and makes the phyto-chemicals more available. The down side is that it takes months to make. The result is an acidic liquid that is easy to ingest in small doses. I've done this with a variety of berries and herbs such as tumeric and dandelion root. I bottled it in beer bottles afterfiltering out the solids.

### Caroline Cooper — 2012-10-10T22:25:23+0300

### Caroline Cooper — 2012-10-10T22:39:04+0300

[http://www.gardenerspantry.ca/microorganisms/effective-microorganisms.html](http://http://www.gardenerspantry.ca/microorganisms/effective-microorganisms.html)
