Please try again later.", "lightbox\_start\_slideshow": "Start slideshow", "lightbox\_stop\_slideshow": "Stop slideshow", "lightbox\_full\_screen": "Full screen", "lightbox\_thumbnails": "Thumbnails", "lightbox\_download": "Download", "lightbox\_share": "Share", "lightbox\_zoom": "Zoom", "lightbox\_new\_window": "New window", "lightbox\_toggle\_sidebar": "Toggle sidebar" }

### Patty Cakes — 2012-10-09T00:19:51+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/garden-help-needed.4200/post-56970>

Tomato plants look so sad. When I lived in the northwest I never attempted to garden in the winter due to the dips in weather. Always had a summer garden. Now in So Cal we can grow year round. I know I have heard of "glazing" or putting a layer of water over oranges when it freezes so they are ice coated and the ice protects them a little. I think some people even cover the plants with plastic to help shield out the cold. But I have a brown thumb so not sure you should listen to me at all!!!

### Caroline Cooper — 2012-10-09T00:35:47+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/garden-help-needed.4200/post-56975>

1\. Harvest you tomatoes right now. Put them in a box in the house and allow them to ripen. Eat right away or freeze for later. If you think they just can't ripen you could make fermented green tomatoes:

[http://eatkamloops.org/winter-storage-kimchi-and-lacto-fermented-green-tomatoes/](http://http://eatkamloops.org/winter-storage-kimchi-and-lacto-fermented-green-tomatoes/)

2\. Mulch and cover your kale and chard with straw. You will be able to eat these for weeks or months, depending on your micro-climate, zone and elevation.

3\. You should have dried your herbs during the summer. If you still have some herbs that are green and in good condition, harvest now and tie into bunches and hang in your kitchen to dry. When dry put into bags for later use. Remember to label. You can also freeze too.

4\. It's too late to plant a winter garden at this point. OR you could set up a hoop-house. These are super easy green houses. Here is a picture of mine. Look around the internet for a "plan":

[http://eatkamloops.org/learning-about-garden-weeds/](http://http://eatkamloops.org/learning-about-garden-weeds/)

5\. Do some container gardening. OR dig up some herbs and bring them indoors. The peppers in these post are now in my living room for the rest of the season. You can see the inside of our big hoop-house:

[http://eatkamloops.org/garden-harvest-photo-essay/](http://http://eatkamloops.org/garden-harvest-photo-essay/)

6\. You have a great "heat-sink" with your paving stone area assuming the area get any winter sun. If you have containers you could have them on the paving stones protected by a hoop-house. Have a large barrel of water for watering and thermal lag.

7\. Sorry, friend. Don't automatic water if you are in an area that freezes. Otherwise you could do thousands of dollars of damage to your automatic watering system. (Around here you have to go six feet down for livestock watering.) Use a bucket for winter watering. The plants wont need much. You will develop great shoulder muscles! It's almost as good as carrying milk pails!

Here's a great book: How to Grow Food in Your Polytunnel All Year Round by Mark Gatter.

### skline@peak.org — 2012-10-09T02:31:51+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/garden-help-needed.4200/post-56997>

There's a great book by a market gardener in Maine. He harvests greens 12 months a year WITHOUT ARTIFICIAL HEAT.

Something of a genius at it.

[http://www.amazon.com/Winter-Harvest-Handbook-Production-Greenhouses/dp/1603580816/ref=sr\_1\_2?s=books&ie=UTF8&qid=1349738783&sr=1-2&keywords=4+season+gardening](http://http://www.amazon.com/Winter-Harvest-Handbook-Production-Greenhouses/dp/1603580816/ref=sr_1_2?s=books&ie=UTF8&qid=1349738783&sr=1-2&keywords=4+season+gardening)

Some reading and some experience will show you which plants are strictly warm weather, like tomatoes, basil, corn, and green beans, and which others can tolerate some frost, like kale (very tough, kale), other cabbage family plants, peas, some herbs, and so on. Some root plants like carrots you can keep after frost by laying down a very thick mulch. Then you get down into it to harvest.

In Colorado you can have some very warm spells separated by bitter frost and snow. The deep frost without snow cover is the most damaging. Wetting things down first can help. When you have a warm spell, always water at once. The plants are very vulnerable to drying out at a time like that. It's amazing what can winter over under a good snow cover, and show up happy as can be come spring.

### hope2conceive — 2012-10-09T02:45:36+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/garden-help-needed.4200/post-57001>

I do kale throughout the winter with mulch/protection unless it's way way cold. I had great cilantro last year too....it took a while though. My favorite was garlic - we got lots and it came out amazing, and I planted it way late for my climate (7).

I lost my basil the first year too...next year you'll know what the blackish leaves mean. So exciting you are doing this!

How neat you have so much space too!!

### hope2conceive — 2012-10-09T02:52:32+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/garden-help-needed.4200/post-57003>

PS Have you heard of this blog? I love it...tells you what to do each month. Mother Earth News also has a great garden planner.

[http://www.agardenforthehouse.com/](http://http://www.agardenforthehouse.com/)

This year, I also really got into gardening with the moon, the way people used to garden. Totally fascinating....and I love what I'm learning about the seasonality and prime time for each of the things I try to grow, and also how they too are dependent on the right light (and temperatures)....

[https://www.biodynamics.com/content/2012-north-american-biodynamic-sowing-and-planting-calendar-maria-matthias-thun](http://https://www.biodynamics.com/content/2012-north-american-biodynamic-sowing-and-planting-calendar-maria-matthias-thun)

for next year:

[http://www.bookdepository.com/book/9780863159183?redirected=true&gclid=CMq8zL\_O8rICFZSd4Aod\_3YARA](http://http://www.bookdepository.com/book/9780863159183?redirected=true&gclid=CMq8zL_O8rICFZSd4Aod_3YARA)

Just something to check out if it floats your boat.

# Thread: buying whole fish from the supermarket?  is that a bad idea?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/buying-whole-fish-from-the-supermarket-is-that-a-bad-idea.4201/>
**Subforum:** Beginners Area
**Posts:** 4
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### x_danny_x — 2012-10-09T01:19:40+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/buying-whole-fish-from-the-supermarket-is-that-a-bad-idea.4201/post-56990>

i was wondering if buying fish from the supermarket lets say shop rite or something, is a bad idea? i generally like to buy fish already boneless and skinless.

i know it is not good to buy anything else from the supermarket such as meat, eggs, etc. since they are probably from steroid/antibiotics animals.

but are fish a different story? buying from the fish market, the prices are sky high

### Integral_D — 2012-10-09T02:36:05+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/buying-whole-fish-from-the-supermarket-is-that-a-bad-idea.4201/post-56999>

Whole fish are a great idea. You can use every bit of it if you can figure out what it's good for. In some markets -- there's an asian market near me -- with a big fish counter, you can get them to clean it for you, and even bone/fillet it. You're not likely to be eating fish entrails, so don't bother with them. However, keep everything else and learn what to do with it. One start -- salmon skin, nice and crispy, just like in a good sushi restaurant.

### x_danny_x — 2012-10-09T05:34:42+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/buying-whole-fish-from-the-supermarket-is-that-a-bad-idea.4201/post-57050>

ah, so it is okay to eat the skin. was wondering about that. thanks for that.

but i also wanted to know if its okay to buy fish from the local supermarket? such as walmart and shop rite.

### Gracelind — 2012-10-12T06:11:16+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/buying-whole-fish-from-the-supermarket-is-that-a-bad-idea.4201/post-57699>

Hi danny,

You might ask the butcher/fishmonger at the market when the fish was caught and when it was delivered. If it's fresh, wild-caught, and has been kept under proper refrigeration I'd think that the fish would be fine.


══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

### vkiernan — 2012-10-18T20:29:58+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/enterolabs-compare-educate-thread.4206/post-58983>

> **Dali Dula;60444 wrote:** This is very interesting. Not all bad news. Sounds like you can have cashews. Love cashews. I am going to do this. So does the sensitivity for tuna include all fish?

I love cashews too! Thanks for the reminder. I believe it only tests those things listed. Perhaps they are the most reactive in the general population??? I don't know how to interpret the meat thing. How can I not eat meat? What about bacon (pork)?

### Patty Cakes — 2012-10-18T22:16:24+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/enterolabs-compare-educate-thread.4206/post-59014>

> **vkiernan;60523 wrote:** I love cashews too! Thanks for the reminder. I believe it only tests those things listed. Perhaps they are the most reactive in the general population??? I don't know how to interpret the meat thing. How can I not eat meat? What about bacon (pork)?

This has a lot of info in it for you. It looks to me like the results are very valuable.

So you cannot eat dairy, eggs, grains (most if not all but I think you are off all), and no chicken (no loss there) and pork. We eat turkey bacon but maybe that's like chicken. But there is also lamb bacon that is wonderful and similar but not totally alike.

I'm curious how the beef result would be interpreted too. I had an allergy test and almonds came up as a very mild allergy. They told me I could have them every four days. I just eliminated them. But I wonder if you can have beef just not all the time.

Also cashews are good for soups, smoothies, and other items for a creamy thickening ingredient. DH has a problem w/cashews so we don't eat them but I've heard good results.

I'm trying to figure out are you Celiac or Celiac Sprue with these results or is that inconclusive on that front?

### vkiernan — 2012-10-19T01:13:18+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/enterolabs-compare-educate-thread.4206/post-59062>

> **Patty Cakes;60554 wrote:** This has a lot of info in it for you. It looks to me like the results are very valuable.
>
>
>
> So you cannot eat dairy, eggs, grains (most if not all but I think you are off all), and no chicken (no loss there) and pork. We eat turkey bacon but maybe that's like chicken. But there is also lamb bacon that is wonderful and similar but not totally alike.
>
>
>
> I'm curious how the beef result would be interpreted too. I had an allergy test and almonds came up as a very mild allergy. They told me I could have them every four days. I just eliminated them. But I wonder if you can have beef just not all the time.
>
>
>
> Also cashews are good for soups, smoothies, and other items for a creamy thickening ingredient. DH has a problem w/cashews so we don't eat them but I've heard good results.
>
>
>
> I'm trying to figure out are you Celiac or Celiac Sprue with these results or is that inconclusive on that front?

Thanks Patty and yes I have dropped eggs, dairy and grains except the little slip of butter here or there but mostly ghee. Pork is REALLY tough for me as I work for a pork mfg company and I live off the stuff. We make awesome bacon, braunsweiger, hams, etc. I would think that turkey falls in the same class as chicken. I think I need to tell my son to get me a deer. He's bow hunting at present and that would probably be a safe bet.

As for celiac vs. celiac sprue, I don't think there is a difference and I believe it is inconclusive as you have to have a biopsy to confirm. All I know is I have the gene and my daughter actually has confirmed celiac by biopsy. Guessing if I'm not there already, I'm well on my way if I don't change things around.

### Patty Cakes — 2012-10-19T01:38:40+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/enterolabs-compare-educate-thread.4206/post-59067>

Good thing you caught it now before it manifested into something worse! I think a lot of people maybe scared to have this done because of the fear of the results. I'm excited for you because you now know what you cannot do. I can't wait to get my testing done after the first of the year.

Working for a pork manufacturer just puts another kink in the wheel of life and you will just work through that. Like I said DH doesn't eat pork so I have gotten used to other meats cured similar to bacon and it's not even my issue! I've have enough foods on my own that must be eliminated!!

My understanding, very limited at best, was that celiac was just ABs produced from the wheat and Celiac Sprue was ABs from both dairy and wheat. Like I said I don't know that much about it and I'm sure you know waaaay more with your daughter being Celiac.

Also I think Cyrex Labs does a pretty conclusive blood test for Celiac. I had their cross-reactive for gluten test done and it was good info. I was told things can change once you get the body healthier so I am hoping to get that test redone again next year sometime. But first is EnteroLab A+C.

You know, as much as I bitch about all of this I am so grateful to Jack for helping all of us figure all this stuff out. WHERE would we all be without him.....

I am going to read more about celiac now as I have never heard of it that way. Very interesting.

### MamaGrok — 2012-10-19T18:42:45+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/enterolabs-compare-educate-thread.4206/post-59185>

Wow, amazing results. THANK YOU for sharing them.

Are we fairly confident that the protein results are accurate across the board, like for pork, tuna, etc.? (I'm wracking my brain to figure out how one would develop a sensitivity just to tuna, or to something as health-crucial as fish in general.)

### vkiernan — 2012-10-19T20:20:06+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/enterolabs-compare-educate-thread.4206/post-59208>

> **MamaGrok;60725 wrote:** Wow, amazing results. THANK YOU for sharing them.
>
>
>
> Are we fairly confident that the protein results are accurate across the board, like for pork, tuna, etc.? (I'm wracking my brain to figure out how one would develop a sensitivity just to tuna, or to something as health-crucial as fish in general.)

I called her today just to get a better explanation of my results. She made it sound it was accurate. She also told me I shouldn't eat salmon right now as sometimes there is a cross reaction to tuna. I have a lot in the freezer. Kinda wondering what I am supposed to eat? Can't eat chicken, pork, tuna and even beef is on the list. Can't eat grains. Veggies are carbs. No eggs. No dairy. Hmmm, what else is left, water! I wonder how shellfish play into this - totally different then tuna. I realize I am exaggerating a bit but still I didn't expect to react to any meats.

She did tell me to start with the top of the list and gradually not eat those things and if I do only eat them every 4 days like Patty said. She said right now things are pretty flared up and by not eating from the top would help. I asked her if I will ever be able to eat some of those things and she said I might if I get my system settled down. She also said to not eat a lot of the things that I didn't react to as I might start. She also mentioned to keep a food journal and note everything I can think of to see what I might react to.

### Lyndra — 2012-10-25T02:53:14+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/enterolabs-compare-educate-thread.4206/post-60062>

Got my results back today and mostly very good news. There's one confusing & contradictory finding perhaps someone can help explain:

> Anti-tissue Transglutaminase IgA: 4 units, normal range is < 10. The level of intestinal IgA antibodies to the human enzyme tissue transglutaminase was below the upper limit of normal. Hence, there is no evidence of a gluten-induced autoimmune reaction to this enzyme.
>
>
>
> Anti-gliadin IgA: 14 units, normal range is < 10. The level of intestinal anti-gliadin IgA antibody was elevated, indicative of active dietary gluten sensitivity. For optimal health; resolution or improvement of gluten-induced syndromes it is recommended that you follow a strict and permanent gluten free diet. As gluten sensitivity is a genetic syndrome, you may want to have your relatives screened as well.

Based on a recent reaction when I inadvertently ate gluten, I'm convinced I am gluten sensitive. I would like to understand the transglutaminase result and why that indicates I'm not gluten sensitive.

The other quandary is that I had low values for casein & eggs (both 4) but I wonder whether it makes sense to eat them or not since I show some level of antibodies...

### MamaGrok — 2012-10-25T16:07:16+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/enterolabs-compare-educate-thread.4206/post-60129>

I totally don't know, but would guess that you're sensitive to the gliadin protein, but not the transglutaminase enzyme that your body produces to help you digest gluten. Thus, still wheat sensitive, just not autoimmune to this specific enzyme at this point.

### bigknitwit — 2012-10-25T17:12:28+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/enterolabs-compare-educate-thread.4206/post-60137>

I don't totally know either. However I do know that tissue transglutaminase antibodies are associated with celiac disease (autoimmune). The body even tries to destroy it's own gluten enzyme. In your case, it looks like this is not happening at a significant level, like MamaGrok said. I wonder if JK would say that any level of ttg antibodies is a marker for celiac (much like he says with low levels of thyroid antibodies and Hashi's). That I really couldn't say.

> **MamaGrok;61731 wrote:** I totally don't know, but would guess that you're sensitive to the gliadin protein, but not the transglutaminase enzyme that your body produces to help you digest gluten. Thus, still wheat sensitive, just not autoimmune to this specific enzyme at this point.

Can your daughter have goat milk? I thought the BCM-7 was strictly a cow dairy test. Also it seemed to me that it was a test for A1 diary. Goat and sheep are A2 dairy and most cows in the US are A1. OK someone straighten me out if I am wrong. I do eat some goat and sheep just now cow.

BTW, I made a chocolate pudding with beef gelatin, coconut milk, and 90% chocolate that was yummy! Also made a cranberry jello thingy with the gelatin, ground up cranberries, orange peel, and a dash of stevia. It was so good I made it again!

### RiverNeighbor — 2012-11-14T00:04:56+0200

> **vkiernan;66045 wrote:** MG, not sure what Jack would say, but my new doc (who is an MD, func. med and anti-aging) told me to consider duck or quail eggs. Of course I won't be eating them all the time if do go that route. I will probably try it and see how I do though.

While on the phone with enterolab this afternoon I asked them specifically if the ovalbumin test was specific to chicken and the answer was yes - it is not indicative of anything other than chicken. She said some folks who test positive can tolerate duck,quail,etc eggs and some can't. So no new info but thought I would post their answer.

Some thoughtsÂ are you already tested positive for the IgA on your GI Effects test so seeing if you have the gene might be a mute point.Â  Sure, it can tell you if you can get Celiac or not but you are already gluten sensitive, yes?Â  But, otoh, if you have the gene then your children are more susceptible and at least you would know it.Â  STTM said something about IgA and transglutaminase testing positive means you have celiac.Â  But, not sureÂ if/what actually means that.Â  You could ask Jack what his thoughts are on the gene testing.Â  I already knowÂ I have the gene and I guess for me it confirms that if I don't have celiac at this point that I will probably get it if I didn't change my ways.Â  So, I guess I do like knowing the fact that I have the gene for it.Â  I didn't do the transgl. and now wish I had.Â  Might confirm it for me.

I got the gene test for my daughters and they each have some form of the gene.Â  Means that they are or can become gluten intolerant.Â  One was already showing signs of it so that is why we tested.Â  She would get a runny nose about an hour after eating gluten.Â  Anything can turn the gene on.Â  So if you are already not eating any gluten ----it would just be wanting to know.Â  I am wondering if these genes are in the 23 and me.Â  I have not seen them yet.Â  I know the 23 and me checks for celiac.Â  Since testing the girls and they have 2 copies we know my husband and I each have to have one.Â

RiverNeighbor - Thank you so much for writing this.Â  I've been lurking for a long time, and seeing this post of yours inspired me to get this test done for my 2-year old daughter (almost 3).Â  I was balking at the price, but your post put me over the edge.Â  Got her results today.Â  She's having an immune reaction to pretty much everything - especially gluten.Â  Now we have the information we needed to really justify a huge lifestyle change.Â  We were half-assing the no-dairy before, and allowing wheat on special occasions.Â  No more of that.Â  I'll be cooking for her a lot more now.Â  Thank you - you never know who you're going to help, and I really appreciate you sharing your story.

### RiverNeighbor — 2012-12-27T19:52:48+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/enterolabs-compare-educate-thread.4206/post-68540>

Lanie, Â  I'm glad it was helpful. Â For us, Â giving up the milk wasn't as hard as I thought it was going to be. Â We said "bye" to it one day and no one has said a peep about it since. Â I never really gave them juice so they just started drinking more water. Â  The harder thing for us has been variety in meat and getting in veggies. Â At least she likes SeaSnax. Â In fact, Â my daughter is doing better than my son (I didn't have him tested but at least for now I've eliminated the same foods for both). Â I'm very pleased to say that we have been 100% compliant with our elimination of the foods she responded to! Â But, Â I was hoping that by now we would be able to try egg yolks but unfortunately she still is having some gut problems. Â I've been giving her probiotics for a long time, Â but I think we've got something else going on gut-wise that isn't getting taken care of. Â I'm thinking that the Metametrix GI test (and maybe some other tests) will be our next step. Â  Â We've got an appointment with a Naturopath next week who I interviewed and think will be helpful - so fingers crossed. Â 

I think we've got some vaccines coming up at 4 years. Â I've decided that we're not doing any vaccines until we get all her gut and immune issues under control - and even then we will take a good hard look at what we really think is necessary. Â

Â I've gotten almost all of them eliminated, Â but it makes me wonder if there is any protein I could put in my body right now that my body wouldn't mount a defense toward. Â  So... Â I also have my own heavy lifting to do...

Yep, just cuz they are carbs and I was running thru the list of no no's in general. Nothing to do with the lab result itself. Just so many things off the "list." I will say though, since I have had a heavy metals test and then a consult with Jack, there is a possibility that my problem might be due to the amount of metals I have and not a "true" autoimmune issue. Now, I will not know until I clear the metals and retest but I wanted to let others know that there might be hope. This is why testing is so critical and to apply the results to your own n=1 and not anyone else's. We are all different even though some things "appear" to be the same.

### hellojtm — 2013-01-04T20:18:59+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/enterolabs-compare-educate-thread.4206/post-69379>

Sounds great V.

I don't think you should avoid vegetables in any case. You can have a very rich diet of vegetables and leafy greens!! Then add in those shellfish and other items people mentioned to round it out. I think avoiding fresh veggies because they are carbs is wild. I can see the value of keeping a diet low-glycemic, etc... or seasonally glycemic... but no veggies seems massively restrictive and I don't know the logic supporting it. We're not Inuits after all

I'm not saying you can't do it, just saying that you should question whether you need to, especially in light of all the new restrictions you are about to make.

I was reading some stuff by Charles Poliquin in which he was testing his body builders regularly for food allergies and they were pretty much all allergic to the things they ate the most and if they stopped eating them they antibodies went away. His response was to have his builders cycle their foods, i.e. eat eggs for a couple months and then drop them for a couple months (hey! it's almost seasonal!). Which is kind of a hack, maybe the common thread amongst the foods was a history or current leaky gut. Maybe the reason you're not showing antibodies to cashews is because you haven't had many of them lately?

Point is, I'm not that worried about your labs (admittedly I'm a total newb) because I don't think you have a real allergy to some of those foods and you'll be back eating them when your other issues are resolved. I mean, imagine you have poor digestion and a leaky gut, you have the opportunity to develop an immune response to any food at that point because the food is getting into your intestines undigested.

BEST OF LUCK.

jesse

Thanks Jesse. You are spot on on all accounts. I did add more veggies in and then Jack reminded me that there is a time and place for them and winter is not it. Although, I do still eat low carb veggies but was trying to really slow down on them. I did have leaky gut for sure and I know I am healing it or things wouldn't be getting better. And yes, I did have a problem with those foods as they were the ones I was eating all the time. Enterolabs told me not to eat things I was ok with very much or I would end up having the same reaction. My take away is to rotate. Sometimes not so easy but I am sure trying. I am going to get my metals cleared somehow and then see where things are at. I will know if I start clearing the metals and feel better and then add foods in and I start tanking again.

### MamaGrok — 2013-03-05T20:59:38+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/enterolabs-compare-educate-thread.4206/post-79168>

I forgot to update this when I did get my results.

Enterolabs (range, then my results)
Fecal Fat 00 299
Anti-Tissue Transglutaminase IgA <10 14

Most Antigenic Foods Sensitivity Stool Panel (500 max possible)
Anti-gliadin IgA <10 66
Anti-casein IgA <10 19
Anti-ovalbumin IgA <10 36
Anti-soy IgA <10 16

Comprehensive Food Sensitivity Stool Panel - Measuring immunologic reactivity
Mean Value 11 Antigenic Foods <10 14
Tuna Most
Rice Most
Beef Most
Oat Most
Chicken Intermediate
Pork Intermediate
Corn Intermediate
Cashew Intermediate
White potato No significant
Almond No significant
Walnut No significant

Gluten Sensitivity Gene Test
HLA-DQB1 Molecular analysis, Allele 1 O202 (DQ2.2)
HLA-DQB1 Molecular analysis, Allele 2 O602 (DQ6.2)
Serologic equivalent: HLA-DQ 2,1 (Subtype 2,6)

At the time of the test, I had been off gluten for one year, 100% off dairy 95% of the previous year, but had had some recently, same with eggs, same with soy. No oats in two years or more. Everything but tuna, beef, & pork are rare or absent in my diet.

Fat malabsorption was way over positive a year ago, so that is a big improvement.
I understand that ovalbumin is only in the egg white, so I'm not sure this means I have to avoid egg yolk.
I'm not at all sure what these results mean I should do, because, for instance, it didn't test salmon or cod. If I eliminate tuna, it will increase the amount of salmon, cod, herrings, etc., and perhaps I'm sensitive to any or all of those more than tuna. It seems it could do more harm than good. Same goes for beef & pork.

I'm also not sure why they don't give absolute values for the 11 most common. After all, they give values for wheat, even though the relative amount is still most important. Let's say I'm a 66 for wheat & 3 on beef. Let's say everything else of the 11 most common is under 3, and sure, beef is "most" reactive, but it doesn't really matter.

Analysis welcomed.

# Thread: Gut Bacteria Show Intelligence?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/gut-bacteria-show-intelligence.4217/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 2
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### AKMan — 2012-10-09T19:46:41+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/gut-bacteria-show-intelligence.4217/post-57121>

[http://phys.org/news/2012-10-intestinal-bacteria-phages-weapons.html](http://http://phys.org/news/2012-10-intestinal-bacteria-phages-weapons.html)

Phages, short for bacteriophages, are viruses created by bacteria that target and destroy other bacteria. Unfortunately, despite a lot of research since their discovery nearly century ago, very little is known about how they function, particularly, in the human gut. In this new research, the team discovered something that had never been seen before, a strain of bacteria that create a phage for the express purpose of killing off other bacteria that are competing for the same resources.

The gut, as most know, is home to trillions of bacteria; some provide benefits to the host, such as helping to digest certain foods, while others are not so good, causing digestive problems. One in particular, the plentiful Enterococcus faecalis, appears to live without creating problems in the gut, but causes a lot of problems when it gets in the bloodstream, accounting for many hospital acquired infections. In looking at a particular strain of E. faecalis known as V583, the researchers found that when it was introduced alone into a germ-free mouse gut, it began churning out phages, which seemed counter-productive as it takes a lot of energy to do so.

In looking closer, the team discovered that V583 did have a purpose, and that was to kill any other strains of E. faecalis that might show up, gobbling resources. Thus, the gut bacteria were creating phages to use as a weapon against closely related bacteria that might consume shared resources. The researchers call it a form of bacterial warfare, but also suggest that the discovery might offer some insight into how bacteria in general might be used to create phages in ways that can be controlled, allowing for the development of targeted anti-bacterial agents that kill offending bacteria without harming those that are beneficial.

### chocolate — 2012-10-10T03:12:35+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/gut-bacteria-show-intelligence.4217/post-57218>

I keep thinking that I got nuttin' after years of antibiotics. This stuff is amazing to me.

# Thread: U.S. Military interested in probable link between DHA deficiency and suicide
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/u-s-military-interested-in-probable-link-between-dha-deficiency-and-suicide.4218/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 1
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Michael — 2012-10-09T21:09:54+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/u-s-military-interested-in-probable-link-between-dha-deficiency-and-suicide.4218/post-57131>

Suicide is currently common among military personnel:

> “The problem is coming to a head with the recognition that in the military, you’re more likely to die by suicide than by enemy combatant – and that’s not acceptable,” said Dr. Ron Acierno, a co-investigator on the project and a professor in the department of psychiatry at the Medical University of South Carolina in Charleston, S.C. ...
>
>
> Here is the interesting bit:
>
>
>
> Omega-3 has been on under the microscope for more than a year as experts have tried to dissect the high number of military suicides. In August 2011, a study published in the Journal of Clinical Psychiatry suggested that certain fish oil components had potent psychiatric benefits and suggested that taking an omega-3 supplement might help service members. That research, performed in part by the Uniformed Services University of Health Sciences, scanned through the medical records of 800 U.S. service members who took their own lives between 2002 and 2008, comparing those against the health files of 800 active-duty personnel who had not attempted suicide. **The scientists found that service members with higher blood amounts of docosahexaenoic acid - an omega-3 fatty acid - were less likely to take their own lives.**

[http://usnews.nbcnews.com/\_news/2012/10/08/14299261-military-suicides-defense-officials-spending-10-million-to-learn-if-fish-oil-can-help](http://http://usnews.nbcnews.com/_news/2012/10/08/14299261-military-suicides-defense-officials-spending-10-million-to-learn-if-fish-oil-can-help)

Well, I'd say that Jack did say "I warned them proof was coming", and it wouldn't be surprising if this study provided more proof of the importance of this nutrient.

It says they're giving them smoothies with fish oil in. Maybe they should be giving them MHS instead!

> With the 2012 Nobel Prize announcements coming up next week, I am reminded that there is probably no other scientist but Carl Woese who could write about having "no use for natural selection" and have Nature magazine respond with a Nobel endorsement. ### Michael — 2012-10-10T09:01:27+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/iodinated-salt.4226/post-57262>

An interesting reminder in the news of the importance of iodine from the International Herald Tribune. As Dr. Cunnane says [here](http://http://www.seafood.net.au/page/?pid=1408&nid=403) the iodine-deficiency story is a story that's been forgotten.

It hasn't been in places where they're in great need of it, however, and here's an example.

They've just set up 34 labs in the Punjab to monitor the process:

> Munawar Hussain, the Punjab programme manager for the Micronutrient Initiative, said that **Iodine deficiency was a major public health problem in Pakistan,** despite progress in the form of universal salt iodisation.

[http://tribune.com.pk/story/448860/health-initiative-34-labs-set-up-to-monitor-salt-iodisation/](http://http://tribune.com.pk/story/448860/health-initiative-34-labs-set-up-to-monitor-salt-iodisation/)

# Thread: T1D, Celiac, Hypothyroid, Osteo,  possible Cushing's: A 20 yo epigenetic nightmare
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/t1d-celiac-hypothyroid-osteo-possible-cushings-a-20-yo-epigenetic-nightmare.4228/>
**Subforum:** Optimal Labs
**Posts:** 45
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### TheHolyCat — 2012-10-10T11:18:04+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/t1d-celiac-hypothyroid-osteo-possible-cushings-a-20-yo-epigenetic-nightmare.4228/post-57267>

Hey, everyone! Although I've been lurking around on the forums for months now, I have yet to post very much. I suppose there's no time like the present! The following lab results were obtained through web-based providers over a number of months between the ages of 19 & 20. In order to put some of my results in context, I feel that I should disclose a few of my past & current health conditions:

Type 1 Diabetes (Dx age 11 months), Mild Concussion (age 8 years), Celiac Disease (Dx age 13 years?), Gastroparesis (suspected since age 13 or so), Hypothyroidism (Dx age 16 years), Hepatic Steatosis (aka Fatty Liver, Dx age 16 years, & normal BMI range), ADHD (Dx age 19 years), Osteopenia (Dx age 19 years), Hypercortisolism/possible Cushing's (FINALLY Dx by Endo after self-ordered labs, age 20 years), Morphea (a form of localized scleroderma, Dx age 17 years), and according to my last Urine Heavy Metals test, very elevated levels of Mercury & Lead.

And finally, for good measure, I should also mention my perpetual raging sinus infections (2 ENTs advocate surgery, but will not proceed until several other issues have been addressed), hair loss, rapidly degenerating TMJ discs (confirmed by MRI), and sudden onset bout of DKA (Diabetic Ketoacidosis) & still inexplicable pneumomediastinum (aka air in my chest cavity) in April. So, all in all, it's been an exciting past few years.

And without further adieu, the numbers:

12/23/11 Metametrix Fat Soluble Vitamin Profile

**Vitamin A

Retinol 0.58mg/L (0.29-1.05mg/L)

Beta-Carotene 0.17mg/L (0.10-2.71mg/L)

**Vitamin E

Alpha-Tocopherol **8.1mg/L (6.8-31.7mg/L)

Gamma-Tocopherol 0.64mg/L (0.06-2.99mg/L)

**Vitamin D

25 Hydroxy D **26**

Vitamin K**

Undercarboxylated Osteocalcin 4.6ng/mL**

CoQ10**

0.53mg/L** (0.48-3.04mg/L)

### TheHolyCat — 2012-10-10T11:25:37+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/t1d-celiac-hypothyroid-osteo-possible-cushings-a-20-yo-epigenetic-nightmare.4228/post-57270>

12/6/11 Thyroid Panel

Free T3 **2.5pg/mL** (2.3-5.0pg/mL)

Free T4 1.05ng/dL (0.93-1.60ng/dL)

I have the results from a number of other labs, including the Metametrix DNA Stool Analysis, HLA DQA/DQB allele (genetic test tied to Celiac & autoimmune disease susceptibility) test, several CBCs, CMPs, a Holman Omega 3 test, a Genova Amino Acids Urinalysis, Heavy Metals Urinalysis, another ASI, a BIA (Bioelectrical impedance analysis), a DEXA scan, an ALCAT (or some other food allergy panel), mold testing done by my ENT, and several other panels done by my functional medicine doc, & Endocrinologist that I will put up at some point in the near future. I also plan to get a more complete hormone panel (Pregnenolone, DHEA, DHEA-s, SHBG, FSH, LH, Prolactin, Progesterone, Testosterone, and all 3 Estrogens if I can convince someone to go for it), full antibody panel a-la cyrex's new array, th1/th2 balance, Renin & Aldosterone (I've been having major blood pressure fluctuations), hs-CRP, D3, & a full lipid panel with whatever else I can get done (hopefully VAP and/or NMR). I should also be receiving the results of a 23andme test, Spectracell's new(ish) Telomere length test, & a 3T MRI scan of my pituitary sometime soon, & will share whatever revelations those things bring. Here's to optimization!!!

**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/t1d-celiac-hypothyroid-osteo-possible-cushings-a-20-yo-epigenetic-nightmare.4228/post-63521>

1\. The 1/13/2012 Hormone Panel was drawn during ovulation phase.

2\. I was 19 during much of the past 12 months' testing, but I am 20 now.

3\. 10/1/2012 CBC: I actually have some Jarrow brand Evening Primrose oil capsules in my refrigerator, & will be sure to use them pending my next EFA ratio test. I also stopped using the FCLO several months ago. Currently eating lots of shellfish, calamari, shrimp, salmon, crab, & cod livers.

4\. 10/5/2012 Thyroid panel: Yes, you are correct in assuming that I was using Cytomel only during this test period (I was attempting to get around my high Reverse T3 problem, but decided that was probably not the best course of action). I have since returned to using Armour Thyroid at a rate of 180mg/day.

Thanks for all of your help!

> **TheHolyCat;66354 wrote:** The 10/26/12 Hormone Panel was drawn on day # 2 or 3.

Estradiol 79 low, should be 120 pg/mL

FSH 3.34 low, should be 6.5 mIU/mL

LH 9.5 high, should be 5 mIU/mL

progesterone 0.68 low, should be 1.5 ng/mL

///////////////

Next time add pregnenolone,DHEAs, cortisol AM/PM to set like this,

then you will be in better position to make some observations.

**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/t1d-celiac-hypothyroid-osteo-possible-cushings-a-20-yo-epigenetic-nightmare.4228/post-64518>

> **RiverNeighbor;64919 wrote:** HolyCat, did they also do an MRI? I think I remember reading that you were scheduled for one....

Yes. I had one done several weeks ago at a local imaging center, and the on-staff radiologist didn't note anything pituitary-related in the report. In spite of the clear report, I have mailed a disc with my images to a couple of neurosurgeons for additional opinions (not sure when I'll actually hear from them), as the pituitary can be a pretty dicey area for a regular radiologist to detect microadenomas, hyperplasia, etc. Also, there's still some question as to whether or not the scan was performed correctly. My doctor & I had decided that a pituitary-focused, dynamic 3T MRI performed with & without IV contrast was appropriate for my situation (best resolution for images), & I even hand carried the requisition form to the imaging center so that I could make sure that the scan would be performed correctly. But, as you can see in the radiologist's report, they list a 1.5T MRI (lower resolution than 3T MRI), and although I've called for clarification, I'm still not sure that it was performed on the 3T machine. Anyway, I hope to reach some sort of resolution on this within the next week, since I'm really hoping that I don't have to repeat the imaging study.

Here's a PDF of the radiologist's report:

[https://docs.google.com/file/d/0BzNBQyEB2JZ9SUhMS1k2bW9pWmc/edit](http://https://docs.google.com/file/d/0BzNBQyEB2JZ9SUhMS1k2bW9pWmc/edit)

HolyCat, Do you look like you have cushings? Buffalo hump, the red/purple stretch marks?

### gsteppe14@gmail.com — 2012-11-25T15:28:35+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/t1d-celiac-hypothyroid-osteo-possible-cushings-a-20-yo-epigenetic-nightmare.4228/post-65572>

> Holycat, I've looked at some of your labs.Â  What jumps out is your uncontrolled blood sugar.Â  You're taking insulin, I presume.Â  How are you controlling your blood sugar?Â  That's the most important thing right now.Â  Your nutrition profile and hormone panels are important but if your BG is that high, they're immaterial.Â  Your A1c is way too high and so is your fasting (I mean 334!).Â  I'd do a Bernstein style VLC diet if you can stand it, that minimizes your insulin usage.Â 
>
> Second, I notice several ANA markers.Â  Your CRP is high probably because of the autoimmune fire that's burning.Â  Celiac is probably the first mover and the most primary autoimmune issue you have.Â  That probably kickstarts all other autoimmune diseases.Â  You don't seem to have lupus symptoms, although you're ANA positive.Â  No RA symptoms though your RAF is high enough.Â  You didn't yet test positive for specific blood markers (antiDS negative).Â  Your thyroid issue isn't that bad and your WBC's haven't started falling.Â  That's a good thing.Â  You're young.Â  There is some time between now when you're ANA positive to when such seropositive markers appear:Â  it usually takes 5+ years.Â  You can try not to become seropositive for those autoimmune diseases that you don't have a specific markers for.Â  You can do that with a Paleo diet and managing your BG.Â  Once clinical symptoms appear, it's too late.Â 
>
> Â

# Thread: Jimmy interviewing physical therapist
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/jimmy-interviewing-physical-therapist.4229/>
**Subforum:** Optimal Fitness
**Posts:** 1
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Michael — 2012-10-10T11:32:49+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/jimmy-interviewing-physical-therapist.4229/post-57271>

This is Jimmy Moore's latest download.

This lady is called Ann Wendel. She's a highly qualified physical therapist who also has experience with a range of bodywork disciplines. She's on his show because she also eats paleo.

The first part of the interview is mostly about diet and mostly familiar. How many times does one need to be told sugar can be addictive? However, about two-thirds of the way in Jimmy finally gets her talking about her own subject and the truth-bombs start going off.

He asks her about the paleo obsession with "crossfit" and she politely says it's a good thing, gets people out, and can be helpful for then ... And then quietly and devastatingly proceeds to lay out how and why it can do harm.

She's also interesting on lifting. I've often thought that when people blithely repeat soundbites like "lift heavy things" the person saying it is probably a well-built 6' 2" athletic person with no gross postural problems. They're just not aware that you can't just give that as blanket advice and assume it's appropriate for everyone. One of your readers or listeners may be a frail 5' 2" person with a touch of scoliosis.

I remember the above example from MDA some time back. A lady who fit that description posted: "I want to 'lift heavy things' but I'm scared."

Ann Wendel says here that if you lack core-stability and spinal-stabilization and start lifting heavier and heavier weights overhead "the weak link is going to be your back".

She's not saying that people shouldn't do more demanding activities sometimes, but she's clearly looking towards something much smarter and much more sensitive to where the individual is.

# Thread: Another CT Win!
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/another-ct-win.4230/>
**Subforum:** Success Stories
**Posts:** 2
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### TheKid — 2012-10-10T13:15:24+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/another-ct-win.4230/post-57283>

I am fighting a nice little uphill battle at the moment, so when I get small wins, I like to share them.

I have Hashis, antiphospholipid syndrome and a whole range of other "stuff" going on. My current state of health has impacted me to significantly that I even had to let go of the one thing that always made me feel good and made me feel like I was doing at least one thing right in my life: exercise. So I've been away from exercising for several months now.

However, lately, I had felt that I wanted to get back to the gym at least on a limited basis. So I thought I would try Dr. Doug McGuff's super-slow lifting protocol. Since you're lifting slowly and at sub-maximal weights, the risk for injury is lower; UNLESS you're me. One time was all it took. BOOM, rotator cuff injury, arthritic flare and tons of pain and inflammation in my shoulder.

I went to the orthopedist and he wanted me to hit the anti-inflammatory drugs. My options, he said, were NSAIDS for a week or a cortizone injection. I told him, no thanks, I'll ice it. Thankfully he didn't argue much, but he did send me to 6 sessions of physical therapy.

Because of scheduling, I was not able to do any CT during the week of physio. So I just let the therapist do his electrical stimulation and I focused on mobility exercises.

But after a week of very slow progress, I was able to get back to CT.

The results have been awesome. And mind you, there has been no actual icing invovled. I have not applied an actual ice pack to my shoulder. What I have done is 5 straight days of CT, doing about 40 minutes at a time in a 50-55 degree shower. Let me tell you, the inflammation came right down. And what impressed me most was the actual impact on the joint itself.

You see, it's one thing for cold to help with pain reduction, and I think we've all experienced that. But what I had not experienced was the almost immediate improvement in the joint that allowed me, for the first time and 10 days, to raise my arm without hearing a sharp POP! in my shoulder. That means that the tendon responsible for that pop is no longer as swollen as it used to be.

So I am hopeful that CT will help speed the healing process. I was told that it would take months for the tendon to return to normal size. I have no way of measuring that, but I surely hope that CT cut the recovery time from months to weeks.

Happy shivering everyone.

### vkiernan — 2012-10-10T14:23:43+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/another-ct-win.4230/post-57288>

Congrats and thanks for sharing. A win is a win and actually this sounds like a big win!

**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 6
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### hope2conceive — 2012-10-10T23:44:18+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cod-liver-any-us-sources.4243/post-57439>

I just had my first can ever of cod liver with my BAB this morning. I couldn't believe how good it was.

Does anyone have a good source...in the US maybe? I got mine from Amazon and it's from Norway and I am pretty sure in bad (BPA) cans.

### JoeBranca — 2012-10-13T21:30:20+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cod-liver-any-us-sources.4243/post-57986>

I believe King Oscar's does not have BPA in their cans. If you have any local Middle Eastern or similar ethnic markets, check those that's where I found some.

### KiwiLauren — 2012-10-13T23:10:58+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cod-liver-any-us-sources.4243/post-58003>

### hope2conceive — 2012-10-14T19:19:08+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cod-liver-any-us-sources.4243/post-58129>

Lauren, that is a great idea. I need to get to a fish store, finally. Great 'seeing' you last night on the chat!

Joe, how do you know this? I was trying to find any info but nowhere does it say it's BPA free...

### JoeBranca — 2012-10-15T05:27:24+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cod-liver-any-us-sources.4243/post-58248>

### Michael — 2012-10-15T11:46:12+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cod-liver-any-us-sources.4243/post-58273>

I think what metal has been used for the can should tip you off as to whether BPA has been used to line it.

Fish tins are often aluminium - I don't know why. Al may not be ideal but is probably safe enough unless the tin contents are very salty or very acidic and subjected to high heat. The point here is that they're probably not lined.

Steel cans, however, will need to be coated - inside and out - otherwise they'd rust. In the old days they were plated with tin (Sn). Hence the alternative name for a can a "tin". Presumably, they are still plated with Sn but some are also lined with a thin layer of plastic. I don't know why - I'd guess that the plastic resists some foods better.

So I think you'll find there's only a plastic lining (the BPA is in that: it's a hardener) if the can is steel. If the can is aluminium, no need. You should be able to tell from the appearance of the metal what it is. Or test with a magnet.

I think you can pretty much tell if there's a plastic lining to a tin, anyway. You can see it. Open something like a can of tomatoes, and you can see it there - like a thin layer of paint.

# Thread: Is it possible to tell ANYTHING from this?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/is-it-possible-to-tell-anything-from-this.4247/>
**Subforum:** Optimal Labs
**Posts:** 4

### Endless — 2012-10-11T02:33:23+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/is-it-possible-to-tell-anything-from-this.4247/post-57479>

My doc is unbelievably stingy with labs...I told her I suspected Hashis and she would not order both the antibody tests, just one of them and the TSH. Nothing else.

TSH 1.25 (ref 0.35-5.50 mU/L)

Thyroid PeroxAB 30 (ref 0-60 U/mL)

In April my TSH was 2.93 and that's all I know. I'm workiong on getting a full thyroid panel from LE but I have to go to the US...hoping to do that next month. In the meantime I have been having new/different symptoms that I feel might be related to my thyroid....pressure/tightness in throat area, pounding heart, palpitations (always have, but more lately). I've lost 12 pounds and while I'd like to think it's from the leptin reset and CT I wonder.

I'd be grateful for any advice, insight, or helpful hints to help convince my doc to get me more tests!

### Patty Cakes — 2012-10-11T04:41:18+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/is-it-possible-to-tell-anything-from-this.4247/post-57518>

Some antibodies means....maybe Hashis. You will have to keep testing for the next 18 mo. Your TSH went down but if you have Hashis the TSH is an unreliable measure of anything. Your doc is sounds totally clueless. My first doc tested me for Hashis that later confirmed the diagnosis but said "You aren't BAD enough for meds". I would eat AI for the next 18 mo. I would suggest to wait for your other results after you get them next month and then you will know more about the story of your thyroid.

### Endless — 2012-10-11T17:39:40+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/is-it-possible-to-tell-anything-from-this.4247/post-57603>

Thanks. I am already eating AI (I think) and proceeding as if I have it. I know that might confuse my test results but I'd rather get a head start.

### Patty Cakes — 2012-10-11T18:31:24+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/is-it-possible-to-tell-anything-from-this.4247/post-57614>

It will most likely only affect the antibodies but from my experience it takes a while for them to calm down after eating AI. Good luck on the new tests!

# Thread: you gotta have heart... ok, but pig heart?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/you-gotta-have-heart-ok-but-pig-heart.4257/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 4
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### granitereno@gmail.com — 2012-10-11T18:10:11+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/you-gotta-have-heart-ok-but-pig-heart.4257/post-57610>

So what I am reading is:

\*heart is good for us.

\*raw heart is very good for us

This seems easy, as I have some good sources for pastured hearts: beef, lamb and pork.

I have been eating the beef and lamb very rare any way, so transitioning to raw won't be that big a lift.

However... what do we think about raw pig?

It is about as clean as you can get: pastured, raised in the woods with no more than two per acre.

Thoughts?

### ealachan — 2012-10-11T18:14:58+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/you-gotta-have-heart-ok-but-pig-heart.4257/post-57611>

I think it probably depends on what they were fed when they were alive, just like any other meat. I don't know if pastured pork is as high in Omega-6's as grain-fed...? It would be interesting to find out.

I like grassfed calf heart, personally. I have some defrosting in my fridge right now, in fact.

### Integral_D — 2012-10-11T19:35:01+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/you-gotta-have-heart-ok-but-pig-heart.4257/post-57622>

I would be wary of eating raw pork-anything. There is a long tradition of eating raw "red" meats (e.g., beef tartare) and seafood, but pretty much every culture stays away from raw pork. I'd take that as an cue.

### granitereno@gmail.com — 2012-10-12T17:07:38+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/you-gotta-have-heart-ok-but-pig-heart.4257/post-57751>

I hadn't thought of this from the traditional/cultural paradigm.

But that makes a lot of sense to me.

Thanks!

# Thread: Arctic Diet
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/arctic-diet.4259/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 2
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Michael — 2012-10-11T20:28:20+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/arctic-diet.4259/post-57632>

Quote from Geoff Bond:

> The Eskimo diet is overbalanced in favor of the omega-3 oils, the opposite to that of the western diet. This causes overproduction of compounds that abnormally _reduce blood platelet stickiness and blood clotting, which explains the Eskimo's unstoppable nosebleeds and immunity to heart disease.
>
>
>
> However, most people in the west have the opposite problem ... Steven Gundry, medical director of the International Heart Institute, in Palm Springs, relates how American surgeons could not understand the difficulty that Japanese surgeons had in controlling bleeding under the surgeon's knife. Belatedly, they realized that this is the normal condition for healthy people: the Japanese with their diet rich in oily fish, have the omega-3/omega-6 balance about right._

_

[http://www.amazon.com/Deadly-Harvest-Intimate-Relationship-Between/dp/0757001424/](http://http://www.amazon.com/Deadly-Harvest-Intimate-Relationship-Between/dp/0757001424/)

I'm not sure about a number of things in Geoff Bond's book, but this one has the ring of truth.

I think the nosebleeds are mentioned in Kabloona_, which is contemporary with the older life and by an actual anthropologist.

Presumably, on a "shoreline diet" you'd be getting plenty of seafood, but you'd also probably be eating some terrestrial animals and abundantly of vegetable foods (and fruits in-season) and perhaps nuts. This is where omega-6s would be coming from, and this is what you can't do in the arctic.

The Mediterranean diet comes to mind again. I don't want to say "low carb Mediterranean". Maybe better to say grain- and legume-free Mediterranean. That might be pretty close.

### SeaHorse — 2012-10-11T22:37:18+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/arctic-diet.4259/post-57654>

_Kabloona was one of my favorite books I read when I was studying arctic anthropology and history...also the essays by Barry Lopez Arctic Dreams_

There are lots of berries and plants in the arctic during the summer...the indigenous people were shore based during the summer... there was a big difference between what they ate during the short summers and what they ate during the winter...clearly emphasizing omega 3, but there would have been carbs and sugars and starches to some degree.


══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# Thread: The 6th most important thing you need to know about Diet and Exercise
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-6th-most-important-thing-you-need-to-know-about-diet-and-exercise.4260/>
**Subforum:** Optimal Fitness
**Posts:** 14
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Barry — 2012-10-11T20:50:56+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-6th-most-important-thing-you-need-to-know-about-diet-and-exercise.4260/post-57634>

If I'm honest, I never expected to write more then the "1st" post. I don't think 5 steps ahead like neurosurgeons. However, I've realised that I've got more to say and hopefully it helps and resonates with some. Again, these are just my opinions of how I see things from where I sit.

Take the Journey to Convince Yourself

I think one of the biggest challenges people face, or more importantly, the biggest challenge that people do not overcome, is to convince themselves. You may have found the best advice and read all the articles and blogs. You may have bought the best coconut oil and the best resveratrol. You may have found the best work-out routine (or what you were told was the â€œbestâ€￾) and you may be following the best CT protocol. But things arenâ€™t working out, weight loss is still a problem and your CRP is still high. Whats going on ? Well before I home in the specific point I am trying to make here, problems are always multi-factorial. Everything counts. My previous 5 posts count. The 30 levees all count. But the point I am trying to make here is that in order for things to truly work, you need to first convince yourself that the decisions you are making and the steps you are taking are right for you. If you do that, the machinery will work better, the process will be more efficient and the end product will be reached when the time is right.

What I find is that people want fast answers and quick solutions. They want to know how to get to the highest point of the mountain without having to climb it. This happens a lot in todayâ€™s modern world with the streams of freely available and easily accessible information. It happens on this forum. It happens in my practice. I get asked about what I think of dairy or what I think of paleo. I get asked lots about my â€œopinionâ€￾ on various health and fitness issues. People want to know what I have learnt. This happens on this forum too. The â€œAsk Jackâ€￾ section. Interest is fine and discussion is welcomed. There is just one big problem that is being overlooked. You should not go on someone elseâ€™s opinion, even if you are asking the most respected professional or referencing the most peer-reviewed literature. They donâ€™t know you. You know you. On top of that, conventional wisdom is outdated and the science doesnâ€™t work. It misinterprets the interpretations. The science of research and studies is the science of research and studies. Itâ€™s not the science of you. We all have to accept that. If you accept that, and believe in yourself and your own monitoring system, everything else is irrelevant. What is relevant is what works for you, this is where the N=1 comes into play and should dominate proceedings.

Iâ€™ll give you a personal example. Many will have read my Fasted State posts. What Iâ€™m doing goes against almost every bit of literature and research about sports nutrition. Running fasted for very long periods, and in my case, on a very low carb diet, is supposed to cause immune system problems, adrenal stress, chronic inflammation, muscle loss and reduced performance. Yet, Iâ€™m never sick, rarely tired or stressed, have a good body comp and win races. Why ? Well, one of the reasons is that what the literature says is not always right and when you look deep into the science you find out the truths. Secondly, and more importantly, Iâ€™ve convinced myself that what Iâ€™m doing is right. I donâ€™t care what some professor has to say about some RCT that says fasted state is bad for the athlete. Heâ€™s not doing it. I am. I know how it feels and it feels good for me. I am getting the results I want and I enjoy doing it.

The same goes for anyone out there regardless of your goal. Learn about what you do and then do it. Through the process of doing it and listening to your body, convince yourself that it is right for you. Donâ€™t do something because you read it somewhere or Dr K said it was the best thing for you to do. I do not doubt Jack here. What I doubt is your lack of trust and belief in the most important part of the whole game, the conductor. I know there is always a caveat to what I say due to the people here who suffer from chronic illness. However, the principle of learning and forming your own opinion still applies. If you learn and then listen to your body as you implement the changes, you will improve your ability to go on â€œfeelâ€￾. If you go on feel, you are then in control. You will know what works for you and what doesnâ€™t so that you can find your own optimal. There is no generic optimal. There is whatâ€™s optimal for you. You need to convince yourself of that in order to achieve it. Find your own way to the top of the mountain. It might not be the same way as everyone else. It may not also be the perfect, safest or quickest route. But the key thing is that you remain convinced that it is the best way for you to go. Knowledge is power for sure. But experience is far more relevant and tangible. Acquire the knowledge but share the experience. Do not go where the path may lead, instead go where there is no path and leave a trail.

â€œBeyond the walls of intelligence, life is definedâ€￾

### Shijin13 — 2012-10-11T21:49:41+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-6th-most-important-thing-you-need-to-know-about-diet-and-exercise.4260/post-57644>

Barry - I think you've just reiterated BG14 but in different words.

btw I've loved all 6 of your posts....

### Jonathin — 2012-10-12T18:19:44+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-6th-most-important-thing-you-need-to-know-about-diet-and-exercise.4260/post-57785>

Most people want to have confidence. I think what this means for many is to have no doubt about what they know or why they choose to act. Instead of internally using reason and evidence over time as one experiences life, many look to authorities, politicans or other sorts of communicative people for explainations and roadmaps to the future. Obviously, the validity of knowledge and truthfulness is sometimes questionable. Sometimes it is amazingly absurd.

Evaulation and judgement can be nebulous in the moment. Often times a lack of energy in the form of boredom or distraction wins out. Sometime it is just a lack of conceptual imagination to envision that something is possible. Maybe we have lost too much physical and psychic energy through bad diet, a semi-toxic environment and a dysfunctional culture to connect to that visionary part of ourselfves. On the other hand, if we can define 'it', we can strive to understand it and possibly overcome our present limitatiions. Personally, I graviate to people and networks of people that are attempting to find their way along that path.

### Barry — 2012-10-12T18:27:08+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-6th-most-important-thing-you-need-to-know-about-diet-and-exercise.4260/post-57787>

> **Shijin13;58964 wrote:** Barry - I think you've just reiterated BG14 but in different words.
>
>
>
> btw I've loved all 6 of your posts....

thanks, maybe myself and Jack think alike after all ;-)

### Linz — 2012-10-12T18:35:19+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-6th-most-important-thing-you-need-to-know-about-diet-and-exercise.4260/post-57789>

Barry, a great post and so true but there is a huge wall to climb before setting out on a solitary journey. First step, how do we convince ourselves the experts might be wrong (and which ones are right)? When the perception of good health is really far from actual good health most have no idea how to search for something better or even know that they could. And public education tells them they CAN'T. Education teaches us to listen to the experts and professors - they will have the answer and yes, usually it is a quick fix - take your statin and your heart is protected, take your steroid and you won't get worse (and someone else will pick up the pieces in a few years time). If those experts actually said loud and clear, 'I can show you how to make huge improvements in performance or health for yourself or I can give you a quick fix to make improvements for the next year or so but it will also do you some harm,' I believe many more would take the better path. And there would be more and more examples for others to follow.

Continual television advertising for 'no fat' fast food. No fat = hungry all the time = can't follow long term advice = need a quick fix, too ignorant or lazy to take good advice. How often are our schoolchildren told, 'question everything'? More often they are told to do what they are told.

If chronic disease also means a declining brain function how can those affected hope to leave the conventional path. With a decent brain (perhaps not by Jack's standards), scientific background, good reason to suspect there might be a better way and respect for the environment, the last thing I wanted to do was question the quick fix. I was lucky, I recognised the decline and when I took my own path a few amazing changes happened (at first not to the symptoms I expected to help) and I found real science supporting the change, but even so it was several years before I had the confidence to stand in the doctor's office and say these are real changes because of what I have done and everyone should, at the very least, be given the basic information on how to start making real change. If you fear fat (and that includes many of the doctors I have seen and most of my friends) why would you decide that it could actually save you.

With hindsight, experience is relevant and tangible but sustained improvement won't be made by luck and intuition, and how many are taught to continue seeking the knowledge they need for themselves?

### Barry — 2012-10-12T20:02:59+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-6th-most-important-thing-you-need-to-know-about-diet-and-exercise.4260/post-57809>

Linz,

I think you answered your own question. It doesn't matter how "huge" the wall is to climb, thats my whole point. The key to everything is that you as an individual climb that wall in continuous forward motion, building your own confidence as you go along.

Sustained improvement comes from individual pursuit. Of course learning and self education are needed too but that is almost mandatory. I'm not saying that people need to just go on "luck".... I'm saying people need to form their own opinions and understandings... regardless of what others say or what is "theoretically" correct.

Or as Henry Ford once said "If you think you can do a thing or think you can't do a thing, you are right"

### Zorica Vuletic — 2012-11-05T01:25:02+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-6th-most-important-thing-you-need-to-know-about-diet-and-exercise.4260/post-62104>

Good post, Barry. Very true.

Btw, have you had any recent races? How are those going? You're inspiring because you do what you love, you don't let ideas and/or people get in your way, and you truly believe in what you do. In other words, you live your own life according to Barry and that is 'good enough' for you.

### Inger — 2012-11-05T09:43:13+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-6th-most-important-thing-you-need-to-know-about-diet-and-exercise.4260/post-62169>

>

Barry...! I LOVE this!! That is why whining has no purpose whatsoever!!! If you think you can not, don't even try. Do something else, what ever you want. Something you believe in.

Or just do it.

The quote from you above is fundamental IMO. That is about to live 100%. It is beautiful!

### prAna303 — 2012-11-05T10:28:03+0200

“But you see," said Roark quietly, "I have, let’s say, sixty years to live. Most of that time will be spent working. I’ve chosen the work I want to do. If I find no joy in it, then I’m only condemning myself to sixty years of torture. And I can find the joy only if I do my work in the best way possible to me. But the best is a matter of standards—and I set my own standards. I inherit nothing. I stand at the end of no tradition. I may, perhaps, stand at the beginning of one.”

### jeanie@truelongmont.com — 2012-10-12T06:44:12+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ox-bile.4264/post-57704>

Any warnings or suggestions or preferences?

### Momma28 — 2012-10-12T07:00:18+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ox-bile.4264/post-57707>

> **Cavemam;59025 wrote:** Any warnings or suggestions or preferences?

I have recommended Standard Process Cholacol to people with gall bladder removed and they have raved about the improvement in their digestion on it.

### jeepifer@gmail.com — 2012-10-12T10:57:17+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ox-bile.4264/post-57718>

Start off with lower amount. You can always add, but hard to subtract!

I just got Nutricology Ox Bile, 125mg, and take 1-3 capsules at meals, depending on how much fat. So far, so good!

### FarmersDaughter — 2012-10-12T16:15:36+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ox-bile.4264/post-57741>

I'm thinking of adding this as well...out of morbid curiosity, what happens with too much?

### jeanie@truelongmont.com — 2012-10-12T16:29:25+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ox-bile.4264/post-57746>

so I was looking around, and saw that there were enzymes that had ox bile along with betaine HCL and bunches of other stuff. . .anybody got any experience? Better to take just the two things seperate or would it make sense to look for a single enzyme package?

### ealachan — 2012-10-12T17:12:54+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ox-bile.4264/post-57754>

I use ox bile in combination with Rainbow Light brand digestive enzymes. The ox bile I use is Nutricology 125mg and I take two with fatty meals. I've gotten so that I can skip it with no adverse effects if I'm not eating a fat-heavy meal...yay, adaptation?

Ox bile really helped me, where Beatine HCL just sent me running for the bathroom. I've never taken more than 250mg of ox bile at a time because the 250mg did what I needed it to do (broke down the fat I was eating). Didn't see much sense in trying to take more just to see if it would give me a stomach ache.

### vkiernan — 2012-10-12T20:37:41+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ox-bile.4264/post-57819>

Diahrea, at least from my own n-1. I use Jarrow, pretty potent and I have to be careful so I don't get the D, but things are still floating so I don't know what is up. Titrate slowly is what Jack told me. I also use enzymes, Now brand, that has some in it and I don't believe I get any benefit from the ox bile in it.

### Destiny — 2012-10-12T22:36:02+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ox-bile.4264/post-57854>

> **FarmersDaughter;59062 wrote:** I'm thinking of adding this as well...out of morbid curiosity, what happens with too much?

You will get a good inside cleaning!

# Thread: Purslane
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/purslane.4267/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 9
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Michael — 2012-10-12T09:50:00+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/purslane.4267/post-57716>

Does anyone collect and eat purslane? Apparently it grows wild all over the southern US and was known to the 49ers as "miners lettuce". I sometimes wonder about exploiting these wild plants morre, although you probably have to be careful where you pick them these days.

### SeaHorse — 2012-10-12T14:14:40+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/purslane.4267/post-57724>

It is a common weed in vegatable gardens in our area of Vermont...I ate it a lot when I was eating greens. It's very tasty and a bit meatier than a lot of greens....the taste is on the lemony, sour side ....not bitter.

### Jonathin — 2012-10-12T17:40:53+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/purslane.4267/post-57772>

It grows commonly on disturbed soil that is largely bare of vegetation. Very common in the West. We probably could have eaten pounds per day this summer! Nice change or compliment from/to other salad green. For a vegetable, relatively high in omega-3s.

### L8F — 2012-10-13T18:55:59+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/purslane.4267/post-57967>

I like it, and we have it in New England for sure. I have heard it is incredibly good for you nutrient-wise. Plus it tastes nice. I kind of think of it a bit like watercress, even though it doesn't exactly taste similar.

### Endless — 2012-10-14T01:22:12+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/purslane.4267/post-58018>

I've eaten it in summer along with chickweed, lambsquarters and sorrel growing as weeds in my garden. Come to think of it maybe I should forget the garden and just eat the weeds.

### Glenn A — 2018-11-27T15:31:29+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/purslane.4267/post-256693>

I eat purslane when I find it, but don't find it where I live now in South Carolina. It was a common weed I always found in my garden in Southern California. Here's a very recent recommendation for purslane due to it's plentiful omega-3 levels, and other health benefits like for liver health in the face of Hep.C.

[http://www.greenmedinfo.com/blog/po...66&ct=t()&mc\_cid=ed4932f5b6&mc\_eid=0c62482334](http://www.greenmedinfo.com/blog/powerful-and-proliferative-formidable-purslane?utm_source=Daily+Greenmmedinfo.com+Email+List&utm_campaign=ed4932f5b6-The+Formidable+Purslane&utm_medium=email&utm_term=0_193c8492fb-ed4932f5b6-86753466&ct=t\(\)&mc_cid=ed4932f5b6&mc_eid=0c62482334)

The site, GreenMedInfo, is a great way to search journal abstracts regarding health. You must be a member to accomplish some things though.

### Katie Durham — 2018-11-27T15:58:25+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/purslane.4267/post-256699>

It's a succulent, which is more obvious in the cultivated varieties that have much larger leaves than what you might find in your lawn. I've ordered it before from superseeds.com. Tasty. A little too easy to grow. Well known for omega-3 if you can trust your conversion rate. (I don't rely on it for any DHA.) I think it's main value is as a nutrient dense supplemental green to the crucifers and the high oxalate greens like spinach which might not agree with everyone in large quantities.

### Sheddie — 2018-11-27T19:05:12+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/purslane.4267/post-256740>

Since, in middle Georgia, Purslane volunteers to fill in the lower area of my container garden items, I let it, thinking it shields the soil nicely, might help keep it from drying out. Never had a completely Purslane salad but very often grab some with herbs, green onions, etc., for inclusion in a salad. Agree with above adjectives, "lemony, watercressy, sour, bitter." You can strip off the green 'leaves' that resemble succulents, or eat the whole stems with leaves. I like that it lasts and stays the same size all summer. It's very attractive garnishing a plate of beautiful fish. I've thrown it in soups but never really saw, or tasted it after that! I've bought some before that had flowers, maybe even the volunteers came from those via my soil recycling? However, none of these small, thin 'wild' ones have ever flowered. Mmmm. How nice to muse about this little plant!!

Some day I want to find a way to collect the free and ample pine and other pollens. Heard some are good for hormonal support...

### Glenn A — 2018-11-29T12:49:04+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/purslane.4267/post-256902>

> [Katie Durham said:](/goto/post?id=256699)
>
> It's a succulent, which is more obvious in the cultivated varieties that have much larger leaves than what you might find in your lawn. I've ordered it before from superseeds.com. Tasty. Well known for omega-3 if you can trust your conversion rate. (I don't rely on it for any DHA.) I think it's main value is as a nutrient dense supplemental green to the crucifers and the high oxalate greens like spinach which might not agree with everyone in large quantities.

Thanks! I'll order some for next year's garden and just let it grow on the fringes with other weeds. It may choke out some other things I don't eat! Actually, I believe I will rely on it for DHA! I'm a fan of getting nutrients from food, and of eating almost everything freshly picked. I'm convinced this is as important as the gross quantity of any food in determining what is available once ingested. So, purslane, being a greater carrier of ALA than spinach by a factor of 2-3, is definitely a potential source of DHA. Yes, it depends on one's conversion rate. The fish oil industry quotes low rates, but there's a distortion going on. See my post titled "What's are the conversion rates for omega-3 to DHA?"
