# Thread: Hot tubs and diabetes
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hot-tubs-and-diabetes.4343/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 1
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### chocolate — 2012-10-17T02:41:03+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hot-tubs-and-diabetes.4343/post-58628>

I know I posted another article about this, but classically, I can't find the @#$% thing. So I went relooking on the internet.

[http://www.ucsdguardian.org/component/k2/item/25608-if-at-risk-for-diabetes-relax-and-hit-the-hot-tub#.UH3utIY9jIU](http://http://www.ucsdguardian.org/component/k2/item/25608-if-at-risk-for-diabetes-relax-and-hit-the-hot-tub#.UH3utIY9jIU)

**If at Risk for Diabetes, Relax and Hit the Hot Tub

It sounds crazy, but sitting in a hot tub might actually help you fight off serious diseases, like hypertension and diabetes

> Type II diabetes is now considered an American epidemic: One out of 10 people over the age of 20 have diabetes. Current trends suggest that one out of three children born after 2000 will develop diabetes during his or her life according to the Diabetes Partnership of Cleveland. There is a pressing need, now more than ever, to find ways to combat type II diabetes.
>
>
>
> Let’s hop in our hot tub time machine and travel back to 1999:
>
>
>
> Philip L. Hooper, M.D., published a study in the most impactful journal in medicine, The New England Journal of Medicine, about the effect of “hot tub therapy” on his diabetic patients. His medical approach: have them sit neck-deep in a hot tub for 30 minutes, six times a week.
>
>
>
> Incredibly, after three weeks of hot tubbing, the average patient experienced a significant decrease in blood glucose levels. One man had to reduce his insulin medication by almost 18 percent to prevent his blood sugar from dropping too low.
>
>
>
> Sitting in a hot tub either increased the effectiveness of the insulin or caused glucose uptake by a different insulin-independent mechanism, similar to the effects of exercise. But nobody actually knows the answer, because nobody followed through.
>
>
>
> Instead, two scientists published replies essentially saying “Nice try, but hot tubs can be lethal.” There are bacteria (pseudomonas) that grow in hot tubs that can kill you, and patients with diabetic neuropathy (nerve damage) can unknowingly burn themselves. Their bottom line: Putting diabetics in a hot tub is a bad idea.
>
>
>
> Shortly after his publication, Hooper must have found out about the heat shock response and about heat shock proteins.To know what this is, we need to send our hot tub time machine further back to 1962.
>
>
>
> _Fifty years ago, in Pavia, Italy, it was a young artist by the name of Ferruccio Ritossa who accidentally discovered the heat shock response. While taking a course in molecular genetics, one of the other students changed the temperature of his drosophila (fruit fly) incubator to higher than it should have been.
>
>
>
> When Ferruccio examined the drosophila chromatin, he noticed that it was changing in a way that makes DNA more accessible to be copied, or transcribed. He figured out it was being caused by the increase in temperature._ Last year, Virginia Vega, Ph.D, and Laura Alexander, M.D., from UCSD demonstrated that the heat shock response improves the condition of non-obese diabetic mice.

**

> This stress response can be activated by sitting in a hot tub, trauma, exposure to metals, free radicals, exercise and even alcohol. No, you shouldn’t go out and get drunk after reading this, just like you shouldn’t ingest harmful metals, but it’s definitely food for thought.

I remember the temperature being 107 in the other article.

# Thread: Link between creativity and mental illness confirmed
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/link-between-creativity-and-mental-illness-confirmed.4347/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 20
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### chocolate — 2012-10-17T05:24:56+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/link-between-creativity-and-mental-illness-confirmed.4347/post-58664>

> People in creative professions are treated more often for mental illness than the general population, there being a particularly salient connection between writing and schizophrenia. This according to researchers at Karolinska Institutet, whose large-scale Swedish registry study is the most comprehensive ever in its field.
>
>
>
> Last year, the team showed that artists and scientists were more common amongst families where bipolar disorder and schizophrenia is present, compared to the population at large. They subsequently expanded their study to many more psychiatric diagnoses â€“ such as schizoaffective disorder, depression, anxiety syndrome, alcohol abuse, drug abuse, autism, ADHD, anorexia nervosa and suicide â€“ and to include people in outpatient care rather than exclusively hospital patients.
>
>
>
> The present study tracked almost 1.2 million patients and their relatives, identified down to second-cousin level. Since all were matched with healthy controls, the study incorporated much of the Swedish population from the most recent decades. All data was anonymized and cannot be linked to any individuals.
>
>
>
> The results confirmed those of their previous study: certain mental illness â€“ bipolar disorder â€“ is more prevalent in the entire group of people with artistic or scientific professions, such as dancers, researchers, photographers and authors. Authors specifically also were more common among most of the other psychiatric diseases (including schizophrenia, depression, anxiety syndrome and substance abuse) and were almost 50 per cent more likely to commit suicide than the general population.
>
>
>
> The researchers also observed that creative professions were more common in the relatives of patients with schizophrenia, bipolar disorder, anorexia nervosa and, to some extent, autism. According to Simon Kyaga, consultant in psychiatry and doctoral student at the Department of Medical Epidemiology and Biostatistics, the results give cause to reconsider approaches to mental illness.
>
>
>
> â€œ**If one takes the view that certain phenomena associated with the patients illness are beneficial, it opens the way for a new approach to treatment,â€￾ he says. â€œIn that case, the doctor and patient must come to an agreement on what is to be treated, and at what cost. In psychiatry and medicine generally there has been a tradition to see the disease in black-and-white terms and to endeavour to treat the patient by removing everything regarded as morbid.**â€￾
>
>
>
> The study was financed with grants from the Swedish Research Council, the Swedish Psychiatry Foundation, the Bror Gadelius Foundation, the Stockholm Centre for Psychiatric Research and the Swedish Council for Working Life and Social Research.

How will the shrinks deal? They want to lord over the vulnerable and need to be assholes is so innate that they went to school for years to have the power. Then I hear that the UN considers psychology alternative medicine. I wonder what Obamacare considers it? I hope they start paying for other alternative medicine. We might get a chance to get well.

### chocolate — 2012-10-18T03:59:59+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/link-between-creativity-and-mental-illness-confirmed.4347/post-58858>

We went through shrinks like toilet paper. We finally got a guy that was kinda like Dr. Kruse. He was a surgeon and hated it. He went on to become a shrink, psychiatrist... the pill pusher. His office was wall to wall books. He just read every thing he could get his hands on. That guy was the bomb, but retired. We had some really stupid therapists partnered with psychiatrists that were also creepy crooks. They have lots of group homes and cherry pick clients from their practice. Each time my kid was hospitalized I met young women that had to escape physician owned group homes. They kindly readmit them to the mental hospital until they can find alternative situations for them. Some may be good, but we have the import breed out here that are looking to get rich off of our stupid system. The really dangerous situation is psychologist /psychiatrist. You have to talk to the psychologist to get your meds from the psychiatrist and they won't split the team. You never know what they are saying to your loved ones. Kind of like a priest telling a kid that what happens between them is no one's business. If they get called out they act like there is confusion. It takes a truly decent person to help someone sort out confusion, which isn't actually confusion, its symptoms of a misdiagnosed disease. There lies the challenge. How many stones do you need to turn over?

### Glamazon — 2012-10-18T06:28:59+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/link-between-creativity-and-mental-illness-confirmed.4347/post-58869>

> **Cavemam;60323 wrote:** My family has lots of docs, and several that deal with the hardcore criminal nutters. I think it's the shrinks that are nuts.

When I was new nurse in the Ice Age I was having a convo with a neuro psychiatrist who told me that nurses are co-dependent and docs are OCD. That's why they work well together. LOL

Even the non behavioral nurses and docs think the psych crews are a bit nutters.

### Glamazon — 2012-10-18T06:34:51+0300

It can vary greatly from country to country. I know some very kind psychiatrists and therapists so I think this broad brush painting does not serve anyone.

### Glamazon — 2012-10-19T04:49:45+0300

### KiwiLauren — 2012-10-17T07:28:53+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/children-removed-from-parents-for-seeing-biomed-drs.4350/post-58679>

TS, this is beyond disgusting. There are no words to describe it. The cruelty of ignorance and power are terrifying.

### kathylu — 2012-10-17T21:10:12+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/children-removed-from-parents-for-seeing-biomed-drs.4350/post-58769>

OMG.

My child's annual checkup is soon. That's our only visit during the year since he never gets sick. I'm always careful to never tell the pediatrician too much. I simply answer her questions, get confirmation that he is supremely healthy and leave. Looks like I've been doing the right thing.

### Shijin13 — 2012-10-17T21:35:04+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/children-removed-from-parents-for-seeing-biomed-drs.4350/post-58776>

so far we've been fortunate - our kids do really well on the growth charts, they're socially at the right point for their age and when we talk about the foods they eat - we always get congratulated, and they keep encouraging me to continue bfing...

It is quite scary that parents, are having their parental decisions usurped by the state, some time even by passing religious beliefs to force treatment upon a family.

Gov't shouldn't, nor will they have the authority to control how I raise my kids, to include feeding them, choosing medical treatments (including decisions to vaccinate, or not vaccinate)

### nonchalant — 2012-10-17T21:49:36+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/children-removed-from-parents-for-seeing-biomed-drs.4350/post-58778>

Kathylu, that sounds most wise. Even if you have a sympathetic doctor, what is written down in the record could be used later by less sympathetic eyes.

### jeanie@truelongmont.com — 2012-10-17T22:32:01+0300

This angers me! The Post, not you KathyLu.

We did the same and kept my sons doc seperate from ours and on a need to know basis. I am finding I'm a little more concerned about busybody parents and the troubles they could cause. I think my doc is too damn busy/overwhelmed to take the time when son seems in good shape. But the random bored housewife who's brain is addled from chronic cardio and all those grains and low fat dairy? Don't trust her!

# Thread: How the stomach functions.
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-the-stomach-functions.4353/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 10
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### seanb4 — 2012-10-17T14:28:00+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-the-stomach-functions.4353/post-58694>

Hi,

Searched everywhere but I cannot get a definative answer on how the stomach works immediately after you start eating or even drinking. Hopefully the answer will help me solve my problem.

Is food constantly being released from the stomach from the moment it goes in?

For example I eat some veg and then some meat at a meal. It should take 30mins ish for the stomach acid to rise to high enough levels to allow for digestion of the meat. In this first 30 mins is the stomach still releasing bits of digested carbs/veg and possibly some protein or is the stomach completely locked of at this time until the stomach acid is at the correct level.

Thanks in advance, Sean.

### AKMan — 2012-10-17T22:01:20+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-the-stomach-functions.4353/post-58780>

It's not an all or nothing game and some foods are digested and absorbed right in the stomach. If you drink booze on an empty stomach or eat a plain potato, for instance, the gut doesn't have to do much work. When you eat fat and protein, the stomach mainly preps it for digestion by the small intestine.

The shape of the stomach allows the mixed, broken down food to be released into the intestines while still preparing newly swallowed foods for the same path.

Food enters the stomach through the cardiac orifice where it is further broken apart and thoroughly mixed with gastric acid, pepsin and other digestive enzymes to break down proteins. The enzymes in the stomach also have an optimum conditions, meaning that they work at a specific pH and temperature better than any others. The acid itself does not break down food molecules, rather it provides an optimum pH for the reaction of the enzyme pepsin and kills many microorganisms that are ingested with the food. It can also denature proteins. This is the process of reducing polypeptide bonds and disrupting salt bridges, which in turn causes a loss of secondary, tertiary, or quaternary protein structure. The parietal cells of the stomach also secrete a glycoprotein called intrinsic factor, which enables the absorption of vitamin B-12. Mucus neck cells are present in the gastric glands of the stomach. They secrete mucus, which along with gastric juice plays an important role in lubrication and protection of the mucosal epithelium from excoriation by the highly concentrated hydrochloric acid. Other small molecules such as alcohol are absorbed in the stomach, passing through the membrane of the stomach and entering the circulatory system directly. Food in the stomach is in semi-liquid form, which upon completion is known as chyme.

After consumption of food, digestive "tonic" and peristaltic contractions begin, which helps break down the food and move it onward. When the chyme reaches the opening to the duodenum known as the pylorus, contractions "squirt" the food back into the stomach through a process called retropulsion, which exerts additional force and further grinds down food into smaller particles. Gastric emptying is the release of food from the stomach into the duodenum; the process is tightly controlled with liquids being emptied much more quickly than solids. Gastric emptying has attracted medical interest as rapid gastric emptying is related to obesity and delayed gastric emptying syndrome is associated with diabetes mellitus, aging, and gastroesophageal reflux.

They secrete mucus, which along with gastric juice plays an important role in lubrication and protection of the mucosal epithelium from excoriation by the highly concentrated hydrochloric acid. When the chyme reaches the opening to the duodenum known as the pylorus, contractions "squirt" the food back into the stomach through a process called retropulsion, which exerts additional force and further grinds down food into smaller particles. Gastric emptying is the release of food from the stomach into the duodenum; the process is tightly controlled with liquids being emptied much more quickly than solids. Gastric emptying has attracted medical interest as rapid gastric emptying is related to obesity and delayed gastric emptying syndrome is associated with diabetes mellitus, aging, and gastroesophageal reflux.

Clap Clap, well done sir!

### nonchalant — 2012-10-17T22:55:42+0300

Wow, AKMan, I'm impressed!

### AKMan — 2012-10-18T00:06:09+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-the-stomach-functions.4353/post-58822>

There used to be a group that talked about the importance of eating foods in specific orders, like no carbs at the same time as fat, Protein three hours before fat, etc... When you see how the stomach really works you quickly see the fallacy of that line of thinking. The body truly is an amazing machine!

Brilliant answer, thanks AKMan.

Since you know so much about the stomach could you speculate on what could cause almost immediate bloating of the lower abdomin upon ingesting anything (water or any food). I also tend to feel slightly full and ever so slightly sickly upon drinking a glass of water on an empty stomach.

Is it because the stomach release acid even in response to water and that irritates an already weak duodenum/stomach? B-12 deficiency causing over acidity? Inadequate mucus protection?

Any thoughts would be appreciated.

### AKMan — 2012-10-18T18:35:58+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-the-stomach-functions.4353/post-58947>

Appendicitis, diverticulitis, IBD, GERD, ulcerative colitis, cancer, pancreatitis, list goes on--none good. SEE DOCTOR!

### seanb4 — 2012-10-18T19:34:38+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-the-stomach-functions.4353/post-58949>

Been to my local GP lots and they haven't been much help, still seem to be stuck in old conventional ways. Have no money for tests or other doctors.

### docwatt@gmail.com — 2012-10-19T05:57:34+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-the-stomach-functions.4353/post-59109>

### seanb4 — 2012-10-19T12:10:30+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-the-stomach-functions.4353/post-59135>

# Thread: Farmed Shrimp from Thailand . . .
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/farmed-shrimp-from-thailand.4356/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 18
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### BJK77 — 2012-10-17T15:56:12+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/farmed-shrimp-from-thailand.4356/post-58704>

Would you buy it? Whole Foods (at least in my area) has a great one-day sale on cooked shrimp this Friday - $6.99 lb. I generally prefer to get it raw, but for a quick meal in a pinch it's great to have some of the cooked stuff on hand. Plus, my kids seem to like it better for some reason.

I tend to steer towards wild caught shrimp whenever possible, but I know Whole Foods does at least have pretty stringent processes in place to assure their seafood is clean/sustainable/high quality. How concerned would you be about nuclear fallout from Fukushima though?

### jeanie@truelongmont.com — 2012-10-17T16:02:00+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/farmed-shrimp-from-thailand.4356/post-58706>

> **BJK77;60240 wrote:** Would you buy it? Whole Foods (at least in my area) has a great one-day sale on cooked shrimp this Friday - $6.99 lb. I generally prefer to get it raw, but for a quick meal in a pinch it's great to have some of the cooked stuff on hand. Plus, my kids seem to like it better for some reason.
>
>
>
> I tend to steer towards wild caught shrimp whenever possible, but I know Whole Foods does at least have pretty stringent processes in place to assure their seafood is clean/sustainable/high quality. How concerned would you be about nuclear fallout from Fukushima though?

Damn, good price for sure! Sorry about hijacking your ask Jack q! I buy farmed at vietnam raw stuff from costco - I think we are taking a bit of a leap of faith on the "all seafood is better than anything else you could do". . . I've decided to branch out into a few farmed things like shrimp (in my mind, compared to god knows what is floating around out in the gulf/japan really how can one know?)

I am trying to get in touch with my spidey-sense though, and go by how it tastes. My costco shrimp are supper yummy, and taste as good if not better than some of the wild ones I've gotten. I think if you pay attention to texture and taste, you can suss out good v bad.

For instance, we went fishing once in a catch your own pond when my son was little. He just HAAAAAD to cook and eat the thing too. . man, scary awful in taste, texture. . . that's what prompted me to kinda go with my gut (harhar) on these seafood things.

### BJK77 — 2012-10-17T20:03:39+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/farmed-shrimp-from-thailand.4356/post-58738>

Cavemam,

You are so right about taste. I just started getting some wild caught pink shrimp from Argentina and it has no flavor, falls apart when peeling and is just nasty. I have had this farmed stuff (Thailand) from Whole Foods before and thought it was phenomenal.

I suppose I shall listen to my taste buds and stock the freezer  Always good to have Jack's a-ok too!

### Jonathin — 2012-10-17T20:35:44+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/farmed-shrimp-from-thailand.4356/post-58756>

Thailand or Vietnam is not exactly close to Japan although who knows whatever fun stuff is going on in that area. NW North America is where the current from Japan goes to.

I would like to find some of those gulf shrimp in Colorado that Jack talks about!

### jeanie@truelongmont.com — 2012-10-17T23:16:51+0300

NOBODY there would dare eat "gulf coast" stuff. I couldn't believe it when I moved here and the whole foods had gulf seafood. . . the gulf of mexico is scary stuff. . .and that was BEFORE the BP spill.

### Glen PDQ — 2012-10-22T00:01:14+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/farmed-shrimp-from-thailand.4356/post-59530>

This is a bit off topic, but 100's of aquaculture farm operations across Thailand and China use EM (Effective Microorganisms) innoculant to control ammonia which relates to the quality of harvested animal whether it is shrimp or catfish. Unfortunately there no way to know which operations use EM by looking at the bag or box in your store. The farmers use this stuff because it reduces livestock disease and die-off and increases harvest weight. I fermented some bottles of this stuff at home to use as a probiotic a long time ago. Its really had a big push in Japan as a remediator of environmental toxins and used to clean up polluted waterways. The three main components in the innoculant are red cyanobacteria, yeast, and lactic bacteria.

### Glen PDQ — 2012-10-22T02:25:37+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/farmed-shrimp-from-thailand.4356/post-59554>

BTW, that EM stuff can be sprayed on animal waste and bedding such as on pig farms, too. The yeast and lactobacteria are symbiotic and the red cyanobacteria feed on both of them.

### kathylu — 2012-10-22T02:37:17+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/farmed-shrimp-from-thailand.4356/post-59557>

> **hope2conceive;60763 wrote:** You are making me want to try crabs, KathyLu! I have yet to try them  Also, are there any restaurants near the Bay you like where they serve good crabs/....?

Mike's Crab House in Riva (Annapolis) is nice, a lot of folk Like Harris' Crab House on Kent Island right over the Bay Bridge and Buddy's Crabs and Ribs is good when they have them. Since Buddy's is in the tourist district of Annapolis, they sell out quickly, so I would call ahead to make sure.

Are you close by, or planning a trip here? Crabs are good here till mid-December...then everyone gets them from Louisiana until spring.

### JanSz — 2012-10-17T18:16:59+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/phospholipids-fatty-acids.4363/post-58721>

Someone pls help me.

I need simple, farmers logic, explanation, once over.

Something that possibly, even I could understand.

How to think about phospholipids consumed separately (each phospholipid individually)

and

fatty acids consumed separately

vs

getting them at the same time.

Process of separating them destroys..... what?

Also

how to measure phospholipid content and adequacy (in blood)?

I have some indirect indicators for phospholipid supplementation, could use some more

(BloodUreaNitrogen(BUN), Alkaline Phosphatase, Creatinine, LDL, HDL, Triglycerides, Creatine Kinase (CK))

morning cortisol

Could use more of them.

\-----------------------------

When we say

**Phosphatidyl-Serine

glyceryl-phosphoryl-choline, or GPC**

Phosphatidyl-Choline

Phosphatidyl-Ethanolamine

Phosphatidyl-Inositol

Phosphatidyl-(Other minor glycolipids)

The group above is generally called phospholipids.

They can contain fatty acids.

At this time, I am thinking Omega3 and Omega6 Fatty Acids.

Food---->(oysters and other shellfish, small sea fish) contain lots of DHA (major Omega3), and phospholipids

Food---->(meats, eggs) contain AA(major Omega6), and likely phospholipids

Supplements---->BodyBio (Phosphatidyl-Choline, -Ethanolamine, -Inositol) with (ALA-omega & LA-omega6)

Supplements---->Krill Oil (have an edge over Fish Oil) because of phospholipid content (they are bad due to excessive EPA content)

Supplements----->PS-DHA (Phosphatidyl-Serine) + DHA, I cant locate it, but is mentioned here: [http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/20523044#](http://http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/20523044#)

Supplements----->DHA(Nutricology/Allergy Research) (any phospholipids theres there?)

Supplements----->Phosphatidyl Choline(Nutricology/Allergy Research) (any fatty acids there?)

Supplements----->PhosSerineÂ® Complex(Nutricology/Allergy Research) (any fatty acids there?)

Life style-------->minding Circadian rhythm influences cortisol profile (so at least PS is influenced by this).

\=====================================================================

Possibly, someone here would be able to get (at least this six) Product Information Sheets

Phosphatidyl Choline

PhosSerineÂ® Complex

DHA

Pregnenolone

DHEA

DIMÂ® Enhanced Delivery System

from

[http://www.allergyresearchgroup.com/Product-Information-Sheets-sp-33.html](http://http://www.allergyresearchgroup.com/Product-Information-Sheets-sp-33.html)

\=====================================================================

### chocolate — 2012-10-21T03:18:20+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/phospholipids-fatty-acids.4363/post-59419>

South Australian carnitine study: volunteers wanted [http://sacfs.asn.au/news/2012/10/10\_18\_south\_australian\_carnitine\_study\_volunteers\_wanted.html](http://http://sacfs.asn.au/news/2012/10/10_18_south_australian_carnitine_study_volunteers_wanted.html)

Thursday 18 October 2012

> We are currently conducting a study with the assistance of the ME/CFS Society of South Australia and are in urgent need of volunteers. **The study follows on from our previous research that has suggested that ME/CFS is associated with mitochondrial dysfunction and an impairment in the way that specific types of fats are processed for energy.**
>
>
>
> Our new study is being conducted to further investigate our previous findings and consists of two parts: Part A (a single blood sample) and Part B (breath testing). Volunteers can choose to take part in Part A only or both Part A and Part B of the study. Blood samples for Part A can be collected at your local IMVS collection centre (located throughout South Australia) at any time that is convenient for you. Part B of the study will be conducted at the University of South Australia, City East Campus after all of the blood samples in Part A have been collected (assessment days will try to be arranged for when is convenient for participants). Study participants will be reimbursed ($20 for Part A and $100 for Part B) for out-of-pocket expenses, inconvenience and time involved.
>
>
>
> We are in desperate need of volunteers to take part in the study; we are looking for ME/CFS patients as well as volunteers without ME/CFS to act as controls. To be suitable, participants must be at least 18 years of age and not be taking any medications/have any medical conditions that would affect the levels we are measuring â€“ note that most conditions and medications are fine.

# Thread: Sharing a babysitting story
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sharing-a-babysitting-story.4364/>
**Subforum:** Optimal Kids
**Posts:** 3
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### jeanie@truelongmont.com — 2012-10-17T19:39:51+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sharing-a-babysitting-story.4364/post-58733>

Friend of my son couldn't go to school today because he's "sick".

I feel with all the epi-paleo goodness, we just don't shy away from germs, virii, sick kids etc. So I happily volunteered to let him hang here this a.m. as his mom had something else to do. My understanding was "and play". Guess I didn't check enough with his mom!!!

He had plenty of energy and didn't act sick, and I let him go out in our nice sunshine and good weather and run around and play. He was super energetic!

Mom was visibly annoyed and not a little upset that I let her sick child outside to play in the sun and have a good time. "He needs to be in watching TV or something" she said.

OY VEY - the brainwashing of CW goes so deep.

This is a really good mom, who used to be paleo!!!

### Shijin13 — 2012-10-17T19:59:13+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sharing-a-babysitting-story.4364/post-58736>

Send them out side and let them play. Sick or not. If they are sick but still have energy. Let them go forth and build their immune system. When they are sick & don't want to get out of bed let them sleep their far more cognizant of what their little bodies need vs what we think they need.

### ealachan — 2012-10-17T20:59:55+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sharing-a-babysitting-story.4364/post-58768>

I wonder if it occurred to her at all that she might have been duped by the kid? I can't be the only one who played the "oh, \*cough cough\*, I'm SIIIICK, I can't go to school today" card.

# Thread: Seafood From Asia Raised on Pig Waste
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/seafood-from-asia-raised-on-pig-waste.4367/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 11
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Michael — 2012-10-17T21:50:07+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/seafood-from-asia-raised-on-pig-waste.4367/post-58779>

> Seafood raised on pig feces and crawling with flies is being sold to U.S. consumers, according to a new report.
>
>
> Lots of places doing it:
>
>
>
> Vietnam. "Flies," it says, "crawl over baskets of processed shrimp." ..
>
>
>
> South Korean shellfish ... might have been exposed to human fecal waste or otherwise contaminated.
>
>
>
> Outside Hong Kong, at a tilapia farm, fish are fed a diet that includes pig and geese feces.

[http://abcnews.go.com/Business/consumers-eating-feces-tainted-shrimp-fish-seafood-asia/](http://http://abcnews.go.com/Business/consumers-eating-feces-tainted-shrimp-fish-seafood-asia/)

### kathylu — 2012-10-17T23:52:18+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/seafood-from-asia-raised-on-pig-waste.4367/post-58808>

Oh geez! Thanks. Shrimp scampi tonight (Thai shrimp). The shrimp looks beautiful, but I'll make sure to wash it well and cook it thoroughly...

### KiwiLauren — 2012-10-18T00:43:40+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/seafood-from-asia-raised-on-pig-waste.4367/post-58829>

From the article....

Kevin Fitzsimmons, a professor and research scientist at the University of Arizona College of Agriculture and Life Sciences, tells ABC News he has read the Bloomberg article and finds it "a little misleading. I do a lot of work in Asia and am headed there now for a conference on tilapia. They \[Bloomberg\] are cherry-picking a few items to make things sound as bad as possible." Fitzsimmons is an officer of the [American Tilapia Association](http://http://ag.arizona.edu/azaqua/ata.html) and an expert on seafood production in Asia.

For starters, he says, seafood shipments from Asia to the U.S. number in the "hundreds of thousands, if not millions," so the fact that 1,380 from Vietnam have been returned since 2007 is relatively insignificant.

Second, he says, the practice in Asia of putting hog feces into fish ponds dates back "thousands of years," and is not as repellent as it at first might sound. Why? "Because the fish are not eating the feces. The feces are added to the water to produce an algae bloom," he says, which in turn produces a form of plankton that the fish then eat.

### Glamazon — 2012-10-18T06:52:34+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/seafood-from-asia-raised-on-pig-waste.4367/post-58875>

Crustaceans are scavengers as well as omnivorous. Plankton to dead things. We are at the top of the predator food chain. We eat what has been processed for us by other living things. It's not like we are biting into a piece of fresh pig poop. I think we all, including me, need to get over our resistance about our food neuroses. I gots plenty of them.

### Michael — 2012-10-18T07:13:04+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/seafood-from-asia-raised-on-pig-waste.4367/post-58877>

> **KiwiLauren;60366 wrote: From the article....
>
>
>
> Kevin Fitzsimmons, a professor and research scientist at the University of Arizona College of Agriculture and Life Sciences, tells ...
>
>
> Yeah, he says all that, and I linked the article so that people could read the whole thing. The short quotes were more fun, though.
>
>
>
> Doubtless everything he says is true, but I doubt poor fish-farming practices are anything to get blasÃ© about. There is some bad stuff going on:
>
>
>
>
> > Fuqing is one of the centers of a booming industry that over two decades has transformed this country into the biggest producer and exporter of seafood in the world, and the fastest-growing supplier to the United States.
> >
> >
> >
> > But that growth is threatened by the two most glaring environmental weaknesses in China: acute water shortages and **water supplies contaminated by sewage, industrial waste and agricultural runoff that includes pesticides. The fish farms, in turn, are discharging wastewater that further pollutes the water supply.**
> >
> >
> >
> > “Our waters here are filthy,” said Ye Chao, an eel and shrimp farmer
>
>
>
> He said it. And he's the one doing it.
>
>
>
> Farmers have coped with the toxic waters by mixing illegal veterinary drugs and pesticides into fish feed,** which helps keep their stocks alive yet leaves poisonous and carcinogenic residues in seafood, posing health threats to consumers.
>
>
>
> Environmental degradation, in other words, has become a food safety problem, and scientists say the long-term risks of consuming contaminated seafood could lead to higher rates of cancer and liver disease and other afflictions.

[http://www.nytimes.com/2007/12/15/world/asia/15fish.html](http://http://www.nytimes.com/2007/12/15/world/asia/15fish.html)

When I eat farmed seafood, I think I'll stick with what's coming from Scotland, thank you very much, Mr. Fitzsimmons. It may be an unnecessary precaution, but it makes me feel happier.

### KiwiLauren — 2012-10-18T07:48:45+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/seafood-from-asia-raised-on-pig-waste.4367/post-58879>

I agree (I buy all my seafood from Australia & New Zealand), just thought the 'balancing' quotes were important. I also wondered something, hopefully wrongly... is this perhaps the practice all over the world? If we got a Chinese newspaper would we read about the nasty practices of fish farms in the US? I hope not.

### Michael — 2012-10-18T08:19:36+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/seafood-from-asia-raised-on-pig-waste.4367/post-58881>

> **KiwiLauren;60418 wrote:** I agree (I buy all my seafood from Australia & New Zealand), just thought the 'balancing' quotes were important. I also wondered something, hopefully wrongly... is this perhaps the practice all over the world? If we got a Chinese newspaper would we read about the nasty practices of fish farms in the US? I hope not.

I'll bet Australian and New Zealand stuff is pretty good. You've a fairly low population density there, so less pressure on resources, and you've got officials who are answerable to the public (unlike in China).

At one time I'd have thought any mature democracy would have functioning governmental and legal systems to keep businesses that step out of line in check. Now I'm not so sure. There's a lawyer who's taking on big food companies in the U.S. because the FDA seems unable or unwilling to enforce the law against them:

[http://www.bbc.co.uk/news/magazine-19953716](http://http://www.bbc.co.uk/news/magazine-19953716)

Is enforcement much better in farming? I don't know.

I'd think it was better than in somewhere like China, but whether it's anywhere near good enough.

Joel Salatin seems to find as a small farmer that he's harassed by regulations that are supposed to guarantee appropriate standards for public health, but don't. But large agribusinesses might get a nod and a wink because they're seen as providing a lot of employment or something.

There are exploding pig farms in America's Mid-West and no-one knows why:

[http://www.wired.com/wiredscience/2012/03/hog-manure-foam/](http://http://www.wired.com/wiredscience/2012/03/hog-manure-foam/)

Can it by right to farm in that way? Would you want to eat the bacon, given the conditions at the farm and what the pigs are likely fed on - or even on humane grounds?

### JanSz — 2012-10-20T00:26:11+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/seafood-from-asia-raised-on-pig-waste.4367/post-59242>

When I buy my seafood at H-mart and it is labeled "wild caught" or "wild"

is that the best I can do?

//

### Joann — 2012-10-20T01:57:26+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/seafood-from-asia-raised-on-pig-waste.4367/post-59257>

I only buy wild caught.....expensive but I dont eat that much. My dh buys wild caught , frozen packs at Sam's club not expensive at all. 20$ for 2.5 lbs salmon or cod

### Stavity — 2012-10-20T13:56:42+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/seafood-from-asia-raised-on-pig-waste.4367/post-59310>

I try and make sure the fish I am buying is not sent to China to be cleaned and deboned. That to me is a bad practice. They unfreeze the fish to clean and debone then freeze it again. I have health issues that are dependent on me eating as clean as I can.

### chocolate — 2012-10-20T16:50:25+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/seafood-from-asia-raised-on-pig-waste.4367/post-59329>

> **Michael;60420 wrote:** I'll bet Australian and New Zealand stuff is pretty good. You've a fairly low population density there, so less pressure on resources, and you've got officials who are answerable to the public (unlike in China).
>
>
>
> At one time I'd have thought any mature democracy would have functioning governmental and legal systems to keep businesses that step out of line in check. Now I'm not so sure. There's a lawyer who's taking on big food companies in the U.S. because the FDA seems unable or unwilling to enforce the law against them:
>
>
>
> [http://www.bbc.co.uk/news/magazine-19953716](http://http://www.bbc.co.uk/news/magazine-19953716)
>
>
>
> Is enforcement much better in farming? I don't know.
>
>
>
> I'd think it was better than in somewhere like China, but whether it's anywhere near good enough.
>
>
>
> Joel Salatin seems to find as a small farmer that he's harassed by regulations that are supposed to guarantee appropriate standards for public health, but don't. But large agribusinesses might get a nod and a wink because they're seen as providing a lot of employment or something.
>
>
>
> There are exploding pig farms in America's Mid-West and no-one knows why:
>
>
>
> [http://www.wired.com/wiredscience/2012/03/hog-manure-foam/](http://http://www.wired.com/wiredscience/2012/03/hog-manure-foam/)
>
>
>
> Can it by right to farm in that way? Would you want to eat the bacon, given the conditions at the farm and what the pigs are likely fed on - or even on humane grounds?

the regulations for the waste water processing often requires lots of density, so the farms are near the packing houses. Then there is too much doo doo in one place for the natural processes to clean things up. The poop regs are different every where. I know there is a disproportionate amount of chicken processing in Arkansas. I think there are only three chicken processing house in CA and they are very close to each other in the central valley. I haven't looked for a few years, but one was bought out by a turkey business. You can't ranch if you can't get your livestock slaughtered with the government stamp. The slaughterhouses have to be close. You can't bring a crew in to slaughter them in place anymore.

>
>
> I'm sure it's a hormonal thing. Low t, perhaps? My lack of motivation is entirely due to low testosterone, but my current dr has make sure it's not low DHEA first. (Eye roll)

Low T has never been my issue... LOL, what w/my PCOS... though definitely hormonal...

waiting on Labs to see where I'm at right now... that reminds me I'm gonna call my PCP to see if the labs are in yet!

### FarmersDaughter — 2012-10-18T17:15:51+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/why-dont-i-want-to-work-out-during-totm.4368/post-58931>

I have high DHT, and PCOS, but I have low free testosterone (for me). I can use absurd amounts of testosterone with no unwanted side effects, but I metabolize a good portion into DHT without a lot of progesterone. I don't fully understand the mechanism at play, but I know where we're going with it.

We have been focusing on blocking 5alpha reductase, and that has been helping a lot.

### FarmersDaughter — 2012-10-18T17:21:02+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/why-dont-i-want-to-work-out-during-totm.4368/post-58933>

Maybe I should add that I don't have cysts to my knowledge, and maybe that changes the context a bit? There are so many confounding factors....

Low cortisol can be aggravated during TOM, but IIRC you don't have that trouble. Idk.

### ealachan — 2012-10-18T17:21:06+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/why-dont-i-want-to-work-out-during-totm.4368/post-58934>

> **Shijin13;60364 wrote:** Any other ladies "run" into this?
>
> I get my routine started and I get in the swing of things and BAM TOTM shows and I just want to do nothing I'm cranky and little stuff irritates me.
>
>
>
> Not enough Pg? General bitchiness? Lazy? I want to work out but right now my body is saying no. I'm listening but I still want to work out. Ughhh
>
>
>
> Anyone else?

I wonder if maybe it's your body saying "hey, I'm going through kind of a resource-intensive process here, cut me some slack for a few days". Think about what happens during TOM (or Shark Week, as I like to call it): there are hormone surges, your uterus muscles do a bunch of gymnastics, your body expells tissue and has to start re-building it...there's a lot of processes going on there.

Just a theory, anyway.

### Destiny — 2012-10-19T02:43:57+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/why-dont-i-want-to-work-out-during-totm.4368/post-59085>

If I could take a guess, I would blame the tanking pg at that time!

### Patty Cakes — 2012-10-19T02:54:57+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/why-dont-i-want-to-work-out-during-totm.4368/post-59092>

I can't even think of CTing at that time. I seem to be colder. Working out, not so much of that either. I don't get cramps any longer so PG helped with that but I still don't have a good Pg/E2 ratio ..... yet. I'm thinking that's the workout issue too. Maybe it's a combo of a bunch of hormonal processes going on at the same time to create this????

# Thread: Euell Gibbons
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/euell-gibbons.4371/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 2
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### nonchalant — 2012-10-18T03:24:15+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/euell-gibbons.4371/post-58850>

I've started reading my field guide copy of Stalking the Blue-Eyed Scallop and it's a great read. Same humorous and engaging style as in Asparagus. Early in the book he discusses the huge shell middens that had been found all over the world.

"All life came from the sea, but few people realize the extent to which the sea nourished the childhood of our race...there is ample evidence that a great many of them were seaside dwellers, fishermen, and eaters of shellfish." (p.3)

"Even after man achieved civilization, he did not forget his marine heritage. Those magnificent gourmets, the ancient Greeks and Romans, featured many kinds of fish and shellfish at their sumptuous banquets. When man goes foraging in that mysterious zone between the tides, he is not raiding an alien or hostile territory; he is returning to his ancient racial home and a noble one." (p.4)

He goes into his usual intricate detail, describing the different species and how and where they might be caught. His recipes are a bit dated, with liberal doses of MSG and bread crumbs, but at least, since the original book was published in 1964, it is free of fat phobia, and no warnings about the "dangerous cholesterol" content of shrimp.

Thanks, Serotonizen, for reminding me of the book. When I bought it used, I was much more interested in eating veggies and land animals, and infrequently ate seafood. So I shelved it without reading it. My frame of reference has changed for the better.

### Patty Cakes — 2012-10-20T01:29:06+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/euell-gibbons.4371/post-59254>

Thanks for sharing this and taking the time to put it up in the forum!


══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# Thread: High-carb diet is linked to early Alzheimer's
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/high-carb-diet-is-linked-to-early-alzheimers.4374/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 7
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Michael — 2012-10-18T08:26:46+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/high-carb-diet-is-linked-to-early-alzheimers.4374/post-58882>

> Older people who load up their plates with carbohydrates have nearly four times the risk of developing mild cognitive impairment, a study out Tuesday finds.

[http://www.usatoday.com/story/news/nation/2012/10/17/carb-diet-alzheimers/1637481/](http://http://www.usatoday.com/story/news/nation/2012/10/17/carb-diet-alzheimers/1637481/)

### nonchalant — 2012-10-18T16:51:21+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/high-carb-diet-is-linked-to-early-alzheimers.4374/post-58922>

High carb...Sounds like what's on the menu at Retirement Homes, prisons, military bases, schools...

### Michael — 2012-10-18T19:56:27+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/high-carb-diet-is-linked-to-early-alzheimers.4374/post-58964>

Yeah, there's interesting link on that topic I came across ... I'll post it in a new thread.

### jeanie@truelongmont.com — 2012-10-18T20:00:50+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/high-carb-diet-is-linked-to-early-alzheimers.4374/post-58967>

I had to spend some time at my Grandmothers facility, a transitionary place where they had "apartments", but a cafeteria and staff and group outings etc.

I noticed what happened at meals, and it's stuck with me! I joke about it all the time. No matter what was being served, all the ladies in my grandma's group ae nothing but sweets. I'd try and stock her up on actual food, but it would be krogers finest cinnamon rolls for her. Breakfast, Lunch and Dinner! I would see someone put something else on their plate now and then, but they ate almost nothing - and it was always processed carb fest.

It was the only grandma ever wanted.

### Michael — 2012-10-18T20:09:48+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/high-carb-diet-is-linked-to-early-alzheimers.4374/post-58972>

I'm sorry. It's tragic, isn't it?

My mother broke her leg and was in hospital a few years ago. When I'd visit I'd bring her some sushi or some fruit or something - more the mainstream idea of what's healthy than what I'd know now, but still better than what they were giving her. At least it was fresh - not trucked in from some facility hundreds of miles away and warmed over. What they do saves money doesn't it? And that's about the only basis they go on.

On the same ward was a lady from Thailand. Her husband was a restaurateur, and when he came to visit her, he'd smile at the other patients and say: "Is there anything you ladies would like?" They'd ask if he wouldn't mind bringing in some salad or something - because they never saw anything fresh - and he'd oblige with something nice.

How do they expect sick people to get well when they feed them garbage? Jamie Oliver would have apoplexy.

### Shijin13 — 2012-10-18T20:24:34+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/high-carb-diet-is-linked-to-early-alzheimers.4374/post-58980>

Hospital food sucks. W/both pregnancies/deliveries I had my mom or MIL bring me food we had cooked for when I was in labor. Way better good than the crap they were serving!!!

### Michael — 2012-10-18T20:48:24+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/high-carb-diet-is-linked-to-early-alzheimers.4374/post-58989>

> **Shijin13;60520 wrote:** Hospital food sucks. W/both pregnancies/deliveries I had my mom or MIL bring me food we had cooked for when I was in labor. Way better good than the crap they were serving!!!

Bring it in sounds like the way to go. You're a very smart lady. But then I don't suppose you'd be here if you weren't.

# Thread: drinking milk all year round and are we supposed to be drinking milk at all?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/drinking-milk-all-year-round-and-are-we-supposed-to-be-drinking-milk-at-all.4375/>
**Subforum:** Beginners Area
**Posts:** 9
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### x_danny_x — 2012-10-18T08:59:56+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/drinking-milk-all-year-round-and-are-we-supposed-to-be-drinking-milk-at-all.4375/post-58884>

Okay, I got to heated discussion with a medical student who still believes in the traditional way of life (eating grains, cholesterol bad, saturated bad for you). first i should of avoided since i couldn't stop thinking about fighting the dude. unwanted high levels of anger, cortisol, etc.

i got to the point about A1 and A2 type milk. he didnt know what i was talking about, go figure.

however, people keep bringing this up. can milk be consumed all year round? i see raw cream as a fat to consume during the fall and winter. so i assume milk can be consume as well.

however, the medical student mentioned we should not be drinking milk at all. i decided to keep my mouth shut since i wasn't sure of the answer either.

i see at my local farmer's market that raw yogurt is available. isn't raw yogurt the same as raw cream? i tasted it and has no sugar whats so ever, lol. talk about being more sour than salt, lol.

### ealachan — 2012-10-18T17:08:53+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/drinking-milk-all-year-round-and-are-we-supposed-to-be-drinking-milk-at-all.4375/post-58928>

No, yogurt is different than cream. Yogurt is populated with bacteria that break down a lot of the milk sugars as they colonize and grow. Yogurt is technically fermented. Cream is just cream. Also, just because dairy doesn't taste sweet doesn't mean there aren't sugars in it.

Some people do ok with dairy products, some do not. You have to test yourself to find out. There is no black-and-white right-or-wrong answer for everyone.

### PaleoCarol — 2012-10-18T17:18:27+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/drinking-milk-all-year-round-and-are-we-supposed-to-be-drinking-milk-at-all.4375/post-58932>

Thanks, Ealachan, for your response.

When you say "test" do you mean allergy test or genetic testing? I was tested to see if I am allergic to dairy, wheat, rye, corn, oats, barley and they all came out negative. Yet when I eliminate them from my diet I feel better. I must be sensitive to the chemicals, preservatives and other things in them.

BTW, I love, love love the photo of you in your hat!

PaleoCarol

Some people do ok with dairy products, some do not. You have to test yourself to find out. There is no black-and-white right-or-wrong answer for everyone.\[/QUOTE\]

### ealachan — 2012-10-18T17:26:06+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/drinking-milk-all-year-round-and-are-we-supposed-to-be-drinking-milk-at-all.4375/post-58938>

> **PaleoCarol;60472 wrote:** Thanks, Ealachan, for your response.
>
>
>
> When you say "test" do you mean allergy test or genetic testing? I was tested to see if I am allergic to dairy, wheat, rye, corn, oats, barley and they all came out negative. Yet when I eliminate them from my diet I feel better. I must be sensitive to the chemicals, preservatives and other things in them.
>
>
>
> BTW, I love, love love the photo of you in your hat!
>
>
>
> PaleoCarol
>
>
>
> Some people do ok with dairy products, some do not. You have to test yourself to find out. There is no black-and-white right-or-wrong answer for everyone.

Thanks...it's my husband's hat. I stole it for that picture...

re: testing - I meant in an N=1 sense, not so much a clinical sense. If you feel better not eating dairy, then don't eat it. Listen to your body. I love, love, love me some raw heavy whipping cream in my coffee in the mornings, but I've noticed that when I cut it out, my sinuses are WAY happier. So...I now view HWC as a once-in-a-while treat, rather than a daily staple.

**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/drinking-milk-all-year-round-and-are-we-supposed-to-be-drinking-milk-at-all.4375/post-58944>

man i am heavy coffee drinker.....do you drink coffee during the winter?

**Posts:** 21

### Jasmin — 2012-10-18T16:49:55+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/coconutoil-and-ghee.4381/post-58921>

I have doubts about coconutoil... I know that it is best to use the oil in summer but when I'm far from optimal is it then ok to use all year? I have low metabolism, osteoporosis, Reanuds, and stress.

I am also in doubt about ghee when I have an AI.

### shilohman — 2012-10-18T17:05:51+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/coconutoil-and-ghee.4381/post-58927>

If you are fixing something, then coconut oil and ghee can be used year round. Dr. K has said this many times on the forum.

### x_danny_x — 2012-10-18T18:51:37+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/coconutoil-and-ghee.4381/post-58948>

we can use coconut oil all year round? from reading BG 6, it mentioned for coconut oil to be used during the spring and summer.

also that we cannot take it with seafood.

### ealachan — 2012-10-18T19:45:12+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/coconutoil-and-ghee.4381/post-58953>

> **x\_danny\_x;60488 wrote: we can use coconut oil all year round? from reading BG 6, it mentioned for coconut oil to be used during the spring and summer.
>
>
>
> also that we cannot take it with seafood.**

**

1\. They're just suggestions, not hard-and-fast rules. Everyone is different, depending on their specific situation**.

2\. Dr. Kruse has said repeatedly in the forums, blogs and comments that coconut oil can be used year-round if your level of metabolic dysfunction is such that the coconut oil would benefit you.

3\. You can eat coconut oil with seafood, you just won't absorb as much DHA from said seafood, which is one of the chief reasons for eating it to begin with.

### Michael — 2012-10-18T19:47:34+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/coconutoil-and-ghee.4381/post-58957>

> **x\_danny\_x;60488 wrote:** we can use coconut oil all year round? from reading BG 6, it mentioned for coconut oil to be used during the spring and summer.
>
>
>
> also that we cannot take it with seafood.
>
>
> Sure, but that's general advice for most people. Jasmin says she has autoimmune problems. Jack has specific advice for those people which is not quite the same. Here it is:
>
>
>
> [http://jackkruse.com/the-leaky-gut-prescription/](http://http://jackkruse.com/the-leaky-gut-prescription/)
>
>
>
> Part-way down that page, under the subheading "HOW DO WE TREAT IT? ..." he says:
>
>
>
> B. Consider use of coconut oil as the main fat in the diet until the syndrome is reversed.

I suppose that coconuts are out of season in the winter, and that might make coconuts, and coconut products, less than ideal in some ways. However, presumably their benefits with regard to AI problems are so great that, if you have those, that outweighs any small downside to consuming them out of season.

EDIT

While I'm typing that, the answer goes up. Ealachan must have quicker fingers, or a quicker mind, than me.

### Michael — 2012-10-19T16:51:01+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/coconutoil-and-ghee.4381/post-59163>

> **x\_danny\_x;60559 wrote:** so mainly if you have some healing to do or if it benefits you in some way you can actually eat it out of season.

I think you have it right.

Jack rarely says things without some pretty deep reasons. (And people out there in the blogosphere ought to realize that, if they don't want to end up with egg on their faces.)

See his latest post on why ladies are different from men in "Ask Jack" where he talks about the ability of our brains to signal our world to us.

I don't know, but maybe we could pick up natural chemicals in the food that "told" us it was ripe - and hence what time of the year it was. Something like that. So there'd be a signalling mismatch, if it wasn't.

But frankly the healing properties of coconut oil are so impressive that if you've got problems it just outweighs anything else.

### Lyndra — 2012-10-19T19:04:49+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/coconutoil-and-ghee.4381/post-59195>

Jack has said no ghee if you have Hashi's or some other AI condition as there may be some trace amount of casein left in the ghee. I got rid of all dairy, even ghee, and feel better, but also got rid of eggs & nightshades so no telling which (if any) made the difference.

### hope2conceive — 2012-10-21T02:50:15+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/coconutoil-and-ghee.4381/post-59415>

> **ealachan;60493 wrote:**
>
> 2\. Dr. Kruse has said repeatedly in the forums, blogs and comments that coconut oil can be used year-round if your level of metabolic dysfunction is such that the coconut oil would benefit you.
>
> 3\. You can eat coconut oil with seafood, you just won't absorb as much DHA from said seafood, which is one of the chief reasons for eating it to begin with.

Ugh! I can't remember reading that in your point number 2. And I even did a search. Oh well.

Eachalan, do you have a favorite fat to use with seafood? I am struggling with giving up butter (and I should, I think). I have used macadamia nut oil...but it changes the flavor a lot. I'm a bit afraid of bacon. Not the fat, the potential parasites...I have been told by my functional MD to avoid any pork stuff.

### ealachan — 2012-10-21T04:50:59+0300

I use bacon fat or olive oil. I've never heard of getting parasites from cooked pork, and I wouldn't think that the rendered fat would carry that potential, but then again I'm not a functional med doc, sooooo...

### jeanie@truelongmont.com — 2012-10-21T17:26:19+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/coconutoil-and-ghee.4381/post-59466>

> **hope2conceive;60962 wrote:** Ugh! I can't remember reading that in your point number 2. And I even did a search. Oh well.
>
>
>
> Eachalan, do you have a favorite fat to use with seafood? I am struggling with giving up butter (and I should, I think). I have used macadamia nut oil...but it changes the flavor a lot. I'm a bit afraid of bacon. Not the fat, the potential parasites...I have been told by my functional MD to avoid any pork stuff.

I think when you look at it in a logical fashion, that's a very very very minor concern. Also goes to sourcing your product. Cured bacon from sun raised pork (piggies in the sun for the vit-d win) is safe. Also, almost any parasites still around we destroy in digestions. The old school worry of parasites in pigs was a good cautionary tale with all the wild pigs (pig ban is found in most religions). . but frankly, you just are not goin to be able to go find a "wild" pig anymore.

The brain tapeworm that has been the scare of this century (trichinosis) is killed with heat - and wouldn't be left around in the rendered fat.

BUT, of course we all do the little steps we can, and if avoidin pork is yours - more power to you. No reason you have to have additional fats with your seafood, either. Seems like we get so caught up in 'MOAR FAT MOAR FAT" that sometimes we forget to step back and go the easy route. Shrimp coctail! Poached Salmon!

The old school worry of parasites in pigs was a good cautionary tale with all the wild pigs (pig ban is found in most religions). . but frankly, you just are not goin to be able to go find a "wild" pig anymore.
>
>

>
>
>
> BUT, of course we all do the little steps we can, and if avoidin pork is yours - more power to you. No reason you have to have additional fats with your seafood, either. Seems like we get so caught up in 'MOAR FAT MOAR FAT" that sometimes we forget to step back and go the easy route. Shrimp coctail! Poached Salmon!

There is also the idea that pigs and meat in general promote libido. This is «carnal» desires and thus banned by religions.

### jeanie@truelongmont.com — 2012-10-18T19:43:29+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/lyndra-rolls.4383/post-58952>

Lyndra suggested, and I ran with it!

1 can sardines

1/2 avocado

tbs rice vinegar (to taste)

Orange bell pepper for crunch

Nori Paper

Stop Drop and Roll!

### jeanie@truelongmont.com — 2012-10-18T19:56:04+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/lyndra-rolls.4383/post-58963>

quite good. tasted like old school tuna-salad that you would eat on wonder bread. The vinegar I used had a little sugar, 5 g, but you could use some that didn't. I personally figure if it's the bridge I need getting in more cheap seafood then I will go with this recipe now and then.

I realized when thinking about carb grams. . I usually do about 40 a day now with changing weather. All in the form of vinegar and 99% dark chocolate with a few veggie carbs thrown in. Livin like a rockstar!

### ealachan — 2012-10-18T19:57:51+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/lyndra-rolls.4383/post-58965>

### Lyndra — 2012-10-19T02:06:17+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/lyndra-rolls.4383/post-59073>

All fancied-up! Looks tasty except I don't do nightshades :-(. I had a dream last night about going out for sushi and my mom (long departed) was in awe of how I eat. I bet chopped mushrooms, black olives, or capers would be good, too.

Oh, and guess what I got at the seafood market the other day - yes, a fish head (actually head plus reamed out body). Supposedly came with roe but I couldn't find it so I just dumped it all in a pot. I didn't eat it as-is but did make stock. Baby steps on this one. Tomorrow I pick up oysters (they are special order these days) along with salmon roe (also available by order). I may have to ask for lessons on cleaning whole fish which he does carry.

### FarmersDaughter — 2012-10-22T02:20:07+0300

I eat nori like tater chips though...I lurve me my seaweed.

**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/lyndra-rolls.4383/post-59600>

### Shijin13 — 2012-10-22T17:35:04+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/lyndra-rolls.4383/post-59643>

I made these today for Chaos and Havoc for lunch, as well as myself.

initially they we're like um I don't think so - so I let them just nosh the nori by itself they kept asking for more... so I made them tuna & sardine salad w/ celery, some apple cubes, spread it out on the nori, added some cucumber, and some avocado - wrapped it up...they were excited for their bento boxes for lunch today! I didn't take a pic - but I will the next time I make them! looking fwd to lunch today!

### ealachan — 2012-10-18T20:22:59+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/tinfoil-hat-chat-tonight-8pm-est-anyone-interested.4384/post-58979>

> **Shijin13;60517 wrote:** Lol. Yeah the Feds can be a total killjoy on lots of stuff.
>
>
>
> I didn't tak DH's last name b/c of the hoops I would have needed to go through to change my name.

Oh gods, I lived that one when I got married. It didn't even occur to me that I'd have to re-do alllll my security stuff for all the federal billing systems I access. Ugh. And it wasn't just "oh, fax us a copy of your new driver's license and we're good". It was notarized copies of marriage license, new driver's license, new social security card, and in one case, I had to go and get a sworn affidavit from not only my employer but also the town official that issued my marriage license, verifying that I had in fact gotten married but was still the same person.

Our tax dollars at work, ladies and gentlemen.

### Shijin13 — 2012-10-19T12:45:00+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/tinfoil-hat-chat-tonight-8pm-est-anyone-interested.4384/post-59137>

How'd the chat go. I fell asleep w/the kids and woke up around 10:30 and figured I'd better call it the night

### ealachan — 2012-10-19T16:40:58+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/tinfoil-hat-chat-tonight-8pm-est-anyone-interested.4384/post-59160>

The tin foil hats were epic. There was lots of me being social awkward in the beginning - I don't do the talking so good. Dr. K showed up with wine and chocolate (the food, not the forum member). Cavemam's husband did a drive-by. My mic was too loud a lot of the time. I couldn't shut my camera off, so everyone had to look at me the whole time. Dr. K made a mini tinfoil yarmulke. We're all supposed to live like polar bears and eat like Great Whites. Everyone got to meet my dog.

I had a good time...hopefully others did, too. Again, I apologize for my lack of conversation skills. I'm a typer, not a talker...LOL

### Shijin13 — 2012-10-19T16:54:19+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/tinfoil-hat-chat-tonight-8pm-est-anyone-interested.4384/post-59164>

I couldn't shut my camera off, so everyone had to look at me the whole time. Dr. K made a mini tinfoil yarmulke. We're all supposed to live like polar bears and eat like Great Whites. Everyone got to meet my dog.
>
>
>
> I had a good time...hopefully others did, too. Again, I apologize for my lack of conversation skills. I'm a typer, not a talker...LOL

OH... I am so sorry I missed this... I thought about joining in but figured since I'd already fell asleep, I should follow my body's cues....

### Patty Cakes — 2012-10-19T16:59:23+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/tinfoil-hat-chat-tonight-8pm-est-anyone-interested.4384/post-59166>

I'm with ya Shelby. Introvert means I'm only a talker when talking to. I'm a better typer than a talker although last night I couldn't spell either! I've always used my extrovert skills at work.

I missed the drive by of Mr. Mam....must have been cooking. :-(

You did a great job Shelby and it was fun. This is a great group! Thank you everyone!

### jeanie@truelongmont.com — 2012-10-19T17:14:43+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/tinfoil-hat-chat-tonight-8pm-est-anyone-interested.4384/post-59173>

>

>
>
>
> You did a great job Shelby and it was fun. This is a great group! Thank you everyone!

hehe I made him take care of caveboy last night so mommy could hang with the homies! I think it's kinda the funny part - I'm somewhat of an extrovert in typing and WRITING, but actually speaking too many lizard brain things go into play.

For many introverts (and most others I think) the speaking pathways are seperate from typing pathways.

### Lyndra — 2012-10-19T18:58:41+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/tinfoil-hat-chat-tonight-8pm-est-anyone-interested.4384/post-59194>

It was good seeing & hearing & reading from y'all. Though on my end, there was a varying degree of delay for the speakers, so often two people on camera were talking at the same time. I noticed Cavemam would hear things and then type about them before I ever heard the comment. Is that typical with spreecast or is it my internet connection? I'm told google has some kind of video conferencing capability - anybody have experience with it?

### ealachan — 2012-10-19T19:05:28+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/tinfoil-hat-chat-tonight-8pm-est-anyone-interested.4384/post-59196>

I've never used the Google chat thing...I think it's called "hangout", maybe? Might be worth a try. I really only used spreecast because I remembered liking the format from the EpcotRx Q&A session. That was before I realized that I couldn't shut my own camera off since I was the "producer", though...LOL

Didn't notice delays on my end, although there were points at which the sound dropped out for a second or two for me. I'm not sure if it was due to having lots of people on camera all at once, maybe?

Does anyone know if Skype has multiple-people-on-camera functionality? I have a skype account but I've never actually used it...haha

### Shijin13 — 2012-10-19T22:29:27+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/tinfoil-hat-chat-tonight-8pm-est-anyone-interested.4384/post-59228>

Skype does have some multi-people functionality w/video - but I can't remember the #. At some point you have to upgrade skype inorder to add more people to video calls. I do believe you can call multiple people on one line - but I don't know if you get a chat w/it or not...

### curves64 — 2012-10-19T23:12:52+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/tinfoil-hat-chat-tonight-8pm-est-anyone-interested.4384/post-59230>

Damn, I can't believe I missed the party!

### KiwiLauren — 2012-10-19T23:41:00+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/tinfoil-hat-chat-tonight-8pm-est-anyone-interested.4384/post-59232>

> **Lyndra;60734 wrote:** It was good seeing & hearing & reading from y'all. Though on my end, there was a varying degree of delay for the speakers, so often two people on camera were talking at the same time. I noticed Cavemam would hear things and then type about them before I ever heard the comment. Is that typical with spreecast or is it my internet connection? I totally had that too, Lyndra. Either that, or hearing nothing at all. Internet speed in New Zealand is slow, even at the fastest rate, so this will be ongoing for me. However, Skype doesn't cause this for me (though I don't usually do video). I'd be happy with just audio even which is free. I just enjoy having a real connection with you all.

### diane — 2012-10-20T01:32:05+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/tinfoil-hat-chat-tonight-8pm-est-anyone-interested.4384/post-59256>

It was a lot of fun last night. Thanks so much for organizing Shelby! The hats were epic. Guess I don't know everyone that well - totally thought you guys were joking about the hats!

Even those who weren't on camera had great comments on chat. There were lots of conversations going on - so hard to keep up! And too bad about the internet connection - that's so frustrating!

# Thread: What can a 100-Year Old Irish Grandmother Teach Us About School Lunches?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-can-a-100-year-old-irish-grandmother-teach-us-about-school-lunches.4386/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 12
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Michael — 2012-10-18T20:02:36+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-can-a-100-year-old-irish-grandmother-teach-us-about-school-lunches.4386/post-58970>

> In a recent issue of the journal Nature, researchers studying the microbiota, or gut bacteria, of 178 older Irish citizens found they could group individuals based on the composition of their gut bacteria, which correlated with whether they lived at home, in a day-hospital, or in a rehabilitation or long-term residence care. Seems diet was the driver.
>
> According to the researchers, people living in their own homes had diets that were rich in â€œfiber, fruits, vegetables, grains, poultry and fish.â€￾ When they measured the composition of their gut bacteria, they found a greater diversity when compared to folks residing in assisted living facilities consuming the more institutionalized meals dominated by staples of mashed potatoes and porridge, puddings, sweetened beverages (several times a day), high-sugar and energy dense foods (several times a day), and less diversity of fruits and vegetables. Sound familiar?

Once you get in an institution, you get fed cheap food, which means processed carbs (those "dense acellular carbohydrates"). One might be forgiven for wondering whether it's all downhill from there.

[http://humanfoodproject.com/what-can-a-100-year-old-irish-grandmother-teach-us-about-school-lunches/](http://http://humanfoodproject.com/what-can-a-100-year-old-irish-grandmother-teach-us-about-school-lunches/)

And, of course this is what children are fed, too. Low "unit cost", because that's what's really important isn't it? That's what the Harvard Business School teaches.

### jeanie@truelongmont.com — 2012-10-18T21:13:31+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-can-a-100-year-old-irish-grandmother-teach-us-about-school-lunches.4386/post-58993>

> [Michael said:](/goto/post?id=60510)
>
> Once you get in an institution, you get fed cheap food, which means processed carbs (those "dense acellular carbohydrates"). One might be forgiven for wondering whether it's all downhill from there.
>
>

>
>
> [http://humanfoodproject.com/what-can-a-100-year-old-irish-grandmother-teach-us-about-school-lunches/](http://http://humanfoodproject.com/what-can-a-100-year-old-irish-grandmother-teach-us-about-school-lunches/)
>
>
>
> And, of course this is what children are fed, too. Low "unit cost", because that's what's really important isn't it? That's what the Harvard Business School teaches.\[/QUOTE
>
>
>
> Honestly, if I'm going to be stuck in my old years living in a group home and being treated like cattle, I would rather just eat crap and get it over with.
>
>
>
> My plan, rather than go to a 'home', is to, in no particular order:
>
>
>
> 1\. Drink good wine.
>
> 2\. Skydive and see if I can will myself not to open the chute.
>
> 3\. eat very lovely and fancy french pastries and chocolates to my hearts content (irony here is that if I stay on this path, my hearts content will be none).
>
> 4\. Try cocaine
>
> 5\. Try to pull off an epic "heist" so I can flee the country and use my ill gotten booty to live out my last few years travelling in luxury.
>
> 6\. If the above doesn't get me, I'll become a mercenary/spy and go take out evil dictator of the day with my home made poison darts.

# Thread: Wow.. there are wrinkles gone from my face..!
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/wow-there-are-wrinkles-gone-from-my-face.4388/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 2
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Inger — 2012-10-18T21:54:22+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/wow-there-are-wrinkles-gone-from-my-face.4388/post-59004>

I need to share this!

I have checked my face the last days and recogniced some strange wrinkles I have had on the both outer sides down from the outer side of my eyes to cheek are totally gone..?! I had them for quite a time already, my younger sister have the same, they makes one look kind of tired. Weird wrinkles like you have slept on your face somehow..

I have done nothing else than adding quite much seaweeds to my diet, and seafood too (but I ate some before too regularely, just never seaweeds). I do have smeared some Buckthorn berries from the garden on my face...lol and some reindeer marrow leftovers from my breakfast once in a while.. because I run out of my argan oil and had no money to buy new..

Then I am quite diziplined with having only candels at night, triple protection when on the computer evenings and sunglasses when out in shops etc. after sunset.

Now I do not know which of these components have really helped but gone they are..!!! How cool!

### nonchalant — 2012-10-18T22:08:51+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/wow-there-are-wrinkles-gone-from-my-face.4388/post-59010>

Inger, that's great! I have expected some improvements in wrinkles, with all the other skin improvements we are seeing.

My skin has never felt like this, even back in my teens or 20's. It's like baby skin.

# Thread: chest is sore and minor cough after CT
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/chest-is-sore-and-minor-cough-after-ct.4391/>
**Subforum:** Cold Thermogenesis
**Posts:** 1
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### growltgc — 2012-10-18T23:43:49+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/chest-is-sore-and-minor-cough-after-ct.4391/post-59038>

So, Yesterday I went swimming in water I would measure to be between 50 to 52 degrees. It's the coldest water I swam in yet. I did two 20 minute sessions in the period of 4 hours. The first time after coming out, I noticed a little pain in my chest that I quickly forgot about. I thought at the time it could be related to the cold exposure. The second time coming out I noticed the same pain. But I didn't notice it again after 5 minutes.

This morning, I have a minor cough and my chest hurts a bit when I cough.

I'm lead to believe the cough / pain is due to the CT I did and perhaps other factors.
