### seanb4 — 2012-10-22T14:00:24+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/h-pylori-regardless-of-negative-stool-test.4447/post-59594>

Hi Guys,

After watching a 4 hour webinar by doctor Amy on H.pylori, which somebody posted on this forum, that suggests h.pylori can be present and problematic regardless of negative tests, I have been entertaining the though that this could be the root of my bloating problems.

So I am going to attempt to eradicate it by taking baking soda on an empty stomach to draw the h.pylori out of the mucosal layer then taking pepto bismul, mastic gum and broccoli sprout extract to attack it.

My question is what would the effective doses be of this product? And do you think this would be affective at getting rid of the troublesome bacteria?

### Mery — 2012-10-24T10:07:17+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/h-pylori-regardless-of-negative-stool-test.4447/post-59953>

I’ve just found out that I tested positive for H Pylori. After the test result I was shocked then I realised why suddenly after stopping taking my hormones everything went crazy, because the monster was masking the symptoms.

I’d like to thank the person who posted the 2 video of Dr Amy Yasko after watching twice the first and second video I know why I was getting bigger. She said that a ketogenic diet feed the Helicobacter no wonder I could not lose any weight, she said that this bacteria cause leaky gut. She recommends high carbohydrate low fat diet to tackle these bacteria.

I could go forever trying to heal my gut and hormone but if I don’t get rid of this fxxx I will never be able to heal my body.

There is one thing she said that when you have Helicobacter it is difficult to absorb B12 and Iron, however my test result were normal B12= 970 and ferritin =98 so I’m confused about this one.

At the moment I’m doing Dr Hulda Clark Bowel cleanse for 3 weeks, I’m already feeling much better, but after this 3 weeks I need to think what to do next. I would like to follow the cycle she mentioned for 3 week.

My Dr have prescribed omeprazole capsules, clarithromycin tables, Metronidazole tables however I’ve not purchased them because I would like to tried, natural staff first.

I’ve cancel the ultrasound scan they wanted to do next week as I don’t wish to stress my body anymore.

Cool, hope it goes well. You should look into using bicarbonate of soda as apparently this confuses the h.pylori and forces it out of hidding in the stomach mucosa. Read some stuff on Dr.Amy's forum.

I know about the bicarbonate but I’ve 2 more weeks before I decide what to do next.

She said that a ketogenic diet feed the Helicobacter no wonder I could not lose any weight, she said that this bacteria cause leaky gut. She recommends high carbohydrate low fat diet to tackle these bacteria.

This doesn't make a lot of sense to me. A high carb diet is going to lead to more gut fermentation. high levels of gut fermentation lead to the production of gasses - hydrogen being one of them. H pylori uses molecular hydrogen as an energy source.

### seanb4 — 2012-10-24T16:10:32+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/h-pylori-regardless-of-negative-stool-test.4447/post-59969>

> **villjamur\_stevenson;61560 wrote:** This doesn't make a lot of sense to me. A high carb diet is going to lead to more gut fermentation. high levels of gut fermentation lead to the production of gasses - hydrogen being one of them. H pylori uses molecular hydrogen as an energy source.

Hmmm yeah, I never like the sound of an high carb diet.

### seanb4 — 2012-10-24T16:12:56+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/h-pylori-regardless-of-negative-stool-test.4447/post-59970>

> **Mery;61558 wrote:** I don't get palpitation apart from being bloated and putting on weight I'm fine.
>
>
>
> At the moment I’m doing Paul Robinson protocol CT3M this is my four week. I feel I’ve more energy and I’m monitoring my B/P, pulse and temperature. I’m taking my first T3 dose 2 hour’s prior wake up time.
>
>
>
> Since staring this Sunday, the bowel cleanse I feel much better and the bloating is going away.
>
>
>
> I know about the bicarbonate but I’ve 2 more weeks before I decide what to do next.

Will look into the protocol and see what it's about, hope all goes well.

### HelicoVictim — 2013-11-12T17:37:43+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/h-pylori-regardless-of-negative-stool-test.4447/post-100514>

> [Mery said:](/goto/post?id=59953)
>
> **
> I’d like to thank the person who posted the 2 video of Dr Amy Yasko after watching twice the first and second video I know why I was getting bigger. She said that a ketogenic diet feed the Helicobacter no wonder I could not lose any weight, she said that this bacteria cause leaky gut. She recommends high carbohydrate low fat diet to tackle these bacteria.**

Guys. Could someone point these videos out to me where Dr Amy Yasko says a ketogenic diet feeds h pylori. Please I registered just to get this information because due to my extreme food sensitivities I was going keto and antibacterials for my h pylori. I can't find anything anywhere which says that Ketones feed h pylori except this statement. I do not that a keto diet can shift the concentration of good to bad bacteria in the gut but nothing more.

Awaiting a response. Desperate.

Thanks

# Thread: Irivingia/fucoxanthin
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/irivingia-fucoxanthin.4450/>
**Subforum:** Cold Thermogenesis
**Posts:** 3
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### FarmersDaughter — 2012-10-22T18:14:35+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/irivingia-fucoxanthin.4450/post-59655>

So once upon a time, I discovered LEF.org, and a couple of magic beans called fucoxanthin and irivingia, respectively.

Rediscovered them in my move.

Guess what they do....act on ucp-1 and BAT.

took one dose this am, and my temp hasn't dropped in the water once...started at 97.4 and one hour later, I'm at 99.86. 60 degree water. (I forgot to referee my blue ices)

I have been hesitant to spend $$$on bitter melon because I've spent so much already, was planning to wait, but as I already have these...

I wonder what the three together would accomplish.

### bigknitwit — 2012-10-22T19:44:00+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/irivingia-fucoxanthin.4450/post-59667>

I've been wondering what the word is on these supplements. A few years back i was researching, trying to figure out my unending appeitite, and came up with the idea that it was a "leptin" problem. So naturally I looke dinto how to solve leptin problems (finding JK around this point wound have been really, really useful!). I found info from Ron Rosedale, who recommended long spaces between very low carb meals (all seemed very impossible) to improve leptin signalling. Then I found information about fucoxanthin and thought that sounded much easier LOL! I tried it, but of course found absolutely zero result (no other diet/lifestyle changes in place. I thought I'd evaluate the "magic pill" idea first). But I do wonder about using it in combination with all I'm doing at the moment...

### FarmersDaughter — 2012-10-23T00:59:53+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/irivingia-fucoxanthin.4450/post-59735>

You and me both. I tried it, got nowhere, ad threw them in the back of my supplement drawer.

I think resurrection is imminent though...at least until I can tell whether they do or don't do anything.

Free experiment, can't pass that up!

# Thread: Lice
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/lice.4454/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 9
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### jeanie@truelongmont.com — 2012-10-23T00:36:52+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/lice.4454/post-59729>

ok, my son's classroom has had several kiddo's with Lice. Any of you home remedy gods/esses have suggestions for PREVENTION?

I'll ask again for cure if we suffer that fate!

### Shijin13 — 2012-10-23T01:22:45+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/lice.4454/post-59739>

> **Cavemam;61302 wrote:** ok, my son's classroom has had several kiddo's with Lice. Any of you home remedy gods/esses have suggestions for PREVENTION?
>
>
>
> I'll ask again for cure if we suffer that fate!

We went through this in March we just used an OTC thing from CVS. Treated the whole house. We think dd got it at ski school from the helmet she was using. No clue on prevention.

### kathylu — 2012-10-23T01:47:12+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/lice.4454/post-59742>

Oh, ick! Here's an article about prevention...sounds like there is not a lot you can do!

[http://pediatrics.about.com/od/headlice/ht/08\_prevent\_lice.htm](http://http://pediatrics.about.com/od/headlice/ht/08_prevent_lice.htm)

### vkiernan — 2012-10-23T01:57:51+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/lice.4454/post-59744>

My daugher was going thru it with her kiddos and the school wasn't publishing that they had a problem so it was on-going. Eventually after treating them several times and notifying the school district or whomever she had to, she used some shampoo that was preventative and she didn't have them anymore. You should be able to ask at Walgreens or some place like that. Mayonaisse on the head overnight with a shower cap works great at getting rid of them and you have no toxins. I believe it smothers them. You do have to comb thru it though to remove the nits I believe.

### chocolate — 2012-10-23T03:51:38+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/lice.4454/post-59768>

I heard it was the egg white that nailed them in the mayo, along with the oil.

### Glen PDQ — 2012-10-23T19:30:53+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/lice.4454/post-59844>

Lice on bedding, clothing, and furniture must feed on human blood or they die within 24 hours, so leaving the home empty of people for say 36 hours should eradicate the already hatched lice. Oily substances such as coconut oil or pomades suffocate them.

### Glen PDQ — 2012-10-23T19:37:38+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/lice.4454/post-59849>

For prevention?

Learn to love the bald look. Keep family members' heads shaved.

### Owl — 2012-10-25T08:46:38+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/lice.4454/post-60096>

Lice don't like tea tree oil, so a shampoo with that will keep them away.

### stacyj — 2012-10-25T14:42:42+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/lice.4454/post-60119>

I know most here are avoiding chemical hair products......but, lice love clean hair. They do not like sticky, hard to move around in hair. Goop up his hair with gel before he goes to school.

**Posts:** 2

### TribalSpice — 2012-10-23T04:52:46+0300

[http://en.avaaz.org/986/100-years-of-wisdom-from-the-worlds-oldest-holocaust-survivor](http://http://en.avaaz.org/986/100-years-of-wisdom-from-the-worlds-oldest-holocaust-survivor)

### Inger — 2012-10-23T09:23:20+0300

# Thread: Leave the heating off and avoid the gym to lose weight
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/leave-the-heating-off-and-avoid-the-gym-to-lose-weight.4458/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 4
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### ruby — 2012-10-23T07:07:17+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/leave-the-heating-off-and-avoid-the-gym-to-lose-weight.4458/post-59789>

Professor Michael Symonds and scientists at the University of Nottinghamâ€™s Queenâ€™s Medical Centre are using thermal imaging techniques â€” and a bucket of cold water â€” to assess changes in brown fat in adult recruits, and see how it affects their weight.

Read more: [http://www.dailymail.co.uk/health/article-2221602/Why-leaving-heating-avoiding-gym-help-lose-weight.html#ixzz2A5kccAUK](http://http://www.dailymail.co.uk/health/article-2221602/Why-leaving-heating-avoiding-gym-help-lose-weight.html#ixzz2A5kccAUK)

### Michael — 2012-10-23T09:25:04+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/leave-the-heating-off-and-avoid-the-gym-to-lose-weight.4458/post-59796>

Top notch stuff.

I like the advice that if you can walk round in a shirt indoors the heating is too high. To be honest, I've thought _that for years. It's always seemed wasteful to me. And people talk airily about global warming!

These days I do wear just a shirt to the office even though_ it's not warm. That's what's changed. It's a small office and a colleague and I have access to the boiler room, and she wants the heating turned down low enough to virtually not trip-in, too. This is one of the troubles of bigger offices: one has no control over that kind of thing. I have little enough control over most things in my life; I'm glad I at least get not to sit in the normal working sauna (which is something I've never liked).

### bigknitwit — 2012-10-23T15:32:46+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/leave-the-heating-off-and-avoid-the-gym-to-lose-weight.4458/post-59807>

Excellent - thanks for the link! I sent it along to inquiring friends and family.

### Michael — 2012-10-23T15:59:45+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/leave-the-heating-off-and-avoid-the-gym-to-lose-weight.4458/post-59810>

Oh, dear. I arrived in late this morning, having been for a bllod test. A girly little man was working in the front office this morning and complained that he was cold..

Winter? Have you heard of it?

It's only about 17 C outdoors

anyway.

The heating is belting out now and is quite unpleasant.

The trouble is that people experience abnormality as normality, and situations that couldn't have existed 50 (even 30) years ago as matters of course.

# Thread: AI and I am searching for a breakfast staple substitute
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ai-and-i-am-searching-for-a-breakfast-staple-substitute.4459/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 23
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### ljbprrfmof — 2012-10-23T07:30:56+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ai-and-i-am-searching-for-a-breakfast-staple-substitute.4459/post-59790>

I am AI and am working at eliminating the foods that I commonly eat.

When I come to working on breakfast, I am at a loss as to what I should replace egg with. I normally eat 2 - 3 large or jumbo eggs in a scramble.

What do you eat for your BAB?

### Paradox — 2012-10-23T08:10:24+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ai-and-i-am-searching-for-a-breakfast-staple-substitute.4459/post-59791>

> **ljbprrfmof;61364 wrote:** I am AI and am working at eliminating the foods that I commonly eat.
>
>
>
> When I come to working on breakfast, I am at a loss as to what I should replace egg with. I normally eat 2 - 3 large or jumbo eggs in a scramble.
>
>
>
> What do you eat for your BAB?

I too have serious AI issue and have found several favorite breakfasts to be smoked salmon with half an avocado diced and finely diced red onion. I wrap the salmon around avocado & onion. Also I will have a big ass salad with half a can of lump crab meat with lots of different lettuces, cilantro, arugula, again the avocado & onion. Third favorite is sashimi it is amazing how much protein is in a small piece of fish. I try to have lots of veggies with my fishy breakfasts too. Wakame salad is really good if you make it yourself because the premade always has sesseme oil and seeds which is bad for AI issues.The least favorite is sausages because you have to eat so many to get the protein count. Although, liver, onions and bacon breakfast is my favorite non fishy one and you can quickly get the protein up there with that.

### fitness@home — 2012-10-23T15:28:45+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ai-and-i-am-searching-for-a-breakfast-staple-substitute.4459/post-59806>

I miss eggs!

If there are no leftovers in the fridge, my simplest "in a hurry" breakfast is shrimp. Boil water, add frozen shrimp, and within minutes it is done. I usually have a couple of slices of bacon with it.

### ljbprrfmof — 2012-10-23T16:01:31+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ai-and-i-am-searching-for-a-breakfast-staple-substitute.4459/post-59811>

I think I can stomach the shrimp with some bacon.

My old self would do a bowl of oatmeal or a toasted PB & J (make the toast first, otherwise it is a gooey mess) or an eggo waffle. But now I are edumacated and looking for more edumacation.

I am going to miss eggs also.

### ealachan — 2012-10-23T16:53:22+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ai-and-i-am-searching-for-a-breakfast-staple-substitute.4459/post-59812>

Couldn't you just cook up some ground beef, a steak, some sausage...something meaty? My go-to breakfast is canned salmon warmed up in a little bacon fat, with some crispy bacon crumbled into it. I do eat it with eggs, but it would still be good without them.

### MamaGrok — 2012-10-23T17:08:40+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ai-and-i-am-searching-for-a-breakfast-staple-substitute.4459/post-59819>

I really miss eggs, and hope to add them back, but I've totally de-brainwashed myself about breakfast. Now, just like all our ancestors, I can eat anything for breakfast, and do. It's just another meal. And a BIG one!

I usually eat some kind of seafood and some kind of landfood. Seafood might be canned sardines, sauteed salmon, shrimp, or ceviche. Landfood might be ground beef w/ some yummy spices, pastured pork sausage, pork chops, or any kind of leftover.

Lately, it's been a soup of bone broth, some non-starchy seasonal veggie, coconut oil, animal fat, seafood, landfood, and a side of raw liver.  It's wonderful to eat on the deck with my lenses off and chill on in the brisk fall morning, with a shaker of cinnamon by my side.

### FarmersDaughter — 2012-10-23T17:36:24+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ai-and-i-am-searching-for-a-breakfast-staple-substitute.4459/post-59822>

I have been struggling to find a food breakfast staple as well. I live in a food rut...I could easily eat the same thing for a week or more at a time. I don't care.

I decided that canned salmon is the answer-for me.

It's edible, and while its not GOOD it's not bad. It's relatively cheap, easy to fix, and after all, food is merely fuel. Hormonal information.

If it at tastes good, that is a bonus, but I got here by being concerned with flavor over function, and it's time to change course.

### MamaGrok — 2012-10-23T17:44:00+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ai-and-i-am-searching-for-a-breakfast-staple-substitute.4459/post-59825>

ITA. It's a cost-benefit analysis. Taste & variety are nice, but "will I do it every day" is much, much more important.

### Patty Cakes — 2012-10-23T19:30:55+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ai-and-i-am-searching-for-a-breakfast-staple-substitute.4459/post-59845>

I have a burger almost every morning and have been doing this since Jan. I prepare burgers three days worth at a time. Also in the evening I may prepare short ribs and cook them on 200 all night and have that for breakfast. Seafood for me is not filling enough for the morning. After breakfast I only eat fish but the morning needs to be beef. Simple, easy and can do them every day.

### Shijin13 — 2012-10-23T19:48:29+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ai-and-i-am-searching-for-a-breakfast-staple-substitute.4459/post-59852>

Not AI...but since the changing of the seasons I no longer want to eat eggs for breakfast. I make breakfast my big SAT FAT meal. So I do sausage, hamburger, left over soup, and I also add a 1/2 coconut milk and 1/2 HWC w/cacao and maca powder to keep my fat intake up and help deal w/the nausea I get w/taking the cycloset...

### ljbprrfmof — 2012-10-23T20:30:47+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ai-and-i-am-searching-for-a-breakfast-staple-substitute.4459/post-59868>

What is HWC? I guess I could switch to meats like sausage, bacon, and burgers. I had two burgers from last night for breakfast this morning.

I do not think I am ready, mentally to handle seafood first thing in the morning. That may change. I was able to get over no toast with my eggs, so seafood is another challenge.

### ealachan — 2012-10-23T20:38:10+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ai-and-i-am-searching-for-a-breakfast-staple-substitute.4459/post-59873>

HWC = heavy whipping cream.

Here's the thing about seafood in the morning - it tastes the same as it does at night. The first couple times I ate salmon for breakfast it kind of freaked me out, but I got over it really quickly. I can't honestly claim to be one of the enlightened few that now sees food as only fuel / hormonal information and not entertainment, because I love to cook and I love to make yummy stuff...but I DO seem to have a better grasp now of the concept that we should eat to live, not live to eat. The Breakfast Police is not going to come take you away if you have fish for breakfast, or soup, or last night's leftovers...know what I mean?

And I don't intend for that to sound snarky, btw. It's a mental hump that a lot of us are still working on getting over. You are by no means alone in this challenge!

### MamaGrok — 2012-10-23T20:57:41+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ai-and-i-am-searching-for-a-breakfast-staple-substitute.4459/post-59883>

It helped me a lot to go to a really fancy (like Ritz Carlton fancy) hotel buffet and see what was on their breakfast bar. Cereals, yeah, sure, toast if you're slack. Omelette bar, yes. Lox bar, YES! That's smoked salmon, cream cheese, capers, onions, and more toppings for the smoked fish. Sausage, fried potatoes, bacon, lots and lots of different breakfast meats. People have been eating lunch & dinner food for breakfast for far longer, and across far more of the world, than they've been having toast & even just eggs.

### FarmersDaughter — 2012-10-24T03:08:46+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ai-and-i-am-searching-for-a-breakfast-staple-substitute.4459/post-59923>

> **MamaGrok;61458 wrote:** It helped me a lot to go to a really fancy (like Ritz Carlton fancy) hotel buffet and see what was on their breakfast bar. Cereals, yeah, sure, toast if you're slack. Omelette bar, yes. Lox bar, YES! That's smoked salmon, cream cheese, capers, onions, and more toppings for the smoked fish. Sausage, fried potatoes, bacon, lots and lots of different breakfast meats. People have been eating lunch & dinner food for breakfast for far longer, and across far more of the world, than they've been having toast & even just eggs.

That is exactly how my "love affair" (artistic license, check) with savory stuff for breakfast got started...I went to a schmancy hotel in Portland for work, had a killer smoked salmon and horseradish cream somethingorother....drool worthy in every way.

And don't kid yourself...there are days I'd far prefer to have my beloved coffee and creamer for breakfast, or better yet, a big nasty plate of biscuits and sausage gravy, or not eat til noon....but I'm on the "fake it til you make it" plan re food as fuel vs. entertainment.and if I can talk myself into it, I'll stay on course. It's just like the cold bath...it doesn't sound good, you don't want it, but take a deep breath and do it anyway. (And curse a few times when you run into chunks of ice)

Smoked salmon and horseradish creamed somethingorother is a looooooooooooong way from canned salmon.

\[Sidebar\] At least you guys can stomach eggs. The texture of cooked eggs gives me the heebiejeebies. Raw is no problem, but cooked triggers a gag reflex like mad. \[/sidebar\]

### chocolate — 2012-10-24T04:05:48+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ai-and-i-am-searching-for-a-breakfast-staple-substitute.4459/post-59930>

> **FarmersDaughter;61510 wrote:** That is exactly how my "love affair" (artistic license, check) with savory stuff for breakfast got started...I went to a schmancy hotel in Portland for work, had a killer smoked salmon and horseradish cream somethingorother....drool worthy in every way.

>
> And don't kid yourself...there are days I'd far prefer to have my beloved coffee and creamer for breakfast, or better yet, a big nasty plate of biscuits and sausage gravy, or not eat til noon....but I'm on the "fake it til you make it" plan re food as fuel vs. entertainment.and if I can talk myself into it, I'll stay on course. It's just like the cold bath...it doesn't sound good, you don't want it, but take a deep breath and do it anyway. (And curse a few times when you run into chunks of ice)
>
>
>
> Smoked salmon and horseradish creamed somethingorother is a looooooooooooong way from canned salmon.
>
>
>
> \[Sidebar\] At least you guys can stomach eggs. The texture of cooked eggs gives me the heebiejeebies. Raw is no problem, but cooked triggers a gag reflex like mad. \[/sidebar\]

Raw egg whites cause biotin problems and lets candida grow.

### FarmersDaughter — 2012-10-24T04:29:17+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ai-and-i-am-searching-for-a-breakfast-staple-substitute.4459/post-59935>

> **chocolate;61519 wrote:** Raw egg whites cause biotin problems and lets candida grow.

(Ive only ever eaten the yolk)

whites=gag worthy in any form.

Shoulda specified but I forgot some peeps do eat raw white.

### Endless — 2012-10-25T04:41:07+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ai-and-i-am-searching-for-a-breakfast-staple-substitute.4459/post-60075>

I throw frozen large shrimp in a pan and cook them until they turn pink...a splash of lemon juice and garlic and some avocado on the side.

Some days I go for the MHS, or some scallops and bacon. I miss eggs too.

### kathylu — 2012-10-25T05:38:34+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ai-and-i-am-searching-for-a-breakfast-staple-substitute.4459/post-60082>

Shrimp (frozen, cooked salad sized, 50-100/lb.) and smoked oyster (canned) salad. Mix with paleo mayo (made with olive oil), lemon and lemon zest, finely diced onion and cilantro. Salt and pepper to taste. Yum.

### Endless — 2012-10-25T05:45:24+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ai-and-i-am-searching-for-a-breakfast-staple-substitute.4459/post-60084>

That sounds yummy....

### Glen PDQ — 2012-10-25T08:35:00+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ai-and-i-am-searching-for-a-breakfast-staple-substitute.4459/post-60094>

I think I found a decent drink to go with breakfast made with tea. I brewed a large cup of black tea, hot, with 2 teaspoons of honey, 1/4 stick of Kerrygold butter, 2 Tbls of coconut oil, and frothed in the blender. Very nice.

I don't eat any other sugar except for small portions of sauteed or poached sweet potato now and then and an 80% dark chocolate bar once in a blue moon.

### Glen PDQ — 2012-10-25T08:38:19+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ai-and-i-am-searching-for-a-breakfast-staple-substitute.4459/post-60095>

Would sweetbreads be a good replacement for eggs?

### SeaJane — 2012-10-25T17:08:42+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ai-and-i-am-searching-for-a-breakfast-staple-substitute.4459/post-60136>

> **Patty Cakes;61420 wrote:** I have a burger almost every morning and have been doing this since Jan. I prepare burgers three days worth at a time. Also in the evening I may prepare short ribs and cook them on 200 all night and have that for breakfast. Seafood for me is not filling enough for the morning. After breakfast I only eat fish but the morning needs to be beef. Simple, easy and can do them every day.

I found this interesting as my experience has been the opposite. I am much more satisfied eating seafood in the morning than landfood. The thing is it is even usually less calories. My typical breakfast is a seafood soup that includes a few dried shiratake mushrooms, some low carb veggies, a tablespoon of Olive oil, and then a mix of seafood that may include mussels, tiny wild oregon shrimp, squid, octopus, salmon, cod depending on what is around. I am in Seattle and have been getting fresh local mussels at Costco for $2.99/lb in 5 lb bags and cook them and shell them on the weekend. Then a have some everyday with breakfast. I also am able to get the tiny shrimp at Costco fresh for about $3.75/lb. These are my regulars and the others I add in. Overall, I eat about 50-55g of protein at breakfast.

### MamaGrok — 2012-10-25T17:45:24+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ai-and-i-am-searching-for-a-breakfast-staple-substitute.4459/post-60144>

I gotta eat landfood with my seafood or it will never fill me up. I have some of each, each morning.

# Thread: Thing on eyelid
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/thing-on-eyelid.4460/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 4
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Michael — 2012-10-23T09:18:03+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/thing-on-eyelid.4460/post-59794>

I thought I'd post this here, as it kind of recalled Inger's disappearing wrinkles. LOL

Anyway, I had this thing on my lower right eyelid. I don't know what it was. I was a bit like a wart only tiny: I was probably the only person that knew it was there. Strangely, it sort of dried up. I could tell that when I touched it with my finger, so I sort of gently prised it off with my fingernail and it came away very easily. That was a week ago, and it hasn't come back. This thing, whatever it was, clearing up could of course have been pure coincidence, but maybe it's related to increasing the percentage of seafood in my diet and adding seaweed a few weeks ago ...

### Huck — 2012-10-23T15:50:16+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/thing-on-eyelid.4460/post-59809>

Sounds like a skin tag.

### ealachan — 2012-10-23T16:54:48+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/thing-on-eyelid.4460/post-59813>

It was your body casting off the last vestiges of conventional wisdom. DEMONS OUT!

### Michael — 2012-10-23T17:03:13+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/thing-on-eyelid.4460/post-59815>

> **ealachan;61387 wrote:** It was your body casting off the last vestiges of conventional wisdom. DEMONS OUT!

It seems to fit the description of the thing Huck mentioned (except it was even smaller if anything than it says those tend to be):

[http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Acrochordon](http://http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Acrochordon)

I prefer your explanation, though. That's really funny.

### Michael — 2012-10-23T09:37:10+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/maori-fishing.4461/post-59797>

[http://www.teara.govt.nz/en/te-hi-ika-maori-fishing](http://http://www.teara.govt.nz/en/te-hi-ika-maori-fishing)

Fishhooks:

[http://collections.tepapa.govt.nz/theme.aspx?irn=1467](http://http://collections.tepapa.govt.nz/theme.aspx?irn=1467)

I found that jewellery in the form of a stylized Maori fishhook made into a pendant is a thriving industry in New Zealand. Whether this really is a tradition from the Maoris or a modern invention of a tradition for purely commercial purposes I don't know. If it actually is something the Maoris wore, I guess it speaks volumes about the importance of the sea in Maori culture.

# Thread: Mesolithic to Neolithic transition in Britain and Denmark
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/mesolithic-to-neolithic-transition-in-britain-and-denmark.4463/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 1
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Michael — 2012-10-23T15:19:11+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/mesolithic-to-neolithic-transition-in-britain-and-denmark.4463/post-59805>

Interestingly, it turns out that the Neolithic in Britain and Denmark is marked by a shift from fish to meat, from "maritime to terrestrial sources".

Not what we're usually told.

[http://eprints.whiterose.ac.uk/5606/](http://http://eprints.whiterose.ac.uk/5606/)

Full text unfortunately not available without subscription:

[http://antiquity.ac.uk/ant/078/Ant0780009b.htm](http://http://antiquity.ac.uk/ant/078/Ant0780009b.htm)

See also this earlier article

[http://www.archaeologyuk.org/ba/ba12/BA12FEAT.HTML](http://http://www.archaeologyuk.org/ba/ba12/BA12FEAT.HTML)

> The small-scale study - the first of its kind - of the bones of about 23 Neolithic people from ten sites in central and southern England, suggests that these \`first farmers' relied heavily on animal meat for food, or on animal by-products such as milk and cheese, and that plant foods in fact formed little importance in their diet. The bones date from throughout the Neolithic, c 4100BC - c 2000BC.

This latter study I was already aware of, and it has long interested me as showing that blanket claims about what people were eating in the past in different periods are quite erroneous. I still frequently hear "paleo" advocates speak as if around 10,000 years ago everyone stopped eating meat and vegetables and started eating grain. Not in North-West Europe they didn't. Neither did they to an appreciable extent, it seems, even into what is recognized as the Neolithic.

# Thread: A different take on calcium and oxalates
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/a-different-take-on-calcium-and-oxalates.4477/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 6
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### chocolate — 2012-10-24T15:39:49+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/a-different-take-on-calcium-and-oxalates.4477/post-59967>

[http://www.greatplainslaboratory.com/home/span/oxalates.asp](http://http://www.greatplainslaboratory.com/home/span/oxalates.asp)

> Oxalate and its acid form oxalic acid are organic acids that are primarily from three sources: the diet, from fungus such as Aspergillus and Penicillium and **possibly Candida (1-9), and also from human metabolism (10).
>
>
>
>
> High oxalate in the urine and plasma was first found in people who were susceptible to kidney stones. Many kidney stones are composed of calcium oxalate. Stones can range in size from the diameter of a grain of rice to the width of a golf ball. It is estimated that 10% of males may have kidney stones some time in their life. Because many kidney stones contain calcium, some people with kidney stones think they should avoid calcium supplements.
>
>
>
> However, the opposite is true. When calcium is taken with foods that are high in oxalates, oxalic acid in the intestine combines with calcium to form insoluble calcium oxalate crystals that are eliminated in the stool. This form of oxalate cannot be absorbed into the body. When calcium is low in the diet, oxalic acid is soluble in the liquid portion of the contents of the intestine (called chyme) and is readily absorbed from the intestine into the bloodstream. If oxalic acid is very high in the blood being filtered by the kidney, it may combine with calcium to form crystals that may block urine flow and cause severe pain**.

### SeaJane — 2012-10-24T16:24:15+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/a-different-take-on-calcium-and-oxalates.4477/post-59971>

The taking of Ca or Mag right before a meal is a pretty standard practice for folks on the Trying Low Oxalates Yahoo group. Most find this helpful and that it does not exacerbate their issues.

### villjamur_stevenson — 2012-10-24T16:24:46+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/a-different-take-on-calcium-and-oxalates.4477/post-59972>

> However, the opposite is true. When calcium is taken with foods that are high in oxalates, oxalic acid in the intestine combines with calcium to form insoluble calcium oxalate crystals that are eliminated in the stool. This form of oxalate cannot be absorbed into the body. When calcium is low in the diet, oxalic acid is soluble in the liquid portion of the contents of the intestine (called chyme) and is readily absorbed from the intestine into the bloodstream. If oxalic acid is very high in the blood being filtered by the kidney, it may combine with calcium to form crystals that may block urine flow and cause severe pain.

If this is the different take, then what is the normal take on it?

I've always thought calcium binding to oxalate in the gut was the normal view???

On a side note, sort of related - casein lowers uric acid levels.

### chocolate — 2012-10-24T16:57:10+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/a-different-take-on-calcium-and-oxalates.4477/post-59977>

Calcium causes kidney stones, so don't supp.

### chocolate — 2012-10-24T16:57:41+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/a-different-take-on-calcium-and-oxalates.4477/post-59978>

That's the other take.

### chocolate — 2012-10-25T03:29:19+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/a-different-take-on-calcium-and-oxalates.4477/post-60070>

> **PSJane;61563 wrote: The taking of Ca or Mag right before a meal is a pretty standard practice for folks on the Trying Low Oxalates Yahoo group. Most find this helpful and that it does not exacerbate their issues.
>
>
> I've been seriously asleep at the wheel on this one.
>
> [http://www.stopthethyroidmadness.com/2011/07/03/low-magnesium-uk-article-hypothyroid-spouse/](http://http://www.stopthethyroidmadness.com/2011/07/03/low-magnesium-uk-article-hypothyroid-spouse/)
>
>
>
> But it turns out if you consistently eat a LOT of food containing high levels of oxalates, on top of what your body naturally produces, your body will have excessive levels. This may be exactly what happened to me this year, also causing chronic hives (scratch~scratch~scratch). The latter pushed me to do research, and voila, I realized I had consumed far too many high-oxalate foods (which in turn produced excessive histamine). And my eyes popped out when I read this:
>
>
>
> “oxalates strongly bind to minerals and vice versa (e.g., calcium, magnesium, zinc and potassium**), and reduces the absorption of your minerals as they both come out via your urine.”
>
>
>
> It’s a negative love affair for your cellular mineral levels!!
>
>
>
> What are foods which are the highest in oxalates? In alphabetical order, they include:
>
>
>
> almonds, amaranth, black beans, brazil nuts, beets, blackberries, buckwheat, carob chips, carrots, cashew nuts, cannellini beans, celery, chocolate, corn meal, dried apricots, cooked tomatoes, great northern beans, green peppers, hazelnuts, marshmallow root, milk thistle, navy beans, oil of oregano, okra, peanuts, pecans, pine nuts, pink beans, pinto beans, potato chips, potato flour, rice bran, rhubarb, sesame seeds and tahini, slippery elm bark, all soy, spinach, star fruit, sweet potatoes, teff (flour and whole grain), quinoa (whole grain), white bean flour, and yucca powder.

# Thread: Project: Bone Broth
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/project-bone-broth.4478/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 12
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### ealachan — 2012-10-24T17:30:12+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/project-bone-broth.4478/post-59980>

So, I went in search of beef bones last night to make a batch of broth. Marrow bones at my local grocery store are $3.99 a friggin' pound!!! NOT EVEN FROM GRASSFED COWS. This seems SO expensive to me. Am I way off base, or does this sound like highway robbery to you, too?

I've started saving bones from roasts and steaks that I cook, but I wanted to supplement those with some bigger bones. Does anyone have any experience using something like knuckle bones (aka: "bones for dogs", per my local deli guy) as opposed to the pre-cut marrow bones? I'm wondering if having lots of little bones as opposed to just one or two BIG bones really matters that much? Also, a knuckle bone has a ton of connective tissue and cartiledge in it - wouldn't that make for a more gelatinous broth, theoretically? I don't have the means to split a big bone like a knuckle, if that matters any...

### Michael — 2012-10-24T17:54:02+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/project-bone-broth.4478/post-59987>

Depends how big your stockpot is I guess.

There's a video online of Cate Shanahan's husband, who's a chef, making stock, and that is exactly what he's using.

You could probably find it by googling. Cate demonstrates how the joint works, and Sean Croxton is assisting, too.

Maybe include some kombu, too?

### ealachan — 2012-10-24T18:00:40+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/project-bone-broth.4478/post-59988>

I want to use a pressure cooker, a la Nom Nom Paleo. I've done it in the crockpot before and it came out well, but it took forever and the smell became totally overwhelming after the first 12 hours or so.

Definitely want to try adding some Kombu, yes. I've been toasting sheets of kombu and eating them like chips and I really enjoy it.

If I can do some recon and figure out when the meat cutter is around at the local mom and pop grocery store, I might be able to get him to split some knuckle bones for me so that they fit into the pressure cooker more easily. It seems fitting that I'd be contemplating stalking the local meatcutter, honestly...LOL

### FarmersDaughter — 2012-10-24T18:14:51+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/project-bone-broth.4478/post-59989>

I use knuckles unsplit all the time, more than marrow bones, actually, and the gelatinous goodness oozes out after a couple hours. Looks yummy, and makes nice firm "jello". I used pork hocks a lot to make broth for split pea soup (or, well, I did, anyway) and they dissolve entirely, all that ligamenty and cartilaginous stuff makes for serious umami in the broth.

I don't have a pressure cooker, but I put my big stewpot into the oven at 250 and let it simmer all night after I've kept an eye on it all day.

If you let them sit for a while in the cooker with some vinegar, you can eventually dig a hole into the bone and let the marrow and other innards leach out. Takes a while with beef bones, veal knuckles are quicker but harder to find around here.

### ealachan — 2012-10-24T18:28:49+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/project-bone-broth.4478/post-59993>

mmm, pork hocks. One of my favorite things in the world is new potatoes and green beans cooked in the crock pot with smoked pork hocks. I haven't made it in forever, but damn it's good.

Sorry, mild pork-hock-related tangent there. Ahem.

### FarmersDaughter — 2012-10-24T19:00:03+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/project-bone-broth.4478/post-59997>

I was recently introduced to the delish that is nÃ©w potatoes this summer.

Omg. How did I not know about those?

Come summer, I'll definitely be indulging in that combo.

### diane — 2012-10-24T19:42:48+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/project-bone-broth.4478/post-60003>

I throw any kind of bones in to make bone broth. Neck bones, knuckles, marrow bones. whatever I buy for the dogs - well, I just take some out for me too. Then I sometimes put some bone broth in their dishes - they love it!

### Shijin13 — 2012-10-24T20:20:11+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/project-bone-broth.4478/post-60010>

Before we got into our meat CSA I'd ask the butcher at WFM if he had any bones left over from local beef. No particular cut. He gave them to me for $1.99/lb which is what we pay for them at the meat CSA now

### BJK77 — 2012-10-24T21:36:45+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/project-bone-broth.4478/post-60022>

I use giant knuckle bones when I can get them and they make an awesome, gelatinous broth. I've only been able to find them at one local farm and they're $1.99 lb here. If I can't get them I just use marrow bones, but my broth is never as thick or flavorful with them. The cheapest I can get them for is $4.99 lb, but I do love eating the marrow. Yum!

### ealachan — 2012-10-24T21:45:54+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/project-bone-broth.4478/post-60025>

> **BJK77;61616 wrote:** I use giant knuckle bones when I can get them and they make an awesome, gelatinous broth. I've only been able to find them at one local farm and they're $1.99 lb here. If I can't get them I just use marrow bones, but my broth is never as thick or flavorful with them. The cheapest I can get them for is $4.99 lb, but I do love eating the marrow. Yum!

Do you bother to have the knuckle bones split, or do you use them whole?

### Glamazon — 2012-10-24T21:51:49+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/project-bone-broth.4478/post-60026>

add some splashes of wine or apple cider vinegar. it helps leach the minerals out of the bones into the broth.

### BJK77 — 2012-10-24T23:37:20+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/project-bone-broth.4478/post-60035>

> **ealachan;61619 wrote:** Do you bother to have the knuckle bones split, or do you use them whole?

I use them whole.

# Thread: Nausea while icing
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/nausea-while-icing.4481/>
**Subforum:** Cold Thermogenesis
**Posts:** 8
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### FarmersDaughter — 2012-10-24T20:34:36+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/nausea-while-icing.4481/post-60012>

The last several times I have either ct or spot iced (belly or between shoulder blades) I have had nausea. Never enough to cause vomiting, but certainly enough to be decidedly unpleasant. Mostly like a carsickish feeling...motion sick.

My water is 55-60 out of the tap so no ice used yet, body temp is consistently 99+, and I maintain a pink to reddish skin color when icing ( no itching or urticaria). I should mention that it doesn't seem coincide with a general feeling of malaise or otherwise, in fact when I am feeling crummy it doesn't seem to happen, so in my mind, that shrinks the odds of a herx reaction, doesn't it? It disappears as soon as I'm out of the cold.

Any ideas what causes this? I'm pushing through, but I'm curious. If its covered in a post and I've missed it in the multiple re readings, please point in the right direction! I'm wondering if its related to ACTH/aMSH production....I had an ACTH stim done to check pit function some years ago, and it caused sudden intense nausea that last about 5 minutes, then vanished as quickly as it appeared.

### nonchalant — 2012-10-25T01:01:25+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/nausea-while-icing.4481/post-60050>

Sorry, FD, I didn't have any nausea when first starting CT. Did you do the ramp-up? Start with face dunks for a couple weeks? It took me several weeks to get down to the 50-55F range for baths.

### Patty Cakes — 2012-10-25T03:07:19+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/nausea-while-icing.4481/post-60065>

I had a little nausea for about the first four months. I think it was the detoxing from estrogen or other bad guys. Keep doing it and the nausea will subside sooner or later. I also started to itch on my back a lot. That went away too. I started in the 60s temps for the bath and worked my way down to 55.

### FarmersDaughter — 2012-10-25T21:05:03+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/nausea-while-icing.4481/post-60183>

Hmm. Yep, ramped up, and actually have been doing the face dunks since April,stopped doing CT because of rapid weight gain...20 pounds in roughly two months. I didnt ramp up (down?) bathtemp, just dove in to +/- 60ish. It comes and goes, seemingly regardless of meals and am/pm.

I'll reread CT6 and see what I've missed there...like I said, not THAT big a deal, I'm just curious about the whys.

I've started to itch a little, which I'm taking as a good sign...and I have significantly reduced "fluff" around my waist, so I'm absolutely continuing. Heh...couple weeks down, couple months to go. DIM and altering estrogen metabolism might have something to do with it too,who knows.

One of the many mysteries I guess.

TY

### BJK77 — 2012-10-25T23:16:13+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/nausea-while-icing.4481/post-60190>

I've had random, off and on episodes of nausea from CT. I'm sure there could be many causes for it, but Jack mentioned to me that in my case it could be a detox reaction, possibly from halide toxicity. Like I said, for me it's totally random. It will happen for a week straight and then not again for a month or two. Very bizarre!

### kcorcoran@comcast.net — 2012-10-26T04:36:14+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/nausea-while-icing.4481/post-60257>

This may be tmi, but I had a major gut reaction clean out from ct last spring. I think it disturbs the gut biofilm and ramps up the immune system. Are you getting salivation with the nausea? Seems to be a vagal nerve stimulation thing is my guess. Maybe give your system a few days to recover between ct sessions. The body needs time to clear out toxins.

### FarmersDaughter — 2012-10-26T07:07:07+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/nausea-while-icing.4481/post-60269>

Hmmm....halides, biofilms, and somewhere I ran across mycotoxins as well.

Truthfully, it's probably a many causes, single symptom thing.

I think I may start taking NAC again...it's good for helping the various hepatic pathways do their thing...glutathione, I think, among others?

You know, I had the random thought that it might be vagus nerve stuff, but I haven't wrapped my head around all that it does, so I may just been whistling Dixie too. I dunno. All I know is that it's affecting my sinuses and my schnozzle in addition to the whole queasiness business.

And THIRSTY. Dear heaven I haven't been this thirsty in years.

### PrimalPam — 2012-10-26T23:32:53+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/nausea-while-icing.4481/post-60367>

I, too, get queasy sometimes. The strange thing for me is I lay on my back in the tub (50-52F) for 20-25 mins, then I turn over and lay on my stomach for 20-25 mins. I don't get the queasiness until I've turned over onto my stomach. I just lay there and deal with it, and it's never been bad enough to get out.

I do eat protein & fat just prior, and I rarely ever drink alcohol. I just think it's a bit of detox going on, so I figure it's a good thing.

# Thread: Look at this, Inger!

**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 9

### Michael — 2012-10-24T21:43:19+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/look-at-this-inger.4484/post-60024>

Quote from Inger:

> I am wondering what is in Birch ash that is so good? When I put the ash leftovers from the sauna out for our sheeps to taste on they love it. If we have burned some threes or bushes on their pasture thei love to lie down in the ash at times...
>
>
>
> I also read about children in the second world war when people were so poor and the children ate ash too. I am thinking there must be some important minerals in it?
>
>
> I don't know whether it works or not, but it turns out that it is sold as a health food.
>
>
>
> It says it provides:
>
>
>
> Potassium (K), Copper (Cu), Magnesium (Mg) Manganese (Mn), calcium (Ca) Molybdenum (Mo), phosphorus (P), iron (Fe), Selenium (Se), Zinc (Zn), Chromium (Cr) Cobalt (Co)

[http://www.halsans.com/en/null/immuforsvarshojandemedel/birch-ash-powder/](http://http://www.halsans.com/en/null/immuforsvarshojandemedel/birch-ash-powder/)

### Inger — 2012-10-25T11:41:09+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/look-at-this-inger.4484/post-60109>

Michael, that is intresting, thank you! We can buy the birch ash extract here too, liquid. It cost 30 € for one bottle..huh. I thought about making it myself. Birch was the magic tree of my ancestors. I read somwhere it was the first tree that popped up after the ice melted in Scandinavia!

### Michael — 2012-10-25T11:57:48+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/look-at-this-inger.4484/post-60110>

It's sown by Vainamoinen in the Kalevala, I see:

[http://www.sacred-texts.com/neu/kveng/kvrune02.htm](http://http://www.sacred-texts.com/neu/kveng/kvrune02.htm)

### Inger — 2012-10-25T12:17:59+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/look-at-this-inger.4484/post-60112>

Michael..?! You are amazing! You kind of find all kind of weird things..! I wonder about you!

I have read this maybe once in school almost 30 years ago..lol I always found the old tales of Finland like Kalevala kind of sad back then... it might be because I am Norwegian and the culture was foreign to me. Sure differs a lot from the Norwegian and Schwedisch one!

### Michael — 2012-10-25T12:46:10+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/look-at-this-inger.4484/post-60114>

It would be a quite different cultural root, I guess. It's a Finno-Ugric language, isn't it? The language and mythology are akin to Lithuanian.

I don't know I like the _Kalevala that much. But that might be because I only know it in translation, where so much is lost.

The most well-known story seems to be that of Kullervo and his sister, which is a very tragic story. But there's knockabout humour in there, too - like the ladies' man Lemminkainen, seducing all the women on an island.

I like the Jean Sibelius settings of the Kalevala_ a lot. Shame he drank himself mad.

### Inger — 2012-10-25T13:41:43+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/look-at-this-inger.4484/post-60115>

Yes it is a Finno-Ugric language. Totally different to Norwegian or Swedish. My parents still cannot speak fluent even if they have lived here for almost 40 years..lol.

You know people drink here a lot. That is sad for sure, they drink so much, until very drunk. I lived in Hamburg (Germany) for 10 years and there was a totally different alcohol-behavior. I liked it much better! But yeah.. maybe people get depressed from long dark winters and need to drink.. if they have not too much love in their life..?

I figure I love the dark now first time in my life. What an amazing change. The street lamps are strarting to bothering me huge! They makes it so hard to see the beautiful stars and the heaven! People have cut themselves out from the most beautiful things with their crazy fake lights for sure! lol

I am laying naked on my balkony right now in the fresh air.. enjoying the sun and my day off.. wow Life cannot be better...!

### Michael — 2012-10-26T08:45:07+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/look-at-this-inger.4484/post-60271>

I dislike street lamps, too. I preferred it when I lived in the country and there were none. You couldn't see your hand in front of your face walking back from the pub.

Did you know the action of the greatest poem in Old English - _Beowulf_ - is set in Sweden? England lies sort of midway between Scandinavia and the European mainstream represented by France and her neighbours in geographical terms. The Norman Conquest moved us decisively out of the orbit of the first and into that of the second.

# Thread: Vast Welsh fish trap spotted on Google Earth
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/vast-welsh-fish-trap-spotted-on-google-earth.4490/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 4
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Michael — 2012-10-25T09:18:31+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/vast-welsh-fish-trap-spotted-on-google-earth.4490/post-60099>

The story's from 2009, but worth seeing anyway.

> Stretching more than 280 yards along the sea bed, the V-shaped structure was used to catch fish without the need for a boat or rod. Scientists believe it is one of the biggest of its kind.
>
>
>
> The trap close to Poppit Sands on the Teifi Estuary in Dyfed was discovered by archaeologists studying aerial photographs of the West Wales coast.

[http://www.dailymail.co.uk/sciencetech/article-1162395/Google-Earth-reveals-fish-trap-rocks-1-000-years-ago-British-coast.html](http://http://www.dailymail.co.uk/sciencetech/article-1162395/Google-Earth-reveals-fish-trap-rocks-1-000-years-ago-British-coast.html)

The sheer size of this thing shows how important fish must have been in the diet of the people at the time it was built. And yet "for a millennium it has lain undisturbed beneath the waves a stone's throw from one of Britain's best-loved beaches".

Interestingly, the article says that fish traps and weirs had become controversial at the time of the Norman Conquest, owing to their effect on fish stocks.

### nonchalant — 2012-10-30T00:59:18+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/vast-welsh-fish-trap-spotted-on-google-earth.4490/post-60826>

Michael, you dig up the most interesting cites...

Must have been aliens helping them. ;-)

### PrimalPam — 2012-11-01T01:26:51+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/vast-welsh-fish-trap-spotted-on-google-earth.4490/post-61186>

Interesting! And yes, it shows that seafood has been important for a long time. Thanks for posting it!

### shah78 — 2012-11-01T02:31:43+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/vast-welsh-fish-trap-spotted-on-google-earth.4490/post-61196>

# Thread: Glucose and fructose
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/glucose-and-fructose.4494/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 6
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Michael — 2012-10-25T15:52:55+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/glucose-and-fructose.4494/post-60128>

I may have the details in this wrong. I was very distracted while listening. But here goes.

So Jimmy Moore's guest on the latest podcast was a university researcher, name of Johnson, who says that he used to believe, like Dr. Lustig, that fructose was responsible for obesity. Then he wondered about all carbohydrates. Now he's somehere in between.

What he said is that they found it easy to produce obesity in rodents by feeding them fructose ("fruit sugar"). However, the animals tend not to get obese when given a high proportion of starch (which is, of course, a polysaccaride, and hence a source of glucose when metabolized) in their feed.

He also said that mice that were genetically-engineered not to be able to break down fructose did not get obese.

For both reasons they were persuaded fructose was the key.

But he says after talking to Jimmy before and finding out that Jimmy just could not tolerate any carbohydrate, he began to doubt whether he'd explored everything. So they tried other things, including putting a glucose solution in the animals water-bottles. _That, apparently, does tend to make animals obese. And they also revised their earlier opinion on starch, finding that even starch will _to a certain extent.

But here's the surprise. He says the special mice that couldn't be damaged by fructose also_ could not get fat on glucose or starch. Looking into this, they decided that fuctose actually is_ the key and that what can happen is that glucose can be converted into fructose by liver enzymes. In some people, he says, this gets uprated like mad. And a good way to do this is to drink soda.

They speculate that what makes beer so fattening is that something else in it - the proteins perhaps - help to uprate these enzymes. And it gets even more speculative after this point. ...

I'm not so sure on the beer. I know the SCD people warn people off it, because they say that the malt sugars in it are too complex to be easily broken down, so that they become food for the wrong sort of bacteria. I'd have thought something like that could be equally plausible.

But, at any rate, it is interesting to know that glucose can be converted to fructose by the liver, and that some people's livers do this to an uncontrolled extent.

### FarmersDaughter — 2012-10-25T16:58:19+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/glucose-and-fructose.4494/post-60132>

Very interesting! Thanks for posting...I enjoy reading your links.

### Michael — 2012-10-25T17:06:49+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/glucose-and-fructose.4494/post-60134>

Thanks.

Actually, maybe a link would be useful here:

[http://www.thelivinlowcarbshow.com/shownotes/6904/630-dr-richard-johnson-shifts-obesity-culprit-position-from-fructose-to-all-carbohydrates/](http://http://www.thelivinlowcarbshow.com/shownotes/6904/630-dr-richard-johnson-shifts-obesity-culprit-position-from-fructose-to-all-carbohydrates/)

### nonchalant — 2012-10-25T18:42:57+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/glucose-and-fructose.4494/post-60157>

Why would the liver convert glucose (a useful molecule) into fructose (a toxic molecule that the liver tries to keep out of the circulating blood)? Or do I have that wrong? I thought the liver will cause itself damage (from the fructose) in an attempt to keep fructose from damaging the rest of the body. Take one for the team, if you will.

### Michael — 2012-10-25T19:47:13+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/glucose-and-fructose.4494/post-60169>

I don't know. He didn't make any suggestions as to why - he just said it did. And darned if I could.

I guess there are actually two questions. Why would it make the conversion at all? (Presumably doing so at a low rate in some situations likely has its uses or we wouldn't do it at all.) And secondly, why does the conversion get up-regulated?

I guess the answer to the second one might be that it's something else to _do with glucose in a hurry (besides turning it into fat) before it does damage.

It seems whatever you do you train yourself to do better. Dr. Cate likes to say that by eating sugar continually (and pretty much continuously, too) most people are training themselves to become very good at burning sugar and very poor at burning fat - because they're down-regulatung their fat-burning enzymes. I'd guess that principle is everywhere in biochemistry - as in life.

I suppose the real takeaway is simply that eating out of the ancestral pattern is going to screw things up in one way or another. It's not that fructose is "bad" or something. It's that we're only likely to be able to deal with it in its "evolutionary package" (to use a Krusism) and at appropriate levels in the appropriate season.

The recent New York Times_ story impresses me. This guy isn't eating as the Paleo people would tell him to. But what he's doing works at least as well (if not better). It's whole foods; it's seasonal; it has the fish. He's not going to be eating anything out of its evolutionary package or out of season, because the island probably doesn't offer an infrastructure where he can.

[http://www.nytimes.com/2012/10/28/magazine/the-island-where-people-forget-to-die.html?pagewanted=all](http://http://www.nytimes.com/2012/10/28/magazine/the-island-where-people-forget-to-die.html?pagewanted=all)

The deeper irony here is that he went to find the American Dream and it nearly killed him. He went back home, and paradise was there all along.

I don't mean it was paradise because he had a long life, but because he had a pain-free and stress-free life running in natural rhythms close to nature, and a social life in cooperation and harmony with his neighbours, and because the food probably tasted of something and satisfied him.

### Destiny — 2012-10-27T13:26:48+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/glucose-and-fructose.4494/post-60420>

Just my practical observation. I had a really hard time losing weight on paleo and/or low carb diet with a bit of fruit (usually berries). It took me 10 months to lose 9 lbs. I also had a lot of times when I was going up and down 4 lbs for no reason. That was really frustrating.

Since cutting ANY fruit out, I am down additional 7 lbs. I am at 136 lbs and finally getting closer to my weight around 130 lbs that I was at most of my life.

This also goes with my FBG that was between 95-110. Now, my FBG is between 78-84.


══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# Thread: Need Accountability Stat!
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/need-accountability-stat.4496/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 24
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### MamaGrok — 2012-10-25T17:25:14+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/need-accountability-stat.4496/post-60140>

It's been almost one year since my last binge. The last two months have been tough, though ... but only with one thing - fruit. And it must stop! I've got crazy cravings for fruit, crazy, and went crazy with dried fruit yesterday (nothin' but fruit).

Today I've allowed myself one apple to taper off (whole, not dried), and that's it. NO MORE FRUIT till like April.

Help me!

So far I've had a 70g protein/fat breakfast with radish greens, and the apple. I'm posting here every time I eat (should be just one more time) or feel compelled to eat for the rest of the day.

Pray for me! I sense this going downhill fast if I don't get these cravings in control now. Right now.

### Shijin13 — 2012-10-25T17:45:04+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/need-accountability-stat.4496/post-60143>

question for you MamaG. wondering if the Hashi's dx, might have triggered something that causing the craving for fruits, as a result of all the restrictions?

Can you increase your fat consumption, to help keep it at bay? Of recent I've had serious chocolate cravings... and for a while I just had chocolate every night until I ran out of my 100% dark chocolate.... then stopped... but suddenly the chocolate craving came back - so I did it again... no there's nothing in the house... and I don't want the chocolate... not saying its the same thing...

I'm having an apple or winter squash ever couple of days right now, b/c they're in season - I don't eat a lot of them...but I always pair it w/something FATTY. could you cook the apple in oodles of ghee (I forget if you were given the OK to eat Ghee - as some w/AI can and other can't) or Coconut Oil to address the craving.... hang in there... you'll make it you always do!

### MamaGrok — 2012-10-25T17:50:00+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/need-accountability-stat.4496/post-60146>

Interesting thought ... but I don't think it's the case here. I'll say why and you can poke holes in my logic, lol.

The cravings started in June. I was restricting almost anything but meat, fat, & well-cooked greens at the time. I ate some fruit here and there, occasionally too much, all summer. The Hashi's dx came in mid-September. At the same time, I decided to ignore the bloating & cramps & runs and just eat. Meat, fat, non-starchy veggies, but allowing FODMAPs again, here and there. The cravings continued. I think it's been worse the last two months, but it may just be that I'm really facing the end of warm weather and can't use the summer excuse to eat fruit anymore.

I always try to eat meat & fat before the fruit, but then I just keep coming back to the fruit again and again even after a full meal. Want to beat my head against a wall! I think it's just going to have to be will power that gets me through this one, but it's so frustrating bc I had NO fruit cravings at all for so long.

On a good note, I think my thyroid is LOVING the Naturethroid. Week 2. On a bad note, I woke up this morning after 8.5hrs feeling like I needed 9 more hrs of sleep and 3lb heavier than yesterday. !

### nonchalant — 2012-10-25T18:02:00+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/need-accountability-stat.4496/post-60147>

Mamagrok, there must be something your body needs. I wonder if taking quercetin (from apples) would help?

One thing else that I have noticed is that I'm used to not having to fight temptation anymore, with this highly satiating diet. So I think my self control gets a bit less vigilant.

### Shijin13 — 2012-10-25T18:10:37+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/need-accountability-stat.4496/post-60151>

remember apples and pears are in season now... no reason you can have 1/day and split it up across your meals?

I wonder if the NDT might be impacting, some of your food cravings? IDK. I do know when my thyroid is off, I crave the chocolate, as well as foods w/K2 (ghee and HWC?) I just upped my compounded Armour from 1/2 grain to 1 grain....

I'm taking the quercetin to address my allergies, and it does help those...

### MamaGrok — 2012-10-25T18:59:52+0300

### Shijin13 — 2012-10-25T19:17:36+0300

>

>

maybe its not the time to have 1. IDK. I'm inclined to think that there's something in the Hashi's driving this for you specifically. on the days you don't have the fruit - how do you feel, how do you feel the following day? what about on the days you have the fruit and spiral into the dried fruit? how do you feel, what about the next day? how's your sleep etc? additionally, could this be something your thyroid (figure low T3 possibly) is driving you towards the fruit?

my n=1, is when carbs decrease - my TSH kicks into high gear, but I'm not making enough T4, to convert to T3, I get HOT "flashes" but not the menopausal kind, I feel like my furnace is smoking, and I just can't cool down, yet I'm having problems getting myself in to the ice bath b/c of the Low T3. go back and re-read BG11... you might find something here... I think your body is trying to carb up to replace the fact you're not making enough T3, so its looking for the natural circadian solution, only you're moving into winter and its not right - but your body doesn't know it. have you run thyroid labs since you started NDT? might be time for another set to see if you need to up the dose? if you fix the supply issues for the T4 ->T3 your cravings might just go away....

### tercay@yahoo.com — 2012-10-25T20:09:54+0300

Dear MG,

I have had years of fruit binging. To eat 10-12 apples a day (or other various fruit combinations) was a normal day. The Leptin Rx helped to control it but this summer the urge began again even though on a slightly lesser scale. Dr Kruse's epi-paleo Rx has helped me to stop fruit binging. The key for me was to include the raw fish or scallops in the form of sashimi or ceviche everyday especially in my BAB with a variety of seaweeds. I have had no cravings these 2 months since I have done this. I feel like my body is actually nourished on a cellular level so I'm not prowling for more. After over 40 years of on again off again fruit binging the urge to prowl and consume quiet.

I used to have the same binges MamaGrok. I could eat a whole bag of dried fruit and an entire meal and still not feel full, but my stomach would be so distended (I would look 4 months+ pregnant and feel drugged/ hungover). I found that usually it was my gut was so imbalanced. Since I have been taking Curcumin, Resveratol, Vit D3, K2, milk thistle, probiotics and progesterone, it has been much better. Large amounts of seafood, tons of bacon and duck fat, and CT. My family thinks I am nuts, but it is helping my gut.

Just trying to get my thyroid back in working condition too. I put on 30 pounds in 2 months this past February and have not been able to get rid of it yet.

I have been eating apples lately usually 1 or 2 because they are in season. I have not had any crazy binges for months, but I do know that I should probably up my fat throughout the day.

If you need accountability, I'm here.
